title: The Najd Expedition to Dhu 'Amarr and the Divine Protection of the Prophet
summary: During the Najd expedition, a polytheist warrior named Ghawrith b. al-Harith attempted to assassinate the Prophet while he was alone drying his clothes, but was repelled by the Angel Gabriel and subsequently embraced Islam.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "News reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) that a force of Ghatafan fighters, from Banu Tha'laba, Ibn Muharib, had gathered at Dhu 'Amarr intending to attack him. He therefore left Medina on Thursday, 12th of Rabi' al-Awwal in 3 AH, placing 'Uthman b. 'Affan in command of Medina. He was away for 11 days, and was accompanied by 450 men. "The bedouin fled from him away into the mountains. He travelled on until he reached a well known as Dhu 'Amarr, where he made camp. Much rain fell and the clothes of the Messenger of God (SAAS) were soaked. He dismounted beneath a tree there and hung out his clothes to dry, in full sight of the polytheists, who were keeping to themselves. "The polytheists then sent out one of their warriors, a man named Ghawrith b. al-Harith, or Du'thur b. al-Harith, telling him, 'God has enabled you to kill Muhammad!' "That man then went forth armed with a well-polished sword and reached the Messenger of God (SAAS). He stood before him, his sword drawn, and asked, 'Muhammad, who will protect you from me now?' 'God will,' he replied. Then Gabriel gave the man a push in the chest and the sword fell from his hand. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) picked it up and said, 'And who will protect you from me?' 'No one', the man replied, 'and I testify that there is no god but God and that Muhammad is God's messenger! I swear, I'll never gather a force to attack you.' "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave him back his sword. "When the man rejoined his men, they criticized him and asked what had happened. He explained, 'I saw a tall man who pushed me in the chest and made me fall over on to my back. I knew him to be an angel. I then testified that Muhammad is the Messenger of God and that I would not gather a force against him.' He then began inviting his people to accept Islam. "It was about this incident that God Almighty stated, 'O you who believe, remember God's favour to you when a force had prepared to stretch out their hands against you, but He held back their hands away from you'" (surat al-Ma'ida; V, v.11).
lessons: True protection comes from God alone; when we place our trust in Him completely, He intervenes in ways beyond human expectation.
Courage in the face of mortal danger, rooted in faith rather than human strength, can transform even an enemy into a believer.
Showing mercy and returning a weapon to a defeated enemy, as the Prophet did, can be more powerful than any act of force in winning hearts.
practical_advice: When facing a threatening situation that feels beyond your control, remind yourself of times God has protected you before and consciously say 'God will protect me' as an affirmation of tawakkul (trust in God).
If someone who has wronged or threatened you shows remorse, practice responding with generosity rather than punishment; this breaks cycles of enmity and can lead to profound transformation.
Keep a personal record of moments when you were in danger or distress and God brought you through unexpectedly; reviewing this list during anxious times builds genuine trust in divine protection.

title: The Affair of Banu Qaynuqa': The First Jewish Tribe to Break Their Covenant
summary: The Jewish tribe of Banu Qaynuqa' broke their covenant with the Prophet after a Muslim woman was publicly humiliated in their marketplace, leading to bloodshed, a siege, and their eventual expulsion from Medina.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "It was at the time of these raids made by the Messenger of God (SAAS) that the affair of the Banu Qaynuqa' arose. "People say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) assembled them in their market and addressed them, saying: 'O Jews, beware of God afflicting you as He did Quraysh. And so accept Islam. You well know that I am a prophet sent with a mission; you find that to be so in your Book and in God's pact with you.' "They responded, 'Muhammad, do you think we are your people? Don't delude yourself, just because you did battle with those who lacked knowledge of warfare, and so you could take advantage of them. If you fight against us, you'll find us to be real men!' "'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada related to me that the Banu Qaynuqa' were the first of the Jews who broke their pact and fought in the period between the battles of Badr and Uhud." Ibn Hisham stated, "'Abd Allah b. Ja'far b. al-Miswar b. Makhrama related, from Abu 'Awn, 'The problem with Banu Qaynuqa' arose as follows. An Arab woman took an ornament of hers and sold it in the market of Banu Qaynuqa'. She sat there with one of their goldsmiths and the men present began pestering her to uncover her face. She refused, but the goldsmith managed to attach the hem of her dress to her back. When she got up, she was exposed, and they all laughed at her. She screamed and one of the Muslim men attacked and killed the goldsmith, who was a Jew. The Jews then seized the Muslim and killed him. The Muslim's family called for help from the other Muslims against the Jews. The Muslims were enraged, and so enmity arose between them and Banu Qaynuqa'." Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada related to me, 'And so the Messenger of God (SAAS), besieged them until they surrendered to him.'"
lessons: Public humiliation and the violation of another person's dignity are serious moral offences that can rupture even the most carefully built social covenants.
Arrogance in the face of clear truth — dismissing guidance because of perceived strength — is a recurring human failure that leads to downfall.
A community's safety and honour are interconnected; an injury to one member, if left unaddressed, destabilises the entire fabric of social trust.
practical_advice: When you witness someone being humiliated or mocked, speak up or intervene in whatever way is safe; silence in the face of public shaming is itself a form of complicity.
If you find yourself responding to wise counsel with arrogance or defensiveness, pause and ask yourself honestly whether pride is blocking you from recognising a truth you need to hear.

After a conflict erupts within your community, reflect on the earliest breach of trust or dignity that triggered it; addressing root causes rather than only surface incidents leads to more lasting peace.

title: 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy Intercedes for Banu Qaynuqa' and the Response of 'Ubada b. al-Samit
summary: After the Prophet besieged and gained power over Banu Qaynuqa', the hypocrite leader 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy physically seized the Prophet's garment to demand clemency for his allies, while 'Ubada b. al-Samit publicly renounced his alliance with them in favour of God and His Messenger.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada related to me, 'And so the Messenger of God (SAAS), besieged them until they surrendered to him. 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy b. Sallal went to him after God had given him power over them and said, "Muhammad, treat my allies kindly!" They were allies of Banu al-Khazraj. He turned away from him, however, and so 'Abd Allah put his hand inside the pocket of the chain-mail of the Messenger of God (SAAS).'" Ibn Hisham stated that this pocket was known as the dhat al-fudull, "the bounteous". Ibn Ishaq continued, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told him 'Let go of me!' His face darkening in rage, he repeated, 'Let go of me, I say!' "'Abd Allah replied, 'No; I'll not do so until you agree to treat my allies well. Four hundred men without armour and three hundred with chain-mail have protected me from all and sundry, and you would mow them down in one morning! I'm a man who is afraid of turns of fate.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said to him, 'They are yours!'" Ibn Hisham stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) placed Abu Lubaba Bashir b. 'Abd al-Mundhir in charge (of Medina) during his siege of them, which lasted for 15 days." Ibn Ishaq stated, "My father related to me, quoting 'Ubada b. al-Walid, from 'Ubada b. al-Samit, who said, 'When Banu Qaynuqa' opposed the Messenger of God (SAAS) 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy remained allied with them and protected them, while 'Ubada b. al-Samit, who had the same alliance with them as did 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy, went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and gave them over to him, renouncing his pact with them in favour of God and His Messenger. 'Ubada told him, "O Messenger of God, I ally myself with God, His messenger and the believers, and I renounce my association and alliance with these unbelievers."'" Ibn Ishaq continued, "It was about him and 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy that the following verses were revealed, 'O you who believe, do not ally yourselves with the Jews and the Christians; they are allied with one another'" (surat al-Ma'ida; V, v.50). This referred to 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy. And "... those who ally themselves with God and His messenger and those who believe; they are the party of God who will..."
lessons: True loyalty is tested when standing with truth becomes costly; 'Ubada's willingness to renounce worldly alliances for the sake of God reflects the highest form of moral integrity.
Anger in leadership, when rooted in justice rather than ego, is a legitimate and necessary human response; the Prophet's face darkening in rage was a righteous reaction to being physically coerced.
The difference between a hypocrite and a sincere believer is often revealed not in times of comfort but in moments of communal crisis when allegiances must be declared openly.
practical_advice: When you are pressured by someone you respect or fear to compromise on a matter of principle, practise clearly and calmly restating your boundary — as the Prophet did by repeating 'Let go of me' — without anger overtaking your composure.

Examine your own allegiances honestly: are there relationships or group loyalties in your life that you maintain at the cost of your deeper values? Name them and consider what a sincere realignment would look like.

When someone in your community publicly chooses integrity over tribal loyalty at personal cost, acknowledge and honour that courage; it reinforces the communal values that make a community trustworthy.

title: Ubada ibn al-Samit Renounces Alliance with Unbelievers
summary: Ubada ibn al-Samit renounces his alliance with the Jews and Christians, declaring his loyalty solely to God, His Messenger, and the believers, in contrast to Abd Allah ibn Ubayy who maintained his alliance with the unbelievers. Quranic verses were subsequently revealed addressing both men.
story: 'Ubada told him, "O Messenger of God, I ally myself with God, His messenger and the believers, and I renounce my association and alliance with these unbelievers." Ibn Ishaq continued, "It was about him and 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy that the following verses were revealed, 'O you who believe, do not ally yourselves with the Jews and the Christians; they are allied with one another' (surat al-Ma'ada; V, v.50). This referred to 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy. And '... those who ally themselves with God and His messenger and those who believe; they are the party of God who will triumph' (surat al-Ma'ida; V, v.56). This referred to 'Ubada b. al-Samit. We have discussed this in our Tafsir."
lessons: True loyalty requires clarity of values — Ubada's decisive renunciation of harmful alliances shows that meaningful commitment sometimes demands difficult public stands.
Divine revelation affirmed Ubada's sincere choice, reminding us that God witnesses and honors genuine acts of faith and moral courage.
The contrast between Ubada and Ibn Ubayy illustrates that outward religious affiliation means little without inner sincerity and willingness to act accordingly.
practical_advice: When you feel torn between loyalty to a community and your deeper values, take time to clarify what you truly stand for before making a decision.
If you are facing social pressure to compromise your principles, remember that sincere alignment with your values — even when costly — brings lasting peace of heart.
Reflect on relationships or alliances in your life that may be pulling you away from your core beliefs, and consider whether realignment is needed.

title: Zayd ibn Haritha's Raid on the Quraysh Caravan at al-Qarada
summary: Six months after the Battle of Badr, the Prophet dispatched Zayd ibn Haritha to intercept a Quraysh caravan led by Abu Sufyan on the Iraq route. Zayd's force seized the caravan and its silver at the well of al-Qarada, returning the spoils to the Prophet who distributed them among the expedition members.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr quoted Ibn Ishaq as follows, "This occurred six months after the battle of Badr." Ibn Ishaq stated, "The account of it related that Quraysh became insecure about the route they usually followed to Syria, after the events of Badr, and so they took the Iraq route. Some of their merchants, including Abu Sufyan, who was bearing a large quantity of silver which was the greater part of their merchandise, left, having hired as a guide for that route a man of Banu Bakr b. Wa'il named Furat b. Hayyan, that is al-'Ijli, who were allied to Banu Sahm. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent forth Zayd b. Haritha who met up with them at a well called al-Qarada. He seized the caravan, and all it contained, but its men escaped. He returned with the spoils to the Messenger of God (SAAS). "Hassan b. Thabit spoke the following verses about this: 'Say farewell to the rivulets of Syria, for in the way are swords like the mouths of pregnant camels hurting from eating arak leaves In the hands of men who have moved off towards their Lord and to His helpers in truth, and in angels' hands. If they proceed to the lowlands from the sand valley, then tell them, "the route is not there!"'" Ibn Hisham stated, "This qasida is included in the verses by Hassan; Abu Sufyan b. al-Harith composed a response to them." Al-Waqidi stated, "This foray by Zayd b. Haritha occurred at the start of Jumada al-Uwla, some 28 months after the Hijra. Safwan b. Umayya was the leader of this caravan. "The reason he sent out Zayd b. Haritha was that it was Nu'aym b. Mas'ud, then still a follower of his own people's religion, who had come to Medina with news of this caravan. This Nu'aym had met with Kinana b. Abi al-Huqayq, along with some of Banu al-Nadir, among whom was Sulayt b. al-Nu'man, of Aslam. They had been drinking, this being before the prohibition of wine. Nu'aym had talked of the departure of the caravan with Safwan b. Umayya, and of the valuables it carried. Sulayt had immediately left and informed the Messenger of God (SAAS), who had promptly despatched Zayd b. Haritha. His force met and seized the caravan, but the men accompanying it escaped. They did capture a man or two, then took command of the caravan and brought it in. The Messenger of God (SAAS) divided it up into five portions, each of which amounted to 20,000 dirhams. He apportioned four-fifths of it to the expedition. Among those captured was the guide, Furat b. Hayyan. He accepted Islam, may God be pleased with him." Ibn Jarir stated, "Al-Waqidi asserted that in the spring of that year 'Uthman b. 'Affan married Umm Kulthum, daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and that she was brought to him in Jumada al-Akhira."
lessons: Strategic adaptation in the face of adversity — as Quraysh changed their route after Badr, the Muslim community remained vigilant and responsive, showing that resilience requires both awareness and action.
Unexpected sources of information, like Nu'aym who was not yet Muslim, can play a pivotal role in unfolding events, reminding us that God's plan operates through many channels.
The Prophet's measured and just distribution of spoils reflects the importance of fairness and communal trust in leadership, even in times of conflict.
practical_advice: When facing setbacks or opposition, assess whether a change of approach — like Zayd's interception via a new route — might be necessary rather than persisting with a failing strategy.
Stay alert to information and opportunities that arise from unexpected places; sometimes guidance comes from the least expected sources.
When resources or responsibilities are shared within a community or team, ensure distribution is fair and transparent to maintain trust and cohesion.

title: The Killing of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf
summary: Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, a Jewish tribal leader who had traveled to Mecca to incite war against the Muslims and composed harmful poetry mocking the Prophet and Muslim women, was killed by Muhammad ibn Maslama and a small group of companions after the Prophet asked who would deal with the threat he posed.
story: In his Sahih collection, al-Bukhari stated, in a section entitled, "The killing of Ka'b b. al-Ashraf", that 'Ali b. 'Abd Allah related to him, quoting Sufyan, that 'Amr stated, "I heard Jabir b. 'Abd Allah say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, "Who will deal with Ka'b b. al-Ashraf? He has sought to harm God and His Messenger." Muhammad b. Maslama arose and replied, "O Messenger of God, do you wish that I kill him?" "Yes," he answered. "Then please permit me to say something." "Do so," he was told. "'Muhammad b. Maslama then approached him (Ka'b) and said, "This fellow (i.e. the Messenger of God (SAAS)), asked us for charity, but he has become a nuisance to us. I've come to you to ask you to give us a loan." "'He responded, "So, by God, you too are tired of him!" "'Muhammad b. Maslama responded. "Having followed him, we don't want to abandon him until we see how it turns out for him. We'd just like you to make us a loan." "'All right; give me some security.' 'What would you like?' "'Pledge me your womenfolk!' "They objected, 'How could we pledge you our women, you being the most handsome of the Arabs!' "'Then pledge me your sons', he asked. "'How could we pledge you our sons?' they demanded, 'and have them insulted by being told they are only worth the value of a wasq or two! That would be shameful for us. But we will pledge you our la'ma.' Sufyan said that by this he meant their weapons. "Muhammad b. Maslama then made a night rendezvous with him and did go to him one night, accompanied by Abu Na'ila, who was Ka'b's foster-brother. Ka'b invited them into his castle and came down to them. His wife asked him, 'Where are you going at this late hour?' (According to another account, one not from 'Amr, she commented, 'I hear a voice dripping blood!') "He replied, 'It's only my brother Muhammad b. Maslama and my foster-brother Abu Na'ila. A man of honour, if invited to a stabbing at night, would still accept!' "Muhammad b. Maslama went in accompanied by two men. He told them, 'When he comes, I'll lean over towards his hair to smell it. When you see me seize his head, then you come on over and strike him.' He went on, 'I'll do it once, then let you smell.' "When Ka'b came down, he was wearing a loose sash and was redolent with perfume. Muhammad b. Maslama commented, 'I never smelled a finer scent than today!' (According to the other tradition, he (Ka'b) commented, 'I have the best-perfumed and best-looking women of all the Arabs!') 'Amr went on, "He then asked, 'Would you permit me to smell your head?' 'Certainly,' Ka'b replied. "He then smelled it and had his companions do the same. He then asked, 'Do you permit me?' 'Yes,' Ka'b replied. And then he (Muhammad b. Maslama) seized him, calling out, 'Get him!' And they killed him. They then went to the Prophet (SAAS) and informed him." Ibn Ishaq stated, "Ka'b b. al-Ashraf was of the Ta'i tribe, of the sub-tribe of Banu Nabhan, though his mother was of Banu al-Nadir. He received news of the deaths of those at Badr after the arrival of Zayd b. al-Haritha and 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha. He commented, 'By God, if Muhammad has really struck down those people, then better be inside the earth than upon it!' "When this enemy of God was sure of this news, he left for Mecca, where he visited al-Muttalib b. Abi Wada'a. Dubayrat al-Sahmi, whose wife was 'Atika, daughter of Abu al-'Is b. Umayya b. 'Abd Shams b. 'Abd Manaf. She accommodated him graciously and he began instigating an attack upon the Messenger of God (SAAS) speaking verses and mourning the deaths of those polytheists killed at Badr." Ibn Ishaq gave his poem which begins with the verse, "The Badr millstone ground to death those killed there, and for the like of Badr one speaks and weeps." He also gave the response to this poem by Hassan b. Thabit, may God be pleased with him, as well as verse by others. He went on, "Ka'b then returned to Medina where he composed verses that were suggestive about the Muslim women and critical of the Prophet (SAAS) and his followers." Musa b. Uqba stated, "Ka'b b. al-Ashraf was of Banu al-Nadir, or associated with them; he harmed the Messenger of God (SAAS) by ridiculing him in verse and he rode in to Quraysh to incite them further. Abu Sufyan, in Mecca, asked him, 'In your view, I pray you tell me, which religion is more favoured by God, that of Muhammad and his friends, or our own? Which of us, do you think, is more correct and appropriate? We slaughter our meat generously, give milk-topped water to drink, providing food for all and sundry who come.' "Ka'b b. al-Ashraf replied, 'You are better-guided in your path than they are.'" Musa went on, "And so God revealed to His Messenger the verses, 'Have you not considered those given a part of the Book who believe in enchantment and devils, and who say to those who disbelieve, "These are better guided on the path than those who believe (in God)." They are indeed cursed by God; those whom God curses you shall find to be without succour'" (surat al-Nisa'; IV, v.51, 2). Both Musa and Ibn Ishaq stated further, "He went to Medina where he proclaimed his enmity and incited people to go to war. He had not left Mecca before he had united them to fight the Messenger of God (SAAS); he then began composing amatory verse about Umm al-Fadl b. al-Harith and other Muslim women." Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, as 'Abd Allah b. al-Mughith b. Abu Burda related to me, 'Who will take care of Ibn al-Ashraf?' "Muhammad b. Maslama, a brother of Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal, responded, 'I'll take care of him for you, Messenger of God. I will kill him!' "'Do so then, if you can,' he told him." Ibn Ishaq went on, "Muhammad b. Maslama then returned and stayed three days and nights eating and drinking nothing except what was essential. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was informed of this and he summoned him and asked, 'Why have you given up food and drink?' He replied, 'O Messenger of God, I promised you something that I don't know whether I can fulfil or not.' He was told, 'You only have to try.' "He replied, 'O Messenger of God, we'll have to say certain things.' "'Say whatever you think fit; you will be free of blame for doing so.' "Muhammad b. Maslama then set about killing Ka'b with Salkan b. Salama b. Waqsh, who was known as Abu Na'ila. He was of Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal. He was, moreover, the foster-brother of Ka'b b. Ashraf. Also with them were 'Abbad b. Bishr b. Waqsh, of Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal, along with al-Harith b. Aws b. Mu'adh, of Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal, and Abu 'Abs b. Jahr, a brother of Banu Haritha." Ibn Ishaq went on, "They then dispatched to Ka'b, that enemy of God, Salkan b. Salama Abu Na'ila."
lessons: When a community faces a persistent and coordinated threat to its safety and dignity, measured and deliberate action — taken with the leader's authorization — reflects responsible protection of the vulnerable.

Muhammad ibn Maslama's three days of fasting and distress before the mission shows that even those who commit to difficult tasks in service of a greater good carry the weight of that responsibility deeply — moral seriousness is not weakness.

The Quranic response to Ka'b's betrayal of religious knowledge for political gain reminds us that knowledge and position carry grave responsibilities; using them to mislead or harm others has spiritual consequences.
practical_advice: When you have committed to a difficult but necessary responsibility and feel overwhelmed by self-doubt, remember that trying sincerely is all that is required — the outcome rests with God.
If you are facing harm from someone who uses words, influence, or social networks against you, seek guidance from trusted leadership rather than reacting impulsively or alone.
Reflect on whether the alliances or relationships in your life are built on shared values or on shared opposition to others — the latter rarely produces lasting good.

title: The Assassination of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf
summary: A group of Muslim men, led by Muhammad b. Maslama, carried out the assassination of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, a Jewish leader hostile to the Prophet, through a night mission involving deception and planning.
story: Muhammad h. Maslama then set about killing Ka'b with Salkm h. S a l m a h. Waqsh, who was known as Aha Nzlila. He was of Bana 'Abd al-Ashhal. He was, moreover, the foster-brother of Ka'b h. Ashraf. Also with them were 'Abbxd b. Bishr h. Waqsh, of Bana 'Ahd al-Ashhal, along with al-H%rith h. Aws b. Mu%dh, of Bana 'Ahd al-Ashhal, and Aha 'Ahs b. Jahr, a brother of Bana Huitha. Ihn Ishnq went on, "They then dispatched to Ka'b, that enemy of God, Salkan b. Salzma Aha Nx'ila. When the latter was with him, he talked with Ka'b and they recited poetry to one another; Aha Nalila was a poetry reciter. He then told Ka'b, 'I'm so sorry, Ihn al-Ashraf, but I've come to you about something I want to discuss, and for you to keep confidential.' 'I'll do that,' he said. "Muhammad h. Maslama went on, LThisman's arrival among us was a disaster! The Arabs became hostile to us, and united against us. They have cut off our routes, so that our young are deprived and scared. We and our families are suffering greatly.' "Ka'b commented, 'I'm al-Ashraf s son! Didn't I tell you, Ibn Salzma, that things would turn out this way?' "Salk= then told Ka'b, 'What I wanted was for you to sell us some food for which we would give you a surety and keep trust with you; you'll do well by that.' "He asked, 'Will you give me your sons as surety?' "'You want to disgrace us? I have companions who share my views, and I wanted to bring them to you for you to sell to us, by which you would do well, and we would give you an equivalent pledge in weapons.' "Salk= wanted him not to refuse to have weapons present when they brought them. Ka'b agreed, saying, 'Weapons would be good security.' "Salk~n then returned to his companions and informed them what had transpired. He told them to get their weapons and to set off, and to meet up with him. They then assembled with the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Ibn Ishzq went on, "Thawr b. Zayd related to me, from 'Ikrima, from Ihn 'Ahhzs, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) travelled with them as far as Baqi' al-Gharqad, and there he dispatched them, saying, "Go in God's name! 0 God, give them help!" He then returned home. It was a moonlit night, and the men set off and reached Ka'b's castle. "'Aha Nz'ila called up to him. Ka'b had recently taken a new wife, and he jumped up wrapped in a coverlet. His wife took hold of its hem and said, "But you're a man engaged in combat! Warriors don't go down at such a time as this!" Ka'b replied, "But it's Aha Nz'ila. He'd not have woken me if I had been asleep." She commented, "I swear I sense evil in his voice!" Ka'b replied, "If a man of honour were invited to a stabbing, he'd still accept!" "'He then went down and engaged them in conversation. Eventually they asked him, "Ibn al-Ashraf, would you like to take a walk with us to Shi'b alLCAjaz, where we can spend the rest of the night talking?" "If you wish," he replied. And so they left and walked for a while. "'Eventually Abii Na'ila touched the hair at Ka'b's temple with his hand, which he then sniffed, saying, "I swear, I've never before this night smelled anything so fine!" He then walked further, then did the same, to make Ka'b feel secure. Having gone yet further, he then took hold of Ka'b's hair at both temples and said, "Strike down the enemy of God!" Their swords rained blows upon him, but to no effect.' "Muhammad b. Maslama continued, 'I then remembered a poignard I had with my sword and drew it, Ka'b meanwhile making such a din that all the forts thereabouts had lit up. I stabbed my dagger into his lower stomach and bore down on it till it reached his scrotum. The enemy of God then collapsed. Meanwhile al-Hzrith b. Aws had suffered a wound from one of our swords in his leg or on his hand. "'Thereafter we left, our path taking us past Bana Umayya b. Zayd, then Bana Qurayza and then Busth. After that we went on up the stony tract of alLCArid. On account of the blood he was losing, our companion al-Hzrith b. Aws was lagging behiid and so we halted for a while. When he came up, following our tracks, we carried him and so arrived at the Messenger of God (SAAS) as the night was endiig and he was engaged in prayer. We greeted him and he came out to us. We told him of the execution of God's enemy. He then spat upon our companion's wound and we went on to our families. By next morning the Jews were in shock at our attack upon God's enemy; every Jew feared for his safety.'" Al-Waqidi claimed that they had brought the head of Ka'b b. al-Ashraf to the Messenger of God (SAAS). Ihn Ishaq stated, "Ka'b b. Malik spoke the following verses about this affair: 'Ka'b having been left lying stretched out, after his demise al-Nadir were humiliated. As he lay prostrate on his hands, our men with drawn swords having overcome hi Upon Muipmmad's order, when he told Ka'b's brother to slip by night secretly to Ka'b. And he tricked him, bringing him down by deceit, yet praiseworthy is he, worthy of trust and brave.'" Ibn Hisham stated, "These verses are from an ode he composed about the battle with Banii &Nadir that will be quoted hereafter." I note that the death of Ka'b h. al-Ashraf at the hands of Aws came after the battle of Badr. Khazraj killed Aba R~fi'b.Aba al-Huqayq after the battle of m u d, as will be explained hereafter, if God, in whom is all trust, so wills it. Ibn Ishaq also gave the following verses by Hassan b. Thahit: "How fine a party of men you met, 0 Ibn al-Huqayq, and you too, Ibn al-Ashraf Men who travelled by night to bring their light swords to you, as cheerful as lions prowling in their lairs. They came to you in your own territory, making you taste death with their swift-killing blades Foreseeing the victory of the religion of their prophet, minimizing every matter of danger."
lessons: Carrying out difficult duties for the community may require great personal courage and risk, even when the outcome is uncertain.
The Prophet's practice of sending companions off with prayer ('Go in God's name! O God, give them help!') teaches that reliance on God should accompany every serious endeavor.
Community threats demand organized, deliberate responses rather than reactive or rash action — careful planning reflects wisdom and responsibility.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult but necessary task, seek God's support through prayer before you begin, as the Prophet did for his companions at Baqi' al-Gharqad.
If you are part of a team carrying out a challenging mission, look out for those who are injured or struggling along the way, as the men did when they carried al-Harith despite their own urgency.
After completing a hard duty, report back to a trusted leader or mentor and allow healing — both physical and spiritual — to take place before moving on.

title: Muhayyisa Kills Ibn Sunayna and His Brother Huwayyisa Accepts Islam
summary: After the Prophet's command to act against hostile Jews, Muhayyisa killed a Jewish merchant he knew, prompting a confrontation with his non-Muslim brother Huwayyisa, whose shock at his brother's total obedience ultimately led him to embrace Islam.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Kill whatever Jew you may conquer!' "Muhayyi~ab. Masctid of Aws thereupon attacked and killed Ibn Sunayna, a Jewish merchant who used to associate with them and do business with them. His brother Huwayyisa b. Masctid was older than Muhayyi~aand had not yet accepted Islam. When the latter killed the Jew, Huwayyi~abegan hitting his brother and shouting, 'You enemy of God, you! Have you really killed him? Yet much of the fat in your belly is from his money!' "Muhayyisa replied, 'I was ordered to kill him by someone who, if he ordered me to kill you, I would strike your neck!' He also said that this was the start of Huwayyi~a'sacceptance of Islam. "Huwayyisa asked, 'Do you really mean that if Muhammad told you to kill me, you would do it?' "Muhayyisa reiterated, 'Yes, by God! If he were to order me to strike your neck, I would do so.' "'By God, any religion that would so affect you is truly wonderful,' said Huwayyisa, and he accepted Islam." Ibn Ishzq continued, "A freed-man of Banii H s i t h a related this to me, from Muhayyisa's daughter, from her father. "Muhayyi~aspoke the following verses about this: "A mother's son complains that if I were ordered to kill him, I would strike him with my sharp sword at the nape of his head. Its blade salt-lie in colour, it is so well burnished; when I aim it, it never deceives. It would not have pleased me to kill you voluntarily, even if we owned all between BUS- and Ma'rib.'" Ibn Hisham recounted, from Abii Wbayda, from Abii 'Amr d-Madani, that this story relates to the time following the battle with Banti Qurayza, and that the man killed was Ka'b b. Yahiidha. He stated that when Muhayyi~aexecuted him on the orders of the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the day of the battle with Bana Qurayza, his brother Huwayyisa said what he did and Muhayyi~areplied as above. And so that day Huwayyisa accepted Islam. But God knows best.
lessons: Witnessing the depth of another person's sincere faith and conviction can be the most powerful catalyst for one's own spiritual awakening.
True faith sometimes demands prioritizing divine command over personal relationships and financial loyalties, a reality that can be profoundly unsettling to observers.
Honest and direct speech about one's beliefs, even when shocking, can open hearts that years of gentle persuasion could not reach.
practical_advice: When someone's level of conviction in their faith astonishes or challenges you, rather than dismissing them, pause and ask yourself what it is about their certainty that moves you.
If you find yourself torn between loyalty to people and loyalty to your principles, reflect deeply on what it is you truly believe and what you are willing to stand for.
Don't underestimate the effect your sincere commitment to your values has on those watching you — your consistency may be the thing that guides someone else toward truth.

title: The Naming and Significance of the Mountain of Uhud
summary: The text provides a scholarly discussion of the origin of the name 'Uhud' and records prophetic traditions affirming a special, beloved relationship between the mountain of Uhud and the Muslim community.
story: A supplementary note from the author on the naming of Uhud: Uhud was so named because of its unique distinction among these mountains. In the jahih collection it states, "Uhud is a mountain that loves us and that we love. It is said that its name refers to its inhabitants. It is also said that it is so named to f l i r m the closeness of one's family on one's return from a trip, as does a lover. It is also said to mean, 'aln zahrrihi, i.e. 'in a concrete and literal interpretation', as in God's words, 'some of them tumble down in fear of God'" @rat al-Baqara; 11, v.74). In a hadith from Abn 'Abbas b. Jabr, it states, "Uhud loves us and we it; it stands at the gate of paradise. And it is (like a) wild ass that loathes us as we it. It stands at one of the gates of hell." Al-Suhayli commented, in substantiation of this hadith: "It is well established that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'A man is with whomever he loves.'" Al-Suhayli's comment is strange. For this hadith relates to people and a mountain could not be referred to as human.
lessons: The Prophet's words that 'a man is with whomever he loves' remind us that love is not merely a feeling but a spiritual bond that shapes our ultimate destination.
Sacred places and sacred memories carry meaning beyond their physical form — the believers' attachment to Uhud reflects the power of shared spiritual history in sustaining community identity.
Scholarly reflection and healthy debate, as seen in al-Suhayli's commentary, are honoured traditions within Islamic learning and do not diminish reverence for sacred texts.
practical_advice: Cultivate love for the places, people, and practices that draw you closer to God, for what you love shapes who you become.
When you visit or reflect on places of spiritual significance, allow yourself to connect emotionally to the history that occurred there and let it renew your sense of purpose.
Engage with scholarly differences of opinion with an open and curious mind rather than anxiety — disagreement among scholars is a sign of a living, thoughtful tradition.

title: The Battle of Uhud: Historical Dating and Context
summary: The text establishes the historical date of the Battle of Uhud as Shawwal 3 AH, drawing on multiple scholarly authorities, and notes the Quranic reference to the believers marching out to face their enemies.
story: This battle took place in Shawwsl, 3 AH. This is stated by al-Zuh-, Qatada, Mosa b. Wqba, Muhammad b. Ishsq and Mzlik. Ibn Ishaq stated, "It occurred in the middle of Shawwal." Qatada said, "It took place on Saturday, the 1lth of Shawwd." Mdik stated, "The battle took place early in the day, and it is commonly agreed that the reference is to it in the words of the Almighty: '... when you went forth from your family in the morning to set the believers in their positions for the battle; and God is All-Hearing, All-Knowing. When two groups from among you were inclined to being...'"
lessons: The meticulous preservation of historical dates and scholarly chains of transmission in Islamic tradition reflects the community's deep sense of accountability to truth and accuracy.
Marching out early in the morning, leaving the comfort of family, is a symbol of the believer's willingness to sacrifice personal ease for the sake of a higher duty.
God's attributes of being 'All-Hearing, All-Knowing' offer comfort even in the midst of battle and danger — nothing the believers experienced was unseen or unheard by God.
practical_advice: When facing a daunting challenge, draw strength from the example of the companions who left their families at dawn with trust in God, knowing that courage is not the absence of fear but the decision to move forward despite it.

Seek grounding in historical knowledge of your faith community's struggles — understanding that those before you faced hardship and prevailed can restore perspective when your own trials feel overwhelming.
In moments of uncertainty, remind yourself that God is All-Hearing and All-Knowing; nothing you are going through is invisible to Him, and that awareness alone can be a source of deep calm.

title: Quraysh Gathers for Revenge After Badr: The Road to Uhud
summary: After their defeat at Badr, the Quraysh leaders — including Abu Sufyan, Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl, and Safwan ibn Umayya — conspire with Meccan merchants to fund a new army and seek revenge against the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS), assembling allies from Kinana and Abyssinian mercenaries.
story:
When those polytheists of Quraysh suffered their fate of burial in the pit, the remnants of their force returned to Mecca, as did Abu Sufyan with his caravan. 'Abd Allah b. Abi Rabi'a, 'Ikrima b. Abi Jahl and Safwan b. Umayya gathered with others of Quraysh whose fathers, sons or brothers had been struck down at Badr. They talked with Abu Sufyan and with others of the Meccan merchants who had been with that caravan, as follows: 'O Quraysh, Muhammad has done you great harm and has killed your elite. Give us your assistance with this wealth to make war on him and to take revenge on him.' They agreed.

Ibn Ishaq stated, 'It was about them, so I have been told by certain scholars, that Almighty God revealed the words, "Those who disbelieve are spending their wealth to block off God's path. And they shall spend it and then they shall suffer regret and be defeated. Those who disbelieve will be herded together into hell" (surat al-Anfal; VIII, v.36).'

And so Quraysh gathered to go to war against the Messenger of God (SAAS) when Abu Sufyan and the caravan owners did this, assembling their Abyssinian mercenaries and those of the tribes of Kinana and the lowlands who accepted their authority.
lessons: Hatred and pride can drive people to invest enormous resources in wrongdoing — yet the Quran reminds us that such efforts ultimately lead to regret and defeat.
Even when enemies conspire and pool their strength, divine wisdom encompasses their plans. Trust in God's knowledge of what is hidden is a shield for the believer.
The aftermath of a trial is itself a test — how we respond to loss, whether with wisdom or with vengeance, reveals the true state of our hearts.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by opposition or hostility from others, remind yourself that those who act from anger and pride carry the seeds of their own downfall — focus on your own integrity rather than their plots.
If you are anxious about powerful forces working against you, reflect on the Quranic verse in this passage: spending effort to 'block God's path' is ultimately futile. Use this as a source of calm.

Channel the stress of conflict into productive preparation — spiritual, mental, and practical — rather than reactive emotion, following the example of the Prophet who consulted his companions rather than acting from fear.

title: Abu 'Azza the Poet Is Persuaded to Incite War Against the Muslims
summary: Abu 'Azza, a poor poet who had been released with kindness by the Prophet after Badr, is pressured by Safwan ibn Umayya with promises of wealth and care for his daughters to use his poetry to rouse the tribes against the Muslims.
story:
Abu 'Azza 'Amr b. 'Abd Allah al-Jumahi had been treated kindly by the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Badr. He was a poor man with children and responsibilities and had been taken captive. Safwan b. Umayya now asked him, 'Abu 'Azza, you are a poet. Help us with your voice and go forth with us.' He replied, 'But Muhammad was kind to me, and I don't want to oppose him.' Safwan persisted, 'Yes indeed, so help us with your mind; I swear that if you return safe, I will make you wealthy. If you are killed, I swear I'll treat your daughters just like my own. Whatever happens to my children, good or bad, will be their lot too.'

So Abu 'Azza went off into the lowlands calling upon Banu Kinana and reciting, 'O Banu 'Abd Mannat, brave men, you are fine warriors, as were your fathers too. Let not your help be promised a year from now; do not let me down, for letting me down is not right.'
lessons: Gratitude for kindness received should anchor our choices — betraying the one who showed us mercy is a form of moral loss that no worldly gain can compensate.
Material desperation can make us vulnerable to manipulation; recognizing this vulnerability is the first step toward protecting our integrity.
Those who use the needs of the poor as leverage to serve their own agendas bear a heavy moral responsibility — injustice that exploits vulnerability is especially grave.
practical_advice: When you face pressure to act against your conscience in exchange for financial security, pause and recall any kindness you have received — let gratitude be a moral compass that steadies you.

If you feel 'lost' because poverty or responsibility is pushing you toward a decision you know is wrong, seek counsel from a trustworthy person before committing to a path that conflicts with your values.

Reflect on whether the person making you promises has your genuine wellbeing at heart or is simply using your vulnerability — real support does not come with conditions that require you to betray others.

title: Jubayr ibn Mut'im Promises Freedom to Wahshi in Exchange for Killing Hamza
summary: Jubayr ibn Mut'im promises his Abyssinian slave Wahshi his freedom if he kills Hamza ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's uncle, in revenge for his own uncle Tu'ayma ibn 'Adi who was killed at Badr.
story: Jubayr b. Mut'im summoned an Abyssinian warrior slave of his, named Wahshi, who could toss a spear in the Abyssinian manner, rarely missing, and told him, 'Go forth with our men; and if you should kill Hamza, the uncle of Muhammad, in revenge for my uncle Tu'ayma b. 'Adi, then you are a free man.'
lessons: The cycle of revenge — when left unchecked by higher moral principles — draws more and more people into violence, including the innocent and the enslaved who have no stake in the conflict.
Freedom is a profound human need; exploiting that need to make someone an instrument of killing reveals the moral bankruptcy of those in power.
Even the most calculated acts of human revenge take place within a larger divine plan — what people intend and what God permits are not always the same thing.
practical_advice: When anger at a loss makes you want to strike back, ask yourself: am I acting from principle or from pain? Decisions made from raw pain often produce consequences that extend far beyond what we intended.

If you feel trapped — like Wahshi, bound to a course of action by circumstances not entirely of your choosing — know that God sees the constraints on your freedom and judges with full knowledge of your situation.
Do not let grief over loss push you to instrumentalize others or make promises that involve harming the innocent — seek instead to process grief through prayer, patience, and community support.

title: Hind bint 'Utba Urges Wahshi to Kill Hamza
summary: Hind bint 'Utba, whose father was killed at Badr, repeatedly goads the Abyssinian spearman Wahshi to kill Hamza as the Quraysh army advances toward Uhud, calling out to him by his nickname 'Abu Dasma.'
story:
And whenever Wahshi passed by Hind, daughter of 'Utba or she passed by him, she would say, 'Go to it, Abu Dasma,' avenge us and help yourself too!' That is, she urged him on to kill Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib.

They advanced and made a halt at 'Aynayn, on a mountain at the Sakhha valley of Qanat, on the edge of the wadi facing Medina.
lessons: Grief, when it transforms into obsessive vengeance, corrupts the heart and can lead a person to orchestrate the suffering of others rather than finding peace.
The way we respond to loss is a defining test of our character — channeling grief into hatred diminishes us spiritually, while channeling it into patience elevates us.
Those who incite others to violence from the safety of the sidelines bear moral responsibility for what is carried out in their name.
practical_advice: If you are carrying deep grief over someone you have lost, be honest with yourself about whether your grief has become something darker — a desire to cause pain to others. Seek spiritual guidance to process it healthily.
When you feel consumed by anger and a sense of injustice, find a trusted scholar, counselor, or community member to speak with, rather than channeling those feelings into harmful actions.
Notice if others around you are encouraging your anger for their own purposes — sometimes those who 'egg us on' benefit from our rage while we bear the consequences.

title: The Prophet's Vision Before the Battle of Uhud
summary: Before the Battle of Uhud, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) shares a series of prophetic dreams with his companions — a cow being slaughtered, a notch in his sword Dhu al-Faqir, and stout armor — which he interprets as signs of both coming hardship and eventual victory.
story:
'When the polytheists came at the Messenger of God (SAAS), at Uhud, it was his view that he should remain in Medina and fight them there. But people who had not witnessed Badr told him, "Let's go out, Messenger of God, to them and fight them at Uhud." They hoped to attain some of the credit that had accrued to those at Badr. They kept on at the Messenger of God (SAAS), until he put on his chain-mail. Then they repented and told him, "O Messenger of God, stay here. Your view must prevail."

'He told them, "It is not proper for a prophet to take off his armour after dressing in it until God decides between him and his enemy."

'Before putting on my armour that day,' he had been telling them, 'I had a vision of myself in stout armour - which I interpreted to be Medina - and that I made the (squadron) leader ride behind me; this I interpreted as the leader of the squadron. I also saw that my sword, Dhu al-Faqir, had been notched; this I interpreted as a weakness in you. Then I saw a cow being slaughtered, its stomach open. I swear by God, it was good.'"

The Messenger of God (SAAS) received a vision on the Friday night. When he arose next morning a group of his Companions came to him and he told them, 'Last night in my sleep I saw a cow being slaughtered, and by God, it was good. And I also saw that my sword, Dhu al-Faqir, had been notched on its blade.' (Or he said, 'And it had notches in it, and I hated this. These were two disasters.') 'And I saw myself in stout armour and that I was making a (squadron) leader ride second saddle.'

Al-Bayhaqi related this hadith marfu'an, through Hammad b. Salama, from 'Ali b. Zayd, from Anas, back to the Messenger of God (SAAS) who said, 'I had a vision as if I had made the (squadron) leader ride second saddle. And the blade of my sword seemed to have been damaged. This I interpreted as meaning that I would kill the enemy's leader. I interpreted the damage to my sword's blade as being the death of a man from my own family.' And Hamza was killed, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) killed Talha, who was in charge of their banner.
lessons: The Prophet (SAAS) did not hide signs of coming difficulty from his companions — honest communication about anticipated hardship, grounded in trust in God, is a form of spiritual strength not weakness.
Even when divine guidance signals hardship ahead, the Prophet's response was acceptance and continued action — not paralysis. Trusting God's plan does not mean being passive.

A leader who proceeds with commitment even after others have wavered — 'It is not proper for a prophet to take off his armour after dressing in it' — demonstrates that integrity means following through on what is right regardless of shifting moods around you.
practical_advice: When you receive clear inner guidance or a strong sense of what the right course of action is, do not abandon it simply because others around you change their minds — commit with full trust in God's outcome.
If you are anxious about a difficult situation ahead, take time in quiet prayer or reflection to seek God's guidance, as the Prophet sought clarity through his vision before a major trial.
When hardship is coming, prepare practically and spiritually — put on your 'armor' in the form of prayer, community, and clear intention — rather than waiting in passive fear.

title: The Companions Who Missed Badr Eagerly Seek Combat at Uhud
summary: Muslims who had not participated in the Battle of Badr pressured the Prophet (SAAS) to march out to Uhud rather than defend Medina, driven by a desire to earn the spiritual distinction their brethren had achieved at Badr, and later regretted their insistence.
story:
But people who had not witnessed Badr told him, 'Let's go out, Messenger of God, to them and fight them at Uhud.' They hoped to attain some of the credit that had accrued to those at Badr. They kept on at the Messenger of God (SAAS), until he put on his chain-mail. Then they repented and told him, 'O Messenger of God, stay here. Your view must prevail.'

He told them, 'It is not proper for a prophet to take off his armour after dressing in it until God decides between him and his enemy.'

When Abu Sufyan and the polytheists halted at the base of Uhud, the Muslims who had not attended Badr were delighted at the enemy's approach to them. They told one another that God had fulfilled their dreams.
lessons: Eagerness to do good — even when sincere — must be tempered by deference to those with greater wisdom and experience. Good intentions do not automatically lead to good outcomes.

The feeling of having 'missed out' on a significant spiritual moment can lead us to make impulsive decisions to compensate; recognizing this pattern in ourselves is an important form of self-awareness.

When we realize we have pushed a leader toward a decision through our insistence and then see consequences unfolding, the mature response is not blame but accountability and renewed trust in God's wisdom.
practical_advice: If you feel you have missed a spiritual opportunity and are anxious to 'make up for it,' take a breath and ask whether you are acting from genuine conviction or from a place of comparison and fear of falling short.

Before insisting on your view in a group or organizational decision, especially when someone wiser than you holds a different position, ask yourself: am I contributing wisdom or just emotional pressure?

If you have pushed someone toward a decision that led to difficulty, resist the urge to retreat into guilt or blame — instead, recommit to supporting the path forward with full sincerity and trust in God's decree.

title: The Prophet's Vision Before Uhud
summary: The night before the Battle of Uhud, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) received a vision involving a slaughtered cow, a notched sword, and stout armour, which he interpreted as portents of what was to come in battle.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) received a vision on the Friday night. When he arose next morning a group of his Companions came to him and he told them, 'Last night in my sleep I saw a cow being slaughtered, and by God, it was good. And I also saw that my sword, Dhzi al-Faqtir, had been notched on its blade.' (Or he said, 'And it had notches in it, and I hated this. These were two disasters.') 'And I saw myself in stout armour and that I was making a (squadron) leader ride second saddle.' "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) told them of his visions, they asked, '0 Messenger of God, what interpretation do you give your visions?' He replied, 'I interpreted the cow I saw as one among us and among the enemy; I disliked what I saw happen to my sword.' "Some men say that what he saw in his sword was what was to strike his face, for the enemy did wound him in the face that day; they broke one of his front teeth and tore his lip. They claim that it was Wtha b. Aha Waqq% who threw it. The cow represented those of the Muslims who were killed at that battle. "(The Messenger of God (SAAS) said) 'I interpret the kabsh, to be the (squadron) leader of the enemy, God damn them, and the stout armow to be Medina. So remain here, drawing the families into the citadels. When the enemy attacks us through the alleys, we'll fight them and fire down at them from on top of the houses.' The Muslims had lined the alleys of Medina with fortifications so that they had become like fortresses."
lessons: Dreams and visions can carry deeper meaning; the Prophet's interpretation of his vision shows the importance of reflection and seeking wisdom before facing difficult situations.
True leadership means sharing difficult truths with those who follow you, even when those truths are hard to bear.
Foreknowledge of hardship does not eliminate the need for courage; it prepares us to face trials with clarity and resolve.
practical_advice: When you feel a sense of dread or anxiety about what lies ahead, take time to reflect on what that feeling may be telling you rather than suppressing it.
Share your worries with trusted companions or mentors; articulating fears out loud can bring clarity and reduce the weight of anxiety.
Prepare practically for challenges you foresee rather than being paralysed by them — the Prophet did not despair at his vision but instead gave strategic guidance.

title: The Debate Over Defending Medina or Going Out to Meet the Enemy
summary: Before the Battle of Uhud, a heated debate arose among the Companions about whether to remain inside Medina or march out to face the Quraysh army, with many overriding the Prophet's preferred strategy.
story: "Those Muslims who had not been present at Badr said, 'We had been looking forward to this day, and praying to God for it. God has brought it to us, and made the outcome close at hand.' "One of the anszr asked, 'When will we do battle with them, 0 Messenger of God, if not right here in our area?' "Others asked, 'What about our people; what will we be able to defend, if we can't defend ourselves in war because of fear?' "Others made statements agreeing with him or going even further. One of these men was Hamza b. 'Abd al-Munalib, who said, 'By Him who revealed the Book to you, we shall take issue with them!' "iVuraym b. Mdik b. Thaclaba, he being of Bana Salim, said, '0 Prophet of God, do not deprive us of paradise! By Him who holds my soul in His hands, I shall enter it!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him, 'By what?' 'By my loving God and His Messenger, and I will not run away on the day of war.' 'You have spoken the truth,' commented the Messenger of God (SAAS). And the man did die a martyr on that day. "Many of the Muslims insisted on going forth to battle, refusing to accept the words and views of the Messenger of God (SAAS). If they had been content with what he had told them, it would have turned out so. But fate and destiny were to triumph. "Most of those advising him to go forth were men who had not been present at Badr; they were aware of the credit that had earlier accrued to those who were present at it."
lessons: Enthusiasm and courage are virtues, but they must be tempered by wisdom and deference to those with greater knowledge and experience.
The desire to prove oneself can sometimes override sound judgment; recognising this tendency in ourselves is the first step to managing it.
Destiny unfolds according to God's will; even when human decisions lead to difficult outcomes, trust in divine wisdom remains the foundation of faith.
practical_advice: Before making an important decision under pressure, pause and genuinely consider the counsel of those you trust and respect, especially when their advice differs from your impulse.
Examine whether your desire to act is driven by wisdom or by a need to prove yourself; both can feel the same in the moment.
Accept that even well-intentioned decisions can lead to painful outcomes, and remind yourself that growth often comes through difficulty rather than despite it.

title: The Prophet Dresses in Armour and Leads the Army Out
summary: After the Companions pressured him to march out to battle, the Prophet (SAAS) put on his armour and declared that it was not fitting for a prophet who had armed for war to turn back without fighting.
story: "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) made the Friday prayer, he preached to the congregation and enjoined them to fight hard and well. Having finished his address and his prayer, he called for his armour and dressed in it. He then made announcement to the people that they were to go forth to battle. "Aware of all this, some men of good sense observed, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered us to remain in Medina and he knows best about God and what He wants; revelation comes to him from heaven.' "They addressed him, '0 Messenger of God, remain here, as you told us to do.' He replied, 'It is not fitting for a prophet who has put on armour for war and given the order to proceed to battle the enemy to return without fighting. I did call upon you to do that, but you insisted on going out to battle. You must fear God and remain firm in the violence when you meet the enemy. Look for what it is God has ordered you, and do it.' The account continued, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left with the Muslims. They took the route through d-Bad@ir and numbered 1,000 men. There were 3,000 in the polytheist force.'"
lessons: A leader of integrity honours their commitments even when circumstances are not ideal; the Prophet's example shows that consistency builds trust.
Once a course of action has been set with sincere intention, moving forward with resolve and tawakkul (trust in God) is more honourable than hesitation.
Reminding oneself and others to fear God and remain firm in the face of hardship is itself a form of spiritual leadership.
practical_advice: When you have committed to a difficult path, remind yourself of your intention and move forward with steadiness rather than second-guessing every step.
If you find yourself in a situation partly of your own making that is now difficult, resist the urge to look back with regret; focus instead on doing what is right from where you stand.
Encourage those around you before a shared challenge; a few sincere words of reminder can steady hearts that are wavering.

title: Abdullah ibn Ubayy Withdraws with Three Hundred Men
summary: On the way to Uhud, the hypocrite Abdullah ibn Ubayy withdrew with three hundred men, abandoning the Prophet and the Muslim army on the eve of battle.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went forth with 1,000 of his men. He went on as far as al-Shawc, between Medina and Uhud, and there 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy withdrew back with one-third of the men. He said, 'He (the Prophet (SAAS)) agreed with them, but not with me. We don't know why we should kill ourselves out here, men!' "He returned home with those of his tribe who were hypocrites and doubters. 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr b. Haran d-Sulami, the father of Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, went after them, saying, 'People, I enjoin you by God not to abandon your people and your Prophet when their enemy is at hand!' They replied, 'If we knew that you would do battle, we would not surrender you; but we don't believe there will be any fighting.' 'When they disobeyed 'Abd Allah and refused to change their decision to retreat, he commented, 'May God banish you, you enemies of God; His Prophet (SAAS) will do without you!'" I comment that these people are those to whom reference is being made in the words of the Almighty, 'And so that He would know the hypocrites who were told, 'Come fight in God's cause, or defend yourselves.' They replied, 'If we knew there would be fighting, we would have followed you.' On that day they were closer to disbelief than to faith. They spoke with their mouths what was not in their hearts. But God knows well what they conceal' (sarat Al 'Imrzn; 111, v.166). This meant that they were lying in their statements, 'If we knew there would be fighting, we would have followed you.' This is because the occurrence of the fighting was completely clear and evident, not something unknown or in doubt. About them God also revealed, 'What ails you then, that you have become two parties about the hypocrites; God had subverted them because of what they had earned' (sarat al-Nisd'i'; IV, v.88)."
lessons: Betrayal and abandonment are among the most painful trials a community can face; yet the Prophet's mission continued without those who deserted, showing that sincerity matters more than numbers.
Hypocrisy — saying one thing and meaning another — ultimately harms the one who practises it far more than those around them.
Standing firm in your values when others retreat is itself a form of worship and a mark of true character.
practical_advice: When people you counted on abandon you in a moment of need, resist the urge to collapse; take stock of who remains and build forward with them.
If you notice a pattern of making excuses to avoid commitment when it becomes costly, reflect honestly on whether your stated values align with your actions.
When someone close to you acts with what you believe is cowardice or betrayal, try to express your concern to them directly before giving up on them entirely, as Abdullah ibn Amr did.

title: The Banu Salama and Banu Haritha Almost Lose Courage
summary: When Abdullah ibn Ubayy deserted with his men, the tribes of Banu Salama and Banu Haritha were on the verge of losing heart, but God strengthened them and revealed a Quranic verse about His protection of them.
story: "Wrwa b. Musa b. Wqha related that when 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy and his men were about to lose courage, so God withdrew, Banu Salama and Bana Haritha were about to lose courage, yet God Almighty gave them strength. He therefore revealed, 'When two of your parties were about to lose courage, yet God was the protector of them both, and it is upon God that believers rely' (surat Al 'Imrzn; 111, v.123). Jabir b. 'Abd Allah stated, as is established about him in both sahih collections, 'I would not have liked it if it had not been revealed, for '... God was the protector of them both.'''"
lessons: Wavering in moments of fear is a deeply human experience; what matters is not that we never feel like retreating, but that we hold on and allow God to strengthen us.
Divine protection does not always come before the moment of weakness — sometimes it comes precisely in that moment, turning near-failure into steadfastness.
The Quran honours human vulnerability by acknowledging it; our struggles are not hidden from God, and they do not disqualify us from His mercy.
practical_advice: In moments when you feel close to giving up, remind yourself that feeling weak is not the same as being abandoned; help can arrive exactly when you need it most.
Reflect on past moments when you almost gave up but didn't, and consider what or who steadied you; let that memory be a resource in future trials.
When you see someone close to losing heart, offer them a word of encouragement; you may be the means through which God strengthens them.

title: The Blind Hypocrite Who Threw Dirt at the Prophet
summary: As the Muslim army passed through the farm of Marbad ibn Qayzi, the blind hypocrite began throwing dirt in their faces and refused them entry, yet the Prophet (SAAS) forbade his Companions from harming the man.
story: "The Prophet (SAAS) then asked his Companions, 'Who can take us close to the enemy by a path that will not take us past them?' Abu Khaythama, brother of Banu Haritha b. al-Haith replied, 'I will, Messenger of God.' He then led them out into the territory of Banu Haritha, passing through their properties. Eventually Abu Khaythama led him into the farm of Mirbacb. Qayzi. This man was a hypocrite and also blind. When he heard the sound of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims with him passing by, he began tossing dirt into their faces, saying, 'If you are the Messenger of God, then I do not permit you to enter my garden!' Ibn Ishaq continued, 'It was told to me that he then took up a handful of dirt, saying, 'By God, if I knew I'd strike no one but you, Muhammad, I'd hit you in the face with this!' The Muslims charged at him to kill him, but the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Don't kill him! This sightless fellow is blind both in his heart and in his eyes!'"
lessons: The Prophet's (SAAS) mercy extended even to those who openly insulted and provoked him; this is one of the most profound models of emotional self-mastery in history.
There is a kind of blindness that has nothing to do with the eyes — the inability to see truth and goodness when they stand directly before us.
Restraining justified anger in defence of a higher principle is one of the most difficult and most rewarding forms of inner struggle.
practical_advice: When someone wrongs you in a way that provokes immediate anger, pause before responding; ask yourself whether retaliation will serve any purpose beyond momentary relief.
Practice distinguishing between people who wrong you out of malice and those who do so out of genuine confusion or spiritual blindness; the response to each may be different.
Let the Prophet's example of forbearance in this moment be a touchstone when you feel humiliated or disrespected; responding with composure is its own form of dignity.

title: Abu 'Amir the 'Sinner' at the Battle of Uhud
summary: Abu 'Amir, a man who had abandoned his people and allied with the Quraysh against the Muslims, called out to his former tribe at Uhud only to be rejected and cursed, leading him to lament that evil had befallen his people since his departure.
story: 'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qaada related to me that Abu 'Amir 'Abdu 'Amr b. Sayfi b. Malik b. al-Nu'man, one of Banu Dubayk, had left for Mecca, having disagreed with the Messenger of God (SAAS); he was accompanied by 50 young men of Aws, though some say they were 15 in number. He had been promising Quraysh that if he were to meet up with his people, no two men of them would quarrel with him. When the sides clashed, the first to meet them was Abu 'Amir with the Abyssinians and the slaves owned by the Meccans. Abu 'Amir shouted out, 'Aws, I am Abu 'Amir!' They responded, 'May God grant you no ease, you sinner!' Before Islam, Abu 'Amir had been known as 'the monk'; the Messenger of God (SAAS) had renamed him 'the sinner'. When Abu 'Amir heard their reply, he shouted, 'So, evil has befallen my people since I left!' He then engaged them in fierce battle, eventually casting stones at them.
lessons: Betraying one's community and principles for personal grievance leads to isolation and regret — Abu 'Amir expected a warm reception and received rejection.
Renouncing one's faith community does not erase one's accountability; the consequences of such choices follow a person into the most critical moments.
The names and reputations we earn through our actions follow us — Abu 'Amir went from being called 'the monk' to 'the sinner' based on his own choices.
practical_advice: When you feel aggrieved by your community or leaders, seek to address the grievance through honest dialogue rather than abandoning your values altogether.
Before making an irreversible decision driven by anger or disagreement, pause and consider how you will feel when facing the long-term consequences of that choice.
Reflect on whether the path you are considering is motivated by principle or by ego — Abu 'Amir's confidence that his people would rally to him was rooted in pride, not reality.

title: The Prophet Sends Back Young Companions from Uhud
summary: Before the Battle of Uhud, the Prophet Muhammad sent back several young Companions including Ibn 'Umar, Usama b. Zayd, and others due to their young age, though some were later permitted to stay based on their abilities.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent back a group of young men from the battle of Uhud, not permitting them to be present at the fighting because of their youthfulness. These included 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar, as is established in both Sahih collections. He stated, 'I was offered to the Messenger of God (SAAS) for the battle of Uhud, but he would not allow me. I was later presented to him for the battle of al-khandaq, 'the trench', at a time when I was 15, and he then did permit me to participate.' On that occasion he also rejected Usama b. Zayd, Zayd b. Thabit, al-Bara' b. 'Azib, Usayd b. Dhahir and 'Araba b. Aws b. Qayzi. Ibn Qutayba related this and al-Suhayli also gave it. Ibn Sa'd b. Khaythama was also one of these. Al-Suhayli mentioned him too. He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) allowed all of these to participate at the battle of al-khandaq. He had sent back from Uhud Samura b. Jundub and Rafi' b. Khadij, both of these being 15 years of age. But someone told him that Rafi' was an archer, and so he let him stay. Then someone told him that Samura could wrestle Rafi' down, and so he let him stay too.
lessons: Being turned away from something you desire is not always a permanent rejection — many of the young Companions were given their opportunity at a later time when they were ready.
True wisdom in leadership includes protecting those in your care even when it disappoints them; the Prophet's concern for the young men's safety was an act of love, not dismissal.

Individual strengths and abilities matter — Rafi' and Samura were ultimately permitted to stay because they demonstrated real competence, showing that preparation and developing one's skills opens doors.
practical_advice: If you feel excluded or turned away from an opportunity, do not interpret it as a permanent verdict on your worth — use the time to grow and prepare for when the door reopens.
Trust that those in authority over you may see risks or limitations you cannot yet see yourself, and respond with patience rather than resentment.
Invest in developing specific skills and strengths; when Rafi' was identified as an archer and Samura as a wrestler, those abilities earned them a place — preparation creates opportunity.

title: The Prophet's Instructions to the Archers at Uhud
summary: Before the Battle of Uhud, the Prophet appointed 'Abd Allah b. Jubayr over fifty archers and gave them a critical command to hold their position no matter the outcome of the battle, instructions that would prove fateful.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) made ready for battle. His force consisted of 700 men; he appointed 'Abd Allah b. Jubayr, brother of Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf, in charge of the archers. He stood out by being dressed in white clothing; the archers totalled 50 men. He (the Prophet (SAAS)) said 'Fend off the cavalry from us with your arrows, so that they don't come at us from the rear. Whether the battle goes for or against us, hold your position so that we can't be attacked from your direction.'
lessons: Clear, decisive instructions given in advance of a crisis can mean the difference between success and catastrophe — the Prophet's command to the archers was precise and unconditional.
Discipline and obedience to a trusted leader, especially under pressure, is one of the most important qualities in both battle and in life's challenges.
The wisdom of a plan is not always immediately visible to those carrying it out; trusting the strategy of someone with greater perspective is an act of faith and humility.
practical_advice: When given clear guidance by someone you trust, follow it fully — partial obedience in critical moments is as dangerous as no obedience at all.
Before entering a difficult situation, take time to agree on clear boundaries and responsibilities with those who are depending on you.

Resist the temptation to abandon your post or your commitment when things seem to be going well; the instruction to 'hold your position whether the battle goes for or against us' is a timeless lesson in consistency.

title: Abu Dujana Spares the Woman on the Battlefield
summary: During the Battle of Uhud, Abu Dujana encountered a woman who was inciting the enemy and, out of respect for the Prophet's sword, chose not to strike her, demonstrating moral restraint even amid fierce combat.
story: Abu Dujana said, 'I saw one man who was giving great encouragement to his side, so I headed for him. When I attacked him with my sword, he let out a scream and I saw it was a woman! I respected the sword of the Messenger of God (SAAS) by refraining from striking a woman with it.'
lessons: The values instilled in us by those we deeply respect can hold us to a higher standard even in our most intense and pressured moments.
Moral principles are not luxuries of peaceful times — they are tested and proven precisely in moments of crisis and danger.
Showing restraint when you have the power to act otherwise is one of the highest expressions of character and integrity.
practical_advice: Identify the values that are truly non-negotiable for you and commit to them in advance, so that when you are under pressure, you do not have to deliberate — the decision is already made.
When you feel the impulse to lash out at someone in anger or frustration, ask yourself whether doing so would honor the trust placed in you by those you respect most.
Let the question 'would this honor the people and principles I hold dear?' serve as a practical check on your actions in heated moments.

title: Hind and the Women Beating Tambourines Behind the Quraysh Army
summary: At the Battle of Uhud, Hind bint 'Utba and a group of women positioned themselves behind the Quraysh army, beating tambourines and reciting verses of incitement to drive the men forward in battle.
story: When the forces met and drew close to one another, Hind, daughter of 'Utba, arose amidst the women accompanying her, and they took up tambourines and began beating them, positioning themselves behind the men and urging them on to battle. Hind spoke the following verses: 'Go to it, 'Abd al-Dar, go to it protectors of the rear! Strike with your sharp weapons!' She also said, 'If you advance we will embrace and spread out cushions; But retreat and we'll leave and love not!'
lessons: Incitement to hatred and conflict, whether through words, poetry, or song, carries moral weight — Hind's role in the battle was not passive, and her words had real consequences.
The tools of influence — language, poetry, music, and public speech — are powerful forces that can be directed toward either harm or good.
Understanding the emotional and social forces that drive people to conflict helps us recognize and resist similar manipulation in our own lives.
practical_advice: Be mindful of the media, voices, and content you consume, especially during times of conflict or stress — not everything presented as motivation is actually good for your soul.

When you feel stirred up to anger or aggression by words, rhetoric, or social pressure, pause to ask whether those feelings are serving a just and considered purpose or simply exploiting your emotions.
Use your own capacity for speech and expression to build, encourage, and unify rather than to incite — the impact of your words on others' actions is real and lasting.

title: Abu Sufyan Goads the Banu 'Abd al-Dar Over the Banner
summary: Before the Battle of Uhud, Abu Sufyan provoked the Banu 'Abd al-Dar by questioning their performance at Badr and implying they should surrender their banner, successfully stoking their pride and securing their fierce commitment to fight.
story: Abu Sufyan had told those of Banu 'Abd al-Dar who were in charge of the banner, inciting them to battle, 'Banu 'Abd al-Dar, you were in charge of our banner at the battle of Badr, and you saw what happened to us. Men will only fight for their flags; if once they are lost, they withdraw. Either take care of our banner for us, or give it over to us and we will relieve you of it.' They were angered at this and threatened him, saying, 'We give over our banner to you? You will learn tomorrow, when we meet in battle, how we will behave!' That was the response that Abu Sufyan wanted.
lessons: Manipulating someone's pride is one of the oldest tactics of those who wish to use others for their own ends — Abu Sufyan deliberately provoked anger to extract commitment he could not command directly.
Reactive decisions made from wounded pride rather than clear judgment often lead us into situations that primarily serve someone else's agenda.
Recognizing when you are being manipulated through your ego is a crucial skill; Abu Sufyan got exactly the response he wanted because the Banu 'Abd al-Dar did not stop to question his motive.
practical_advice: Before making a strong commitment in response to a challenge or provocation, pause to ask whether you are responding from your own considered values or from a reactive need to protect your pride.
