Jubayr, who said, "I heard one of the muhajrrin saying, 'I was present at Uhud and saw the arrows coming in from all directions, with the Messenger of God (SAAS) right in their middle, but all of them were diverted from him. And I saw 'Ahd Allah b. Shihab al-Zuhri that day as he said, "Direct me to Muhammad; 1'11 not survive if he does." The Messenger of God (SAAS) was right there at his side, with no one else with him. He went on past and Safwan b. Umayya criticized him for that. 'Abd All& responded, "By God, I didn't see him! I swear by God, he is forbidden to us. There were four of us who agreed together and made a pact to kill him, but we couldn't get to him.""'
lessons: God's protection operates in ways beyond human comprehension; what appears as chaos may conceal divine order.
Even the most organized plans of those who intend harm can be rendered powerless when God wills otherwise.
Witnessing unexplainable protection in moments of danger can deepen one's trust in God's care for the righteous.
practical_advice: When you feel surrounded by threats on all sides, recall accounts of divine protection to strengthen your trust that God can turn circumstances in an instant.
Anxiety about outcomes is natural, but reminding yourself that ultimate control belongs to God can bring genuine calm in moments of crisis.
Reflect on times in your own life when danger passed inexplicably — these moments can become anchors of faith during future trials.

title: The Curse Upon Utba b. Abi Waqqas for Wounding the Prophet
summary: After Utba b. Abi Waqqas broke the Prophet's lower teeth and bloodied his face at Uhud, the Prophet cursed him that he would die as an unbeliever. The narration confirms this curse was fulfilled.
story: 'Abd al-Razzzq stated that Ma'mar related to him, from al-Zuhri, from Wthmm al-Hariiri, from Miqsam, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke a curse against Wtba b. Abii Waqqq when he broke his lower teeth and bloodied his face, saying, "0 God, may change not come to him, so that he dies an unbeliever!" And no change did come over him, so that he did die an unbeliever, bound for hell-fire.
lessons: Harming those who carry a divine message carries grave spiritual consequences that extend beyond the immediate moment.
The Prophet's prayer was not born of personal vengeance but of spiritual reality — those who persist in oppression close their own hearts to guidance.
Every action leaves a mark on the soul; cruelty and transgression, when left unrepented, can harden the heart beyond return.
practical_advice: When you have been wronged, channel your pain into sincere supplication rather than seeking personal revenge — leave justice to God.
Reflect on whether any of your recurring behaviors are hardening your heart, and make sincere repentance before those patterns become permanent.
Use awareness of consequences — spiritual and otherwise — as motivation to treat others with integrity even when it is difficult.

title: Ibn Qami'a's False Claim That He Had Killed the Prophet
summary: After wounding the Prophet at Uhud, Abd Allah b. Qami'a falsely proclaimed he had killed Muhammad, causing widespread panic and confusion among the Muslim ranks.
story: When he had done injury to the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Abd Alhh b. Qami'a went back and said, "I've killed Muhammad!" And on that occasion that devil the 'izb9 of al-'Aqaba shouted at the top of its voice, "Hear this! Muhammad has been killed!" Consternation spread among the Muslims. Many people believed it, and yet remained determined to fight on in defence of Islam until they died in the same way as had the Messenger of God (SAAS). Anas b. al-Nadr and others of whom mention will be made were among these.
lessons: In moments of crisis, false information can spread rapidly and cause devastation — composure and verification are essential.
True faith is revealed not in comfortable times but in the moments when everything appears to be lost.
Even those who believed the worst had happened chose to remain steadfast, showing that commitment to one's values must transcend circumstance.
practical_advice: When alarming news reaches you, pause before reacting — verify its truth before allowing fear and panic to drive your decisions.
In your darkest moments, ask yourself: even if the worst were true, what is the right and honorable thing to do? Act from that place.
Build your sense of purpose on principles deeper than outcomes, so that even devastating news does not cause you to abandon what you believe in.

title: The Quranic Verses Revealed About the Rumor of the Prophet's Death
summary: When the false rumor spread at Uhud that the Prophet had been killed, Allah revealed verses reminding the believers that Muhammad was a messenger like those before him and that they must not turn back from their faith.
story: Almighty God had sent down consolatory verses to deal with this eventuality. He had stated, "And M u h a d is no more than a messenger; messengers have passed away before him. And if he dies or is killed, will you turn on your heels? Whoever should turn on his heels will do no harm to God thereby. And God will reward the grateful. And no soul shall die except with God's permission at a specified time. And whoever desires the rewards of this world, those We will give him; and whoever desires the rewards of the hereafter We will give him those. We will reward the grateful. And many are the prophets who have fought along with many worshippers! And they did not weaken because of what befell them in God's cause. They neither weakened nor abased themselves. God loves those who stand firm. And what they said was merely, 'O God of ours, forgive us our sins and our excesses! Set our feet firmly and give us victory against the disbelieving enemy!' And so God did give them the reward of this world and the fine reward of the hereafter; God does love those who do good. O you who believe, if you obey those who disbelieve, they will turn you back on your heels and you will go back having lost. But God is indeed your master and He is best of all helpers. We will cast terror into the hearts of those who disbelieve for their having associated with God that for which He sent down no authority. Hell-fue shall be their abode. And terrible is the abode of the unjust!" (sarat Al-'Imrrin; III, v.143-50).
lessons: Faith must be grounded in God and His truth, not in the continued presence of any individual — even the most beloved of people.
God does not abandon His community in crisis; divine guidance arrives precisely in the moments of greatest confusion and need.
Prophets and their companions throughout history faced hardship and loss, yet did not weaken — we are invited into that same lineage of resilience.
practical_advice: When a source of strength in your life is suddenly removed, return to your foundational beliefs rather than allowing grief to unravel your sense of purpose.
Memorize or reflect regularly on Quranic verses that speak of steadfastness, so they are available to you in moments of shock and crisis.
Distinguish between grieving a loss — which is human and necessary — and abandoning your values because of that loss; the two are not the same.

title: Abu Bakr's Address After the Prophet's Death Recalling the Verse from Uhud
summary: When the Prophet passed away, Abu Bakr addressed the people and recited the verse revealed at Uhud — 'Muhammad is no more than a messenger' — and the companions felt as though they had never heard it before.
story: Abii Bakr, "the trusting", made an address at the first assembly he held following the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS) in which he said, "0my people, regarding those who worshipped Muhammad, Muhammad has died. Regarding those who worshipped God, God is alive and shall not die!" He then recited this verse: "And Muhammad is no more than a messenger." He said, "It was as though the people had never heard this before, and so all, without exception, recited it thereafter."
lessons: In the greatest moments of grief, a single clear word of truth spoken with conviction can anchor an entire community.
The difference between worshipping a person and following a message is the difference between a faith that collapses and one that endures.
Abu Bakr's composure in the face of personal devastation is a model of how to lead with truth even when your own heart is broken.
practical_advice: When grief threatens to overwhelm you, return to the foundational truths of your faith — they exist precisely to hold you in those moments.
If you are in a position of leadership or trust and those around you are in panic, speak clearly, speak truly, and speak with compassion.
Reflect on whether your sense of peace is tied to a person, a situation, or to God — and work to anchor it in the One who does not change.

title: The Ansari Companion Who Continued Fighting After Hearing the Prophet Was Killed
summary: At Uhud, an Ansari companion covered in blood was told the Prophet had been killed. He responded that if the Prophet had fulfilled his mission, they must fight on for their religion — a response so profound it prompted a Quranic revelation.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related in his Dala'tl al-Nubamma (Sagns of the Prophethood), through Ibn Abii Najih, from his father, who said, "At m u d , one of the muhajirin passed by one of the atgar, a man who was covered in his own blood, and asked hi, 'Are you aware that Muhammad has been killed?' The atgari responded, 'If Muhammad (SAAS) has been killed, then he must have fulfilled his mission! So fight on for your religion!' "And so the verse was revealed, 'And Muhammad is no more than a messenger; messengers have passed away before hi.'" That atgltri might well have been Anas b. al-Nadr, may God be pleased with him; he was the uncle of Anas b. Mzlik.
lessons: True conviction means that your commitment to what is right does not depend on whether you see those you love surviving alongside you.
The greatest responses in history often come from ordinary people in their most desperate moments — your character in crisis is your truest character.
This companion, wounded and alone, still found clarity — showing that faith can cut through pain and confusion when it is deeply rooted.
practical_advice: Ask yourself: if everything external that sustains your motivation were removed, would you still know what the right thing to do is? Build that inner clarity.
When you feel that a source of hope or guidance has been lost, ask what that source stood for — and carry that forward.
Even in your most wounded state, you may have more to offer others than you realize. Do not dismiss the significance of small acts of courage.

title: Anas b. al-Nadr's Vow and Martyrdom at Uhud
summary: Anas b. al-Nadr, who had missed the Battle of Badr, vowed to show his devotion in the next battle. At Uhud he advanced fearlessly, was found with over 80 wounds, and could only be identified by his fingertips — fulfilling his vow completely.
story: Imiim Ahmad stated, "Yazid related to us, quoting Hamid, from Anas, that his uncle was absent from the fighting at Badr and said, 'I was absent from the fust battle fought by the Prophet (SAAS) against the polytheists; if God should have me witness a battle against the polytheists, I'll certainly show him what I can do!' "When, at the battle of Uhud, the Muslims gave way, he said, 'O God, I apologize to you for what these men have done' - meaning his fellow fighters - 'and I declare myself innocent of what these others' -meaning the polytheists - have done against You.' He then advanced and was met before Uhud by Sa'd h. Mu%dh. Sa'd told hi,'I am with you!' And Sa'd also said, 'But I could not do what he did.' "Some 80 wounds were found on him, consisting of sword cuts, spear thrusts and arrow punctures." He went on, "We used to say that it was about him and his Companions that the verse was revealed, 'and of them there are those who fulfilled their vow; and of them there are those who wait'" (sarat al-Ahzab; XXXIII, v.23). Al-Tirmidhi related this from 'Abd b. Hamid; al-NasPi related it from Ishxq b. m a w a y h ; both gave Yazid b. Hzriin as a link. Al-Tirmidhi stated, "This tradition is hasan, 'good'." I would comment that this hadith conforms to the criteria of the sahih collections, from this source. Imiim A b a d stated that both Bahz and Hzshirn related to him that Sulaymzn b. al-Mughira quoted Thzhit as having said, "Anas (b. M&) stated, 'My uncle' - Hashirn gave his name as Anas b. al-Nadr - 'was the one for whom I was named. He was not present at Badr. This fact troubled him, and so he said, "I was absent from the first battle fought by the Messenger of God (SAAS); if God allows me to participate with him later in another battle, then he will certainly see what it is I can do!"'" He (Anas b. Mali) went on, "He was too much in awe to say anything different. And he was present at Uhud with the Messenger of God (SAAS). He met up with SaU b. Mu%dh, and Anas asked him, 'Abii 'Amr, where to? How wonderful is the fragrance of paradise I sense before Uhud!'" He (Anas b. Malik) continued, "He fought them until he was killed. Some 80 wounds were found on his body - sword cuts, spear thrusts and arrow punctures. His sister, my aunt al-Rabic, daughter of al-Nadr, said, 'I only recognized my brother by his fingertips.' And this verse was revealed, 'and of them are those who fulfilled their vow, and of them there are those who wait - and they have not changed at all'" (s~ratal-Ahzdb; XXXIII, v.23). He concluded, "They used to consider that this verse was revealed about h i and his Companions."
lessons: Guilt over a missed opportunity can be transformed into extraordinary devotion — the key is channeling regret into positive resolve rather than self-punishment.
Anas b. al-Nadr's words — 'I sense the fragrance of paradise before Uhud' — show that sincere conviction can replace fear with anticipation even in the face of death.
Fulfilling a vow made to God completely, holding nothing back, is one of the highest expressions of integrity a person can achieve.
practical_advice: If you carry guilt about a time you fell short, make a sincere commitment to do better going forward — then act on that commitment with everything you have.
When facing something frightening, try to reconnect with your deeper purpose and what lies beyond the difficulty; this can transform anxiety into resolve.
Share your intentions and commitments with trusted companions who will walk alongside you and hold you accountable, as Sa'd b. Mu'adh offered to do for Anas.

title: Ubayy b. Khalaf's Oath to Kill the Prophet and His Death at Uhud
summary: Ubayy b. Khalaf swore in Makkah to kill the Prophet, but at the Battle of Uhud the Prophet struck him in the neck with a spear, and Ubayy died of his wound — fulfilling the Prophet's own earlier warning that he would be the one to kill him.
story: Abii al-Aswad stated, from 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, who said, "Ubayy h. Khalaf, a brother of Bane Jumah, had made an oath in Mecca that he would certainly kill the Messenger of God (SAAS). When news of the oath reached him, he said, 'No; I will kill him, if God wills it!' "When it was time for the battle of Uhud, Ubayy came forth armoured and helmeted, saying, 'I will not live on if Muhammad does!' He attacked the Messenger of God (SAAS) trying to kill hi, but Mus'ah b. Wmayr, a brother of Banii <Abdal-Dw, met him in defence of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Muscab b. Wmayr was killed. The Messenger of God (SAAS) noticed that Ubayy's collarbone was visible between his helmet and his suit of chain-mail, and so he thrust him there with his spear. Uhayy fell from his horse, though no blood was coming from his wound. "His companions came and carried him off; he was bellowing like a bull, but people told hi,'He's not harmed you; it's just a scratch.' "He then told them of what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said, namely that he would kill Ubayy. He then said, 'I swear by Him who holds my soul in His hand, if what afflicts me were to afflict all the people of Dhii al-Maje, they'd aU die of it!' "And he did die and go to hell; and damned be all the denizens of hell-fire!'' Miisa b. Wqba related this tradition in his military chronicle, from al-Zuhri, from Sa'id h. al-Musayyab, in similar terms. Ibn Iswq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) made his way up into the defde, Ubayy h. Khalaf caught up with him and said, 'I shall not live on if you do!' "The Muslims asked, '0 Messenger of God, should one of our men go for him?' He replied, 'Just leave him alone.' "When Ubayy came close, the Messenger of God (SAAS) took a spear from al-Harith b. al-Summa. Some people said, as I have been told, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) took it from hi, he shook himself so hard that they flew off away from him like a hair from the back of a camel stung by a fly! The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went towards Ubayy and thrust h i in the neck, so that he swayed back and forth several times, then fell from his horse." Al-Waqidi recounted much the same from Yiinus b. Bukayr, from Muhammad b. Iswq, from 'Asim b. Wmar b. Qasda, from 'Abd All& b. Ka'b b. Malik, from his father. Al-Wnqidi stated that Ibn Wmar used to say, "Ubayy b. Khalaf died in the FGbigh valley. I was once passing through the B b i g h valley late at night when all of a sudden flames flared up, scaring me greatly. And there was a man emerging from it, dragging a chain and with a raging thirst. Then another man said, 'Don't give him anything to drink! He was killed by the Messenger of God (SAAS); this man is Ubayy b. Khalaf.'" This hadirh is fully authenticated in both sahih collections, as stated above, through 'Ahd d-Razznq, from M a k a r , from Hammam, from Ahti Hurayra, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'Great is God's wrath against a man whom the Messenger of God kills in God's cause.'" Al-Bukh~ri related this through Ibn Jurayj, from 'Amr h. Dinw, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, with the wording, "Great is God's wrath against someone killed by the hand of the Messenger of God (SAAS) in God's cause."
lessons: Hatred and the desire for revenge ultimately destroy the one who harbors them, not the one they are directed against.
Divine protection surrounds those who sincerely dedicate themselves to God's path, even in the most dangerous circumstances.
Arrogance and enmity toward truth carry their own inescapable consequences.
practical_advice: When someone directs hostility or ill-will toward you, trust in God's protection rather than retaliating out of fear or anger.
Reflect on how harboring long-term hatred corrodes one's own soul and redirect that energy toward constructive purpose.
Use awareness of accountability — that our choices have lasting consequences — as motivation to choose integrity over revenge.

title: Jabir's Father Killed at Uhud, and the Angels Who Shaded His Body
summary: Jabir began weeping and uncovering his father's face after he was killed at Uhud, but the Prophet told him not to weep, saying the angels had shaded his father with their wings until he was raised up.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Aba al-Walid said, quoting Shu'ba, from Ibn d-Munkadii, who said, "I heard Jabir say, 'When my father was killed, I began to weep and to uncover the shroud from his face. The Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) stopped me, but he himself did not. Instead, he told me, "Don't weep for him; the angels kept him shaded with their wings until he was raised up."'" This tradition is related here in this form with a full chain of authorities. In the section (of his work) on funerals, he (al-Bukhari) gives its provenance as from Bindar, from Ghundar, from Shu'ba. Muslim and al-Nasa'i related it from various paths, all quoting Shu'ba.
lessons: Grief is a natural human response to loss, and even the Prophet did not prevent Jabir from expressing it — compassion toward mourners is a prophetic quality.
Divine honor is granted to those who sacrifice themselves in God's cause, even when their worldly end appears sorrowful.
The comfort offered by the Prophet to Jabir teaches us that genuine consolation comes from reframing loss through a higher spiritual lens.
practical_advice: When grieving a loss, allow yourself to feel the emotion without shame, while also seeking spiritual perspective that can bring peace.
Offer comfort to those who are grieving by sharing words of hope about the honored status of their loved one rather than simply telling them to stop crying.
Remind yourself during moments of loss that those who lived with sincerity and faith are not abandoned — they are honored in ways beyond what we can see.

title: Mus'ab b. Umayr's Simple Burial and the Companions' Reflection on Worldly Favors
summary: Mus'ab b. Umayr was martyred at Uhud and had only a small cloak for a shroud — too short to cover both his head and feet. His companions reflected on this with sorrow and gratitude, fearing that their own worldly comfort might be a sign their reward had come prematurely.
story: Al-Bukhsi stated that 'Ahdm related to him, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Mubzak, from Shu'ba, from Sa'd b. Ibrzhim, from Ibrahim's father, to the effect that 'Ahd al-Ra)?mxn b. 'Awf was brought food while he was fasting. He then said, "Muscab b. Wmayr was killed, yet he was better than me. A cloak was used as his shroud. If it was placed over his head, his feet were visible, while if his feet were covered his head was visible." He is also quoted as saying, "Hamza was killed, yet he was better than me, while we have been granted earthly favours." He is also quoted as saying, "Things have been given to us here on earth, and we fear that the favours have come prematurely for us." He then wept, till the food was cold. Al-Bukhai is alone in giving this hadith. Al-Bukhzri stated that A h a d b. Yanus related to hi, quoting Zuhayr and al-Akash, from Shaqiq, from Khabbah b. al-Aratt, who said, "We went forth with the Prophet (SAAS), seeking to work in God's cause, and reward from God was our due. Some of us passed away or left without enjoying any part of their reward. Muv'ab b. Wmayr was one of these; he was killed at the battle of Uhud, leaving nothing but a woollen cloak. When we covered his head with it, his feet emerged. When his feet were covered with it, his head emerged. And so the Prophet (SAAS) told us, 'Cover his head with it and place the al-rdhkcir, (an aromatic rush), over his feet. Some of us have their fruit already ripened; he is harvesting them.'"
lessons: Material simplicity at death can reflect spiritual richness — the absence of worldly possessions at one's end is not a sign of failure but can be a mark of sincerity.
Gratitude for worldly blessings should be accompanied by humility and the awareness that ease in this life is not necessarily the highest reward.
Remembering those who sacrificed more than us is a powerful antidote to self-satisfaction and a prompt for deeper devotion.
practical_advice: When you enjoy comfort and abundance, pause to reflect on whether you are using those gifts in ways that align with your values, rather than treating them as ends in themselves.
Practice voluntary simplicity or fasting occasionally to reconnect with gratitude and to keep perspective on what truly matters.
When feeling overwhelmed by your own relative comfort compared to others who suffered, channel that feeling into increased generosity and service rather than guilt.

title: Hudhayfa's Father Al-Yaman Killed by Mistake by the Muslims at Uhud
summary: During the chaos of Uhud, Hudhayfa's elderly father al-Yaman left the rear defenses to join the fighting and was accidentally killed by his fellow Muslims. Hudhayfa forgave them entirely and harbored no ill will for the rest of his life.
story: Al-Bukh~ also stated that Wbayd Allah b. Sacid related to hi, quoting Abii Usma, from Hisham b. Wrwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, "When the battle of Uhud arrived, the polytheists were being defeated. So Satan, God damn him, called out, 'Worshippers of God, look to your rear!' At this the forward lines turned around and began battling with their rear lines. Hudhayfa saw his father al-Yam= right there and so he shouted, 'Worshippers of God! My father!'" 'A'isha went on, "But, I swear by God they continued fighting until they had killed him. And Hudhayfa said, 'May God forgive them.'" Wrwa commented, 'I swear that goodwill continued on in Hudhayfa until he joined Almighty and Glorious God.' I note that the reason this happened was that al-Yaman and Thabit b. Waqsh were hack in the defences with the women because of their age and debility. The two men had said, 'There's no more than a donkey's life-span left for our generation.' And so they had dismounted to be there at the fighting and their course had taken them over towards the polytheists. Thxbit was killed by the polytheists, while al-Yam% was killed by mistake by the Muslims. Hudhayfa gave up his claim against the Muslims to blood money for his father and blamed none of them because of the obvious excuse for it.
lessons: Forgiveness in the face of devastating, even accidental loss is one of the highest expressions of faith and moral character.
Chaos and confusion can lead to terrible mistakes — responding with grace rather than vengeance is what defines a person's true character.
Holding onto goodwill after being wronged, as Hudhayfa did for the rest of his life, brings peace to the soul that resentment never could.
practical_advice: When you are hurt by someone's mistake — even a serious one — ask yourself whether holding onto anger serves you or the situation, and consider whether forgiveness might bring more healing.
Practice saying 'May God forgive them' or an equivalent prayer for those who have harmed you, even when the harm feels unforgivable, as a way of releasing the burden of resentment.
Remember that in moments of crisis, people act imperfectly — extending charitable interpretation to others' mistakes protects your own inner peace.

title: Qatada b. al-Nu'man's Eye Restored by the Prophet at Uhud
summary: At the Battle of Uhud, Qatada b. al-Nu'man was struck in the eye so severely it slipped out onto his cheek, but the Prophet placed it back with his own hand and it became the better and sharper of his two eyes.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "And Qatada b. al-Nucman was struck in the eye that day, so that it fell out on to his cheek. The Messenger of God (SAAS) put it back in for hi, using his hand; it was thereafter always the better and sharper of Qatada's eyes." There is a hadith from Jahii b. 'Abd Allah that Qamda b. al-Nucm% was struck in the eye at Uhud, so that it slipped out on to his cheek. The Messenger of God (SAAS) put it back in its place and it was thereafter the better and sharper of his eyes. It did not become inflamed when his other eye was so inflicted. Al-D%qumi related, with a hadith that was gharib, "unilateral", from Malik, from Muhammad b. 'Abd All& b. Abn Sa'Sa'a, from his father, from Abn Sacid, from his brother Qatzda b. d-Nucmm, who said, "Both my eyes were struck at Uhud and they fell out on to my cheeks. I went with them to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he returned them both to their places. He spat into them and they regained sight." The first version is the one generally accepted — that he was struck in the one eye. This is why, when his son presented himself to Wmar b. 'Abd alLCAziz, and the latter asked him to identify himself, he replied, in verse: "I'm the one whose father's eye slipped out on to his cheek / And it was replaced most effectively by the hand of the Chosen One. / It became again as it had been at first. / How fine an eye it became and how fine a cheek!" Thereupon Wmar b. 'Abd al-CAziz responded with the line: "Those noble qualities were not two cups of milk mixed with water that would later change into urine!" Wmar then welcomed him and rewarded him richly; may God be pleased with hi!
lessons: Divine healing and miraculous assistance come to those who remain in service to God even at the cost of personal harm.
The legacy of sincere sacrifice endures across generations — Qatada's son was honored by a caliph generations later because of his father's devotion.
Trusting in God's power to restore what has been lost or broken is a source of hope and courage when facing injury or hardship.
practical_advice: When you are wounded — physically, emotionally, or spiritually — turn toward those with wisdom and care rather than withdrawing alone, just as Qatada turned to the Prophet.
Preserve and share stories of resilience and miraculous recovery within your family and community, as they become a source of honor and identity for generations.
Use moments of physical vulnerability as reminders of your ultimate dependence on God, and let that dependence be a comfort rather than a source of anxiety.

title: Nusayba (Umm Imarah) Fights to Defend the Prophet at Uhud and Is Wounded
summary: Nusayba bint Ka'b, known as Umm Imarah, went to the Battle of Uhud with water but took up arms when the Muslims were defeated, defending the Prophet with sword and bow until she received a deep shoulder wound from Ibn Qami'a.
story: Ibn Hishzm stated, '"Umara's mother, Nasiba, daughter of Ka'b al-Mainiyya, fought at Uhud. Sacid b. Abn Zayd al-Ansm recounted that Sacd's mother, daughter of Sa'd b. d-Rabic, used to say, "Um%a's mother came in to me and I asked her, 'Aunt, tell me your story.' She replied, 'Early in the day I went out to see what our people were doing, bearing a water-skim fded with water. At the time I reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was among his Companions, everything was going in the Muslims' favour. When the Muslims were defeated, I stood near the Messenger of God (SAAS), engaging in the battle, defending hi with my sword and shooting with my bow until I was ultimately wounded.'"" Umm Sa'd went on, "And on her shoulder I saw a deep scar, so I asked her, 'Who was it gave you this wound?' She replied, 'It was Ibn Qami'a, God damn him! When our men deserted the Messenger of God (SAAS), he advanced, saying, "Direct me towards Muhammad; I will not live on if he survives!" I blocked his way, along with Mus'ab b. Wmayr and others who stayed firm with the Messenger of God (SAAS). It was then that he gave me this wound; and I struck him many times back, but that enemy of God was wearing double armour.'"
lessons: Courage and faithfulness are not defined by gender or role — Nusayba came as a water-bearer and became a warrior because duty demanded it.
Standing firm when others flee is one of the most powerful expressions of loyalty and trust in God.
The scars we carry from standing up for what is right are marks of honor, not shame.
practical_advice: When a situation calls for more than you originally prepared for, be willing to adapt and give what you can rather than retreating because it falls outside your expected role.
If you feel abandoned or alone in standing for something important, draw strength from the knowledge that sincere effort in God's cause is never wasted.
Carry your own 'scars' — the wounds from doing the right thing — with dignity, knowing they testify to your integrity.

title: Abu Dujana Shields the Prophet with His Own Body from Arrows at Uhud
summary: During the most intense moments of the Battle of Uhud, Abu Dujana shielded the Prophet with his own body, hunching over him so that arrows struck his back instead.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Abo Dujana shielded the Messenger of God (SAAS) with his own body. The arrows fell upon his back while Abti Dujana leaned over him."
lessons: Selfless love and loyalty move a person to place another's safety above their own survival instinct.
True devotion is demonstrated not in words but in the body — in what we are willing to endure for those we love and the causes we believe in.
Even a single act of extraordinary courage and sacrifice can define a life and become a model for all who come after.
practical_advice: Reflect on who in your life you would protect at personal cost — and let that reflection deepen your commitment to those relationships.
Practice small daily acts of self-sacrifice for others as training for the greater moments when real courage is required.
When you feel afraid, ask yourself what you love or believe in enough to act despite the fear, and let that answer guide your next step.

title: Abu Dujana Shields the Prophet at Uhud
summary: Abu Dujana shielded the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) with his own body during the Battle of Uhud, absorbing arrows in his back while leaning over him protectively.
story: Abū Dujāna shielded the Messenger of God (SAAS) with his own body. The arrows fell upon his back while Abū Dujāna leaned over him.
lessons: True loyalty means placing another's safety above your own wellbeing without hesitation.
Acts of selfless protection, even when painful, are among the highest expressions of love and devotion.
In moments of crisis, those who stand firm reveal the true depth of their character.
practical_advice: When someone you care about is in difficulty, ask yourself what concrete action you can take to shield or support them rather than stepping back.
Practice small acts of self-sacrifice in daily life so that when larger tests come, generosity becomes your instinct.
Reflect on who in your life has 'taken arrows' for you, and find a meaningful way to express gratitude to them.

title: Anas b. al-Nadr Charges the Enemy After Hearing the Prophet Was Killed
summary: When some Muslims sat down believing the Prophet had been killed at Uhud, Anas b. al-Nadr rebuked them and charged alone into the enemy ranks until he was martyred, his body found with over 70 wounds.
story: Anas b. al-Nadr, uncle of Anas b. Mālik, reached 'Umar b. al-Khaṭṭāb and Ṭalḥa b. 'Ubayd Allāh along with some muhājirīn and anṣār who wanted to surrender. He asked them, "Why are you just sitting?" They replied, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) has been killed." "And what will you do with your lives after he has gone?" Anas asked. "Get up and die for the same cause as he has!" He then advanced to the enemy and fought until he was killed. Anas b. Mālik was named after him. That day we found 70 wounds on Anas b. al-Nadr. The only person to recognize him, from his fingertips, was his sister.
lessons: Despair and surrender are never the only options; a person of faith finds purpose even in the darkest moment.
When others lose direction, one courageous voice can remind an entire group of their values and reason for standing.
The depth of one's commitment is revealed not in comfort but in the hardest moments of loss.
practical_advice: When you feel like giving up because something important seems lost, pause and ask: 'What would I be living for if I stop now?'
In moments when others around you are despairing, consider whether you have the strength to speak a word of resolve — it can change the direction of a group.
Honor the memory and sacrifice of those who came before you by continuing to strive toward the values they died for.

title: Ubayy b. Khalaf's Threat and the Prophet's Response
summary: Ubayy b. Khalaf repeatedly threatened the Prophet in Mecca that he would kill him on horseback, but the Prophet calmly replied it would be he who killed Ubayy — a prophecy fulfilled at Uhud when Ubayy died from a minor wound the Prophet inflicted.
story: Ubayy b. Khalaf used to tell the Messenger of God (SAAS), when he met him in Mecca, 'Hey, Muhammad, I've a horse, al-'Awdh, that I feed every day with measures of corn. I'll be riding it when I kill you!' To this the Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'No; it is I who will kill you, if God wills it.' When Ubayy was taken back to Quraysh having suffered a small injury in his neck, the blood having stopped flowing, he said, 'By God, Muhammad has killed me!' They told him, 'You've lost your courage! You're not badly hurt.' He replied, 'But he told me in Mecca that he was going to kill me. And, I swear, he'd have killed me if he'd only spat on me!' And that enemy of God did die, at Sarif, while they were transporting him to Mecca.
lessons: The threats of those who oppose truth ultimately return upon themselves; arrogance does not protect anyone from consequences.
The Prophet's calm, certain response to threats reflects a deep reliance on God rather than fear or anger.
Boastful aggression is often rooted in self-delusion, while quiet confidence grounded in truth endures.
practical_advice: When faced with intimidation or threats, respond with calm confidence rather than matching the aggressor's energy.
Reflect on whether your fears of others' threats are proportionate; often the threat itself is far larger than the actual danger.
Cultivate trust in God so thoroughly that when others try to unsettle you, your foundation remains firm.

title: Ali Brings Water and the Prophet Washes His Wounds
summary: After the battle of Uhud, Ali brought water in his shield for the wounded Prophet, who declined to drink due to its odour but used it to wash the blood from his face, expressing God's wrath at those who harmed His prophet.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached the start of the defile, 'Alī emerged and filled up his shield with water from the well al-Miḥāḍ. He brought it to the Messenger of God (SAAS) so that he would drink from it. But the latter found it had an unpleasant odour and declined to drink it. But he did wash the blood from his face and poured it over his head, saying, 'Great is God's wrath against whoever bloodies the face of His prophet!'
lessons: Even in suffering, the Prophet's words pointed toward God rather than personal grievance — a model of how to frame pain within a larger spiritual context.
Acts of care offered in difficulty, even when imperfect, are deeply meaningful; Ali's effort was not wasted even though the water could not be drunk.
Expressing righteous emotion — such as invoking God's justice against oppression — is a healthy and dignified response to being wronged.
practical_advice: When you are hurt or wronged, try to express your pain in a way that is rooted in principle rather than personal revenge.
When someone cares for you in a difficult moment, receive their effort with gratitude even if it does not fully meet your need.
After a painful experience, find a small act of cleansing or self-care — physical or spiritual — that helps you reorient and move forward.

title: The Noon Prayer Performed Seated at Uhud
summary: Due to his wounds at the Battle of Uhud, the Prophet performed the noon prayer seated, and the Muslims followed his lead and prayed seated behind him.
story: 'Umar, the freed-man of 'Afra, related that the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained seated for the noon prayer the day of the battle of Uḥud because of his wounds, while the Muslims remained seated behind him.
lessons: Worship is not abandoned in hardship; the Prophet maintained prayer even while wounded, demonstrating that connection to God is the anchor in every state.
Following the example of a leader in difficulty creates unity and solidarity within a community.
Adapting acts of worship to one's capacity is a mercy; God does not burden a soul beyond what it can bear.
practical_advice: When you are exhausted, unwell, or overwhelmed, do not abandon your spiritual practice — adapt it to what you can manage and continue.
In group settings during hardship, follow the lead of those who model calm and continued devotion rather than those who abandon practice under pressure.
Recognize that performing even a reduced act of worship during suffering is a profound achievement deserving self-compassion and gratitude.

title: Quzmān: The Man Who Fought Bravely but Killed Himself
summary: A man named Quzmān fought ferociously at Uhud, killing many enemies, but confessed he fought only for tribal pride and ultimately took his own life from his wounds, confirming the Prophet's earlier statement that he would go to hell.
story: There was with us a man none of us knew who went by the name of Quzmān. Whenever he was mentioned, the Messenger of God (SAAS) would say, 'He will go to hell!' At the battle of Uḥud he fought very fiercely, himself killing some seven or eight of the polytheists. He was a man of great courage. Eventually his wounds disabled him, and he was carried off to the Banū Ẓafar quarter. Some of the Muslims told him, 'You fought superbly, Quzmān, cheer up!' 'Why should I?' he replied. 'It was only for my people's account that I fought; otherwise, I would not have.' When the pain from his wounds became extreme he took an arrow from his quiver and used it to kill himself.
lessons: Outward deeds, no matter how impressive, carry no lasting value if they are not rooted in sincere intention and genuine faith.
The Prophet's knowledge of people's inner states was a gift from God, reminding us that true character cannot be hidden indefinitely.
Intention is the soul of action; doing the right thing for the wrong reason ultimately leaves a person hollow and without true peace.
practical_advice: Regularly examine your motivations: are you acting for recognition, pride, or social pressure — or from genuine values and faith?
If you notice that your efforts feel hollow or meaningless, pause to reconnect with your deeper 'why' before continuing.
Seek help and support when pain — physical or emotional — becomes overwhelming; do not face it alone.

title: Mukhayriq the Jew Who Fought and Died for the Prophet
summary: Mukhayriq, a Jewish man of Banu Tha'laba, declared it his duty to support the Prophet at Uhud despite his community invoking the Sabbath as an excuse, pledged his wealth to Muhammad if he fell, and was killed fighting alongside the Muslims.
story: At Uḥud, one of those killed was Mukhayrīq. He was of Banū Tha'laba b. al-Fiṭyawn. On the day of the battle of Uḥud he proclaimed, 'O Jews, you have learned, I swear by God, that it is your duty to help Muḥammad to victory.' They responded, 'But today is the sabbath.' 'You shall have no sabbath,' he insisted. He then took his sword and equipment saying, 'If I should fall, then my wealth goes to Muḥammad to do with as he wishes.' He then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and fought along with him until he was killed. The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, so I have been told, 'Mukhayrīq is the very best of the Jews.' Al-Suhayli stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) converted Mukhayrīq's property, consisting of seven orchards, into a waqf, a charitable endowment, in Medina.' Muḥammad b. Ka'b al-Quraẓī stated, 'This was Medina's very first waqf.'
lessons: Moral courage means following what you know to be right even when your own community refuses to join you.
A person's generosity with what they have — including their life and their wealth — reflects the sincerity of their convictions.
Truth and nobility of character are not limited by religious or communal boundaries; God recognizes goodness wherever it is found.
practical_advice: When your community or peers resist doing what is right, be willing to act alone rather than wait for collective approval.
Consider designating some of your resources — time, money, skills — for a lasting charitable purpose that outlives you.
Challenge yourself to recognize and affirm goodness and integrity in people from backgrounds different from your own.

title: Amr b. al-Jamuh's Determination to Fight at Uhud Despite Lameness
summary: Amr b. al-Jamuh, a severely lame man, insists on joining the battle of Uhud despite his sons' attempts to restrain him, seeking martyrdom. The Prophet allows him to go, and he is killed at Uhud.
story: Ibn Ishāq continued, "My father related to me, from elders of Banū Salama, who said, 'Amr b. al-Jamūh was a man who was exceedingly lame. He had four sons who fought battles like lions alongside the Messenger of God (SAAS). When it came time for the battle of Uhud, they wanted to restrain 'Amr, saying that God had excused him. So he went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, "My sons want to restrain me from going forth to battle with you. But, by God, I hope to set foot in paradise despite my lameness!" "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, "Regarding yourself, God has indeed excused you; it is not your duty to fight." Then he addressed the sons, saying, "It is your duty not to restrain him; God might well grant him martyrdom." "'And so al-Jamūh did accompany him to battle and he was killed at Uhud. May God be pleased with him.'"
lessons: Physical limitations do not diminish one's spiritual aspiration or worth before God.
True courage lies in striving for what one believes in despite obstacles and the opposition of others.
God honors sincere intention and effort, regardless of worldly capability.
practical_advice: When others discourage you from a meaningful goal, seek guidance rather than giving up — consult trusted mentors or reflect deeply before abandoning your aspiration.
Recognize that your desire to contribute and participate in something greater than yourself is valid, even when circumstances seem to work against you.
Channel feelings of limitation or inadequacy into renewed focus on what you can do, trusting that sincere effort is recognized and valued.

title: Hind's Mutilation of the Fallen Companions and Desecration of Hamza's Body
summary: After the battle of Uhud, Hind bint 'Utba and other women mutilate the bodies of the fallen Muslim Companions, cutting off ears and noses to make jewelry, and Hind cuts open and chews the liver of Hamza.
story: Ibn Ishāq stated, "As Sālih b. Kaysān related to me, Hind, daughter of 'Utba, along with other women, mutilated the bodies of the dead Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS), cutting off their ears and their noses. Hind would make the noses and ears of the men into anklets, necklaces and pendants and then give them over to Wahshi. She also cut open Hamza's liver and chewed it. But she couldn't swallow it and so she spat it out." Mūsā b. 'Uqba related that it was Wahshi who cut open Hamza's liver and then took it to Hind who chewed it but was unable to swallow it. But God knows best.
lessons: Evil deeds, no matter how extreme, do not diminish the honor and status of those who died with faith and integrity.
Hatred and vengeance taken to excess reveal the moral bankruptcy of the one who commits them, not the one who suffers them.
The dignity of believers is preserved with God even when it is violated by enemies in the world.
practical_advice: When you witness or experience cruelty and injustice, allow yourself to grieve without letting that grief turn into consuming hatred.
Seek comfort in the knowledge that ultimate justice belongs to God, and that the honor of those wronged is preserved beyond what any human can destroy.
If you feel overwhelmed by the evil actions of others, return to your values and faith as an anchor rather than retaliating in kind.

title: Hind's Boastful Verses After Uhud and the Response of Hind bint Athatha
summary: After the battle of Uhud, Hind bint 'Utba climbs a rock and recites boastful verses celebrating her revenge, and is answered by Hind bint Athatha with verses defending the honor of the Hashimites.
story: Ibn Ishāq stated, "Hind then climbed upon a rock overlooking the place and, at the top of her voice, spoke the following verses, 'We've now repaid you for the battle of Badr and terrible is the warfare that follows war! I could not stand the loss of 'Utba, of my brother, of his uncle, and of my first-born son. Now I have assuaged myself and fulfilled my vow; you, Wahshi, have relieved my burning breast. My thanks to Wahshi will be due for all my life, until my bones rot in my grave.'" Ibn Ishāq went on, "She was answered by Hind, daughter of Athātha b. 'Abbād b. al-Muṭṭalib, who recited, 'You were dishonoured at Badr and after Badr too, you daughter of a worthless fellow extreme in his disbelief! God brought against you in the early dawn tall, fit Hāshimite warriors. Each man wielding his sharp sword, Hamza, my lion, 'Ali, my falcon. When Shayb and your father sought to deceive me, they put sacrificial blood upon their chests; Your oath was evil in the extreme.'"
lessons: Responding to provocation with dignity and truth is more powerful than descending to the same level of cruelty.
Grief over loss can either purify the soul or corrupt it — the difference lies in how one channels it.
Defending the honor of those you love is a noble impulse, but the manner of defense matters greatly.
practical_advice: When someone attacks your dignity or the honor of those you love, respond with truth and calm rather than escalating into bitterness.
Reflect on whether your grief is drawing you closer to God and good character, or pulling you toward destructive emotions and actions.
Use poetry, journaling, or creative expression as a healthy outlet to process feelings of injustice and loss.

title: Al-Hulays Rebukes Abu Sufyan for Mutilating Hamza's Body
summary: Al-Hulays, commander of the Abyssinian levies, witnesses Abu Sufyan striking the body of Hamza with his spear and publicly rebukes him for mutilating his own cousin's corpse.
story: Ibn Ishāq went on, "Al-Hulays b. Zahba, a brother of Banū al-Hārith b. 'Abd Manāf, who was at that time commander of the Abyssinian levies, passed by Abū Sufyān while he was striking the jawbone of Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muṭṭalib with the tip of his spear and saying, 'Taste this, traitor!' "At this al-Hulays asked, 'Banū Kināna, tell me, can this really be, as you see, the Lord of Quraysh mutilating the flesh of his cousin?' "The reply came, 'Confound you, keep quiet about this; it was done in error.'"
lessons: Speaking out against wrong, even among those in power, is a mark of moral courage.
The embarrassment felt when one's wrongdoing is exposed publicly is a sign of remaining conscience.
Social pressure and tribal loyalty should never silence one's sense of justice and decency.
practical_advice: Find the courage to name injustice when you witness it, even if those around you want to stay silent.
If you have done something you are ashamed of, acknowledge it honestly rather than making excuses or dismissing it as an error.
Surround yourself with people who hold you accountable and speak truth, even when it is uncomfortable to hear.

title: Abu Sufyan's Departure Speech and the Prophet's Response Through Umar
summary: As the Quraysh depart after Uhud, Abu Sufyan shouts boastfully invoking the idol Hubal, and the Prophet instructs Umar to respond affirming God's supremacy and that the Muslim dead are in paradise.
story: Ibn Ishāq stated, "When wishing to depart, Abū Sufyān ascended the mountain and shouted at the top of his voice, 'A splendid achievement! Warfare goes in cycles. This day for that at Badr! Arise, Hubal!' By this he meant, 'Hubal, your religion triumphs.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to 'Umar, 'Get up, 'Umar, and respond to him. Say, "God is far higher and more glorious! There is no equivalence: our dead are in paradise, yours in hell."' "Abū Sufyān then said, 'Come on up to me, 'Umar!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) told 'Umar, 'Go to him and see what he wants.' "'Umar did so and Abū Sufyān told him, 'I adjure you by God, 'Umar, have we killed Muhammad?' 'Umar replied, 'I swear by God, no; he is listening now to your words.' Abū Sufyān commented, 'I consider you more truthful and artless than Ibn Qāmi'a.'" Ibn Ishāq continued, "Then Abū Sufyān called out, 'Some mutilation was done to your men. I swear this neither pleased nor angered me; I neither ordered nor forbad it.' "As Abū Sufyān moved off, he called out, 'We have an appointment with you next year, at Badr!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) told one of his men to reply, 'Say: "Yes there is indeed an appointment we have with you."'"
lessons: Responding to provocation with calm confidence in God is more powerful than emotional reaction.
Acknowledging God's supremacy in moments of apparent defeat is an act of profound faith.
Honesty and integrity, even toward an enemy, commands respect and disarms hostility.
practical_advice: When others try to diminish or humiliate you, respond from your values and your trust in God rather than from wounded pride.
In moments of loss or setback, affirm your faith aloud — this strengthens your own conviction as much as it answers others.
Do not be drawn into future confrontations from a place of anger; accept challenges with composure and preparation.

title: Ali Scouts the Retreating Quraysh Army
summary: After the battle of Uhud, the Prophet sends Ali to follow the departing Quraysh army and determine whether they are heading to Mecca or planning to attack Madinah.
story: Ibn Ishāq went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then despatched 'Ali b. Abī Ṭālib saying, 'Follow after the enemy and see what they do and plan. If they lead their horses and ride their camels, then they will be heading for Mecca. If they ride the horses and lead the camels, they will be heading for Medina. By Him in whose hands is my soul, if they head for Medina, I'll fight them there and annihilate them!' "'Ali said, 'So I followed them to see what they would do; they led their horses and rode their camels and headed for Mecca.'"
lessons: Prudent preparation and gathering information after a trial helps prevent further harm and demonstrates wise leadership.
Trusting those closest to you with critical responsibilities is a sign of good judgment and strong community bonds.
Resolve in the face of continued threat — willingness to face the enemy again if needed — reflects unshaken commitment to protecting what matters.
practical_advice: After a difficult experience, take stock of your situation carefully before assuming the danger has fully passed.
Delegate tasks you trust others to handle well; you cannot and should not carry every burden alone.
Prepare yourself mentally for the possibility that challenges may return, so that you are not caught off guard if they do.

title: The Prophet's Prayer of Praise After the Battle of Uhud
summary: After the Quraysh withdraw following the battle of Uhud, the Prophet leads the Muslims in a profound prayer of praise and supplication, asking God for blessings, security, and guidance.
story: Imām Ahmad stated that Marwān b. Mu'āwiya al-Fazāri related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Wāhid b. Ayman al-Makki, from Ibn Rifā'a al-Zurqi, from his father, who said, "When the polytheists withdrew after the battle of Uhud, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Stand up, so that I may give praise to my Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He.' The Muslims assembled behind him in ranks and he then said, 'O God, to You is due all praise. O God, there is no grasping what You have dispersed, nor dispersing what You grasp. There is no guiding aright those whom You have led astray, nor any leading astray of those You have guided. There is no giving of what You have prevented, nor any prevention of what You have given. There is no drawing close what You have distanced, nor any distancing what You have drawn close. O God, spread forth Your blessings, Your mercy, Your generosity and Your favours upon us. "'O God, I ask of You those enduring blessings that never change nor cease. O God, I ask You for blessings at a time of deprivation, and security at a time of fear. "'O God, I seek refuge with You against the evil of what You have given us, as well as from the evil from which You have saved us. "'O God, have us view faith with favour, and endear it to us well. Have us view disbelief, immorality and disobedience with antipathy. Place us among those who are well-guided. "'O God, take us to Yourself as Muslims and give us life as Muslims. Place us among the virtuous, not the shamed or the demented. "'O God, do battle with those disbelievers who give the lie to Your messengers and who oppose them. Torment and punish them. O God, do battle against those disbelievers who were given the scripture, O God of the Truth!'"
lessons: Turning immediately to God in praise and supplication after hardship is the mark of a soul grounded in faith.
Acknowledging God's absolute sovereignty — that all matters are in His hands — brings peace even after devastating loss.
Asking God to make faith beloved to one's heart is itself a form of spiritual self-care and renewal.
practical_advice: After a painful experience, begin your recovery by returning to prayer or a spiritual practice that reconnects you with your source of meaning.
Memorize or reflect on prayers and supplications that affirm God's power and your dependence on Him; recite them in moments of grief or fear.
Ask God sincerely to make good deeds and faith feel natural and beloved to you, especially when you feel spiritually weakened or distant.

title: The Search for Sa'd b. al-Rabi' Among the Dead
summary: After the battle of Uhud, the Prophet sends a companion to search for Sa'd b. al-Rabi' among the dead. Sa'd is found mortally wounded and delivers a final message of love and warning to the Muslim community before dying.
story: Ibn Ishāq continued, "The Muslims looked for their dead. Muhammad b. 'Abd Allāh b. 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Abī Sa'd al-Māzini, a brother of Banū al-Najjār, related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Who will find out for me what became of Sa'd b. al-Rabī', whether he is alive or dead?' One of them said he would. "He searched and found him wounded and barely alive amidst the dead. The anṣāri told him, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered me to find out whether you are among the living or the dead.' Sa'd replied, 'I'm among the dead; inform the Messenger of God (SAAS) of this: "Sa'd b. al-Rabī' says to you: May God reward you on our account better than He ever rewarded any prophet on account of his nation." And greet your people from me and tell them: "Sa'd b. al-Rabī' says to you, You will find no excuse with God if harm comes to your prophet while you still have one eye to blink!"' "Thereupon he died. I went to the Prophet (SAAS) and told him what had happened to him."
lessons: Even in death, the companions of the Prophet prioritized the welfare and protection of their community over their own suffering.
A person's final words reveal the depth of their values and what they held most dear in life.
The care and concern shown by the Prophet in searching for each companion reflects the profound bonds of a true community.
practical_advice: When facing your own trials, ask yourself what legacy of care and encouragement you would want to leave for those who come after you.
In times of community hardship, take personal responsibility for the well-being of those around you rather than waiting for others to act.
Let the loyalty and love of those who have sacrificed for you motivate you to live up to what they believed you could be.

title: The Prophet Discovers Hamza's Mutilated Body
summary: After the battle of Uhud, the Prophet finds his uncle Hamza's body mutilated — his liver removed, and his ears and nose cut off — and is overcome with grief and anger, vowing to mutilate thirty enemies before being counseled to patience.
story: Ibn Ishāq continued, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) set off, so I have been told, to locate Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muṭṭalib. He found him in the middle of the valley, his liver having been cut from his mutilated body, from which his nose and ears had been removed. "Muhammad b. Ja'far b. al-Zuhayr related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, when he saw the sight, 'If it were not for the sorrow it would cause Safiyya and become a tradition after myself, I would leave him here to find his way into the stomachs of wild beasts and the crops of birds. And if God gives me victory over Quraysh anywhere else, I will have 30 of their men mutilated!' "When the Muslims saw the sadness of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his anger at those who had done this to his uncle, they determined to mutilate their enemy as no Arabs had ever done if God one day gave them victory over them." Ibn Ishāq went on, "Burayda b. Sufyān b. Farwa al-Aslamī related to me, from Muḥammad b. Ka'b, and another reliable source related to me from Ibn 'Abbās, that in that regard God revealed the verse, 'If you inflict punishment then do so in the like manner that you were punished; but if you are patient, it is indeed better for those who are patient' (sūrat al-Naḥl; XVI, v.126). "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) forgave, was patient and forbad mutilation."
lessons: Even the Prophet, upon seeing the worst of cruelty toward those he loved most, was guided to choose forgiveness and patience over revenge.
Grief and anger are natural human responses to injustice — what matters is the choice made in the wake of those emotions.
Forbidding cruelty even toward enemies is a mark of profound moral integrity and divine guidance.
practical_advice: When grief and anger arise together after a loss or injustice, give yourself permission to feel them fully, but pause before acting on them.
Seek guidance from your faith or trusted counsel before making decisions in moments of intense emotional pain — those decisions will define your character.
Practice forbearance not as weakness but as a conscious, courageous choice that protects your own soul from being consumed by what harmed you.

title: The Prophet's Grief Over Hamza and Gabriel's Testimony of His Honor
summary: The Prophet stands over Hamza's mutilated body, declaring he has never been more grieved, and then relays Gabriel's message that Hamza's name is written in the seven heavens as 'the lion of God and lion of His messenger.'
story: Ibn Hishām stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) stood over Hamza, he said, 'I shall never be afflicted as you have been; I have never experienced anything that has angered me more than this!' He then said, 'Gabriel came to me and told me that Hamza's name is written in the seven heavens in the words, "Hamza, the son of 'Abd al-Muṭṭalib — lion of God and lion of His messenger."'"
lessons: Those who suffer the greatest indignities in God's cause are often honored the most profoundly in the sight of God.
The Prophet's grief shows that deep emotional pain is not incompatible with strong faith — it is a mark of his humanity.
Divine recognition of the righteous is not diminished by what is done to them on earth; their honor before God remains intact and elevated.
practical_advice: When someone you love has been wronged or humiliated, find comfort in the belief that God sees their dignity and honor even when the world does not.
Allow grief to be a form of love expressed — do not rush to suppress it, but carry it with faith that loss is not the final word.
Reflect on the names and stories of those who came before you in faith; their examples can sustain and inspire you through your own hardship.

title: The Prophet's Grief Over Hamza and the Revelation of Hamza's Heavenly Status
summary: Upon standing over the mutilated body of his uncle Hamza, the Prophet expressed profound grief and anger, after which Gabriel revealed that Hamza's name is recorded in the seven heavens as 'lion of God and lion of His messenger.'
story: Ibn Hishsm stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) stood over Hamza, he said, 'I shall never he afflicted as you have been; I have never experienced anything that has angered me more than this!' He then said, 'Gabriel came to me and told me that Hamza's name is written in the seven heavens in the words, "Hamza, the son of 'Abd al-Mucplib - lion of God and lion of His messenger."'" Ihn H i s b m also said, "Both Hamza and Abii Salama b. 'Abd al-Asad were foster-brothers of the Messenger of God (SAAS). All three were suckled by Thuwayba, the freed-woman of Abn Lahab."
lessons: Even the Prophet experienced profound grief and anger at injustice, showing that strong emotions in the face of loss are human and natural.
Divine recognition of sacrifice comes regardless of how the world treats the righteous — Hamza's honor was recorded in the highest heavens.
Grief shared openly, as the Prophet demonstrated, can be a source of healing rather than weakness.
practical_advice: When overwhelmed by grief or anger at a loss, allow yourself to feel those emotions fully rather than suppressing them, as even the Prophet expressed his sorrow openly.
Remind yourself that those who suffer unjustly are seen and honored by God, even when the world fails to recognize their sacrifice.
Seek comfort in the knowledge that the bonds of love — like the foster-brotherhood of Hamza and the Prophet — are spiritually meaningful and lasting.

title: The Prayers Said Over Hamza and the Martyrs of Uhud
summary: The Prophet ordered Hamza to be covered and prayed over him repeatedly, then prayed jointly over each of the other martyrs alongside Hamza, demonstrating profound love and honor for the fallen.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that he was told by a reliable source, from Miqsam, that Ibn 'Abba said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered that Hamza be covered with a cloak and he then prayed over him. He spoke the words, Allahu Akbar, 'God is most Great', seven times. The other dead were then placed next to Hamza and he prayed over them and him together, so that a total of seventy-two prayers were spoken over hi." This hadith is gharib, "unilateral", and its line of transmission is weak. Al-Suhayli stated, "None of the scholars at the major cenues of learning support this tradition." I m m Ahmad stated that 'Affm related to h i , quoting Hammad, quoting 'A@ b. al-Sz'ib, from al-Sha'bi, from Ibn Mas'iid, who said, "At Uhud the women were following behind the Muslim fighters finishing off the polytheists who were wounded. If I had sworn an oath that day that I would have hoped to fulfil, it would have been that none of us desired this world. But this was before God sent down the verse, 'Some of you desired this world, while others of you desired the hereafter; then He turned you away from them, so that he might test you' (sarat 21 'ImrZn; 111, v. 15 1)."
lessons: Honoring those who have sacrificed everything is a spiritual duty and a source of communal healing.
The Prophet's repeated prayers over Hamza reflect how deeply personal loss can be, even for the greatest of leaders.
Sacred rituals of farewell — prayer, remembrance, honoring the dead — help the living process grief and maintain hope.
practical_advice: When grieving a loss, establish a meaningful ritual of remembrance — whether prayer, reflection, or charitable action — to honor those who have passed.
Do not rush through grief; the Prophet's repeated prayers show that honoring loss takes time and deliberate attention.
Use moments of communal mourning to reconnect with your deeper values and what truly matters in life, beyond worldly concerns.

title: Ibn Mas'ud's Account: The Test of Worldly Desire at Uhud
summary: Ibn Mas'ud recalls how at Uhud many companions were tested by desire for worldly gain, leading to disobedience of orders, and how God revealed a verse acknowledging this division between those who sought the world and those who sought the hereafter.
story: I m m Ahmad stated that 'Affm related to h i , quoting Hammad, quoting 'A@ b. al-Sz'ib, from al-Sha'bi, from Ibn Mas'iid, who said, "At Uhud the women were following behind the Muslim fighters finishing off the polytheists who were wounded. If I had sworn an oath that day that I would have hoped to fulfil, it would have been that none of us desired this world. But this was before God sent down the verse, 'Some of you desired this world, while others of you desired the hereafter; then He turned you away from them, so that he might test you' (sarat 21 'ImrZn; 111, v. 15 1). "When the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) disobeyed the orders they had received, he was left with only nine men about h i . Seven of these were an$&-, two were of Quraysh; with himself added, they totalled ten men. When the enemy set upon him, he said, 'May God have mercy upon any man who repels them from us!' He continued saying this until all seven had been killed. Then he said to his two Companions, 'Our comrades did not treat us fairly!'"
lessons: Disobedience of clear commands, even from good people, can lead to devastating consequences — a reminder to prioritize obedience to God and righteous leadership over personal gain.
God tests people through the allure of the world; recognizing this test is the first step to passing it.
Even in failure, the Prophet's response was to pray for mercy rather than curse those who had abandoned him — modeling gracious leadership under pressure.
practical_advice: When you face temptation to prioritize short-term gain over your deeper commitments, pause and ask yourself which choice you will be proud of when looking back.
If you have made a mistake by prioritizing worldly things over your principles, acknowledge it honestly — God's mercy is available to those who return to Him with sincerity.
In positions of leadership or trust, reinforce clear expectations before challenges arise, as the Prophet did with the archers at Uhud.

title: The Exchange Between the Prophet and Abu Sufyan After Uhud
summary: After the Battle of Uhud, Abu Sufyan taunted the Muslims by invoking pagan idols, but the Prophet instructed his companions to respond with affirmations of God's greatness, declaring that the Muslim dead are honored while the pagan dead are in hell.
story: "Then Abii Sufym came and said, 'Arise, Hubal!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) told his men to respond, 'God is far higher and more glorious!' "To this Abn Sufym replied, 'We have (the goddess) al-'Uzzz; yon have no 'zzzd, "consolation"!' "Ahn Sufym then called out, 'A battle for that of Badr. One for us, one against us. One gave us distress, one pleasure. Tit for tat. Man for man.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, LThere'sno equivalence. Our dead live on and are rewarded; your dead are in hell being tortured.' "Then Aba Sufyan said, 'There has been some mutilation engaged in by our people, though not the result of any decision by us. I neither ordered nor forbad it, liked nor disliked it. It neither annoyed nor pleased me.' "When they searched, they found Hamza. His liver had been cut out and chewed by =nd, but she had been unable to swallow it. The Messenger of God (SAAS), asked, 'Did she eat any of it?' 'No,' they told him. He commented, 'God would never have allowed any part of Hamza to enter hell-fire!'"
lessons: In the face of taunting and humiliation, respond with dignity and truth rather than matching the aggressor's level.
The Prophet's calm declaration that the Muslim dead 'live on and are rewarded' shows how faith in the afterlife transforms the meaning of worldly defeat.
God's protection extends even over the bodies of the righteous — the Prophet's confidence that Hamza's remains would not be desecrated spiritually reflects a deep trust in divine honor.
practical_advice: When someone mocks your beliefs or your losses, do not descend to their level; instead respond from a place of deep conviction and composure, as the Prophet modeled.
In moments of apparent defeat or humiliation, anchor yourself in the bigger picture of what you believe — temporary setbacks do not define ultimate outcomes.

Practice responding to provocation with affirmations of your values rather than reactive anger, as the Prophet instructed his companions to counter Abu Sufyan's taunts with 'God is far higher and more glorious.'

title: The Burial of the Martyrs of Uhud and the Prophet's Testimony for Them
summary: The Prophet gave careful instructions for the burial of the martyrs of Uhud, prioritizing those with greater knowledge of the Quran, burying companions together who were close friends, and leaving the blood and wounds untouched as a mark of honor.
story: Al-Bukhai stated, "Qutayba related to us, quoting al-Layth, from Ibn Shibab, from 'Abd a l - w m z n b. Ka'b b. MQik, (who said) that Jzbir b. 'Abd Allah recounted to him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would place one piece of clothing over each two of the men killed at Uhud and then would ask, 'Which of these had a better knowledge of the Qur'an?' He would then place the one indicated in the grave first, saying, 'I will testify for these at Judgement Day.' He ordered that they be buried in their blood; he did not pray over them, nor were they washed." Al-Bukhari alone gave this; Muslim did not. Other scholars relate it from a hadith of al-Layth b. SaCd. A h a d stated, "Muhammad -meaning Ihn Jacfar - related to us, quoting Ibn Jacfar, quoting Shu'ba, having heard 'Abd Rabbihi relate from d-Zuhri, from Ibn Jsbir, from Jiibir b. 'Abd Allah, that the Prophet (SAAS) said about those killed at Uhud, 'Each wound, each drop of blood will exude musk at Judgement Day.' And that he did not pray over them." It is well established that he did pray for them some years thereafter, shortly before his death. It is also given in, as above, al-Bukhai's sahib collection that the Messenger of God (SAAS) used to bury two or three men in one grave, and even in one shroud. He permitted this because of the wounds the Muslims had that would have made it hard for them to dig a grave for each individual. And he would position first in the grave whichever dead Muslim had had a greater knowledge of the Qur'm. He would join in the same grave two men who had been close friends, as he did 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr b. Haram, Jabir's father, along with 'Amr b. al-Jumnh. This was because they were friends. They were not washed, the wounds and blood being left untouched. Ibn Ishaq also related from al-Zuhri, from 'Abd Allah b. Tha'laba b. SU+, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said as he was leaving the dead at Uhud, "I shall bear witness for these men; every man wounded in God's cause will be raised by God on Judgement Day, his wounds still bleeding. The colour shall be that of blood, the fragrance that of musk."
lessons: Knowledge of the Quran is a mark of distinction that carries weight even beyond this life, as the Prophet honored those with greater Quranic knowledge first.
Friendship and close bonds between believers are recognized and honored spiritually — the Prophet buried close friends together.
The marks of struggle in God's cause are not shameful wounds to be hidden but are transformed into signs of honor in the sight of God.
practical_advice: Invest time in learning and memorizing the Quran, knowing that such knowledge carries profound spiritual weight and honor.
Cherish your close spiritual friendships — they are bonds recognized by God and worth nurturing with care.
When you carry visible or invisible scars from hardship endured for what is right, remember that those marks are not signs of defeat but of dignity in God's sight.

title: The Prophet's Farewell Prayer for the Martyrs of Uhud Eight Years Later
summary: Eight years after Uhud, the Prophet prayed over the martyrs as if bidding farewell to the living and the dead, then ascended the pulpit to warn his community about the danger of competing over worldly possessions.
story: It is well established that he did pray for them some years thereafter, shortly before his death. As d-Bukhzri stated, "Muhammad b. 'Abd al-R*im related to us, quoting Zakariyys' b. CAdi, and Ihn al-Mubeak recounted to us, from Haywa, from Yazid b. Abii Habib, from Abii d-Khayr, from Wqba b. CAmir, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) delivered a prayer for those killed at W u d eight years thereafter, just as though he were saying farewell to the living or the dead. He then mounted the mrnbar and said, "I shall stand before you, having preceded you, and will be a witness for you. Your appointment is at the al-hand, 'the celestial pool', and I look at it now from this position of mine. I do not fear for you that you will (later) become polytheists. What I do fear for you is the influence of this world, that you will compete with one another over it."'" Wqba concluded, "And that was the last time I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: The Prophet's greatest fear for his community was not outright disbelief but the slow corruption of competing over worldly possessions — a warning deeply relevant to every era.
Returning to remember those who have sacrificed before us is a spiritually vital practice that keeps our hearts connected to what truly matters.
The Prophet's promise to be a witness for his community at the celestial pool is a source of comfort and motivation to live righteously.
practical_advice: Periodically revisit the memories and lessons of those who have sacrificed for your community or family — their stories are anchors against being consumed by worldly competition.
Examine your relationship with material possessions and competition: ask honestly whether the pursuit of worldly things is pulling you away from your deeper values.
Hold onto the Prophet's assurance that he will be a witness for his community — let this motivate you to act in ways you would be glad for him to witness.

title: Safiyya's Resolve Upon Hearing of Hamza's Mutilation
summary: When Safiyya, the Prophet's aunt and Hamza's sister, came forward to see her brother's mutilated body, she told the Prophet's messenger that she would remain composed, and when she arrived she prayed over Hamza and sought forgiveness for him.
story: Ihn Ishaq stated, "Safiyya, daughter of 'Abd al-Mumlib had come forward to see h i (Hamza), who was her full brother. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told , and meet her and take her back so that she her son al-Zubayr b. a l - c A w w ~'Go does not see her brother's state.' Al-Zuhayr told her, 'Mother, the Messenger of God (SAAS) orders you to go back.' She replied, 'Why? I have been told how my brother has been mutilated; that was in God's cause. And that fact has comforted us. I will keep myself under control and remain calm, if God wills it.' "When al-Zubayr went back to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and reported this, he said, 'Let her through.' When she came, she looked at him (Hamza), said a prayer over him, asked forgiveness for him and spoke the words, 'To God we belong, and to Him do we return.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then gave orders that he be buried, and this was done. With him was buried his cousin 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh, whose mother was Umayma, daughter of 'Abd al-MutMib. He too had been mutilated, though his liver had not been cut out. May God be pleased with them both."
lessons: True faith does not eliminate grief but transforms it — Safiyya's composure came not from indifference but from sincere conviction that her brother's sacrifice was honored by God.
The words 'To God we belong, and to Him do we return' are not merely a formula but a profound act of surrendering grief to God.
Protecting loved ones from traumatic sights is an act of compassion, but ultimately respecting their choice to face reality with faith is also honoring their strength.
practical_advice: When facing devastating news, practice saying 'To God we belong, and to Him do we return' not as a rote phrase but as a genuine act of releasing your grief to God.
When grief threatens to overwhelm you, find one aspect of the situation that reflects a higher meaning or purpose — as Safiyya found comfort in knowing Hamza died in God's cause.
Respect the capacity of those around you to face hard truths with faith; do not assume others need to be shielded from reality when their trust in God may be their greatest strength.

title: Abd Allah ibn Jahsh's Answered Prayer and the Miraculous Sword
summary: Abd Allah ibn Jahsh prayed to be martyred and mutilated in God's cause, and his prayer was answered at Uhud; additionally, when his sword broke, the Prophet gave him a palm branch that miraculously became a sword.
story: Al-Suhayli stated, "He was later known as al-mujadda: 'he who was mutilated in God's cause'. And Sacd related that he and 'Abd All& b. Jahsh had said prayers, both of which had been answered.
