summary: Hassan b. Thabit responds sharply to Ibn al-Ziba'ra's gloating over Uhud, reminding him of the Muslims' deeper victory at Badr, their piety, and the divine support they received through the angel Gabriel.
story: Ibn Ishsq continued, "Hasszn b. Thsbit, God be pleased with him, responded as follows, 'Ihn al-Zihacn got carried away by a battle in which we had the advantage; if only he had been fair! We damaged you and you us; warfare sometimes goes in cycles like that. We put our swords to your shoulders and there we drank your blood time and again. We made your backsides flow, as happens to old camels eating 'a$aP6for medicine! You took to your heels in flight in the defile, following along like sheep. Then we attacked you fiercely, forcing you down to the foot of the mountain; Our groups of warriors were like ashhq in the desert wastes, terrifying whomever came across them. The defile pressed in upon us and we crossed through it and filled its heights and low places alike, With men whose equal you are not and who were aided by Gabriel who came down. It was our piety that won the day at Badr, our obedience to God and our belief in the prophets. We killed every one of their chieftains and all their nobles in flowing robes. At the battle of Badr we left shame upon Quraysh and exemplary tales to be told. The Messenger of God (SAAS) in truth was witness to the battle of Badr, while the short, corpulent men Of Quraysh assembled there were like unattended camels gathering in a pasture. It is we, not people like you born to your mothers' arses, who face up to the fray when warfare comes!'"
lessons: Piety and sincere obedience to God are the true sources of strength — not numbers, wealth, or worldly power alone.
Responding to falsehood with clarity and confidence is an act of defending the truth, not mere pride.
Divine assistance, symbolized here by the descent of Gabriel, reminds believers that they are never alone in their struggles when they are aligned with God's path.
practical_advice: When someone belittles your efforts or misrepresents your situation, respond with dignity and grounded confidence, focusing on truth rather than stooping to personal attacks.
Recall that your greatest resource in any difficulty is your relationship with God. Piety — consistent prayer, honesty, and gratitude — builds the inner strength that no external force can take away.
Rather than dwelling on a painful episode, remind yourself of the larger narrative: past victories, answered prayers, and moments of divine support in your life.

title: Ka'b b. Malik's Elegy for Hamza and the Muslim Martyrs of Uhud
summary: Ka'b b. Malik composes a moving elegy honouring Hamza and the Muslim martyrs of Uhud, expressing deep grief, longing, and heartfelt remembrance for those who gave their lives for Islam.
story: Ibn Ishnq stated, "Ka'b recited the following verses honouring Hamza and those Muslims, may God be pleased with them all, killed with him at Uhud, 'You wept. And was it another who made you do so, when it was you who cried when remembering? Your memory was of a people about whom stories have reached me in this tortuous time. To think of them your heart throbs in longing and tear-filled sadness. But those'"
lessons: Grief over the loss of righteous people is a deeply human and honourable emotion — weeping for those who sacrificed themselves for God is itself a form of love and gratitude.
Preserving the memory of those who gave everything for a noble cause keeps their legacy alive and inspires future generations.
Loss and longing, when channelled into poetry, prayer, or remembrance, can become a source of healing rather than despair.
practical_advice: Allow yourself to grieve fully when you lose someone dear. Suppressing grief does not heal it — expressing it in healthy ways, such as through words, prayer, or remembrance, is part of the healing process.
Keep alive the memory of those who inspired you or sacrificed for you. Speak of them, pray for them, and let their example motivate your own commitment to what is good.
When sadness and longing feel overwhelming, turn them into acts of devotion — make du'a for those you have lost, give charity in their name, or simply sit with God in quiet reflection.

title: Ka'b's Verses Honouring Hamza and the Martyrs of Uhud
summary: The poet Ka'b recites verses honouring Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib and the Muslim martyrs fallen at the battle of Uhud, describing their courage under the Prophet's flag and their reward in paradise.
story: Ibn Ishnq stated, "Ka'b recited the following verses honouring Hamza and those Muslims, may God be pleased with them all, killed with him at Uhud, 'You wept. And was it another who made you do so, when it was you who cried when remembering? Your memory was of a people about whom stories have reached me in this tortuous time. To think of them your heart throbs in longing and tear-filled sadness. But those they killed are in pleasant gardens, honoured therein both when they enter or leave For what they endured beneath the flag, the flag of the prophet at Dhii al-Aduj That day when all of Aws and Khazraj gave answer with their swords. Along with Ahmad's men following the truth, its path so well illuminated. They kept on striking the warriors as they traversed through the swirling dust. So it was until the King summoned them to a garden whose entrance was shielded by much foliage. All of them died heroically, in God's faith, and received no punishment, Like Hamza when he held true, wielding a sharp sword, cutting deep. The slave of Banii Nawfal confronted him, snorting like a black camel, And ran him through with a spear, fast as a flame blazing up in a fire. Nukan fulfilled his promise, and Hanzalla, the good, did not turn away From the truth until his spirit passed on to a place splendid with jewels. Those were men, not those of yours lying in the furthest depths of hell.'"
lessons: Those who sacrifice for a noble cause are remembered with honour across generations, showing that meaningful deeds outlast physical life.
Grief and poetry can be acts of reverence — expressing sorrow for the righteous is itself a form of acknowledging the value of what was lost.
True courage is described not as the absence of danger but as holding firm to one's convictions in the face of it, as Hamza and Hanzalla did.
practical_advice: When you lose someone dear, allow yourself to grieve fully — suppressing sorrow can delay healing. Channel that grief into remembrance of their good qualities.
In moments of loss, remind yourself that those who lived and died with integrity are in a better place; this perspective can ease the weight of mourning.
Write, recite, or speak about those you have lost as a way of processing grief — giving expression to sorrow, as Ka'b did, is a healthy and honoured tradition.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit's Elegy for Hamza and the Martyrs of Uhud
summary: The poet Hassan ibn Thabit composes a lengthy elegy lamenting the death of Hamza and the noble warriors slain at Uhud, describing the community's grief and Hamza's role as a protector of orphans, widows, and the Prophet.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Hassan b. Thabit spoke the following verses in mourning for Hamza and the Muslims struck down at the battle of Uhud. It is in the same rhyme as the poem of Umayya b. Abi al-Salt eulogizing the polytheists killed at the battle of Badr." Ibn Hisham stated, "There are some scholars expert in the poetry who deny that this poem should be attributed to Hassan. But God knows best." (Hassan b. Thabit recited), "0 Mayy, arise and lament loudly as the mourning women do, Like hags bearing heavy burdens, complaining, overladen, Wailing, and scratching at the faces of other women, Their flowing tears reminding one of pillars stained with the blood of sacrifice, Tearing at their hair and letting its ends appear, Looking like the tails of restive horses being tossed in the morning, Looking like scraps of meat cut up and left to be blown dry in the wind. They cry in sadness, mourning women overwhelmed by tragedy, Their hearts wounded, emitting pus from beneath scabs, Now that fate has struck down those we relied upon when we feared The warriors at Uhud injured, and destroyed by fate, Our knights and protectors when warriors are sent forth. 0 Hamza, I will not, I swear, ever forget you as long as time goes on. You were the refuge of orphans, strangers and modest-eyed widows, Protecting against that fate that brings successive, searing wars. 0 knight and champion, 0 Hamza, you were our gallant defender Protecting us from terrible, calamitous blows of hate. I thought of you as the lion of the Prophet, who is himself our champion, our protector, He who has always been considered when our noble and generous lords are counted. Towering above all nobles in fame, a man of generosity and pre-eminence, Not an irresponsible or fearful or weak man who complains at bearing burdens. A liberal man who would never withhold any generosity or kindness from his neighbours. Fine young men have perished, men of great esteem, Men who would provide food in winter when the cattle were dispersed. Camel meat with chunks and slices of fat on top of it, Defending their neighbours for so long as those hating them attack. I grieve for the young men of whom we are deprived, men like shining lamps, Proud men, lords, nobles, men of generosity and breeding, Men who purchased praise with their wealth, for praise brings dividends, Men who jumped to their bridles if there cried out for them Someone beset by the misfortunes of an evil time, His mounts moving steadily along on the flat land, Competing with one another, he being in one group whose chests poured with sweat, Moving on until success came, but not from the luck of a winning arrow of chance. 0 Hamza, you have rendered me lonely, like a branch severed by someone pruning it. I express my pain to you, now that earth and stones are piled above you, And a flagstone placed on top of you by the gravedigger, completing his work, On an open plain, covering the grave with earth and smoothing it well. Our consolation is for us to say, though our speaking it is very hard, that whoever has been spared life's misfortunes Should come to us for his eyes to weep for our noble dead Who acted as they spoke, men of generosity and good deeds, Men whose hands were always stretched forth in liberality." Ibn Hisham commented, "Most scholars of poetry refute attribution of this poem to Hassan."
lessons: The loss of generous, noble people leaves a community deeply wounded — recognising the value of such individuals while they are alive is a lesson for every generation.
Communal mourning is a natural and important process; coming together to grieve prevents isolation and affirms shared love for those who have passed.
Hamza's legacy as a protector of orphans and widows reminds us that true greatness lies in caring for the most vulnerable, not merely in martial prowess.
practical_advice: When grief feels isolating, seek out others who share your loss — communal mourning, as depicted in this elegy, is a deeply human and healthy response to tragedy.
Allow yourself to name specifically what you have lost in a person — their generosity, their protection, their presence — as this makes grief more concrete and easier to process over time.
Find ways to honour the memory of those you have lost by embodying the qualities you admired in them, whether generosity, courage, or care for others.

title: Ka'b ibn Malik's Verses on Hamza, Badr, and the Grief of Loss
summary: Ka'b ibn Malik recites verses processing grief over Hamza's death at Uhud, connecting it to the earlier Muslim victory at Badr and rebuking Hind bint Utba for her exultation over Hamza's killing.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "Ka'b b. Malik spoke the following verses eulogizing Hamza and his fellow warriors, 'Struck by anxiety, you lay sleepless, worried that joyful youth had been stripped from you, While a Damri girl invited your heart to loving; but your passion is of Ghawr, and your wakefulness of Najd. Abandon excessive pursuit of passion; you were always blamed for acting that way. It is now time for you to refrain, obediently, and to pay heed when counsel warns. You were devastated by the loss of Hamza, your stomach in confusion. If Mt. Hira' itself suffered such a shock its peaks would collapse. A chief he was, formed from Hashim's stock wherein lie prophethood, nobility and leadership, Leaders who would slaughter big-humped camels when the wind almost froze the water, Men who would leave their brave foes prostrate on the battlefield, their lances broken in two. This was a man who strutted in his armour as though he were a huge, brown, big-pawed lion. He was the uncle and the confidant of the Prophet, Muhammad, and he went most gloriously to his death. He went with distinction to his fate, one of a family who aided the Prophet, and of whom some sought martyrdom. Hind was told the good tidings of this, to alleviate the burning pain inside her, When we met her folk that morning on the sandhill, that day her happiness fled. (And we talked) of the well at Badr, when Gabriel and Muhammad, beneath our flag, repelled the enemy, So that the Prophet saw their hosts as two parts whom we could either kill or drive away at will, Left prostrate at the site, they were 70 in number, Utba and al-Aswad among them. And Ibn al-Mughira too whom we struck above his neck-artery, making it flow and foam. And it was a sharp sword in the hands of the believers that set Umayya al-Jumahi straight. The polytheist rabble came to you, chased by the cavalry, like ostriches in retreat. What a difference there is in those for ever in hell and those who dwell in paradise in eternity!'"
lessons: Grief over a great loss can shake a person to their very foundation — even the poet imagines that a mountain would crumble under such sorrow, validating the depth of human pain.
Recalling past victories and divine assistance in times of grief helps restore perspective and faith, reminding the bereaved that God's support has never wavered.
Anger at those who rejoice in the suffering of the righteous is a natural human emotion; acknowledging it honestly is part of healthy emotional processing.
practical_advice: When grief overwhelms you, try to recall moments when you or your community were supported through difficulty — this historical grounding can restore hope.
If you feel anger alongside grief, find a constructive outlet such as writing, speaking to a trusted friend, or physical activity rather than letting it fester inwardly.
Recognise that some losses are so great they shake the whole self — be gentle with yourself during such times and do not expect recovery to be quick or linear.

title: Abd Allah ibn Rawaha's Mourning Verses for Hamza
summary: Abd Allah ibn Rawaha recites verses mourning Hamza's death at Uhud, lamenting the wound his loss inflicted on all Muslims including the Prophet, and recalling the earlier battle of Badr to console the grieving community.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on to say that Abd Allah b. Rawaha spoke the following verses in mourning for Hamza and his fellow warriors killed at the battle of Uhud. Ibn Hisham, however, stated that Abu Zayd recited them to him as having been composed by Ka'b b. Malik. But God knows best. "My eyes wept, and they had a right to do so; but weeping and wailing will do no good. For the lion of God, that day they asked, 'Could that man slain be Hamza!' Through his death all the Muslims were injured there, the Messenger as well. 0 Hamza, Ya'la's father, the very foundations were destroyed; you were the epitome of glory, piety and kindness. May God's peace be upon you in paradise, in pleasure that will never end! 0 Hashim, of all men the best, be steadfast; and all your deeds were good and generous. The Messenger of God is noble and patient; it is at God's command he enunciates when he speaks. Who will inform Lu'ayy for me, for after today, the world will be utterly changed. And before this day they have known and tasted deeds of ours by which the thirst for vengeance was satisfied. You have all forgotten our blows at the pool of Badr, that day when sudden death struck you. That day when Abu Jahl fell prostrate, the vultures circled and hovered above him, And Utba and his son also fell, and Shayba was bitten by the burnished sword. There we left Umayya stretched out, a slim spear between his ribs. Ask the heads of Banu Rabi'a; our swords have notches because of them. 0 Hind, weep and tire not of it; for you are passionate, shedding tears as though bereaved of a son. And You, Hind, show no joy at Hamza's death; your exultation is despicable."
lessons: Acknowledging that tears have a right to fall — that grief is legitimate — is itself an act of emotional honesty and self-compassion.
The Prophet's patience in the face of the loss of his beloved uncle models for believers that steadfastness and grief can coexist without one cancelling the other.
Reminding oneself and others of past moments of divine support during times of loss is not denial of grief but a deepening of trust in God's ongoing care.
practical_advice: Give yourself permission to cry — as the poet says, eyes have a right to weep. Suppressing tears in an effort to appear strong can prolong suffering.
When you feel that a loss has changed your world irrevocably, seek out community — the poet calls out to his people, showing that grief is not meant to be carried alone.
In the depths of grief, try to recall specific moments of goodness and strength from the past — not to dismiss the pain, but to remind yourself that hardship has been survived before.

title: Safiyya bint Abd al-Muttalib Mourns Her Brother Hamza
summary: Safiyya, sister of Hamza and aunt of the Prophet, recites verses mourning her brother's martyrdom at Uhud, expressing her wish she had died in his place and her trust that he now enjoys the reward of paradise.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "Safiyya daughter of 'Abd al-Muttalib spoke the following verses, mourning her brother Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib. She was the mother of al-Zubayr and was the aunt of the Prophet (SAAS); may God be pleased with them all. 'Are you, sisters of mine, asking, in fear, those present at Uhud, the ignorant and knowledgeable alike? The knowledgeable replied, "Hamza is dead! The adviser, the very best adviser of the Messenger of God! The God of truth and the throne has summoned him to paradise, where he will live in joy." That was what we had hoped for; we anticipate for Hamza the best of fortunes on assembly day. By God, I'll not forget you so long as the winds blow; I will mourn and be sad whether I am at home or away. For God's lion who was a defender who protected Islam from all who disbelieved. How I wish my limbs and bones had been there then, for the hyenas and vultures to attack me! I say, my family having made louder their lament, "May God reward well this brother, this champion!"'"
lessons: Safiyya's wish to have died in her brother's place reveals the depth of sibling love — grief for a sibling is one of the most profound forms of human loss and deserves its own recognition.
Her trust that Hamza was summoned to paradise shows how faith can coexist with overwhelming personal sorrow, providing a horizon of hope beyond the immediate pain.
A woman of her stature — aunt of the Prophet, mother of a great companion — mourning openly teaches that expressing grief is not weakness but a mark of deep humanity.
practical_advice: If you are grieving a sibling or close family member, know that the depth of your pain is proportionate to the depth of your love — do not minimise it.
When grief feels endless, hold onto the belief that those who lived righteously are in a place of peace — this is not wishful thinking but a sincere anchor of faith.
Share your grief with family members rather than isolating yourself — Safiyya mourned among her sisters and family, finding strength in collective sorrow rather than solitude.

title: Nu'man, Wife of Shammas ibn Uthman, Mourns Her Husband
summary: Nu'man, wife of the martyr Shammas ibn Uthman, recites verses lamenting the death of her husband, a noble standard-bearer and horseman killed at Uhud, crying out at the loss of his generosity.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Nu'man, wife of Shammas b. Uthman, spoke the following verses in mourning for her husband; but God, to whom all praise and credit are due, knows best. 'Eyes, weep copiously and without stop for a noble, brave warrior, A man of firm conviction and fine disposition, a standard-bearer and a horseman. When his death was announced, I cried in pain, "Gone is the man of generosity! Gone"'"
lessons: The grief of a wife over her husband illustrates how martyrdom ripples outward, touching not just the community but the most intimate bonds of family and love.
Describing the deceased by their character — firm conviction, fine disposition, generosity — is a way of preserving their legacy and finding meaning in the loss.
Even a few words of mourning, honestly expressed, carry the full weight of grief — depth of feeling does not require length of expression.
practical_advice: When you lose a spouse or partner, allow yourself to grieve the specific qualities you loved in them — naming what you have lost is an important step in processing it.
Do not feel you must articulate your grief perfectly or at length — sometimes a few honest words carry more healing than a long silence.
Reach out to others who knew your loved one — sharing memories of their character and goodness can be a source of comfort and a way of keeping their legacy alive.

title: Elegies and Poems After the Battle of Uhud
summary: Companions and their wives composed mournful elegies after the Battle of Uhud, lamenting fallen warriors including Shammas ibn Uthman and Hamza, while poets like Hassan ibn Thabit responded to the Quraysh's battle cries.
story: bones had been there then, for the hyenas and vultures to attack me! I say, my family having made louder their lament, "May God reward well this brother, this champion!"' Ibn Ishaq stated, "Nucm%n, wife of Shammas b. WthmZn, spoke the following verses in mourning for her husband; but God, to whom all praise and credit are due, knows best. 'Eyes, weep copiously and without stop for a noble, brave warrior, A man of firm conviction and fine disposition, a standard-bearer and a horseman. When his death was announced, I cried in pain, "Gone is the man of generosity! Gone is he who fed and clothed!" And I said, when his usual places were empty of him, "May God not distance from us our nearness to ShammB!"' "Her brother al-Hakam b. SaFd b. Yarbiic responded as follows in consoling her, 'Guard your modesty, in seclusion and dignity; Shammi% was just one of our men. Do not kiU yourself because his death has come, in obedience of God, and on a day of violence and terror. Now Jjanua was God's lion; so bear up, for he tasted from S h m ' s cup that day.' "Hind, daughter of Wtba, the wife of Aba Sufyzn, recited, upon their return from m u d , 'I have returned home with my mind in much confusion, some of those I was after having escaped me, Those of Quraysh at Badr, and others, including some of Bano H%shimand of the people of Yathrih. However, I did gain one thing, even if it was not all as I had hoped in my journey.'" Ibn Ishnq gives many more such poems. We have left out the others because of our concern not to be long-winded and boring; those poems we have given suffice. And to God be all praise. Al-Umawi gave in his military chronicles even more poems than Ibn Ishxq; this was his usual practice, though even more evident here. Among the verses quoted from Hassnn b. Thi%bitwere the following delivered about the battle of m u d , "It was the devil they obeyed, when he let them down and their disgrace and failure was fully evident, When they shouted in unison with Abii Sufya, 'Rise up, Hubal!' We all answered back, 'Our Lord, the All-Merciful, is greater and more splendid!' Be sure, you used this cry once at the 'pool of death' (at Badr), death being a place to drink. You should know that when thoughts of death must be slaked, we are cooking puts on the boil."'
lessons: Poetry and lamentation can be a dignified way to process grief and honor the fallen, as the companions used verse to express their sorrow and faith simultaneously.
Even in mourning, believers are reminded to maintain trust in God's decree — al-Hakam consoled his sister by reminding her that Shammas died in obedience to God.
The contrast between the Muslim poets affirming God's greatness and the Quraysh invoking Hubal illustrates how faith provides a source of identity and strength even in defeat.
practical_advice: When grieving a loss, allow yourself to express sorrow fully — whether through words, writing, or poetry — as suppressing grief is not a sign of faith.
Seek comfort from trusted family or community members who can gently remind you of God's wisdom when you are overwhelmed by loss.
Reaffirm your values and beliefs during difficult moments, as Hassan ibn Thabit did, responding to the enemy's taunts by declaring God's greatness rather than sinking into despair.

title: Martyrdom of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib at Uhud
summary: Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the uncle and foster-brother of the Prophet, was martyred at the Battle of Uhud alongside seventy other companions, remembered as 'God's lion' and one of the earliest and bravest believers.
story: At m u d , there died as a martyr Aba Ya'ln, also known as Aba 'Amara, Hamza b. 'Abd al-Mu$@lib, the uncle of the Messenger of God (SAAS) who was known as 'God's lion' and 'His messenger's lion'. He was the foster-brother of the Prophet (SAAS), as also was Aba Salama b. 'Abd al-Asad. All were suckled by Thuwayba, the freed-woman of Aba Lahab, this fact being established in a reliable hadith account. H e would, moreover, have been more than 50 when he was killed. He was a brave, heroic man and a major early trusting follower; at that battle others totalling 70 were killed along with him. May God be pleased with them all!
lessons: True courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to sacrifice oneself for what is right, as Hamza demonstrated throughout his life and at his death.
Greatness in God's sight is not measured by age or worldly status but by sincerity of faith and courage of conviction — Hamza was over fifty yet remained at the forefront of sacrifice.
The deaths of beloved companions are a reminder that this world is temporary and that those who give their lives in God's cause are honored across generations.
practical_advice: When you feel powerless in the face of loss, remember that those who lived and died with sincere faith are remembered and honored — your own sincere efforts are never wasted in God's sight.
Honor the memory of those who sacrificed for your community or faith by learning their stories and carrying forward their values of courage and devotion.
Reflect on your own legacy: what qualities — bravery, generosity, faithfulness — would you want to be remembered by, and how can you cultivate them today?

title: Marriage of Uthman ibn Affan to Umm Kulthum and Birth of al-Hasan
summary: In 3 AH, Uthman ibn Affan married Umm Kulthum, daughter of the Prophet, after the death of her sister Ruqayya, and al-Hasan ibn Ali was born to Fatimah, daughter of the Prophet.
story: That same year Wthmm b. 'Affan married Umm K u l t h m , the daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS), following the death of her sister Ruqayya. His betrothal to her took place in Rabi' al-Awwal of that year, and the marriage was consummated in JumZdS al-Akhira, as has been told above. Al-Wnqidi related this. Ibn Jarir also said of that year: "Al-Hasan b. 'Ali b. Abti T d i b was born to Fahma, daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." He added that the same year she conceived al-Husayn; may God be pleased with them all!
lessons: Life continues to hold moments of joy and renewal even in the aftermath of grief and loss, as the community celebrated a new marriage and the birth of al-Hasan in the same year as major losses at Uhud.
Family ties and community bonds are strengthened through the milestones of marriage and the birth of children, which carry hope for the future.
God's blessings do not stop in times of trial — new life and new beginnings arrive even amid hardship, reminding believers to remain grateful.
practical_advice: In seasons of grief, consciously look for and acknowledge the moments of blessing and new beginning around you, as gratitude and sorrow can coexist.
Celebrate the joyful milestones of your community — marriages, births, and new beginnings — as these are reminders of God's ongoing mercy.
Allow the arrival of new blessings to gently loosen the grip of grief, not by forgetting those you've lost, but by recognizing that life and mercy continue.

title: The Expedition of Abu Salama to Qatan Against Banu Asad
summary: In Muharram of 4 AH, the Prophet sent Abu Salama, still recovering from a wound received at Uhud, to lead 150 men against Banu Asad after receiving intelligence that Tulayha al-Asadi was assembling forces to attack the Muslims.
story: In Muharram of that year Abti Salama b. 'Abd al-Asad made his foray against Tulaylp al-Asadi, and he went on as far as the place known as Qatan. Al-Waqidi quoted 'Umar b. Wthmm b. 'Abd al-Rahrnm b. Sacid al-Yarbti'i, from Salama b. 'Abd Allah b. Wmar b. Aba Salama and others, as having said, "Abo Salama was present at Uhud, where he was seriously wounded in the upper arm. He rested for a month recuperating and then, in Muharram, at the beginning of 35 months after the Hijra, the Messenger of God (SAAS) summoned him and told him, 'I want you to go forth into Bana Asad territory and to attack them.'" He charged him to behave with piety and to treat the Muslims with him well. One hundred and fifty men accompanied him on this expedition. He went out to near Qamn, which is a well belonging to Bana Asad, where Tulayha al-Asadi and his brother Salama, both sons of Khuwaylid, were also present. He had assembled his allies from Bann Asad with the intention of making war against the Prophet (SAAS). But one of their men had come to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him of what they were planning against him. He had therefore dispatched Abii Salama on this expedition. When they (the Muslims) reached their territory, they dispersed, leaving large quantities of camels and goats as booty for them. Aba Salama took all this into his possession, along with three of their slaves he had taken prisoner, and set out to return to Medina. He then awarded a substantial portion of the booty to that man of Bana Asad who had given them information. Having extracted the portion due to the Prophet (SAAS), a slave and one-fifth of the booty, he divided up the rest among his companions. Then he entered Medina.
lessons: Acting on reliable intelligence and taking preemptive, measured action to protect one's community is a form of wisdom and responsible leadership.

The Prophet's instruction to Abu Salama to 'behave with piety and treat the Muslims well' shows that how a mission is conducted matters as much as its outcome — means and ends are both important in Islam.
Recognizing and rewarding those who help the community — as Abu Salama rewarded the informant — cultivates loyalty, trust, and a spirit of cooperation.
practical_advice: When you are tasked with a difficult responsibility while still recovering from a previous hardship, focus on the task at hand with the support of your community rather than waiting for perfect conditions.
In any leadership role, prioritize the wellbeing and fair treatment of those under your care — this builds trust and brings God's blessing to your efforts.
When someone helps you in a time of need, acknowledge and reward their contribution generously; gratitude expressed in action strengthens bonds and encourages future support.

title: Death of Abu Salama from His Uhud Wound and the Prophet's Marriage to Umm Salama
summary: Abu Salama returned from his expedition but his old wound from Uhud overtook him and he died, after which his widow Umm Salama observed her waiting period and the Prophet married her in Shawwal of 4 AH.
story: Wmar b. Wthman stated that 'Abd al-Malik b. 'Ubayd related to him, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Sa'id b. Yarba', from Wmar b. Abti Salama, who said, "It was Aba Usama al-Jushami who injured my father. He spent a month recovering and when he had done so, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him, in Muharram of that year, 4 AH that is, to Qamn. He was away for some ten days and when he returned to Medina his wound got the better of him and he died, three days prior to the end of Jumada al-Uwla." Wmar then added, "My mother then observed the 'rddam period of four months and ten days. Thereafter the Messenger of God (SAAS) contracted marriage with her and this he consummated a few days prior to the end of Shawwa. My mother used to say, 'There's nothing wrong with contracting and consummating a marriage in Shawwal; it was then that the Messenger of God (SAAS) both contracted and consummated marriage with me.'" He stated that Umm Salama died in Dhii al-Qa'da, 59 AH. Al-Bayhaqi related this.
lessons: Wounds — physical, emotional, or spiritual — that seem healed on the surface can resurface; it is important to be patient with one's own recovery and not rush back into hardship before true healing occurs.

The Islamic prescribed waiting period (iddah) after the death of a spouse is not only a legal requirement but a compassionate structure that gives a grieving person time and dignity before major life changes.
New beginnings, even marriage after widowhood, are not a betrayal of the past but a continuation of life — Umm Salama's remarriage was honored and blessed by the Prophet himself.
practical_advice: If you are grieving the loss of a spouse or loved one, allow yourself the full period of mourning without rushing to 'move on' — healing takes the time it takes, and that time is sacred.
When old wounds — whether from past trauma, illness, or grief — resurface unexpectedly, approach them with self-compassion rather than frustration, recognizing that healing is rarely linear.
Trust that God can open new doors of mercy and companionship even after devastating loss; Umm Salama's story is a reminder that a painful ending does not mean the end of goodness in your life.

title: The Expedition to al-Raji and the Betrayal of the Muslim Scouts
summary: The Prophet sent a small reconnaissance group led by Asim ibn Thabit toward Mecca, but they were tracked and ambushed by Banu Lihyan who violated their pledge of safe passage, killing most of the Muslims and capturing Khubayb and Zayd.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "In Safar - of the year 4 AH - the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent men to the Meccans to receive safe passage from them." He added, "Al-Rajic is positioned some eight miles from 'Asfm." Al-Bukhari stated that Ibrzhim b. Mas2 related to him, quoting Hishian b. Yasuf, from Macmar, from al-Zuhri, from 'Amr b. Aba Sufym al-Thaqafi, from Aha Hurayra, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) sent out a reconnaissance expedition, having appointed CAsim b. Thabit as their leader; he was the grandfather of 'Asim b. 'Umar b. al-Khatpb. "They travelled out to a point between 'Asfm and Mecca and there news of them reached a tribe of Banii Hudhayl known as Banii Lihym. The latter proceeded to pursue them, their force consisting of some 100 archers. They followed their tracks to a camp site where they had made a halt and there they found kernels of the dates with which the Muslims had provisioned themselves in Medina. The Banii Lihyan men recognized these as being from Medina, followed on after them and eventually caught up with them. "When 'A~imand his companions could go no further, they took refuge on an outcrop of rock where they were surrounded by Bana Lihyan. The latter told them, 'If you come on down, we give you our pledge and promise not to kill any one of you.' 'Asim replied, 'For myself, I'll not place myself under the security of a non-believer. 0 God, infonn Your prophet about us!' "The Bana Lihyan men then attacked and killed CAsim and seven of his men with arrows. Khubayh, Zayd and one other man remained alive. When Banii Lihym repeated their pledge and promise, Khubayb and the others came down. When they had them in their power, their enemy untied some bow strings with which they hound their prisoners. The third Muslim remonstrated, 'So now the treachery begins!' He then refused to accompany them. His captors dragged him along, trying to force him to accompany them, but when he resisted, they killed him. "They then set off with Khubayb and Zayd and sold them in Mecca."
lessons: Asim ibn Thabit's refusal to place himself under the protection of those who did not share his faith, and his immediate turning to God in prayer, illustrates that ultimate trust must rest in God alone, not in the promises of those who are hostile to faith.

The unnamed third companion who refused to continue when he recognized the treachery, even at the cost of his life, demonstrates that maintaining one's integrity and dignity is worth more than a compromised survival.
Betrayal by those who gave a promise of safety is a profound injustice — yet the response of the Muslims was supplication to God rather than panic, modeling how to face helplessness with faith.
practical_advice: When you find yourself trapped or betrayed with no clear escape, turn to God in direct, urgent prayer as your first response — not as a last resort after all other options are exhausted.

Recognize the signs of bad faith early in your relationships and dealings; when someone's actions begin to contradict their words, trust your instincts and do not override them out of wishful thinking.
Even when you cannot control your circumstances, you can control your integrity — commit to never compromising your values to save yourself from temporary difficulty, as the companions demonstrated.

title: The Noble Captivity and Martyrdom of Khubayb ibn Adi
summary: Khubayb ibn Adi was sold into captivity in Mecca, where he was treated with remarkable grace, performed two rak'at of prayer before his execution, and was killed with steadfast faith, becoming the first to establish the sunnah of praying before death.
story: "They then set off with Khubayb and Zayd and sold them in Mecca. T h e tribe of al-Haith b. CAmir b. Nawfal bought Khubayb; it had been Khubayb who had killed al-H&th at the battle of Badr. He remained with them for some time as their prisoner until they decided to kill him. Khubayb then asked one of al-Hiirith's daughters if he could borrow a razor with which to shave his pubic hair and she loaned him one. "She later said, 'My attention then left a baby of mine who crawled over to Khubayb, who lifted it up on to his lap. When I saw the baby there I was terrified and Khubayb, holding the razor, saw this and asked, "Are you scared I might kill him? I would hope to God I'd never do any such thing!"' "She used to comment, 'I never saw a better prisoner than Khubayb. I once saw him, bound in irons, eating a large bunch of grapes at a time when there were no such fruit in Mecca; the grapes were nothing hut bounty that God bestowed on him.' "His captors ultimately took him outside the sanctuary to kill hi. He asked, 'Allow me to make two rakcat in prayer.' Having done so, he went over to them and said, 'Except for the fact that you would have thought me scared of dying, I would have prayed longer.' He was the first to institute the practice of making two rak'zt before execution. Khubayb then said, '0 God, count them one by one, and give them violent death!' "He also spoke the following verses, 'Being killed as a Muslim, I care not how my death comes, since it is in God's cause. For that is God's prerogative; and if He wishes He will give His blessings to severed limbs.' "Thereupon Wqba b. d-Haith went up and killed"
lessons: Khubayb's gentleness toward the child of his captors, even while bound and facing death, shows that true faith produces noble character in every circumstance — our treatment of others reflects who we are, not what is being done to us.
The miraculous grapes given to Khubayb in captivity, when no grapes existed in Mecca, are a reminder that God's provision reaches His sincere servants even in the most impossible circumstances.

Khubayb's two rak'at before execution — and his wish that he could have prayed longer — illustrate that connection to God through prayer is the ultimate source of peace and courage when facing the most terrifying moments of life.
practical_advice: When you are in a situation that feels like captivity — whether physical, emotional, or circumstantial — maintain your character and your worship; these are the things no one can take from you.
Practice prayer not only in moments of desperation but as a regular discipline, so that when the most difficult moments arrive, turning to God in prayer is already a deep and natural reflex.

Reflect on Khubayb's verses in your own moments of fear: 'Being killed as a Muslim, I care not how my death comes, since it is in God's cause.' Ask yourself what core conviction, if held firmly, would give you peace regardless of what comes.

title: The Martyrdom of Khubayb ibn 'Adi
summary: Khubayb ibn 'Adi, captured by Quraysh after the expedition to al-Raji', displayed extraordinary faith and composure before his execution, praying two rak'at, reciting verses of steadfastness, and supplicating against his killers.
story: to them and said, 'Except for the fact that you would have thought me scared of dying, I would have prayed longer.' He was the first to institute the practice of making two rak'zt before execution. Khubayb then said, '0 God, count them one by one, and give them violent death!' "He also spoke the following verses, 'Being killed as a Muslim, I care not how my death comes, since it is in God's cause. For that is God's prerogative; and if He wishes He will give His blessings to severed limbs.' "Thereupon Wqba b. d-Haith went up and killed hi. Quraysh later sent for a part of 'Avim's body that they would recognize; 'Asim had killed one of their leaders at Badr. But God sent down a cloud of wasps; these protected his body from the Quraysh emissaries who could not gain access to hi."
lessons: True courage is maintaining dignity and worship even when facing death, as Khubayb's final prayer demonstrated.
A believer's ultimate concern is pleasing God, not what others think — Khubayb shortened his prayer only out of concern it would be misread as fear.
Placing one's fate entirely in God's hands brings a peace that transcends even the most terrifying circumstances.
practical_advice: When facing an overwhelming trial, begin with an act of worship — even a brief prayer — to anchor yourself in your relationship with God before anything else.
Release yourself from worrying about how others perceive your emotions during hardship; focus instead on sincerity before God.
Memorize or keep a short verse or prayer that reminds you of God's ultimate control, so you can call on it in your most anxious moments.

title: The Divine Protection of 'Asim ibn Thabit's Body
summary: After 'Asim ibn Thabit was killed at al-Raji', God sent a swarm of wasps and then a flood to protect his body from the Quraysh, who wished to desecrate it — fulfilling his own vow that no polytheist would ever touch him.
story: "When 'Asim was killed, Hudhayl wanted to take his head and sell it to Sulafa, daughter of Sa'd b. Suhayl. She had sworn after he had killed her two sons at Uhud that if she got possession of 'Asim's head, she would drink wine from his skull. "But when wasps prevented their getting access to it, they said, 'Let's leave him till nightfall; then they will leave and we will take it.' And so God sent (a flood) into the wadi which bore 'Asim away. He had made a pledge to God that no polytheist would ever touch him, or he one of them, because they might sully him. "'Umar b. al-Khattab used to say, when someone said that the wasps had protected him, 'God does protect believers.' 'Asim had sworn that no polytheist would ever touch him or he one of them during his life; and God protected him after his death just as he had defended God while living!'"
lessons: God honors the sincere commitments a believer makes — 'Asim's vow in life was protected even after his death.
Consistency between one's convictions in life and one's ultimate fate is the hallmark of a person of integrity.
Divine protection takes forms we cannot anticipate; what appears to be ordinary nature — wasps, a flood — can be the instrument of God's care for His servants.
practical_advice: Make sincere commitments and intentions to God in your daily life; these private pledges have weight and God is aware of them.
When you feel unprotected or abandoned, reflect on the story of 'Asim — God's protection may be working in ways you cannot yet see.
Strive for consistency between your stated values and your daily actions, so that your life itself becomes a form of prayer.

title: The Steadfastness of Zayd ibn al-Dathina Before Execution
summary: When Zayd ibn al-Dathina was brought out to be executed, Abu Sufyan asked if he would trade places with the Prophet. Zayd refused, saying he would not wish even a thorn's harm to come to the Prophet, prompting Abu Sufyan to marvel at the love the Companions had for Muhammad.
story: "Zayd b. al-Dathina was bought by Safwan b. Umayya to kill him in revenge for his own father. Safwan despatched him to al-Tan'im in the company of a freed-man of his named Nistas who took him outside the sanctuary to kill him. A group of Quraysh gathered among whom was Abu Sufyan b. Harb. Abu Sufyan asked him, as he was being brought forth for execution, 'I implore you, by God, Zayd; wouldn't you like it to be Muhammad here in your place with us now for us to strike off his head, and for you to be with your own people?' "He replied, 'By God, I would not like Muhammad, wherever he now is, to be harmed by even a single thorn and for me to be sitting among my people.' "Abu Sufyan used to say, 'I never saw anyone so love anyone else as Muhammad's Companions loved Muhammad.' "Nistas then killed him."
lessons: Love rooted in faith transcends self-preservation — Zayd's love for the Prophet was so complete that he placed the Prophet's safety above his own life.
The sincerity of a believer's character can make an impression even on an enemy, as Abu Sufyan's own testimony shows.
True loyalty is not contingent on circumstances or personal benefit; it endures even in the face of death.
practical_advice: Reflect on who and what you love most deeply, and ask whether those relationships are grounded in shared values and faith.
When under pressure to compromise your loyalties or values, let the clarity of your love for what is good guide your response.
Practice gratitude for the people in your life whose love for you is unconditional — and strive to offer that same quality of love to others.

title: The Miraculous Grapes in Khubayb's Prison
summary: While Khubayb ibn 'Adi was imprisoned in Mecca awaiting execution, his captor's freed-woman witnessed him eating a large bunch of grapes — a fruit not available anywhere on earth at that season — which was understood as a miraculous provision from God.
story: "Regarding Khubayb b. 'Adi. 'Abd Allah b. Abi Najih related to me that it was related to him from Mawiyya, the freed-woman of Hujayr b. Abi Ihab, who had accepted Islam, who said, 'Khubayb was kept prisoner in my home. One day I looked at him and saw that he was eating from his hand a bunch of grapes as large as a man's head - and that at a time when I don't know of any grapes being eaten anywhere on God's earth!'"
lessons: God does not abandon those who place their trust in Him; even in the darkest imprisonment, provision can come from unexpected sources.
A believer's inner peace and reliance on God can be visible to others, sometimes planting seeds of faith in those who witness it.
Small miracles in times of hardship are reminders that the material world is not the limit of God's care for His servants.
practical_advice: In moments of deprivation or hardship, remind yourself that God's provision is not limited by the constraints you see around you.
Try to maintain a calm and dignified presence in difficulty — your character under pressure may be an unspoken witness to your faith for those watching.
Keep a record or journal of unexpected blessings that came to you during difficult periods; reviewing it can renew trust in God during future trials.

title: Khubayb's Request for a Blade and His Mercy Toward the Child
summary: Before his execution, Khubayb requested a razor to cleanse himself. His captor's freed-woman handed it to a young boy to deliver, then feared Khubayb would take revenge by killing the child. Instead, Khubayb let the boy go unharmed, reflecting his moral nobility even facing death.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada and 'Abd Allah b. Abi Najih related to me that she also said, 'When the time for his execution came, he said, "Send for a steel blade with which I can clean myself off for the execution."' She went on, 'So I gave a razor to a young man from the tribe and told him, "Take this to the man in the house." But no sooner had the youth left with it than I began asking myself what I had done. The man will take his revenge by killing this youth, making it one man for another, I thought. But when the youth handed Khubayb the blade, the latter commented, "Wasn't your mother afraid of my treachery when she sent you to me with this blade?" He then allowed the youth to leave.'" Ibn Hisham stated, 'It is said that the youth was her own son.'"
lessons: Moral integrity does not bend under the pressure of injustice — Khubayb had every worldly reason for revenge yet chose mercy.
Ritual purity and preparation for worship, even before death, shows that a believer's first concern is their standing before God, not their standing before people.
The character of a person is most clearly revealed in moments where no one could hold them accountable — Khubayb's mercy was chosen freely.
practical_advice: When you are wronged, pause before reacting and ask yourself what response you could make that you would be proud of before God, regardless of what others expect.
Maintain your personal rituals of worship and cleanliness even in chaotic or stressful periods; they anchor your identity and calm your spirit.
Practice small acts of unexpected mercy or restraint today — these build the character that will hold firm when larger tests come.

title: Khubayb's Prayer Before Crucifixion and His Curse Upon Quraysh
summary: Raised on the scaffold at al-Tan'im, Khubayb prayed two rak'at calmly, then called upon God to inform the Prophet of what had been done to the group and to count the oppressors and destroy them — and Mu'awiya later testified he saw his own father throw him to the ground in fear of the curse.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on to quote 'Asim as saying, "They then took Khubayb out to al-Tan'im to crucify him. "He spoke to them as follows, 'I'd appreciate you letting me alone to make two raka'at in prayer.' They agreed and he did so very well. Then he went over to those present and said, 'I swear, if I had not been concerned that you would think I had taken a long time out of fear of dying, I would have prayed more!' "It was Khubayb who was the first to begin the practice by Muslims of making two prayer prostrations before execution. "They then raised him up to the scaffold and when they had bound him there he said, 'O God, we have announced your messenger's mission, so inform him tomorrow of what is being done to us.' He then said, 'O God, count them and kill them violently; let none of them escape.' They then killed him. "Mu'awiya b. Abi Sufyan used to say, 'I was there along with others in the company of Abu Sufyan and had him throw me to the ground in fear of Khubayb's curse. People used to say, "A man who has an oath spoken against him can escape it by falling on his side."'"
lessons: Prayer is the believer's most powerful resource — Khubayb's first act before his death was to turn to God, not to plead with his captors.
Speaking truth and completing one's mission until the very end, as Khubayb did in announcing the Prophet's message even on the scaffold, is the highest form of faithfulness.
The supplications of the oppressed carry a weight that even enemies acknowledge, as the terror Quraysh felt at Khubayb's curse demonstrated.
practical_advice: When you feel powerless, remember that prayer is always available to you as a direct connection to God — make it your first response to crisis, not your last.
Identify the core mission or purpose that defines your life, and recommit to it during difficult moments rather than abandoning it under pressure.
If you have been wronged and feel the urge to retaliate, channel that energy into sincere supplication; leave justice in God's hands and focus on your own integrity.

title: The Expedition to al-Raji': Betrayal and the Death of the Missionaries
summary: Six Companions sent by the Prophet to teach Islam to the tribes of 'Adal and al-Qara were betrayed at the well of al-Raji' and ambushed by Hudhayl. Three were killed in battle and three were taken prisoner to be sold in Mecca.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that 'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada related to him, "A group of men from 'Adal and from al-Qara approached the Messenger of God (SAAS) after the battle of Uhud and asked, 'O Messenger of God, Islam has already reached us. Could you now send a group of your Companions to give us instruction in the religion, in how to recite the Qur'an, and in the laws of Islam?' "The Messenger of God (SAAS), therefore did send with them a group of six of his Companions. These were Marthad b. Abi Marthad al-Ghanawi, an ally of Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib - Ibn Ishaq relates that he was their leader - Khalid b. al-Bukayr al-Laythi, ally of Banu 'Adi, 'Asim b. Thabit b. Abi al-Aqlah, brother of Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf, Khubayb b. 'Adi, brother of Banu Jahjaba b. Kulfa b. 'Amr b. 'Awf, Zayd b. al-Dathina, brother of Banu Bayada b. 'Amir, 'Abd Allah b. Tariq, ally of Banu Zafar. May God be pleased with them all." Ibn Ishaq, therefore, is stating that they were six in number... Ibn Ishaq went on, "These men went forth as far as al-Raji', a well belonging to Hudhayl in the Hijaz, above al-Hada'. There (other persons) betrayed them and aroused Hudhayl against them. The party were there in camp when men bearing swords suddenly descended upon them. They therefore took up their own swords to do battle. Their attackers, however, told them, 'We swear by God we don't want to kill you; we just want to get some money for you from the Meccans. We pledge and promise by God that we will not kill you.' "Marthad, Khalid b. al-Bukayr and 'Asim b. Thabit, however, replied, 'By God, we'll never accept any pledge or oath made by a polytheist.' 'Asim b. Thabit then recited - and God knows best and to Him is all praise and reliance - 'What weakness do I have, a brave archer with a bow that has a tough string? Long, thick arrows can wing from it; and death is truth, life false. All that God determines comes about for man and man to Him returns. If I do not battle you, then may my mother be bereaved!' ... "He then fought until he was killed, as were also two of his companions. "However, Khubayb, Zayd b. al-Dathina and 'Abd Allah b. Tariq were weak and pliant and protective of their lives and so gave themselves up. The enemy took them prisoner and then took them into Mecca to sell them there. "When they were in al-Zahran, 'Abd Allah b. Tariq extracted his hand from his bonds and drew his sword. The enemy, however, kept their distance from him and pelted him with stones until they had killed him. His grave is there at al-Zahran."
lessons: Treachery from those who appear to come with goodwill is a recurring test; maintaining firm principles, as Marthad and 'Asim did in refusing to trust the polytheists' oath, is a form of wisdom.
Responding to injustice with courage rather than compliance, even at great personal cost, preserves one's dignity and integrity before God.
The spread of knowledge and faith carries real risk; those who undertake it with sincerity, like these six missionaries, are among the most honored in God's sight.
practical_advice: Be discerning about whom you place your trust in, particularly when the stakes are high; sincerity on your side does not guarantee sincerity on theirs.
When facing a situation where your values are being tested by external pressure, write down your core commitments beforehand so they remain clear under stress.
Honor the memory and sacrifice of those who came before you in faith by deepening your own knowledge of the religion they died to teach.

title: The Martyrdom of Khubayb ibn 'Adi and Zayd ibn al-Dathina at al-Raji'
summary: Khubayb ibn 'Adi and Zayd ibn al-Dathina were captured and executed by Quraysh after being betrayed at al-Raji'. Khubayb faced crucifixion and death with unwavering faith, refusing to wish any harm on the Prophet even in his final moments.
story:
Zayd b. al-Dathina were killed on the same day and the Messenger of God (SAAS) heard of it that very day. He then said, 'May peace he upon you both' - or 'upon you', singular. 'Quraysh have killed Khuhayb!'

It is said that when they crucified Zayd b. al-Dathina, they cast arrows at him to subvert him away from his religion, but this only increased his faith and his acceptance of his fate. Wrwa and Mnsa b. Wqha related that when they raised Khuhayb on to the wooden frame, those present called out to him, 'Would you not prefer Muhammad to be in your place?' He replied, 'No, by God Almighty, I'd not have him ransom me by having a thorn pierce his foot!' But they laughed at him.

Ibn Ishaq stated that the following verses were spoken about this expedition by Khubayb when people gathered to kill him. Ibn Hisham observed that some authorities deny that he was their author:

'The parties gathered around me, assembled their tribes and collected all the groups together; All of them showed enmity towards me and antagonism, because I was in bonds, tightly held. They had gathered their women and children and I was taken to a tall, stout tree-trunk; I complain to God at my isolation and my torment and at the death the enemy planned for me. O Lord of the Throne, give me patience to withstand what it is they intend; they have slashed my flesh and my hope despairs. This is happening in God's cause, and if He wishes He will bless torn-off limbs. They offered me the choice between disbelief and death, and my eyes were wet from something other than fear. I have no fear of death - and am already dead; but what I dread is the consuming fire of hell. By God, if I die a Muslim, I care not in what way my death in God's cause may occur. I shall not express subservience to the enemy, nor any fear, for it is to God I am returning.'

Two of these verses are given also in the Sahih collection of al-Bukhari. These are the lines, 'I do not care, when I die as a Muslim, in what manner my death in God's cause may occur. This is happening in God's cause; and if He wishes, He will bless torn-off limbs.'

Ibn Hisham stated, 'Khubayb remained in their captivity until the sacred months had expired and then they killed him.'
lessons: Unwavering faith in the face of death is one of the highest expressions of trust in God — Khubayb's refusal to abandon his religion even under torture shows that true conviction cannot be broken by worldly threat.

Love for the Prophet is a defining mark of a believer — Khubayb would not even wish a thorn to harm the Prophet in exchange for his own life, illustrating that genuine love prioritises the beloved over oneself.

Death with dignity and purpose is not a loss — Khubayb's final poem reveals that his greatest fear was not physical death but spiritual failure, reorienting us toward what truly matters in our hardest moments.
practical_advice: When you feel trapped in a painful situation with no way out, write or speak your feelings honestly to God as Khubayb did in his poem — articulating your distress to God is itself an act of worship and trust.

Remind yourself in moments of despair that the question is not 'will this suffering end?' but 'will I remain true to my values through it?' — anchor your identity in your faith, not your circumstances.

Reflect on someone you love deeply and ask yourself whether that love has made you more selfless or more self-serving — let Khubayb's example challenge you to cultivate a love that gives rather than demands.

title: Sa'id ibn 'Amir's Fainting Spells from Witnessing Khubayb's Execution
summary: Sa'id ibn 'Amir, later appointed governor of part of Syria by Umar ibn al-Khattab, suffered recurring fainting spells because he had been present at the execution of Khubayb ibn 'Adi and could not forget the martyr's dying curse. Umar honoured him all the more for this.
story: 'Umar b. al-Khattab appointed Sa'id b. 'Amir b. Hidhym al-Jumahi over part of Syria. He would suffer from fainting spells when he was with people and this was reported to 'Umar. It was said that the man had some affliction. 'Umar asked him on a visit he made to him, 'Sa'id, what is it that ails you?' He replied, 'By God, Commander of the Faithful, there's nothing the matter with me; it's just that I was among those present when Khubayb b. 'Adi was killed. And I heard his curse. And whenever I'm meeting people and recall this, I feel faint.' 'Umar thought all the better of him thereafter. Al-Umawi stated, 'My father quoted Ibn Ishaq as having said, "We have been told that 'Umar said, 'Anyone who wants to see a man who is unique should look at Sa'id b. 'Amir.""'
lessons: The weight of having witnessed or participated in wrongdoing — even indirectly — can follow a person for life, and acknowledging that weight honestly is a mark of moral seriousness, not weakness.
Guilt and remorse, when they arise from a sincere conscience, can be signs of a living heart rather than a broken one — Umar's esteem for Sa'id increased precisely because his grief was genuine.
Being in the presence of injustice and not stopping it is something a person of conscience remembers; this memory can become a lifelong motivator to stand up for truth whenever given the opportunity.
practical_advice: If a past event — something you witnessed or participated in — continues to haunt you, do not suppress it. Acknowledge it honestly as Sa'id did, and let it deepen your compassion and commitment to justice rather than consuming you with shame.
Speak to a trusted person about what you carry — Sa'id's confession to Umar brought him understanding rather than judgment, and honest disclosure often lightens burdens we have been carrying alone.
Channel recurring guilt or grief into purposeful action: ask yourself what the person or situation you feel you failed would want you to do differently now, and do that.

title: 'Amr ibn Umayya Retrieves Khubayb's Body from the Wooden Frame
summary: The Prophet sent 'Amr ibn Umayya alone on a secret mission to retrieve Khubayb's body from the crucifixion post in Makkah. When 'Amr cut him down, the body miraculously disappeared into the earth without a trace.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related, from Ibrahim b. Isma'il, as follows, 'Ja'far b. 'Amr b. Umayya related to me, from his father, from his grandfather 'Amr b. Umayya, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent him forth alone to get information. He said, "I made my way to the wooden frame where Khubayb was and climbed up, fearing I might be seen. I released him and he fell to the ground. I then drew away a little, then turned but could not see anything there; it was as if the earth had swallowed him. And not a trace of Khubayb has appeared up to this time."'
lessons: God honours those who honour His servants — the miraculous disappearance of Khubayb's body is understood by believers as a sign of divine care for the martyr even after death.

Courage in service of others, even at great personal risk and alone, is one of the most dignified expressions of loyalty — 'Amr went alone, in enemy territory, climbing a crucifixion post out of love and duty.
We are often sent on missions whose full meaning we do not understand at the time; trust in the one who sends you and act faithfully, and God may complete the work in ways beyond your expectation.
practical_advice: When you are asked to do something difficult for someone you love or for a righteous cause, and fear holds you back, recall 'Amr climbing that post alone at night — ask what love would require of you and take one step toward it.
After completing a difficult act of service, do not be troubled if you do not see the full outcome — 'Amr did not see where Khubayb went, and that uncertainty did not diminish what he had done.
Volunteer for tasks that others avoid when justice or care for another person requires it; the willingness to act alone when no one else will is itself a form of moral strength worth cultivating.

title: The Hypocrites' Mockery of the Martyrs of al-Raji' and the Quranic Response
summary: After the men of al-Raji' were killed, hypocrites in Madinah mocked them as misguided people who died pointlessly. God revealed verses of the Quran defending the martyrs and exposing the hypocrites.
story:
Ibn Ishaq then quoted Muhammad b. Abu Muhammad, from Sa'id or 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'When the men were killed at al-Raji', some hypocrites said, "How sad for those misguided people who died that way. They neither stayed with their families, nor did they carry out their master's mission."'

It was about them that God revealed the words, 'There may be a person whose words about this life may please you, while he invokes God to witness what is in his heart. Yet he may be the most vehement of enemies' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.204). And God revealed concerning those who went on this expedition, 'There are those who sell themselves seeking God's favour, and God is compassionate to His servants' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.207).
lessons: Those who sacrifice themselves for truth will always face the mockery of those who do not understand their values — being misunderstood by the world is not evidence that you are wrong.

God Himself defends the sincere — when the hypocrites mocked the martyrs, the Quran was revealed to vindicate those who had given everything, reminding us that divine judgment is the only one that ultimately counts.
Beautiful words can hide hollow hearts, and sincere hearts can be dismissed by a mocking world — discern people not by their eloquent speech but by the consistency of their actions and sacrifices.
practical_advice: When you are ridiculed for a principled decision — especially one that cost you something — do not measure its worth by how others receive it. Ask instead whether it was done sincerely for God.
If someone around you consistently has persuasive and flattering speech but no real sacrifice or integrity behind it, weigh their words carefully — the Quranic warning about such people is timeless.
Write down the names or qualities of people you genuinely admire for their sacrifice and sincerity. Return to that list when the voices of cynicism around you grow loud.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit's Elegies and Satires Following al-Raji'
summary: The poet Hassan ibn Thabit composed elegies honouring the martyrs of al-Raji' and scathing satirical verses condemning the treachery of Banu Lihyan and Hudhayl who had betrayed and killed the Prophet's companions.
story:
Ibn Ishaq quotes Hassan b. Thabit as having spoken the following elegy for Khubayb:

'What is wrong with your eyes, ceaselessly letting fall their teardrops on to your breast like pearls, Over Khubayb, a hero of the brave, no coward when you challenge him and not frivolous, as is well known. Go forth, Khubayb, and may God reward you well, in eternal paradise with houris for companions. What will you respond when the Prophet asks you, while pure angels are there on the horizon, Why it was you killed God's martyr for a tyrant who committed crimes throughout the land?'

Hassan also, according to Ibn Ishaq, recited the following verses attacking those of Banu Lihyan who had betrayed those who went to al-Raji':

'If pure, unadulterated treachery delights you, then go to al-Raji' and ask where Lihyan live; They are a people who advise one another to eat the neighbours who come among them, dogs, monkeys and men being all alike! If a billy-goat could talk and arose one day to address them, they would consider him someone of honour and importance!'

Hassan b. Thabit also said, satirizing Hudhayl and Banu Lihyan for their treachery towards those who had gone to al-Raji':

'By my life, the stories told of Khubayb and 'Asim have disgraced Hudhayl b. Mudrik, Tales told of Lihyan have brought them ignominy, For Lihyan have committed the most evil of crimes. Their men, those from the very best of their tribe, those like the very hairs behind the fetlock of a horse's front legs. They betrayed at al-Raji' and surrendered their charges, men of decency and fine qualities. They betrayed the apostle's messenger; Hudhayl gave no protection against horrendous crimes. They will one day see victory go against them for killing one protected against evil actions, By swarms of wasps guarding his flesh, protecting the flesh of one who witnessed mighty battles. Hudhayl may well see the bodies of those killed laid out or at funerals. We will engage them in fierce battles that riders will relate to fairgoers. At the orders of the Messenger of God; His messenger has made his decision against Lihyan, forcefully and with knowledge. A despicable tribe that cares nothing for fidelity and does not repel the tyrant's hand when wronged. When (some) people live in the open desert, them you find at the very rivers, amidst the streams. Their abode is that of death; their response when harmed is like that of dumb beasts.'

Hassan, may God be pleased with him, also spoke the following in praise of the men at al-Raji', naming them in his verses:

'May God bless those who went forth together at al-Raji' and who were honoured and rewarded. The party's leader and commander was Marthad, and Ibn al-Bukayr was their Imam, along with Khubayb. And there was a son of Tariq and Ibn Dathina was of them; it was there he met his pre-determined fate. And there was al-'Asim, slain at al-Raji', who reached the very heights (of heaven); how much he gained! He prevented the panderers from striking his back by fighting fiercely; he was splendid.'
lessons: Grief for those who died righteously is itself an act of honour — Hassan's tears and verses were not weakness but a communal acknowledgment that these lives mattered and their loss deserved to be mourned.
Naming and remembering those who sacrificed for truth preserves their legacy and strengthens the moral fabric of a community — forgetting the righteous is its own form of injustice.

Treachery has social consequences — Hassan's satire functioned as a public moral reckoning, reminding communities that betrayal of trust carries lasting shame and that integrity is a collective, not merely individual, value.
practical_advice: Find your own form of honouring those who have suffered or sacrificed for good — whether through writing, speaking, donating, or simply remembering them in your prayers. Do not let their stories be forgotten.

When you witness injustice that you cannot immediately correct, express your moral response honestly — through words, art, or conversation — as Hassan did. Moral clarity expressed aloud helps others locate their own conscience.

Reflect on the people in your life who have been loyal and trustworthy and tell them so explicitly. Gratitude for fidelity, like Hassan's praise for the martyrs, is something people rarely hear and always need.

title: Abu Sufyan's Plot to Assassinate the Prophet and the Bedouin Assassin
summary: Following the execution of Khubayb, Abu Sufyan recruited a bedouin assassin with a concealed dagger to travel to Madinah and kill the Prophet Muhammad. The Prophet was forewarned by God and the plot was foiled when the dagger was discovered.
story:
Al-Waqidi stated, Ibrahim b. Ja'far related to me, from his father, and (as did) 'Abd Allah b. Abu 'Ubayda, from Ja'far b. al-Fadl b. al-Hasan b. 'Amr b. Umayya al-Damri, and (as did) 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far, from 'Abd al-Wahid b. Abu 'Awf, some of these giving longer accounts than others. They stated, 'Abu Sufyan had asked a number of Quraysh men in Mecca, "Won't someone assassinate Muhammad? He goes walking in the markets. We would then have our revenge." A bedouin came to him, went inside his house and told him, "If you will hire me, I'll go out and assassinate him. I'm a skilled tracker and I have a dagger that is (concealed) like the under-wing feathers of an eagle." "Then you're our man!" he replied, and gave him a camel and expense money. He told him, "Keep this secret; I don't want anyone to hear of this and report it to Muhammad." The bedouin assured him that no one would know of it.

'He then left by night on his mount, travelling on for five days, arriving in the quarter on the morning of the sixth. He then went about enquiring after the Messenger of God (SAAS); when he reached the place for prayer someone told him, "He has gone off to see Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal."

'The bedouin then left, leading his mount. When he arrived where Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal were, he hobbled his camel and went to seek the Messenger of God (SAAS). He found him amidst a group of his Companions, addressing them in his mosque. When the bedouin entered and the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw him, he told his men, "This man intends some treachery, but God will foil his plan." The bedouin stood there and asked, "Which one of you is 'Abd al-Muttalib's son?" The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "I'm 'Abd al-Muttalib's son." The bedouin approached and leaned down over the Messenger of God (SAAS) as though confiding a secret to him. But Usayd b. Hudayr pulled him away, saying, "Keep away from the Messenger of God (SAAS)!" Usayd then felt down in his loin cloth and there found the dagger. He then announced, "Messenger of God, this man is a traitor!"

'The bedouin stood there aghast and said, "Take my blood! Take my blood, Muhammad!"'
lessons: Divine protection is real and operates through both miraculous forewarning and the alertness of those around the righteous — the Prophet's announcement of the man's intent before anyone else suspected him is a reminder that God guards those who trust in Him.
Evil plots, however secretly laid, carry within them the seeds of their own exposure — the bedouin's mission was known only to Abu Sufyan, yet it was seen through before a single word was spoken.
Those around a leader of truth have a role to play in protecting what is good — Usayd ibn Hudayr's swift action shows that communal vigilance is itself a form of worship and service.
practical_advice: When you feel threatened or unsafe, take both practical precautions and spiritual ones — the Prophet's community had guards and also had prayer; neither replaces the other.

If you sense that someone approaching you has harmful intentions, trust your instincts and act calmly and firmly rather than with panic — composure in danger, as the Prophet modelled, is itself a form of strength.
Reflect on the people in your life who protect and look out for you. Express gratitude for them — unseen acts of care and vigilance by those around us often go unacknowledged.

title: The Bedouin Assassin Sent by Abu Sufyan
summary: A bedouin sent by Abu Sufyan attempted to assassinate the Prophet Muhammad, but was caught by Usayd ibn Hudayr. Upon being shown mercy and truth by the Prophet, the bedouin converted to Islam.
story: of you is 'Ahd al-MutGb's son?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'I'm 'Abd al-Mut@hh's son.' The bedouin approached and leaned down over the Messenger of God (SAAS) as though confiding a secret to him. But Usayd b. Hudayr pulled him away, saying, 'Keep away from the Messenger of God (SAAS)!' Usayd then felt down in his loin cloth and there found the dagger. He then announced, 'Messenger of God, this man is a traitor!' "The bedouin stood there aghast and said, 'Take my blood! Take my blood, Muhammad!' "Usayd b. Hudayr seized him and was about to slit his throat when the Prophet (SAAS), said, 'Tell me the truth about yourself and why you have come. And if you tell the truth your honesty will serve you well; but if you lie to me, I'll proceed as I intended.' "'Then I'll be free?' the bedouin asked. "'Yes, you'll be free,' he replied. "The bedouin then told him about Abu Sufyan and what he told him to do, and he was then given over to the charge of Usayd b. Hudayr. Next day the Messenger of God (SAAS) summoned him and told him, 'I will either set you free to go wherever you want, or would you prefer something better than that?' 'What would that be?' the bedouin asked. 'That you give testimony that there is no god but God, and that I am God's messenger.' "The bedouin then said, 'I do testify that there is no god but God and that you are the Messenger of God. I swear by God, Muhammad, that I was never afraid of any man. But as soon as I saw you all my strength left me. Then you perceived what I had intended, though no rider had reached you before myself with any information and no one had any information. So I realized that you were inviolate, that you were truth and that the party of Abu Sufyan was that of the devil.' "The Prophet (SAAS) began to smile at this. He (the bedouin) remained there for some days then asked permission of the Prophet (SAAS) to leave and, having done so, nothing more was heard of him."
lessons: Mercy and truth can transform even the most hardened heart — responding to hostility with compassion rather than revenge opens doors that force never could.
Courage to be honest, even when one's life is at stake, is rewarded; the Prophet's promise of safety in exchange for truth reflects the power of a covenant kept.
A sincere encounter with righteousness can completely change a person's worldview, as the bedouin experienced when his enmity dissolved into faith.
practical_advice: When someone wrongs you, consider offering them a path to honesty before reacting with punishment — it may reveal a heart ready to change.
In moments of fear or conflict, anchoring yourself in your values rather than reacting from anger can lead to outcomes far better than you imagined.
Reflect on moments when witnessing someone's character shifted your perspective — let those experiences remind you that goodness has a real, tangible power.

title: The Mission to Kill Abu Sufyan: 'Amr ibn Umayya and Salama's Dangerous Journey
summary: The Prophet sent 'Amr ibn Umayya and Salama ibn Aslam on a secret mission to Mecca to kill Abu Sufyan, during which they faced pursuit, discovery, and harrowing escapes through the mountains and caves of Mecca.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said to 'Amr b. Umayya al-Damri and to Salama b. Aslam b. Haris, 'Go to Abu Sufyan b. Harb and if you should catch him unawares, then kill him.' "'Amr related, 'I and my companion then left, travelling on until we reached the Ya'jij valley. We tied up our mounts and my companion asked me, "'Amr, would you like for us to go on into Mecca and circumambulate the ka'ba seven times and make two rak'as in prayer?"
