I replied, "I'm better informed about the people of Mecca than you are and after dark they dampen down their courtyards and sit down there. I know Mecca better than a piebald mare!" But he disagreed with me and so we went on into Mecca. We made seven circumambulations and did make two rak'as. When I went outside (the sanctuary) I was met by Mu'awiya b. Abu Sufyan, who recognized me. "It's 'Amr b. Umayya! What a pity," he said. He then drew the attention of the Meccans to us, who said, "'Amr has not come here for anything good!" - 'Amr had been renowned before Islam as an assassin. "The Meccans then gathered men together and 'Amr and Salama left, being pursued, and all hurried up into the mountains. "'Amr went on, 'I entered a cave and hid from them till next morning. They had spent the night searching for us, but God blinded their sight from finding their way to the road to Medina. The morning of the next day, 'Uthman b. Malik b. 'Ubayd Allah al-Taymi approached, looking for grass for his horse. I commented to Salama b. Aslam, "If he sees us, he'll tell the Meccans where we are, and they will discover us." He kept on approaching until he was right over us. I then went out to him and thrust my dagger once beneath a nipple. He fell down, screaming. So the Meccans gathered and advanced together, having previously been separated. I went back inside and told my companion, "Don't move!" The Meccans approached until they were right there and then they asked ('Uthman b. Malik) who had killed him. "It was 'Amr b. Umayya al-Damri," they replied. Abu Sufyan then said, "We knew he had not come for any good reason!" But ('Uthman) was not able to tell them where we were; he was at his last gasp and died. They were distracted from seeking us by their (dead) comrade and carried him away. "'We remained in our place for two nights until the search for us had died down; we then left for al-Tan'im.'"
lessons: Divine protection can manifest in unexpected ways — sometimes the greatest dangers pass us by not through our own cleverness alone, but through a mercy we did not arrange.
Patience in hiding, restraining oneself from premature action, is itself a form of wisdom and strength.
Even on dangerous missions undertaken for a righteous cause, companions who disagree with us may lead us into unforeseen difficulties — yet God can still bring good out of flawed decisions.
practical_advice: When you are in a frightening or high-stakes situation, resist the urge to act impulsively; waiting and observing often reveals opportunities that panic would have destroyed.
Trust that difficulty and close calls are not signs of abandonment — reflect on past moments where you narrowly escaped harm and recognize the hand of Providence.
When someone close to you makes a decision you disagree with, voice your concern clearly but be prepared to face the consequences together with grace rather than blame.

title: 'Amr ibn Umayya Retrieves the Body of Khubayb ibn 'Adi
summary: 'Amr ibn Umayya risked his life to retrieve the crucified body of the martyr Khubayb ibn 'Adi from the Meccans, carrying it away on his back before being discovered and barely escaping.
story: "'We remained in our place for two nights until the search for us had died down; we then left for al-Tan'im. My companion asked me, "'Amr b. Umayya, would you like us to go for Khubayb b. 'Adi, to bring him down?" "Where is he?" I asked. "Over there, crucified," he replied, "with a guard around him." I answered, "Leave me here awhile, and you go on. If something frightens you, then move over to your camel and sit on it. Then proceed to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and tell him the news. Leave me here; I know the town." I then searched for him (Khubayb's body) until I found him and carried him away on my back. I had not walked more than 20 arms' lengths before they all woke up and set off after me. I threw down the wooden scaffold, and I'll never forget the sound it made. Then I piled earth up over it with my feet and took off on the Safra' road. Eventually they tired (of following) and went back, leaving me much relieved. My companion hurried over and mounted his camel, which he rode off to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him what had happened. I then travelled on until I was overlooking al-Ghalil, al-Ghalil Dajnan; there I went into a cave carrying my bow, arrows and dagger."
lessons: Honoring those who have sacrificed their lives for a noble cause is itself an act of courage and love — some forms of loyalty cost us everything.
Even in the direst circumstances, a calm and calculated mind serves better than panic; 'Amr's knowledge of the terrain and deliberate planning saved his life.
The bond between believers transcends death — going out of one's way to dignify the departed reflects the depth of faith and brotherhood.
practical_advice: When you feel called to do something honorable but risky, assess it honestly and plan carefully rather than either rushing in or abandoning it out of fear.
Honor the memory of those who have suffered or sacrificed for what you love — whether through remembrance, prayer, or action — as this strengthens your own sense of purpose.
In moments of pursuit or pressure, focus on the next step rather than the whole overwhelming picture; 'Amr's escape happened one stride at a time.

title: 'Amr ibn Umayya in the Cave: The Singing Pagan and the Two Qurayshi Spies
summary: While hiding in a cave after rescuing Khubayb's body, 'Amr ibn Umayya encountered a pagan herdsman who declared his hostility to Islam, then killed him, and afterward captured two Qurayshi spies before returning safely to the Prophet.
story: "While I was there a man of Banu al-Dil b. Bakr arrived; he was tall, one-eyed and herding sheep and goats. He came inside the cave and asked, "What man is here?" I replied, "A man of Banu Bakr." "I too am of Banu Bakr," he said. He then made himself comfortable, and raised his voice in a song, "I'll be not Muslim so long as I live; I'll never profess the Muslims' faith." "'I said to myself, "By God, I hope I get to kill you!" When he was asleep, I went over to him and killed him more brutally than I ever have anyone. "'I then left there and proceeded on down. When I got to the road, there were two men who had been sent by Quraysh to gather news. I told them, "Consider yourselves prisoners!" One of them refused, so I shot and killed him. When the other saw this, he gave himself up and I bound him and took him on in to the Prophet (SAAS). "'When I reached Medina, Ansar children gathered around me, playing, and they heard their elders say, "This is 'Amr." The children raced off to tell the Prophet (SAAS), and I took the prisoner in to him, having bound his thumbs together with my bow string. I noticed that the Prophet (SAAS) was laughing. He then said a prayer for me.'"
lessons: Those who commit themselves to a dangerous mission for a righteous cause will face multiple unexpected trials — perseverance through each one, not just the first, is what defines character.
The Prophet's laughter and prayer upon 'Amr's return shows that even after harrowing experiences, there is relief and warmth waiting on the other side of hardship.
Remaining resourceful and decisive when threats arise unexpectedly, rather than being paralyzed, is a survival skill rooted in tawakkul — trust in God combined with action.
practical_advice: When you feel that hardships keep compounding one after another, remind yourself that each challenge overcome brings you closer to the relief and recognition that awaits at the end.
After a period of sustained stress or danger, allow yourself to receive care and warmth from those who love you — do not feel you must immediately move on without acknowledging what you endured.
Prepare yourself mentally for the reality that difficult tasks rarely go exactly as planned; flexibility and calm adaptability in the face of the unexpected are skills worth cultivating.

title: The Massacre at Bi'r Ma'una: Seventy Qurra' Killed
summary: Seventy of the Prophet's most devoted companions, known as the Qurra' (reciters of Quran), were sent on a mission and were treacherously massacred at the well of Ma'una by the tribes of Ri'l and Dhakwan, causing the Prophet profound grief.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Abu Ma'mar related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Warith, quoting 'Abd al-'Aziz, from Anas b. Malik who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out 70 men, known as al-qurra', on a mission. Their way was blocked by two tribes of Banu Sulaym, Ri'l and Dhakwan, at a well called Ma'una. The Muslims told them, 'We have not come out to fight you; we're sent on a mission for the Messenger of God (SAAS).' But the tribesmen killed them. "Consequently, the Prophet (SAAS) spoke against them for a month at each morning prayer. This began the practice of saying the qunut; previously we had not done so." Muslim related it from a hadith of Hammad b. Salama, from Thabit, from Anas in similar words. Al-Bukhari then stated, "'Abd al-A'la b. Hammad related to us, quoting Yazid b. Zuray', quoting Sa'id, from Qatada, from Anas b. Malik, that Ri'l, Dhakwan, 'Usayya and Banu Lihyan asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) for assistance against an enemy and so he provided them the help of 70 of the ansar. We used at that time to name them the al-qurra'; they would collect wood by day and pray by night. Eventually, when they were at Bi'r Ma'una the tribesmen killed them, having betrayed them. After this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), he said the qunut prayers for a month, each morning saying prayers against these bedouin tribes of Ri'l, Dhakwan, 'Usayya and Banu Lihyan." Anas stated, "And we would recite Qur'an verses too, but these were later rescinded. These words were, 'Inform our people about us - that we have met our Lord. And He is pleased with us and has pleased us.'"
lessons: Betrayal by those you intended to help is among the deepest wounds — yet responding with prayer rather than immediate revenge reflects the highest spiritual maturity.
The Qurra' who collected wood by day and prayed by night show that the most honored among people are often those whose devotion is both practical and spiritual, not merely one or the other.

Even in the face of catastrophic loss, the Prophet institutionalized a practice of supplication — transforming grief into a structured form of spiritual expression that would benefit the community for generations.
practical_advice: When you experience betrayal by someone you tried to help, resist the temptation to close your heart entirely — instead channel your grief into prayer and community, as the Prophet modeled.
Honor the memory of those you have lost by continuing to do the good they were known for — the Qurra' lived prayerfully; keep their spirit alive by maintaining your own devotional practices.
If grief feels too heavy to carry silently, give it a form — through supplication, remembrance, or gathering with others who share your loss — rather than allowing it to fester in isolation.

title: The Dying Words of the Martyrs of Bi'r Ma'una
summary: Quranic verses were temporarily revealed to convey the message of the martyrs of Bi'r Ma'una, expressing that they had met their Lord who was pleased with them, offering the community consolation amid their grief.
story: Anas stated, "And we would recite Qur'an verses too, but these were later rescinded. These words were, 'Inform our people about us - that we have met our Lord. And He is pleased with us and has pleased us.'"
lessons: Even in martyrdom there is a message of hope — those who die in service of what is right are not lost but received; this truth is a balm for those who mourn them.
Divine pleasure is the ultimate destination of a life lived in service and sacrifice — the martyrs' final message was not one of regret but of fulfillment.

The temporary nature of these verses teaches that revelation responded to human need in real time — God was attentive to the grief of the community and offered comfort in a form they could hear and hold.
practical_advice: When you grieve someone you have lost, allow yourself to hold onto the possibility that their suffering has ended and they are at peace — this is not denial but a profound form of hope.
Reflect on whether the life you are living right now is one that, at its end, you could describe as a meeting with your Lord with mutual pleasure — let that reflection guide your daily choices.
Share words of comfort with those who are grieving, as the martyrs' message was specifically meant to reach and reassure their people — your presence and words matter to those in mourning.

title: Haram ibn Milhan and the Mission That Led to Bi'r Ma'una: 'Amir ibn al-Tufayl's Three Demands
summary: The Prophet sent Haram ibn Milhan, the brother of Umm Sulaym, leading 70 men on a mission; meanwhile the pagan chief 'Amir ibn al-Tufayl had made three arrogant demands to the Prophet and threatened war, setting the stage for the tragedy at Bi'r Ma'una.
story: Al-Bukhari went on, "Musa b. Isma'il related to us, quoting Hammam, from Ishaq b. 'Abd Allah b. Abu Talha, quoting Anas b. Malik, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out Haram, the brother of Umm Sulaym, in charge of a party of 70 mounted men. The pagan chief 'Amir b. al-Tufayl had proposed three choices to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He had told him, 'You can have control over the bedouin, while I will control the townspeople. Or I could be appointed as your successor. Otherwise, I will do battle with you at the head of 2,000 men of Banu Ghatafan.' "But 'Amir contracted typhoid at the home of Umm So-and-so. He then said, 'Shall I suffer from a swelling like that of a she-camel in the home of a woman from the So-and-so family? Bring me my horse.' And subsequently he died on the back of his horse."
lessons: Those who demand power and issue ultimatums from a place of arrogance often meet their end at the height of their hubris — 'Amir's death in the midst of his threats is a reminder that pride has consequences.

The Prophet's decision to send helpers in good faith, even to hostile territories, reflects a commitment to outreach over isolation — generosity of spirit does not always protect against betrayal, but it remains the right posture.
Human plans and ultimatums, no matter how forceful, are subject to a higher will — 'Amir assembled threats against an entire community and was stopped not by armies but by illness.
practical_advice: When someone presents you with aggressive demands designed to intimidate, remember that the loudest threats are often the most fragile — respond with steadiness rather than panic.
Before sending yourself or others into a situation of potential hostility, assess the signs carefully; good intentions must be paired with wisdom and situational awareness.

Reflect on times when your own plans or ultimatums were disrupted by circumstances beyond your control — humility before the unpredictability of life is a protection against the arrogance that brought 'Amir low.

title: The Massacre at Bi'r Ma'una: Betrayal and Martyrdom of the Muslim Envoys
summary: A group of 40 elite Muslim companions sent to Najd at the request of Abu Bara' were ambushed and massacred at the well of Bi'r Ma'una after 'Amir b. al-Tufayl broke Abu Bara's pledge of protection and incited surrounding tribes to attack them.
story: Aba Bar2 'Amir b. Malik b. Ja'far, (known as) mula%bal-asmna, "He who Toys with Spears", came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina, who explained Islam to him and invited him to accept it. But Abn Bars' neither did so nor refused, hut said, "0Muhammad, if you were to despatch some of your Companions to the people of Najd, inviting them to join you, I would hope they would respond positively to you." But the Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "But I would fear for them from the people of Najd." Aha Bar2 replied that he would give them protection. And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) despatched al-Mundhir b. 'Am, brother of Bana Sa4da, (known as) al-muhrq 11yamtit, "Eager for Death", with a party of 40 of his companions, some of the best of the Muslims. They included al-Haith b. al-Simma, Har%n b. Miban, brother of Bana 'Adi h. al-Najjar, Wrwa h. Asm2 b. al-Salt al-Sulami, Nstic b. Budayl b. Warqa' al-KhuaCi,'Amu b. Fuhayra, the freed-man of Ahii Bakr, along with other warriors of the Muslim elite. They travelled as far as the well at Ma'iina, between the territory of Bann 'Amir and the h a m , "the lava field", of Bann Sulaym, where they halted. Having made camp, they sent out Haram b. Millpln with a letter from the Messenger of God (SAAS), addressed to 'Amir h. al-Tufayl. When H a r m reached him, 'Amir did not look at the document hut attacked and killed the man. He then invited Bana 'Amir to attack, but they refused to do as he wanted, responding, "We'll not betray Aba Bars'; he has given them a pledge and his protection." 'Amir b. al-Tufayl then invited the tribes of Bana Sulaym, Wgayya, Ri'l, Dhakwan and al*ra, and they agreed to attack the Muslims. They came out against them by surprising and surrounding them while the Muslims were with their baggage. When they saw the enemy attacking, they drew their swords and fought them until all the Muslims were killed, except for Ka'b h. Zayd, brother of Banii Dinsr b. al-Najjs. The left him there barely alive; he was picked up from among the dead and lived on until he was killed at the battle of al-khandaq.
lessons: Treachery and the breaking of solemn pledges is among the gravest moral failures, and history records it with shame while honoring those betrayed.
The martyrs of Bi'r Ma'una demonstrate that dying in sincere service to a noble cause is not defeat — their deaths were met with divine recognition and honor.
Trust in God does not mean naivety; the Prophet's initial hesitation to send companions into danger shows that prudence and caution are also part of faith.
practical_advice: When you have been betrayed by someone you trusted, allow yourself to grieve the breach without letting bitterness define your character — those who kept their honor, like Banu 'Amir who refused to betray the pledge, are the moral example to follow.
When facing outcomes you feared and tried to prevent, remind yourself that exercising wise judgment beforehand is your responsibility; the outcome belongs to God.
In moments of loss caused by others' wrongdoing, focus on the integrity and courage of those who were harmed rather than allowing the wrongdoer's actions to eclipse their memory and sacrifice.

title: Haram b. Milhan Delivers the Prophet's Letter and Is Martyred
summary: Haram b. Milhan, uncle of Anas b. Malik, bravely advanced alone to deliver the Prophet's letter to 'Amir b. al-Tufayl and was stabbed from behind, dying with the words 'I have succeeded, by the Lord of the Ka'ba!' on his lips.
story: Haram, Umm Sulaym's brother, along with one man who was lame and another man of Bann So-and-So then went further on. Haram told his companions, 'Stay close by me until 1get near them; if they are peaceful, you'll he right there, hut if they kill me, then you can go back to your comrades.' Haram then went out to them and asked them, 'Will you let me deliver in peace a message to you from the Messenger of God (SAAS)?' He then began addressing them, hut they signalled to a man who came up to him from behind and stabbed him. Haram cried out, 'God is very great! I have succeeded, by the Lord of the ka'ba!' The tribesmen then attacked and killed all Haram's companions except for the man who was lame, and escaped up into the mountain. It was then that God revealed to us the verses, later rescinded, 'We have met our Lord. And He is pleased with us and has pleased us.' Each morning for the following 30 days the Prophet (SAAS) spoke prayers against Ri'l, Dhakwm, Bana Lihyan and 'U~ayya, who had disobeyed God and His messenger. Al-Bukhzri also said that Hibbm related to hi, quoting 'Abd All&, who said that Macmar informed him as follows, 'Thmmna b. 'Abd All& b. Anas related to me that he heard Anas b. Malik say, "When Ha%n b. Milhm - who was Anas's uncle - was stabbed at al-Ma'iina, he said, 'Thus I do with my blood!' And he began wiping it all over his face and head. Then he said, 'I have succeeded, by the Lord of the ka'ba!"'
lessons: True courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to advance despite it, trusting that fulfilling one's duty is its own reward regardless of outcome.
The final words and actions of Haram b. Milhan — smearing his own blood on his face and declaring success — show that a person of faith can transform the moment of death into a moment of triumph.
God honored these martyrs by revealing Quranic verses on their behalf, showing that no sincere sacrifice goes unwitnessed or unacknowledged by the Divine.
practical_advice: When you are asked to do something frightening for a righteous cause, take the step forward as Haram did — make your intention clear, do your part, and leave the result to God.
In your darkest moments of loss, look for the spiritual meaning embedded in the event; the martyrs themselves declared joy at the moment of their greatest trial.
When you lose someone who died with dignity and faith, honor their memory by focusing on how they lived and the values they embodied, not only on the grief of their absence.

title: The Miraculous Ascent of 'Amir b. Fuhayra After Martyrdom
summary: After being killed at Bi'r Ma'una, 'Amir b. Fuhayra — the freed slave of Abu Bakr — was witnessed being raised up into the sky by his killer, and his body was never found, with accounts saying the angels took it.
story: Al-Bukhzri also recounted, from Whayd b. Isma'il, from Aba Usama, from Hisham b. Wrwa, who said, "My father told me as follows, 'When the men were killed at Bi'r Macana and 'Amr b. Umayya al-Damri was taken prisoner, 'Amir b. al-Tufayl asked the father, pointing to a corpse, "Who is this?" 'Amr replied, "This was 'Amir b. Fuhayra. And after he was killed, I saw hi raised up into the sky until I could actually see sky between him and the earth. He was then brought back down."' When news of them reached the Prophet (SAAS), he announced their death, saying, "Your comrades have been killed; they asked their Lord, '0 God, inform our brothers about us, and of how we are pleased with You and You are pleased with us.'" And so it was that He had informed them (the Prophet (SAAS) and his Companions) about them. On that day Wrwa b. Asm2 b. al-Salt, who was among them, was killed. And Wrwa (b. al-Zubayrz) was named after him, while Mundhir (b. al-Zubayrz) was named after Mundhir b. 'Amr (also killed that day)." In the Maghai, the military chronicle, of Masa b. 'Uqba, Wrwa is quoted as saying, 'The body of CAmir b. Fuhayra was not found; it is said that the angels hid it.' Al-Waqidi recounted it from Mus'ab b. Thabit, Aba al-Aswad, from Wrwa. He related the story of 'Amir b. Fuhayra, and how 'Amir b. al-Tufayl reported how he was raised up to heaven. His account tells that the person who killed hi was Jabbar b. Salma al-Kilahi. He stated, 'And when the man pierced him with the spear, he exclaimed, "I have succeeded, by the Lord of the kacba!"' Then Jabbzr asked, 'What did he mean when he said, "I have succeeded?"' People replied, 'He meant with paradise.' And so he then said, 'He was right, by God!' Thereafter Jabbzr accepted Islam, for that reason.
lessons: God can transform the moment of a believer's death into a sign so powerful it changes the heart of the very person who caused that death, as happened when 'Amir's killer Jabbar accepted Islam.
The honor given to 'Amir b. Fuhayra — once an enslaved man, now carried by angels — is a reminder that divine worth is measured by faith and sincerity, not by social status.
The martyrs' prayer that God inform their brothers of their contentment shows that concern for the community and for those left behind is a mark of the highest character.
practical_advice: When you feel that your struggles and sacrifices go unnoticed by those around you, remember that what is invisible to people may be fully seen and honored by God.

If you have ever caused harm to others and feel the weight of it, know that transformation is always possible — even Jabbar, who killed a companion of the Prophet, found his way to faith through witnessing sincerity.

When grieving someone whose loss feels particularly unjust, reflect on the spiritual legacy they left behind — 'Amir b. Fuhayra was remembered so deeply that families named children after those who fell with him.

title: 'Amr b. Umayya Mistakenly Kills Two Men Under the Prophet's Protection
summary: After surviving the massacre at Bi'r Ma'una, 'Amr b. Umayya unknowingly killed two men of Banu 'Amir who held a peace pact with the Prophet, believing he was taking revenge, leading the Prophet to declare he must pay blood money for them.
story: 'Amr h. Umayya al-pamri and another Muslim, one of the an@, of Banii 'Amr b. 'Awf, were out grazing the camels and did not know of the death of their comrades until they saw vultures circling over their camp. They assumed something was wrong when they saw the birds and when they came in to see, they found them lying in their blood while the horsemen who had killed them were still there. The man of the an:ar asked 'Amr b. Umayya what they should do; 'Amr suggested they return to the Messenger of God (SAAS), to give him the news. But the ansiW could not bring himself to leave the place where al-Mundhii h. 'Am had heeli killed, and he also did not want to be the one to inform the others of the had news. He therefore fought the enemy until he was killed. 'Amr was taken prisoner, hut when he told them he was of Banii Mudar, 'Amir b. al-Tufayl released him, after cutting off his forelock. It is claimed that he let him go because of a pledge his mother had made. 'Amr then left, travelling on till he reached al-Qarqara, before Qanat; there he was met by two men of Bann 'Amir, who made a halt at the shady place where he had stopped. The two 'Amir men had made a pact of peace and protection with the Messenger of God (SAAS), of which 'Amr h. Umayya was unaware. When the two men dismounted, 'Amr asked them who they were and they replied that they were of Bann 'Amir. He left them alone until they were asleep and then he attacked and kidled them, believing that by doing so he was takmg revenge against their tribe for the attack they had made against the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS). When 'Amr h. Umayya reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), however, and told him the news, the latter told hi, "You have killed two men for whom I must pay the bloodwit." He then went on to comment, "This must he the work of Abn Barn); I was reluctant and apprehensive about this."
lessons: Acting on incomplete information, even with the sincerest of intentions, can lead to serious harm — 'Amr's revenge was misdirected because he did not know about the peace treaty those men held.
The Prophet's response — accepting accountability for the bloodwit without condemning 'Amr's intention — demonstrates that justice and compassion can coexist in addressing mistakes.
Grief and trauma can impair judgment; 'Amr acted out of raw pain and anger after witnessing the massacre of his companions, which is a deeply human response that still requires correction.
practical_advice: Before acting on anger or grief, pause to ask whether you have all the information you need — emotions are valid, but decisions made in their grip can cause unintended harm that compounds your burden.
If you have acted in error while trying to do right, do not let guilt paralyze you; acknowledge the mistake honestly as 'Amr did, accept the consequences, and move forward.
When someone you care about makes a well-intentioned mistake that causes harm, respond as the Prophet did — address the consequence clearly and justly without crushing the person's spirit.

title: Abu Bara's Grief Over 'Amir b. al-Tufayl's Betrayal
summary: When Abu Bara' learned that his nephew 'Amir b. al-Tufayl had broken his pledge of protection and caused the massacre of the Prophet's companions, he was deeply grieved and his son Rabi'a took revenge by wounding 'Amir.
story: When news of it reached Aba Bar%),he was very upset at how 'Amir b. al-Tufayl had betrayed him, and at what had befallen the men of the Messenger of God (SAAS) because of hi and while under his own protection. Hassan h. Thahit spoke the following verses about 'Amir's betrayal of Ahn Barn) and to incite the latter's tribe against 'Amir, "0tribe of Umm al-Banin, does it not distress you, fine men of Najd, That 'Amir has treated Aba B d ' with scorn in betraying him? And making a mistake is not the same as acting deliberately. Won't you ask Rabi'a, a man of deeds, 'What things have you accomplished after I left you? Your father, Abii Bars", is a man of war, while your uncle Hakam b. Sa'd is a nobleman.'" Rabi'a b. 'Amir h. Mdik attacked 'Amir h. al-Tufayl and pierced him in the thigh, but not fatally, though he did fall from his horse. 'Amir then said, 'This is the work of Abii Bar%). If I die, I award my blood to my uncle, and he is not to he pursued for it (in revenge). If I live, I will later decide what to do.'
lessons: Abu Bara's anguish shows that being associated with wrongdoing through a bond of kinship or trust — even when you did not commit it yourself — carries a profound moral weight.
There is an important distinction between a deliberate betrayal and an honest mistake, a distinction Hassan b. Thabit's poetry explicitly draws — intention matters in moral accountability.

Even in the culture of pre-Islamic Arabia, a man of honor like Abu Bara' was expected to feel shame and take action when someone under his name caused harm, illustrating that moral responsibility extends to those who vouch for others.
practical_advice: If someone you vouched for or trusted causes harm to others, resist the urge to minimize your role — honest acknowledgment of your connection to the outcome, as Abu Bara' demonstrated, is the beginning of accountability.
When you feel the weight of shame for what someone close to you has done, remember that your grief itself is evidence of your own integrity — you are not your relative's or colleague's worst actions.
Channel feelings of helpless anger at injustice into whatever constructive action is available to you, as Rabi'a did — taking a stand against wrongdoing, even imperfectly, matters.

title: The Prophet's Thirty Days of Supplication Against Those Who Killed His Companions
summary: For thirty consecutive mornings after the massacre at Bi'r Ma'una, the Prophet Muhammad invoked God against the tribes of Ri'l, Dhakwan, Banu Lihyan, and 'Usayya, who had disobeyed God and His Messenger.
story: It was then that God revealed to us the verses, later rescinded, 'We have met our Lord. And He is pleased with us and has pleased us.' Each morning for the following 30 days the Prophet (SAAS) spoke prayers against Ri'l, Dhakwm, Bana Lihyan and 'U~ayya, who had disobeyed God and His messenger.
lessons: The Prophet's sustained daily supplication shows that turning pain and injustice over to God through sincere prayer is not passive resignation but an active, dignified response to oppression.
Grief and righteous anger, when channeled through prayer rather than vengeance, preserve both the soul of the one who was wronged and the moral integrity of the community.
The fact that God temporarily revealed verses honoring the martyrs' own words shows divine responsiveness to sincere suffering — heaven listens to those who cry out in faith.
practical_advice: When you have experienced a profound injustice and feel powerless, establish a consistent daily practice of prayer or supplication for a set period — the discipline of returning to it each day is itself a form of healing.
Do not suppress righteous anger; instead, give it a sacred outlet through prayer, journaling, or other structured expression, rather than letting it fester or explode outward destructively.
Allow yourself a defined season of mourning and intercession — the Prophet's thirty days of supplication model the wisdom of fully honoring grief before moving forward.

title: The Poem of Lamentation for Those Killed at Ma'una
summary: A poem lamenting the companions killed at Bi'r Ma'una, mourning the loss of brave men who were betrayed by those who broke their pledges.
story: "For those killed at Ma'iina weep endless, copious tears. For the Prophet's cavalry who met their death, (God's) fate. Annihilation struck them because of men who broke their pledge in treachery. Alas for Mundhir who passed away, who accepted his death with willing stoicism! What a lot of fme men of 'Amr's people died then!"
lessons: Grief and lamentation for those lost to treachery is a natural and honourable response, not a sign of weakness.
Those who die due to the betrayal of others are remembered with honour, as their courage and commitment remain untarnished.
Stoicism in the face of death, as shown by Mundhir, reflects the highest form of trust in God's decree.
practical_advice: When you grieve a loss caused by someone else's wrongdoing, allow yourself to feel and express that grief — it is part of healing.
Honour the memory of those who suffered unjustly by reflecting on their patience and courage rather than only the pain of their loss.
When you feel overwhelmed by a tragedy you could not prevent, remind yourself that God's decree encompasses all things, and trust that nothing is wasted in His plan.

title: The Expedition Against Banu al-Nadir: The Assassination Plot
summary: The Prophet Muhammad visited Banu al-Nadir to seek their help in paying bloodwit, but they secretly plotted to drop a rock on him from a rooftop. He was divinely warned and departed safely.
story: Ibn Ishnq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went to Bann al-Nadir to ask their help in paying for the bloodwit for the two men of Bann 'Amir who had been killed by 'Amr b. Umayya; this was because of the pact of protection he had given the two men. There was, moreover, a pact and a treaty between Bann &-Nadir and Bana 'Amir. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to them, they replied, 'Yes, Abii al-Qsim, we'll help you as you wish.' "But then some of them met secretly and concluded that they would never have a better opportunity to attack the Messenger of God (SAAS), since he was seated at the wall of one of their houses. They asked for a volunteer to climb up on to the house to drop a rock down on him so that they would be rid of him. "'Amr b. J&ash b. Ka'b volunteered to do this and climbed up to drop the rock as suggested. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had been accompanied by his Companions, who included Abii Bakr, Wmar and 'Ali. He received information from heaven about what the enemy intended, so he got up and set off back to Medina. When his Companions tired of waiting for him, they set about looking for h i and met a man arriving from Medina. When they asked h i about the Prophet (SAAS), he replied, 'I saw h i entering Medina.' The Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) then went to him and told him of the treachery Bann al-Nadir had planned against him."
lessons: Divine protection operates in ways beyond human expectation — safety can come through knowledge, warning, and quiet departure rather than confrontation.
Treachery often hides behind a facade of friendliness and agreement; discernment and trust in God are essential companions.
When danger is averted through God's mercy, it is an occasion for gratitude and reflection, not pride or vengeance.
practical_advice: When you sense that someone's words and intentions do not align, trust your instincts and seek guidance rather than ignoring the signs.
If you find yourself in a dangerous situation, sometimes the wisest course is a calm, quiet withdrawal rather than direct confrontation.
Reflect on moments in your life when you were protected from harm you did not even see coming, and let that build your trust in God's watchfulness over you.

title: Banu al-Nadir Defy the Prophet and Are Besieged
summary: After the assassination plot was revealed, the Prophet demanded Banu al-Nadir leave Medina. Encouraged by the hypocrites' false promises of support, they refused and were subsequently besieged.
story: Al-Wsqidi stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent forth Muhammad b. Maslama to tell them (Banii &Nadir) to leave his territory and protection. But the hypocrites sent messages to them giving them their support, urging them to stay and promising them aid. This encouraged Banii al-Nadir, and Huyayy b. Akhmb became adamant. They then sent word to the Messenger of God (SAAS) that they would not leave and warned him against breaking the agreements. "It was then that the Prophet (SAAS) sent his forces out against them." Al-Waqidi continued, "They kept them besieged for 15 days." Ibn Ishnq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered that preparations be made to go forth to war against them." Ibn Hisham stated, "He placed Ibn Umm Makmm in command of Medina; this took place in Rabi' al-Awwal." Ibn Ishaq went on, "He journeyed forth and besieged them for six days. It was then that the prohibition of wine came down. The enemy set up defences in their forts and the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered that their date-palms be cut down and burned. They appealed to him, saying, 'Muhammad, you used to forbid causing destruction and criticized those who did so. Why are you cutting down and burning our date-palms?'"
lessons: False promises from unreliable allies lead to poor decisions and ultimately greater loss — the counsel we accept shapes our fate.
Standing firm against wrongdoing sometimes requires decisive and difficult action, even when the other party protests.
Those who rely on hollow support will eventually find themselves abandoned when their situation becomes truly critical.
practical_advice: Before holding firm in a conflict based on others' promised support, honestly assess whether those people have the ability and commitment to follow through.
When someone who has betrayed your trust appeals to past norms and agreements, respond with justice but do not be manipulated by selective appeals to principle.
Reflect on whether the support you are counting on in a difficult situation is real and reliable, or whether it is encouraging you to take risks you cannot sustain alone.

title: The Hypocrites Abandon Banu al-Nadir
summary: The hypocrites led by Abdullah ibn Ubayy had promised to support Banu al-Nadir but abandoned them entirely when the siege began, leaving them to face their situation alone.
story: "A group of Banii 'Awf b. al-Khazraj, including 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy, Wadi'a, Mdik, Suwayd and Dacis, bad advised Banii al-Nadir as follows: 'If you stay fum and resist, we will not give up on you. If you fight, we'll fight along with you, and if you are expelled, we will leave with you.' And so Banti al-Nadir waited for their help, but they did nothing. And so God cast fear into Banii &Nadir, and they asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) to evacuate them and to spare their blood, allowing them to depart with whatever of their belongings they could transport, except for their amour."
lessons: Those who make grand promises in times of ease often vanish in times of hardship — true allies are proven by action, not words.
When human support fails, turning to God is not a last resort but the most reliable refuge from the very beginning.
The collapse of false alliances, while painful, can be the moment of clarity that leads to honest decisions and a new path forward.
practical_advice: When you feel abandoned by people you counted on, resist the urge to collapse into bitterness — instead use the clarity of that moment to reassess who your true supporters are.
Do not delay making a necessary decision simply because you are waiting for support that may never come; act based on what is real and present.
Whenever you make a commitment to support someone, honour it — and be cautious about making promises under the emotion of solidarity that you may not be able to keep.

title: The Exile of Banu al-Nadir from Medina
summary: Having been besieged and abandoned by the hypocrites, Banu al-Nadir agreed to leave Medina, loading their belongings on camels and departing to Khaybar and Syria in a dramatic procession.
story: Ibn Ishnq stated, "They therefore loaded their belongings on their camels to the extent they could bear. Some of their men tore down their houses and removed the door lintels which they mounted on the backs of their camels and took away. Some of them left for Khaybar, while some went to Syria. Among their leaders who went to Khaybar were S a l h n b. Ahn al-Huqayq, Kinana b. al-Rabic b. Abn al-Huqayq, and Huyayy b. Akbab. When they arrived there the people of Khaybar accepted their control. "'Abd Allah b. Abn Bakr related to me that it was related to him that they were received by women and young men and gifts, with tambourines, flutes and girls walking behind them singing boldly and with pride; nothing similar is related of any hibe of people in their era. "They left their properties for the Messenger of God (SAAS); these consisted of their palm trees and their farms. These became his personal property to dispose of as he wished. He divided them up among those who fvst migrated with h i , to the exclusion of the aqar, except for Sahl b. Hanif and Aba Dujnna. These both complained of poverty and so he gave to them."
lessons: Even in the midst of loss and displacement, people find ways to preserve dignity — how we carry ourselves in defeat reflects our inner character.
The redistribution of wealth to those in genuine need reflects a principle of justice that transforms loss into provision for others.
Exile and loss of home, however painful, can be the beginning of a new chapter rather than the end of a story.
practical_advice: When you are forced to leave behind something important — a home, a relationship, a chapter of life — focus on what you can carry forward rather than only what you must leave.
If you are going through a painful transition, notice how you present yourself to the world; maintaining dignity during difficulty is itself a form of strength.
Consider whether there are people in your community who are in genuine need and whether resources you have access to could be directed toward them.

title: Two Men of Banu al-Nadir Accept Islam and Retain Their Property
summary: While most of Banu al-Nadir went into exile, two of their men accepted Islam and were permitted to keep their properties as a result of their conversion.
story: Ibn Ishnq went on, "Only two men of Bann al-Nadir accepted Islam: Yzmin b. Wmayr b. Ka'b, the cousin of 'Amr b. Ja&sh, and Abn Sa'd b. Wahh; these men retained their properties. "A member of Y b i n ' s family related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked Y-in, 'Did you see how your cousin treated me, and what he intended to do to me?' Y m n therefore gave a fee to a man to ki 'Amr b. Ja$%h, God damn him, and he did so."
lessons: The choice to align oneself with truth, even when it means departing from one's own community, is among the most consequential and courageous decisions a person can make.
Conversion and sincerity of heart can transform one's standing entirely — what was at risk becomes preserved through a change of heart.
The weight of a relative's wrongdoing can fall on an innocent person; how one responds to that burden reveals true character.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a group or community whose direction conflicts with your values, remember that choosing your own integrity over group loyalty is always possible, even if difficult.
If someone close to you has harmed others, consider what responsibility, if any, you bear and what honest action looks like in response.
Moments of moral clarity — where truth becomes undeniable — are gifts; do not let fear of social cost cause you to ignore them.

title: The Revelation of Surah al-Hashr Concerning Banu al-Nadir
summary: God revealed Surah al-Hashr in its entirety about the expedition against Banu al-Nadir, explaining the divine wisdom behind their expulsion, the cutting of their palms, and the fear cast into their hearts.
story: Ibn Ishnq continued, "God revealed siirat al-Hashr in its entirety about them, telling in it of the vengeance he took on them, how He empowered His messenger over them and what He did to them." God Almighty stated, "All in the heavens and all on earth declare God's glory; He is the Almighty and All-wise. He it is who drew forth from their homes for the first deportation those people of the scriptures who disbelieved. You did not think they would leave; and they thought that their forts would protect them from God. But God came at them from where they did not expect, casting fear into them. They demolished their houses with their own hands and with those of the believers. Consider this, you who have eyes! Had God not decreed for them that they should leave, He would have punished them severely on earth; and in the hereafter, they shall have the torture of hellfire. That is because they opposed God and His messenger. And for those who oppose God, God shall be severe in His punishment. Whatever palm trees you might cut down or leave standing over their roots shall be by God's permission, so that He may humiliate the evil-doers" (swat al-Hashr; LIX, v.1-5). God Almighty, may He be praised, declared His own glory and related how all creatures, high and low, glorify Him. He stated that He is All-Powerful, and unassailable, His greatness and majesty insurmountable, and that He is All-Wise in all He has created and in all He has planned and purposed. It is from all this that stem the empowerment, management and effectiveness (He gave) to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and to the believers who serve Him, in their victory over their enemies the Jews who disobeyed God and His messenger and opposed His messenger and His law, all of which was the cause leading to conflict with them, as told above, until he with (God's) support encompassed them with fear and dread for a month. In addition, he imprisoned them by besieging them with his forces and his own noble self for six days. Their terror put them to total confusion, so that they ultimately co-operated and made peace to spare their blood, removing of their property what their mounts would carry. This was conditional upon their not bearing away any of their weapons, a shameful and humiliating circumstance. They proceeded, moreover, to demolish their homes, by their own hands and by those of the believers. Consider this, you who have eyes to see! The Almighty then stated that if they had not suffered withdrawal and the remove into exile from the protection of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and from Medina, then He would have afflicted them with far worse punishment. here on earth, namely execution, along with what He had in store for them in the after-life, namely the painful punishment He has decreed for them. The Almighty went on to refer to the wisdom that was evident in the burning of their palm trees and their abandonment of the rest of their property, all of this being palatable. He stated, "Whatever palm trees you might cut down ..." The word used here, lina, 'Lpalmtrees", refers to palms giving much fruit. And He went on, "... or leave standing over their roots shall be by God's permission."
lessons: Divine revelation provides meaning and context for difficult events that might otherwise seem arbitrary or unjust — nothing happens outside of God's knowledge and purpose.
The reminder to 'consider this, you who have eyes' is an invitation to move from passive observation to active reflection on the lessons embedded in events.
What appears to be a merely political or military event is often, at a deeper level, a spiritual lesson about the consequences of opposing truth and breaking covenants.
practical_advice: When you are trying to make sense of a painful or confusing event in your life, seek out wisdom — in scripture, in the counsel of the wise — that can help you find meaning in what happened.
Train yourself to look for lessons in the events around you, not only in your own life but in history and in the experiences of others.
When you face outcomes that feel harsh or difficult, reflect on whether there were choices or directions that led to that point, and what a different path might look like going forward.

title: Burning and Cutting of Banu al-Nadir's Date-Palms at al-Buwayra
summary: The Prophet Muhammad ordered the burning and cutting of the date-palms of Banu al-Nadir at al-Buwayra, an act that was divinely sanctioned and referenced in Quranic revelation as a means of humiliating the treacherous disbelievers.
story: Whatever palm trees you might cut down ... The word used here, lina, 'palm trees', refers to palms giving much fruit. And He went on, '... or leave standing over their roots shall be by God's permission.' (This implies) the following, that all of this was permitted by law and by authority, and that they should not object to it but (recognize) that it was all for their own good, not some iniquity, as some evil-minded men have said. It was, on the contrary, an expression of power, and purposed the disgrace of the shameless unbelievers. Al-Bukhari and Muslim both related from Qutayba, from al-Layth, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) burned and cut down the date-palms of Banu al-Nadir, this being at al-Buwayra, and so God revealed, 'Whatever palm trees you might cut down or leave standing over their roots shall be by God's permission, and so that He may humiliate the evil-doers' (surat al-Hashr; LIX, v.5). Al-Bukhari quotes from Juwayriyya b. Asma: from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) cut down and burned the date-palms of Banu al-Nadir at al-Buwayra and that on that occasion Hassan b. Thabit spoke the verses, 'It left unmoved the nobles of Banu Lu'ayy that there was a terrible fire at al-Buwayra.' In response to this Abu Sufyan b. al-Harith composed the following, 'May God bless that action and set aflame all its (Medina's) parts. You will learn which of us it is who gains thereby, and which of our lands it will harm.'
lessons: Divine permission can sanctify difficult actions that may appear harsh on the surface but serve a greater purpose of justice.
Actions taken in obedience to God are not oppression, even when they involve real loss for the other party; context and intention matter deeply.
Treachery and broken covenants carry serious consequences, reminding us that integrity in our commitments is foundational to communal trust.
practical_advice: When facing criticism for difficult but principled decisions, seek clarity from a higher moral framework rather than reacting defensively to those who mischaracterize your actions.
In times of conflict, consult trusted guidance and scripture to distinguish between righteous firmness and needless harm.
Reflect on how your own commitments and covenants are maintained, and strengthen your integrity so that your word is one others can rely upon.

title: Hassan b. Thabit and Abu Sufyan b. al-Harith Exchange Verses on the Burning of al-Buwayra
summary: The burning of Banu al-Nadir's date-palms at al-Buwayra inspired an exchange of poetic verses between the Muslim poet Hassan b. Thabit and the opposing poet Abu Sufyan b. al-Harith, reflecting the emotional and ideological tensions of the conflict.
story: Al-Bukhari quotes from Juwayriyya b. Asma: from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) cut down and burned the date-palms of Banu al-Nadir at al-Buwayra and that on that occasion Hassan b. Thabit spoke the verses, 'It left unmoved the nobles of Banu Lu'ayy that there was a terrible fire at al-Buwayra.' In response to this Abu Sufyan b. al-Harith composed the following, 'May God bless that action and set aflame all its (Medina's) parts. You will learn which of us it is who gains thereby, and which of our lands it will harm.'
lessons: Words and expression — even in the form of poetry — are powerful tools that reflect the values and convictions people carry in their hearts.
Conflict often produces a battle of narratives; maintaining truthfulness and moral clarity in how we speak about difficult events is essential.
Even in adversarial exchanges, there is dignity in measured, principled response rather than descending into personal attack.
practical_advice: When you feel the urge to respond harshly to criticism, channel that energy into articulating your values clearly rather than simply attacking the other side.
Use creative expression — writing, journaling, or poetry — as an outlet for processing difficult emotions during times of conflict or injustice.
Remind yourself that history and God's judgment are the ultimate arbiters of who was on the right side; you do not need to win every argument in the moment.

title: Ka'b b. Malik's Poem on the Treachery of Banu al-Nadir and the Killing of Ka'b b. al-Ashraf
summary: Ka'b b. Malik composed verses recounting the treachery of Banu al-Nadir's rabbis, the killing of Ka'b b. al-Ashraf, and the Prophet's divinely aided campaign against them, celebrating God's support for the believers and the humiliation of those who rejected truth.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'Ka'b b. Malik recited, in reference to the withdrawal of Banu al-Nadir and the killing of Ka'b b. al-Ashraf, (though) God knows best (whether the verses are authentic), "The rabbis were dishonoured by their treachery; thus it is that the vicissitudes of fate revolve. This was because they disbelieved in a great Lord whose orders are of great import. They were brought understanding and knowledge combined, and the warner came to them from God. A truthful warner, who brought a book and verses that were plain and illuminating. But they said, 'You did not bring truth, and you are more worthy of being denied than are we.' He said, 'Not so; I tell the truth. Those who are understanding and knowledgeable believe me.' Those who follow him will be guided to all that is true; those who disbelieve in him will be shamed for disbelief. When they were made to drink of deceit and disbelief, and rejection of him turned them from the truth God made the Prophet see accurately, and God deals wisely and does not deceive. He aided him and empowered him over them; He was his helper, a fine helper. Of them, Ka'b was left there prostrate, and al-Nadir were humbled after his downfall, There on his two hands; flashing swords in our hands overwhelmed him By God's command, when he sent Ka'b's brother secretly to go by night to Ka'b. He tricked him, defeating him by guile; and Mahmud is brave and trustworthy. Those of Banu al-Nadir were in a state of evil; the Destroyer ruined them for the crimes they committed. That morning when the Messenger of God came slowly towards them, he being well aware of them. Ghassan, the defenders, were helping him against his enemies, as he would aid them. He told them, 'Peace - or woe to you!' But they rejected him, and deceit and corruption allied with them. So they tasted the outcome of their actions in distress, each three of them having one camel. They were expelled and headed for Qaynuqa', abandoning their homes and date-palms."'
lessons: Those who possess knowledge and scripture but reject truth out of pride suffer consequences that serve as a lesson for generations to come.
Divine help arrives for those who remain patient and steadfast in the face of betrayal and enmity.
The outcome of treachery is ultimately humiliation, while those who remain truthful and faithful are ultimately honoured.
practical_advice: When you feel betrayed by those who should have known better, find solace in the understanding that God sees all actions and justice will ultimately be served.
Reflect on moments in your own life where you may have let pride or self-interest cause you to reject good counsel, and work to correct that pattern.
Use the stories of those who chose deception over integrity as motivation to remain honest and transparent in your own dealings, even when it is difficult.

title: Ibn Luqaym al-'Absi's Verses About Banu al-Nadir and the Prophet's Authority
summary: Verses attributed to Ibn Luqaym al-'Absi — or possibly Qays b. Budhr — addressed the exile of Banu al-Nadir and called upon Quraysh to recognize the Prophet Muhammad's divine authority and noble lineage, urging them to submit before suffering further loss.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'The following verses of Ibn Luqaym al-'Absi were also spoken about Banu al-Nadir. It is also said that they were spoken by Qays b. Budhr b. Tarif al-Ashja'i, "May my people be a ransom for that man who will never die who resettled the Jews in territories not their own. They sleep in embers of thorn trees, having exchanged uplands where young date-palms sprouted. If my view about Muhammad is correct, you will see his cavalry between al-Salla and Mt. Yaramyam, Directing them towards 'Amr b. Buhtha. They are an enemy; and an honest tribe is unlike one that is evil. On the horses are heroes as they race on to battle, waving their straight wooden lances. And each of their fine thin-bladed swords inherited from the eras of 'Ad and Jurhum. Who will deliver to Quraysh a message from me? Shall anyone succeed them in being so honoured, For the fact - know this well - that their brother Muhammad, is of the line of those between al-Hajun and Zamzam? If you serve him in truth, you will prosper and you will rise from below to the very heights. He is a prophet, with mercy ordained him by God; do not ask of him anything undisclosed (by God) or improper. By my life, there was a lesson for you, Quraysh, in what happened at Badr, and at the crowded well. That morning he came among the Khazrajis, heading for you in obedience to God, the Powerful and Much-Honoured. Being helped by the holy spirit, striking his enemies, a true messenger with knowledge from the All-Merciful. A messenger from the All-Merciful reciting His Book, not having hesitated. I see his authority rising everywhere, in accord with a command decreed by God."'
lessons: The rise of truth and divine authority is gradual but inevitable; those who align themselves with it early are the ones who truly prosper.
Even adversaries and outside observers sometimes recognize signs of divine favour before those closest to the matter do — humility in seeking truth is essential.
Past defeats and lessons — like Badr — are meant to awaken those still resistant to the truth; ignoring such signs compounds loss.
practical_advice: When you see repeated signs pointing in a clear direction in your life, take them seriously rather than dismissing them out of habit or stubbornness.
Assess your relationships and allegiances honestly: are you aligned with what is truthful and just, or are you holding on out of loyalty to what is familiar but wrong?
Consider what 'lessons' past hardships in your own life were meant to teach you, and reflect on whether you have genuinely learned from them or are at risk of repeating old patterns.

title: The Distribution of Banu al-Nadir's Property as Al-Fay' to the Prophet and the Muslims
summary: After the exile of Banu al-Nadir, God decreed that their properties constituted al-fay' — property acquired without battle — and awarded it to the Prophet, who distributed it according to divine guidance, setting aside his family's yearly expenses and using the rest for the cause of God.
story: God Almighty then went on to refer to the awarding of al-fay' and to the fact that God had judged that the property of Banu al-Nadir should go to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He therefore gave him possession of it and the Messenger of God (SAAS) disposed of it as God Almighty wished. It is similarly established in both Sahih collections, from the Commander of the Believers, 'Umar b. al-Khattab, who said, 'The properties of Banu al-Nadir were awarded by God to His messenger from whatever the Muslim forces, mounted on horse or camel, had not themselves taken.' These were specifically for the Messenger of God (SAAS). He would set aside his family's expenses for a year and then whatever riding beast or weapons remained he would treat as military supplies to be used in the cause of God, the Almighty and Glorious. The Almighty then made clear His judgement about the al-fay', to the effect that it was for the muhajirin and the ansar as well as for their dependants in whatever ways and manner was customary to them. (God stated) '... and for those close in kin, orphans, the needy, wayfarers, so that it not be something taken in turns by those of you who are wealthy. Whatever the Messenger gives you, you should take. And from whatever he forbids you, keep away. And fear God; God is severe in punishing' (surat al-Hashr; LIX, v.7).
lessons: Wealth acquired through God's blessing carries with it a divine responsibility — it is not merely for personal accumulation but for the welfare of the community.
The Prophet's practice of securing only his family's basic needs and directing surplus toward communal benefit is a timeless model of selfless leadership.
Divine guidance on the distribution of resources ensures fairness and prevents wealth from circulating only among the powerful.
practical_advice: Review how you manage financial windfalls or unexpected resources: are you ensuring that those most vulnerable in your community also benefit?
Practice intentional budgeting that separates your genuine needs from wants, and identify a portion of your surplus to direct toward those in need.
Reflect on the Quranic principle that wealth should not circulate only among the wealthy, and consider what structural or personal changes you can make to embody that value.

title: The Story of Umm Ayman and the Date-Palms — A Lesson in Generosity and Persistence
summary: A man came to the Prophet requesting the return of date-palms his family had donated, but Umm Ayman, who had been gifted some of them, fiercely refused to give them up, leading the Prophet to repeatedly offer her substitute compensation until he had given her ten times what she originally owned.
story: Imam Ahmad stated, ''Arim and 'Affan quoted Mu'tamir as having related to them both, "I heard my father say, 'Anas b. Malik related to us, from the Prophet (SAAS), that a man might donate to him his palms, or whatever else God might wish, up to when Qurayza and al-Nadir were conquered for him; thereafter he began giving it back."' Mu'tamir's father went on, 'My family told me to go to the Prophet (SAAS), and to ask him back for all or part of what they had given. But the Prophet (SAAS) had given some of the trees to Umm Ayman - or he had done whatever it was that God had wanted.' He went on, 'So I asked the Prophet (SAAS), and he gave me some of them. Then Umm Ayman came up angrily, pulled my clothing up around my neck and began saying, "No! By the One and Only God, I won't give them to you! He gave them to me!" - or some such comments. The Prophet (SAAS) then said, "But you can have so-and-such." She responded, "No! By God!" He repeated, "Then I'll give you so-and-such." "No! By God!" she replied. "Then I'll give you so-and-such!" he offered until he had given her in full. I think he must have given her ten times as much as she had formerly owned.'
lessons: The Prophet's patience and generosity in resolving disputes — offering more and more until all parties are satisfied — is a model for conflict resolution in personal relationships.
Umm Ayman's fierce insistence on her rights, while bold, resulted in her receiving far more than she originally held, illustrating that knowing and asserting one's rights is not shameful.
The Prophet honoured the trust placed in him by both donors and recipients, showing that true leadership means protecting the interests of the vulnerable, not just the powerful.
practical_advice: When mediating a disagreement between people you care about, look for creative solutions that leave both parties feeling honoured rather than simply splitting the difference.
Do not be embarrassed to assert your rights or to advocate for what you have legitimately been given, especially when that is the honest and just position.
Practice the art of generous resolution: when a conflict arises over resources, consider whether giving more — rather than less — might dissolve the tension entirely and strengthen the relationship.

title: The Hypocrisy of Those Who Promised Help to Banu al-Nadir but Abandoned Them
summary: God revealed verses exposing the hypocrisy of those in Madinah who secretly promised Banu al-Nadir military support if they were expelled or attacked, but ultimately abandoned them entirely when the moment of truth arrived, drawing a comparison between them and Satan.
story: God Almighty then stated that blame was due the hypocrites who were secretly sympathetic towards Banu al-Nadir, as we have mentioned above, promising them victory, though none came. In fact they abandoned them completely, having misled them about themselves. And so God stated, '(Have you not) seen those who were hypocritical, telling their disbelieving brethren from among the people of the scriptures, "If you are driven out, we will go forth with you. We will never obey anyone concerning you. If you are attacked, we will certainly assist you." But God bears witness that they are liars. If they were expelled, these people would not leave with them. If they were attacked, these would not help them. Even if they did help them, they would then turn their backs. Then they will not be helped.' The Almighty condemned them for their cowardice, ignorance and foolishness. He then drew an ugly and awful comparison regarding them and Satan, '... who says to mankind, "Disbelieve!" But when they disbelieve, he (Satan) tells them, "I am innocent of (responsibility for) you! I fear God, the Lord of the worlds." And the outcome for them both is to enter hell-fire, to live therein forever. That is the reward of the unjust' (surat al-Hashr).
lessons: Empty promises made out of self-interest or fear are among the most damaging forms of betrayal, leaving the abandoned party more vulnerable than if no promise had been made.
God sees through the outward declarations of those whose hearts are not aligned with their words; sincerity cannot be permanently disguised.
The comparison of the hypocrites to Satan is a stark warning: those who encourage others toward harm and then abandon them in their hour of need share in the moral responsibility for that harm.
practical_advice: Before making commitments to others in difficult circumstances, honestly assess whether you have both the capability and the genuine intention to follow through — it is better not to promise than to abandon someone at a critical moment.
If you have made promises you now realise you cannot keep, address that directly and honestly with the person rather than allowing them to rely on false hope.
Reflect on whether there are areas in your life where your outward expressions of support to others do not match your actual intentions or actions, and work to bring those into alignment.

title: The Story of 'Amr b. Su'da al-Qurazi and His Plea to Banu Qurayza
summary: After witnessing the abandoned homes of Banu al-Nadir, 'Amr b. Su'da urged Banu Qurayza to follow Prophet Muhammad, citing Jewish scholars' prophecies about him, but was ultimately rejected by their leader Ka'b b. Asad.
story: T h e Story of 'Amr b. Su'da al-Qurqi. He passed by the homes of Banii &Nadir and found them laid to waste and completely abandoned. The fact that Banii &Nadir had been of higher status than (his own) Banii Qurayza stimulated his interest in Islam and he brought forth a description of the Messenger of God (SAAS), from the Old Testament. Al-Wiiqidi quoted Ibmhim b. Jacfar as stating that his father said, "When Banii al-Nadir left Medina, 'Amr b. Su'da arrived and when he viewed their houses and saw their destruction, he went into deep thought. When he returned to Banii Qurayza, he found them assembled in the temple. He blew on their horn and when they had gathered together, al-Zubayr b. Bx@asked him, 'Ahii Sacid, where were you? You've never been away before this.' 'Amr used never to leave the temple and absolutely worshipped Judaism. "He replied, 'Today, I have seen things that should be a lesson for us. I have seen our brothers' houses empty that before were places of honour, comfort, great virtue, prestige and intellectuality. They have abandoned their possessions, given them away to others, and gone into humiliating exile. By the Torah, the fact is that God never before gave such power to a people with whom He was dissatisfied. Previously, he had been in dispute with Ibn d-Ashraf, their respected leader, and then had kept him secure in his house. He was also at odds with their chief, Ibn Sunayna, and with Banii Qaynuq~'. These people, of the greatest prestige among the Jews, he expelled, even though they were possessed of much arms and equipment and support. Then he besieged them, taking prisoner any one of them who emerged. He then addressed them, telling them he would leave them alone if they moved out of Medina. 'My people, you have seen what you have seen! Obey me! Come, let us follow Muhammad! You well know, by God, that he is a prophet; Ibn al-Hayyibxn Abii Wmayr and Ibn Hirzsh foretold us of him, these being the most learned of Jews. They expected him to come and told us to follow him. They came to us from Jerusalem and ordered us to deliver him their greetings. They then died in their faith, and we buried them in this piece of land of ours.' "His people were silent at this, not one of them speaking. He then repeated something similar and warned them of warfare, capture and exile. Al-Zubayr b. BS@ then said, 'By the Torah, I have read the description of him in BZ@'s book, the Torah that was revealed to Moses, though it is not in the works we instituted.' "Ka'b b. Asad then asked him, 'Well, what's preventing you, Abii 'Abd al-Wmsn, from following him?' 'You, Ka'b, are preventing me,' he replied. 'Why so?' Ka'b asked. 'I have never, I swear by the Torah, come between you and him.' "Al-Zubayr insisted, 'On the contrary, you are the custodian of our pacts and pledges; if you followed him, so would we. If you refused, so will we.' "At this 'Amr b. Su'da approached Ka'b and referred to discussion between them on that. Eventually, 'Amr stated, 'I've said all I'm going to about him; I would not feel comfortable being his follower.'"
lessons: Truth can be recognized even by those who ultimately resist it — 'Amr saw the signs clearly but was held back by social pressure, reminding us that peer influence can override personal conviction.
Witnessing the consequences faced by others who chose wrongly is itself a form of divine guidance and a call to reflection.
Stubbornness and loyalty to social structures can prevent a person from embracing what they know to be right, making communal accountability a spiritual responsibility.
practical_advice: When you find yourself knowing the right path but feeling held back by what others will think, write down your reasoning privately and ask yourself: whose approval matters most — God's or those around you?

Reflect on cautionary examples in your own life where you witnessed others suffer consequences of poor choices, and use those observations as motivation to course-correct before facing similar outcomes.
If social pressure is preventing you from doing what you believe is right, seek out at least one trusted person who can support your decision and reduce the weight of going against the crowd.

title: The Expedition Against Banu Lihyan and the Salat al-Khawf at 'Usfan
summary: The Prophet set out with 200 men to avenge the martyrs of Khubayb by attacking Banu Lihyan, taking a deceptive route toward Syria before heading to 'Usfan, where he performed the 'Prayer of Fear' as enemy forces under Khalid b. al-Walid positioned themselves between the Muslims and the qibla.
story: THE EXPEDITION AGAINST BANO LIHYAN DURING WHICH HE PERFORMED, AT 'USFAN, THE SALATAL-KHAWF, THE "PRAYER OF FEAR". Al-Bayhaqi related this in his Dalz1il(The Signs). Ibn Ishaq, so far as I have seen, related it through Hisham, from Ziyad, to the effect that it happened in Jumsds al-Uwlx of the year 2 AH, after the battle of al-khandaq, "the ditch", and after the expedition against Banu Qurayza. This is similar to the account given by al-Bayhaqi. But God knows best. The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated, "Abu 'Abd Allah, the hafiz, related to us, quoting Abu al-'Abbas al-Asamm, quoting Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar and others, as follows: 'After Khubayb and his companions had been struck down, the Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth to revenge them by making a surprise attack against Banu Lihyan. He took the route to Syria to give the impression that he was not attacking them, then headed for their territory. But he found that they had taken the precaution of moving into defensive positions in the mountain heights. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "If we go on down to 'Usfan, Quraysh will think we're going on to Mecca." And so he proceeded on with 200 men and encamped at 'Usfan. He then sent on two horsemen to Kura' al-Ghamim; they then left there.' Abu 'Ayyash al-Zurqi narrated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the salat al-khawf at 'Usfan. Imam Ahmad stated, "'Abd al-Razzaq related to us, quoting al-Thawri, from Mansur, from Mujahid, from Ibn 'Ayyash, who said, 'We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS), at 'Usfan. The unbelievers met us, under the command of Khalid b. al-Walid. They were positioned between us and the qibla. The Messenger of God (SAAS) led the noon prayer and the enemy told themselves, "In the position they were in we could have taken them unawares." They then observed, "They have a prayer approaching that is more dear to them than their children or themselves." "'But between the noon and the early evening prayer Gabriel brought down the verse, "When you are among them and lead the prayer for them" (surat al-Nisa'; IV, v. 102). "'Then time for prayer arrived and the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered his men to take up their weapons. We lined up in two rows behind him. He then performed a rak'a, and we all did the same. After that he stood upright and we all did the same. He then made the prostration with the line next to him, while the others stood guarding them. When these had made their prostrations and stood up again, the others prostrated in their place. Then those others occupied the lines of the former who exchanged places with them. Thereafter, he performed one rak'a and they all did so too, after which he stood up, as did all of them. Following that, the next row prostrated while the others remained standing guarding them. When they sat, the others did so too, then they prostrated. He then delivered the salam greeting to them, after which he left. "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the prayer this way twice - once at 'Usfan and again in the territory of Banu Sulaym.'" Ahmad related this from Ghundar, from Shu'ba, from Mansur in similar terms. Abu Da'ud related it from Sa'id b. Mansur, from Jarir b. 'Abd al-Hamid; al-Nasa'i related it from al-Fallaj, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. 'Abd al-Samad, from Muhammad b. al-Muthanna and Bundar, from Ghundar, from Shu'ba, all three deriving it from Mansur. This chain of authorities is in accord with the criteria of both sahih collections; (yet) neither of them included it. However, Muslim related through Abu Khaythama Zuhayr b. Mu'awiya, from Abu al-Zubayr, from Jabir, who said, "We went forth on an expedition with the Messenger of God (SAAS), against a tribe from Juhaynam. They engaged in a fierce battle. When he (the Prophet (SAAS)) led the noon prayer, the unbelievers said, 'If we had attacked them (then) we would have decimated them!' Gabriel then informed the Messenger of God (SAAS) of this and he said, 'They said, "A prayer is coming for them that is dearer to them than their children."'" The hadith is then recounted in similar words as above. Abu Da'ud al-Tayalisi stated, "Hisham related to us, from Abu al-Zubayr, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the noon prayers among his Companions at Nakhl. The polytheists observed this, then said, "Leave them alone; they have a prayer coming after this one that is dearer to them than their children." 'Gabriel then made a revelation to the Messenger of God (SAAS), informing him of this comment. When, therefore, he led the evening prayer he lined up his men in two rows with himself standing in front of them and the enemy out in front of him. He spoke the words Allahu Akbar! "God is most Great", and all his men said the same. Then they all performed a rak'a. After that those in front performed the prostration, while the rest remained standing. When they brought their heads up, the others made their prostration. Then that group went forward, while the next group stayed back. They all then proclaimed the takbir and performed their bows. Thereafter the ones next to him made their prostrations while the others stood, and when they raised their heads the others prostrated.'" Al-Bukhari testified to this in his sahih collection, giving this account of Hisham from Abu al-Zubayr, from Jabir. Imam Ahmad stated, "'Abd al-Samad related to us, from Sa'id b. 'Ubayd al-Huna'i, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Shaqiq, quoting Abu Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) made a halt between Dajnan and 'Usfan and that the polytheists commented, 'They have a prayer to come that is dearer to them than their sons and daughters - the evening prayer, that is. Prepare to have everyone attack them all together (then).' "But Gabriel came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him to divide up his men into two groups; some would perform the prayer while the others would stand at the ready behind them. He told them to be careful and to have their weapons prepared. The troops would perform a single rak'a each, in groups in sequence with the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he would perform two." Al-Tirmidhi and al-Nasa'i related this from an account of 'Abd al-Samad. Al-Tirmidhi categorized the tradition as hasan sahih, "good and authentic".
lessons: Prayer is so central to the believer's identity that even enemies recognized its supreme importance to Muslims — worship is not abandoned even under the most extreme threat.
Divine guidance responds to human circumstances — when the Muslims faced a dilemma between duty to God and duty to safety, God sent revelation that resolved both without sacrificing either.
Discipline and structure in worship, even when modified for necessity, reflect the deep internalization of faith that no external pressure can extinguish.
practical_advice: When life circumstances make your regular spiritual practices difficult — illness, travel, overwhelming stress — remember that Islam provides flexibility without abandonment; adapt your worship rather than skip it entirely.
In moments of fear or danger, grounding yourself in prayer and remembrance of God can restore your sense of stability and purpose, even when circumstances feel out of your control.

Take inspiration from the companions' prioritization of prayer even on the battlefield: build a non-negotiable daily spiritual anchor into your routine so that it holds firm even when everything around you is chaotic.

title: The Expedition to Dhat al-Riqa'
