summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) led an expedition to Najd against Banu Muharib and Banu Tha'laba of Ghatafan, reaching a place called Nakhl. The two forces came close without engaging in battle, each wary of the other, and the Prophet performed the salat al-khawf (prayer of fear).
story: Ihn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed in Medina following the expedition against Banti al-Nadir for the two months of Rabic al-Awwal and Rabic al-Thni, and also for part of Jum2d2. He then sent forces into Najd against Bana Muhwib and Banii Thaclabaof Ghatafa. He placed Abn Dharr in command of Medina." Ibn H i s h a stated, "It is also said that he appointed Wthman b. 'Affan in command there." Ihn Ishaq went on, "He then went forth as far as Nakhl, this being on the expedition to Dhat al-RiqaC." Ihn Hishim commented, "(It is so named) because they patched up their banners there. Others say it is so named because there was a tree there known as Dhat al-Riqa'." Al-Waqidi stated that it was named after a mountain there that had patches of red, black and white. In the hadith of Aha Mas%it is said that it was so named because of the rags they tied to their feet on account of the excessive heat. Ihn Ishaq stated, "There he encountered a force of Ghatafm. The forces came close together without engaging in battle, each side being wary of the other; then the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the ~alat al-khawf prayer."
lessons: Preparation and caution are virtues — the Prophet organized leadership in Medina before departing, showing that responsibility to those left behind is part of wise leadership.
Even in tense standoffs, restraint can prevent bloodshed; sometimes the greatest strength is choosing not to escalate.
Trust in God does not mean abandoning practical precaution; the Prophet combined reliance on God with careful planning.
practical_advice: When facing a stressful or uncertain situation, take time to put proper support structures in place before proceeding — do not leave responsibilities unattended.
In moments of tension or conflict, consider whether restraint and patience might resolve the situation better than immediate action.
Maintain your spiritual practices — like prayer — even in the most stressful circumstances, as they provide grounding and clarity.

title: The Story of Ghawrath b. al-Harith — The Assassination Attempt Foiled by God
summary: A man named Ghawrath b. al-Harith from Banu Muharib attempted to assassinate the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) by seizing his sword while he slept under a tree, but was unable to strike and ultimately returned the sword, testifying to the Prophet's protection by God.
story:
Regarding this expedition, Ibn Ishxq stated, "'Amr b. Wbayd related to me, from al-Hasan, from Jnbir Ibn 'Abd All&, that a man of Banii Muhiirib named Ghawrath said to his tribe of Ghatafnn and Muhsrib, 'Shall I kill Muhammad for you?' 'Yes indeed! And how will you kill him?' 'I'll assassinate him,' he replied. "So he went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he was seated with his sword in his lap. Ghawrath asked him, 'M&ammad, could I see that sword of yours?' 'Yes.' He took it and began to raise it to strike, but God prevented him. He asked, 'M&ammad, don't you fear me?' 'No,' he replied. 'What should I fear from you?' 'You're not afraid of me even though I have a sword in my hands?' 'No; God is protecting me from you.' "Ghawrath then returned the sword to hi. Thereafter, God, Great and Glorious is He, revealed the verse, '0yon who believe, remember God's favour to you when a people intended to lay hands on you but He turned their hands away from you. Fear God, let believers place their reliance upon God!'"
It is established in both ~ahihcollections from a hadith of al-Zuhri, from Sin= b. Aba Sinm, and Aha Salama from Jnbir, that he accompanied the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the expedition to Najd. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was on the way home, the time for the afternoon rest came when they were in a valley with many thorn trees. The force divided up as the men sought the shade of the trees. The Messenger of God (SAAS) stopped in the shade of a tree on which he hung up his sword. Jshii said, "And so we slept. Then suddenly, the Messenger of God (SAAS) called out to us; we responded and found that there was a bedouin seated near him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'This fellow unsheathed my sword while I was asleep. I awoke to find him brandishing it in his hand. He then asked me, "Who is there to protect you from me?" I replied, "God." He then sheathed the sword and sat down.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not punish the man for having done this."
Muslim also related this, from Abii Bakr b. Aba Shayba, from 'Affan, from Abm, from Y a h y ~b. Aha Kathir, from Aba Salama, from Jabir, who said, "We advanced with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and reached Dhat d-Riqa'. Whenever we came across a shade-giving tree we would reserve it for the Messenger of God (SAAS). (We did so now) and one of the polytheists came over to h i while his sword was hanging on a tree. The man took the sword, unsheathed it, then asked the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Do you fear me?' 'No,' he replied. 'Who is to protect you from me?' 'God protects me from you,' he answered. Then the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) intimidated the man and he sheathed the sword and hung it up again."
Al-Bayhaqi drew his account through Abn 'Awana, from Aba Bishr, from Sulaymm b. Q y s , from Jabir, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) engaged Muharib and Ghatafm in battle at Nakhl. The enemy detected a deficiencey in the Muslim defences, and sent through one of their men, Ghawrath b. d-H&th, who went and stood with his sword right over the head of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Ghawrath asked him, 'Who is there to defend you from me?' 'God,' he replied. And the sword fell from the man's grasp. The Messenger of God (SAAS) picked up the sword and asked h i , 'And who protects you from me?' 'Be compassionate!' 'Do you testify that there is no god but God?' he asked. 'No', Ghawrath replied, 'but I will give you my word that I'll not oppose you nor assist any others who do.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) released him, and when Ghawrath returned to his men, he commented, 'I come to you from the very best of men!'"
lessons: True courage is not the absence of fear but complete trust in God's protection — the Prophet's calm response 'God is protecting me' illustrates that faith can be a source of profound fearlessness.
Mercy and forgiveness, even toward those who intended harm, can transform enemies into witnesses of good character, as Ghawrath himself testified upon returning to his people.
God's protection of the believer can manifest in unexpected ways — sometimes through miraculous intervention, sometimes through the softening of an enemy's heart.
practical_advice: When you feel threatened or vulnerable, return to the remembrance of God as your ultimate protector; this is not passive resignation but an active spiritual anchor.
Practice responding to hostility with calm dignity rather than panic or aggression — the Prophet's composure disarmed his attacker more effectively than any weapon.
When someone who has wronged you shows remorse or offers a gesture of peace, consider extending forgiveness; mercy can open doors that force cannot.

title: The Ansari Guard Who Refused to Interrupt His Prayer Despite Being Shot with Arrows
summary: During the expedition to Dhat al-Riqa, an Ansari companion standing guard was shot three times by a polytheist yet refused to interrupt his Quranic recitation, continuing his prayer until he completed it despite severe wounds.
story:
Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, 'My uncle Sadaqa b. Yasar related to me, from 'Aqayl b. Jabir, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, "We went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the expedition to Dhat al-Riqa' near Nakhl and one of our men killed the wife of a polytheist. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had left to return home, her husband, who had been away, came back. When told the news, he swore he would never rest till he had taken blood revenge against Muhammad's men.

"So he followed the tracks of the Messenger of God (SAAS), who when he had made camp had asked, 'Who will stand guard for us tonight?' A muhajiri and an ansari each volunteered and he told them to take up position at the entrance to the mouth of the valley. The two men were 'Ammar b. Yasir and 'Abbad b. Bishr. When they reached the mouth of the valley, the ansari asked the muhajiri, 'Which part of the watch do you want me to take care of, the first or the last?' 'You take the first watch,' the muhajiri told him and settled down to sleep, while the ansari prayed.

"When the polytheist arrived and saw the watchman's form, he realized that he was on guard and shot an arrow at him which stuck in him. The ansari pulled out the arrow, laid it down and stood his guard, standing there in prayer. The polytheist then shot again and struck him, but the guard again removed it, laid it down and went on praying. A third time the polytheist struck him, and once more he withdrew it and put it down, continuing his prayer with a bow and a prostration. He then aroused his companion, saying, 'Remain seated; I've been hit.'

"At this, his companion arose and when the polytheist saw both, he knew that they were on guard against him, and so he fled.

"When the muhajiri saw that the ansari was bleeding, he exclaimed, 'For God's sake, why did you not awaken me when he first struck you?'

"The ansari replied, 'I was reciting a sura (of the Qur'an) and did not want to break it off before I had completed it. When he continued shooting, I finished the prostration, then awoke you. I swear by God, that unless it meant losing a position the Messenger of God (SAAS) had ordered me to hold, he could have killed me before I would have interrupted a recitation before completing it!'""'
lessons: Deep connection with worship can transcend physical pain and fear, showing that spiritual strength can carry us through moments that seem unbearable.
Fulfilling one's duty and trust — even at great personal cost — reflects a level of integrity and commitment that inspires others for generations.
When we are absorbed in remembrance of God, we access a source of inner calm and fortitude that the world cannot take from us.
practical_advice: When facing anxiety or fear, try grounding yourself in a form of worship or recitation you find meaningful — even a few verses can shift your inner state.
Identify one responsibility or commitment you hold that matters deeply to you, and reflect on how honoring it consistently builds your character over time.
In moments of pain or stress, complete the task or act of worship you began before pausing — finishing what you start trains resilience and focus.

title: The Bird That Bowed to Protect Its Chick — A Lesson on Divine Compassion
summary: During the Dhat al-Riqa expedition, a companion captured a chick and its parent bird threw itself down in distress before the man; the Prophet used this moment to teach that God's compassion for His servants exceeds even a mother bird's love for her chick.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "Jabir b. 'Abd Allah used to say, 'I was once with the Messenger of God (SAAS), when one of his Companions brought in a chick. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was looking at it when in came the chick's parents (or one of them) and threw themselves down before the man who had taken it. I saw that everyone present was amazed at this. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "So you're surprised that this bird, whose chick you took, is bowing to offer itself to gain mercy for its chick? I swear by God, your God is more compassionate for you than this bird is for its chick!"'"
lessons: God's mercy and compassion for His creation surpasses anything we can observe in the natural world, making despair in His mercy unjustifiable.
The natural world is filled with signs of divine love — pausing to observe creation can deepen our awareness of how cared for we truly are.
Even in the midst of difficulty and fear, the Prophet found moments to teach his companions about hope and God's boundless compassion.
practical_advice: When you feel alone or unloved, recall this teaching: if a bird will risk its life for its chick, consider how much more God cares for you — let this thought soften anxiety and loneliness.
Spend a few minutes in nature observing animals or creation around you, and use what you see as a reminder of the mercy and care built into the universe.
Write down or memorize one statement about God's mercy that you can return to in moments of hopelessness or feeling overwhelmed.

title: The Story of Jabir's Weak Camel and the Prophet's Generous Transaction
summary: During the Dhat al-Riqa expedition, the Prophet miraculously revived Jabir's feeble camel, then negotiated to buy it, and ultimately returned it to Jabir along with generous payment — an act of profound personal kindness and care.
story:
Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, "Wahb b. Kaysan related to me, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, 'I went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS), on the expedition to Dhat al-Riqa' at Nakhl, riding a feeble camel. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was returning home, I began falling behind my comrades who went on ahead. Eventually the Messenger of God (SAAS) came up to me and asked, "What's wrong, Jabir?" I replied, "O Messenger of God, this camel of mine is slowing me down."

"Make it kneel," he told me. I did so and he also made his camel kneel. He then said, "Give me that stick in your hand, or cut one from a tree." I did so and he took it and prodded my camel with it several times. He then said, "Mount up!" I did so. And, I swear by Him who sent him with the truth, my weak male camel competed with his female camel!

"When I was talking with the Messenger of God (SAAS), he asked me, "Would you sell me this camel of yours, Jabir?" "I'll give it to you," I told him. "No; just sell it to me." "Make me an offer," I asked. "I'll take it for one dirham," he suggested. "No way!" I replied. "Then you would be cheating me, Messenger of God!" "Two dirhams then?" he offered. "No," I refused. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then set about raising the offer until he reached one ounce of gold. I asked him, "Would that be agreeable to you?" "Yes," he replied. "Then he's yours!" I told him. And he accepted it.

"He then asked, "Jabir, are you married yet?" "Yes, I am," I told him. "To a woman previously married, or to a virgin?" "Previously married," I replied. "Why not a young girl with whom you could have much fun?" he asked. "My father," I explained to him, "was killed at the battle of Uhud and left seven daughters. So I married a mature woman to look after them and bring them up well." "You did well, let's hope to God," he commented. "And when we get to Sirar we'll slaughter a camel and stay there the day with her and she'll hear about us and dust off her cushions for us." I replied, "I swear, Messenger of God, we don't have cushions." "You will have," he said. "When we get there behave wisely."

"When we reached Sirar, the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered camels be slaughtered; this was done. We remained there that day and in the evening the Messenger of God (SAAS) went home, as did we all. I reported to my wife what he had said and she advised me to hurry up and do as I was told.

"Next morning I led my camel up to the door of the Messenger of God (SAAS), then sat down close to him in the mosque. Eventually the Messenger of God (SAAS) came outside and, when he saw the camel, he asked to whom it belonged. He was told that Jabir had brought it, and he asked where I was. Then they called for me to go to him, and he said, "Cousin, lead your camel away; it is yours." Then he called Bilal over and told him, "Take Jabir away and give him an ounce of gold." So I went off with Bilal and he did give me an ounce, and a little more as well. And, by God, my financial affairs improved constantly thereafter until they suffered recently in what befell us.'"
lessons: True leadership and care means noticing those who are falling behind and pausing to help them — no one is too small to deserve attention.
Jabir's sacrifice — marrying a mature woman to care for his orphaned sisters rather than following personal preference — shows how selfless choices made for family carry their own reward.
God's provision often comes through unexpected channels; what seems like a loss or a weak camel can become the beginning of lasting blessing.
practical_advice: When you are stressed about finances or feel you are falling behind in life, recall Jabir's camel — sometimes what seems broken or insufficient becomes the very vehicle of your improvement.
If you have made a personal sacrifice for family or others and wonder if it was worth it, take comfort: noble intentions compound over time, just as Jabir's financial affairs improved constantly.
Look around you today for someone who is falling behind the group — a friend, colleague, or family member — and take a moment to check in on them the way the Prophet checked in on Jabir.

title: The Second Expedition to Badr — The Appointment Kept and Abu Sufyan's Retreat
summary: The Prophet led his companions to Badr to fulfill the appointment made after Uhud, waited eight days for Abu Sufyan and the Meccan army, but Abu Sufyan turned his forces back citing a barren year, leaving the Meccans to be mocked as the 'sawiq army.'
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned to Medina from the Dhat al-Riqa' expedition, he remained there for the rest of Jumada al-Ula, Jumada al-Akhira and Rajab. In Sha'ban he left for Badr and his appointment with Abu Sufyan."

Ibn Hisham stated, "He placed 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy b. Salul in charge of Medina."

Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth to Badr and remained there for eight days waiting for Abu Sufyan. Abu Sufyan set out with his Meccan force and encamped at Majanna, near al-Zahran. Some authorities state that he reached 'Usfan then decided to go back. He announced, 'Men of Quraysh, it is right for you to make such an expedition only in a fruitful year when you can pasture on trees and drink milk; this year it is barren. I am going back; you do the same.'

"So they all returned. The Meccans referred to them as the samid' army, saying to them, 'So you only went forth to drink sawiq then!'"
lessons: Showing up and fulfilling your commitments — even when the other party does not — is itself a form of integrity and quiet victory.
Those who lead through intimidation and bluster often retreat when the moment of real courage arrives; steadfastness outlasts posturing.
Waiting faithfully, without panic or retreat, when circumstances seem uncertain is itself an act of trust and courage.
practical_advice: When you have made a commitment and the other person fails to show up or follow through, do not let their retreat become your defeat — honor what you said you would do.

If you feel anxious waiting for a resolution or outcome that is out of your hands, reframe the waiting period as an opportunity to demonstrate patience and resolve rather than a sign that things are going wrong.
Reflect on a time when someone's failure to follow through on a threat or challenge actually revealed their weakness rather than your vulnerability — let this build your confidence.

title: The Second Expedition to Badr – Abu Sufyan's Retreat
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) led a force to Badr to fulfill the appointment made after Uhud, but Abu Sufyan turned back from Majanna claiming the year was barren, and the Muslims returned safely after waiting eight days at the well.
story: u f y a set out with his Meccan force and encamped at Majanna, near al-Zahm. Some authorities state that he reached 'Usfan then decided to go back. He announced, 'Men of Quraysh, it is right for you to make such an expedition only in a fruitful year when you can pasture on trees and drink milk; this year it is barren. I am going back; you do the same.' So they all returned. The Meccans referred to them as the samid army, saying to them, 'So you only went forth to drink sawiq then!' Makhshi b. 'Am al-Dam, who had made a contract with the Prophet (SAAS) concerning Bana Damra at the Waddan expedition, now arrived and said, 'Muhammad, have you come here to this well to meet with Quraysh?' He replied, 'Yes, brother from Bana Damra; but if you want, we'll release you from your agreement with us and we'll battle you until God decides between us.' 'No, by God, Muhammad, there's no need for that!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then returned to Medina without incident.
lessons: Strategic patience and willingness to engage without recklessness reflects deep trust in God's plan.
True courage is knowing when to stand firm and when a confrontation is not required — the Prophet showed both.
Honoring agreements and offering fair terms even to potential enemies reflects the highest moral character.
practical_advice: When you prepare for a difficult confrontation and it does not materialize, recognize this as a mercy from God rather than a wasted effort.
In tense negotiations or conflicts, model the Prophet's example by stating your position clearly while leaving the door open for peaceful resolution.
If an adversary backs down from a commitment, do not let their cowardice diminish your own integrity — return home with your honor intact.

title: The Poetry Exchange After the Second Badr Expedition
summary: Poets on both the Muslim and Qurayshi sides exchanged verses after the Second Badr expedition, with the Muslims celebrating their faithfulness and taunting Abu Sufyan's retreat, while Abu Sufyan's cousin responded in kind.
story: Ibn Ishaq quoted 'Abd Allah b. Rawha as having delivered the following verses as they waited for Abu Sufyan to arrive on his return that year to Badr. However, Ibn Hisham stated that this poetry was delivered to him by Abu Zayd who attributed it to Ka'b b. Malik: 'We agreed on Badr with Abu Sufyan, but found him untruthful and untrustworthy in his appointment. I swear that had you and we met you would have returned home humiliated and with your supporters gone. There we previously left limbs of 'Utba and of his son, along with 'Amr Abu Jahl whom we left prostrate. You disobeyed the Messenger of God! How vile your faith and your evil and misguided conduct! If you were to belabour me, I'd still say, My wealth and family be a ransom for the Messenger of God! We obey him, treating none other of us as his equal; he is a flame guiding us in the dark of night!' Hassan b. Thabit spoke the following verses on that occasion: 'Forget the streams of Syria! For intervening are swords (blood-stained) like the mouths of pregnant camels fed on (thorny) arak trees. In the hands of men who have gone out to their Lord, to His true helpers, and to the hands of angels. If they pass on to the lowlands from the sandy valleys, tell them that is not the route. We remained eight days at the shallow well with a fully equipped force and many camels tethered. And round-bellied horses, sleek-sided stallions high of withers, The yearly arfaj plants being uprooted by tall, speeding mounts. If we should meet Furat b. Sayyan in our journeyings, he will be as good as dead. If, after him, Qays b. Imr al-Qays, the pallor of death will blacken his face even more! Deliver to Abu Sufyan this message from me; for you are the best of those beggar-like men!' Ibn Ishaq then quoted Abu Sufyan b. al-Harith b. 'Abd al-Muttalib, who later accepted Islam, as answering him with the following verses: 'Hassan, you son of a woman who eats offal, I swear we devour great deserts like that! When we go forth, young gazelles can't escape between us, though seeking defence in full-speed flight. When we leave after a halt you'd think it had been trashed by crowds at a fair! You remained at the shallow well, wanting us to come, but leaving us in the date groves near by. Our horses and mounts trample the vegetation underfoot, levelling all into the sand. We halted for three days between Sal' and Farir, our horses fine and our camels swift-paced. You would have thought that attacking people near their homes would be as easy as buying white lead with cash! Don't vaunt your fine horses when you talk to them as one who protects them and holds them back. You express joy in them, but others should, such as the knights with descent from Fihr b. Malik. You are not engaged in a migration, though you speak thereof, nor do you respect the prohibitions of its faith.'
lessons: Poetry and words have historically been powerful tools for preserving morale and communal identity during conflict.
Devotion to a just leader — expressed here as willingness to sacrifice wealth and family — is a mark of sincere faith.
Even those who once opposed the truth, like Abu Sufyan b. al-Harith who later accepted Islam, can be transformed — no soul is beyond guidance.
practical_advice: Channel strong emotions like anger or frustration into constructive expression rather than destructive retaliation.
Reflect on those who were once opponents of good but later changed — this can soften your heart toward difficult people in your own life.
When you feel unrecognized for your efforts, recall that steadfastness in the right path is its own reward regardless of how others respond.

title: The Hypocrites Try to Dissuade the Muslims from Going to Badr
summary: When the Prophet called the Muslims to march toward Badr for the second appointment, hypocrites sent agents to discourage them, but God protected His supporters and the believers went forward.
story: Musa b. 'Uqba recounted from al-Zuhri and Ibn Lahi'a, from Abu al-Aswad, from 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) called for men to go forth to meet Abu Sufyan, the hypocrites sent agents out to dissuade them. But God kept His supporters from harm, and the Muslims did proceed in the company of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Badr. They took various goods with them for sale, saying they would fight if they met Abu Sufyan, and that otherwise they would sell these at the Badr fair.
lessons: Internal enemies who spread doubt and fear can be more dangerous than external opponents, yet God protects those who are sincere.
Practical planning — such as the Muslims bringing goods to trade — does not contradict reliance on God; rather, it reflects wisdom alongside faith.
Community cohesion and following the Prophet's leadership protected the Muslims from the manipulation of hypocrites.
practical_advice: Be wary of voices around you that use fear to prevent you from doing what is right — discern whether counsel comes from wisdom or weakness.
Pair your spiritual intentions with practical preparation; the two are not opposites but complements.
When others try to discourage you from a good action, return to your own conviction and the guidance of those you trust.

title: The Muslims Profit at the Badr Fair and Return Safely
summary: The Muslims remained at Badr for eight days during the fair, traded profitably, and returned home safely, fulfilling the Quranic promise that no harm would come to those who follow God's pleasure.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) headed for it (Badr) with a force of 1,500 of his Companions. He placed 'Abd Allah b. Rawha in command of Medina. His departure for there occurred early in Dhu al-Qa'da.' Others say that they returned just as God, Almighty and Glorious is He, had said, 'And so they returned with favour from God and His grace. No harm came to them. For they followed the pleasure of God, and God is He of mighty grace' (Surat Al-'Imran; III, v. 173). Al-Waqidi stated, 'They remained at Badr for the duration of the fair that lasted eight days. Then they returned home, having profited with two dirhams for each one invested.'
lessons: Divine protection is promised to those who sincerely follow God's guidance, as the Quran attests in Surat Al-'Imran.
Material provision can accompany acts of faith — the Muslims gained both spiritual reward and worldly profit from this expedition.
Leadership that provides clear delegation, as the Prophet did by appointing someone to oversee Medina, enables a community to function and thrive.
practical_advice: Trust that when you act in accordance with your conscience and faith, you will be sustained even in circumstances that seem risky.
Look for opportunities to be productive during periods of waiting — the Muslims turned eight days of waiting into trade and profit.
Reflect on Quranic verses during difficult or uncertain moments; this passage from Al-'Imran is a source of comfort for any trial.

title: The Death of 'Abd Allah, Son of 'Uthman ibn 'Affan
summary: In Jumada al-Uwla of 4 AH, 'Abd Allah, the six-year-old son of 'Uthman ibn 'Affan and Ruqayya daughter of the Prophet, died, and the Prophet performed his funeral prayer while his father accompanied him into the grave.
story: Ibn Jarir stated, 'In Jumada al-Uwla of that year 'Abd Allah, son of 'Uthman b. 'Affan, God be pleased with him, by Ruqayya, daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS), died. He was six years of age. The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the funeral prayer over him, and his father, 'Uthman b. 'Affan, God be pleased with him, accompanied him down into the grave.'
lessons: The grief of losing a child touches even the most noble and beloved of families, reminding us that suffering is part of the human condition without exception.
The Prophet's act of personally performing the funeral prayer for his grandson reflects the depth of communal and familial love in Islam.
Death at a young age is a reminder of the fragility of life and the importance of cherishing those we love while we have them.
practical_advice: When grieving the loss of a child or loved one, follow the example of the Prophet's family — participate in the rituals of farewell as a form of honoring and releasing them.
Allow yourself to grieve fully; the Prophet himself wept at the deaths of loved ones, showing that expressing grief is not weakness.
In moments of loss, lean into community and religious ritual — they provide structure and shared comfort when the heart is overwhelmed.

title: The Death of Abu Salama, Foster-Brother of the Prophet
summary: Abu Salama, the foster-brother of the Prophet who had been among the earliest Muslims and a veteran of Badr and Uhud, died in 4 AH from wounds sustained at Uhud after his injuries reopened following a raid.
story: I would add that the same year Abu Salama 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Asad b. Hilal b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar b. Makhzum al-Qurashi al-Makhzumi died; his mother was Barra, daughter of 'Abd al-Muttalib, aunt of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He was the foster-brother of the Messenger of God (SAAS), since both were suckled by Thuwayba, freed-woman of Abu Lahab. Abu Salama, Abu 'Ubayda, 'Uthman b. 'Affan and al-Arqam b. Abu al-Arqam had accepted Islam long before, on the same day. Abu Salama and his wife had emigrated to Abyssinia and then returned to Mecca. They had had children in Abyssinia. He then migrated from Mecca to Medina and Umm Salama followed him there, as is related above. He was present at the battles of Badr and Uhud and died following wounds he suffered at Uhud. May God be pleased with him and give him pleasure. There is one hadith, on the subject of istirja', that is attributed to him. This will be related shortly in the text dealing with the marriage of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Umm Salama.
lessons: A life of sacrifice — emigration, battle, and wounds endured for faith — is a life of profound dignity even if it ends early.
The lasting legacy of a believer is carried forward through their family, their companions, and the hadith they transmit.
Abu Salama's double emigration and participation in the earliest battles show that perseverance through multiple hardships is the mark of true resolve.
practical_advice: When you feel that your own sacrifices go unrecognized, remember that the record of every hardship endured for what is right is preserved.
Draw strength from the biographies of those who faced repeated hardship — emigration, battle, injury, and death — and still remained steadfast.
If you are recovering from a physical or emotional wound, be gentle with yourself; even the strongest among the Prophet's companions suffered relapses and needed time.

title: The Birth of Al-Husayn ibn 'Ali
summary: Al-Husayn, son of 'Ali and Fatima daughter of the Prophet, was born in Sha'ban of 4 AH, bringing joy to the Prophet's family.
story: Ibn Jarir stated, 'Some days before the end of Sha'ban that year, al-Husayn b. 'Ali was born to Fatima, daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS); may God be pleased with them all.'
lessons: New life born into a family of faith is a gift and a continuation of a blessed legacy.
The Prophet's grandchildren were cherished not only as family but as a spiritual trust for the entire Muslim community.
Joy and grief can coexist in the same season of life — this birth came during a year that also brought significant losses.
practical_advice: Celebrate the births, milestones, and new beginnings in your community as actively as you mourn losses — both deserve full presence.
When a child is born into your family or community, reflect on the legacy you hope to pass on to them.
In seasons that mix sorrow and joy, allow yourself to feel both fully rather than suppressing one in deference to the other.

title: The Prophet's Marriage to Zaynab bint Khuzayma (Umm al-Masakin)
summary: In Ramadan of 4 AH, the Prophet married Zaynab bint Khuzayma, a widow known as 'Mother of the Poor' for her extraordinary generosity, who passed away just two or three months after the marriage during the Prophet's lifetime.
story: In Ramadan of that year the Messenger of God (SAAS) married Zaynab, daughter of Khuzayma b. al-Harith b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr b. 'Abd Manaf. Hilal b. 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a, a woman of Hilal. She was known as Umm al-Masakin, 'mother of the poor', for the many donations she made to them and for her kindness and compassion towards them. The Prophet (SAAS) gave her a dowry of 12 and a half ounces of gold and consummated his marriage with her in Ramadan. She had previously been married to al-Tufayl b. al-Harith who had divorced her. Abu 'Umar b. 'Abd al-Barr quoted 'Ali b. 'Abd al-'Aziz al-Jurjani as saying, 'Thereafter his brother 'Ubayda b. al-Harith b. al-Muttalib b. 'Abd Manaf had married her.' Ibn al-Athir stated, in his work Al-Ghaba, 'It is said she was also the wife of 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh who was killed at Uhud while still married to her.' Abu 'Umar stated, 'There is no dispute over the fact that she died during the lifetime of the Messenger of God (SAAS). It is said that she remained living with him only for two or three months before she died; may God be pleased with her.'
lessons: A person's true legacy is built through generosity and compassion — Zaynab's title 'Mother of the Poor' endures because of how she gave, not what she possessed.
The Prophet honored women who had experienced loss and hardship by offering them care, dignity, and a secure household.
The shortness of a life or a relationship does not diminish its meaningfulness — Zaynab's brief marriage was blessed and remembered forever.
practical_advice: Consider how you might earn a meaningful title through your actions — what would those around you call you based on how you treat them?
When someone you love is taken from you quickly, resist the urge to feel that the connection was wasted; brief relationships can carry deep and lasting blessing.
In times of personal loss or widowhood, know that seeking new community and support is not a betrayal of the past but a step toward healing.

title: The Prophet's Marriage to Umm Salama and Her Reluctance
summary: After the death of her husband Abu Salama from his Uhud wounds, Umm Salama was courted by the Prophet who sent 'Umar ibn al-Khattab to arrange the marriage; she expressed her concerns about jealousy and her children before giving her consent.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, 'In Shawwal of that year the Messenger of God (SAAS) married Umm Salama, daughter of Abu Umayya.' I comment that previously she had been the wife of her children's father, Abu Salama b. 'Abd al-Asad. He had been present at Uhud, as is told above. He was wounded at that battle and nursed his wounds for a month until he was restored. He then went forth on a sariyya, a raid, at which he earned considerable booty. He then stayed home for seventeen days, but his wounds erupted again and he died, three days before the end of Jumada al-Uwla of that year. When in Shawwal it was permissible for her to remarry, the Messenger of God (SAAS) became engaged to her, sending 'Umar b. al-Khattab to her several times to arrange this. She told him that she was a very jealous woman and that she had children; that is, that she had children who would divert her attention from him and for whose food and support she had to work. He responded, 'Regarding the children, God and His messenger will care for them; that is, you won't be responsible for their expenses. As for the jealousy, I will pray to God and He will remove it.' She then gave her consent in the matter, finally telling 'Umar, 'Off you go; arrange the marriage of the Prophet (SAAS).' By this she meant that she had agreed and had given her permission. Some scholars imagine her to have said this to her son 'Umar b. Abu Salama who was too young at that time to be involved with contracts. I have combined all that is correct into one specific account, all praise and trust being in God. (There is also an account that states) that the person who arranged her marriage with him was her oldest son Salama b. Abu Salama. This account is widely known.
lessons: Umm Salama's honest articulation of her fears — jealousy and the burden of her children — teaches that true consent requires the freedom to voice concerns without shame.
The Prophet's response to her worries with prayer and personal assurance models how to address a grieving person's needs with gentleness and practical care.
Widowhood is not the end of a full life; Umm Salama went on to become one of the most learned and beloved of the Prophet's wives.
practical_advice: When facing a major life decision after loss, give yourself permission to name your fears honestly before committing, as Umm Salama did.
If you are supporting someone through grief and transition, do not rush their process — the Prophet sent 'Umar several times, allowing Umm Salama to arrive at consent in her own time.
Trust that your children's wellbeing is a shared communal and spiritual responsibility, not yours alone to carry — seek support and accept it when offered.

title: Umm Salama's Marriage to the Prophet
summary: After the death of her husband Abu Salama, Umm Salama mourned and recited the supplication he had taught her, wondering how she could ever find better than him. The Prophet proposed marriage to her, addressing her concerns about jealousy, age, and children, and she accepted.
story: Imam Ahmad stated, "Yunus related to us, quoting al-Layth - that is, Ibn Sacd - from Yazid b. 'Abd Allah b. Usama b. al-Had, from 'Amr b. Abi 'Amr, from al-Muttalib, from Umm Salama, who said, 'Abu Salama came to me one day from the Messenger of God (SAAS), and said, "I heard something from the Messenger of God (SAAS) that greatly pleased me. He said, 'Any Muslim who suffers a loss should repeat, "To God we belong, and to Him we return," and then should pray, "O God, compensate me for my loss and give me better in exchange.""' She went on, 'And I learned this from him by heart. "'When Abu Salama died, I spoke the words, "To God we belong, and to Him we return", then said, "O God, compensate me for my loss and give me better in exchange." But then I realized what I had said and wondered how I could ever have better than Abu Salama. "'When my 'adda, my waiting period, was over, the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked to come to see me while I was tanning a hide I had. I washed my hands clean of the tanning solution, asked him to come in and set out a leather cushion stuffed with palm fronds for him to sit upon. He sat down on it and made me an offer of marriage. When he had finished speaking, I replied, "O Messenger of God! It's not fit for me to question your wanting this, but I'm an extremely jealous woman and I'm afraid you may see in me something for which God will punish me. And I'm a woman who is getting on in years, and I have children." '"He replied, "Regarding the jealousy, God will remove it from you. As for your reference to your age, I am afflicted just as you are. Regarding your children, they shall be as my own." So I consented to the Messenger of God (SAAS). And God did give me better than Abu Salama - the Messenger of God (SAAS)!'"
lessons: A sincere supplication made in grief, even when its fulfillment seems unimaginable, can be answered by God in ways beyond our comprehension.
Expressing honest concerns and vulnerabilities is not a sign of weakness; it is a foundation for genuine trust and resolution.
God's promise of compensation for loss is not limited to material replacement but can encompass far greater blessings than what was lost.
practical_advice: When facing loss, adopt the supplication of Umm Salama: acknowledge that all things belong to God, and ask Him specifically to replace your loss with something better.
When you feel unworthy of a blessing or opportunity, voice your genuine concerns rather than withdrawing in silence; honest dialogue can remove barriers you thought were insurmountable.
Keep a record of past moments when your circumstances improved after hardship; revisiting these moments builds the trust that your current grief will also give way to goodness.

title: Zayd ibn Thabit Ordered to Learn Hebrew Script
summary: In the year 4 AH, the Prophet ordered his companion Zayd ibn Thabit to learn Hebrew script, which Zayd accomplished within fifteen days.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "That year, 4 AH, the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered Zayd b. Thabit to learn Hebrew script." I would comment that it is established in the Sahih collection that he said, "I learned it in 15 days." But God knows best.
lessons: Seeking knowledge across different languages and cultures is an act encouraged by prophetic example, not a compromise of one's identity.
Dedication and sincere effort can compress what seems like a long learning journey into a remarkably short time.
A leader who equips those around him with diverse skills strengthens the entire community's resilience and capability.
practical_advice: Commit to learning one new skill or body of knowledge this month with the focused intensity Zayd showed; set a concrete deadline as he effectively did.
Do not dismiss a new area of learning as foreign or outside your domain; approach it with openness and the understanding that all beneficial knowledge has value.
When given a task by a mentor or leader, begin immediately rather than procrastinating; Zayd's fifteen-day achievement began with prompt action.

title: The Expedition to Dumat al-Jandal
summary: In Rabi' al-Awwal of 5 AH, the Prophet led a force of 1,000 Muslims toward Dumat al-Jandal after receiving reports of a large hostile group there, travelling by night under cover of darkness. Upon their approach the hostile forces dispersed, one prisoner was taken and embraced Islam, and the Prophet returned to Medina without battle.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then made an expedition to Dumat al-Jandal." Ibn Hisham observed that it took place in Rabi' al-Awwal that year and that he appointed Siba' b. 'Urfuta al-Ghifari in charge of Medina. Ibn Ishaq went on, "He then, however, returned to Medina before reaching it, and without engaging in battle. He remained in Medina for the remainder of that year." That is the statement made by Ibn Ishaq. Muhammad b. 'Umar al-Waqidi quoted lines of authority back to various early scholars who stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had wanted to proceed to the closest areas in Syria but was told that this would alarm Caesar. He was also informed that there was a large force at Dumat al-Jandal who maltreated passers-by and that it also had a substantial market; these people wanted him to approach the town. He therefore organized his men and left with a force of 1,000 Muslims. They travelled by night, keeping under cover by day. He had a guide with him from Banu 'Udhra named Madhkur, who was a skilled tracker. When they neared Dumat al-Jandal, his guide directed him to the pasturing cattle belonging to Banu Tamim. He went on and attacked their sheep and goats; some people were killed, while others fled in disarray. When news of this reached people in Dumat al-Jandal, those there dispersed. The Messenger of God (SAAS) proceeded to encamp on their square, finding no one there. He remained there for several days, sending out raiding parties. They returned, with Muhammad b. Salama having taken one of the enemy prisoner. He was brought to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who asked him about his comrades. The man replied that they had fled the previous day. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then explained Islam to him, and he became a Muslim. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went back to Medina.
lessons: Careful preparation, including gathering intelligence and appointing trustworthy leadership in one's absence, is a mark of responsible stewardship.
A goal may be achieved without direct confrontation; sometimes the mere resolve and organized advance of a principled force is enough to remove a threat.
Even an adversary, when approached with sincere explanation rather than only force, may open his heart; the prisoner who embraced Islam is a testament to this.
practical_advice: Before undertaking a significant challenge, appoint someone reliable to manage your responsibilities at home, as the Prophet appointed a deputy over Medina.
When navigating unfamiliar territory, seek out those with specialized knowledge as guides rather than proceeding blindly; the skilled tracker Madhkur was indispensable to the expedition.
Look for opportunities to share your values or perspective with those who oppose you; a moment of genuine dialogue can transform an adversary into an ally.

title: The Death of Umm Sa'd During the Expedition to Dumat al-Jandal
summary: During the expedition to Dumat al-Jandal, Umm Sa'd bint 'Ubada passed away while her son Sa'd was absent with the Prophet. Upon the Prophet's return, he prayed over her grave even though a month had passed.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth to Dumat al-Jandal in Rabi' al-Akhir, 5 AH. And the same year Umm Sa'd b. 'Ubada died while her son was away on this expedition with the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Abu 'Isa al-Tirmidhi stated in his compendium, "Muhammad b. Bashshar related to us, quoting Yahya b. Sa'id, from Sa'id b. Abi 'Uruba, from Qatada, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, that Umm Sa'd died while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was absent. When he returned he prayed for her, a month having passed."
lessons: Death does not wait for the convenient return of loved ones; accepting this reality is part of trusting in God's timing and wisdom.
Acts of spiritual care and supplication for the deceased are never too late; the Prophet's prayer over the grave after a month shows that love and intercession transcend time.
Those who serve a greater cause may miss personal moments of grief and farewell; their sacrifice is seen by God even when unseen by others.
practical_advice: If you were unable to be present at the passing or burial of a loved one, know that you can still pray for them sincerely at any time; your supplication reaches them regardless of the delay.
When someone close to you is grieving a loss that occurred in your absence, do not let the passage of time stop you from acknowledging their loss and offering a prayer or kind word.
Prepare yourself and your family spiritually for the reality that loss can come at any time; regular remembrance of God softens the shock of sudden grief.

title: The Battle of the Ditch — Quranic Revelation and Overview
summary: The Battle of al-Khandaq, also known as the Battle of the Clans, was a defining siege of early Islam in which confederate forces attacked Medina, testing the believers severely and exposing the hypocrisy of some, while the Quran in Surah al-Ahzab commemorated both the trial and the divine assistance that saved the believers.
story: It was about this event that the main part of surat al-Ahzab (XXXIII) was revealed. In it the Almighty stated, "O you who believe! Remember God's favour to you when enemy forces attacked you and We sent a wind against them, along with forces you could not see. God sees well what you are doing. (Remember) when they came at you from above and from below, and when your sight was blinded and hearts arose into your throats and you were confused in your thoughts about God. There believers were put to the test, and they were violently shaken. (Remember) when the hypocrites and those with disease in their hearts said, 'It was all misleading, what God and His messenger promised us!' And (remember) when one group of them said, 'O people of Yathrib, there is no place for you here, so go back!' And a group of them did ask the Prophet, saying, 'Our homes lie exposed.' Yet they were not exposed; all they wanted was to flee. And if they (the enemy) had gained entry from outside and had asked them to revolt, they would have done so without great hesitation. Whereas they had earlier promised God that they would not retreat; and promises made to God are answerable. Say: 'It will not avail you to flee, whether from death or from battle; in such case you will be allowed enjoyment only briefly.' Say: 'Who is it can protect you from God if He wishes to do you harm, or be merciful to you?' They shall not find anyone to help or aid them apart from God. God knows which of you hinder and which of you say to your brethren, 'Come over to us!' Yet they rarely go to battle. They are ungenerous to you; and when fear arrives you see them looking at you with their eyes rolling, like those who faint at death. But when the fear is gone, they pursue you with sharp tongues, greedy for the good (booty). Those people did not believe and so God nullified their action; and this was an easy thing for God. They consider that the clans have not gone; and if the clans should again come, they would wish to be among the bedouin asking for news of you; yet if they were among you, they would only do in battle a little. In the Messenger of God there has been for you an excellent model for those who have hope in God and in the last Day and who make frequent mention of God. When the believers saw the clans, they said, 'This is what God and His messenger promised us. God and His messenger spoke the truth.' And this only increased their faith and submission. The believers include some men who are true to what they promised God. Of them are those who have already fulfilled their vows, while some of them still wait but have not changed at all. God will reward those who are true in their sincerity; and He will punish the hypocrites, if He wishes, or will turn aside from them. God is very forgiving and merciful. God turned back those who disbelieved; by their rage they gained nothing good. Yet God gave satisfaction to the believers in the fighting; and God is strong and powerful. He brought down from their forts those of the People of the Book who allied with them, and cast terror into their hearts; some you killed, others you took captive. He made you heir to their land, properties and belongings, as well as to lands you have not yet trod. And God is capable of all things" (surat al-Ahzab, XXXIII, v.9-13). The battle of al-khandaq took place in Shawwal, 5 AH, according to Ibn Ishaq, 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, Qatada and al-Bayhaqi, along with various scholars of the earlier and later periods.
lessons: The most extreme fear and confusion experienced in a crisis does not indicate the absence of God; the Quran immortalized the believers' terror at the Ditch precisely to validate that faith is tested, not abandoned, in such moments.
The distinction between true believers and hypocrites is most clearly revealed under pressure; a crisis is therefore an opportunity for self-examination about the sincerity of one's own commitments.
Divine aid often comes through unexpected means — wind, unseen forces, and the dispersal of enemies — reminding us that our own limited resources are never the whole picture.
practical_advice: When you feel that 'hearts are rising into throats' with fear, recall that this exact sensation was experienced by the companions of the Prophet and named in the Quran; you are not alone and your fear does not disqualify your faith.
Audit your own commitments: are there promises you have made to God or others that fear or self-interest is causing you to quietly abandon? Renew them deliberately.

In times of collective crisis, identify the voices around you that spread despair or urge retreat, and consciously choose instead to hold to the prophetic model of steadfastness and trust in God's promise.

title: The Date of the Battle of Al-Khandaq (The Trench): Scholarly Debate
summary: Islamic scholars debate whether the Battle of Al-Khandaq occurred in 4 AH or 5 AH, with Ibn Ishaq, Urwa b. al-Zubayr, and the majority holding it was Shawwal of 5 AH, while al-Zuhri and others suggest 4 AH. Al-Bayhaqi reconciles the difference by noting the disagreement is about counting partial years.
story: The battle of al-khandaq took place in Shawwal, 5 AH, according to Ibn Ishaq, Wrwa b. al-Zubayr, Qatada and al-Bayhaqi, along with various scholars of the earlier and later periods. Mas%b. Wqba quoted al-Zuhri as stating, "Then the waq'at al-ahaib, the 'battle of the clans', took place, in Shawwal of 4 AH." I m m Mdik b. Anas stated the same, according to a quotation attributed to him by Ahmad h. Hanhal, quoting Mnsa b. D%'ad. Al-Bayhaqi stated, "There is in fact no dispute between them in this; this is because their intent was that this occurred after the passage of four years, hut before the completion of five." There is no doubt that when the polytheists left Uhud they made an arrangement to meet the Muslims at Badr in the following year. The Messenger of God (SAAS) therefore went forth with his force in Sha'ban of 4 AH, as told IMAM ABU AL-FIDA' ISMA'IL IBN KATHIR above, while Aba Sufyan returned home with the Quraysh because of the drought that year. They would not have been likely to have gone to Medina two months later. And so it may he concluded that the battle of al-khandaq took place in 5 AH. But God knows best. Al-Zuhri declared that the battle of al-khandaq took place two years following that of Uhud. And there is no dispute over the fact that Uhud occurred in Shaww~lof 3 AH, except in so far as some maintain that the dating System began with Muharram of the second year after the hegira, and they do not count the remaining mouths of that year, from Rabic al-Awwal to its end, as al-Bayhaqi related it. Ya'qiib b. Sufyan al-Fasawi maintained this, declaring that Badr took place in the first year, Uhud in the second, the "appointment at Badr" in Sha'bm of year 3 AH, and al-khandaq in Shawwal of year 4 AH. This view is contrary to that of the majority, for it is widely accepted that the Commander of the Believers, Wmar b. al-Khattab, established the beginning of the era from Muharram of the year of the hegira, though Mdik states it to have been from Rabi' al-Awwal of that year. There are therefore the three views. And God knows best. What seems true is what the majority hold -that Uhud was in Shawwal of 3 m, while al-khandaq took place in Shawwal of 5 AH. But God knows best. (Also revelant) is the hadirh accepted in both ~ a h i hcollections derived through Wbayd All* from Nafi', who quoted Ibn Wmar as saying, "I was presented to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the Battle of Uhud when I was 14, but he would not give me permission to fight. I was also presented to him at al-khandaq when I was 15, and he then gave me permission." Some scholars, including al-Bayhaqi, respond to this by saying that he was presented at Uhud at the beginning of his 14th year, and that al-Abzb (al-khandaq) was at the end of his 15th year. I would comment that it is probable that what he meant was that when he was presented at al-Awb he had already completed 15 years, an age at which youths are permitted to engage in battle; and so nothing more is to be added. Therefore, when Nsc related this hadith to Wmar h. 'Abd al-'Aziz, he said, "This constitutes the difference between a youth and a man." He then wrote this to the provinces and the mass of scholars thereafter relied upon it. But God knows best.
lessons: Scholarly disagreement does not mean chaos — even in matters of historical dating, scholars sought reconciliation and truth with humility, saying 'God knows best.'
Uncertainty about details does not undermine the core truths of faith; learning to sit with ambiguity is a sign of intellectual maturity.
The Islamic scholarly tradition models careful reasoning, cross-referencing sources, and epistemic humility — qualities that lead to inner peace rather than anxiety over the unknown.
practical_advice: When you face confusion or conflicting information, follow the approach of scholars: gather perspectives, seek the most coherent explanation, and then trust God with what remains unclear.
Practice saying 'I don't know, but God does' as a calming phrase when overwhelmed by uncertainty — it is a prophetic and scholarly tradition, not a weakness.
Rather than letting unresolved questions cause anxiety, write them down, seek knowledge gradually, and accept that some answers will come with time and learning.

title: The Jewish Leaders Incite the Clans Against the Muslims
summary: A group of Jewish leaders from Banu al-Nadir travel to Mecca and Ghatafan, deliberately inciting the Arab tribes against the Prophet Muhammad, even declaring the polytheists' religion superior to Islam to win their alliance, prompting God to reveal verses condemning this betrayal.
story: The following text of the account is derived from Ibn Ishaq and others. Ibn Ishzq stated, "The battle of al-khandaq took place, in Shawwal, 5 AH. "Yazid b. Rumm related to me, from Wrwa and another trustworthy source, from Wbayd Allah'5 h. Ka'b b. Mdik and Muhammad b. Ka'b al-Qurazi and al-Zuhri, and CAsi b. Wmar b. Qamda, and 'Abd Allah h. Aba Bakr and other scholars, some of these giving information not included by others, as follows. What happened at al-khandaq was that a group of Jews had divided up the clans against the Messenger of God (SAAS). These included Sallzm b. Aba al-Huqayq al-Nadri, Huyayy b. Akhrah al-Nadri, Kinana b. al-Rabic b. Aba al-Huqayq, Hawdha h. Qays al-Wa'ili, and Aba 'Ammx al-Wa'ili, along with groups from Bana al-Nadir and Bana Wa'il. These went to Quraysh in Mecca and suggested they engage in warfare against the Messenger of God (SAAS). They told them that they would participate until they had completely destroyed h i . Quraysh responded as follows, '0Jews, you are the frst people to receive scripture and knowledge in those matters in which we and Muhammad differ. Which religion is better, ours or his?' "They replied, 'Your religion is definitely better than his; you are closer the truth than he is.' "It is of these people that God revealed the words, 'Have you not seen those who were brought a share of the Book? They believe in idols and evil powers, and say to those who disbelieve, "These are more rightly guided than those who believe!" Those are they whom God has cursed; and whomever God curses shall find none to help him' (sfirat a l - N d ; IV, v.51-2). "This pleased Quraysh when they said it to them and they gladly accepted the suggestion of going to war against the Messenger of God (SAAS). They then assembled and made preparations. "That group of Jews then visited Ghatafan of Qays 'Aylan and invited them to battle the Messenger of God (SAAS), telling them that they would themselves participate along with them, and that Quraysh would do so too. They therefore joined up with them. "Quraysh then set out, their leader being Aba Sufym. Ghatafm, under their leader 'Uyayna b. Hisn b. Hudhayfa b. Badr, also went forth with Bann Fazaa, as did al-H&th b. CAwf b. Aba H%itha al-Murri with Banti Murra, along with Mis'ar b. Rukhayla b. Nuwayra b. Tarif b. Suhma b. 'Ahd Allah b. Hilal b. Khalawa b. Ashja' b. Rayth b. Gha~fZn,along with those of his people from Ashjacwho followed him."
lessons: Betrayal by those you once trusted is one of the most painful human experiences — yet the Prophet responded with strategy and patience, not panic or despair.
When people distort truth for political gain, divine revelation provides clarity and validation for those who are wronged.
The formation of alliances against the believers teaches that external pressure, no matter how overwhelming it seems, can be faced with unity, preparation, and trust in God.
practical_advice: When you feel betrayed or conspired against, resist the urge to retaliate impulsively — take time to assess the situation calmly and plan a wise response.
Seek validation not only from people but from your principles and faith; sometimes the acknowledgment of wrongdoing comes from higher sources than human opinion.
Build strong, trustworthy relationships in your community before a crisis strikes — isolation during difficulty is far more dangerous than any external threat.

title: The Prophet Orders the Digging of the Trench Around Medina
summary: Upon hearing of the massive coalition of tribes marching against Medina, the Prophet Muhammad acts swiftly on Salman al-Farisi's suggestion to dig a defensive trench around the city, personally participating in the labor alongside his companions.
story: "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) heard of all this and what they had agreed, he dug the ditch around Medina." Ibn Hishim commented that it is said that it was Salmm who suggested this. Al-Tahari and al-Suhayli stated that the frst man to dig ditches was Manushehr b. Ayraj h. Afridiin and that that happened in the time of Moses, peace be upon him. Ibn Ishaq went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) worked on this himself, inspiring the Muslims to seek reward in heaven, and they worked alongside him. Some hypocrites held back, complaining of their weakness. Some of these left surreptitiously and without his permission or knowledge. "It was on this subject that Almighty God revealed, 'Believers are only those who believe in God and in His messenger. And if engaged with him in a joint effort they do not leave without seeking his permission. Those who do seek your permission are those who believe in God and in His messenger. If they do ask IMAM ABU AL-FIDA' ISMA'IL IBN K A T H ~ R 129 your permission to attend to some affair, then permit it to whichever of them you wish. And ask God to forgive them; God is indeed Forgiving, Merciful. Do not consider the Messenger's appealing to you while among you the same as you making appeals to one another. God is well aware which of you steal away in hiding. Let those beware who oppose his orders, lest trouble or painful punishment afflict them. No indeed! All in the heavens and all on earth belong to God; He well knows what you do. On the day on which they will be returned to Him, He will inform them what it was they did. God is aware of all things!" (sfirat al-Niir; XXIV, v.62-4).
lessons: True leadership means working alongside your team, not above them — the Prophet's personal participation in digging inspired the entire community to give their best.
Innovation and adaptability in the face of crisis is itself a form of wisdom; adopting the trench strategy from a Persian companion showed that truth and good ideas transcend cultural boundaries.
Those who shirk communal responsibility during collective hardship not only harm the group but also reveal the weakness of their own character and commitment.
practical_advice: When facing an overwhelming challenge, break it into practical steps and begin immediately — the Prophet's response to the threat was swift, practical action rather than paralysis.
Do not be too proud to adopt good ideas from others, regardless of their background; wisdom is the lost property of the believer.
If you are part of a team or community facing difficulty, show up fully — your presence and effort, even in small ways, strengthen everyone around you.

title: The Hypocrites Abandon the Trench-Digging
summary: While the Prophet and his companions labored together digging the trench, some hypocrites made excuses and slipped away without permission, prompting God to reveal Quranic verses distinguishing true believers from those who abandon communal duty.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) worked on this himself, inspiring the Muslims to seek reward in heaven, and they worked alongside him. Some hypocrites held back, complaining of their weakness. Some of these left surreptitiously and without his permission or knowledge. "It was on this subject that Almighty God revealed, 'Believers are only those who believe in God and in His messenger. And if engaged with him in a joint effort they do not leave without seeking his permission. Those who do seek your permission are those who believe in God and in His messenger. If they do ask your permission to attend to some affair, then permit it to whichever of them you wish. And ask God to forgive them; God is indeed Forgiving, Merciful. Do not consider the Messenger's appealing to you while among you the same as you making appeals to one another. God is well aware which of you steal away in hiding. Let those beware who oppose his orders, lest trouble or painful punishment afflict them. No indeed! All in the heavens and all on earth belong to God; He well knows what you do. On the day on which they will be returned to Him, He will inform them what it was they did. God is aware of all things!" (sfirat al-Niir; XXIV, v.62-4).'
lessons: Hypocrisy — saying one thing and doing another — is ultimately self-defeating; those who abandon their responsibilities during hardship lose the trust and respect of their community.
Divine awareness of all actions, even those done in secret, is both a warning and a comfort: wrongdoers are seen, but so are the sincere efforts of those who stay faithful.
Accountability to one's community is an expression of faith — genuine belief manifests in action, especially when action is difficult and costly.
practical_advice: When you feel the urge to quietly withdraw from a responsibility you agreed to, pause and ask yourself whether you are acting with integrity — communicate openly instead of disappearing.
If you have been let down by someone who abandoned you during a hard time, remember that God sees the sincerity of your effort and the absence of theirs; do not let their failure diminish your work.
Cultivate the habit of asking permission and communicating clearly in your commitments — it is a mark of trustworthiness that strengthens all your relationships.

title: Renaming Ju'ayl to 'Amr: The Prophet's Encouragement During Trench-Digging
summary: While digging the trench, the Muslim workers composed chants about a companion named Ju'ayl, whom the Prophet had honorably renamed 'Amr, and the Prophet joined in their chanting, lifting the spirits of the workers through shared song and humor.
story: Ibn I s h ~ qcontinued, "The Muslims continued working on the trench until they had fmished it. They made a rhyme about one of the Muslims, a man named Ju'ayl whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) referred to as 'Arnr. They would repeat, 'To 'Amr &omJukyl he changed his name; and soon "the weak are strong", he'll proclaim.' "When they pronounced the name "Amr', so would the Messenger of God (SAAS) do so along with them. When they said the word Lproclaim', so would he."
lessons: The Prophet's willingness to join in the workers' chants shows that lightening the mood and sharing in joy — even during hardship — is a form of compassionate leadership.
Renaming someone to honor them is an act of dignity; the Prophet's care for the feelings and self-worth of individuals, even the seemingly insignificant, is a model for how we treat others.
Shared songs, humor, and communal spirit during hard labor are not frivolities — they sustain the human spirit and strengthen bonds of brotherhood.
practical_advice: During difficult group tasks, don't underestimate the power of humor, song, or shared chanting to lift morale — find small moments of lightness to share with those working alongside you.
Look for ways to honor and dignify the people around you, especially those who may feel overlooked — a kind gesture or word of recognition can transform someone's sense of self.
When you are exhausted and the task feels endless, join your energy with those around you; collective rhythm and spirit make even the hardest work more bearable.

title: The Prophet's Prayer and Chant During the Cold and Hungry Labor of the Trench
summary: During the bitterly cold and hungry days of digging the trench, the Prophet and his companions endured great physical suffering, with the Prophet praying aloud for the hereafter and the companions responding with chants of devotion, eating only meager scraps of barley and rancid fat.
story: Al-Bukhzri stated, "'Abd AUsh b. Mulpmmad related to us, quoting Mu'awiya b. 'Amr, quoting Ahii Ishaq, from Humayd, who said, 'I heard Anas say, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went out to the trench and found that the muhdjirin and the an@r were busy digging. It was a very cold morning and they had no slaves to do that work for them. When he saw what they were suffering from the hard labour and their hunger, he said, '0 God, their life is, indeed, that of the after-life; forgive the aqdr and the muhdjirin.' They responded in unison to this by chanting, 'We are those who gave allegiance to Muhammad, for the jihad,the holy work, forever, for as long as we live.'""" In both ~ahihcollections there is similar wording given, on the authority of Mu'xwiya h. Qurra, from Anas. Muslim related this from a hadith of Hammad b. Salama, from Thxbit and Humayd, from Anas, in similar words. Al-Bukhai stated, "Abn Macmar related to us, quoting 'Abd al-Waith, from 'Abd al-'Aziz, from Anas, who said, 'The muhdjirin and the a q a r set to work digging the trench around Medina carrying away the dirt on their backs, chanting, "We are those who gave allegiance to Muhammad, for Islam, for ever, for as long as we live." "'In response, the Messenger of God (SAAS) would say, LLOGod, there is no good except that of the hereafter, so bless the anjdr and the rnuhdjirin." "'They would bring a handful of barley and some ill-smelling fat as a meal for them, hungry as they were; it stuck in their throats and smelled bad.'" Al-Bukhari also stated, "Qutayba b. Sa'id related to us, quoting 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Aba H z i m , from Sahl b. Sa'd, who said, 'We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the trench while men were digging it. We would carry the dirt away on our shoulders. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "0 God, there is no life but that of the hereafter; forgive the muh~jirinand the an~Zr."'" M u s l i also related this, from al-Qa'nabi, from 'Abd al-'Aziz.
lessons: The Prophet's prayer — 'O God, there is no life but that of the hereafter' — teaches that reframing earthly suffering in light of eternal reward is one of the most powerful tools for enduring hardship.
The companions' spontaneous chant of loyalty in response to the Prophet's prayer shows that shared suffering, when met with shared purpose, creates unbreakable bonds.
Even the most basic food becomes meaningful when shared in the spirit of sacrifice and community — material deprivation does not diminish dignity or spiritual richness.
practical_advice: When you are physically exhausted and your circumstances feel hopeless, try to consciously reframe your suffering: ask yourself what eternal or lasting good might come from enduring this moment with patience.
Find or create a mantra or affirmation that connects your present difficulty to your deeper values and commitments — repeat it, especially when the work is hardest.
Share meals and hardships openly with those around you rather than suffering alone in silence; communal endurance transforms individual pain into collective strength.

title: The Prophet Carries Dirt Until It Covers His Stomach, Reciting Verses of Ibn Rawaha
summary: Al-Bara' ibn 'Azib narrates that during the digging of the trench, the Prophet himself carried so much dirt that it covered the skin of his stomach, all while reciting the motivational verses of the companion Abdullah ibn Rawaha, raising his voice at the line about resisting the enemy.
story: Al-Bukhzi also stated, "Muslim b. Ibrhim related to us, quoting Shu'ba, from Abo Ishaq, from &Bars' b. 'Azib, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) was carrying away the dirt (before) the battle of al-khandaq till dust covered his stomach' (or 'his stomach became dust-covered'), 'and reciting, "0God, without God we would not have been well-guided, nor would we have been charitable; nor would we have prayed. So do send down tranquillity upon us and make us stand firmwhen we meet the foe. They have wronged us; if they want trouble, we'll resist them!" "'And he raised his voice at the words "resist them"."' Muslim related this from a hadith of Shu'ba. Al-Bukhari went on to state thatAhmad b. Wthman related to h i , quoting Shurayh h. Maslama, quoting Ibrahim b. Ynsuf, from his father, from Ahn Ishaq, from al-Barn", who related the following hadith: "At the (time of) the battle of al-Ahah, 'the clans', and the trench of the Messenger of God (SAAS), I saw him carrying the din from the trench so much that it hid the s k i of his stomach from me; and he had much body hair. I heard him reciting verses of 'Abd Allxh b. Raw*a"
lessons: A leader who does the hardest and most unglamorous work alongside his people earns a loyalty that no command or authority can manufacture.
The verses the Prophet recited acknowledge both complete dependence on God and a firm resolve to resist injustice — this combination of humility and courage is the foundation of resilience.
Asking God for tranquillity in the face of an approaching enemy is a deeply human response; it teaches us that seeking inner calm before an external storm is both permitted and praiseworthy.
practical_advice: When facing a looming threat or challenge, use prayer and recitation as tools to center yourself — choose words that acknowledge your dependence on God while affirming your resolve to act.
Do not wait for others to do the difficult, unglamorous work; leading by example in the hardest moments builds trust and inspires those around you far more than words.
If you feel wronged and are about to face a confrontation, internally affirm both your grievance and your determination to stand firm — this mental clarity reduces panic and builds courage.

title: The Prophet Digging the Trench – Chanting Verses of Ibn Rawaha
summary: During the digging of the trench before the Battle of al-Ahzab, the Prophet was seen carrying dirt until it covered his stomach, and he was heard chanting verses of Ibn Rawaha asking God for tranquility and steadfastness against the enemy.
story: At the (time of) the battle of al-Ahzab, 'the clans', and the trench of the Messenger of God (SAAS), I saw him carrying the dirt from the trench so much that it hid the skin of his stomach from me; and he had much body hair. I heard him reciting verses of 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha as he moved the dirt, 'O God, without you we would not have been well-guided; nor would we have been charitable, nor would we have prayed. So do send tranquillity upon us and make us stand firm when we meet the foe. They have wronged us; if they want trouble, we'll resist them!' He would stretch out the final syllables.
lessons: In times of great hardship, turning to God through prayer and remembrance is the first refuge of the believer.
A leader who works alongside his people and shares their burdens builds genuine trust and uplifts morale.
Expressing one's reliance on God through words and supplication is itself a form of strength, not weakness.
practical_advice: When facing a stressful or overwhelming task, recite a short du'a or dhikr to keep your heart anchored and your spirit from breaking.
Remind yourself vocally of God's blessings and guidance, especially when the work feels endless or thankless.
Find or memorize verses of supplication that speak to your situation so you can draw on them naturally in hard moments.

title: The Prophet's Chant While Digging – 'What a Fine Lord, What a Fine Religion'
summary: Salman al-Farisi narrated that the Prophet chanted words of praise and gratitude to God while digging the trench, declaring that without God they would have suffered and affirming the beauty of their Lord and religion.
story: Salman said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) chanted as he dug the trench, 'In God's name, and through Him we were guided, and if we had worshipped any but Him we would have suffered, What a fine Lord! What a fine religion!'
lessons: Gratitude to God in the middle of difficulty is one of the highest spiritual stations a person can reach.
Affirming the beauty of one's faith during hardship strengthens conviction and prevents despair.
Physical labor done in the way of God carries a dignity and devotion that elevates even the most exhausting work.
practical_advice: In moments of exhaustion or frustration, pause and say something that reconnects you to gratitude, even just a few sincere words.
Develop a habit of praising God not only when things go well but especially when conditions are difficult.
Reflect on what your faith has saved you from, not only what it asks of you, and let that reflection renew your energy.

title: The Prophet's Prayer for the Muhajirun and Ansar While Digging
summary: As the companions dug the trench, the Prophet prayed aloud for the Muhajirun and the Ansar, acknowledging that true good belongs only to the hereafter.
story: Sulayman related to us, quoting Shu'ba, from Mu'awiya b. Qurra, from Anas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke the following while they were digging the trench, 'O God, there is no good except that of the hereafter, so bless the ansar and the muhajirun.'
lessons: Orienting the heart toward the hereafter during worldly hardship provides perspective that makes suffering bearable.
A leader who prays for his people by name and category demonstrates the depth of his care and responsibility.
The trials of this life are temporary; remembering that the real reward lies ahead frees the heart from despair.
practical_advice: When you are suffering through a difficult period, remind yourself that this world's trials are finite and that something better awaits.
Pray specifically for those who are struggling alongside you; naming them in your du'a strengthens the bond of community.
Reframe hardship as a means of earning reward in the hereafter rather than as a sign that God has abandoned you.

title: The Miracle of the Hard Ground Crumbling at the Trench
summary: When the companions encountered an extremely hard patch of ground they could not break while digging the trench, the Prophet called for water, spat in it, prayed over it, and sprinkled it on the ground, after which it crumbled completely.
