story: Jabir b. 'Abd Allah would relate how they had great difficulty digging one patch of hard ground, and so they complained about this to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He called for a pitcher of water, spat in it and spoke over it some prayer that God wished him to say. He then sprinkled the water on the hard ground. Those present swear that it crumbled so much that it could not withstand an axe or a shovel.
lessons: When human effort reaches its limit, the believer turns to God, and God's power has no limit.
Obstacles that appear immovable can be removed through sincere supplication and trust in divine assistance.
The miracles granted to the Prophet were not coincidences but signs confirming his Prophethood and encouraging the believers in their darkest moments.
practical_advice: When you encounter a problem that seems impossible to solve by your own means, make it an occasion for sincere du'a before assuming the matter is hopeless.
Do not let a single stubborn obstacle convince you that the entire mission is unachievable; seek help and keep moving.
Record or remember moments in your own life when an apparently impossible barrier was removed, and let those memories reinforce your trust in God.

title: Jabir's Meal for the Prophet – The Miracle of the Multiplied Food at the Trench
summary: Jabir noticed the Prophet suffering from severe hunger during the digging of the trench and secretly invited him to a small meal of barley bread and goat meat, only for the Prophet to invite all the workers, after which the food miraculously fed hundreds while the pot and dough remained full.
story: 'When the trench was dug, I could see that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was suffering severe malnutrition, so I asked my wife what livestock she had, saying, "Do you have anything? I have noticed that the Messenger of God (SAAS) is suffering serious malnutrition." She produced a bag containing a measure of barley, and we also did have a she-kid. I slaughtered it, while she ground the barley; we both finished at the same time. Then I cut it up and put it in the cooking pot and turned to go back to the Messenger of God (SAAS), when she said, "Don't disgrace me by having the Messenger of God (SAAS) come and bring others who are with him!" I went up to him and, speaking confidentially, I told him, "Messenger of God, I've slaughtered a she-kid of ours and ground up a measure of barley we had. Please come, and bring another with you." At this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) called out, "Hey, everyone at the trench! Jabir has made a feast for us! Let's go to it!" He then told me, "Don't take down the pot or bake the bread before I get there." So I went home and eventually the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived, leading his men. I went to my wife and she berated me, but I told her, "I did just as you asked." She then brought out the dough to us, and he spat on it and blessed it. After that he went over to our cooking pot and spat and blessed again. He told my wife, "Call over a bakeress to bake along with you and keep on ladling out from the pot, but don't take it off the fire." A thousand people were present. And, I swear by God, they ate, then stopped and went away and our pot was still bubbling as before, and our dough was still there too, as it had been."
lessons: A small act of generosity offered sincerely, even when resources are scarce, can be the occasion for great divine blessing.
Embarrassment and anxiety about not having enough to give should not stop a person from offering what they have; God can make any gift sufficient.
When a person's intention is sincere and they trust in God's blessing, outcomes can exceed every reasonable expectation.
practical_advice: Do not withhold generosity out of fear that what you have is too little; offer it sincerely and trust that God can make it more than enough.
When anxiety about being embarrassed or failing publicly threatens to stop you from doing good, remember that God honors sincere effort regardless of outcome.
Practice generosity even in your own times of scarcity, for it is precisely then that the spiritual and communal rewards are greatest.

title: Jabir's Wife's Reassurance – 'God and His Messenger Know Best'
summary: When Jabir panicked that the Prophet was bringing hundreds of people to eat a small meal, his wife calmed him by saying 'God and His messenger know best,' demonstrating profound trust in the Prophet's wisdom.
story: "God only knows how embarrassed I felt! I told myself that he was coming with (many) people for a measure of barley and a she-kid! So I went in to my wife and told her, 'You're going to be put to shame; the Messenger of God (SAAS) is coming to you with everyone from the trench!' She replied, 'Did he ask you how much food you have?' 'Yes,' I replied. 'Well then,' she commented, 'God and His messenger know best!' And so she relieved my great concern.
lessons: True trust in God and His Prophet brings calm where anxiety would otherwise overwhelm, and that calm itself becomes a gift to those around you.
A person of faith who remains composed during a crisis can stabilize others who are panicking.
Surrendering to God's plan, even when it seems logistically impossible, is an act of worship that invites divine intervention.
practical_advice: When a situation spirals beyond what you planned for, pause before panicking and ask yourself whether you have done your part sincerely; if yes, leave the rest to God.
Surround yourself with people whose faith is steady, because their composure in difficult moments can bring you back to your own sense of trust.
Practice saying 'God knows best' not as a dismissal but as a genuine act of surrender, and notice how it shifts your emotional state.

title: Jabir's Evening Invitation and the Feast for All the Trench Workers
summary: Jabir privately invited the Prophet to come home and eat the roasted goat he had prepared, intending only for one or two guests, but the Prophet announced it to all the trench workers, and the food miraculously fed all of them.
story: "We worked with the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the trench. I had a young goat, not fully fattened. So I said how nice it would be to serve it for the Messenger of God (SAAS). I told my wife to grind up some barley for us, and she made some bread with it. I slaughtered the goat and we roasted it for the Messenger of God (SAAS). When evening came and the Messenger of God (SAAS) was about to leave the trench (we were working in it during the day, then going home to our families each night) I told him, 'We've cooked a young goat we had and baked some barley bread. I do hope you will accompany me home.' I had only wanted him to come with me, but when I told him this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) accepted, but then he had someone call out, 'Everyone is to go with the Messenger of God (SAAS), to the home of Jabir b. 'Abd Allah!' At this I said, 'O dear! We belong to God, and to Him do we return!' Then along came the Messenger of God (SAAS), accompanied by his men. He sat down and we brought out the food which he blessed, invoking the name of Almighty God over it. He then ate and the others set about it too. When one group was finished another came until all those working at the ditch had done."
lessons: A generous intention, even when it falls short of the need, is enough for God to intervene and multiply its impact.
The phrase 'We belong to God and to Him we return' is not only for times of death but for any moment when matters exceed our control.
Hospitality offered to one can become a blessing to many when it is offered sincerely and in the name of God.
practical_advice: Make your intention for generosity solely for God's pleasure, and then release the anxiety about outcomes; your role is the intention and the effort, not the result.
When a situation feels catastrophically out of control, recalling 'Inna lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un' can reset your emotional state and return you to trust.
Offer what hospitality you can afford even in lean times, and let God determine how far it reaches.

title: The Miracle of Multiplying Dates at the Trench
summary: A young girl brings a handful of dates for her father and uncle working at the trench. The Prophet places them on a cloth and miraculously the dates multiply, feeding all the workers until they overflow the cloth.
story: Muhammad b. Ishq stated, "Sa'id b. Mins] related to me that it was related to him that a daughter of Bashir b. Sag, sister of al-Nu'msn b. Bashir said, 'My invited me over and gave me a handful of mother, 'Amra, daughter of Raw*, dates, putting it in my dress, saying, "Daughter, take this to your father and your uncle 'Ahd All& b. Ra w ~ b for their lunch." So I passed by the Messenger of God (SAAS), while I was looking for my father and my uncle. He (the Prophet (SAAS)) said to me, "Come over here, girl! What are you carrying?" I replied, "This, Messenger of God, is some dates my mother sent me to deliver for lunch to my father Bashi h. Saw and my uncle 'Ahd Allah b. Raw%a." He told me, "Bring it over here." So I poured out the dates into his hands and they did not fill them. "'He then asked for a cloth and it was laid out for him. He piled up the dates on it and they towered above it. He then told one of his men there to shout out to those working on the trench to come and have lunch. The men all gathered around and began eating from it. But it grew in quantity and when they went off again, it was spilling over the sides of the cloth."'
lessons: Divine blessing can transform the smallest provision into abundance when placed in righteous hands — never underestimate what little you have.
Generosity and sharing, even when resources seem scarce, invites barakah (blessing) that defies ordinary expectation.
Gratitude in moments of hardship, such as physically demanding labor, is deepened when God sends unexpected relief and sustenance.
practical_advice: When you feel your resources are insufficient for the task ahead, give what you have with sincerity — trust that God can multiply small efforts and provisions.
Practice sharing food or resources with those around you even during your own difficult times; this act of generosity can uplift an entire community.
In moments of physical exhaustion or stress, pause and reflect on unexpected blessings that arrive — this cultivates gratitude and resilience.

title: Salman and the Miraculous Flashes from the Rock
summary: While digging the trench, Salman the Persian struggles with an immovable rock. The Prophet strikes it three times with a pickaxe, producing three flashes of light, and announces divine visions of future Muslim victories over Yemen, Syria, and the East.
story: Ibn Ishsq stated, "It was related to me that Salmnn the Persian said, 'I was digging in the trench and one rock was too tough for me. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was nearby, and when he saw me striking it and how difficult was all the trouble I was having, he came down, took the pickaxe from my hands and struck with it. A flash glowed from beneath the pickaxe. He then struck with it again and again it glowed beneath it. He struck it a third time and again it glowed. I said, "By my father and mother, Messenger of God! What is it I see glowing beneath your pickaxe when you strike with it?" He replied, "Did you see that, Salrnan?" "Yes, I did," I replied. He explained, "The first glow was God opening up the gates to Yemen for me. The second was God opening up the gates of Syria and the maghrib, the west. The third was God opening up by it the gates of the East for me.""'
lessons: Hardship and seemingly insurmountable obstacles can be the very moments in which divine signs and future openings are revealed.
The Prophet's personal involvement in the suffering of his companions — coming down to help Salman himself — shows that true leadership means sharing the burden of those you lead.
Hopeful visions of the future, even in moments of exhausting labor and siege, are a mercy from God to sustain the hearts of believers.
practical_advice: When you feel stuck or overwhelmed by a problem that seems too hard to break through, remain steadfast — what feels like an immovable obstacle may be the threshold of a great opening.
Seek support from those around you when a task exceeds your strength; accepting help is not weakness but wisdom.
In times of collective hardship, hold onto a vision of a positive future — allow hope to fuel your endurance through difficult seasons.

title: The Prophetic Announcements of Future Victories at the Trench
summary: As Muslim workers struggle under siege conditions at the Trench, the Prophet announces future victories over Persia, Byzantium, and Yemen after striking a rock, causing conflict between the believers who are strengthened and the hypocrites who mock the promise.
story: 'Amr b. 'Awf stated, "I, Salmm, Hudhayfa, al-Nu'mm b. Muqarrin and six of the an@r were working on forty arms' lengths of the trench. We dug on until we reached al-Nads, where we came across a huge, white rock. It broke our metal tools and gave us great difficulty. So Salmm went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) who was then in a Turkish pavilion, and told him of it. He came and took the pickaxe from Salman and struck the rock so hard it split. From it there came a flash of light so strong as to illuminate all the way between the two lava fields - (at both ends) of Medina, that is. It was like a great lantern on a very dark night. The Messenger of God (SAAS) invoked God's greatness, saying All~huAkbar, 'God is Most Great', as at a victory, and the Muslims did so as well. He then struck it again and the same thing happened, a third time with the same result. Salmm and the Muslims asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) what the light meant. He replied, 'From the fust, the castles of al-Hira and cities of Chosroe were lit up to be seen like the fangs of dogs. Gabriel announced to me that my nation would be victorious over them. From the second, the red castles of Byzantium were illuminated, as though they were the fangs of dogs; Gabriel announced to me that my nation would be victorious over them. From the third, the castles of S a n s were lit up like the fangs of dogs; Gabriel announced to me that my nation would be victorious over them. So be joyful at this news!' The Muslims were delighted, and said, 'Praise be to God! A true omen!' "When the enemy clans appeared for battle, the believers told one another that that was what God and His messenger had promised them and was true, and so their faith and determination were reinforced. The hypocrites said, 'He tells you he can see from Yathrih to the castles of al-Hira and the cities of Chosroe, and that they will be rendered up to you, yet here you are digging the trench and can't even come out into the open!' "And so it was that about them God revealed, '(Remember) when the hypocrites and those with sickness in their hearts say, "What God and His messenger promise us is mere fancy!"'"(s@rat al-Ahznb; XXXIII, v.12).
lessons: Faith means holding to divine promises even when your present circumstances seem to contradict them entirely — the believers at the trench were hungry and besieged, yet the promise of future victories was real.
Those with doubt in their hearts will always find reasons to mock what they cannot see; the test of true faith is trusting God's word beyond what is immediately visible.
Prophecy fulfilled is a source of renewed strength — when God's promises come true, faith and determination are deepened, not just for the individual but for the entire community.
practical_advice: When your current situation feels desperate and hopeless, remind yourself of the promises God has made to those who persevere — let those promises be an anchor for your heart.
Do not let the mockery or skepticism of others erode your trust in God's plan; people ridiculed the Prophet's vision from a trench, yet history vindicated every word.
Document or memorize divine promises from scripture; returning to them during hardship can restore your sense of direction and purpose.

title: Salman Declared a Member of the Prophet's Household
summary: During the digging of the Trench, the Muhajirun and Ansar both claimed Salman the Persian as one of their own. The Prophet resolved the dispute by declaring Salman an honorary member of his own household.
story: The account also says that the rnuhaj~rinand the an@ quarrelled about Salman and that the Messenger of God (SAAS) therefore stated, "Salman is one of us - one of the ahlal-bayt, 'a member (honorary) of the household of the Prophet (SAAS).'"
lessons: Belonging is a fundamental human need, and the Prophet honored that need by personally embracing Salman — showing that spiritual kinship transcends ethnicity and origin.
The Islamic community is built on faith and character, not on tribal lineage or nationality; Salman's story is a permanent declaration of that principle.
A leader who sees the worth of every individual and acts to include the marginalized creates a community where everyone feels they truly belong.
practical_advice: If you feel like an outsider — in your community, workplace, or family — know that your worth is not determined by where you come from but by who you are and what you carry in your heart.
Actively work to include those around you who may feel they don't belong; a single word of inclusion from someone in a position of trust can transform a person's sense of self.
Reflect on the companions of the Prophet who came from vastly different backgrounds — their unity was built on shared values, and you can build the same with those around you.

title: The Miracle of Food Multiplying at the Companion's Home During the Trench
summary: During the Trench campaign, with companions so hungry they tied stones to their stomachs, a man invites the Prophet home. A small amount of food — a kid, some ground wheat, and broth — miraculously feeds wave after wave of companions until two-thirds remains.
story: Al-Tabrmi also stated, "'Abd All& b. Ahmad b. Hanbal related to us, Sa'id b. Muhammad al-Jurmi related to me, Abii Numayla related to us, quoting Nu'aym b. Sacid al-Gharri, that 'Ikrima quoted Ibn 'Abb% as saying, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) dug the trench, his Companions had tied stones to their stomachs they were so hungry. When he saw this, he said, "Can't you find someone who could give us a meal?" A man replied, "Yes." The Prophet (SAAS) then said, "Since you didn't say no, then lead us to h i ." So off they went to the man's house, but he was doing his share of work in the ditch. His wife sent a message to him to come home because the Messenger of God (SAAS) had come to visit them. The man hurried home expressing his concern. He did have a goat with a kid, and the man sprang at the goat. But the Prophet (SAAS) said, "No; there's the kid behind her. Slaughter the kid." The wife prepared some ground wheat she had and made it into dough and baked it. The pot was prepared and she crumbled the bread into the stew in her wooden bowl that served ten and offered it to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions. The Messenger of God (SAAS) placed a fmger in it, invoked God's name and asked Him to bless it, then told them to eat. They did so until they were satisfied. Yet all they had eaten was one-third; two-thirds of it remained. He then told the ten men with h i to go and bring back a like number. They left and the next ten arrived and ate till they were satisfied. He then arose and said a prayer for the mistress of the house, invoking God's name over her and her family. They then returned to the trench."'
lessons: Sacrifice made with sincerity — even from the little one has — opens the door to divine blessing that far exceeds the original gift.
The Prophet's instinct when he saw his companions in physical distress was immediately to seek a solution, modeling that a caring leader does not ignore the suffering of those in their charge.
Invoking God's name and blessing over what you have before partaking of it is a prophetic practice that transforms the ordinary into the sacred.
practical_advice: When you are experiencing scarcity or stress around basic needs, resist despair — offer what you have with a good heart and trust that God's barakah can make it sufficient.
When you are in a position of hosting or giving, do not hold back out of fear that what you have is too little; give generously and leave the sufficiency to God.
Begin your meals with 'Bismillah' — the prophetic practice of invoking God's name over food — as a daily reminder that all provision comes from God and that gratitude should precede consumption.

title: The Prophet Strikes the Rock and Announces Victories Over Persia and Byzantium
summary: During the Trench campaign, the Prophet strikes an unyielding rock three times with a pickaxe, each time producing a loud crash and declaring divine victories over Persia, Byzantium, and Yemen, while the hypocrites mock his proclamations.
story: Al-Tabrmi also stated, "'Abd All& b. Ahmad b. Hanbal related to us... that 'Ikrima quoted Ibn 'Abb% as saying... He asked them to accompany h i to Salman who had a rock in his way for which he had insufficient strength. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Let me be the first to strike it." He said God's name over it, struck it and one-third of it split away. He then said, "God is most great! It's the castles of Syria, by the Lord of the ka'ba!" He hit it again, and another section came away. He said, Alliihu Akbar! "God is most Great! It's the castles of Syria, by the Lord of the ka'ba!" He then hit it again and another section came away. He said, Alliihu Akbar! "It's the castles of Persia, by the Lord of the ka'ba!" Thereupon the hypocrites commented, "So we dig a trench for ourselves while he promises us the castles of Persia and Byzantium!"'"
lessons: In moments of collective hardship, a leader who maintains hope and speaks of a larger vision provides the psychological anchor that keeps a community from despair.
The Prophet's willingness to be the first to strike — to personally take on the hardest task — is a model of servant leadership and solidarity.
Skeptics and cynics will always measure divine promises by present circumstances; faith measures present circumstances by divine promises.
practical_advice: When facing a challenge that seems immovable, approach it with intention and the remembrance of God — begin with 'Bismillah' and take the first strike yourself rather than waiting for someone else.
Cultivate the habit of speaking hopeful visions to those around you who are struggling; a word of genuine hope, grounded in faith, can sustain people through situations that feel impossible.
When others mock your goals or the promises you believe in, do not be destabilized — hold your vision firmly, act with patience, and let outcomes speak in time.

title: The Prophet Declares the Keys of Syria, Persia, and Yemen
summary: Al-Bara' b. 'Azib and Maymun b. Ustad narrate that as the Prophet struck a stubborn rock three times during the Trench digging, he declared receiving the keys to Syria, Persia, and Yemen, swearing by God he could see their castles from where he stood.
story: The hi$& al-Bayhaqi stated further, "'Ali b. Ahmad b. 'Abdiin informed us, quoting A h a d b. 'Ubayd al-Saffar, that Muhammad h. Ghalib b. Harb related to us, quoting Hawdha, that 'Awf related to us, from Maymiin b. U s t ~ d hal-Zuhri, and al-Bars) b. 'Azib al-Ansari related to us, as follows, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had ordered us to dig the ditch, a large, tough rock was in our way but the pickaxes made no impression on it. The problem was brought to the attention of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and when he saw it, he took the pickaxe and spoke God's name. Then he struck it hard and a thud of it broke off. He then exclaimed, "Allzhu Akbar! I have been given the keys to Syria! I swear by God I can see its red castles, by God's grace!" He then struck it a second blow and cut off another third. Again he exclaimed, "I have been given the keys to Persia! I swear by God, I can see the castles of the white cities!" He then struck a third time, speaking God's name and broke the rest of the rock. He exclaimed, "Alliihu Akbar! I have been given the keys to Yemen! I swear by God, I can see the gates of S a n s from where I am now!"'"
lessons: Certainty of faith produces a kind of spiritual sight — the Prophet's vision of distant castles from a besieged trench was a function of his unshakeable trust in God's promise.
Every blow made in the name of God carries purpose beyond what is physically visible; spiritual effort and physical effort are not opposites but complements.
The repetition of Allahu Akbar — God is Most Great — in moments of breakthrough is a reminder that all victory, whether breaking a rock or overcoming a life challenge, belongs ultimately to God.
practical_advice: When you are exhausted and feel your efforts are making no impact, begin again with God's name — the breakthrough may come on the next strike, not the ones before.
Train yourself to say 'Allahu Akbar' in moments of personal breakthrough, however small; this practice of attributing success to God prevents arrogance and builds lasting gratitude.

Visualize your goals not as distant impossibilities but as realities already decreed by God — the Prophet described seeing distant cities from a siege trench, teaching us to see beyond present limitations through the lens of faith.

title: The Prophet's Visions While Striking the Rock During the Trench Digging
summary: During the digging of the trench, the Prophet Muhammad struck a large rock three times, each blow accompanied by a flash of light and a vision of future Muslim conquests over Persia, Byzantium, and Yemen.
story:
the keys to Syria! I swear by God I can see its red castles, by God's grace!" He then struck it a second blow and cut off another third. Again he exclaimed, "I have been given the keys to Persia! I swear by God, I can see the castles of the white cities!" He then struck a third time, speaking God's name and broke the rest of the rock. He exclaimed, "Alliihu Akbar! I have been given the keys to Yemen! I swear by God, I can see the gates of Sana'a from where I am now!"

Al-Nasa'i stated, "'Isa b. Yunus related to us, quoting Damra, from Abu Zur'a al-Saybani, from Abu Sakima, a man from Bahrayn, from one of the Companions of the Prophet (SAAS), who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered the digging of the trench, a large rock got in the way of their work. So the Messenger of God (SAAS) went and took the pickaxe and placed his cloak down in the direction of the trench. Then he said the words, "The word of your Lord will be fulfilled in truth and justice. Nothing can alter His words. He it is who hears and knows" (surat al-An'am; VI, v.115). At this a third of the rock split off. Salman al-Farisi was standing watching. At the blow struck by the Messenger of God (SAAS), there had been a flash of light. Then he hit it a second time and repeated, "The word of your Lord will be fulfilled in truth and justice. Nothing can alter His words. He it is who hears and knows." Another third broke off, and a light again flashed that Salman saw. He then struck a third time, saying, "The word of your Lord will be fulfilled in truth and justice. Nothing can alter His words. He it is who hears and knows." At that the remaining third split up.

'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left, picked up his cloak and sat down. Salman asked him, "Messenger of God, I noticed that each blow you struck was accompanied by a flash of light." The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, "So, Salman, you saw that?" "Yes, I did, I swear by Him who sent you with the truth, Messenger of God." The Messenger of God (SAAS) went on, "When I struck the first blow the cities of Chosroe and their surroundings, along with many other towns were raised up before me so that I saw them with my own eyes." One of his Companions present there then asked, "Messenger of God, please pray to God to grant us victory, give us their progeny as booty and to let us destroy their lands with our own hands." And he did speak that prayer.

'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went on, "Then I struck the second blow and Caesar's cities and their surroundings were raised before me so that I saw them with my own eyes." Those present said, "Please, Messenger of God, pray to God to grant us victory, to give us their progeny as booty and to let us destroy their lands with our own hands." And he did so pray.

'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went on, "When I struck the third blow, the cities of Abyssinia were raised before me, along with the villages there about, so that I saw them with my own eyes." Then he said, "Now Abyssinia what they allowed you, and leave the Turks undisturbed for as long as they so leave you."'"
lessons: Divine promise and prophetic vision can sustain a community through the hardest physical and emotional labor — hope in God's plan transforms exhausting work into an act of worship.

Sharing glad tidings with those who are struggling is a prophetic practice; the Prophet did not keep his visions to himself but announced them to uplift the spirits of those digging the trench under duress.

Trust in God's word — as expressed in the Quranic verse recited with each blow — is itself a source of spiritual power; coupling effort with remembrance of God transforms obstacles into breakthroughs.
practical_advice: When facing an overwhelming task or obstacle, recite words of remembrance and trust in God before and during the effort, just as the Prophet recited the Quranic verse with each strike.
In moments of collective struggle, share encouraging news and positive hopes with those around you to maintain morale and a sense of shared purpose.
Reflect on past instances where seemingly impossible difficulties were resolved through patience and trust in God, allowing those memories to fuel your perseverance in current hardships.

title: Abu Hurayra's Testimony on the Keys of the World's Treasuries
summary: Abu Hurayra narrated that the Prophet Muhammad was given, while sleeping, the keys to the treasuries of the entire world placed in his hands, and later testified that every city conquered by Muslims had its keys given beforehand to the Prophet.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, "A trustworthy source related to me from Abu Hurayra that when these territories were conquered in the eras of 'Umar and of 'Uthman and thereafter, he would say, 'You should conquer what seems appropriate to you; for, I swear by Him who holds Abu Hurayra's soul, you have not conquered any town, and you never will right on up to Judgement Day, the keys to which were not given beforehand to Muhammad (SAAS).'"

Imam Ahmad stated, "Hajjaj related to us, quoting Layth, that 'Uqayl b. Khalid related to him, from Ibn Shihab, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyah, that Abu Hurayra said, 'I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, "I was provided for my mission with words that united and I was made victorious through awe. And while I slept I was brought the keys of all the treasuries on earth and they were placed in my hand."'"

Imam Ahmad stated, "Yazid related to us, quoting Muhammad b. 'Amr, from Abu Salama, from Abu Hurayra, who quoted the Messenger of God (SAAS) as saying, 'I was made victorious through awe and was provided with words that united. The earth was made for me a mosque and a purification; and while I was asleep, I was brought the keys to the treasuries of the world, and they piled up in my hand.'"

In both sahih collections are the words (of the Prophet (SAAS)), "And when Caesar expires there will be no other Caesar after him; when Chosroe expires there will be no Chosroe after him. And by Him in whose hand is my soul, the treasuries of both these shall be used in God's cause."

In the authentic hadith it is stated, "God gathered up for me the earth, all its parts to the east and to the west; all of that He gathered up for me shall be the domain of my people."
lessons: God's support for a righteous mission can come through means that transcend ordinary human power — awe, unified speech, and divine grant — reminding us that ultimate success belongs to God alone.
The fulfillment of prophecy across generations is a source of sustained faith; what seems distant or impossible in one era becomes reality in another, so never lose hope in God's promise.
Gratitude for divine gifts, even those experienced privately or in sleep, is part of a believer's recognition that all capacity and provision ultimately come from God.
practical_advice: When you feel powerless or outmatched in a difficult situation, remind yourself that the strength of your mission does not depend solely on your own resources but on God's support.
Study the fulfilled prophecies in Islamic history as a means of strengthening your faith during periods of doubt or despair — seeing past promises fulfilled builds confidence in future ones.
Practice gratitude for blessings that come quietly and unexpectedly, not only for those that arrive through obvious effort, as a way of deepening your awareness of God's constant provision.

title: The Confederate Armies Besiege Medina at the Battle of the Trench
summary: The allied confederate forces of Quraysh, Ghatafan, and their allies — numbering around 10,000 — advanced and encircled Medina while the Prophet and 3,000 Muslims positioned themselves behind the trench with Mt. Sal' at their backs.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had completed the trench, Quraysh advanced at the confluence of the streams from Ruma, between al-Jurf and Zaghaba. They were accompanied by 10,000 of their Abyssinian mercenaries and their allies of Banu Kinana and people from Tihama. Ghatafan advanced as well, along with their allies from Najd. They came to a halt at Dhanab Naqama, over by the side of Mt. Uhud. The Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims, 3,000 strong, came forth until they had Mt. Sal' behind them. There he encamped, with the trench between his forces and the enemy. The women and children were taken above into the forts."

Ibn Hisham stated, "He placed Ibn Umm Maktum in command of Medina."

I would comment that this makes reference to the Almighty's words, "(And remember) when they came at you from above you and from below you, and when eyes turned dim and hearts rose into throats, and you were having doubts about God" (surat al-Ahzab; XXXIII, v.10).

Al-Bukhari stated, "'Uthman b. Abu Shayba related to us, quoting 'Ubayd, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, 'The words, "when they came at you from above you and from below you" refer to the battle of al-Khandaq.'"
lessons: In the face of overwhelming odds, proper preparation — such as the digging of the trench — combined with a sound defensive strategy reflects the Islamic principle of tawakkul: trust in God after taking every available means.

The Quran's honest description of the fear the believers felt ('eyes turned dim and hearts rose into throats') shows that acknowledging fear is not weakness; what matters is remaining steadfast despite it.
Protecting the vulnerable — the women, children, and those unable to fight — is a communal responsibility, and leadership in crisis includes making arrangements for those who depend on us.
practical_advice: When surrounded by difficulties on multiple sides, focus on the boundaries and defenses you can realistically put in place rather than being paralyzed by the enormity of the threat.

Allow yourself to honestly acknowledge fear and anxiety in difficult situations rather than suppressing them; the Companions experienced these emotions and are praised for their steadfastness nonetheless.
In times of collective crisis, ensure that those in your care — family members, dependents, or vulnerable community members — are protected and accounted for before addressing other concerns.

title: Huyayy b. Akhtab Persuades Banu Qurayza to Break Their Pact with the Prophet
summary: During the siege of Medina, the Jewish tribal leader Huyayy b. Akhtab pressured Banu Qurayza's chief Ka'b b. Asad to betray their treaty with the Prophet, eventually succeeding after persistent manipulation, leading to the breakdown of the agreement.
story:
Musa b. 'Uqba stated, "When al-Ahzab, the clans, positioned themselves around Medina, Banu Qurayza locked their fortresses against them."

Ibn Ishaq stated, "Huyayy b. Akhtab al-Nadri went to see Ka'b b. Asad al-Qurazi, who was their designated contractor and trustee. When Ka'b heard of his coming he locked the gate of his fortress against Huyayy. When the latter asked permission to enter, Ka'b refused to open up for him. Huyayy called up to him, 'Ka'b, open up for me, damn you!' 'Damn you, Huyayy,' he replied. 'You're an evil man! I've made a pact with Muhammad and I'm not going to break my agreement with him. I've never found him to be anything but trustworthy and truthful.'

"Huyayy insisted, 'Damn you, open up so I can talk to you!' 'I will not,' Ka'b replied. Huyayy then said, 'I swear, the only reason you're locking me out is that you're scared at losing your jashisha, your cornbread, of having me eat it with you!'

"This so embarrassed Ka'b that he did open up. Then Huyayy told him, 'Ka'b, I bring you fame forever, and a huge mass.' 'And what is that?' he asked. 'I bring you Quraysh with their chieftains and nobles whom I have left encamped at the confluence of the streams at Ruma, along with Ghatafan, with their chiefs and nobles whom I have positioned at Dhanb Naqama, near Uhud. They have made a treaty that they will not leave until we completely destroy Muhammad and those with him.'

"Ka'b responded, 'What you bring me, I swear by God, is shame forever and a cloud that has lost its water; it thunders and there is lightning, but there is nothing in it. Damn you, Huyayy, leave me to maintain my pact with him; I have never found Muhammad to be anything but trustworthy and honest.'

"'Amr b. Sa'd al-Qurazi spoke too, and did so well, according to the account given by Musa b. 'Uqba. He reminded them of the pact with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and of the treaty made by Ka'b and them to aid him. He said, 'If you won't actually help him, then just leave him to his enemy.'"

Ibn Ishaq went on, "Huyayy continued badgering Ka'b to the extreme until he gave way in agreeing to break his pact with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and fighting him alongside the ahzab, the clans. This was on the understanding that Huyayy would guarantee him that if Quraysh and Ghatafan were to withdraw without destroying Muhammad that he would join Ka'b in his fort and they would share the same fate.

"And so Ka'b b. Asad abrogated and dissolved the pact he had with the Messenger of God (SAAS)."

Musa b. 'Uqba stated, "Ka'b b. Asad and Banu Qurayza required of Huyayy b. Akhtab that he take from Quraysh and Ghatafan hostages who would remain in custody so that no harm would come to themselves if his enemies were forced to retreat after failing to destroy Muhammad. The hostages were to number 90 of their leading men, and Huyayy agreed to this. Thereupon Ka'b b. Asad and Qurayza did break their pact and tore up the document on which it was recorded; the tribes Banu Sa'na, Asad, Usayd and Tha'laba, however, went forth to join the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Peer pressure and social embarrassment can be weaponized to erode a person's integrity; Ka'b initially held firm on the basis of the Prophet's trustworthiness but capitulated when taunted about trivial matters, showing how manipulation often targets pride rather than reason.

Maintaining commitments and honoring agreements is a matter of profound moral integrity; Ka'b himself recognized that the Prophet had always been trustworthy, yet allowed himself to be manipulated away from acting on that knowledge.

Some members of Banu Qurayza, like 'Amr b. Sa'd, chose to speak the truth and counsel their community toward loyalty even when the pressure was immense — a reminder that moral courage in a group setting is rare and deeply valuable.
practical_advice: When someone persistently pressures you to act against your values, recognize that relentless badgering is itself a manipulation tactic; step back and return to what you know to be right rather than yielding to exhaustion or embarrassment.

Before breaking a commitment or agreement, honestly ask yourself whether your reason is principled or whether you are simply responding to social pressure, flattery, or fear — Ka'b knew the right answer but let his emotions override it.

If you are in a position of influence within a community or group facing a difficult decision, speak up clearly for what is right, as 'Amr b. Sa'd did, even if the majority goes another way — your voice matters and may be remembered long after.

title: The Prophet Sends Emissaries to Verify Banu Qurayza's Betrayal
summary: Upon hearing reports that Banu Qurayza had broken their treaty, the Prophet dispatched Sa'd b. Mu'adh, Sa'd b. 'Ubada, and two other companions to assess the situation and confirm whether the news was true.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "When news of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims, he sent out Sa'd b. Mu'adh, he being at that time the leader of al-Aws, along with Sa'd b. 'Ubada, leader of Khazraj, accompanied by 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha and Khawwat b. Jubayr, telling them, 'Go to those people and determine whether what we have heard is true.'"
lessons: Sound leadership requires verification before reaction; the Prophet did not act on rumor alone but sent trusted representatives to confirm the facts, modeling the importance of due diligence in crisis management.
Delegating sensitive inquiries to respected and capable people — those with established relationships and authority — is a sign of wise leadership, not weakness.
In times of siege and danger, gathering accurate information is itself a courageous act; those sent on the mission faced significant personal risk to serve the community's need for clarity.
practical_advice: Before reacting to alarming news or rumors, especially during a stressful period, take deliberate steps to verify the information through reliable sources rather than acting on fear or hearsay.
When dealing with a sensitive conflict or potential betrayal, choose mediators or investigators who are calm, credible, and respected by all parties involved.
During a crisis, assign specific, clear tasks to trustworthy people around you so that the burden of uncertainty is distributed and managed rather than allowed to spiral into panic.

title: Banu Qurayza Breaks Their Pact
summary: Ka'b b. Asad and the Banu Qurayza tribe broke their pact with the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) during the Siege of Madinah, tearing up their treaty document, while some allied tribes remained loyal.
story: Thereupon Ka'b b. Asad and Qurayza did break their pact and tore up the document on which it was recorded; the tribes Bann Sacna,Asad, Usayd and Thaclaba,however, went forth to join the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Betrayal and treachery are tests from God that the believer must endure with patience and trust in divine justice.
Not everyone will abandon you in times of hardship; some will remain loyal and those loyalties are precious gifts.
True character is revealed in moments of crisis — both among enemies who betray and allies who stay faithful.
practical_advice: When someone betrays your trust, take time to grieve the loss rather than suppressing it, then channel your energy into those who remain loyal.
In difficult moments, identify the people in your life who have stood by you and invest in those relationships.
Remember that not all bonds break under pressure — focus on what holds rather than what fractures.

title: The Prophet's Reaction Upon Confirming Banu Qurayza's Betrayal
summary: Upon receiving confirmation of Banu Qurayza's betrayal, the Prophet (SAAS) covered himself in his cloak and lay down in visible distress, before eventually raising his head to announce God's coming victory.
story: "At this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) announced, 'Allahu Akbar! Rejoice, Muslims!' " Mosa b. Wqba stated, "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) covered himself in his cloak, after receiving the news of Banu Qurayza, and went to lie down. He was absent a long time; and his men had become extremely concerned and fearful when they saw him lie down, for they realized no good news had come from Banu Qurayza. But eventually he raised his head and said, 'Rejoice at God's victory and for His help!' "Next morning the two opposing sides closed and they exchanged fire with arrows and stones. Sa'id b. al-Musayyad said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke the words, "O God, I beseech You to fulfil Your pact and promise! O God, if it pleases You, You will (no longer) be worshipped!"'"
lessons: Even the greatest of human beings experienced moments of deep grief and withdrew to process overwhelming pain — this is not weakness but humanity.
After every period of darkness and withdrawal, the believer rises and announces hope — grief is a station, not a permanent dwelling.
Turning to God in prayer during the most desperate moments is the most powerful act of resilience available to the believer.
practical_advice: Give yourself permission to grieve difficult news privately before you must face the world — withdrawal to process emotion is not avoidance, it is wisdom.
When you feel overwhelmed, make a simple supplication or prayer expressing your need for God's help; this act of surrender can unlock relief.
After processing a painful moment, make a conscious effort to re-emerge and re-engage — prolonged isolation can deepen despair rather than resolve it.

title: The Hypocrites' Complaints and Doubts During the Siege
summary: As conditions worsened during the Siege of Madinah, the hypocrites and weak-hearted among the Muslims voiced their doubts about the Prophet's promises and sought excuses to flee, which the Quran directly addressed.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Thereupon the anxiety and fear increased. Their enemy came at them from above and below, so that the Muslims were very confused. Dissension arose, to the point where Mu'attib b. Qushayr, a brother of Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf, commented, 'Muhammad used to promise us that we would eat the treasures of Chosroe and Caesar, yet now we don't feel safe going to the toilet.' Similarly, Aws b. Qayzi said, while in the presence of others of his people, 'O Messenger of God, our homes are open to the enemy. Give us leave to return to our homes; they are outside Medina.' "It is these persons and those like them who are referred to in the words of the Almighty, '(Remember) when the hypocrites and those with disease in their hearts said, "it was all misleading, what God and His messenger promised us!" And (remember) when one group of them said, "O people of Yathrib, there is no place for you here, so go back!" And a group of them did ask the Prophet (SAAS), saying, "Our homes are undefended!" Yet they were not exposed; all they wanted was to flee.' (Surat al-Ahzab; XXXIII, v.12, 13)."
lessons: When faith is shallow, pressure reveals it — difficulty exposes what comfort conceals about a person's true conviction.
Doubt and despair, when dressed in rational-sounding complaints, can spread through a community like a disease and must be recognized for what they are.
God's promises are fulfilled on His timeline, not ours — what appears to be delay or failure may be the buildup to a greater victory.
practical_advice: When anxiety makes you question whether your efforts and faith have any meaning, write down past moments when you saw God's help come through, and return to that list.
Be mindful of who you surround yourself with during a crisis — those who amplify panic and despair can weaken your resolve even when your circumstances have not changed.
If you notice yourself looking for reasons to give up or escape a commitment, pause and ask honestly whether fear, rather than wisdom, is driving that impulse.

title: The Prophet's Offer of a Third of Madinah's Dates to Ghatafan
summary: As the siege intensified, the Prophet (SAAS) considered offering Ghatafan tribal leaders a third of Madinah's dates to persuade them to withdraw, consulting Sa'd b. Mu'adh and Sa'd b. 'Ubada before the idea was rejected.
story: "It has been related to me by 'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada, and from another source I do not doubt, from al-Zuhri, that when conditions there became worse, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent to 'Uyayna b. Hisn and al-Harith b. 'Awf al-Murri — these men being leaders of Ghatafan — offering them a third of the dates of Medina if they were to go home and leave him and his men alone. And so peace talks were held between him and them, to the extent that they did sign a document. However, it was not properly witnessed with true intent for peace, but merely to reduce tension. "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) wished to do this, he sent for Sa'd b. Mu'adh and Sa'd b. 'Ubada, informed them and sought their counsel. They responded, 'Messenger of God, is this something that you wish to do, something God has ordered you to do and which we must put into effect, or is it something you are doing for us?' "He replied, 'It is something I am doing for you. I swear by God, I am only doing this because I see the Arabs shooting at you as if from one bow, and because they are attacking you from all sides. I want to break up their coalition against you.'"
lessons: True leadership sometimes means considering difficult compromises for the welfare of those you are responsible for, even when it is personally costly.
Consulting trusted companions in moments of crisis is a mark of wisdom, not weakness, and often leads to a better path forward.
A leader's willingness to sacrifice their own preferences or resources for those under their care reflects the deepest form of service.
practical_advice: When facing an overwhelming situation, consider whether there is a difficult compromise or cost that could bring relief to those who depend on you, and have the humility to explore it.
When you carry a heavy responsibility, seek counsel from those you trust before making major decisions under pressure — wisdom shared is wisdom multiplied.
Reflect on what you are willing to give up for the people you love and lead; generosity in crisis cements bonds that last a lifetime.

title: Sa'd b. Mu'adh Rejects the Offer and Erases the Document
summary: Sa'd b. Mu'adh passionately rejected the Prophet's proposed concession to Ghatafan, declaring that Islam had honored the Muslims and they would give nothing but the sword, then erased the unsigned document.
story: "Sa'd b. Mu'adh then told him, 'Messenger of God, we used to be disbelievers in God, and worshipped idols along with those people, not worshipping nor knowing God. At that time, they would never have dreamed of eating a single date of ours except as our guests or after purchase. Should we give them our property now, after God has honoured us with Islam, led us to Him and given us esteem through you and through Him? We'll have none of this! By God, all we'll give them is the sword, until God decides between us and them!' "The Prophet (SAAS) responded, 'As you wish.' Thereupon Sa'd b. Mu'adh took the document and erased the writing from it, saying, 'Now let them fight us!'"
lessons: Honor and dignity, once discovered through faith, become something a believer refuses to trade away for convenience or safety.
Gratitude for what God has given you can be the strongest source of courage in the face of pressure to compromise your principles.
Standing firm in one's convictions, even when a seemingly reasonable concession is offered, is sometimes the most powerful statement of faith.
practical_advice: Recall what your faith, values, or community have given you that you once lacked — let that gratitude reinforce your resolve when you are tempted to compromise what matters most.
When facing pressure to give up something precious, ask yourself: am I making this concession out of wisdom, or out of fear? The answer should guide your choice.
Practice identifying your non-negotiables — the values and commitments you would not trade regardless of circumstances — so that when pressure comes, you already know where you stand.

title: Quraysh Cavalry Crosses the Trench and Ali b. Abi Talib Blocks the Breach
summary: A group of Quraysh cavalry including 'Amr b. 'Abd Wudd found a narrow point in the trench and crossed it, but Ali b. Abi Talib led a group of Muslims to block the breach and confront the invaders.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained there with his men under siege but without warfare underway between them and their enemy, except that some of the Quraysh cavalry, consisting of 'Amr b. 'Abd Wudd b. Abi Qays, of Banu 'Amir b. Lu'ayy, 'Ikrima b. Abi Jahl, Hubayra b. Abi Wahb, both of Banu Makhzum, Dira b. al-Khattab b. Mirdas, of Banu Muharib b. Fihr, donned their armour and then went forth on horseback to the positions held by Banu Kinana. There they called out, 'Banu Kinana, prepare for battle! You're going to discover who are the true knights today!' "They charged forward on their mounts, but stopped at the trench saying, when they saw it, 'By God, this is a trick no Arab used before!' They then headed for a narrow part of the trench and beat their horses, which charged over it and carried them to swampy ground between the trench and a fissure. Thereupon 'Ali b. Abi Talib and a group of Muslims came out and blocked the opening through which they had forced their horses."
lessons: Preparation and ingenuity in the face of a powerful enemy can neutralize an apparent advantage — the trench itself bewildered the mightiest cavalry of Arabia.
Courage means responding swiftly to plug the breach when the defenses are threatened, rather than waiting for someone else to act.
When the enemy finds a crack in your defenses — whether in battle, in life, or in faith — the response must be immediate and resolute.
practical_advice: Identify the 'narrow points' in your own life — the areas of vulnerability that need reinforcing — before an adversity forces its way through them.
When a crisis breaches your defenses, act decisively rather than standing paralyzed by fear; swift action, even imperfect, is almost always better than hesitation.
Surround yourself with people who will move toward the breach with you rather than away from it — courage is contagious and so is cowardice.

title: Ali b. Abi Talib's Single Combat with 'Amr b. 'Abd Wudd
summary: The legendary warrior 'Amr b. 'Abd Wudd challenged the Muslims to single combat three times, and Ali b. Abi Talib persisted in volunteering despite the Prophet's initial reluctance, ultimately killing 'Amr and routing the Quraysh cavalry.
story: "'Amr b. 'Abd Wudd had fought at Badr but been badly wounded and consequently had not been present at Uhud. At al-Khandaq he had come out to battle wearing his insignia so that his rank would be evident. Now there on his horse, he cried out, 'Who will accept my challenge to combat?' 'Ali b. Abi Talib, God be pleased with him, came forward and said, ''Amr, you used to swear by God that if any man of Quraysh offered you two choices, you would accept one of them.' 'That is so,' 'Amr replied. 'Ali then said, 'Well then, I invite you to God and to His messenger and to Islam!' 'I don't need that,' replied 'Amr. 'Then', 'Ali continued, 'I invite you to combat!' 'Amr responded, 'Why this, nephew? I swear, I don't want to kill you.' 'Ali replied, 'But, by God, I want to kill you!' "This enraged 'Amr and he leaped off his horse and hobbled it and beat its head. He then advanced at 'Ali and they fought, circling around one another until 'Ali, God be pleased with him, eventually killed him. Their horsemen left in defeat, charging back across the trench in flight." According to Ibn Ishaq, 'Ali b. Abi Talib spoke the following verses on that: "He sought aid from the stone (idol) in his stupidity, while I rightly sought support from the Lord of Muhammad. I felt proud when I left him prostrate like a tree stump amid the fine sand and rocks. And refrained from taking his armour, though if I had been defeated he would have had mine. Do not think that God would abandon His faith and His prophet, you clansmen!" The hadith of al-Bayhaqi recounted in his Dala'il al-Nubuwwa, quoting Ibn Ishaq in another place in the sira, the life of the Prophet (SAAS), as follows, "'Amr b. 'Abd Wudd went forth in full armour and called out, 'Who will meet me in combat?' 'Ali b. Abi Talib arose and said, 'I'll do it, Prophet of God.' 'But that is 'Amr,' he replied. 'Sit down!' 'Amr called out again, 'Will no man accept combat?' He then began teasing them, saying, 'What about that paradise of yours? Don't you claim that any of you who are killed will enter there? So will not any man of you accept combat with me?' 'Ali again arose and asked, 'May I, Messenger of God?' 'Sit down!' he replied. Then 'Amr called out a third time, speaking the verses, 'I'm hoarse from calling out to them. Won't someone come forth? While those thought brave are too cowardly, I've stood here like an accomplished champion. That is why I have always hastened forth before the fighting begins. In a warrior, it is bravery and generosity that are the best qualities.' "'Ali again arose and went over to the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'May I go, Messenger of God?' He replied, 'But it is 'Amr.' 'Even though it is 'Amr!' he insisted. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then gave him permission and he walked out towards him, reciting the verses, 'Do not hurry away, for someone who is not weak is responding to your challenge. He is a man of determination and wisdom, and truth is the resort of all who succeed. I hope that I will make mourning women stand...'"
lessons: Persistent, principled courage — offering yourself again and again despite being told to wait — is not recklessness but a deep commitment to serving what you believe in.
Ali first offered his opponent the path of faith before the path of battle, demonstrating that the believer's first instinct is toward guidance, not destruction.
True bravery is not the absence of fear but the willingness to step forward even when you fully understand the danger before you.
practical_advice: When you feel called to face a difficult challenge that others are avoiding, do not allow repeated discouragement to silence that calling — discern whether it is fear or wisdom telling you to hold back.

Before confronting a conflict, ask yourself whether you have genuinely offered the other party a path toward reconciliation or understanding, so that if confrontation becomes unavoidable, it is truly a last resort.
Reflect on a moment when you had to persist in volunteering for something difficult; what sustained you through the resistance? Return to that source of strength when you face the next challenge.

title: Ali ibn Abi Talib's Duel with Amr at the Battle of the Trench
summary: At the Battle of the Trench, Ali ibn Abi Talib stepped forward to accept the challenge of the fearsome warrior Amr, ultimately defeating him in single combat and calling out 'Allahu Akbar' in victory.
story: bravery and generosity that are the best qualities.' "'Ali again arose and went over to the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'May I go, Messenger of God?' He replied, 'But it is 'Amr.' 'Even though it is 'Amr!' he insisted. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then gave him permission and he walked out towards him, reciting the verses, 'Do not hurry away, for someone who is not weak is responding to your challenge. He is a man of determination and wisdom, and truth is the resort of all who succeed. I hope that I will make mourning women stand over you From the thrust of a spear that will always be mentioned in talk of warfare.' "'Amr asked him, 'Who are you?' 'I am 'Ali,' he replied. "'You mean 'Abd Manafs son,' 'Amr enquired. 'No; I'm 'Ali son of Abi Talib,' he responded. 'Nephew,' 'Amr told him, 'you have uncles who are more mature than you; I am reluctant to shed your blood.' 'Ali replied, 'But, by God, I'm not reluctant to shed your blood.' This angered 'Amr and he dismounted and drew his sword like fire flashing. He then advanced furiously towards 'Ali. 'Ali met him with his leather shield. 'Amr struck it and pierced it, his sword remaining stuck in it while it also struck 'Ali's head. Then 'Ali struck him in his neck vein and he fell down. The dust had risen and the Messenger of God (SAAS) heard someone saying, 'Allahu Akbar! God is most Great!' And so we knew that 'Ali had killed him. 'Ali then spoke the lines, 'Is it against me that the horsemen thus charge; my companions held me back from them. My zeal today and a resolve in my mind, not just in my (mouth) tooth.' "He then went on to speak the line, 'He worshipped the stone (idol) in his stupidity, while I wisely sought aid from Muhammad's Lord.' "Then 'Ali went on towards the Messenger of God (SAAS), his face beaming, and 'Umar b. al-Khattab asked him, 'Did you strip off his armour? It's the best armour among the Arabs.' 'Ali responded, 'As I struck him, he shielded his private parts from me, and I felt embarrassed to despoil my own cousin.' "'Amr's cavalry then left in defeat, charging back across the trench." Ibn Ishaq further stated, as quoted by al-Bayhaqi, that 'Ali struck him at the collarbone, his weapon going on down to emerge from his belly, and that 'Amr died in the trench.
lessons: Courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to act rightly despite it — Ali stepped forward even knowing the danger.
True nobility shows itself even in victory; Ali declined to despoil his fallen cousin out of a sense of honour and decency.
Faith and conviction give strength that physical advantage alone cannot provide, as Ali's calm trust in God contrasted with Amr's fury.
practical_advice: When facing an intimidating challenge, focus on your purpose and values rather than the scale of the obstacle before you.
In moments of conflict or competition, maintain your dignity and sense of honour even after you have prevailed.
Recite words of remembrance or gratitude — as Ali called out 'Allahu Akbar' — to anchor yourself in faith during high-pressure moments.

title: The Polytheists Offer to Purchase the Corpse of Their Fallen Warrior
summary: After a polytheist warrior was killed at the Battle of the Trench, the enemy offered large sums to purchase his corpse, but the Prophet refused to profit from the dead and returned or waived the payment.
story: The polytheists then sent word to the Messenger of God (SAAS), seeking to purchase his corpse for 10,000 (dirhams). But he responded, "He's yours. We don't profit from the dead." Imam Ahmad stated, "Nasr b. Bab related to us, quoting Hajjaj, from al-Hakam, from Miqsam, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'The Muslims killed at the trench a polytheist and were given money for his corpse. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said "Give them back the money for his corpse; his corpse is evil, and so would be the blood-money." He would not accept anything from them.'" Al-Bayhaqi narrated this from a hadith of Hammad b. Salama, from Hajjaj, he being Ibn Arfat, from al-Hakam, from Miqsam, from Ibn 'Abbas, whom he quoted as saying that a polytheist was killed at the battle of al-Ahzab and that a message was then sent to the Messenger of God (SAAS) offering payment of 12,000 (dirhams) for his body. He responded, "There's no good in his body, nor in the price for it." Musa b. Uqba recounted that it was actually the body of Nawfal b. 'Abd Allah al-Makhzumi they requested after he was killed, and that it was for him that they offered blood-money. But he (the Prophet (SAAS)) responded, "He is evil, and so would be the blood-money; God has cursed him and his blood-money. We have no intent to take blood-money for him. And we will not prevent you from burying him."
lessons: Integrity means refusing to benefit from what is morally compromised, even when material gain is on the table.
Generosity and dignity can coexist — the Prophet allowed the enemy to bury their dead while refusing to profit from the exchange.
Principles are not negotiable based on the size of the offer; consistency in values builds lasting trust and moral authority.
practical_advice: When offered something that feels wrong even if it appears beneficial, pause and ask whether accepting it aligns with your values.
Practice letting go of potential gains that come at the cost of your integrity — the peace of a clear conscience outweighs material reward.
In disputes or difficult situations, find ways to be firm on principles while still allowing the other party a dignified path forward.

title: Al-Zubayr's Ferocity in Battle and His Son's Witness
summary: During the Battle of the Trench, young Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr watched his father charge fearlessly across the battlefield from the safety of the fortress, and the father's joyful response upon learning his son had witnessed his bravery became a cherished memory.
story: Al-Bayhaqi narrated through Hammad b. Yazid, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, who said, "At the battle of al-khandaq, I was put in the fortress with the women and children. With me was 'Umar b. Abi Salama, who would bend over so that I could get on his back and observe. I saw my father charging first here, then there, attacking everything that came before him. That evening he joined us in the fortress, and I commented to him, 'Father, I watched what you did today!' He responded, 'So you saw me, son!' 'Yes,' I affirmed. 'Well, may my father and mother be your ransom!'"
lessons: Acts of courage and sacrifice leave lasting impressions on those who witness them, especially children who look to adults for examples of strength.
The bond between parent and child is deepened when they share in difficult experiences, even from a distance.
True bravery is not performed for an audience, yet when others witness it, its impact multiplies beyond the moment itself.
practical_advice: Share stories of perseverance and courage with younger family members — what children witness shapes their understanding of strength and faith.
When you have done something difficult or honourable, allow yourself a moment of quiet gratitude that you were able to rise to the occasion.
In times of fear and uncertainty, recall the examples of those who faced greater dangers with trust in God, and let their example steady your heart.

title: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh Is Struck by an Arrow at the Battle of the Trench
summary: During the Battle of the Trench, the noble companion Sa'd ibn Mu'adh was struck by an arrow that severed the artery of his arm — a wound that would ultimately lead to his death — yet he made a heartfelt supplication to God before it happened.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Abu Layla, that is 'Abd Allah b. Sahl b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. Sahl al-Ansari, brother of Banu Haritha, related to me that 'A'isha, mother of the faithful, was in the fort of Banu Haritha, one of the most secure of the Medina forts, during the battle of al-khandaq. Umm Sa'd b. Mu'adh was with her there. 'A'isha said, 'That was before the veil was imposed on us. Sa'd passed by wearing armour so small that his whole arm was exposed. He was carrying a spear and as he brandished it, he would call out, "Stay awhile and let Hamal watch the fight / There's no harm in death when the time is right!"' 'His mother called out to him, "Hurry along, son! By God, you're late already!"' I commented to her, "Umm Sa'd, I just wish Sa'd's armour was larger." I was afraid he would be struck in those very places; and indeed an arrow was shot at Sa'd b. Mu'adh that severed the artery of his arm.'" Ibn Ishaq went on, "'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada told me, 'It was Hibban b. Qays b. al-'Ariqa of Banu 'Amir b. Lu'ayy who shot at him and when the arrow struck, he called out, "Take that from me; I'm Ibn al-'Ariqa!" Sa'd responded, "May God make your face sweat in hell! O God, if You will prolong this battle with Quraysh, preserve me for it! For there is no people I would rather fight than those who have harmed Your messenger, disbelieved in him and expelled him. But if You decree (continued) war between us and them, then make me a witness to it, and do not make me die before letting me engage in battle with Banu Qurayza!"'" I would comment that God did respond to the plea of His servant Sa'd b. Mu'adh concerning Banu Qurayza. God did please him, and He thus ruled regarding them with strength and ease; and He made it be they who requested that, as will be explained hereafter. He decreed that their warriors be slain and their children be taken captive. And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to him, "You issued judgement concerning them, by the decision of God above the seven heavens."
lessons: Even at the moment of being struck down, a believer's instinct is to turn to God in supplication rather than despair — Sa'd's prayer is a model of trust under suffering.
God hears and answers the sincere prayers of those who dedicate their trials to His cause, as Sa'd's plea was fulfilled before his death.
Physical vulnerability does not diminish inner strength; Sa'd faced his wound with composure, concern for the greater mission, and faith in divine justice.
practical_advice: When you face a painful or frightening situation, direct your energy toward prayer and purposeful intention rather than panic or despair.
Use moments of difficulty as an opportunity to clarify what truly matters to you and to entrust those things to God.
Remember that God's response to sincere supplication may come in ways and timings we do not expect — trust the process even when the outcome is unclear.

title: Safiyya bint Abd al-Muttalib Kills a Jewish Spy at the Fortress
summary: While the Muslim men were fully engaged at the Battle of the Trench, Safiyya bint Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's aunt, took matters into her own hands and killed a Jewish spy circling the women's fortress when the poet Hassan ibn Thabit refused to act.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Yahya b. 'Abbad b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr related to me, quoting his father 'Abbad as having said, 'Safiyya, daughter of 'Abd al-Muttalib, was in Fari', the castle of Hassan b. Thabit and she said, "Hassan was there with us along with the women and children. A Jew passed by and began circling around the castle. Banu Qurayza had begun fighting and had cut off its relations with the Messenger of God (SAAS). There was no one there to protect us while the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims were so engaged with the enemy that there was no one who could leave them and come to us if someone should attack us. So I said, 'Hassan, as you can see, that Jew is circling the castle and I am concerned that he might inform the Jews over behind us of where our weakness lies, now that the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his men are so busy. Go down to him and kill him!' He replied, 'God forgive you, daughter of 'Abd al-Muttalib! You must know I could never do such a thing as that.'" "She went on, 'When he said this to me and I saw I could expect nothing from him, I put on a waist-wrapper then took a cudgel and went down from the fort towards him. Then I struck him with the cudgel till I had killed him. When I had finished with him, I returned to the fort and said, "Hassan, now you go down and plunder him; the only thing that prevented me doing it was his being a man." He replied, "I don't need to plunder him, daughter of 'Abd al-Muttalib!"'"
lessons: Courage and decisive action in defence of others can come from unexpected places — those overlooked as weak are sometimes the ones who step forward.
True leadership means taking responsibility when others will not, even when the situation is frightening and the burden falls on you alone.
A person's worth in a crisis is not determined by their social role but by their willingness to act with resolve when it matters most.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a situation where others are paralysed by fear or unwilling to act, ask yourself what you are capable of doing and take that first step.
Do not wait for validation or permission to protect those who depend on you — act with what you have in the moment.
Reflect on stories of unexpected heroism to remind yourself that ordinary people are capable of extraordinary courage when faith and necessity align.

title: The Muslims Besieged and Unable to Pray at the Battle of the Trench
summary: The polytheists besieged the Muslims for approximately twenty nights during the Battle of the Trench, surrounding them from every side, sending a large detachment that battled all day and prevented the Prophet and his companions from performing the evening prayer.
story: Musa b. Uqba stated, "The polytheists surrounded the Muslims, so rendering them with their squadrons inside a kind of fortress. They besieged them for some 20 nights, coming at them from every side, so that they never knew whether or not (the attack) was over." He went on, "They sent one large detachment towards where the Messenger of God (SAAS) was stationed, and they did battle all day until night came. When it was time for the al-'asr prayer, the enemy force was still at hand and so neither the Prophet (SAAS) nor any of the men with him could perform the"
lessons: Even the most devoted believers can find their regular acts of worship disrupted by overwhelming circumstances — this does not diminish their standing with God.
Sustained pressure and uncertainty are among the hardest trials to endure; the companions' perseverance through twenty nights of siege is a testament to the strength faith provides.
Community and solidarity in crisis are essential — the Muslims endured together, and no one faced the siege alone.
practical_advice: When relentless pressures prevent you from your normal routines of worship or self-care, do not compound the stress with guilt — God knows what you are facing.
In prolonged periods of anxiety or siege-like stress, break the experience into smaller increments rather than trying to see the whole overwhelming picture at once.
Surround yourself with a community of support during extended trials; isolation amplifies fear while shared endurance builds resilience.

title: Missed Prayers at the Battle of al-Khandaq
summary: During the Battle of al-Khandaq, the polytheists besieged the Muslims so intensely that the Prophet and his companions were prevented from performing multiple daily prayers. The Prophet cursed the enemy for this and later led the prayers in sequence after the battle pressure eased.
story: The polytheists surrounded the Muslims, so rendering them with their squadrons inside a kind of fortress. They besieged them for some 20 nights, coming at them from every side, so that they never knew whether or not (the attack) was over. They sent one large detachment towards where the Messenger of God (SAAS) was stationed, and they did battle all day until night came. When it was time for the al-'asr prayer, the enemy force was still at hand and so neither the Prophet (SAAS) nor any of the men with him could perform the al-'asr prayer in the manner they wished. When, at night, the enemy force turned away, people claim that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'They kept us from the 'asr prayer; may God fill their bellies and their hearts (and their graves, according to one report) with fire!' Al-Bukhari stated, 'Ishaq related to us, quoting Rawh, quoting Hisham, from Muhammad, from 'Abida, from 'Ali, who quoted the Prophet (SAAS) as having said at the battle of al-khandaq, "May God fill with fire their houses above them and their graves, for them having kept us from the middle prayer until the sun set."' Then al-Bukhari stated, 'Al-Makki b. Ibrahim related to us, quoting Hisham, from Yahya, from Abu Salama, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, that 'Umar b. al-Khattab came along, at the battle of al-khandaq, after the sun had set, and began cursing the Quraysh unbelievers. He said, "Messenger of God, I could scarcely perform my prayers before the sun had almost set." The Prophet (SAAS) responded, "By God, I didn't perform it!" We then went on down with the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Buthan, and he performed the prayer ablutions, as did we too. He then performed the 'asr prayer after the sun had set and thereafter went on to perform the maghrib, after sunset, prayer.' Imam Ahmad stated, ''Abd al-Samad related to us, quoting Thabit, quoting Hilal, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) engaged the enemy in battle and had not finished doing so when it became too late to perform the al-'asr prayer. When he realized this, he said, 'O God, may you fill with fire the homes and graves of those who kept us from performing the salat al-wusta (the medial prayer).'" So the Messenger of God (SAAS) called for Bilal, gave him his orders, and set about performing the al-zuhr prayer as he would do at its normal time. Then he performed in turn the prayers for al-'asr, al-maghrib and al-'isha'. He did this before he dismounted. At al-khandaq the polytheists kept us from the al-zuhr prayer until the sun had set. Ahmad stated, 'Hushaym related to us, quoting Abu al-Zubayr, from Nafi' b. Jubayr, from Abu 'Ubayda b. 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud, from his father, to the effect that at al-khandaq the polytheists kept the Messenger of God (SAAS) from performing four prayers until such part of the night had passed that God had determined. And so he gave orders to Bilal, who gave the call to prayer. He then set about praying the al-zuhr, followed by the al-'asr, al-maghrib and then al-'isha'.' The hafiz Abu Bakr al-Bazzar stated, 'Muhammad b. Ma'mar related to us, quoting Mu'ammil - meaning Ibn Isma'il - quoting Hammad - meaning Ibn Salama, from 'Abd al-Karim - meaning Ibn Abi al-Mukhariq - from Mujahid, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, that the Prophet (SAAS) was kept at al-khandaq from performing the prayers of al-zuhr, al-'asr, al-maghrib, and al-'isha'. Eventually he gave orders to Bilal who made the call for prayer, and then he performed the al-zuhr. Thereafter, he ordered Bilal to again make the call and then performed the al-'asr. Again he ordered Bilal to make the call and then performed the al-maghrib, after which he once more ordered him to make the call and then performed the al-'isha'. He then stated, "There is not upon the face of the earth any people but you making mention of God's name at this hour."'
lessons: Even in the most extreme circumstances, the obligation to worship God is never forgotten — the Prophet made up every missed prayer in full order.
When unjustly harmed, it is permissible to express righteous indignation and invoke God against oppressors, as the Prophet did with heartfelt supplication.
Consistency in worship, even when delayed by forces beyond one's control, is a mark of spiritual strength and devotion.
practical_advice: When life's pressures cause you to miss your regular spiritual practices, do not abandon them altogether — return to them as soon as you are able, even if delayed.
When you feel wronged or blocked from what is sacred to you, channel your frustration into prayer and supplication rather than destructive reactions.
Remember that difficulty in maintaining your faith practice does not mean failure; the Prophet himself faced this, and his response was to persist with even greater intention.

title: The Prophet Encourages His Companions During the Siege of al-Khandaq
summary: As conditions at the Battle of al-Khandaq worsened and the morale of the Muslims deteriorated, the Prophet gave his companions words of hope, promising them future victory over the Persian and Byzantine empires.
