story: When conditions worsened, so did the attitude of many people and they began saying ugly things. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw the state of his men and their ill mood, he tried to encourage them, saying, 'By Him who bears my soul in His hand, He will give you relief from the distress you are suffering. I certainly hope to circumambulate in safety around the Ancient House and that God will present me with the keys to the ka'ba, and that God will destroy Caesar and Chosroe and that we will expend their wealth in God's cause!'
lessons: Hope is not naive optimism — it is a deliberate act of trust in God's plan even when circumstances appear completely bleak.
A leader's role in times of crisis is to speak truth that uplifts, grounding people in a vision beyond the present suffering.
The most desperate moments in history have often been the turning points toward the greatest triumphs — perseverance through hardship is itself the path to relief.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by your circumstances, seek out reminders of God's bigger plan; the present difficulty is rarely the final chapter.
Speak words of encouragement to those around you who are struggling — your voice of hope in someone's darkness can be profoundly transformative.
Write down or memorize a statement of hope and faith that you can return to when anxiety and despair threaten to overwhelm you.

title: The Prophet's Supplications Against the Clans at al-Khandaq
summary: At the Battle of al-Khandaq, when the Muslims' hearts had nearly reached their throats with fear, the Prophet taught them a supplication for relief and himself stood invoking God against the enemy, after which God sent a fierce wind against the attackers.
story: Concerning the prayers spoken by the Messenger of God (SAAS) against the Ahzab, the Clans. And how God used His might and power to deal with them in response to His messenger (SAAS), and to guard his noble person. He therefore sent their minds into turmoil and thereafter turned a fierce wind upon them and shook their bodies. Imam Ahmad stated, 'Abu 'Amir related to us, quoting al-Zubayr - meaning Ibn 'Abd Allah - quoting Rabih b. Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, from his father, who said, "At al-khandaq, we asked, 'Messenger of God, is there something we should speak? For the hearts have reached the throats!' He replied, 'Yes: O God protect our weaknesses and pacify our fears.' And then God smote with wind the faces of His enemies."' Imam Ahmad stated, 'Husayn related to us, from Ibn Abi Dhi'b, from a man of Banu Salama, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, that the Prophet (SAAS) came to the place of prayer at the battle of al-Ahzab, took off his cloak, and stood with his hands raised and outstretched invoking God against them; and he did not pray.' He (Jabir) then said, 'After that he again came and invoked God against them, then prayed.' It is established in both Sahih collections, from ahadith of Isma'il b. Abi Khalid, from 'Abd Allah b. Abi Awfa, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) invoked God against the clans, saying, "O God, Revealer of the Book, Quick to take account, defeat the clans! O God, defeat them, and shake them!"' In one account the wording is, 'O God, defeat them and give us victory over them!' Al-Bukhari related from Qutayba, from al-Layth, from Sa'id al-Maqburi, from his father, from Abu Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) used to say, 'There is no god but God alone; He gave power to His forces, gave victory to His slave and overcame the clans by Himself. There is nothing after Him.'
lessons: When human strength reaches its limit, turning to God in supplication is not an act of weakness but of the highest wisdom and trust.
The Prophet modeled for his community that heartfelt du'a (supplication) is a real and powerful tool — one that God responds to in ways beyond human capability.
Glorifying God and affirming His oneness even in moments of extreme fear is itself a source of spiritual strength and divine protection.
practical_advice: When anxiety overwhelms you, use this prophetic supplication: 'O God, protect our weaknesses and pacify our fears' — it is a concise, powerful prayer for relief that comes directly from the Sunnah.
In your most fearful moments, physically raise your hands in du'a as the Prophet did — the act of surrender to God through your body as well as your heart can shift your inner state.

Memorize and repeat the tahlil — 'There is no god but God alone; He gave power to His forces, gave victory to His slave' — as a daily reminder that ultimate control belongs to God, not to your circumstances.

title: Nu'aym ibn Mas'ud's Secret Conversion and Strategic Deception at the Battle of the Trench
summary: Nu'aym ibn Mas'ud secretly embraced Islam during the siege of al-Khandaq and, with the Prophet's permission, used strategic deception to sow discord between Banu Qurayza, Quraysh, and Ghatafan, ultimately breaking their alliance.
story: Nu'aym b. Mas'nd then left and went out to Bana Qurayza whose close companion he had been before Islam. He reminded them of his friendship and association with them. They accepted this and expressed their confidence in him. He then told them that Quraysh and Ghatafa were not in the same situation as themselves. The battle area, he reminded them, was their territory and the site of their property, wives and children. He observed that they had nowhere else to go, whereas Quraysh and Ghatafan had come in to do battle against Muhammad and his men from elsewhere. Bann Qurayza, he observed, had joined in battling him even though territory, women and property of the other tribes were elsewhere. This meant that Quraysh and Ghatafan would take any opportunity they could, but if they were unsuccessful, they would return home, leaving the lands of Bann Qurayza between themselves and their enemy. And then Bann Qurayza would have insufficient power to withstand him if they were left alone. He advised them, therefore, not to participate with Quraysh and Ghatafan in the fighting without taking some of the leaders of the two tribes as hostages to guarantee that they would fight on against Muhammad until they defeated him. Bana Qurayza agreed to this. Nu'aym then went to Quraysh and reminded Abu Sufyan and those with him of his friendship for them and alienation from Muhammad. He then told them he had some information he thought he should impart to them and he asked them to keep it confidential. They said they would. He told them that the Jews had regretted the way they had behaved towards Muhammad and had sent him a message telling him of this. They had asked him if he would like them to take hostage some of the leaders of Quraysh and Ghatafan and then hand them over to him for execution. After that, the Jews promised him their support in annihilating them and the Prophet (SAAS) had agreed to this. 'Now therefore,' he went on, 'if the Jews should send asking for some of their men as hostages you should not agree to hand over a single one.' Nu'aym then went to Ghatafan, reminded them of his close ties and friendship and of their trust he enjoyed. They agreed that they trusted him, and he asked them to keep his remarks confidential. They accepted this, and he then told them what he had told Quraysh and advised them to be cautious. When the night of the sabbath of Shawwal in 5 AH arrived, God so arranged it for His messenger (SAAS) that Abu Sufyan b. Harb and the leaders of Ghatafan sent 'Ikrima b. Abu Jahl and some other Quraysh and Ghatafan men to Banu Qurayza and told them that they were not in permanent quarters and that their horses and camels were dying. They asked them to prepare for battle that would destroy and rid them of Muhammad. Banu Qurayza replied that it was a day when they could do nothing; they referred to the fate of some of them who had acted previously on that day (the sabbath). They also refused to participate in fighting Muhammad until they received hostages who would guarantee continued action until he was defeated. They expressed their fear that if their allies suffered severe losses in the fighting, they would retreat home, leaving themselves alone with the enemy on their territory and that they would not have the power to defeat him. When their messengers returned with news of the response made by Banu Qurayza, Quraysh and Ghatafan believed in what Nu'aym b. Mas'ud had told them. And so they sent the message back that they would not deliver a single man over to Banu Qurayza, and that it was up to them whether or not to come out to engage in battle. When the messengers brought them this response, Banu Qurayza believed that what Nu'aym b. Mas'ud had told them was true. They were sure that Quraysh and Ghatafan would seize any opportunities they had, but otherwise would disperse into their own territories, thus leaving them alone to cope with the Muslims. They therefore sent back to Quraysh and Ghatafan that they would only do battle along with them if they were to hand over hostages to them. Quraysh and Ghatafan refused this; God thus created dissension between them and then that night He sent a fierce wind against them, on an extremely cold winter's night that overturned their cooking pots and vessels.
lessons: A single person with sincerity and wisdom can turn the tide of an overwhelming crisis — individual contribution, however small it seems, matters enormously in times of collective struggle.

The Prophet's principle that 'war is deception' teaches that intelligence and strategy are legitimate tools when facing injustice, and that seeking lawful, non-violent solutions is always preferable to direct confrontation.

Divine assistance often comes through unexpected people and seemingly ordinary means — Nu'aym was one man, yet God used him to dissolve a massive coalition, reminding us that outcomes are ultimately in God's hands.
practical_advice: When facing an overwhelming problem, look for the one strategic action — the 'Nu'aym move' — that could resolve multiple issues at once rather than trying to address every front simultaneously.
When you feel powerless against forces larger than yourself, remind yourself that God can bring relief through channels you never anticipated; maintain hope and continue seeking solutions.

In conflict or difficult negotiations, seek to understand what each party fears and desires — misaligned interests among your adversaries can dissolve their unity without requiring direct confrontation.

title: Hudhayfa ibn al-Yaman Volunteers as a Spy on a Cold, Fearful Night at al-Khandaq
summary: On a bitterly cold and terrifying night during the siege of al-Khandaq, the Prophet called for a volunteer to spy on the enemy camp. With everyone paralyzed by fear and cold, only Hudhayfa ibn al-Yaman responded when called upon by name.
story: Hudhayfa replied, 'It was difficult for us, I swear by God.' 'I swear', commented the Kufan, 'if we'd lived at his time, we'd never have let his foot so much as touch the ground; we'd have carried him on our shoulders.' Hudhayfa responded, 'Nephew, I swear I see us now with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at al-khandaq, and after he had prayed for part of the night, he turned to us and asked, "Who will volunteer to go and see what the enemy is doing, and then come back?" The Messenger of God (SAAS) stipulated that if the volunteer would come back, he would ask God that the man be his Companion in paradise. But so severe was the fear, cold and hunger, that not one person volunteered. Since no one had risen, he called upon me, and once he had done so, I had no alternative but to do so. He then told me, "Hudhayfa, go and infiltrate the enemy. Go and see what they are doing, but don't cause any trouble; then return to us." So I did so. The gale and God's soldiers were greatly damaging them, leaving them no fire nor pots, nor tents upright. Abu Sufyan arose and said, "Quraysh, every man check out the person sitting next to him." At this I took the hand of the man next to me and asked him his name. He told me who he was. Abu Sufyan then said, "We're not in a permanent camp, our horses and camels are dying, and Banu Qurayza are at odds with us, and we've heard things about them that we don't like. You can see what we've lost due to the gale, how our cooking pots won't stay upright, and we can't keep fires burning and our tents standing. You should leave, as I am about to do." He then went to his camel which was hobbled, mounted it, struck it so that it arose on three legs since he only unhobbled it when it was standing. If it had not been for the promise the Messenger of God (SAAS) made me make not to cause trouble, I could have shot him with an arrow.' Hudhayfa went on, 'So I returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and found him standing in prayer shrouded in a wrap belonging to one of his wives. When he saw me, he asked me to come in (to sit) at his feet and threw an end of the wrap over me. He then bowed and prostrated, while I was under it. When he was finished, I related the news to him. When Ghatafan heard what Quraysh had done, they hurried off home.'
lessons: True courage is not the absence of fear — Hudhayfa was terrified and cold, yet he answered the call when his name was spoken, demonstrating that bravery is acting despite fear, not in its absence.

The Prophet's tender gesture of covering Hudhayfa with his own wrap after the mission illustrates that great leaders care for those who serve alongside them, and that compassion and warmth are as important as strategic strength.

It is easy to claim heroism in comfort but far harder to act in the moment of real danger — Hudhayfa's story is a reminder to be honest with ourselves about our actual capacity for courage rather than theoretical bravado.
practical_advice: When you are overwhelmed by anxiety and feel paralyzed like the companions that cold night, try to take just one small, concrete action — answer one call, take one step — and trust that momentum will follow.

In moments of exhaustion and fear, remember that the people around you may be equally afraid; extending warmth and care to others (as the Prophet did with his wrap) can restore both their strength and yours.

Before boasting about what you would do in difficult circumstances, reflect honestly on how you respond to the smaller fears and discomforts in your daily life — building courage in small moments prepares you for the larger ones.

title: The Prophet's Du'a of Victory at the Battle of the Trench
summary: During the intense fear and hardship of the Battle of the Trench, the Prophet Muhammad recited a supplication declaring God's singular power, victory, and sufficiency, affirming divine sovereignty over all forces.
story: Al-Bukhari related from Qutayba, from al-Layth, from Sa'id al-Maqhuri, from his father, from Abu Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) used to say, 'There is no god but God alone; He gave power to His forces, gave victory to His slave and overcame the clans by Himself. There is nothing after Him.'
lessons: Turning to God through remembrance and supplication during a crisis is itself a form of action — it realigns the heart, restores perspective, and invites divine assistance.

The declaration that God 'overcame the clans by Himself' teaches that ultimate victory belongs to God alone; human effort is necessary but not the final cause of success, which releases us from the crushing pressure of believing everything depends solely on us.
Maintaining the habit of remembrance (dhikr) in moments of extreme fear and stress, as the Prophet did during the siege, is a powerful anchor for emotional stability.
practical_advice: When anxiety feels overwhelming, pause and recite a short, meaningful phrase that reminds you of a power greater than your circumstances — this interrupts the cycle of panic and reorients your focus.
Memorize and use the supplication 'La ilaha illa Allah wahdahu, la sharika lah, lahul mulku wa lahul hamd, wa huwa ala kulli shay'in qadir' during moments of stress as a practical grounding tool.

Separate your responsibility to act from the responsibility for the outcome — do your part with full effort, then consciously release the result to God, reducing the anxiety of feeling solely responsible for things beyond your control.

title: The State of Fear and Difficulty Endured by the Muslims During the Siege of al-Khandaq
summary: The Muslim community at Medina endured a prolonged state of extreme fear, hunger, and hardship during the siege of al-Khandaq, facing enemies attacking from multiple directions as God described in the Quran.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) and his men remained in that state of fear and difficulty as God described at the attacks of their enemy who came at them from above and from below.
lessons: Being attacked from multiple fronts simultaneously — whether in life's trials or literal battle — is one of the most severe forms of human suffering; acknowledging the difficulty honestly is not weakness but the beginning of endurance.

The fact that God Himself documented the fear and difficulty of the companions in the Quran validates that experiencing fear and hardship does not indicate a lack of faith — it is part of the human condition even for the best of people.

Communities that endure shared hardship together forge bonds of trust and solidarity that cannot be built any other way; the struggle of al-Khandaq united the early Muslim community in an irreplaceable way.
practical_advice: When you feel attacked from every direction in life — financial pressure, relationship strain, health worries all at once — name each stressor separately rather than facing them as one undifferentiated mass; this makes them more manageable.

Read accounts of how the companions endured their trials as a form of perspective and comfort — knowing that even the greatest believers experienced overwhelming fear normalizes your own emotional response to crisis.

In overwhelming circumstances, focus only on what must be done in the next hour rather than the entirety of the problem; the companions held the trench one day at a time, not by solving the whole siege at once.

title: Hudhayfa's Night Mission at the Battle of al-Khandaq
summary: During the Battle of al-Khandaq, the Prophet sent Hudhayfa alone into the enemy camp at night during a fierce storm to gather intelligence. Despite overwhelming fear and cold, Hudhayfa completed his mission and returned safely under the Prophet's protection and prayer.
story: Messenger of God (SAAS) I would have fought alongside h i heroically.' 'Would you, indeed?' Hudhayfa asked him and went on, 'Once we were there at the battle of al-Ahztib with the Messenger of God (SAAS). It was night time and there was a fierce, cold gale blowing. He asked us, "Is there no man who will bring me news of the enemy and be with me at Judgement Day?" When none of us responded, he asked the same question a second and a third time. Then he said, "Hudhayfa, you go and bring us news of the enemy." Since he had called upon me by name, I had no alternative but to get up. He then said, "Bring me news of them without giving them any cause for alarm about me." So I left and made my way to them, feeling as if I was walking through a public bath! I found Abn Sufyan there warming his back to the fire. I placed an arrow in the centre of my bow and was about to release it when I remembered how the Messenger of God (SAAS) had told me not to give them any alarm about him. If I had shot it, I would have hit him. I then left to return to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and again the feeling of walking in a public bath overcame me. When I arrived back, I felt very cold and chilled once again. I told this to him and he put over me part of the cloak he was wearing as he prayed. I slept right on through till morning, when he spoke to me, saying, "Up you get, sleepy-head!"'
lessons: True courage is not the absence of fear — Hudhayfa was terrified and cold, yet he obeyed and completed his mission, showing that bravery means acting despite fear.
Obedience to a trusted leader, especially when called by name, carries a sense of personal responsibility that can move us beyond paralysis into action.
The Prophet's prayer for Hudhayfa's protection and his act of covering him with his own cloak illustrate that spiritual support and human care can transform a person's inner state entirely.
practical_advice: When you feel frozen by fear or anxiety, remind yourself that being chosen or trusted for a task means others believe in your capacity — let that belief carry you forward.
Before undertaking something frightening, pause and ask God for specific protection, naming the directions of your vulnerability, as the Prophet did for Hudhayfa.
After a difficult ordeal, allow yourself to rest and recover fully before resuming obligations — the Prophet let Hudhayfa sleep through till morning without reproach.

title: The Darkness, Cold, and Despair of the Muslim Camp During al-Khandaq
summary: At the Battle of al-Khandaq, the Muslim camp endured an extraordinarily severe night of darkness, violent winds, freezing cold, and fear for their families, while deserters slipped away and only about 300 men remained with the Prophet.
story: 'You shouldn't wish for that. There we were, drawn up in battle array, keeping down, while Aba Sufysn and his forces were above us, while the Jews of Bana Qurayza were down below us, making us fear for our families. We never experienced any night so black and violent as that; the winds blew fiercely with great explosions like thunderbolts. It was so dark we couldn't even see our fingers. "'The backsliders began asking the Prophet (SAAS) permission to withdraw, saying that their homes were exposed -though they were not exposed. He gave his permission to leave to each person who asked and so they would slip away. We were some 300 men in number. The Messenger of God (SAAS) received us one by one until eventually my turn came; I had nothing on to protect me from the enemy and the cold except a woollen coat of my wife that came down no further than my knees. When he came to me I was kneeling down. He asked, "And who is this?" "Hudhayfa," I replied, "Hudhayfa, eh?" "Yes, Messenger of God," I responded, keeping down close to the ground and unwilling to get up. But I did rise and he said, "There's activity among the enemy; bring me news of them."'
lessons: Extreme collective hardship — darkness, cold, fear, and abandonment by companions — tests the soul in ways that reveal who we truly are and what we truly believe.
Watching others desert does not obligate us to do the same; remaining steadfast when the majority flee is a profound act of faith and character.
Even in the most exposed and vulnerable state, being seen and personally addressed by a leader who trusts you can restore dignity and purpose.
practical_advice: In moments when you feel surrounded on all sides and abandoned by those you relied on, focus on what is still within your control — your presence, your steadiness, your next small action.
When overwhelmed, resist the urge to compare your situation to others who seem to have it easier; your endurance in difficulty has its own unique weight and reward.
Acknowledge honestly to yourself when you are afraid and inadequately resourced, as Hudhayfa did, rather than pretending strength — honesty about vulnerability is the first step to genuine courage.

title: The Prophet's Prayer for Hudhayfa Before His Mission
summary: Before sending Hudhayfa into the enemy camp alone, the Prophet prayed specifically for his protection from every direction, and Hudhayfa felt the fear and cold completely leave him as a result.
story: 'When I left, the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke the words, "O God, protect him from danger from his front and rear, from his left and his right, from above him and from below him." And, I swear, I felt from then on entirely empty of the fear and cold God had previously created inside me! As I was turning to leave, he called out, "Hudhayfa, don't cause any disturbance in the enemy before you come back to me."'
lessons: Specific, sincere supplication — naming each direction of danger — demonstrates a depth of care and intentionality that itself becomes a source of comfort and strength for the one being prayed for.
Fear and physical suffering are not permanent states; God can lift them completely and instantly when the time and purpose are right.
The Prophet's final instruction — not to cause disturbance — shows that even miraculous inner peace must be coupled with disciplined, purposeful action.
practical_advice: When praying for someone facing a difficult or dangerous situation, be specific and thorough in your supplication rather than vague, covering all dimensions of their vulnerability.
If you experience a sudden and unexpected calm in the midst of crisis, receive it as a gift and use it purposefully rather than questioning it or wasting it.
Pair your trust in God's protection with careful, disciplined conduct — spiritual reliance and practical wisdom are meant to work together, not substitute for each other.

title: Hudhayfa Enters the Enemy Camp and Restrains Himself from Shooting Abu Sufyan
summary: Deep inside the enemy camp at night, Hudhayfa identified Abu Sufyan by the firelight, drew his bow to shoot him, but restrained himself out of obedience to the Prophet's instruction not to cause any disturbance before returning.
story: 'As I approached the enemy camp, I saw the light of a fire they had burning. There I saw a dark-skinned, heavy-set man holding his hands out above the fire and then wiping them on his waist, saying, "Get the mounts ready!" I had not known Abii Sufym before that; I took out an arrow with white feathered flights from my quiver and positioned it in my how to shoot it in the light of the fire, but then remembered how the Messenger of God (SAAS) had told me not to cause any disturbance before I returned to hi. So I stopped and returned the arrow to my quiver. Then I mustered up my courage and entered their camp. Those men closest to me were of Banii 'Amir, and they were saying that they should mount up and leave, since they had no permanent quarters there and that the wind was blowing straight through their camp. I could hear the sounds of the stones raised by the wind striking their mounts and their equipment. I left and went on my way back to the Messenger of God (SAAS). Half way or thereabouts, I came across 20 or so horsemen, fully shrouded, who told me, "Tell your master that God has saved him!"'
lessons: The highest form of self-discipline is restraining a powerful urge — even one that seems justified and beneficial — out of loyalty to a prior commitment and a trusted instruction.
Obedience in the most critical, high-stakes moment is what separates a trustworthy person from a merely capable one.
God's deliverance often comes through unseen means — the wind disrupting the enemy camp, the horsemen bearing good news — when the servant fulfills their part of the mission faithfully.
practical_advice: When you are in the middle of a high-pressure situation and feel the impulse to act outside your agreed boundaries, pause and recall the specific instruction you were given — the temptation to do more than asked can sometimes undo everything.
Practice restraint in small daily situations so that it becomes available to you in moments of intense pressure when it matters most.
Look for signs of God's help in the circumstances around you — a shift in the environment, an unexpected encounter, a change in an adversary's plans — as reminders that you are not acting alone.

title: Hudhayfa Returns to the Prophet and Is Wrapped in His Blanket
summary: Returning from his spy mission, Hudhayfa found the Prophet wrapped in a blanket in prayer, was drawn near and covered with it, reported on the enemy's withdrawal, and then God revealed verses of Surah al-Ahzab confirming divine intervention.
story: 'So I returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and found hi wrapped up in a blanket, praying. No sooner was I back than I felt terribly cold again and began shivering. As he prayed, the Messenger of God (SAAS) gestured to me and when I drew near him, he spread his blanket out over me. When something troubled the Messenger of God (SAAS), he would pray. I made my report about the enemy and how they had been moving out when I left the camp. Then Almighty God revealed, "O you who believe, remember God's favour to you, when troops came to you and We sent down against them a wind and (Our) troops you could not see. God is fully aware of what you do ..." up to and including, "And God was sufficient for the believers in battle. God was sufficient for the believers in battle. God is Almighty, Powerful"' (siirat al-Ahznb; XXXIII, v.9-25). That is, God removed their enemy from them by the wind He sent against them and the troops of angels and others He sent to them.
lessons: The Prophet's first response to trouble was prayer — not panic, not hasty action — modeling that turning to God is the most powerful form of problem-solving available.
Sharing one's physical covering with someone cold and shivering is a profound act of human compassion that costs the giver real comfort and communicates deep care.
Divine intervention does not always come in the form we expect — armies and weapons were not what defeated the Ahzab, but wind and unseen forces — reminding us that ultimate outcomes belong to God.
practical_advice: When you are in a crisis and waiting for news or results you cannot control, fill that waiting time with prayer and remembrance of God rather than anxiety and speculation.
Offer tangible physical comfort to someone returning from a difficult or exhausting experience before asking for their report — warmth, rest, and care come first.
When reflecting on how a difficult situation resolved, look for the role of factors entirely outside human control, and let that recognition deepen your gratitude and reliance on God.

title: The Prophet's Declaration After al-Khandaq: 'Now We Attack Them'
summary: After the withdrawal of the Ahzab from the trench, the Prophet declared that Quraysh would never again initiate war against the Muslims, but the Muslims would go to them — a turning point that marked the end of Quraysh's offensive power.
story: As Muhammad b. Ishnq, God be pleased with him, stated, "When the troops at al-khandaq left there, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, so it has been related to me, 'After this year, Quraysh will not go to war against you, but you will against them.'" He went on, "And Quraysh did not attack thereafter, whereas they (the Muslims) did attack them, until God gave him victory over Mecca." This report comes from Ibn Ishnq. Imam Ahmad stated, "Yahya related to us, from Sufyan, that Abu Ishaq related to him, 'I heard Sulayman b. Surad, God be pleased with him, say, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'Now we will attack them, but not they us."""' Al-Bukhari related this thus from a hadith of Isma'il and Sufyan al-Thawri, both of whom quoted Abu Ishaq al-Sabi'i, from Sulayman b. Sard.
lessons: A moment of divine rescue is also a turning point in history — recognizing it as such and naming it clearly gives people a renewed sense of direction and momentum.
The shift from defensive endurance to confident initiative is a natural progression in the journey of those who trust in God — suffering is not the permanent state of the believer.
Leaders who read the spiritual and strategic landscape clearly, and communicate it plainly to their people, give those people the gift of hope grounded in reality.
practical_advice: After surviving a prolonged period of difficulty and threat, take time to consciously mark the turning point and acknowledge that the dynamic has changed — this prevents you from continuing to live in a defensive, fearful posture after the danger has passed.
Use periods of relief after hardship to build capacity and initiative rather than simply collapsing into passivity — recovery and preparation are active, not just restful.
When you sense that a long struggle is shifting in your favor, communicate that clearly to those around you who are still operating from fear and exhaustion.

title: The Martyrs and Casualties of the Battle of al-Khandaq
summary: Ibn Ishaq records the names of the Muslims martyred and the polytheists killed at the Battle of al-Khandaq, noting the relatively small number of deaths on both sides given the scale of the siege.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Three Muslims of Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal were martyred at the battle of al-kbandaq; they were Sa'd b. Mu'adh - extensive information about his death will follow - Anas b. Aws b. 'Atik b. 'Amr, and 'Abd Allah b. Sahl. Also killed were al-Tufayl b. al-Nu'man, Tha'laba b. Ghanama, both of Jashm and of Sulaym, and Ka'b b. Zayd al-Najjari, who was killed by a stray arrow. "Three of the polytheists were killed. These were Munabih b. 'Uthman b. 'Ubayd b. al-Sabbaq b. 'Abd al-Dar, who was struck by an arrow and later died from his wound in Mecca, Nawfal b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Mughira who charged across the trench on his horse and became bogged down and killed there; the polytheists offered a large sum for his body, as is related above. And there was 'Amr b. 'Abd Wudd al-'Amiri, who was killed by 'Ali b. Abi Talib."
lessons: Every name recorded among the martyrs represents a complete human life and a family's grief — honoring those names is an act of moral memory that prevents sacrifice from being abstracted into mere statistics.
The relatively small number of casualties compared to the scale of the siege reflects that divine protection can limit harm even in the midst of overwhelming threat.
Remembering those who gave their lives in a cause they believed in is a source of both humility and motivation for those who come after them.
practical_advice: When reflecting on collective hardship, take time to remember specific individuals who bore the greatest cost — this keeps compassion personal and prevents emotional numbness.
Grieve losses fully and specifically rather than rushing past them in the relief of survival — those who were lost deserve to be mourned with care and named with honor.
Let the memory of those who sacrificed before you inform the seriousness with which you approach your own commitments and responsibilities.

title: Gabriel Commands the Prophet to March Against Banu Qurayza
summary: Immediately after returning from the trench, while the Prophet and Muslims had laid down their arms, the angel Gabriel appeared and commanded the Prophet to march at once against Banu Qurayza for their betrayal during the siege.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq, God be pleased with him, stated, "Next morning the Messenger of God (SAAS) left the trench accompanied by the Muslims and returned to Medina, there laying down their arms. "At the noon prayer, Gabriel, so al-Zuhri told me, came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), wearing a turban embroidered in silk and gold, riding a mule with a saddle adorned with a brocade cloth, and asked, 'Have you put down your weapons then, Messenger of God?' 'Yes,' he replied. 'Well,' Gabriel informed him, 'the angels have not yet put down theirs; I've come to you now from pursuing the enemy. God commands you, Muhammad, to go against Banu Qurayza. I myself am heading for them and am going to shake them up!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then summoned a muezzin and an announcement was made: 'All those who hear and obey should perform the al-'asr prayer only among Banu [Qurayza].'"
lessons: Justice and accountability do not wait for convenience — the command to address Banu Qurayza's betrayal came before the Muslims had even rested from the siege, showing that upholding trust and covenant cannot be indefinitely deferred.
The image of Gabriel still armed while the Muslims had laid down their weapons is a powerful reminder that the spiritual and moral work of justice continues beyond the physical battle.

Leaders sometimes must redirect their people from rest to action precisely when rest seems most deserved — and doing so with clarity and immediacy is itself a form of mercy, because delay can allow injustice to deepen.
practical_advice: When you have just completed something enormously difficult and are tempted to fully disengage, remain open to the possibility that an immediate next step is being presented to you — exhaustion is real, but so is the cost of delay in matters of justice.
When accountability is required after a betrayal of trust, act promptly and clearly rather than allowing the moment to pass in the name of generosity or fatigue.

Follow through on commitments even when it is physically and emotionally costly to do so — the Prophet mobilized immediately despite the Muslims' exhaustion, modeling that integrity does not pause for convenience.

title: Gabriel Commands the March to Banu Qurayza
summary: Immediately after the Battle of the Trench, the angel Gabriel appeared to the Prophet still dusty from battle and commanded him to march against Banu Qurayza without delay, even as the Prophet had already laid down his arms and bathed.
story: silk and gold, riding a mule with a saddle adorned with a brocade cloth, and asked, 'Have you put down your weapons then, Messenger of God?' 'Yes,' he replied. 'Well,' Gabriel informed him, 'the angels have not yet put down theirs; I've come to you now from pursuing the enemy. God commands you, Muhammad, to go against Banu Qurayza. I myself am heading for them and am going to shake them up!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then summoned a muezzin and an announcement was made: 'All those who hear and obey should perform the al-'asr prayer only among Bana Qurayza.'" Ibn Hisham stated that he placed Ibn Umm Maktum in command over Medina. Al-Bukhari stated, "It was related to me by 'Abd Allah b. Aba Shayba, quoting Ibn Numayr, from Hisham, from his father, who quoted 'A'isha as saying, 'When the Prophet (SAAS) returned from the trench and he had put down his arms and taken a bath, Gabriel came to him and said, "So you have put down your arms! We've not done so, I swear by God! Go forth to them!" "Where to?" he asked. "Over here." Gabriel said, gesturing towards Bana Qurayza. And so the Prophet (SAAS) did go forth.'" Ahmad stated, "Hasan related to us, quoting Hammad b. Salama, from Hisham b. Urwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had disposed of the ahzab, he entered the bathhouse to bathe. Then Gabriel came and I could see, across the house, that his head was reddened with dust. He said, "Muhammad, you've laid down your arms then!" "We have laid down our arms," he replied. Gabriel said, "Well, we've not laid down ours yet. Get up after Banu Qurayza!"'" Al-Bukhari then stated, "Musa related to us, quoting Jarir b. Hazim, from Humayd b. Hilal, from Anas b. Malik, who said, 'It was as though I could see the dust rising in the street of Bana Ghanm (in Medina) due to the marching of Gabriel's troops when the Messenger of God (SAAS) set out against Banu Qurayza.'" Al-Bayhaqi related, through 'Abd Allah al-Umari, from his brother Ubayd Allah, from al-Qasim b. Muhammad, from 'A'isha, (who stated) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was with her, '... and while we were there inside the house, in came a man who greeted us. The Messenger of God (SAAS) got up in a fright, and I arose too; suddenly, there was Dihya al-Kalbi. He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)), said, "This is Gabriel! He has ordered me to attack Banu Qurayza. He said to me, 'You have put down your arms, but we have not.'" We had pursued the polytheists until we reached Hamra' al-Asad, following the return of the Messenger of God (SAAS) from al-khandaq. So the Messenger of God (SAAS) arose in great concern and said to his Companions, "I require you not to perform the al-'asr prayer until you arrive at Banu Qurayza."'
lessons: Even after completing an exhausting trial, divine duty may call us immediately onward — rest is not always available when purpose demands action.
The Prophet's immediate and obedient response to a command that arrived during a moment of rest teaches that true commitment to one's values does not wait for a convenient moment.
Seeing Gabriel appear covered in dust was a powerful reminder that the unseen forces supporting truth are always active; the believer is never truly alone in their struggle.
practical_advice: When you feel you have finally finished something difficult and deserve rest, and a new responsibility suddenly arrives, take a moment to acknowledge the feeling of overwhelm before responding — then take the next single step forward rather than focusing on the full weight of what lies ahead.
If you are anxious about being called to face something difficult without adequate preparation, remind yourself that support often comes from sources you cannot fully see or measure.

After completing a major stressor, before allowing yourself to fully decompress, briefly assess whether any immediate obligations remain — addressing them promptly prevents the compounding anxiety of unfinished duty.

title: The Dispute Over the Al-Asr Prayer on the March to Banu Qurayza
summary: On the urgent march to Banu Qurayza, the prayer time for al-asr arrived while the Muslims were still travelling. The companions disagreed over whether to pray immediately or wait, and the Prophet did not rebuke either group.
story: Al-Bukhari also stated, "'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. Asma' related to us, quoting Juwayriyya b. Asma', from Nafi', from Ibn Umar, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated at the battle of al-Ahzab, "Let no one perform the al-'asr prayer unless it be among Banu Qurayza!" Time for the al-'asr prayer came for some, while they were still travelling. And some of these decided not to perform the prayer before arriving, while others decided to pray then and that they had not been required to wait. This was reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS), but he was not severe with any one of them.'" The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated, "Abu 'Abd Allah al-Hafiz and Abu Bakr Ahmad b. al-Hasan al-Qadi both stated, 'Abu al-Abbas Muhammad b. Ya'qub related to us, quoting Muhammad b. Khalid b. 'Ali, from Bishr b. Harb, from his father, (who said) "Al-Zuhri related to us that 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Abd Allah b. Ka'b b. Malik informed him that his uncle 'Ubayd Allah told him that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from fighting the al-ahzab, he took off his breast-plate, washed and bathed. Then Gabriel, upon whom be peace, appeared before him and said, 'Some warrior you are! It seems you have taken off your breast-plate! Well, we haven't done that yet!'" "At this, the Prophet (SAAS) got up in a fright and insisted that everyone should refrain from performing the al-'asr prayer unless it be among Banu Qurayza. "And so the Muslims dressed in their armour. However, they arrived at Banu Qurayza only after the sun had set. At sunset, disputes arose. Some maintained that since the Messenger of God (SAAS) had told them not to pray the al-'asr unless it be at Banu Qurayza, there could be no sin in them complying. One group did pray out of their caution, while others refrained from praying until the sun had set, after which they prayed among Banu Qurayza; they too acted out of caution. The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not berate either one of the groups."' The scholars differ over which of the two groups was correct that day. In fact the consensus is that each of them was worthy and justified in their actions and not to be severely criticized. One group of scholars, however, maintains that those who postponed the prayer from its predetermined time until they could perform it among Banu Qurayza were correct, because their order to postpone it that day was specific and special and therefore should have been given precedence over the normal time for which it was determined under the law. Abu Muhammad b. Hazm al-Zahiri stated in his biography (of the Prophet (SAAS)), "God knows that had we been there we would not have performed the al-'asr prayer unless it had been among Banu Qurayza, even if it would have had to be several days thereafter!" This statement of his accords with the principle he holds fundamental, to act in accord with what is apparent. Another group of scholars considered, on the contrary, that those who performed the prayer at its regular time while they were travelling were the ones who were correct because they had understood that the intent was to hasten their journey to Banu Qurayza and not to postpone the prayer. They acted in accord with the precepts suggesting the superiority of prayer being performed at its proper time, even though they understood what the legislator had wanted. Because of this, he did not berate them or order them to repeat the prayer at the time to which it had been changed that day, as the others claimed. Those who did postpone it were forgiven, since they acted in accord with their understanding; most things they were ordered to do they did in fact accomplish.
lessons: When sincere, thoughtful people of good faith reach different conclusions about how to act in an ambiguous situation, both can be honoring the spirit of what is right — harshness and condemnation in such cases is unjustified.
The Prophet's refusal to rebuke either group demonstrates that God judges by sincere intention and earnest effort, not merely by outward correctness of action.
Ambiguity and difficult judgment calls are a natural part of life; dwelling in guilt over a sincere decision made under pressure is often unnecessary and counterproductive.
practical_advice: When you face a situation where two conscientious choices are both defensible and you must pick one under pressure, choose according to your best understanding, then release the guilt — second-guessing a sincere decision made in good faith is rarely productive.
If you find yourself in disagreement with someone who made a different but equally sincere choice in a difficult moment, practice extending to them the same mercy you would want extended to yourself.

Use moments of ambiguity as opportunities to reflect on your core values and intentions rather than fixating on whether you selected the 'correct' option — integrity of intention is what grounds you when circumstances are unclear.

title: Ali Shields the Prophet from Harsh Words at Banu Qurayza
summary: As the Prophet approached Banu Qurayza, Ali went out to meet him and urged him to turn back, having heard the tribe speak offensively about the Prophet and his wives, wishing to spare him the pain of hearing it.
story: Ibn Ishaq then stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) thereafter sent forward 'Ali b. Abi Talib bearing his banner and the men hurried towards it." Musa b. Uqba stated in his work on the maghazi, the military expeditions, quoting al-Zuhri, "While the Messenger of God (SAAS) was in his bathroom, so they claim, having combed out one side of his hair, Gabriel came to him riding a horse and dressed in his breast-plate. He halted at the door of the mosque where the funeral biers are placed. The Messenger of God (SAAS) came out to him, whereupon Gabriel said, 'May God forgive you, have you put down your arms?' 'Yes,' he replied. Gabriel commented, 'Well, we've not put ours down ever since your enemy came at you; and I'm continuing to pursue them until God defeats them.' And they say that there was actually dust present upon Gabriel's face. He then went on to say, 'God commanded you to fight Banu Qurayza, and I will pursue them along with the angels accompanying me; we will make their fortresses shake around them. Go forth you too, with your men!' "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) did follow after Gabriel. He passed by a seated group of Banu Ghanam who were awaiting the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he asked them whether a horseman had recently passed them. They replied that Dihya al-Kalbi had passed them on a white horse, seated on a saddle cloth or a piece of velvet brocade, and wearing his breast-plate. They say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'That was Gabriel!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to liken Dihya al-Kalbi to Gabriel. He then said, 'Join me at where Banu Qurayza are, and perform the al-'asr prayer among them.' "And so they arose, along with those other Muslims God wished, and they set off for Banu Qurayza. The time for the al-'asr prayer came while they were en route, and they remembered the prayer. Some of them asked others, 'Don't you know that the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered you to perform the al-'asr prayer among Banu Qurayza?' Others said, 'It's the prayer (time).' And so some did pray while others postponed it until they could perform it among Banu Qurayza, after the sun had set. They informed the Messenger of God (SAAS) which of them had performed it earlier and those who had postponed it, and people say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) did not berate either of the two groups. "When 'Ali saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) approaching, he met him and said to him, 'Go back, Messenger of God, God will take care of the Jews for you.' 'Ali had heard from them some ugly talk with reference to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his wives, may God be pleased with them, and he disliked for him to hear this.
lessons: True loyalty and love sometimes expresses itself not in words of flattery but in quietly shielding someone you care about from pain they do not need to carry.
The act of trying to protect someone from hurtful words — rather than inflaming the situation by repeating them — reflects emotional maturity and deep care.
Leaders and those who carry heavy responsibilities are still human beings who can be wounded by harsh words; those close to them have a meaningful role in protecting their dignity.
practical_advice: When someone you care about is walking toward a situation that you know will hurt them emotionally, gently and honestly let them know without over-dramatizing — offer your support rather than forcing the issue.
If you have heard hurtful things said about a person you love, consider carefully whether repeating those words to them serves any constructive purpose before choosing to share them.

Recognize that protecting a person's peace in a difficult moment is a form of service — not every piece of information needs to be passed on, and choosing what not to say can be as important as choosing what to say.

title: Ali Intercepts the Prophet Before Banu Qurayza
summary: When the Prophet approached Banu Qurayza, Ali met him and urged him to turn back, having heard ugly speech from the Jews about the Prophet and his wives. The Prophet perceived what had happened and insisted on continuing.
story: When 'Ali saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) approaching, he met him and said to him, 'Go back, Messenger of God, God will take care of the Jews for you.' 'Ali had heard from them some ugly talk with reference to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his wives, may God be pleased with them, and he disliked for him to hear this. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him why he was advising him to go back, but 'Ali concealed from him what he had heard of them. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'I think you have heard them say something harmful about me; I will go on, for if the enemies of God saw me they would not say any of what you heard.'
lessons: True loyalty sometimes means trying to protect those we love from pain, even when we cannot fully shield them.
Leadership requires courage to face hostility directly rather than avoiding it, knowing that presence itself can disarm opposition.
Those with wisdom often perceive what others try to conceal, and discern the right course of action regardless.
practical_advice: When you feel the urge to protect someone you care about from hurtful words, reflect on whether avoidance or confrontation better serves the situation.
If you are in a leadership role and sense your team is shielding you from bad news, gently invite openness — clarity helps you respond with strength.
When facing people who speak ill of you, remember that your dignified presence can silence hostility more effectively than avoidance.

title: The Prophet Addresses Banu Qurayza at Their Fortress
summary: The Prophet approached the fortresses of Banu Qurayza and addressed their nobles loudly, declaring that disgrace from God had come upon them for their treachery.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) alighted at their fortresses, they being high above him, he addressed a group of their nobles at the top of his voice so that they could hear him saying, 'Respond, you tribe of Jews, you brothers of apes, for disgrace from God, Almighty and Glorious is He, has come down upon you!'
lessons: Moral accountability must be proclaimed clearly, especially when treachery has endangered a whole community.
Facing wrongdoers with direct, fearless speech is sometimes the only language that communicates the gravity of betrayal.
Divine justice is not delayed indefinitely; actions have consequences that arrive in due time.
practical_advice: When someone has seriously wronged you or your community, do not suppress the truth out of politeness — address it clearly and directly.
Practice speaking difficult truths with composure and authority rather than with uncontrolled anger.
Reflect on the consequences of broken trust in your own relationships and take seriously the harm that betrayal causes.

title: The Siege of Banu Qurayza Lasts Twenty-Five Days
summary: The Prophet besieged Banu Qurayza for approximately twenty-five days, during which the hardship and anxiety of the siege became severe, and Huyayy ibn Akhtab had joined them inside the fortress.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) made camp at one of the wells of Banu Qurayza, called the Anna well; it was among properties they owned. He besieged them for 25 days, by which time the siege was causing them great hardship and much anxiety. Huyayy b. Akhtab had joined them in their fortress when Quraysh and Ghatafan had withdrawn, thus fulfilling the pact he had made with Ka'b b. Asad.
lessons: Prolonged hardship and anxiety, though deeply painful, can force difficult truths to the surface that were previously avoided.
Those who sow treachery often find themselves trapped by its consequences, with no honourable exit available.
Patience and steadfastness in the face of prolonged difficulty is itself a form of strength.
practical_advice: When you are going through a prolonged period of stress or anxiety, break it into smaller windows of endurance rather than contemplating the whole weight at once.
Recognize when difficult circumstances are the result of your own choices, and use that reflection as motivation to choose differently going forward.
Find one anchor — a prayer, a person, a principle — that you return to each day when a trial feels unending.

title: Ka'b ibn Asad Offers Banu Qurayza Three Alternatives
summary: As the siege tightened, Ka'b ibn Asad presented Banu Qurayza with three choices: accepting Islam, killing their families and fighting to the end, or launching a surprise attack on the Sabbath. All three were rejected.
story: When they were sure that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had no intention of departing before he had utterly defeated them, Ka'b b. Asad addressed Banu Qurayza. He told them, 'You well know the crisis you face. I suggest three alternatives; choose the one you like best.' 'What are they?' they asked. He replied, 'First, that we follow and believe this man. I swear, it is clear to you now that he is a prophet who has been sent, and that it is he whom you find mentioned in your scripture. You should give him control over your lives, properties, children and women.' They replied, 'We will never abandon the law of the Torah, nor exchange it for another!' He then said, 'If you refuse me this, let us kill our children and our women and then go forth to Muhammad and his companions with our swords drawn; that way we will not leave any responsibilities behind us, and God will decide between us and Muhammad. If we perish, we perish; but we won't be leaving behind any children to worry about. And if we are victorious, we'll certainly acquire other women and children.' They replied, 'Shall we kill these unfortunates? What good would there be in living on after them?' He continued, 'If you refuse this, then tonight it is the sabbath. It may well be that Muhammad and his men feel secure now. Let's go forth and seek to take Muhammad and his men by surprise.' They replied, 'Should we desecrate our sabbath; if we do, there will befall that metamorphosis you well know has always befallen those who have done so.' He commented, 'Not one of you men has ever, since the day you were born, acted decisively.'
lessons: When a community is trapped by the consequences of its own poor decisions, even wise counsel can be rejected because no option feels bearable.
Clinging to religious form while abandoning its spirit — refusing truth out of tradition while still rejecting decisive action — leads to paralysis.
The moment of crisis often reveals the truth about what we truly believe, and whether we have the courage to act on it.
practical_advice: When you feel paralysed by an impossible situation, honestly examine whether your resistance to change is based on genuine principle or on fear and pride.
If you find yourself in a crisis partly of your own making, focus on what small, honest step you can still take rather than being overwhelmed by the full weight of consequences.
Seek counsel from people who will speak truth to you even when it is painful, and practice being the kind of person who can receive it.

title: Abu Lubaba's Gesture of Betrayal and His Immediate Remorse
summary: Sent to Banu Qurayza to advise them, Abu Lubaba gestured across his throat to indicate execution awaited them if they surrendered. He immediately recognized this as a betrayal of the Prophet and was overcome with remorse.
story: They then sent to the Messenger of God (SAAS), asking him to dispatch to them for consultations Abu Lubaba b. 'Abd al-Mundhir, a brother of Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf who were allies of al-Aws. He did dispatch Abu Lubaba and when they saw him, the men arose to meet him. The women and children broke into sobs before him. He felt very sorry for them; they asked him whether they should submit to Muhammad's judgement. He said that they should. He then gestured with his hand across his neck, indicating that execution was coming. Abu Lubaba (later) commented, 'I swear by God, my feet had not left that place before I knew I had betrayed God and His messenger.' Abu Lubaba then left hurriedly. He did not, however, return to the Messenger of God (SAAS), but tied himself to one of the supports of the mosque, saying, 'I will not leave here until God forgives me for what I have done. I swear to God that I will never again go to Banu Qurayza, nor ever be seen again in any place where I have betrayed God and His messenger!'
lessons: Genuine conscience acts immediately — the recognition of wrongdoing can strike even before we have left the scene of the act.
True repentance is not merely feeling sorry but making a concrete, costly commitment that reflects the seriousness of what was done.
Compassion for the suffering of others is not wrong, but it must never override our obligations to truth and loyalty.
practical_advice: If you act against your values and feel that instant pang of conscience, do not suppress it — let it guide you immediately toward making things right.
When you have done something you are deeply ashamed of, find a concrete act of contrition that matches the gravity of the mistake rather than simply waiting for the feeling to pass.
Reflect on moments when sympathy for one party led you to act unfairly to another, and think about how to better hold both compassion and integrity at the same time.

title: Abu Lubaba Binds Himself to the Mosque Pillar for Twenty Days
summary: Overcome with guilt for his betrayal, Abu Lubaba tied himself to a pillar of the mosque for approximately twenty days, refusing to be freed until God forgave him, with his wife untying him only for prayer.
story: He remained tied there for six days. His wife would come to him at each prayer time and untied him so that he could perform the ablutions and pray. He would then be tied up again. Eventually forgiveness did come down for him, in the Almighty's words, 'And others confessed their sins, having mixed good deeds with bad; God may well forgive them. God is indeed forgiving, merciful' (surat al-Tawba or al-Bara'a; IX, v.102). According to Musa b. Uqba he remained bound for 20 days. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, when Abu Lubaba absented himself, 'Hasn't Abu Lubaba yet finished dealing with his allies?' When it was related to him what Abu Lubaba had done, he said, 'After (he left) me some urge overcame him; if he had come to me, I would have sought forgiveness for him. Since he has now done what he has, I will not move him from where he is until God decrees His will upon him.'
lessons: The willingness to endure prolonged discomfort as an act of sincere repentance demonstrates the depth of one's remorse and commitment to God.
God's mercy meets sincere repentance — even a serious lapse followed by wholehearted turning back can be forgiven.
The Prophet's compassion is evident even toward those who erred: he said he would have sought forgiveness for Abu Lubaba had he come directly to him.
practical_advice: When you have made a serious mistake, resist the temptation to move on quickly. Allow yourself a proper period of reflection and genuine repentance before expecting relief.
If shame is keeping you from seeking forgiveness from someone you have wronged, remember that the Prophet's door was open — most people who love you want reconciliation more than punishment.
Use the story of Abu Lubaba when guilt feels unbearable: it shows that God's forgiveness is real, that sincere remorse is seen, and that relief does eventually come.

title: The Divine Forgiveness of Abu Lubaba Is Revealed
summary: While in the home of Umm Salama, the Prophet smiled as he received the revelation of God's forgiveness for Abu Lubaba. He allowed Umm Salama to deliver the good news, and insisted that only the Prophet himself could untie him.
story: God revealed His forgiveness to His messenger at the very end of the night, while he was in the home of Umm Salama. He began to smile and when Umm Salama asked him why, he told her that God had forgiven Abu Lubaba. She asked his permission to leave to inform him. He agreed, and she went and told him the good news. People crowded around him joyfully, wishing to untie him from his bonds. But he said, 'No one but the Messenger of God (SAAS) shall untie me.' When the Messenger of God (SAAS) came out to perform the dawn prayer, he did untie him, may God be pleased with him.
lessons: Divine forgiveness, when it comes, brings joy not only to the one forgiven but to the entire community that witnessed the repentance.
Completing an act of repentance with the same intentionality with which it was begun — insisting the Prophet himself untie him — shows integrity and wholeness of character.
God's mercy is not just a private transaction; its announcement can become a moment of collective joy and spiritual renewal.
practical_advice: When you hear that someone who sincerely repented has been forgiven or restored, let yourself feel genuine joy for them — it strengthens the bonds of community.
If you are carrying guilt, hold on to the truth that sincere repentance is always met with divine mercy. The story of Abu Lubaba is a direct proof of this.
When you set a meaningful commitment for yourself as part of making things right, see it through completely rather than cutting it short when you begin to feel better.

title: Three Men of Banu Hadal Accept Islam the Night Banu Qurayza Surrendered
summary: On the night Banu Qurayza came under the Prophet's authority, three men — Tha'laba ibn Sa'ya, Usayd ibn Sa'ya, and Asad ibn Ubayd — accepted Islam, distancing themselves from the treachery of their tribe.
story: Thereafter Tha'laba b. Sa'ya, Usayd b. Sa'ya and Asad b. Ubayd, all of Banu Hadal and not from either Qurayza or al-Nadir, their own pedigree being superior to that, they being Banu 'Amm al-Qawm, accepted Islam that night during which Qurayza had come under the control of the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Even in the midst of collective wrongdoing, individuals retain the capacity to choose truth and righteousness for themselves.
The darkest moments in history are also moments when some individuals move toward the light, no matter the social cost.
True nobility of character is shown not by lineage but by the willingness to embrace truth when it becomes clear, even when those around you refuse.
practical_advice: When your community or social group is moving in a direction you believe is wrong, remember that you are not bound by their choices — you can always make your own.
Do not wait for a perfect moment or a perfect community to commit to your values. The three men of Banu Hadal chose the right path on the very night of crisis.
Reflect on what it would take for you to make a courageous decision that separates you from the wrong choices of those around you.

title: Amr ibn Su'da Al-Qurazi Is Released for His Trustworthiness
summary: Amr ibn Su'da, who had refused to participate in Banu Qurayza's betrayal of the Prophet, slipped past the Muslim guard at night. Recognizing him, the guard let him pass, and the Prophet later praised him as a man God saved because of his trustworthiness.
story: Also that night 'Amr b. Su'da al-Qurazi came out and passed by the guard of the Messenger of God (SAAS); Muhammad b. Maslama was in charge of them that night and when he saw 'Amr, he called out and asked him his name, which he gave. 'Amr had refused to participate in Banu Qurayza treachery towards the Messenger of God (SAAS), having said, 'I will never betray Muhammad!' When he recognized him, Muhammad b. Maslama said, 'O God, do not forbid me to set aside the minor sins of noble men!' He then let him pass. 'Amr then hurriedly left and spent that night in the mosque of the Messenger of God (SAAS), in Medina. Thereafter he departed for some still unknown destination. When what had happened to him was mentioned to the Messenger of God (SAAS), he commented, 'That is a man whom God saved because of his trustworthiness.'
lessons: Trustworthiness is not merely a social virtue — it is a spiritual one that God Himself honours and rewards.
Those who maintain their integrity even when everyone around them compromises it will find that their reputation speaks for them even among former enemies.
A single act of principled refusal, maintained under enormous pressure, can define a person's entire legacy.
practical_advice: In situations where everyone around you seems to be compromising their principles, ask yourself: what is the one line I will not cross, no matter what?
Build a reputation for trustworthiness in small daily matters — it is in moments of crisis that this reputation will matter most and may even protect you.
Reflect on the phrase 'God saved him because of his trustworthiness' and ask what quality of character you want to be remembered for.

title: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh Is Appointed to Judge Banu Qurayza
summary: When the Aws tribe appealed for clemency toward Banu Qurayza, the Prophet offered to let one of their own men decide the matter. They agreed, and Sa'd ibn Mu'adh — a wounded, gravely ill leader — was brought to give judgement.
story: Next morning they came under the authority of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Banu Aws promptly claimed that Banu Qurayza had been allied with them rather than with Khazraj. They also referred to how the Messenger of God (SAAS) had given control over Banu Qurayza to 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy when he had requested this, as is related above. When Aws had spoken to him, the Messenger of God (SAAS) responded to them, 'O Aws, will you be content to have one of your men decide about them?' When they agreed to this, he placed them under the charge of Sa'd b. Mu'adh. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had placed Sa'd b. Mu'adh inside his mosque in a tent belonging to an Aslam woman named Rufayda; she was caring for the wounded. When he placed Sa'd in charge of Banu Qurayza, his people approached him and mounted him on a donkey bearing a leather cushion; he was a handsome, large man. They then brought him to the Messenger of God (SAAS), telling him to treat his charges kindly, since it was with that expectation that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had appointed him over them. When they insisted upon this, he responded, 'It is now time for Sa'd to take no account of anyone's criticism for what he does in God's cause!' One of those of his people who was accompanying him returned to Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal and told them of the death of the men of Banu Qurayza even before Sa'd reached them, because of what he had heard him say. When Sa'd arrived at where the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims were, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Arise to greet your master.'
lessons: True justice requires independence from social pressure — Sa'd's declaration that he would take no account of criticism is a model of impartiality.
Appointing a judge from within the affected community's own allies demonstrates the Prophet's commitment to procedural fairness even in grave circumstances.
Those who carry the weight of serious judgement must be prepared to stand alone in their verdict, answerable only to God.
practical_advice: When you are asked to make a difficult decision that affects others, be honest about the standard you are applying and resist pressure to soften it out of social convenience.
If you are in a position of leadership or arbitration, reflect on Sa'd's example: the time to be truly just is precisely when the stakes are highest.
Prepare yourself spiritually and mentally before making high-stakes decisions by seeking clarity on your values and your accountability to God rather than to the crowd.

title: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh Arrives to Judge Banu Qurayza
summary: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, wounded from the Battle of the Trench, is brought to judge the fate of Banu Qurayza after their treachery. The Prophet commands the Muslims to rise for their master, and Sa'd decrees execution of the men, captivity for women and children, and division of property.
story: them. When they insisted upon this, he responded, 'It is now time for Sa'd to take no account of anyone's criticism for what he does in God's cause!' One of those of his people who was accompanying him returned to Bann 'Ahd al-Ashhal and told them of the death of the men of Bann Qurayza even before Sard reached them, because of what he had heard him say. "When Sa'd arrived at where the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims were, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Arise to greet your master.' The Quraysh muhiijrrin, said he was referring to the angzr, while the latter thought that his order had been directed towards all the Muslims, and so they stood up. They told him, 'Abo 'Amr, the Messenger of God (SAAS) has delegated to you the question of deciding what to do with your charges.' Sa'd replied, 'Is it your duty to swear by a pact with God that whatever decision I reach about them will be enforced?' 'Yes,' they agreed. Sard then asked, 'And does that apply to him who is over here?' He was referring to the direction where the Messenger of God (SAAS) was, while looking away from him out of his respect for him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) answered, 'Yes.' Sa'd then said, 'I therefore decree that the men among them shall be executed, while their properties shall be liquidated and their offspring and women shall he taken captive.'" Ibn Is@q went on, "cA~imb. Wmar b. Qatzda related to me, from 'Abd &&an h. Wmar b. SaCd b. Musdh, from 'Alqama b. Waqqa~al-Laythi, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to Sa'd, "You have judged them with the decision of God, from seven heavens above."'"
lessons: Acting with conviction in moments of great responsibility, even under immense pressure, reflects true moral courage.
Seeking and delegating judgment to those most qualified — even at personal cost — is a mark of wise and just leadership.
Divine confirmation of a righteous decision brings peace to the heart, reminding us that sincere efforts made for God's sake are never overlooked.
practical_advice: When facing a heavy responsibility, focus on what is right rather than what others will think of you, trusting that integrity is its own reward.
In moments of community conflict, identify the most trustworthy and knowledgeable person to help guide decisions rather than acting impulsively.
Reflect on moments in your own life where you stood firm under pressure — use those memories as anchors of strength when new challenges arise.

title: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh's Prayer and Wound at the Battle of the Trench
summary: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh is struck by an arrow at the Battle of the Trench, severing the main vein in his arm. He prays not to die before seeing justice for Banu Qurayza's treachery, and God answers his prayer — his wound stops bleeding until after his judgment is delivered, after which he passes away.
story: Imzm Ahmad stated, "Hujayn and Yiinus related to us, saying, 'Al-Layth b. Sa'd related to us, from Abii al-Zubayr, from Jabu b. 'Abd Allah, who said, "At the battle of al-Ahznb an arrow was shot at Sa'd b. Musdh and the medial vein in his arm was severed. The Messenger of God (SAAS) cauterized it with fire. Sacd's hand then swelled up and he bled it. Seeing this, Sa'd said, '0 God, do not extract my soul before you settle Banii Qurayza for me.' At this the flow ceased, with not another drop falling, until Banii Qurayza submitted to Sa'd's authority. A message was sent to him to this effect and he decreed that their men should be executed and that their women and children should he made captive and should serve the Muslims. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'You have correctly given God's judgement regarding them.' They were 400 in number. When their execution was over, Sa'd's artery burst and he died.""' Both al-Tirmidhi and al-Nasa'i related this from Qutayba, back to al-Layt$. Al-Tirmidhi categorized it as hasan sahib, "good and authentic".
lessons: Sincere supplication made at the moment of greatest vulnerability demonstrates profound trust in God's power and timing.

God's answers to prayer sometimes come in ways that are undeniable — Sa'd's wound miraculously ceasing and only reopening after his mission was complete is a powerful reminder that God responds to the sincere heart.
Completing one's duty before departing this world gives life deep meaning; Sa'd's final act was one of justice and devotion.
practical_advice: In your most painful or difficult moments, turn to prayer and articulate clearly what you need from God — this act of trust itself is healing.
When you feel you cannot continue, ask yourself: is there one meaningful purpose still calling you? Let that purpose sustain you through the hardship.
Reflect on times God has sustained you through something you thought you could not survive — write these down as reminders of His responsiveness to sincere prayer.

title: Gabriel Appears After the Battle of the Trench, Commanding the Prophet to March on Banu Qurayza
summary: After returning from the Battle of the Trench and laying down his weapons, the Prophet is visited by Gabriel — dust-covered and still armed — who commands him immediately to march against Banu Qurayza without rest.
story: Imzm Ahmad stated, "Ibn Numayr related to us, from Hishzm, saying, 'My father quoted 'A'isha to me as having said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) came back from the Battle of al-AbiIb and had put down his arms and bathed, Gabriel, his face dust-covered, came to h i and said, 'So, you've put down your arms! I swear by God, we have not! Go out and battle them!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Where to?' 'Over here,' Gabriel responded, gesturing towards Bana Quraym. And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth against them.'" H i s h k stated, 'My father informed me that they accepted the authority of the Prophet (SAAS) over them and that he then delegated that authority to Sa'd. He then stated, "I judge that the warriors be executed, that their women and offspring he taken captive and that their properties be divided up."'" H i s h w went on, "My father stated further, 'I was told that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "You have judged them by God's judgement."'"
lessons: True readiness in service to God sometimes means answering the call even when we are exhausted — divine missions do not always wait for our comfort.
The image of Gabriel still armed and dust-covered while the Prophet rested illustrates that the spiritual struggle continues even when we think a chapter is closed.
Obedience to divine guidance, even when it is demanding and unexpected, is the foundation of a life of purpose and faith.
practical_advice: When you feel you have done enough and deserve rest, check whether there is still something important left unfinished — sometimes the most critical work comes at the moment of exhaustion.
Develop a habit of responding to clear calls of duty promptly, even when inconvenient, trusting that God's timing is purposeful.
Reflect on moments when you were called to action just as you thought a trial was over — how did pushing through shape your character and deepen your reliance on God?

title: 'A'isha's Account of Sa'd's Wound, Prayer, and Death
summary: 'A'isha recounts witnessing Sa'd ibn Mu'adh march to battle despite his great stature making him vulnerable, being struck by an arrow from Ibn al-'Ariqa, praying for survival until justice was served against Banu Qurayza, and ultimately dying from his wound after his prayer was answered.
story: Al-Bukhnri stated, "Zakariyyz' b. Yahyx related to us, quoting 'Ahd Allnh b. Numayr, quoting H i s h k , from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, 'At al-khandaq Sa'd was wounded. One of Quraysh, a man named Hihhxn h. CAriqa, shot an arrow at him and hit h i in the main vein in his arm. The Prophet (SAAS) set up a tent for him in the mosque so that he could visit h i from nearby. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had returned from al-khandaq and had put down his weapons and had bathed, Gabriel, shaking dust off his head, came to h i and said, "So, you have put down your weapons? I swear by God, I've not done that! Go forth against them!" The Prophet (SAAS) asked, "Where to?" Gabriel then gestured towards B a n Qurayza. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then did go out against them and they accepted his authority; he then delegated this authority to Sa'd. The latter decreed, "I judge that their warriors be executed, that their women and offspring be taken captive and that their properties be divided up."'" Hishzm went on, "My father quoted 'A'isha as saying that Sakl stated, '0 God, You well know that there is no one I would rather fight on your behalf than a people who have called Your messenger a liar and have exiled h i . 0 God, I believe You have put an end to warfare between us and them. Give me life for so long as there is any warfare against e r a y s h , so that I may fight against them for You. And if You have brought the war to an end, recommence it and place my death therein.' Then there was an explosive emission from his upper chest, hut this did not alarm anyone. But there was a tent (for the wounded) belonging to Banii Ghifa in the mosque. And, when the blood began streaming down towards them, they shouted, 'Hey, you in the tent, what is this coming down to us from you?'
