It was from Sa'd; his wound was streaming blood and he died of it." This was related by Muslim from a hadith of 'Abd All& b. Numayr. I would comment that Sa'd frst spoke this prayer before he passed judgement on Bann Qurama. This is why he prayed that his death not occur before he had settled with Bann Qurayza. And God did answer his prayer. After issuing his judgement on them, and God had pleased h i greatly thereby, he prayed a second time in this way and God then gave him martyrdom, may God he pleased with him and give h i pleasure.
lessons: Sa'd's prayer — asking God to let him live only as long as he was needed in God's cause — reflects the ultimate surrender of one's life to divine purpose.
The answered prayer of a sincere believer, even in the most extreme circumstances of physical suffering, demonstrates that God is intimately aware of every soul's sincerity.
Martyrdom in Islam is not merely death in battle — it is the culmination of a life lived in complete dedication to God, as Sa'd's death illustrates.
practical_advice: Examine your own prayers — are they aligned with a higher purpose, or only with personal comfort? Praying for strength to fulfill your duty before praying for ease deepens your spiritual life.
When facing illness or pain, try to find a meaningful goal to work toward — having a sense of purpose has been shown to support both emotional and physical resilience.
Honour the legacy of those who sacrificed for what they believed in by reflecting on what cause you yourself are willing to give your best energy and effort to.

title: 'A'isha Witnesses the Battle of the Trench and Is Rebuked by Umar
summary: 'A'isha follows the Muslim forces during the Battle of the Trench and encounters Umar ibn al-Khattab, who rebukes her sharply for her presence in the battlefield. Talha ibn Ubaydullah then defends her, reminding Umar to be less harsh.
story: Imam Ahmad related it also from another line at great length; in that account there are other interesting aspects. He stated that Yazid related to him that Muhammad b. 'Amr narrated to h i , from his father, from his father 'Alqama b. Waqqa~, who said, "'A'isha stated to me as follows, 'I was leaving on the day of the battle of al-khandaq, following after our forces, when I heard the sound of movement behind me. Then I saw it was Sa'd b. Musdh, accompanied by his brother al-Hiwith b. Aws, carrying his child. I sat down on the ground and Sa'd passed by, wearing an iron coat of mail from which his arms and legs protruded. I was concerned about injury to these limbs of Sa'd; Sa'd was one of the greatest and tallest of men. As he passed, he was speaking the verse, "Remain awhile; let Hamal see the battle. How fine is death when its time has come!"'" She continued, '"I got up and ran into a garden. There I found a group of Muslims; among them were Wmar b. al-Khar(i%b. One of the men had on a subgha" (by which she meant a m~ghfar, a "helmet"). "Wmar asked, 'Why are you here? I swear, you're bold! What's to protect you if some disaster should happen or you got cut off?' He went on criticizing me until I wished the earth would open to swallow me on the spot! Then the man lifted the helmet off his face and I saw that it was Talha b. Whayd Allah. He commented, 'Hey, Wmar, shame on you! You're overdoing it! From today on, whatever aligning or taking refuge there is will be to God, Almighty and Glorious is He!'"
lessons: Moments of public criticism or embarrassment, even when well-intentioned, are part of communal life — how we recover from them defines our character.
Talha's gentle but firm defense of 'A'isha illustrates the importance of standing up for others when criticism crosses the line into harshness.
Even the most devoted individuals — including the Prophet's own companions — navigated human conflict, misunderstanding, and emotional difficulty, reminding us that such experiences are universal.
practical_advice: When you feel humiliated or publicly criticized, take a breath before reacting — the moment will pass, and your response in that moment shapes how others see your character.
If you witness someone being treated harshly or unfairly, find a respectful way to intervene as Talha did — defending another's dignity is an act of great moral courage.
Reflect on an experience where you felt you wanted the earth to swallow you — what did you learn from surviving that moment, and how did it shape your resilience?

title: The Siege of Banu Qurayza and Their Surrender to Sa'd's Judgment
summary: After the Battle of the Trench, the Prophet besieges Banu Qurayza for 25 days. When their suffering becomes unbearable, they seek counsel from Abu Lubaba, who signals to them what awaits, before they ultimately surrender to Sa'd ibn Mu'adh's authority.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then put on his breast-plate and had it announced that the men should prepare to depart for battle. He passed by Bana Ghanam who were his neighbours close by the mosque. He asked them, 'Who was it passed by you?' They replied, 'Dihya al-Kalbi went by.' Now Dihya al-Kalhi was similar in beard, age and face to Gabriel, upon whom be peace. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) proceeded on to Banii Qurayza and besieged them for 25 days. When their suffering from the siege grew intense, a message was sent telling them to accept the authority of the Messenger of God (SAAS). They sought advice from Aha Lubxha b. 'Abd al-Mundhir, and he made a sign to them indicating execution. Bann Qurayza then said they would accept the authority of Sa'd b. Mu5dh. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told them, 'Then do accept the authority of Sa'd h. Mu%dh!' He was brought to him on a donkey, seated on a saddle stuffed with palm fronds and"
lessons: The consequences of betrayal during a time of covenant and existential crisis are severe — the siege of Banu Qurayza stands as a historical lesson in the weight of broken trust.
Even in the darkest moments of a siege, the human instinct to seek counsel demonstrates our need for community and guidance when facing impossible choices.
Gabriel appearing in the form of Dihya al-Kalbi as the army marched out is a reminder that divine support accompanies those who move with righteousness and purpose.
practical_advice: When you have made a serious mistake that has damaged trust with others, resist the temptation to delay accountability — facing the consequences with courage is always better than prolonged avoidance.
Seek trustworthy counsel when you are in a crisis, but be discerning — not every advisor has your best interest at heart, as Abu Lubaba's signal demonstrates.

In times of prolonged difficulty or 'siege' in your own life — whether financial, relational, or emotional — reflect on whether the situation calls for surrender, negotiation, or perseverance, and consult those wiser than yourself.

title: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh's Prayer and Death After the Judgment
summary: After rendering his judgment on Banu Qurayza, Sa'd ibn Mu'adh prayed to be taken by God if the wars with Quraysh had ended. His wound then burst open, and the Prophet, Abu Bakr, and 'Umar wept at his bedside as he passed away.
story: 'Thereupon, Sa'd spoke the following prayer, 'O God, if you have left for Your prophet any further warfare against Quraysh, then let me remain for it. But if You have now ended the warfare between him and them, then take me to Yourself.' 'A'isha went on, 'His wound then burst; up to then he had been free of it, nothing being visible except for a small circular spot. He went back to the tent that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had set up for him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) went to him, along with Abu Bakr and 'Umar. And I swear by Him in whose hands is Muhammad, that I could well differentiate the weeping of 'Umar from that of Abu Bakr while I was there in my room. They were, as God said, '... compassionate among themselves'' (surat al-Fath; XLVIII, v.29). 'Alqama stated, 'I then asked, 'How did the Messenger of God (SAAS) react?' 'A'isha replied, 'His eyes never wept for anyone. However, if he was deeply moved, he would take hold of his beard.''
lessons: A believer who has fulfilled their purpose with sincerity may be at peace surrendering the rest of their life to God, free of attachment to worldly continuance.
The grief of those who love us is itself a testament to the goodness we brought into their lives — being mourned by righteous people is itself an honor.
Emotional expression among the companions of the Prophet, including weeping, was a sign of genuine compassion and deep human connection, not weakness.
practical_advice: When facing illness, loss, or approaching death — your own or a loved one's — try to frame it as a release into God's mercy rather than only an ending.
Allow yourself to grieve when you lose someone dear. The companions of the Prophet wept openly; grief is not contrary to faith.
Reflect on whether your life's efforts have been directed toward something larger than yourself — as Sa'd's were — so that when your chapter closes, it closes with meaning.

title: The Execution of Banu Qurayza's Men in the Market of Madinah
summary: The men of Banu Qurayza, numbering in the hundreds, were brought in groups to trenches dug in the Madinah market and executed, with their leader Ka'b ibn Asad warning them of their fate as each group departed.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'They (Banu Qurayza) were then brought down and the Messenger of God (SAAS) imprisoned them in the home of the daughter of al-Harith, a woman of Banu al-Najjar.' Ibn Ishaq went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went out into the Medina market and had some trenches dug. Then he sent for Banu Qurayza men to have their heads severed in those trenches. They came out in groups. Among them was that enemy of God Huyayy b. Akhtab, along with Ka'b b. Asad, their leader. They were 600 or 700 in number. Some estimate their number to have been as many as 800 or 900.' Ibn Ishaq continued, 'As they were being taken in groups to the Messenger of God (SAAS), they asked Ka'b b. Asad, 'Ka'b, what do you think he will do to us?' He replied, 'Will you not understand anywhere? Can't you see that the one calling never stops, and that those of you who go never return? It means, by God, execution!'
lessons: Betrayal of a covenant carries grave consequences — the fate of Banu Qurayza was a direct result of their treachery during the siege of Madinah.
Even in moments of doom, some individuals perceive the truth clearly, as Ka'b ibn Asad did — clarity of mind in crisis is both a gift and a burden.
History records the consequences of choosing enmity over covenant-keeping; the Seerah is a reminder that moral choices shape collective destinies.
practical_advice: Reflect on the covenants and commitments in your own life — are you honoring them, especially under pressure, or rationalizing their violation?
When you find yourself in a situation that feels overwhelming and irreversible, seek clarity about what led to it so that lessons can be carried forward.
Study history and the Seerah not to dwell in sorrow, but to understand the weight of collective moral choices and their long-term consequences.

title: Huyayy ibn Akhtab's Final Defiance Before Execution
summary: Huyayy ibn Akhtab, the chief instigator of the Confederates' coalition against the Muslims, was brought to execution wearing a defiant posture, declaring that he did not blame himself for his enmity but acknowledged that whoever abandons God will be abandoned.
story: Huyayy b. Akhtab was brought to him wearing a flowered robe all over which he had made finger-size holes so that it not be taken from him; his hands were drawn up and tied by a rope to his neck. When he looked over at the Messenger of God (SAAS) he said, 'Well, I swear by God, I don't blame myself for being your enemy, but whoever abandons God will be abandoned!' He then went forward to the men and said, 'O people, there is no harm in the command of God; a decree, fate and a massacre have been written by God against Banu Isra'il!' He then sat down and he was struck in the neck. Jabal b. Jawwal al-Tha'labi then spoke the verses, 'By your life, I swear that it was not himself that Ibn Akhtab blamed, but whoever abandons God will be abandoned. He strove hard, pushing himself to the limits, exerting himself vigorously to achieve glory.'
lessons: Even an enemy of Islam inadvertently testified to a universal truth: whoever abandons God will be abandoned — a principle that transcends sides.
Pride and defiance in the face of consequences cannot undo the reality of one's choices; Huyayy's end was the culmination of a path he had chosen.
The Seerah presents even opponents with historical honesty — acknowledging their courage or eloquence while not excusing their actions.
practical_advice: Examine the areas of your life where you may have 'abandoned' God's guidance — neglecting prayer, honesty, or covenant — and take steps to return before consequences accumulate.

When you reflect on the phrase 'whoever abandons God will be abandoned,' use it as a diagnostic: where do you feel abandoned or alone, and is there a corresponding area where you have drifted from your spiritual commitments?
Study the lives of those who opposed what was right not to condemn them, but to understand how intelligent, capable people can take wrong paths — and learn to guard yourself against similar errors.

title: Thabit ibn Qays Repays His Debt to al-Zubayr ibn Bata
summary: Thabit ibn Qays, seeking to repay a past debt of honor to the elderly al-Zubayr ibn Bata of Banu Qurayza, interceded repeatedly with the Prophet to spare his life, restore his family, and return his property — yet al-Zubayr ultimately requested to join his executed kinsmen.
story: Ibn Ishaq then related the story of al-Zubayr b. Bata', an old man who had become blind. 'At the battle of Bu'ath he had been given over to the charge of Thabit b. Qays b. Shammas who had cut off his forelock. On this day Thabit had wanted to repay him,' so he went to al-Zubayr and asked, 'Do you know me, Abu 'Abd al-Rahman?' 'Would the like of me not know the like of you?' he responded. Thabit then said, 'I want to pay you back!' 'Well,' replied al-Zubayr, 'an honourable man would repay an honourable man.' Thabit then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked permission to release him, and he did release al-Zubayr b. Bata'. When he informed him of this, al-Zubayr responded, 'What would an old man who would have no son and no family do with his life?' Thabit then returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked him to restore to the man his wife and his son, and to this he agreed. When he returned to al-Zubayr with this news, the old man now said, 'What's to become of a household in Hijaz that has no property; how could they survive?' Again Thabit went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked that the property of al-Zubayr be released to him, and he did so. When he went back and told him this, al-Zubayr asked, 'Thabit, what became of Ka'b b. Asad, whose face was like a Chinese mirror that reflects the faces of the tribe's virgins?' 'He has been killed,' he replied. 'And what of our vanguard in the attack and our protector in flight, 'Azal b. Shamwal?' 'He has been killed,' Thabit replied. 'What became of the two counsel' - meaning that of Banu Ka'b b. Qurayza and Banu 'Amr b. Qurayza. 'They have dispersed and been killed,' he replied. 'I ask you then, Thabit, by your obligation to me, to let me join my own people. There is no good in life for me now that they have gone and I can't stand postponing joining my loved ones for so long as it would take to fill and empty a bucket being borne along by a camel!' At this Thabit went over to him and cut off his head.
lessons: Gratitude and honor extend beyond religious or tribal boundaries — Thabit's faithfulness to a pre-Islamic debt of honor is itself a lesson in integrity.

When a person has lost everyone and everything that gave their life meaning, grief can become the deepest form of longing — al-Zubayr's story is a profound portrait of a man who had nothing left to live for.
The Prophet's willingness to grant intercession for an individual even in extreme circumstances shows that mercy and compassion are always present alongside justice.
practical_advice: Honor your debts of gratitude — not just financial ones, but emotional and relational ones. Consider who in your life has shown you kindness that you have not yet acknowledged or repaid.

If you are experiencing grief over the loss of community, family, or purpose, recognize that this pain is real and deserves attention. Seek connection with others who share your values rather than isolating yourself.
Reflect on what it means to 'have something to live for' — and if you are in a season where that feels unclear, actively invest in relationships and purposes that can anchor you.

title: The Woman of Banu Qurayza Who Killed Khallad ibn Suwayd
summary: A woman of Banu Qurayza who had thrown a millstone and killed a Muslim named Khallad ibn Suwayd was the only woman executed, and 'A'isha recalled with astonishment how she laughed and remained high-spirited even as she was led to her death.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, who said, 'He only executed one of their women. She was talking with me and laughing heartily all the time the Messenger of God (SAAS) was executing her menfolk in the market-place. Then a voice called out asking where was so-and-so. She exclaimed, 'It's me, by God!' I asked her, 'For pity's sake, what's wrong?' She said, 'I'm to be killed!' 'Why?' I asked. 'For something I did,' she told me. Then she was taken away and beheaded.' The account continues, ''A'isha used to say, 'I swear, I'll never forget how high-spirited she was and how much she laughed when she knew she was to be killed.'' Ibn Ishaq stated, 'It was she who had thrown down the millstone on to Khallad b. Suwayd and had killed him.' He is inferring that it was for him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had her executed. Ibn Ishaq stated elsewhere, 'He named her as Nabata, wife of al-Hakam al-Qurazi.'
lessons: People face fear and mortality in deeply varied ways; some respond with defiance or acceptance that can be difficult to comprehend from the outside.
The fact that 'A'isha, decades later, still remembered this woman's laughter reflects how powerful and haunting moments of extreme human behavior can be.
History is made of individual human stories, each with its own moral complexity — the Seerah preserves these accounts so that every person's reality is acknowledged.
practical_advice: Reflect on how you personally respond to fear and uncertainty — do you find yourself masking anxiety with laughter or bravado, or can you sit with your true feelings?
Consider journaling about a memory that has stayed with you the way this moment stayed with 'A'isha — what does its persistence tell you about what matters most to you?

When others around you seem to be handling crisis in a way that confuses or unsettles you, practice compassion rather than judgment — you may not fully understand the interior world they are drawing from.

title: Division of Banu Qurayza Spoils and the Story of Rayhana
summary: After the defeat of Banu Qurayza, the Prophet divided the spoils among the Muslims and chose Rayhana bint Amr for himself; she initially refused Islam but eventually accepted it, bringing great pleasure to the Prophet.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then divided up among the Muslims the property, wives and children of Bano Qurayza, after he had taken out one-fifth. He awarded each horseman three shares, two for the horse and one for its rider. He gave one share to each foot soldier. The horses numbered thirty-six at that battle. This was the first time the booty was divided into division of two shares and the first in which it was divided into five parts. The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Sacid b. Zayd with the captives taken from Bano Qurayza to Najd, where he sold them in exchange for horses and weapons. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had chosen for himself one of their women, Rayhma, daughter of 'Amr b. Khannfa, of Banii 'Amr b. Qurayza. He stayed intimate with her until he died, she still being at that time in his household. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had suggested that she accept Islam, but she had refused. Eventually she did accept Islam and this brought great pleasure to him. He suggested releasing and marrying her, but she chose to continue in slavery, considering that easier for her. She remained with him until the Messenger of God (SAAS) died.
lessons: Guidance to faith is a gift from God that can come at any time, even after prolonged resistance — Rayhana's eventual acceptance of Islam reminds us never to give up hope for those we care about.
The Prophet's joy at Rayhana's acceptance of Islam shows that spiritual growth in others brings genuine happiness to those who sincerely care for souls.
People make choices according to their own comfort and understanding; respecting those choices while remaining a source of goodness is itself a noble character trait.
practical_advice: If someone you care about has not yet found faith or peace, continue making sincere supplication for them without pressure or despair.
When someone close to you makes a choice you would not make for yourself, practice patience and respect their agency while remaining a steady presence of support.
Reflect on moments when you yourself resisted something good before eventually embracing it — use that memory to cultivate compassion toward others who are still on their journey.

title: The Execution of the Woman of Banu Qurayza Who Killed Khallad b. Suwayd
summary: The only woman of Banu Qurayza to be executed was the one who had thrown a millstone onto the Muslim Khallad b. Suwayd, killing him during the siege; the Prophet ordered her execution for this act.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'It was she who had thrown down the millstone on to Khallad b. Suwayd and had killed him.' He is inferring that it was for him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had her executed. Ibn Ishaq stated elsewhere, 'He named her as Nabata, wife of al-Hakam al-Quraqi.'
lessons: Accountability for one's actions applies in times of conflict just as in times of peace — justice must be served even in difficult circumstances.
The companions of the Prophet accepted loss and martyrdom with patience, knowing that justice would ultimately be established.
Even in the midst of war and grief, the Prophet maintained a system of measured justice rather than collective punishment or unrestrained revenge.
practical_advice: When you have been wronged, seek justice through proper channels rather than taking matters into your own hands in anger.
In moments of loss, remind yourself that those who wrong others will face accountability — this can bring peace to a grieving heart.
Channel feelings of anger over injustice into constructive advocacy rather than letting them fester into bitterness.

title: The Martyrdom of Khallad b. Suwayd and His Double Reward
summary: Khallad b. Suwayd was killed when a millstone was thrown upon him during the siege of Banu Qurayza; the Prophet said he would receive the reward of two martyrs.
story: Those Muslims martyred in the conflict with Bann Quraym were Khallzd b. Suwayd b. Tha'laba b. CAmr al-Khazraji, who was crushed when a millstone was cast down on him. They claim that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'He will receive the reward of two martyrs.'
lessons: God's generosity multiplies the reward of those who suffer in unique or exceptionally painful ways for His cause — no sacrifice goes unrecognized.
The Prophet's words of comfort to the companions of the fallen remind us of the importance of honoring those who have given everything.
Dying in the service of one's community and faith, even in an unexpected or painful way, is not a diminished death but an elevated one in the eyes of God.
practical_advice: When you face suffering that feels unfair or unusually harsh, trust that God sees the full weight of what you are enduring and will not leave it unrewarded.
Honor the sacrifices of those who came before you — remembering their stories strengthens your own resolve during hardship.
Do not measure the value of your struggles by how they appear to others; God measures by sincerity and what is within the heart.

title: Abu Sinan b. Mihsan Dies During the Siege of Banu Qurayza
summary: Abu Sinan b. Mihsan of Banu Asad died while the Prophet was besieging Banu Qurayza and was buried in their cemetery that same day.
story: Aba Sinan b. Mihsan b. Hurt h of Bana Asad b. Khuzayma died while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was besieging Banii Quaym; he was buried in their cemetery that (same) day.
lessons: Death does not wait for convenient moments — it comes during trials and campaigns just as it does in times of ease, reminding us to always be spiritually prepared.
A believer who dies while engaged in righteous effort dies in a state of purpose; the manner and moment of death carry meaning.
Community members who quietly pass away during times of collective struggle deserve to be remembered and honored, not forgotten in the noise of events around them.
practical_advice: Use times of difficulty and collective striving as reminders to examine your own spiritual readiness — none of us knows when our time will come.
When someone in your community passes away during a particularly busy or turbulent time, make the effort to pause and properly honor their memory.
Reflect on what you are engaged in at any given moment — strive to ensure that your daily occupation is something you would be content to be found doing when you meet God.

title: Sa'd b. Mu'adh's Prayer for Martyrdom and the Bursting of His Wound
summary: Sa'd b. Mu'adh had been struck by an arrow during the Battle of the Trench and prayed to God not to let him die before seeing justice done against Banu Qurayza; after pronouncing judgment upon them, his wound burst open and he died a martyr.
story: It has been related above that Hihbm b. al-'Ariqa, God damn him, had shot an arrow at him and struck him in the vein of his ann. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then cauterized him with fire and stopped up the wound. Sa'd prayed to God not to make him die before he had been relieved of Banii Quray~a. This occurred following their breaking of the pacts and agreements they had made with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and their alignment with the ahzab, the enemy clans. When the latter withdrew, dispersing away from Medina, Bann Qurayza were totally left to disaster, both on earth and in the after-life. The Messenger of God (SAAS) went to them and besieged them, as told above. When they were in real suffering, beset from all sides, they decided to submit to the authority of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to do with them as ever God wished. He delegated that authority to the chief of Aws, who had been allied with them before the coming of Islam; he was Sacd h. Musdh. Banii Quray~a were pleased with this. It is said that they initially gave themselves over to Sacd's authority because they had high hopes of his kindness and partiality towards them. They did not know that they were more hateful in his view than the apes and pigs that were their peers! This was because of the intensity of his faith, and his high trust. May God be pleased with him and give him pleasure. The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent for him; Sa'd was in a tent in the mosque. He was brought on a donkey riding on a soft saddle placed under him because of his injury. When he drew near the tent of the Messenger of God (SAAS), the latter ordered all there to rise for him. It was said that this was so that he would dismount because of the severity of his injury; also it was said that this was to accord him respect in the presence of those about whom he was to decide, so that he would be more effective in carrying out his judgement. But God knows best. When he decreed death and captivity for them and God had pleased and satisfied him by relieving him of them, and he had returned to the tent in the mosque accompanied by the Messenger of God (SAAS), he prayed to God the Almighty and Glorious that he have martyrdom. And God had chosen his reward; his wound burst open that night. Blood went on escaping from him until he died. May God be pleased with him.
lessons: When a believer makes a sincere and purposeful supplication to God, God hears it and responds — Sa'd asked to live long enough to see justice done, and God granted him exactly that before taking his soul.
Carrying pain and injury in service of one's community without complaint is a mark of extraordinary character; Sa'd fulfilled his duty while gravely wounded and then returned his soul to God.
God chooses the timing and manner of a believer's departure from this world with wisdom — what may look like tragedy from outside is often a mercy and an elevation from God's perspective.
practical_advice: When you are going through physical or emotional pain, identify one meaningful goal or duty you want to fulfill before you rest — this sense of purpose can sustain you through enormous difficulty.
Do not assume that someone in a position of authority or judgment is motivated by worldly loyalty; true faith can transform a person's priorities completely, as Sa'd's example shows.
Make dua — sincere supplication — even in your darkest moments; Sa'd's prayer from his sickbed was answered in full. God is always listening.

title: The Throne of God Shakes at the Death of Sa'd b. Mu'adh
summary: At the death of Sa'd b. Mu'adh, the Angel Jibreel came to the Prophet to announce that the gates of heaven had been opened and the throne of God had moved in honor of this noble companion; seventy thousand angels descended to earth that night.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'When the Bana Qurayp affair was concluded, the wound of Sa'd b. Musdh burst open and he died of it, a martyr. Musdh b. Rifs'a al-Zurqi related to me as follows, "It was related to me by whomever you like of my people, that Gabriel, wearing an embroidered turban, came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), when Sa'd b. Musdh was taken late at night. He said, 'Muhammad, who is that dead man for whom the gates of heaven were opened and for whom the throne shook?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) arose hurriedly, dragging his clothing behind him, and went to Sa'd, whom he found to have died; may God be pleased with him."' The Messenger of God (SAAS) sat down by his grave as he was being buried. While thus seated, he twice recited, subhana Allah, 'God be praised'. So everyone said the same. He then spoke the words, 'AlltZhu Akbar! Allzhu Akbar! God is most Great! God is most great!'. So everyone spoke the same. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'I was amazed at this fine worshipper who was constrained in his grave until this (time), when he was relieved.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to Sa'd the day he died and while he was being buried, 'Praise be to God for this fine man for whom the throne of the All-Merciful moved, and for whom the gates of heaven were opened; he was constrained, and then God released him.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'The day Sa'd b. Mu%dhdied 70,000 angels descended to earth; they had never done so before. The grave was severely constraining him!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) went into his grave and stayed there awhile. When he emerged, he was asked, 'Messenger of God, what kept you?' He replied, 'Sacd was very much constrained in the grave; and so I prayed to God, and it was widened for him.'
lessons: The honor God bestows on a sincere believer transcends what any human title or recognition can provide — Sa'd's death moved the very throne of God and brought seventy thousand angels to earth.
Even the most righteous of believers are subject to the realities of the unseen — the pressure of the grave — reminding us that none of us should feel spiritually complacent or self-sufficient.
The Prophet's personal grief, expressed by rushing to Sa'd's side and staying by his grave, shows us that expressing love and sorrow for the deceased is not weakness but a mark of noble character.
practical_advice: When you feel unseen or unappreciated in your daily acts of worship and service, remember that God's recognition is the only recognition that truly matters and that it comes in ways beyond what we can imagine.
Attend to the funerals and burials of those in your community — the Prophet himself stayed at Sa'd's graveside and prayed for him, showing us the immense value of this act of care.
When grief overwhelms you at the loss of someone beloved, follow the Prophet's example: say subhana Allah and Allahu Akbar — returning the heart to God's greatness is the truest comfort in loss.

title: The Death of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh and the Shaking of the Throne
summary: The death of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh was so significant that the throne of the Merciful One shook, angels carried his bier, and the Prophet testified that his kerchiefs in heaven surpass the finest silk on earth.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, "Muhammad b. al-Muthanna related to us, quoting al-Fadl b. Musawir, quoting Abu Mu'awiya, from al-A'mash, from Abu Sufyan, from Jabir, who said, 'I heard the Prophet (SAAS) say, "The throne shook at the death of Sa'd b. Mu'adh."'" Also from al-Acmash, "Abu Salih related to us, from Jabir, from the Prophet (SAAS) the same words. A man asked Jabir, 'How is it that al-Bara' b. 'Azib says (that the statement was) "the al-sarir, the couch, shook?" He (Jabir) replied, "There was some animosity between these two tribes; I heard the Prophet (SAAS) say, 'The al-'arsh, the throne, of the al-Rahman, the Merciful One, shook at the death of Sa'd b. Mu'adh.'"" Muslim related this, from 'Amr al-Naqid, from 'Abd Allah b. Idris and Ibn Maja, from 'Ali b. Muhammad, from Abu Mu'awiya, both of whom quoted al-A'mash. Their version does not add anything to the account as given by al-A'mash from Abu Salih, from Jabir. Ahmad stated, "'Abd al-Razzaq related to us, from Ibn Jurayj, 'Abu al-Zubayr informed me that he heard Jabir b. 'Abd Allah say, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, while the bier of Sa'd b. Mu'adh was there in front of them, 'The throne of the Merciful One shook for it.'"'" Muslim related it from 'Abd b. Humayd, as did al-Tirmidhi from Muhammad b. Ghaylan, both quoting 'Abd al-Razzaq. Imam Ahmad stated, "Yahya b. Sa'id related to us, quoting 'Awf, quoting Abu Nadra (who said) 'I heard Abu Sa'id quote the Prophet (SAAS) as having said, "The throne shook at the death of Sa'd b. Mu'adh."'" Al-Nasa'i related it from Ya'qub b. Ibrahim. Ahmad stated, "'Abd al-Wahhab related to us, from Sa'id, (who quoted) Qatada as saying, 'Anas b. Malik related to us that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, his bier having been put down, "The throne of the Merciful One shook for it."'" Muslim related it from Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah al-Azdi, from 'Abd al-Wahhab. Al-Bayhaqi narrated from a hadith of al-Mu'tamir b. Sulayman, from his father, from al-Hasan al-Basri, who said, "The throne of the Merciful One shook with joy for his spirit." The hafiz al-Bazzar stated, "Zuhayr b. Muhammad related to us, quoting 'Abd al-Razzaq, quoting Ma'mar, from Qatada, from Anas, who said, 'When Sa'd's bier was carried, the hypocrites said, "How light his bier is! That's due to his having passed judgement over Banu Qurayza." The Messenger of God (SAAS) was asked about this, and he said, "No; but the angels carried him."'" (This has) an excellent line of transmission.
lessons: The sincerity and righteousness of a believer can elevate them to a rank so high that even the heavens respond to their passing — a reminder that our deeds, though unseen, carry immense weight with God.
True honour comes not from worldly status or physical strength, but from faithfulness to God and justice in one's actions, as Sa'd demonstrated throughout his life.
When we feel our efforts go unrecognised, the story of Sa'd reminds us that God misses nothing — every act of integrity is recorded and honoured in ways beyond human comprehension.
practical_advice: When you feel unseen or undervalued in your efforts to do good, remind yourself that God's acknowledgement transcends all human recognition — write down one righteous act you did today that no one else noticed.
Reflect on the legacy you wish to leave behind. Ask yourself: what would people say at my passing, and what would I want God to say? Use this as a compass for daily decisions.
When grieving the loss of someone righteous, channel that grief into gratitude for their example and a renewed commitment to the values they embodied.

title: The Angels Carry Sa'd's Bier and the Hypocrites' Mockery
summary: When Sa'd ibn Mu'adh's bier was carried after his death, hypocrites mocked that it was light because of his harsh judgment on Banu Qurayza, but the Prophet replied that angels themselves had carried him.
story: The hafiz al-Bazzar stated, "Zuhayr b. Muhammad related to us, quoting 'Abd al-Razzaq, quoting Ma'mar, from Qatada, from Anas, who said, 'When Sa'd's bier was carried, the hypocrites said, "How light his bier is! That's due to his having passed judgement over Banu Qurayza." The Messenger of God (SAAS) was asked about this, and he said, "No; but the angels carried him."'" (This has) an excellent line of transmission.
lessons: Those who act with justice and sincerity will be defended by God even when critics try to diminish their legacy — do not let the mockery of others define your worth.
Hypocrisy often disguises itself as criticism of the righteous; learning to distinguish sincere feedback from malicious mockery protects your inner peace and your commitment to good.
Divine assistance — like the angels carrying Sa'd — often operates invisibly, reminding us that we are never truly alone when we stand for what is right.
practical_advice: When others mock or belittle your sincere efforts, resist the urge to defend yourself to them — instead, quietly renew your intention with God, knowing He is the true witness.
Identify one situation in your life where you are facing unfair criticism. Write a short reflection on why you believe your action was right, and entrust the outcome to God.

Practice recognising the difference between constructive criticism from a sincere advisor and dismissive mockery from someone with ill intent — respond to the first with openness and to the second with dignified silence.

title: The Prophet's Praise of Sa'd's Kerchiefs in Heaven Over Worldly Silk
summary: When a luxurious silk garment was admired by companions, the Prophet declared that the kerchiefs of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh in paradise were far finer, affirming the incomparable rewards awaiting the righteous.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, "Muhammad b. Bashshar related to us, quoting Ghundar, quoting Shu'ba, from Abu Ishaq (who said), 'I heard al-Bara' b. Azib say, "I was presented with a silk robe for the Prophet (SAAS), and his Companions began touching it and admiring its softness. He said, 'Are you admiring the softness of this? The kerchiefs of Sa'd b. Mu'adh are better than them, or softer.'"'" He (the source) then said, "Qatada related this, as did al-Zuhri who reported hearing it from Anas, from the Prophet (SAAS)." Ahmad stated, "'Abd al-Wahhab related to us, from Sa'id, he being the son of Abu 'Uruba, from Qatada, from Anas b. Malik, that Ukaydir Duma made a present of a jubba, a long outer garment, to the Messenger of God (SAAS); this was before the wearing of silk was prohibited. He put it on and when people admired it, he said, 'By Him in whose hands is my soul, the kerchiefs of Sa'd in heaven are finer than this!'" This hadith accords with the criteria of both al-Bukhari and Muslim, but they did not include it. Al-Bukhari, however, quoted it as commentary. Ahmad stated that Yazid quoted Muhammad b. 'Amr as follows, "Waqid b. 'Amr b. Sa'd b. Mu'adh related to me (and Muhammad said that Waqid was one of the finest, best and tallest of men) as follows, 'I went in to Anas b. Malik and he asked me who I was. I replied that I was Waqid, son of 'Amr, the son of Sa'd b. Mu'adh. He then said, "You take after Sa'd." He then wept copiously, saying, "May God grant Sa'd His mercy! He was one of the finest and tallest of men." He then went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched an army out to fight Ukaydir Duma, who sent to him a jubba of brocade woven with gold thread. The Messenger of God (SAAS) put it on, went up on to the minbar, the pulpit, and sat down without speaking. He then came down again. People felt and gazed admiringly at the jubba, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked them, 'So you admire this then? The kerchiefs of Sa'd b. Mu'adh in heaven are much finer than what you can now see.'"'" Al-Tirmidhi related it thus, as did al-Nasa'i, from a hadith of Muhammad b. 'Amr. Al-Tirmidhi classified it as hasan sahih, "good and authentic".
lessons: The most dazzling worldly luxuries are nothing compared to what God has prepared for those who are faithful — keeping this perspective prevents us from being enslaved to material desires.
Anas wept upon meeting Sa'd's grandson, showing that loving and honouring the righteous is itself a form of spiritual connection and gratitude to God.
The Prophet used a moment of collective admiration for something beautiful as a teaching opportunity — reminding us to redirect wonder toward the eternal rather than the temporary.
practical_advice: The next time you find yourself strongly desiring a luxury or feeling envious of someone's possessions, pause and ask yourself: what am I building that will outlast this world?
Make it a habit to remember and speak well of righteous people from history — their stories are a source of motivation and a reminder of what human beings are capable of.
When you are in a position of influence, like the Prophet on the minbar, use moments of shared attention to gently redirect people toward deeper values rather than surface-level admiration.

title: Sa'd's Mother's Elegy and the Prophet's Endorsement of Her Mourning
summary: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh's mother recited an elegy for her son, and the Prophet — who generally cautioned against exaggerated mourning — declared that mourning women exaggerate except for she who mourned for Sa'd.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "His mother - Kubaysha, daughter of Rafi' b. Mu'awiya b. 'Ubayd b. Tha'laba al-Khudariyya al-Khazrajiyya, that is - spoke the following elegy when Sa'd was being borne away on his bier, 'Sa'd's mother grieves for Sa'd, the fierce and impetuous, A man of leadership and glory, an eager knight. He would fill every breach, active in cutting off enemy heads.'" Ibn Ishaq went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'All mourning women exaggerate - except for she who mourned for Sa'd b. Mu'adh!'"
lessons: Grief is a natural and honourable human response to loss; the Prophet's validation of Sa'd's mother's mourning shows that acknowledging pain is not weakness but authenticity.
A mother's love for a righteous child is itself a form of devotion — raising children of character and courage is one of the most lasting contributions a person can make.
Even in traditions that caution against excess, room is made for genuine, proportionate grief — this teaches us to distinguish between healthy mourning and self-destructive despair.
practical_advice: When you are grieving, allow yourself to feel the loss fully without guilt — suppressing grief does not heal it. Give yourself permission to mourn, then gradually move toward remembrance and gratitude.
If you are supporting someone who is grieving, do not rush them through their pain. Sit with them, listen, and validate what they feel rather than offering quick fixes.
Write a tribute — even a private one — for someone you have lost whom you deeply admire. Articulating their qualities keeps their legacy alive and helps process your grief.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit's Eulogy for Sa'd ibn Mu'adh
summary: The poet Hassan ibn Thabit composed moving verses eulogising Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, celebrating his righteousness, his just judgment over Banu Qurayza, and his entry into paradise clothed in honour.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that Hassan b. Thabit spoke the following verses eulogizing Sa'd b. Mu'adh, may God be pleased with him, "My tears descended in a stream, and it was right for my eyes that they should weep for Sa'd. Lying dead, prostrate on a battlefield. Eyes grieving at his loss flowed copiously, in constant pain. A member of the religion of the Merciful, he will inherit paradise along with the (other) martyrs; theirs is the most noble of groups. Even though you have said good-bye to us and left us and went into the dust of the dark grave You have, Sa'd, through your noble martyrdom, gone back (to God) clothed in finery and glory. By your judgement on both Qurayza tribes with what God had decreed, you did not decide wilfully. The decree of God matched your judgement on them; you did not absolve them when you were reminded of a former pact. Even though fate has placed you with those others who exchanged this life for heaven's eternal gardens Happy is the end of the trustworthy who one day are called to God in honour and glory."
lessons: Poetry and art have always been vehicles for processing collective grief and preserving the memory of the righteous — creative expression is a legitimate and powerful tool for emotional healing.
The eulogy frames death not as an ending but as a return to God 'clothed in finery and glory' — this perspective transforms how we understand loss and suffering.
Being called to God 'in honour and glory' is the aspiration of a life well lived; every day offers choices that move us either toward or away from that end.
practical_advice: If you are struggling with the death of a loved one, try expressing your feelings through writing, poetry, or art — giving grief a form can make it more bearable and meaningful.
Memorise or reflect on a piece of literature or verse that frames death as a dignified return rather than a tragedy — let it reshape your relationship with mortality.
Ask yourself today: if someone were to write about my life at its end, what would I want them to say? Then take one concrete step toward living that story.

title: The Verse of Dirar ibn al-Khattab Boasting of the Siege at al-Khandaq
summary: Before his conversion to Islam, the Qurayshi warrior Dirar ibn al-Khattab composed defiant verses boasting of the confederate army's size and ferocity during the Battle of the Trench, lamenting only that the trench protected the Muslims.
story: Ibn Ishaq, may God have mercy on him, stated, "Dirar b. al-Khattab b. Mirdas, brother of Banu Muhsrib b. Fihr, spoke the following on the battle of al-khandaq." I would add that this was prior to the acceptance by Dirar of Islam. "Many were the sympathetic women who doubted us, though we commanded a formidable force. Its size was almost as large as Mt. Uhud when the onlooker saw its dimensions. You saw their short armour amply fitting their heroes and their tough leather shields. And their fast horses charging like arrows we aimed at the misguided sinners It was as if, when we and they charged, they were shaking hands with us, at the entry to the trenches. Men among whom you could find not one wise, even though they would ask, 'Are we not rightly-guided?' We besieged them for an entire month, standing above them like conquerors. We attacked them morning and evening every day, fully accoutred in our armour. With sharp, cutting swords in our hands, we used them to slice their skulls and head-bones. The gleam of their drawn blades shining in the hands of those who unsheathed them Was like the flash of lightning on clouds brightening the night that you could see with full clarity. Had it not been for the trench where they were, we would have demolished them all. It, however, protected them; they took refuge with it from us for fear of us. Even though we withdrew, we left Sa'd laying dead by your tents. When darkness came, you could hear the women chanting their lamentation for Sa'd. We will revisit you shortly, assisting one another as we did before, A group of men of Kinana who will not be unarmed, acting like lions of the forest, defending their dens."
lessons: Even the words of an adversary can reveal truth: Dirar's poem admits it was the trench — a strategic innovation inspired by wisdom — that saved the Muslims, showing how preparation and creative thinking can overcome seemingly overwhelming odds.
Fear and intimidation are tools aggressors use, but a community that holds firm, trusts in God, and prepares wisely can withstand even the most fearsome opposition.
Dirar himself later embraced Islam, reminding us that today's opponent can become tomorrow's ally — we should never permanently write off anyone's capacity for growth and change.
practical_advice: When facing a situation that feels overwhelming, focus on what practical preparation you can make — like the trench — rather than being paralysed by the scale of the challenge.
If someone currently opposes or belittles you, resist the urge to permanently categorise them as an enemy. People change; respond with firmness where necessary but leave the door open.
Reflect on a time when a creative or unconventional solution helped you navigate a difficult situation. What did that teach you about thinking outside conventional limits?

title: Ka'b ibn Malik's Poetic Reply Affirming the Muslims' Steadfastness at al-Khandaq
summary: The companion-poet Ka'b ibn Malik composed verses in reply to Dirar's boast, affirming that the Muslims stood firm at the Battle of the Trench by relying on God and the trustworthy Prophet, not on their own strength.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "He was answered by Ka'b b. Malik, brother of Banu Salama, may God be pleased with him, 'Many were the questioning women who asked us how we battled, though if they had been witness to it they would have seen us stand firm. We did stand fast, seeing nothing equal to God against what faced us, relying on Him. On our side was the Prophet, a trustworthy ally; with him we can overcome all mankind. We were fighting a people who did wrong and were disrespectful and who were waiting a chance to attack us. When they arose against us, we fought them hard with blows that destroyed the over-hasty. The women would have seen us in full,'"
lessons: When facing overwhelming pressure, the most stabilising force is not numbers or weapons but a clear sense of whose side you are on — standing with truth and with God is itself a source of courage.
Ka'b's poem is a reminder that how we narrate our struggles matters — framing hardship as a test we endured with faith rather than a catastrophe we suffered transforms our relationship to difficulty.
Community solidarity in times of crisis — standing firm together, relying on shared values — is one of the most powerful forms of resilience available to human beings.
practical_advice: In moments of anxiety or fear, write down what you are standing for — your values, your purpose, your faith. Clarity about why you are enduring something makes the endurance itself more bearable.
When you feel outnumbered or overwhelmed by opposition, identify one trustworthy person or community you can lean on, just as the Muslims leaned on the Prophet and each other.

Practice reframing a current difficulty: instead of 'this is happening to me,' try 'this is a moment where I get to demonstrate what I am made of.' Notice how the shift in narrative changes your emotional state.

title: The Prophet Encourages Hassan ibn Thabit to Satirize the Polytheists with Gabriel's Support
summary: During the Battle of Qurayza, the Prophet encouraged the poet Hassan ibn Thabit to satirize the polytheists with his poetry, assuring him that the Angel Gabriel would support him.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, "Hajjaj b. Muhammad related to us, quoting Shu'ba, quoting 'Adi b. Thabit, as saying that he heard al-Bara' b. 'Azib state, 'The Prophet (SAAS) told Hassan, "Satirize them; and Gabriel is with you!"'" Al-Bukhari also said, "Additional material was given by Ibrahim b. Tahman, from al-Shaybani, from 'Adi b. Thabit, from al-Bara' b. 'Azib, who said, 'The Prophet (SAAS), at the battle of Qurayza, told Hassan b. Thabit, "Satirize the polytheists, for Gabriel is with you."'" Al-Bukhari also related this, as did Muslim and al-Nasa'i, with lines of transmission from Shu'ba, but without the addition related above by al-Bukhari.
lessons: God equips each person with different gifts — some fight with swords, others with words — and both forms of contribution matter in the struggle for what is right.
Divine support does not only come in dramatic forms; sometimes it comes as confidence behind a creative act, a conversation, or a piece of writing that shifts hearts.
The Prophet's encouragement of Hassan shows the importance of leaders affirming and deploying the unique gifts of those around them, rather than expecting everyone to contribute in the same way.
practical_advice: Identify your own particular gift or strength — whether it is writing, speaking, art, problem-solving, or care-giving — and ask yourself how you can use it more deliberately in service of something meaningful.
If you are in a leadership role, take time this week to specifically acknowledge and encourage someone's unique contribution rather than giving generic praise.

When you feel that your particular form of contribution is less valued than others', remember that the Prophet valued the poet's pen as much as the warrior's sword — every sincere contribution counts.

title: Poetry of the Battle of al-Khandaq (The Trench) – Verses on Standing Firm
summary: Verses composed about the Battle of al-Khandaq describe the Muslim forces standing firm in their defense, relying on God and the Prophet, facing a large coalition of enemies who ultimately withdrew in failure.
story: questioning women who asked us how we battled, though if they had been witness to it they would have seen us stand firm. We did stand fast, seeing nothing equal to God against what faced us, relying on Him. On our side was the Prophet, a trustworthy ally; with him we can overcome all mankind. We were fighting a people who did wrong and were disrespectful and who were waiting a chance to attack us. When they arose against us, we fought them hard with blows that destroyed the over-hasty. The women would have seen us in full, long chain-mail gleaming like streams in the desert, Bearing light swords in our hands with which to puncture the zeal of the trouble-makers. At the entrance to the trenches like lions whose thickets protect their dens. And our cavalry attacking by morning and evening gazed at the enemy in disdain and wore insignia In support of Ahmad and God, so that we may be sincere in worship of truth. And the people of Mecca should know when they travel, and the tribes who came in clans, That God has no partner, and that God is the Lord of those who believe. Though you may have killed Sa'd impudently, God it is who best determines. He will enter him in fine gardens which shall be the abode of the righteous, Just as He repelled you, defeated, in flight, for all your anger, shamed and in failure. Shamed, you gained no good there, and came close to being destroyed. By a gale that blew upon you so that you were rendered blind beneath it.
lessons: True strength in adversity comes from relying on God rather than one's own power alone; the believers stood firm precisely because their trust was placed in the Divine.
Steadfastness in the face of overwhelming odds is a mark of sincere faith; the Muslim defenders did not waver even when a massive coalition attacked them.
God's aid arrives in unexpected forms — here, a powerful gale — reminding believers that divine assistance is not always what the eye expects.
practical_advice: When facing a situation that feels overwhelming, consciously remind yourself that your ultimate reliance is on God, not solely on your own resources or abilities.
Recall past instances where you or others stood firm under pressure; use those memories as anchors of confidence when new trials arise.
When you feel surrounded or outnumbered in a difficult situation, focus on what is within your control — your character, your effort, your sincerity — and leave the outcome to God.

title: Verses of 'Abd Allah b. al-Ziba'ra on the Battle of al-Khandaq (Before His Acceptance of Islam)
summary: The pre-Islam poet 'Abd Allah b. al-Ziba'ra composed verses describing the great coalition army that marched on Medina — its size, its leaders, and how the trench ultimately prevented their victory.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Abd Allah b. al-Ziba'ra al-Sahmi spoke the following verses on the battle of al-khandaq." I would add that this poet had not yet accepted Islam. "Greetings to a home whose features have been obliterated by lengthy neglect and the passage of time. It is as though Jews had written their tracing, except for the corrals and tent pegs. A wasteland, as though you had not sported there, pleasantly, with girls of your own age. Think no more of a life now gone and a place in ruins and abandoned. And remember with thanks the trials of those people who went out together past the stone monuments," The monuments at Mecca, heading for Yathrib, multivoiced, numerous, many in number. Avoiding the high ground and usual routes in all the heights and passes Leading along with them their lean steeds, their bellies thin, their flanks slim, All offspring of long-bodied mares and stallions, (moving stealthily) like a lion attacking an inattentive watchman. An army with whose banner 'Uyayna led, while Sakhr commanded the ahub, the clans. Two leaders, splendid like full moons, in them the rescue of the poor, the refuge of those in flight Until when they arrived in Medina and dressed for death, bearing sharp swords. For a month and ten days they fought Muhammad, his Companions in the fighting being the very best. They announced their withdrawal that morning, and you said, 'This has almost finished us off!' Had it not been for the trenches, they would have left their forces lying dead, the prey for birds and wolves.
lessons: Even those who oppose a righteous cause may inadvertently testify to its strength; the enemy poet acknowledged that only the trench saved the attackers from catastrophe.
A person's acknowledgment of truth, even while still on a wrong path, can be a stepping stone toward eventual guidance, as this poet later accepted Islam.
Difficult situations — like a month-long siege — often test endurance to the breaking point, yet perseverance through them builds a legacy that even opponents must acknowledge.
practical_advice: When you are enduring a prolonged hardship, remind yourself that endurance itself is a form of victory — the believers at the trench held on day after day until relief came.
Notice when even critics or opponents acknowledge something admirable about how you handled a trial; let that recognition strengthen your resolve rather than inflate your ego.

If you find yourself in a situation where you once opposed something good, allow yourself to honestly reassess — this poet's eventual conversion shows that honest reflection can lead to profound change.

title: Hassan b. Thabit Answers the Enemy's Poet on the Battle of al-Khandaq
summary: Hassan b. Thabit responds to the enemy poet's verses, rebuking those who attacked the Prophet and praising God's intervention through a gale that broke the coalition army apart.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "He was answered by Hassan b. Thabit, God be pleased with him, 'Do the effaced remains of a deserted campsite respond to one who addresses them? A wasteland, its traces wiped away by frequent rain and constant gusts of wind? There I once saw dwellings made lovely by fresh-faced women of high ancestry. But enough reminiscence of fair maidens, sweet-talking and full-breasted, And complain to God of your cares and what you think of a people who maltreated the Messenger in anger. Who attacked him en masse, gathering townsmen and bedouin tribesmen to fight, An army including 'Uyayna and Ibn Harb, combining with the cavalry of the clans. And when they arrived at Medina, they hoped to kill the Messenger and to gain much plunder. They attacked us in the morning with all their strength but were repelled into retreat for all their rage By a gale that broke them apart and by the forces of your Lord, the Lord of Lords. God spared the believers their doing battle and assured them the greatest of rewards After they had despaired; the sending down of our generous Lord's assistance broke them apart. He gave relief to Muhammad and to his Companions and disgraced all the doubting defamers. Hard in heart, aggressive, doubt-filled, disbelieving men, impure. May failure cling to them, for they maintained their disbelief until the end.'"
lessons: Gratitude to God for relief after despair is the believer's natural response; the turning point at the trench came after the believers had nearly reached the end of their endurance.
Defending the honor of the Prophet and the community through lawful means — including words and poetry — is a form of service to truth.
God's help often arrives when human means seem exhausted; the gale that dispersed the enemy was a divine intervention that no military strategy could have produced.
practical_advice: When you have reached a point of near-despair in a difficult circumstance, remind yourself that divine relief is not bound by human timelines — it often arrives precisely at the lowest point.
Channel feelings of anger at injustice into constructive expression rather than destructive action; Hassan b. Thabit used his gifts of eloquence in defense of truth.
After a trial passes, take time to explicitly acknowledge and thank God for the relief; this practice of gratitude reinforces faith and builds resilience for future challenges.

title: Ka'b b. Malik's Verses on the Battle of al-Khandaq – The Reward of Faith and Valor
summary: Ka'b b. Malik composes verses praising the Muslim warriors' armor, horses, weapons, and devotion, describing the battle as a divinely aided victory and celebrating the bounty God gave as a reward.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "Ka'b b. Malik also answered him, saying, 'The battles have given us to keep the finest gift of our bountiful Lord White forts, well-watered camel pastures, dark green, where milk is plentiful. (Abundant) as lava fields, their bounty and plenty goes to neighbour, relative and guest With captured horses as swift as light, raised on barley and cut fodder. Their feet unshod, firm-fleshed, lean of body and legs Long-necked, responding eagerly to the call, like hunting dogs obeying their master Now circling the grazing livestock and then bringing down the enemy and retrieving the kill, Chasing wild beasts, light-footed, in war, grim in battle, their pedigree evident, Fed with kindness and so well-bodied and fleshed, but slim bellied. They (the warriors) arrive wearing double-meshed coats of mail and bearing strong, accurate spears. And sharp swords, their tarnish removed by the polishers, borne by fine men of high birth. His right hand gripping a slim spear at the ready, its making entrusted to Khabbab. There is a bright blue in the spear and it is like a flaring flame in the dark of night, A squadron whose chain-mail repels the arrows and turns aside their blows, Dark red, all gathered together, as though their spears in each engagement were a forest on fire, Heading for the shade of the banner as though they were an eagle's shadow, a straight lance of al-Khatt. Their heroism overcame Abu Karib, Tubba' and the bedouins. It was admonitions from our Lord that guided us, through the tongue of one splendid and good. They were presented before us and we wanted to remember them after they were set out before the clans, Wise words that the criminals thought too severe but men of wisdom understand. Sakhina came to defeat their Lord, but those seeking to overcome the All-Conquering will be overcome!'"
lessons: Material blessings and provisions are understood by the believer as gifts from God, not merely the fruit of one's own effort — gratitude transforms how one relates to prosperity.
Preparation and excellence in one's craft are not in conflict with reliance on God; the warriors were thoroughly equipped and trained, yet attributed their victory to divine guidance.
Those who attempt to overcome God's purpose will ultimately fail; the poetic warning that 'those seeking to overcome the All-Conquering will be overcome' applies to every age.
practical_advice: When you achieve something after a long struggle, deliberately attribute the outcome to God's grace alongside your effort — this keeps pride in check and gratitude alive.
Invest in preparation and excellence in your responsibilities; the detailed care described for horses, armor, and weapons reflects a community that honored their commitments thoroughly.
When facing opposition from those who seem powerful, remember that history consistently shows that forces aligned against sincere truth ultimately fail — let this perspective reduce your anxiety.

title: The Prophet's Commendation of Ka'b b. Malik's Poetry
summary: The Prophet Muhammad praised Ka'b b. Malik upon hearing one of his verses about the Battle of al-Khandaq, saying 'God thanks you for having spoken those lines.'
story: Ibn Hisham stated, "Someone I trust related to me, quoting 'Abd al-Malik b. Yahya b. 'Abbad b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'When he heard this verse from him, he said, "O Ka'b, God thanks you for having spoken those lines!"'" I would comment that by the above word sakhina Quraysh were meant. The Arabs would use this word, meaning 'hot', in referring to them because of their liking for eating hot food, unusual for the desert dwellers. But God knows best.
lessons: Recognition from a person of noble character for sincere work is a profound encouragement; the Prophet's acknowledgment of Ka'b's poetry shows how leadership uplifts those who serve with sincerity.
Using one's talents — whether poetry, art, writing, or speech — in service of truth is a form of worship that can earn divine acknowledgment.
Small words of recognition, when given sincerely by a trusted person, carry extraordinary weight; the Prophet understood the power of affirming good work.
practical_advice: Make it a practice to sincerely acknowledge the good work of others around you — a genuine word of recognition can have a lasting impact on someone's sense of purpose.
If you have a talent or skill, reflect on how it might be used in service of something meaningful and true rather than merely for personal gain or recognition.

When someone you respect acknowledges your effort, let it motivate you without letting it become the primary source of your self-worth — ground your sense of value in God's awareness of your sincerity.

title: Ka'b b. Malik's Second Set of Verses on the Battle of al-Khandaq – Obedience and Divine Aid
summary: Ka'b b. Malik composes further verses about the Battle of al-Khandaq, describing the warriors' armor, horses, and devotion to the Prophet's command, affirming that obedience to the Prophet brings divine aid and glory.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "Ka'b b. Malik also spoke the following, 'May he who enjoys the clash of battle when its noise resembles that of the crackling of flaming reeds Come to a battle site where swords were whetted, between al-Madhad and the trench's sides. They were skilled in striking at the emblazoned knights and entrusted their lives to the Lord of the East In a force God employed to aid His prophet, and He was gracious to His servant thereby. They all wore suits of armour the full lengths of which rippled like wind on the surface of a pool. Of white, finely-wrought steel, its rivets (protruding) like locusts' eyes in the tight-woven links. Held up by the belt of an Indian-crafted sword, its steel pure, sharp and shining. That along with our piety, was our dress on the day of the battle, and every hour a trial. If our swords were unable to reach we would step onward to make them do so. And you could see skulls with their tops exposed, as well as hands severed as though never created. We met the foe with a strong, united force that drove off their army as though heading for the heights of Mt. al-Mashriq. We prepared against the enemy every light-bodied, white-footed piebald horse, Swiftly bearing riders whose steeds seemed in the fray as (speedy) as lions on a damp hillside. Trusted steeds which bring death to the brave from deadly spears amidst the dust. God ordered that the horses be tied to his enemy in the warfare; God is indeed the best to bring success. That they should harass the enemy and protect the homes if the evildoers' cavalry approached. God the Almighty aided us with His power and reliable endurance at the time we met them. We obey the order of our Prophet and respond to him; if He calls us to an unpleasant task, we are not to be outpassed. When there is a summons to engage in dangerous acts, we attend them, and when we see fighting rage, we take part. Those following the Prophet's words (are right), for among us his orders are obeyed, and He is fully believed. By that He will give us aid and show our glory and ensure thereby that we achieve a pleasant life. Those who deny Muhammad are disbelievers and have strayed from the path of the God-fearing.'"
lessons: Obedience to righteous leadership, even when the commanded task is difficult or unpleasant, is a hallmark of sincere commitment and is connected to communal success.

Piety and preparation are not opposites; the poet describes both armor and taqwa (piety) as the believers' 'dress on the day of battle,' showing that spiritual and practical readiness complement each other.
Communal unity in facing adversity multiplies individual strength — the 'strong, united force' in the poem succeeded where scattered individuals would have failed.
practical_advice: When you are asked to take on a difficult or uncomfortable responsibility by someone you trust and respect, reflect before declining — sometimes the hardest tasks are the ones most worth doing.
Balance your practical preparation for challenges with spiritual preparation; neglecting either leaves you less equipped than you could be.
Invest in building and maintaining your community relationships before a crisis arrives — unity forged in peaceful times is the foundation that holds under pressure.

title: Ka'b b. Malik's Third Set of Verses – The Clans' Failure to Shake the Believers' Faith
summary: Ka'b b. Malik's verses describe how the combined tribal coalition tried to turn the Muslims from their religion but failed, affirming that God's broad help repelled their assault.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "Ka'b b. Malik also spoke the following, 'The clans well knew, when they joined against us and attacked our faith, that we would not give up Joint forces of Qays b. 'Aylam and Khindif united, but they did not know what was going to happen. They seek to turn us from our religion, while we seek to turn them from disbelief; and the All-Merciful sees and hears. When they stormed against us in battle, broad help from God helped us against their rage; That was God's grace and protection for us; and whomever God does not protect is lost. He led us to the religion of the truth, and chose it for us: and God has deeds above those of all others.'" Ibn Hisham commented, "These verses are in a qasida of his" — by which he meant a long ode.
lessons: When people or forces attempt to undermine your values and identity, clarity about what you stand for is the first line of defense — the believers' resolve was rooted in knowing what they believed and why.
God's protection is the ultimate safeguard; no coalition of human forces, however large, can overcome those whom God chooses to protect.

The believer's aim is not merely to resist attack but to embody an invitation to truth — 'they seek to turn us from our religion, while we seek to turn them from disbelief' describes a posture of active, positive witness.
practical_advice: When external pressures threaten your values or sense of identity, return to your foundational beliefs and articulate them clearly to yourself — clarity is a shield against confusion.
Avoid the trap of measuring your security by the size of the forces aligned against you; instead measure it by the sincerity of your reliance on God.
Even in adversarial situations, maintain a posture of genuine goodwill toward those who oppose you — the believer's goal is their guidance, not their defeat.

title: Hassan b. Thabit's Verses on the Execution of Banu Qurayza
summary: Hassan b. Thabit composed verses about the fate of Banu Qurayza following their treachery during the Battle of al-Khandaq, describing it as a consequence of their betrayal and warning Quraysh of a similar fate.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "Hassan b. Thabit spoke the following lines on the execution of Banu Qurayza, 'Qurayza encountered what harmed them and found no aid against disgrace. Disaster hit them equal to that which struck Banu al-Nadir That day when the Messenger of God came down at them like the light-giving moon. He had eager horses bearing riders like eagles. We left them having gained nothing, their blood smeared on them like perfume, Prostrate, vultures circling above them; thus are the evil and obstinate paid back. So warn Quraysh of the like from the All-Merciful, if they will take my advice.'"
lessons: Betrayal of a covenant carries serious consequences; the fate of Banu Qurayza was understood by the early Muslim community as a direct result of their choice to violate their treaty during a time of existential crisis.
History carries warnings for those who repeat the mistakes of others; Hassan's closing line — warning Quraysh — reflects the Quranic principle that past events are lessons for those who reflect.
Obstinacy in wrongdoing, when one has been given clear guidance and opportunity to choose rightly, intensifies the consequences of one's choices.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether you are honoring the commitments and covenants in your life — to God, to family, to community — especially when circumstances make it tempting to abandon them.
Study the patterns of history, both in sacred texts and in your own life, to identify where choices led to predictable outcomes; this awareness can guide better decisions.
When you observe the consequences of others' wrongdoing, resist both gloating and dismissiveness — instead let it deepen your resolve to act with integrity in your own life.

title: Hassan b. Thabit's Second Poem on Banu Qurayza – Their Rejection of Guidance
summary: Hassan b. Thabit's second poem about Banu Qurayza laments that they had been given the Torah and proof of the Prophet's message but rejected both, leading to their destruction.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "Hassan b. Thabit also spoke the following verses about Banu Qurayza, 'A people who helped Quraysh had made a pact, and they shall find no aid in their land. The Book was brought them, but they neglected it; they are blind to the Torah, ruined. You disbelieved in the Qur'an, though you had been brought proof of what the Warner spoke But the leaders of Banu Lu'ayy ignored the great conflagration at al-Buwayra.' "Abu Sufyan b. al-Harith b. 'Abd al-Muttalib answered him as follows, 'May God eternalize such a deed as that!'"
lessons: Having access to guidance — scripture, knowledge, clear signs — does not automatically produce faith; it must be engaged with sincerely and acted upon.
Neglecting the guidance one has been given, whether scripture, sound advice, or inner conscience, leaves a person spiritually blind even while possessing the outward form of knowledge.
Political or tribal alliances that are built on betrayal and opportunism rather than principle tend to collapse at the very moment they are most needed.
practical_advice: Regularly engage with the guidance you have access to — whether scripture, the counsel of wise people, or your own values — rather than possessing it passively without acting on it.
Examine whether any of your current commitments or relationships are based on genuine principle or merely on convenience; those built on convenience tend to fail under pressure.

If you find yourself 'going through the motions' of a belief or practice without genuine engagement, treat that as an early warning sign and take steps to reconnect with the meaning behind your actions.

title: Hassan b. Thabit's Eulogy for the Martyrs of Banu Qurayza
summary: Hassan b. Thabit composed verses eulogizing Sa'd and the companions who achieved martyrdom fighting Banu Qurayza, lamenting their loss while celebrating their loyalty and sacrifice for the Messenger of God.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Hassan b. Thabit also spoke the following eulogizing Sa'd and those men who achieved martyrdom fighting Bana Qurayza, 'My people, is there defence against matters ordained? And will good times now gone ever return? I remembered a time now gone, my emotions were disturbed and my tears flowed down. The pain of sorrow reminded me of brethren and of dead now gone, including Tufayl and Bfi' And Sa'd; they are in paradise, while their homes are empty and the earth a waste-land without them. At the battle of Badr they were loyal to the Messenger, while above them were the shades of death and flashing swords. He called, and they responded properly, all of them responsive and obedient to every command. They did not withdraw until all were gone; and it is only battles that shorten a life-span. For they hope for intercession from him, at a time when only prophets will intercede. And so that, 0 best of worshippers, was our brave action, our response to God while death awaited. We made the first step towards You, while behind us in God's religion others will follow our first. We know that dominion is God's alone, and that God's decree will surely prevail.'"
lessons: Grief over the loss of loved ones is a natural and honored emotion; even the companions of the Prophet wept openly for those they lost.
True loyalty is measured not by the absence of fear but by remaining steadfast in obedience even when surrounded by death.
Remembering the virtues and sacrifices of the righteous dead is a spiritually enriching act that strengthens the living community.
practical_advice: When grieving the loss of someone dear, honor their memory by reflecting on what made them noble rather than only dwelling on the pain of absence.
Find comfort in the belief that those who lived with sincerity and obedience to God are in a better place, even when their absence leaves a void.
Channel sorrow into gratitude by recalling the good deeds shared together and letting that legacy inspire your own continued commitment.

title: The Rivalry of Aws and Khazraj in Serving the Prophet
summary: The tribes of Aws and Khazraj competed with one another like rivals to serve the Prophet, each striving to match or surpass the other in acts of loyalty and sacrifice for Islam.
story: "Muhammad b. Musallam al-Zuhri related to me as follows, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Ka'b b. Malik as having said, 'God had so ordered it for His messenger (SAAS) that Aws and Khazraj used to compete with one another like two stallions. If Aws ever accomplished something for him, then Khazraj would not want this to give them any advantageous position with him over themselves. So they would strive hard to achieve something similar. And when Khazraj accomplished something, Aws would say the same.
