When Aws struck down Ka'b b. al-Ashraf for his enmity to the Messenger of God (SAAS), Khazraj determined that Aws would have no advantage with him over themselves thereby. And so they asked themselves who had expressed an enmity similar to that of Ibn al-Ashraf; they thought of Ibn Abi al-Huqayq, who was at Khaybar. They therefore asked permission of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to kill him, and he agreed.'"
lessons: Healthy competition in acts of goodness and service elevates an entire community rather than dividing it.
A community united around a noble cause will naturally channel its energy into constructive rivalry rather than destructive conflict.
The desire to be close to righteous leadership can be a powerful motivator for personal and communal excellence.
practical_advice: When you feel competitive with peers, redirect that energy toward competing in acts of service, generosity, or personal growth rather than tearing others down.
Use the accomplishments of others as inspiration to raise your own standard of contribution rather than as a source of resentment.
Reflect on what cause or community gives your life meaning, and consider how you can increase your dedication to it.

title: The Mission to Kill Sallam b. Abi al-Huqayq (Abu Rafi') at Khaybar
summary: Five men of Khazraj were sent by the Prophet to neutralize Abu Rafi', a merchant who had gathered the clans against the Muslims, and they carried out a dangerous night mission inside his fortress at Khaybar.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the battle of al-khandaq and the Banu Qurayza affair were over, the matter of Sallam b. Abi al-Huqayq, he being known as Abu Rafi', arose. He was one of those who had gathered the clans against the Messenger of God (SAAS). Prior to the Battle of Uhud, Aws had killed Ka'b b. al-Ashraf and so the Khazraj asked permission of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to kill Sallam b. Abi al-Huqayq, who was at Khaybar, and he agreed. Five men of Khazraj, of Banu Salama, then set out to do this. They were 'Abd Allah b. 'Atik, Mas'ud b. Sinan, 'Abd Allah b. Unays, Abu Qatada al-Harith b. Rib'i, and Khuza'a b. Aswad, an ally of theirs from Banu Aslam. The Messenger of God (SAAS) placed 'Abd Allah b. 'Atik in command of them and told them to refrain from killing any woman or children. It was night when they arrived at the compound of Ibn Abi al-Huqayq. They took care to close all the doors upon those who lived there. He himself was in an upper chamber reached by a ladder. They climbed it, reached his door and asked to come in. His wife came out to them and asked who they were. They replied that they were bedouin seeking supplies. She told them they had come to the right man and let them in. When we went in, we shut him and ourselves inside the room, fearing someone might interfere between us. His wife screamed and gave him warning about us, but we set upon him with our swords while he was still in bed. I swear it was only his whiteness - like that of a Coptic dress lying there - that showed us where he was in the black of the night. As his wife screamed, we raised our swords to her, but remembered what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had forbidden and lowered them again. We would otherwise have killed her too that night. As we struck him with our swords, 'Abd Allah b. Unays plunged his into his stomach and pierced him through, as he said the words, 'Qapli! Qapli!' 'That's enough! That's enough!' Then we left. 'Abd Allah b. 'Atik had poor sight and he fell off the ladder and severely sprained his arm. We carried him off into a water channel at one of their wells. They lit torches and chased all around looking for us, but gave up and assembled around him as he expired. We wondered how we could ascertain that he had died, so one of us suggested that he would go back to see. He mingled with those there and reported how he had seen her - Sallam's wife - holding a torch and telling people as she looked into his face, 'I swear, I heard the voice of Ibn 'Atik. But then I told myself I must be wrong, because there was no way he could be in these parts!' She then went close and, looking into his face, said, 'He's dead, by the God of the Jews!' And I swear, I never heard words sweeter to me than those! When he returned and told us this, we carried our companion away and reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS) the death of God's enemy. When with him, we argued over which of us had actually killed him, each of us claiming it. He then said, 'Hand me your swords.' When we did so, he examined them and said of the sword of 'Abd Allah b. Unays, 'This one killed him; I can see food traces on it.'"
lessons: Restraint in the heat of danger — such as sparing the woman despite her interference — reflects the depth of one's moral training and obedience to higher command.
Even in moments of great danger, precision of command and discipline within a group determines the difference between success and chaos.
The Prophet's calm wisdom in settling disputes — examining the swords rather than accepting each person's claim — models how leadership resolves conflict with evidence rather than favoritism.
practical_advice: When you face a high-stakes situation, revisit the boundaries you have committed to beforehand so that in the moment of pressure you do not act against your values.
In group efforts, agree on clear leadership and decision-making processes before beginning, so that disputes after the fact do not overshadow the accomplishment.
When tensions arise over credit or recognition, look for objective evidence and fair criteria rather than allowing personal feelings to escalate the disagreement.

title: 'Abd Allah b. 'Atik's Entry into the Fortress Through Trickery and His Broken Leg Healed by the Prophet
summary: In the Bukhari narration of the mission against Abu Rafi', 'Abd Allah b. 'Atik gained entry into the fortress by disguising himself near the gate, navigated the darkened chamber alone to kill Abu Rafi', broke his leg falling from the stairs, and had it miraculously healed by the Prophet's touch.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, "Yusuf b. Musa related to us, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Musa, from Isra'il, from Abu Ishaq, from al-Bara', who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out some men of the ansar to attack Abu Rafi', the Jew. He appointed 'Abd Allah b. 'Atik in charge of them. Abu Rafi' had been doing harm to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and assisting his enemies. He was inside a fort he owned in Hijaz. When they drew near him, by which time the sun had set and the people were taking their livestock inside, 'Abd Allah told his companions, "Sit down here, and I'll go off and play a trick on the gatekeeper to get inside." He then went on, close to the gate, where he shielded himself with his clothing as though relieving himself, the other residents already having gone inside. The gatekeeper called out to him to come on inside since he wanted to close the gate. So I went in and hid. When everyone was inside, he locked the gate and hung the keys on a peg. I then took them and opened the gate. Abu Rafi' was entertaining in an upstairs room of his. When his guests left, I went on up to him, closing each door behind me from the inside, determined that if they spotted me they would not reach me before I killed him. When I reached his chambers, he was inside a figh, a dark apartment, but I did not precisely know his whereabouts. So I called out, "Abu Rafi'!" "Who's there?" he asked. I then hurried towards the voice and struck out at him with my sword. But I was confused and accomplished nothing, while he shouted out. So I left the chamber and went a little off, then came back in again, asking, "What was that sound, Abu Rafi'?" He replied, "Woe on your mother, I swear, there's a man in the house who just hit me with a sword!" At that I hit him hard once more but still did not kill him. Then I put the tip of my sword into his stomach until it reached his back. I then knew I had killed him. After that I opened the doors again, one by one, and reached a stairway. When I put my foot down, thinking I had finished climbing down I fell. It was a moon-lit night. I broke my leg but tied it up with a turban and went on as far as the gate, where I sat, telling myself I should not leave until I knew I had killed him. At the cock's crow the death-announcer got up on a wall and shouted, "I lament the death of Abu Rafi', saviour of the people of Hijaz." I then left to join my companions, telling them, "Let's go! God has killed Abu Rafi'!" When I reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, he said, "Stretch out your leg!" I did so and he touched it. And it was as if it had never caused me pain!'"
lessons: Perseverance through physical pain in pursuit of a mission one believes to be right is a mark of extraordinary commitment and faith.
The healing touch of the Prophet serves as a reminder that God's support can come in unexpected, tangible ways to those who strive sincerely in His path.

Composure under pressure — returning to the room a second time after initial failure rather than fleeing in panic — demonstrates that courage is not the absence of fear but the ability to act despite it.
practical_advice: When you encounter setbacks in the middle of an important task, pause, regroup, and return with a steadier approach rather than abandoning the effort entirely.
Physical pain or difficulty encountered while doing what is right can be endured more easily when you hold on to a clear sense of purpose and trust that help will come.
After completing a difficult mission or challenge, take time to report back to those who sent you and reflect on what happened — accountability and reflection are essential parts of growth.

title: The Assassination of Abu Rafi' by Abdullah b. Atik
summary: The Prophet sent Abdullah b. Atik and companions to assassinate Abu Rafi', a Jewish leader who was inciting hostility against the Muslims. Abdullah infiltrated the fortress through trickery, killed Abu Rafi', fell from a ladder and dislocated his leg, but returned to give the Prophet the news of the mission's success.
story: Al-Bukhari also stated, "Ahmad b. 'Uthman b. Hakim al-Awdi related to us, quoting Shurayh, quoting Ibrahim b. Ynsuf, from his father, from Abn Ishaq, who said he heard al-Bar2 say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out 'Abd A113h b. 'Atik, and 'Ahd Allah b. Wtha, along with others, against Aba &fic. When they drew near the fortress, 'Abd Allah b. 'Atik told them to wait while he went on ahead.' (He recounted), 'I played a mck to get inside the fortress. They could not find a donkey of theirs and went out with a lantern to find it. I was afraid I would be discovered, so I concealed my head and crouched as though relieving myself. He (the gatekeeper) called out, "Whoever wants to go in should do so before I lock the gate." So I went inside then hid in a donkey stall and they kept on at the fortress gate. There were people dining with Abn WlC conversing with him until it was night. Then they went home. 'When the voices died down and I could hear no movement, I came out. I saw where the gatekeeper had placed the key to the fortress, in a niche, and I took it. So I used it to open the door, telling myself I would quietly sneak out if people detected me. I then barred the doors of all their dwellings from the outside. 'After that I cliibed a ladder up to Abn Rafi' and found his apartment dark, the lantern having been extinguished. I did not know where the man was, so I called out his name. When he responded, I made for the voice and struck him. He cried out, but my blow did no injury. Later I came back to h i as though to help him and, disguising my voice, asked him, "What's wrong, Abn Rxfi'?" He replied, "Woe on your mother! A man came in to me and struck me with a sword!" I then made for him and struck him again, but once more to no avail. He shouted out and aroused his family. After that I again returned, changing my voice as though coming to help. He was lying on his back. So I placed my sword on his stomach and leaned on it until I heard the sound of (it striking) hone. I then left, in some confusion, found my way to the ladder I wanted to descend, hut I fell off it and dislocated my leg. I bandaged it and went on to my companions, limping. I told them, "Get going! Tell the Messenger of God, the good news! I'll not leave until I hear the announcement of his death." When dawn broke, the announcer climbed up and lamented the death of Aha &tic. So I got up and left, feeling no pain, and rejoined my companions before they reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and I told him the good news.' Al-Bukhari, alone of the authors of the six canonical books of tradition, related this text. He then went on, 'Al-Zuhri stated, Ubayy b. Ka'b said, "So they went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was on the mznblir, the pulpit, at the time. He asked, 'Are faces happy?' They replied, 'May yours be, Messenger of God!' 'Did you slay him?' 'Yes,' they replied. 'Hand me the sword,' he asked. He unsheathed it and then said, 'Yes, that's his food on the tip of the sword.'"' I would comment that it is likely that when 'Abd All* h. 'Atik fell from the ladder that he dislocated his shank and sprained his foot and his leg. When he bound it up, it had the immediate effect of calming the pain. And when he wanted to walk, he was aided (by unseen forces) for he was engaged in a beneficial exercise. When he reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and came to a rest, the pain in his leg again flared up. When he stretched it out and the Messenger of God (SAAS) touched it, that took away the pain there had been, as well as what he might have expected there would be thereafter. This would reconcile the former and latter accounts. But God knows best.
lessons: Steadfast commitment to duty can carry a person through physical pain and hardship that would otherwise seem insurmountable.
Trust in divine aid is not passive; it accompanies active effort and perseverance in one's mission.
The Prophet's blessed touch and presence brought healing, reminding us that closeness to righteous guidance is itself a source of relief.
practical_advice: When facing a daunting or painful task, focus on completing the step directly in front of you rather than the full scope of the difficulty ahead.
In moments of physical or emotional exhaustion, remind yourself of your purpose and the people counting on you — this can unlock reserves of strength you did not know you had.
Seek out the company of righteous, grounding people when you are struggling; their presence and prayers can bring a sense of relief and healing to your burdens.

title: The Execution of Khalid b. Sufyan al-Hudhali by Abdullah b. Unays
summary: The Prophet sent Abdullah b. Unays alone to kill Khalid b. Sufyan al-Hudhali, who was gathering a hostile force against the Muslims. Abdullah successfully carried out the mission, praying while walking toward his target, and was rewarded by the Prophet with a special stick as a sign for the Day of Judgement.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Ya'qob related to him that his father quoted Ibn Ishaq as saying, "Muhammad b. Jacfar b. al-Zuhayr related to me, from Ibn 'Abd Allah b. Unays, from his father, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) called for me and said, "I have been informed that Khdid b. Sufym b. Nubayh al-Hudhali is gathering a force of men to attack me. He is at Wrzna. Go to him and kill him." I replied, "Messenger of God, please describe him to me so that I can recognize him." "When you see hi", he replied, "you'll tremble." 'So I left, bearing my sword; I came upon him at Wrma where he had litter-borne women for whom he had sought a campsite. When it was time for the al-'asr prayer - and I had experienced that shivering that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had described for me when I should see him - I went towards him. As I did so, I was worried that some conflict might develop between us that would preoccupy me from performing the prayer. So I prayed as I walked towards him, nodding my head for the prayer bows and prostrations. When I reached him, he said, "And who might you be?" I replied, "I'm a bedouin Arab who has heard of you and of your gathering a force against that fellow, and I've therefore come to you." He replied, "Yes, I am engaged in that." I then accompanied him a while until, when I could, I attacked him with my sword and killed him. I then departed, leaving his women all crouched over him. 'When I reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he saw me, he asked, "Happy, I hope?" I replied, "I've killed him, Messenger of God!" "You spoke true," he said. He then arose with me, entered his house and presented me with a stick, saying, "Keep this with you, 'Abd Allah h. Unays." 'So I took it and went outside where people asked me what it was. I replied that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had given it to me and had told me to keep it. They then suggested I return to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and ask him to explain about that. So I did go hack and asked him why he had given me the stick. He replied, "It is a sign between yourself and me for Judgement Day. There will be very few that day with something to lean upon."' The account continues, 'And so 'Abd All& attached it to his sword and it was always with him until, upon his death, it was included in his winding sheet and he and it were buried together.'"
lessons: Even in the midst of danger and urgency, Abdullah b. Unays did not abandon his prayer, teaching us that worship is the anchor that keeps us grounded no matter the circumstances.
The Prophet honoured and remembered those who served sincerely, showing that genuine effort is seen and valued by God and His messenger.
Carrying a reminder of one's covenant — like Abdullah keeping the stick — is a powerful practice of keeping one's purpose and connection to faith ever-present.
practical_advice: No matter how busy or stressed you are, protect your moments of prayer and reflection; they are not an interruption to your responsibilities but the foundation that makes you capable of fulfilling them.
When you accomplish something difficult, take a moment to return to your mentor or a trusted person and share it — this act of accountability deepens your bond and invites blessing.
Find a small, meaningful object or ritual that reminds you of your values and commitments; having a physical anchor can help you stay spiritually grounded through life's challenges.

title: Amr b. al-As Considers Joining the Negus After the Battle of al-Khandaq
summary: After the battle of al-Khandaq, Amr b. al-As, still not yet a Muslim, gathered like-minded Qurayshi men and proposed they seek refuge with the Negus of Abyssinia, fearing the growing power of the Prophet Muhammad. This decision would eventually lead to his conversion to Islam.
story: Ibn Ishaq related the following after his account of the killing of Abii MI'. "Yazid b. Aba Hahib related to me, from Rashid, freed-man of Habib b. Aws al-Thaqafi, from Habib h. Aws, as follows, ''Amr b. al-'AS related to me directly, saying, "When we left with the ahziib, the clans, after the battle of al-khandaq, I gathered some Quraysh who thought as I did and respected my opinion and told them, 'You know how I think that the Muhammad affair is escalating badly. You know my opinion; what is yours?' "'"'How do you mean?' they asked. "'"'Well,' I went on, 'I think we should join the Negus and stay with him. If Mnhammad defeats our people, we'll be there with the Negus, and we'd prefer to be under his authority than that of Mnhammad. If our people prevail, they already know us, and we'll experience nothing hut good from them.' ""'They responded, 'That's a (good) opinion.' ""'I then said, 'Let's gather something to present to him.' The gift he liked most from our country was leather, so we collected a large quantity of it for him. "'"After that we left and joined him. While we were there with him, 'Amr b. Umayya al-Damri arrived; the Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent him to the Negus concerning Ja'far and his companions. He went in to see the Negus and then came out again. I told my friends, 'That's 'Amr b."
lessons: Even those who once opposed Islam could be moved by witnessing its unstoppable moral and social power, reminding us that hearts can change when truth becomes undeniable.
Uncertainty about the future is a universal human experience; how we respond to that uncertainty — through consultation, reflection, and seeking wise counsel — defines our character.
The journey to faith is often gradual and prompted by unexpected circumstances; no one should despair of finding guidance no matter how far they feel from it.
practical_advice: When you feel lost about which direction to take in life, do not rush into action out of fear; instead, gather trusted people around you and think through the options carefully before committing.

If you are at a crossroads and feel pulled in different directions, take a moment to examine what your fears are actually telling you — sometimes anxiety points toward a deeper need for change and growth.
Be open to the possibility that the path you have been resisting may be the very one meant for you; approach unfamiliar ideas with curiosity rather than defensiveness.

title: Amr ibn al-As and Khalid ibn al-Walid Accept Islam Together on the Road to Madinah
summary: Amr ibn al-As, traveling to Madinah to accept Islam, encounters Khalid ibn al-Walid on the road who is heading for the same purpose; together they go to the Prophet and pledge allegiance.
story: I met Khalid b. al-Walid - that being before the conquest of Mecca - while he was coming from Mecca. I asked him, 'Where are you headed, Aba Sulayman?' He replied, 'I swear by God, the way has become clear. The man really is a prophet. I am going to accept Islam; until when (should I delay)?' I then told him, 'I swear by God, I myself have only come to accept Islam!' ""'So we went to Medina to see the Prophet (SAAS). Khalid arrived first, accepted Islam and declared his allegiance. I went up to him and said, 'Messenger of God, I pledge allegiance to you on condition that you forgive me all my past sins, without reference to what is yet to come.' ""'The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "Amr, do pledge, for Islam erases all before it, just as the Hijra erased all that preceded it.' ""'I then made my pledge to him and left.'" Ibn Ishaq went on, "A reliable source related to me that 'Uthman b. Talha b. Aba Talha was with them both and accepted Islam at the same time.
lessons: No matter how long or deeply one has opposed the truth, the door of sincere repentance and acceptance remains open, and God's mercy can erase all that came before.

The Prophet's response to 'Amr's condition illustrates that Islam does not demand that a person carry the burden of their past into their new beginning; a clean slate is part of the gift of genuine turning to God.
Shared conviction with a companion on the path to truth can strengthen one's resolve; we are often encouraged toward the right decision when we discover others have reached the same conclusion.
practical_advice: If you have been delaying a right decision out of pride or fear, ask yourself honestly: 'Until when should I delay?' The longer you wait, the heavier the weight of what you are postponing.

When you are ready to make a sincere new beginning, do not let anxiety about your past prevent you from taking the step; trust that genuine commitment to good is honored and that the past does not have to define your future.
Seek out people on a similar journey of growth and renewal; traveling toward positive change alongside even one like-minded companion makes the path far less daunting.

title: The Prophet's Marriage to Umm Habiba While She Was in Abyssinia
summary: Umm Habiba, widowed in Abyssinia after her husband apostasized and died a Christian, receives the joyful news that the Prophet has arranged to marry her, with the Negus acting as his agent and providing her dowry.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related following the battle of al-khandaq, quoting al-Kalbi from Aba Salih from Ibn 'Abbas, who said with relation to the words of the Almighty, "Perhaps God will bring about friendship between you and those of them you consider enemies" (surat al-Mumtahana; LX, v.7). "This relates to the marriage of the Prophet (SAAS) to Umm Habiba, daughter of Abu Sufyan; she thus became the umm al-mu'minin, the 'mother of the believers,'" while Mu'awiya (son of Abu Sufyan), became the khal al-mu'minin, the 'uncle of the believers'." Al-Bayhaqi then stated, "Abu 'Abd Allah al-Hafiz recounted to us, quoting Ahmad b. Najda, quoting Yahya b. 'Abd al-Hamid, quoting Ibn al-Mubarak, from Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, who said that Umm Habiba was with 'Ubayd Allah b. Jahsh who had gone to the Negus and had died. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had then arranged marriage with Umm Habiba while she was there in Abyssinia, and the Negus had arranged this, giving her a dowry of 4,000 dirhams which he had taken to her by Shurahbil b. Hasana; it was he who provided her trousseau, the Messenger of God (SAAS) not sending her anything." Al-Bayhaqi then recounted through Ibn Lahi'a, from Abu al-Aswad, from 'Urwa, that 'Ubayd Allah b. Jahsh died a Christian in Abyssinia. The next to marry Umm Habiba was the Messenger of God (SAAS); it was 'Uthman b. 'Affan, God be pleased with him, who arranged her marriage to him. Al-Zubayr b. Bakkar stated, "Muhammad b. al-Hasan related to me, from his father, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr b. Zuhayr, from Isma'il b. 'Amr, that Umm Habiba, daughter of Abu Sufyan said, 'While I was in Abyssinia, I was surprised by a visit from an envoy of the Negus, a slave-girl called Abraha who used to care for his clothes and anoint him. She asked to come in and I agreed. Then she said, "The king says to you, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) has written to me asking me to arrange your marriage to him.'" I replied, "God has sent you with good news." Abraha then continued, "The king says to you, 'Appoint an agent to arrange your marriage.'" "'So I sent for Khalid b. Sa'id b. al-'As and appointed him my agent. Then I gave to Abraha two silver bracelets and two silver camel decorations I was wearing and silver rings from each of my toes, I was so pleased with the good news she had brought me. "'That evening the Negus ordered Ja'far b. Abi Talib and the Muslims there to attend him and he made an address in which he said, "Praise be to God, the King, the All-Holy, the Faithful, the Powerful, the Almighty! I testify that there is no god but God, that Muhammad is His servant and His messenger, and that it is he whose coming 'Isa (Jesus), son of Mary predicted. Further, the Messenger of God (SAAS) has requested that I marry him to Umm Habiba, daughter of Abu Sufyan. I have responded to the request made by the Messenger of God (SAAS), who has given 400 dinars as her dowry." He then poured out the coins before everyone. "'Khalid b. Sa'id spoke, saying, "Praise be to God! I give Him praise and ask His forgiveness. I testify that there is no god but God, and that Muhammad is His servant and messenger whom He sent with guidance and the religion of truth to show its superiority to all religions, however much the polytheists may dislike this. Moreover, I have responded to what the Messenger of God (SAAS) has requested, and I have contracted his marriage to Umm Habiba, daughter of Abu Sufyan. May God bless the Messenger of God (SAAS)." "'The Negus then paid the dinars over to Khalid b. Sa'id who took them. Then everyone was about to rise, but he (the Negus) then said, "Take your seats, for it is the practice of prophets to have a feast when they marry." He then called for food and they ate and dispersed thereafter.'"
lessons: Even in the midst of personal loss and displacement far from home, God's care for a believer can arrive in the most unexpected and dignified of forms, transforming grief into a new chapter of honor.

The Negus's generous and respectful conduct in arranging and funding the marriage ceremony demonstrates that righteousness and compassion are not limited to one community, and that God can place helpers for believers in surprising places.

Umm Habiba's experience teaches that profound hardship — losing a husband to apostasy, being widowed in a foreign land, separated from family — does not mean one has been abandoned; God's provision can arrive when least expected.
practical_advice: When you are going through a period of loneliness or loss, try to remain open rather than closed; Umm Habiba's story reminds us that the next chapter of life can begin arriving even before the grief of the current one has fully lifted.

Practice expressing gratitude generously in moments of good news, even in small ways, as Umm Habiba gave her silver jewelry to the messenger who brought her glad tidings; gratitude expressed outwardly reinforces its feeling inwardly.

If you are far from home and feel unsupported, reflect on the fact that God's provision does not require your usual network to be present; help and honor can come through entirely unexpected channels.

title: The Apostasy and Death of Ubayd Allah ibn Jahsh in Abyssinia
summary: Ubayd Allah ibn Jahsh, who had migrated to Abyssinia with the Muslims, was subverted by Satan and converted to Christianity, dying upon that faith in Abyssinia and leaving his wife Umm Habiba a widow.
story: Al-Bayhaqi then recounted through Ibn Lahi'a, from Abu al-Aswad, from 'Urwa, that 'Ubayd Allah b. Jahsh died a Christian in Abyssinia. I comment that the conversion to Christianity of 'Ubayd Allah b. Jahsh has been described above. That occurred after he had migrated to Abyssinia with the Muslims. There Satan subverted him and made Christianity seem attractive to him, and he therefore embraced it until he died. May God curse him! He used to mock the Muslims by telling them, "We see clearly; your eyes are still half closed!"
lessons: Outward migration or association with the righteous does not by itself protect a person from spiritual decline; the internal commitment of the heart requires constant vigilance and sincere effort.
Mocking those who are striving sincerely on a difficult path is a sign of spiritual arrogance, and such arrogance can be the beginning of a person's undoing.

The story is a reminder that those around us may sometimes choose paths that lead them away from what is good; we cannot control others' choices, but we can grieve those losses while continuing to hold firm ourselves.
practical_advice: Take your own spiritual and moral commitments seriously regardless of the company you keep; the presence of good people around you is a blessing, but your own inner work cannot be delegated to them.
If someone you care about makes choices that move them away from what you believe is good and true, allow yourself to grieve that loss without letting it destabilize your own convictions.
Be cautious of the internal habit of looking down on others who are struggling; spiritual arrogance is one of the subtler and more dangerous forms of self-deception.

title: The Marriage of the Prophet to Umm Habiba: Historical Debate and Scholarly Opinions
summary: Scholars debate the timing and circumstances of Prophet Muhammad's marriage to Umm Habiba, including conflicting narrations about whether Abu Sufyan arranged the marriage and the reliability of certain hadith.
story: b. Umayya, when seen by <Amrb. al-As emerging from the presence of the Negus, following the battle of al-khandaq, had been active in the matter of Umm Habiba. But God knows best. However, the h & z al-Bayhaqi stated, "Aba 'Abd Allah b. Mandah related that the marriage of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Umm Habiba was in the year 6 AH, while his marriage to Umm Salama was in the year 4 AH.'' I comment that Khalifa, Aba 'Ubayd Allah Macmar b. al-Muthanna and Ibn al-Baraqi said the same, and that the marriage to Umm Habiba was in 6 AH. Some people said that it was 7 AH. Al-Bayhaqi said, "That is more likely." I also note that, as has been related heretofore, his marriage to Umm Salama was late in the year 4 AH. It is likely that the marriage to Umm Habiba occurred before that, though it could also have been thereafter. It is most probable that it was following the battle of al-khandaq, because of what we have related above regarding 'Amr b. al-'AS, and how he saw 'Amr b. Umayya with the Negus, engaged in that matter. But God knows best. The hi@? Ibn al-Athir recounted in al-Ghzba, quoting Qatada, that when Umm Habiha migrated from Abyssinia to Medina, the Messenger of God (SAAS) became engaged to her and married her. Some scholars maintain that he married her after her father had accepted Islam, following the conquest of Mecca. But that contradicts what was related by Muslim, through 'Ikrima b. CAmmS al-Yamani, from Abii Zumayl Simxk b. al-Walid, from Ibn CAbbas, that Abii Sufy2n said, "0 Messenger of God, grant me three wishes!" "Yes," he replied. "That you give me authority to battle the unbelievers just as I used to battle the Muslims." 'LYes," he replied. "And that you appoint Mu'awiya as a clerk of yours." "Yes," he replied. "And I have at home the best and most lovely of all the Arab women, Umm Habiba, daughter of Abii Sufya; let me marry you to her." That is the hadith in its entirety. Ibn al-Athir stated, "This hadith is one for which Muslim is criticized. This is because when Abii Sufyan came to renew the pact before the conquest, he went in to see his daughter Umm Habiba and she rolled up the rug of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He then asked, 'By God, I don't know whether you consider the carpet too good for me or me too good for it!' She replied, 'This rug belongs to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and you are a polytheist.' He replied, 'I swear by God, you've changed for the worse since you left me, daughter!' Ibn Hazm stated, 'This hadirh was invented by 'Ikrima b. 'AmmiXr. This statement from him should not be considered dependable.' Others stated that he wanted to renew the pact because of the disadvantages in it (tbat had been included) without his permission. Others say it was because he believed that his daughter's marriage would be dissolved because of his acceptance of Islam. But all this is unconvincing. What is most likely is that he wanted to marry him (the Prophet (SAAS)) to his other daughter 'Amra, since he thought tbat would bring honour to himself. And he therefore sought help from his daughter Umm Habiba, as is stated in both ~ahihcollections. The narrator was merely in error in his using the name Umm Habiba. That is why we have given (only) one report. Abii 'Ubayd al-Qsim b. S a l l h stated, 'Umm Habiba died in the year 4 4 AH.' Abn Bakr b. Abii Khaythama said, 'She died one year before Mu'zwiya.' The death of Mu'itwiya occurred in Rajab of 60 AH.
lessons: Scholarly disagreement and careful examination of sources is a sign of intellectual honesty and integrity in preserving truth.
When faced with conflicting information, we should seek the most reliable sources and defer to those with greater knowledge rather than jumping to conclusions.
Faith and family loyalty are sometimes tested when loved ones hold different beliefs, as seen in Umm Habiba's steadfast devotion to the Prophet even in the presence of her polytheist father.
practical_advice: When you encounter conflicting accounts or information, take time to research carefully before forming a firm opinion, and remain humble about the limits of your knowledge.
If a family member or loved one holds beliefs very different from yours, maintain respect and boundaries without compromising your own values, as Umm Habiba demonstrated.
In moments of confusion about complex historical or religious matters, consult knowledgeable and trustworthy scholars rather than relying solely on your own interpretation.

title: Umm Habiba Confronts Her Polytheist Father Abu Sufyan
summary: When Abu Sufyan visited his daughter Umm Habiba before the conquest of Makkah, she refused to let him sit on the Prophet's rug because he was still a polytheist, demonstrating her unwavering faith over family ties.
story: This is because when Abii Sufyan came to renew the pact before the conquest, he went in to see his daughter Umm Habiba and she rolled up the rug of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He then asked, "By God, I don't know whether you consider the carpet too good for me or me too good for it!" She replied, "This rug belongs to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and you are a polytheist." He replied, "I swear by God, you've changed for the worse since you left me, daughter!"
lessons: True faith sometimes requires us to place our convictions above family approval or social comfort, even when it is painful.
Our relationship with what is sacred deserves to be protected and honored, even in the face of pressure from those we love.
Standing firm in one's values in a difficult moment, as Umm Habiba did, is an act of profound moral courage that can leave a lasting impression.
practical_advice: When pressured by family or peers to compromise on your core values, pause and ask yourself whether the approval you are seeking is worth the integrity you would be sacrificing.
Set respectful but clear boundaries with those who may not share your values, as Umm Habiba did, without resorting to hostility or unkindness.
Reflect on how your faith shapes your daily decisions and relationships, and identify areas where you might need more courage to live consistently with your beliefs.

title: The Marriage of the Prophet to Zaynab bint Jahsh: A Divine Command
summary: The Prophet Muhammad married Zaynab bint Jahsh after her divorce from his freed-man Zayd, fulfilling a direct divine command that abolished the pre-Islamic custom of treating adopted sons as biological sons.
story: THE MARRIAGE OF THE MESSENGER OF GOD (SAAS) TO ZAYNAB, DAUGHTER OFJAHSH B. RPAB B. YA'MUR B. SABRA B. MURRA B. KABiR B. GHANAM B. DUDAN B. ASAD B. KHUZAYMA, AL-ASADIYYA, UMM A L - M V M I N i N , 'MOTHER OF THE BELIEVERS'. Her mother was Umayya, daughter of 'Ahd al-Murtalib, aunt of the Messenger of God (SAAS). She was previously the wife of his freed-man Zayd b. ljnritha, God be pleased with him. Qatiida, al-Wiiqidi and some of the people of Medina said that he married her in the year 5 AH; some added the month, Dhii al-Qa'da. The h ~ & al-Bayhaqi stated, "He married her after the attack on Banii Qurayza." Khalifa b. Khayyzy, Abii Whayda Macmar b. al-Muthannit and Ibn Mandah said that he married her in the year 3 AH. The first possibility is better known and it is the one accepted by Ibn Jarir and several historians. Various commentators, theologians and historians give as the reason for his marrying her reference to a hadith related by Ahmad b. Hanbal in his collection that we have deliberately refrained from including so that those lacking understanding would not place it in the wrong context.
lessons: Divine wisdom sometimes works through circumstances that appear difficult or unconventional to human understanding, and trust in God's plan requires patience and submission.
Social customs and traditions, no matter how deeply ingrained, can be reformed when they cause injustice, and courageous action in the face of societal pressure is sometimes required.
The highest spiritual honour can come through the most unexpected paths, as Zaynab's marriage was arranged by God Himself in a way none could have anticipated.
practical_advice: When facing a situation that feels socially awkward or that others may misunderstand, trust in the righteousness of your intentions and the guidance you have received rather than seeking constant external validation.
Recognize that some of life's most meaningful chapters begin with endings — a divorce, a loss, or a change — and remain open to what God may be preparing for you beyond your current circumstances.

If you are navigating a major life transition that involves breaking from social expectations, seek counsel from knowledgeable and trustworthy people who can help you understand the wisdom behind your path.

title: The Quranic Verse Revealed About Zaynab's Marriage and the Veil
summary: God revealed Quranic verses directly addressing the Prophet's marriage to Zaynab bint Jahsh, explaining the divine wisdom behind it and simultaneously establishing the rule of the hijab for the Prophet's household.
story: In His Glorious Qur'm, Ahnighty God stated, "And (consider) when you say to him whom God had favoured and whom you had favoured, 'Keep your wife for yourself, and fear God,' and you hide within yourself what God makes evident, and you fear men. Yet God has greater right that you fear Him. And since Zayd has now finished with her, We give her to you in marriage so that there should he no distress for believers regarding the wives of their adopted sons when the latter have divorced them. God's command must be fulfilled. And there should be no distress for the Prophet in accomplishing what God has imposed upon him. Such has been God's course with those who went before. God's decree is determined'" (s~ r u at l - A h z ~ kXXXIII, v.37, 38). We have discussed this at sufficient length in our Tafsir (Exegesis). The person referred to as having received God's favour is Zayd h. Hzritha, the freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS). God favoured him with Islam, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) also favoured him by setting him free and by marrying him to Zaynab, daughter of Jahsh, the daughter of his own maternal aunt. Muqatil b. H a b b a stated that the dowry he provided for her was ten dinars and sixty dirhams, a veil for her head and face, a wrap, a coat of armour, fifty mudd (a dry measure) and ten mudd of dates. She (Zaynab) remained with him (Zayd) close to a year, or longer, but then they quarrelled. Her husband complained about her to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he would say to him, "Keep your wife for yourself and fear God." God stated, "You hide within yourself what God makes evident." Regarding this, 'Ali b. al-Husayn Zayn b. alLCAbidinand al-Suddi said, "He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) knew that she was to be one of his wives and it is he who hid (this) within himself." Many early authorities discussed this matter in various strange ways. Some of these are controversial and so we are omitting them. God Almighty stated, "And since Zayd has now finished with her, We give her to you in mamage ..." This refers to Zayd having divorced her. And when her 'idda period was over, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent to her so that she would become engaged to himself, and he then married her. And it was the Lord of the universe, Blessed and Almighty is He, who married him to her, as is established in the ~ahihcollection of al-Rukh%ri, from Anas b. Mzlik. The latter related that Zaynab, daughter of Jahsh, used to claim superiority over the other wives of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and say, "It was your families who arranged your marriage, whereas it was Almighty God, from above the seven heavens, who arranged mine!" There is a report through Cisa b. Tahman, From Anas, which says, "Zaynab would claim superiority over the wives of the Prophet (SAAS) and say, 'It was God who arranged my marriage from heaven.' And it was regarding her that the verse requiring use of the hyab, the veil, was revealed, namely, '0you who believe, do not enter the chambers of the Prophet unless you are invited to eat, without waiting while it he cooked'" (siirat al-Ahzzh XXXIII, v.53).
lessons: God's commands, even when they appear difficult to us in the moment, carry profound wisdom and serve to establish lasting principles of justice and clarity for entire communities.

Fear of people's judgment can hold us back from fulfilling what is right; the Quran's gentle reminder that 'God has greater right that you fear Him' is a call to prioritize divine guidance over social approval.

Even the most honoured people experience internal conflict and hesitation, and the Quran's acknowledgment of the Prophet's human feelings teaches us that struggling with difficult decisions does not diminish one's faith or character.
practical_advice: When you find yourself delaying a right action out of fear of what others will say, consciously remind yourself of who you are ultimately accountable to and allow that awareness to give you courage.

Reflect on moments in your life where an ending — a relationship, a job, a chapter — eventually led to something better that you could not have foreseen, and use those memories to build trust in God's plan during current hardships.

If you are experiencing internal conflict about a decision that feels socially taboo but spiritually sound, seek grounded counsel from scholars or trusted mentors who can help you discern between genuine divine guidance and personal desire.

title: Zayd Delivers the Marriage Proposal to Zaynab on Behalf of the Prophet
summary: Zayd ibn Haritha visited Zaynab bint Jahsh to convey the Prophet's proposal of marriage, and she responded with humility and gratitude, turning to prayer before the Quranic verse confirming the marriage was revealed.
story: Im%n Ahmad stated, "Hashim (meaning Ibn al-Q~im)related to us, quoting d-Nadr, quoting Sulayman b. al-Mughira, from Thahit, from Anas, who said, 'When Zaynab's & a period was over, the Prophet (SAAS) said to Zayd, "Go and make mention of me to her." He went to her - she was leavening her dough at the time - and (later said), "When I saw her, I felt so emotional I could not look at her, because the Messenger of God (SAAS) had made mention of her. So I turned my back to her and withdrew, saying, 'Zaynab, rejoice! The Messenger of God (SAAS) makes reference to you (for marriage).' She exclaimed, 'But I have not done anything to cause my Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He, to so honour me!' Then she went to her place of prayer." Thereafter the (passage in the) Q u r ' a was revealed, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) came and went in to her without (seeking) permission.'"
lessons: Zaynab's first response to extraordinary honour was not pride but humility and gratitude directed toward God, which is the hallmark of a truly grateful heart.
When God's blessings arrive unexpectedly and in ways we did not plan or engineer, the most fitting response is to turn in prayer and acknowledge that the honour came from Him alone.
Zayd's emotional restraint and selflessness in delivering a proposal that marked the end of his own marriage exemplifies an extraordinary level of spiritual maturity and sincerity.
practical_advice: When you receive unexpected good news or a blessing you feel unworthy of, resist the urge to immediately celebrate with others and instead begin with private gratitude and prayer to ground yourself in humility.

Practice recognising moments in your life where God's favour has arrived not through your own effort but as pure grace, and let those moments deepen your sense of reliance on Him rather than on your own merit.

If you are ever asked to do something difficult and selfless for someone you care about, as Zayd was, draw on your faith and sense of duty rather than your emotions, trusting that God sees and honours your sacrifice.

title: The Wedding Feast of the Prophet and Zaynab, and the Revelation of the Verse of the Veil
summary: After the Prophet consummated his marriage with Zaynab bint Jahsh, a wedding feast was held, but some guests overstayed, causing discomfort to the Prophet, which led directly to the revelation of the Quranic verse of the veil.
story: Anas went on, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) consummated his marriage with her, we made a feast of meat and bread for her sake. Some people left while some men stayed in the house talking following the food. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left, and I followed him as he went to each of the chambers of his wives to greet them. They called out to him, '0 Messenger of God, how did you fmd your family?' I don't know whether it was I who informed him that the people had left, or
lessons: Even in joyful moments, being mindful of others' comfort and space is an act of consideration and good character.
The Prophet's sensitivity to social discomfort without expressing irritation shows that emotional maturity includes managing one's own boundaries gracefully.
Celebrations and joy in community are a blessed part of life, but they require awareness of when to step back and allow others their privacy and peace.
practical_advice: When attending gatherings, be attentive to social cues that indicate it is time to leave, and do not let your own comfort or enjoyment come at the expense of your host's peace.
After a period of celebration or joy, take time to reconnect with those you love through small gestures of acknowledgment and care, as the Prophet did by visiting each of his wives to greet them.
If you find yourself overwhelmed by social obligations even during happy occasions, give yourself permission to gently step away and tend to your own emotional and spiritual needs without guilt.

title: The Institution of the Hijab (Screen) at Zaynab's Wedding
summary: After the Prophet's marriage to Zaynab bint Jahsh, some wedding guests lingered too long in conversation, causing the Prophet distress. This occasion led to the divine revelation of the verse instituting the hijab (screen) and etiquette for visiting the Prophet's household.
story: went on, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) consummated his marriage with her, we made a feast of meat and bread for her sake. Some people left while some men stayed in the house talking following the food. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left, and I followed him as he went to each of the chambers of his wives to greet them. They called out to him, '0Messenger of God, how did you find your family?' I don't know whether it was I who informed him that the people had left, or whether he was told of this (by someone else)." He went on, "Then he left and entered his chamber and I went to enter it with him. But he threw down the screen between myself and him. (Then it was) revealed about the hqrb, the screen and the people were admonished by the words, 'Do not enter the chambers of the Prophet unless you are invited.'" Muslim and al-Nasali related it thus, through Sulayman b. al-Mughira. An Account of the institution of the hqzb, the screen, on the morning of Zaynab's wedding arranged by God. It was appropriate for the screen to be instituted during this wedding, as a protection for her and for her sisters, also known as the umahat al-mu'mmin, the "mothers of the faithful". This was in accord with the previously held view of Wmar Ibn al-Khat~b. Al-Bukhari stated that Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah al-Raqqash related to him, quoting Mu'tamir b. Sulayman, who said, "I heard my father say, 'Abii Mijlaz quoted Anas b. Mdik as having said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) married Zaynab, daughter of J&sh, he invited people in and they ate and sat there conversing. When he was ready to arise, they did not do so. When he saw that, he himself arose. When he did so, others also arose, though three men remained seated. When the Prophet (SAAS) came (back) to go inside, the people were still sitting, but they then got up and left. I went and told the Prophet (SAAS) that they had left and he returned and went inside. I went as if to go inside too, but he let down the screen between myself and him. Then God Almighty revealed, "0 YOU who believe, do not enter the chambers of the Prophet."'" Al-Bukhsi related this in other passages; and Muslim and al-Nasa'i did so with other lines of transmission from Mu'tamir. Al-Bukhari then related it - he alone doing so - from a hadith of A y F b from Abti Qulkba, from Anas in similar form. Al-Bukhari stated that Abii Mu'ammar related to him, quoting 'Abd d-Waith, quoting 'Abd alLCAzizb. Suhayb, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "A feast of bread and meat was given for the marriage of the Prophet (SAAS) to Zaynab, daughter of Jahsh. I was dispatched to invite people to come for the food, and they would come and eat then leave; others would come and do the same. I continued inviting until I could not find anyone left to invite. I then said, '0 Prophet of God, I don't find anyone to invite.' He responded, 'Take the food away.' But there were three men who stayed in the house conversing. So the Prophet (SAAS) left and went to the chamber of 'A'isha and said, 'Peace be upon you, people of the house, along with the mercy and blessings of God.' She replied, 'Peace be upon you, along with the mercy and blessings of God. How did you find your family, may God bless you!' He then went and visited the chambers of each of his wives, spoke to them as he had to 'A'isha and they responded to him as she had done. He then came back and the three men were still in the house conversing. The Prophet (SAAS) was exceedingly modest and he then left again, heading for 'A'isha's chamber. And I don't know whether I told him or whether he was (otherwise) informed that the people had left, but he came out, and, placing one foot over the threshold of the door and leaving the other outside it, he let down the screen between myself and him. Then the verse relating to the htjdb was revealed." Al-Bukhai was alone in giving this from this line. He then related it, also uniquely, from Ishaq, he being Ibn Nasr, from 'Abd Allah b. Bukayr alSahmi, from Humayd b. Anas in similar form. That account refers to two men, instead of three. But God knows best. Al-Bukhai also gave the chain of a similar hudith from Ibrzhim b. Tahmm, from al-Ja'd Aba Wthmm, from Anas. Ibn Aha Hatim said, "My father related to us, quoting Aba al-Mqaffar, quoting Ja'far b. Sulayman, from al-Ja'd Aba 'UthmZn al-Yashkuri, from Anas h. Mdik, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) and some of his wives gave a wedding feast. Umm Salim made some hays, a dish of date, butter and curds, and placed it in a bowl, saying, "Go to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and tell him that this is a little something for him from us."' "Anas went on, 'People at that time were under strain. I took it to h i , saying, "Messenger of God, Umm Salim has sent you this, with her greetings, and says, 'This is a little something for you from us.'" He looked at it and said, "Place it over near the house." He then said, "Go and invite for me so-and-so" and he named a large number of men. He added, "And any other Muslims you may meet." So I invited those he had told me, along with the other Muslims I met. When I returned, I found the house, the stone benches and the courtyard filled with people. I asked, "Aba Wthmm, how many must there have been?" "Some 300" he replied.' "Anas went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told me to accompany him and I took it (the bowl of food) to him. He placed his hand over it and prayed, saying, md sh2u Allgh! "Whatever God wills!" He then said, "Have them form groups of ten and invoke the name of God; every person should eat what he finds." They began invoking God's name and to eat until all had done so. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told me, "Pick it up." I then went and took the bowl and looked inside. And I don't know whether there was more when I had put it down or when I picked it up!' "'Some men stayed behind conversing in the house of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Meanwhile, his wife, whom he had brought in with them, was there turning her face to the wall. The men stayed so long chatting that they annoyed the Messenger of God (SAAS). He was the most modest of men; if they had realized it, they would have felt badly. Eventually he went off into his chambers to his wives. When they (the men there) saw him do so, they thought they had annoyed him, and so they moved to the door and left. Then he returned, let down the screen and went inside while I was there in the (outer) room. The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed inside his house for a while and God made a revelation of the Qur'an. He came out reciting this verse, "0 you who believe, do not enter the chambers of the Prophet unless you are invited to eat, without waiting while it be cooked. If you are invited, then do enter and when you have eaten, disperse. Do not linger on for conversation. That would annoy the Prophet, though he would be too shy (to tell you so); but God is not shy of the truth. And if you should ask something of them (his wives), do so from behiid a screen. That is more pure for both you and them. And you should not annoy the Messenger of God, nor should you ever marry his wives after him. That would indeed be offensive to God. And whether you divulge something or keep it co~lcealed, God has knowledge of all."'" (sarat al-Ahznb; XXXIII, v.53, 54). Anas went on, "He recited these to me before the others. And so I have known them longer than them." Muslim, al-Tirmidhi and al-Nasx'i all related this tradition from Qutayba, from Ja'far b. Sulaymxn, from al-Jakl Abn Wthmzn. Al-Tirmidhi categorized it as hasan sahib, "good and authentic".
lessons: Divine guidance can emerge from ordinary social situations — God uses real-life moments to establish principles that protect dignity and privacy for generations.

The Prophet's exceptional modesty meant he would endure discomfort rather than confront others directly, teaching us that God compensates for the gaps left by human shyness by providing clear boundaries through revelation.
Boundaries and etiquette in social settings are not merely cultural norms but can carry deep spiritual significance, protecting the sacred and the vulnerable.
practical_advice: When you feel uncomfortable asking someone to leave or change their behavior, recognize that setting boundaries is not rudeness — it is a form of respect for everyone involved, and seeking divine guidance can help clarify how to do so.
Practice social awareness by reading the cues of those around you; overstaying your welcome or lingering when others wish for privacy causes harm even without ill intent.

When you feel overwhelmed by others' presence or demands on your personal space, it is permissible and healthy to create a 'screen' — physical, emotional, or communicative boundaries — that preserve your peace and dignity.

title: The Miracle of the Food Multiplying at Zaynab's Wedding Feast
summary: At the wedding feast for the Prophet's marriage to Zaynab bint Jahsh, Umm Salim sent a small bowl of hays. The Prophet prayed over it and it miraculously fed approximately 300 people, with the bowl appearing even fuller afterward.
story: Ibn Aha Hatim said, "My father related to us, quoting Aba al-Mqaffar, quoting Ja'far b. Sulayman, from al-Ja'd Aba 'UthmZn al-Yashkuri, from Anas h. Mdik, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) and some of his wives gave a wedding feast. Umm Salim made some hays, a dish of date, butter and curds, and placed it in a bowl, saying, "Go to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and tell him that this is a little something for him from us."' "Anas went on, 'People at that time were under strain. I took it to h i , saying, "Messenger of God, Umm Salim has sent you this, with her greetings, and says, 'This is a little something for you from us.'" He looked at it and said, "Place it over near the house." He then said, "Go and invite for me so-and-so" and he named a large number of men. He added, "And any other Muslims you may meet." So I invited those he had told me, along with the other Muslims I met. When I returned, I found the house, the stone benches and the courtyard filled with people. I asked, "Aba Wthmm, how many must there have been?" "Some 300" he replied.' "Anas went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told me to accompany him and I took it (the bowl of food) to him. He placed his hand over it and prayed, saying, md sh2u Allgh! "Whatever God wills!" He then said, "Have them form groups of ten and invoke the name of God; every person should eat what he finds." They began invoking God's name and to eat until all had done so. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told me, "Pick it up." I then went and took the bowl and looked inside. And I don't know whether there was more when I had put it down or when I picked it up!'" Muslim, al-Tirmidhi and al-Nasx'i all related this tradition from Qutayba, from Ja'far b. Sulaymxn, from al-Jakl Abn Wthmzn. Al-Tirmidhi categorized it as hasan sahib, "good and authentic".
lessons: Giving what little you have with a sincere heart — as Umm Salim did — opens the door for divine abundance that far exceeds your expectations.
Invoking the name of God before any action, even eating, transforms the mundane into the sacred and invites blessing into ordinary moments.
Material scarcity is no barrier to generosity; the truest form of giving is offering what you have, however small, and trusting God to multiply its benefit.
practical_advice: When you feel you have too little to offer — whether in material resources, energy, or capability — give what you can anyway with a sincere intention; you may be surprised by the abundance that follows.
Before meals, work, or any effort, pause to invoke God's name and ask for blessing; this simple practice shifts your mindset from anxiety about scarcity to trust in provision.
During times of communal hardship, small acts of generosity — a gift of food, a kind gesture — carry outsized impact and can unite people around gratitude rather than stress.

title: The Character and Legacy of Zaynab bint Jahsh
summary: Aisha praised Zaynab bint Jahsh as one of the most religious, charitable, and truthful of women, and the Prophet foretold that the first of his wives to join him in the Hereafter would be the most generous — which proved to be Zaynab.
story: I note that Zaynab, daughter of Jahsh, may God be pleased with her, was one of the fust women who migrated. She was a woman of great goodness and charity. At fust her name was Tarra, then the Messenger of God (SAAS) renamed her Zaynab. She was known as Umm al-Hakam, "al-Hakam's mother". 'A'isha, may God be pleased with her, said, "I never knew a woman more religious, more God-fearing, more truthful in her speech, more protective of family ties, more trustworthy or more charitable than Zaynab." It is established in both jahih collections, as will he seen in the hadirh al-ifk, "the calumnious account", that 'A'isha stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked Zaynab, daughter of Jahsh, about me. She was the (only) wife of the Prophet who could compete with me for his favour. God encompassed her with goodness, and she said, '0 Messenger of God, in all truth and obedience, I say I never knew of her ('A'isha) anything but good.'" Muslim b. al-Hajjsj stated in his ~ahih collection that M h t i d b. 'Ayliln related to him, quoting al-Fadl b. Masa al-Shaybzni, quoting Talha b. Y&y%b. Talha, from 'A'isha 'mother of the believers', who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'The women who will be the earliest to join me (in the Hereafter) are the ones who are the most generous.' We used to vie with one another over which of us was the most generous. In fact it was Zaynab, because she made things herself and gave to charity." Muslim is alone in giving this. Al-Waqidi and other authorities on the biography of the Prophet (SAAS), on the early military campaigns and on history, state that she died in 80 AH. The Commander of the Believers Wmar b. d-Khatgb, may God be pleased with h i , prayed over her. She was buried at al-Baqic; and she was the first woman for whom a bier was made.
lessons: True nobility of character is measured not by status or beauty but by consistent qualities of honesty, generosity, piety, and protection of family bonds — qualities Zaynab embodied throughout her life.

Even when we are rivals or competitors with others, God's grace can lead us to speak the truth about them and wish them well, as Zaynab did for Aisha despite their competition for the Prophet's affection.
The Prophet's prophecy that the most generous wife would be first to join him in the Hereafter teaches that generosity is one of the highest spiritual virtues, with eternal reward.
practical_advice: Reflect on the qualities Aisha praised in Zaynab — religiosity, truthfulness, protection of family ties, trustworthiness, and charity — and honestly assess which of these you most need to cultivate in your own life.

When you feel competitive or envious of someone, challenge yourself to speak truthfully and generously about them as Zaynab did; this act of integrity protects your own soul far more than winning any rivalry.

Find a form of charitable giving that involves your own hands and effort — making something, crafting, cooking — as Zaynab did, because personally invested generosity carries a unique spiritual weight.

title: The Expedition Against Banu Lihyan and the Capture of Thumama ibn Uthal
summary: In the year 6 AH, the Muslims mounted an expedition against Banu Lihyan, and separately Muhammad ibn Maslama led an expedition to Najd during which Thumama ibn Uthal al-Yamani was captured, though the precise dating of these events is debated among scholars.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated, "People used to say that it was in Muharram of that year that there was the expedition of Muhammad b. Maslama to Najd. It was during this that they captured Thumama b. Athal al-Yamai." I note, however, that in the text of Ihn Ishaq from Sa'id al-Maqburi, from Aha Hurayra, that he (Abii Hurayra) was present then and it was after the expedition against Khaybar that he (Aba Hurayra) emigrated. It (the expedition to Najd) would therefore have to be dated thereafter. But God knows best. It was during the early part of this year, for certain, that the expedition was mounted against Banii Lihym. Ibn Ishaq stated, "The conquest of Banii @rayza took place in Dhii dQacda and the" [text continues].
lessons: Even great scholars acknowledge the limits of their knowledge with the phrase 'God knows best,' reminding us that humility before uncertainty is a mark of wisdom, not weakness.
Military and political challenges required the early Muslim community to remain organized and responsive, demonstrating that protecting a community sometimes requires difficult proactive decisions.

Historical scholarship requires careful cross-referencing and intellectual honesty — when sources conflict, the truthful response is to acknowledge the discrepancy rather than forcing a false certainty.
practical_advice: When you face situations where you lack complete information or certainty, practice saying 'I don't know, but here is what I do know' — this honesty builds trust and keeps you from compounding confusion with false confidence.

When your community faces external threats or pressures, resist the temptation toward paralysis; take measured, considered action while remaining open to revising your understanding as more information emerges.
Study history and the accounts of those who came before you, even imperfect and debated accounts, because wrestling with complexity in the past sharpens your judgment for challenges in the present.

title: Expedition Against Banu Lihyan
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) mounted an expedition against Banu Lihyan seeking revenge for the martyrs of al-Raji', disguising his true direction by appearing to head toward Syria.
story: It was during the early part of this year, for certain, that the expedition was mounted against Banii Lihym. Ibn Ishaq stated, "The conquest of Banii @rayza took place in Dhii dQacda and the start of Dha al-Hijja. The polytheists had charge of the pilgrimage that year, 5 AH that is." As is related above. He went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained in Medina thereafter for the months of Dha al-Hijja, Muharram, Safar and both Rabic al-Awwal and Rabical-Th&i. In Jumada al-Uwla, at the start of six months following the conquest of Banii Qnrayva, he went forth against Bana Lihym seeking revenge for Khubayb and his companions who were killed at al-Rajic. In order to surprise the enemy, he made it appear that he was heading for Syria." According to Ibn H i s h a , he left Ibn Umm Makmm in command of Medina. What Ibn I s h ~ qis referring to here is as follows. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had reached their homes (in his attack on Banii Lihyan) they retreated before him and sought refuge in the mountain heights. He therefore turned off to 'Usfiln, where he encountered a force of polytheists. It was there that he performed the ~aliital-khawf; the L'fearprayer". This expedition has been described above as having occurred in 4 AH. Al-Bayhaqi related it there. But what Ihn Ishaq relates is most probable, namely that it took place after the battle of al-khandaq. It is established that he did perform the fala al-khawf at Wsfan on the expedition against Banii Lihyan. It should best, therefore, be included here and removed from there, in accord with the Imam, the prime authority, on the military expeditions in his own era and thereafter. As al-Shafi'i, may God be pleased with him, stated, "Whoever wants information of the military expeditions is dependent upon Muhammad b. Ishsq." Ka'b b. Mdik spoke the following verses on the expedition against Banii Lihyan: "If Bann Libya had stayed they would have met at their dwellings a hand of brave fighting men Mounted on fast horses; their hearts would have filled with terror at the great force of their host, vast as heaven's gate. But they were mere weasels, keeping to the high and inaccessible Hijnz passes."
lessons: Strategic wisdom and planning are essential in facing adversity; the Prophet's decision to disguise his direction shows that prudent preparation is part of reliance on God.
Seeking justice for the wronged is a noble cause; the expedition was motivated by avenging the martyrs of al-Raji', showing that standing up for the oppressed is a righteous pursuit.
When enemies flee rather than face justice, it does not mean the effort was wasted; the moral and strategic message sent can itself be a form of victory.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult confrontation, prepare thoroughly and think strategically rather than acting impulsively, trusting that careful planning is part of God's guidance.
If you are seeking to right a wrong done to others, let your motivation be justice and not revenge; keep your heart focused on the righteous purpose.
When your efforts do not yield the outcome you hoped for, reflect on what was still accomplished and trust that God's wisdom encompasses what you cannot see.

title: The Expedition Against Dhu Qarad: The Raid on the Prophet's Camels
summary: Uyayna ibn Hisn and Ghatafan horsemen raided the Prophet's milch-camels at al-Ghaba, killing a Ghifari guard and abducting his wife, prompting a swift Muslim pursuit.
story: Ibn Ishxq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then proceeded to Medina. He had been there only a few nights when Wyayna b. Hisn h. Hudhayfa b. Badr al-Faziiri and some horsemen of Ghapfm attacked milch-camels belonging to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at al-Ghxha. A man of Banii Ghifar and his wife had been guarding the camels; the raiders killed him and abducted his wife along with the camels. "'Asim b. Wmar b. Qatada related the following to me, as did 'Abd All& h. Aha Bakr, along with another man I trust, from 'Ahd Allah b. Ka'b b. Mdik; each of these sources gave part of this account. The first to become aware what they had done was Salama b.'Amr h. al-Akwac al-Aslami who was heading for al-GhZha armed with his bow and arrows and accompanied by a slave belonging to Tabs b. 'Ubayd Allah leading his horse. When he had climbed up to the al-Wadac pass, he saw some of their horsemen. Then he looked down towards Sale and cried out (in alarm), 'WE~~baha!'" He hurried off in pursuit of the raiders, like a lion. When he caught up with them, he began shooting his arrows at them, exclaiming, 'Take that! I'm Ibn al-Akwac! Today the wicked will die!' "Whenever their horsemen headed for h i he would flee, then return and shoot again at them whenever he could, repeating the same battle cry. One of them exclaimed (using a pun on his name, Ibn al-Akwa') 'Is he trying to yukammicu, "to divert us", so early in the day!' "News of Ibn al-AkwaQsbattle cry reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina, and he called out there: 'Alarm! Alarm!' At that the cavalry hurried to the Messenger of God (SAAS). The fvst to reach him was al-Miqdad b. al-Aswad, followed by (Abbad b. Bishr, Sacd b. Zayd, Usayd h. Zuhayr (though there is some doubt about this man), Wkzsha h. Mihsan, M u k z b. Nadla, brother of Banii Asad b. Khuzayma, Ahii Qatada al-Harith b. Ribci,brother of Bann Salama, Abii 'Ayyzsh 'Ubayd b. Zayd b. S*t, brother of Bana Zurayq. "When they were gathered around the Messenger of God (SAAS), he appointed Sag h. Zayd as their leader. He then told them, 'Get out and pursue them, and wait till I join you with our men.' "I have been told by some men of Banii Zurayq that the Prophet (SAAS) said the following to Aba <Ayyash:'What if I were to give this horse over to a better rider than yourself so that he can catch up the enemy?' Abii Ayy%h says, 'I responded, "Messenger of God (SAAS), I'm the best of all horsemen!" Then I slapped my horse and I swear by God, he'd not carried me 50 arm-lengths before he tossed me! I was amazed.' "Some men of (Banii) Zurayq say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave over Abii Ayyash's horse to Musdh h. M a 4 ~or to 'A'idh b. Mzcis b. Qays b. Khalda, the eighth (of the horsemen there). Some count Salama b. al-Akwac as the eighth and drop Usayd b. Zuhayr. But God knows best. Salama b. al-Akwac was not a rider that day; he was the first to catch up with the raiders on foot."
lessons: Courage in the face of immediate danger, like Salama's lone pursuit of the raiders, shows that one person's bravery can make a tremendous difference and inspire an entire community to act.
Community response to crisis should be swift and organized; the Prophet's immediate call to alarm and organized dispatch of cavalry demonstrates the importance of collective action.
Overconfidence in one's own abilities, as shown by Abu Ayyash's claim to be the best horseman, can lead to humbling moments that teach us to remain humble before God.
practical_advice: When you witness an injustice or crisis, do not wait for others to act first; raise the alarm and do what you can within your capacity, trusting that others will follow.
In moments of communal crisis, organize yourself under leadership and act swiftly rather than descending into chaos or hesitation.
Regularly examine your own pride and overconfidence; acknowledge your limitations and remain open to the possibility that others may be better suited for certain tasks.

title: The Death of Muhriz ibn Nadla at Dhu Qarad
summary: The brave companion Muhriz ibn Nadla was the first Muslim rider to catch the raiders at Dhu Qarad and was killed after boldly calling them to stop and face justice.
story: The account continues, "The horsemen left and caught up with the enemy. 'A~imh. Wmar b. Qatiida related to me that the first rider to catch up with them was M u b i z h. Nadla, known as al-Akhram, and also as Qumayr. The horse he rode belonged to M&miid b. Maslama. The name of the horse was Dhii al-Lamma. When he reached the enemy, he said, 'Stop, you tribe of no-goods, and let the an@r and the rnuhajirin pursuing you catch up with you!' One of the raiders then attacked and killed him. His horse wheeled and could not he caught; it raced off without stopping until it reached a stable of Banii 'Abd al-Ashhal; that is, it went back to the stall in Medina where it had been." Ibn Ishaq went on, "No other Muslim than him was killed that day." Ibn Hisham stated, "Some other scholars refer to WaqqQ b. Mujjaziz al-Mudliji as having been killed along with him." Ibn Ishaq continued, "A source I trust related to me, quoting 'Ahd Allah b. Ka'b h. Mdik, that Muhriz was riding a horse named &Jan*, belonging to Wkzsha b. Mihvan. Muhriz was killed and Janiih was captured. But God knows best."
lessons: The willingness to sacrifice oneself for the protection of the community is among the highest acts of courage; Muhriz's death calling the enemy to face justice is a testament to selfless bravery.
Death in the defense of what is right is not a defeat but an honor; the companions understood that their lives were in God's hands and acted accordingly.
Even the most courageous efforts do not always end in worldly success, and we must trust that God honors the intention and the sacrifice regardless of the outcome.
practical_advice: When you face situations where standing up for justice carries personal risk, draw strength from the example of those who came before you and placed their trust entirely in God.
Do not measure the value of a righteous action solely by its worldly outcome; act with sincerity and leave the results to God.
Honor the memory and sacrifice of those who gave everything for a just cause by continuing their work and not letting their sacrifice be in vain.

title: Abu Qatada Kills Habib ibn Uyayna and Covers Him with His Cloak
summary: Abu Qatada killed Habib ibn Uyayna during the Dhu Qarad pursuit and covered him with his cloak as a sign of his deed, causing initial alarm among the Muslims who feared Abu Qatada himself had been slain.
story: "When the cavalry engaged, Aba Qatada L i e d Hahih h. Wyayna, covered h i with his cloak, then caught up with the rest. Soon the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived there with the other Muslim warriors." Ihn H i s h h stated, "He placed Ibn Umm Maktiim in command of Medina. "When the Muslim force found Habib (b. Wyayna) covered with Ahii Qanda's cloak, they were shocked, believing the latter had been killed. But the Messenger of God (SAAS) told them, 'It's not Abii Qatzda; it's someone he has killed, then covered with his cloak to let you know that he did it.'"
lessons: Even in the heat of battle, composure and clear communication are vital; the Prophet's calm clarification prevented unnecessary grief and confusion among his companions.
Appearances can be deceiving; we should not rush to conclusions based on incomplete information but seek clarity before reacting.
Acts of bravery and accountability, like Abu Qatada covering his slain enemy to mark his deed, reflect a culture of honesty and responsibility even in chaotic circumstances.
practical_advice: When you receive alarming news, pause before reacting emotionally; seek clarification and full information before drawing conclusions or expressing grief.
In your own life, practice transparency and accountability in your actions so that others can trust and understand your intentions.
Trust in the wisdom and guidance of those with greater knowledge and perspective when you are confused or distressed by what you witness.

title: Ukkasha ibn Mihsan Kills Two Raiders with One Lance
summary: During the pursuit at Dhu Qarad, the companion Ukkasha ibn Mihsan caught two raiders riding the same camel and killed them both with a single thrust of his lance, helping recover the stolen camels.
story: "Wk2sha h. Mih~anthen caught up with Awh& and his son 'Amr who were riding the same camel. He pierced both with his lance, killing them both together. They then retrieved some of the milch-camels. The Messenger of God (SAAS) proceeded on and encamped at Mt. Dhn Qarad; he was joined by his force and they remained there for a day and a night. Salama h. al-Akwa' told hi,'Messenger of God, if you were to send me on with 100 men, I could recover the rest of the herd and kill the enemy.' But the Messenger of God (SAAS), so I have been told, replied, 'By now they are being served their evening drinks among Ghatafzn.' He then provided a slaughtered camel for each 100 men and they remained there. After that he returned to Medina."
