Ibn Ishaq then gave evidence for the first person named by reporting that an aqltri slave-girl came to the well while Najiyya was down filling a bucket. "She spoke the following verses, 'Hey, you drawing water, my bucket is before you. I saw our people praising you Wishing you well and congratulating you.' The man then responded to her with the following verses, 'A Yemeni slave-girl has learned that it is I who draw the water, and that my name is Nājiyya. Many a deep, gushing wound I've made, piercing the chests of the enemy.'"
lessons: Uncertainty about historical details is acknowledged honestly by scholars, reflecting intellectual humility.
Simple acts of service — drawing water for the Prophet's caravan — were honored and remembered by the community.
Courage and dedication to serving a noble cause bring recognition and praise even from strangers.
practical_advice: When you are unsure about the facts of a situation, acknowledge the uncertainty rather than forcing a conclusion — this protects your integrity.
Find honor in whatever role you play in a worthy cause, no matter how seemingly small or unglamorous.
Let the praise of others remind you of your worth when you feel overlooked or undervalued.

title: Budayl b. Warqa' and Khuza'a Advise Quraysh on the Prophet's Intentions
summary: Budayl b. Warqa' and men of Khuza'a met the Prophet at Hudaybiyya, heard his peaceful intentions, and returned to warn Quraysh against hasty hostility — but Quraysh dismissed them as cowards.
story:
Al-Zuhri stated in his hadith, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had rested, Budayl b. Warqa', along with some men of Khuza'a, came to talk with him and to ask him why he had come. He told them that he had not come intending to do battle, but to visit the ka'ba and to venerate its sanctity. He then said to them much the same as he had to Bishr b. Sufrān. The men then returned to Quraysh and told them that they were treating Muhammad too hastily, and that he had only come to visit the ka'ba. Quraysh responded by making accusations against the men and by calling them cowards, saying, 'Even if he has come not wanting to fight, he'll never enter it by compulsion! The Arabs will never say that of us!'"
Al-Zuhri stated, "Khuza'a, both their Muslims and their polytheists, were advisers to the Messenger of God (SAAS), hiding from him nothing that happened in Mecca."
lessons: Speaking truth to power is a moral duty even when the message is unwelcome and the messenger is insulted.
Those who carry a message of peace are often dismissed or mocked, but their role is still vital.
Tribal pride and fear of public perception can blind people to wisdom and prevent them from accepting the truth.
practical_advice: When you deliver honest advice and it is rejected, do not take it as a failure of your character — you have fulfilled your responsibility.
Do not let the fear of being called weak stop you from advocating for peace and reason in tense situations.
Reflect on whether your own decisions are driven by wisdom or by concern for how others will perceive you.

title: Mikraz b. Hafs Sent to the Prophet — Deemed Untrustworthy
summary: Quraysh sent Mikraz b. Hafs as an envoy to the Prophet, who upon seeing him declared him untrustworthy. The Prophet nonetheless responded to him as he had to earlier envoys.
story: "They then sent to him Mikraz b. Hafṣ b. al-Akhyaf, a brother of Banū 'Āmir b. Lu'ayy. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw him approaching, he said, 'This man is untrustworthy!' When Mikraz reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and addressed him, the latter replied to him much as he had to Budayl and his companions. Mikraz then returned to Quraysh and reported what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had told him."
lessons: The Prophet's ability to read character was a God-given insight, yet he still engaged with the envoy with dignity and consistency.
Maintaining consistent, principled communication regardless of the character of the person before you reflects true integrity.
Recognizing untrustworthiness in others does not justify abandoning one's own ethical conduct.
practical_advice: When dealing with someone you suspect is not acting in good faith, remain consistent in your message and behavior — do not lower yourself to their level.
Trust your instincts about people's character, but do not let those instincts cause you to act with hostility or rudeness.
Maintain your principles in every interaction, whether the other person deserves it or not — your conduct is a reflection of your values, not theirs.

title: Hulays b. 'Alqama Sees the Sacrificial Camels and Returns in Awe
summary: Hulays b. 'Alqama, leader of the Abyssinian fighters and an ally of Quraysh, was sent to assess the Prophet's camp. Upon seeing the sacrificial camels adorned and worn from long confinement, he returned without meeting the Prophet, deeply moved, and rebuked Quraysh for their arrogance.
story:
"They then dispatched Hulays b. 'Alqama, or Ibn Zabbān, who at that time was the leader of the Abyssinian fighters, he being a member of Banū al-Hārith b. 'Abd Manāt b. Kinnāna. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw him, he commented, 'This man is from people who are devout; drive out the animals for sacrifice in front of him so he will see them.' When he saw them going past from the valley side, wearing their neck decorations and having eaten their own hair from having been long confined, he went back to Quraysh without actually having gone to the Messenger of God (SAAS), since they were so impressed with what he had seen. He reported this to them (Quraysh). They told him, 'Just sit down! You're only a bedouin, without any sense!'"
Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Abd Allāh b. Abī Bakr related to me that al-Hulays was angry at this and retorted, 'Quraysh, it wasn't for this that we allied with you and made a pact with you! Shall someone who has come to venerate God's house be barred from it? By Him who holds the soul of al-Hulays, either you allow Muhammad to do what he has come for, or I'll take away the Abyssinians to the last man of them!' They responded, 'Keep silent and leave us alone until we get for ourselves what we want!'"
lessons: Visible signs of sincere devotion — the worn, adorned sacrificial animals — spoke more powerfully than any words or arguments.
A person of genuine piety and fairness, even from outside one's own community, can recognize truth when they see it.
Arrogance and dismissiveness toward sincere counsel can fracture alliances and isolate the arrogant party.
practical_advice: When you want to convey sincerity in a situation of conflict, let your actions and state speak for themselves rather than relying solely on words.
Respect the honest observations of those outside your circle — an outsider's perspective can often see what insiders cannot.
When your sincere advice is dismissed with condescension, do not abandon your moral position — stand firm as Hulays did, with principled conviction.

title: 'Urwa b. Mas'ud Observes the Companions' Devotion to the Prophet
summary: 'Urwa b. Mas'ud al-Thaqafi was sent by Quraysh to negotiate with the Prophet. He witnessed the extraordinary devotion of the Companions and returned to tell Quraysh he had never seen a king so loved by his people.
story:
Al-Zuhri stated in his hadith, "Then they (decided to send) to the Messenger of God (SAAS) 'Urwa b. Mas'ūd al-Thaqafī and he told them, 'Tribe of Quraysh, I saw the violence and bad language you directed at those who came back to you after you dispatched them to Muhammad. You well know that you are as my father and myself the son' ('Urwa was in fact the son of Subay'a, daughter of 'Abd al-Shams). 'Having heard what has occurred to you, I gathered some of my people who obey me and so have come to you to help you myself.'
"They replied, 'You speak the truth; you have our trust.'
"So he went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, 'Muhammad, so have you gathered together a whole mix of people, then brought them to your own home to destroy it with them? Quraysh have come forth with their milch-camels, accompanied by their young and dressed in tiger skins, having sworn to God that you will never enter contrary to their wish. By God, I'll wager these people will abandon you tomorrow!'
"Abū Bakr, the Trusting, God be pleased with him, was behind the Messenger of God (SAAS) and commented, 'Go suck al-Lāt's tits! Us abandon him, indeed!' 'Urwa then asked, 'So who is this, Muhammad?' He replied, 'This is Ibn Abī Quhāfa.' 'Umar then said, 'If I wasn't obligated to you, I'd punish you for that. But now we are even.'
"'Urwa then began fondling the beard of the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he was speaking to him. Al-Mughīra b. Shu'ba, who was standing close by, dressed in armour, began tapping at 'Urwa's hand as it touched the beard of the Messenger of God (SAAS), saying, 'Remove your hand from the face of the Messenger of God, or you'll never get it back!' 'Urwa commented, 'Damn you, how churlish and rude you are!'
"The Messenger of God (SAAS) smiled at this, so 'Urwa asked him, 'Who is this, then, Muhammad?' 'This', he replied, 'is your nephew al-Mughīra b. Shu'ba!' At this 'Urwa commented, 'You wretch, wasn't it only yesterday you learned how to wash your own private parts!'"
Al-Zuhri went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to 'Urwa approximately what he had to the others, telling him that he had not come seeking battle. 'Urwa then left, having observed how the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) treated him. When he washed, they rushed to the water he had used. If he spat, they hurried to retrieve it, and if a hair fell from his head, they would take it. Consequently, when 'Urwa returned to Quraysh, he told them, 'Men of Quraysh, I've been to Chosroe in his kingdom, Caesar in his and the Negus in his; but I swear, I never saw a king amidst his people like Muhammad amidst his Companions. I have seen people who would never abandon him for any reason. So draw your own conclusions.'"
lessons: True leadership is measured not by power or wealth but by the love and loyalty it inspires in those who follow freely.
Even an adversary, if honest and observant, can recognize genuine greatness and bear witness to it.
The Companions' devotion was itself a form of da'wa — their behavior was more persuasive than any argument.
practical_advice: Consider how the quality of your relationships reflects the quality of your character — those who love and respect you are a mirror of who you truly are.
When you find yourself skeptical of someone's claims, look at how the people closest to them treat them — devotion is hard to fake.
If someone who disagreed with you comes to acknowledge your integrity, receive that acknowledgment graciously without gloating — it is a sign of your own steadfastness.

title: Khirash b. Umayya Sent to Mecca — His Camel Hamstrung
summary: The Prophet sent Khirash b. Umayya al-Khuza'i to Quraysh as an envoy, but Quraysh hamstrung the Prophet's camel and attempted to kill Khirash, who was only saved by the intervention of the Abyssinian troops.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "A scholar related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) called for Khirāsh b. Umayya al-Khuza'ī and sent him to Quraysh in Mecca, mounting him on one of his camels called al-Tha'lab, to tell their leaders on his behalf why he had come. But they hamstrung the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and wanted to kill him. The Abyssinian troops, however, prevented this and they released him, so he made his way back to the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Quraysh's violation of the sanctity of an envoy exposed the depths of their hostility and their disregard for established codes of honor.
Divine protection operates through human means — the Abyssinian troops were an instrument of God's preservation of the envoy.
Even when goodwill is met with aggression, the one sent on a mission of peace is morally vindicated.
practical_advice: When your sincere outreach is met with hostility, remember that the failure belongs to those who responded with aggression, not to you.
Do not abandon efforts at communication and peace even after setbacks — the Prophet continued diplomatic efforts despite this provocation.
Recognize the people in your life who, like the Abyssinian troops, intervene to protect you from harm — express gratitude for them.

title: Quraysh's Raiding Party Captured and Released by the Prophet
summary: Quraysh sent forty to fifty men to encircle and capture the Muslims' camp. They were caught and brought before the Prophet, who forgave them and released them all.
story: Ibn Ishāq also stated, "A reliable source related to me, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbās, that Quraysh had sent 40 or 50 of their men with orders to encircle the camp of the Messenger of God (SAAS), to capture one of his Companions. But they were taken prisoner and brought to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He forgave them and released them. They had hurled stones and shot arrows at the camp of the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Forgiving an enemy who has shown active hostility is one of the highest expressions of moral strength and confidence.
The Prophet's clemency in the face of provocation was not weakness — it was a deliberate, powerful choice that demonstrated his character.
Responding to aggression with forgiveness can disarm an enemy more effectively than retaliation.
practical_advice: When someone who has wronged you falls into your power, consider the long-term impact of forgiveness versus punishment — clemency often transforms enemies into allies.
Practice releasing the anger you feel toward those who have attacked you, not for their sake, but for your own peace and clarity.
Remember that the most influential people in history are often remembered for their mercy, not their revenge.

title: 'Umar Declines to Go to Mecca and Recommends 'Uthman as Envoy
summary: 'Umar b. al-Khattab was asked by the Prophet to travel to Mecca to communicate his peaceful intentions to Quraysh, but 'Umar honestly explained his fear for his safety and recommended 'Uthman b. 'Affan instead.
story:
"He then summoned 'Umar b. al-Khaṭṭāb to send him to Mecca to inform the Quraysh leaders for him why he had come. 'Umar responded, 'Messenger of God, I fear for myself from Quraysh. There is no one in Mecca of Banū 'Adī who could protect me. Quraysh are well aware of my enmity towards them and of the roughness with which I have treated them. But I will suggest to you a man they respect more than myself, 'Uthmān b. 'Affān.'
"The Messenger of God (SAAS) then summoned 'Uthmān b. 'Affān and did send him to Abū Sufyān and the Quraysh leaders to tell them that he had not come to wage war, but to visit the ka'ba in veneration of its sanctity."
lessons: Self-knowledge and honesty about one's limitations is a form of wisdom, not cowardice — 'Umar's candor served the mission better than false bravado would have.
Recommending a better-suited person for a task when you recognize your own limitations is an act of genuine loyalty to a cause.
Leadership involves knowing when to step forward and when to step aside for the good of the mission.
practical_advice: Be honest with yourself and those who rely on you about what you can and cannot handle — overcommitting out of pride often harms the people you serve.
When you are not the right person for a task, proactively suggest someone who is — this shows maturity and commitment to outcomes over ego.
Acknowledge your fears without shame; naming them honestly is the first step to addressing them constructively.

title: 'Uthman b. 'Affan Sent to Quraysh — Detained and Rumored Killed
summary: 'Uthman b. 'Affan was sent as the Prophet's envoy to Mecca, was protected by Aban b. Sa'id, delivered the Prophet's message, refused to circumambulate the Ka'ba before the Prophet, and was then detained by Quraysh. A false rumor of his death reached the Muslims.
story:
"'Uthmān set out for Mecca, where he was met by Abān b. Sa'īd b. al-'Āṣ, either before or after he had entered the city. Abān mounted 'Uthmān before himself and gave him protection until he delivered the message of the Messenger of God (SAAS). 'Uthmān went on to meet Abū Sufyān and the other Quraysh leaders. He gave them the message entrusted to him by the Messenger of God (SAAS). When he had done so, they told 'Uthmān, 'If what you want is to circumambulate the ka'ba, then do so.' He replied, 'I could never circumambulate it before the Messenger of God (SAAS) did so.'
"Quraysh then kept 'Uthmān as their prisoner. The Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims were informed that 'Uthmān had been killed. 'Abd Allāh b. Abū Bakr related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, when he was informed that 'Uthmān had been killed, 'We'll not leave before engaging the enemy in battle!'"
lessons: 'Uthman's refusal to circumambulate the Ka'ba without the Prophet was a profound expression of loyalty — he would not accept a privilege denied to the one he served.
False information can escalate a crisis dramatically; the rumor of 'Uthman's death nearly led to open warfare.
Steadfastness in fulfilling a trust, even under detention and pressure, is a mark of the highest character.
practical_advice: Loyalty sometimes means declining a personal benefit that others offer you when it would not be right to accept it without those you serve alongside.
Be cautious about acting on unverified information, especially in high-stakes situations — verify before escalating.
When you are detained or constrained unjustly, focus on maintaining your dignity and the integrity of the message you carry.

title: The Pledge of Ridwan Beneath the Tree at Hudaybiyya
summary: Upon hearing the false news of 'Uthman's death, the Prophet called upon his Companions to pledge allegiance beneath a tree at Hudaybiyya — the famous Bay'at al-Ridwan — with all but one man participating.
story:
Ibn Ishāq went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then called upon the men to pledge allegiance to him. This pledge, the bay'at al-riḍwān, the 'willing pledge', was made beneath a tree. People would say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) received a pledge of allegiance unto death. Jābir b. 'Abd Allāh, however, used to say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not have us pledge allegiance unto death, but had us swear that we would not flee.'
"The Messenger of God (SAAS) received their pledge and the only Muslim present who abstained from it was al-Jadd b. Qays, brother of Banū Salama. Jābir b. 'Abd Allāh used to say, 'I swear, I can almost see him now, standing glued to the side of his camel, trying to hide from everyone.'
"Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) received the news that the report about 'Uthmān was false."
Ibn Hishām stated, "Wākī' recounted, from Ismā'īl b. Abī Khālid, from al-Sha'bī, that the first man to make the bay'at al-riḍwān to the Messenger of God (SAAS) was Abū Sinān al-Asadī.
"And a reliable source related to me a tradition from Ibn Abī Mulayka, from Ibn 'Umar, that the Messenger of God himself (SAAS) made the pledge for 'Uthmān, clapping one of his hands to the other." This tradition mentioned by Ibn Hishām with this line of authority is weak, however, it is confirmed in both ṣaḥīḥ collections.
lessons: The Bay'at al-Ridwan demonstrates that in moments of true crisis, genuine faith transforms fear into committed resolve.
The Prophet's act of pledging on behalf of the absent 'Uthman shows that loyalty to a faithful servant is reciprocal.
The one who hid from the pledge stands as a reminder that even in great communities, individual moral courage can waver.
practical_advice: In moments when your community or cause faces a serious threat, ask yourself whether your commitment is conditional — true loyalty does not depend on favorable circumstances.
When someone you care about cannot be present for a significant moment, find a way to honor them and include them symbolically, as the Prophet did for 'Uthman.
Do not hide from commitments out of self-preservation when those around you are standing firm — the regret of that cowardice outlasts the fear that caused it.

title: Suhayl b. 'Amr Sent to Negotiate Peace at Hudaybiyya
summary: Quraysh sent Suhayl b. 'Amr to negotiate a peace agreement with the Prophet. Upon seeing him approach, the Prophet declared that Quraysh intended peace. Negotiations began and a document was to be drawn up.
story:
Ibn Ishaq went on, "Al-Zuhri stated, 'Quraysh then sent Suhayl b. 'Amr, brother of Banū 'Āmir b. Lu'ayy, to the Messenger of God (SAAS), telling him, "Go to Muhammad and make peace with him. The only condition for peace with him is that he withdraw from us this year. By God, the Arabs shall never say that he entered it by compulsion!"'
"Suhayl b. 'Amr went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who said, seeing him approach, 'The enemy wanted peace when they dispatched this man.'
"When Suhayl reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he spoke with him at length; they reconciled and made peace. The matter having been concluded, with nothing but a document remaining to be drawn up, 'Umar jumped up and went over to Abū Bakr. He asked him..."
lessons: The Prophet's ability to read the intent of an approaching envoy — and name it as a desire for peace — reflects perceptiveness and an orientation toward reconciliation.
Even enemies who have shown aggression are capable of choosing peace when circumstances demand it; remain open to that possibility.
The willingness to negotiate and put agreements in writing reflects seriousness of purpose and a desire for lasting resolution rather than temporary advantage.
practical_advice: When someone who has been hostile toward you reaches out to talk, be open to the possibility that they genuinely seek resolution — look for signs of sincerity rather than assuming continued bad faith.
In any serious negotiation or reconciliation, formalize the agreement clearly — a documented understanding protects all parties and prevents future disputes.
Learn to read the situation around you with clarity — the Prophet named the dynamic the moment he saw Suhayl, which gave him a composed and confident footing entering the conversation.

title: Umar's Anguish and Abu Bakr's Counsel at Hudaybiyyah
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab, distressed by the terms of the Hudaybiyyah peace treaty, confronts Abu Bakr questioning why Muslims should agree to something that demeans their religion, and Abu Bakr counsels him to obey the Prophet.
story: The matter having been concluded, with nothing but a document remaining to be drawn up, Umar jumped up and went over to Abu Bakr. He asked him, 'Abu Bakr, is he not God's messenger?' 'Indeed yes,' he replied. 'And are we not Muslims?' he asked. 'Indeed yes,' Abu Bakr replied. 'And are they not polytheists?' 'Indeed yes,' he agreed. 'So why should we agree to something that demeans our religion?' Umar asked. Abu Bakr replied, 'Obey whatever he says; for I testify that he is the Messenger of God.' Umar added, 'I too testify that he is the Messenger of God.' He (Umar) then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked him, 'Are you not the Messenger of God?' 'Yes, indeed.' 'And are we not Muslims?' 'Indeed yes.' 'And are they not polytheists?' 'Indeed yes.' 'So why then should we demean our religion?' 'I', he replied, 'am the servant of God and His messenger. I shall not oppose His order and He will not let me fail.' Umar, God be pleased with him, used to say, 'I have gone on fasting, giving alms, praying and freeing slaves because of what I did that day and out of fear for what I said that day, until I felt that I had made up.'
lessons: Even the most devoted believers can experience moments of doubt and frustration when divine wisdom is not immediately apparent.
True faith sometimes means trusting in God's plan even when circumstances seem to go against what we believe is right.
Sincere repentance and increased worship are meaningful ways to address feelings of guilt over past reactions or mistakes.
practical_advice: When you feel angry or confused about a situation beyond your control, seek counsel from a wise and trusted person before acting on your emotions.
If you have reacted poorly in a moment of stress, channel that regret into positive actions such as increased prayer, charity, or service rather than dwelling in guilt.
Practice reminding yourself that those in positions of spiritual or moral authority may have insight into a situation that you do not yet possess.

title: The Writing of the Hudaybiyyah Treaty Document
summary: The Prophet Muhammad dictates the terms of the Hudaybiyyah peace treaty to Ali ibn Abi Talib, but Suhayl ibn Amr objects to both the opening phrase and the Prophet's title, forcing compromise in the wording.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then summoned 'Ali b. Abi Talib, God be pleased with him, and told him, 'Write: "In the name of God the Most Merciful, the Most Beneficent".' Suhayl objected, 'I don't know that (phrase).' 'Write instead, "In Your name, O God."' And 'Ali wrote it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Write down: "This is the peace agreement made by Muhammad, the Messenger of God, and Suhayl b. 'Amr."' Suhayl then said, 'If I were to testify that you are the Messenger of God, I would not oppose you. Write down your name and that of your father.' So the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Write down, "This is the peace agreement made by Muhammad, son of 'Abd Allah, with Suhayl b. 'Amr. They have made peace by agreeing to put aside warfare from the people for ten years. During this period, people will be safe and leave one another alone. Provided that if anyone from Quraysh comes to Muhammad without permission of his guardian, then he must send him back to them. If, however, anyone goes to Quraysh from Muhammad, they are not to send him back to him. There are to be no secret agreements, bad faith or antagonism between us. Anyone wishing to enter into a pact or agreement with Muhammad may do so. Anyone wishing to enter into an agreement or pact with Quraysh may do so."' At this Khuza'a promptly announced, 'We have made a pact and agreement with Muhammad!' And Banu Bakr responded at once, 'We have made a pact and agreement with Quraysh!' The agreement went on, 'You shall retire from us this year and not enter Mecca against our will. Next year we will make way for you, and you and your people may enter and stay for three nights, each rider bearing his own weapons - swords in sheaths, that is, and bringing in no other weapons.'
lessons: Achieving peace sometimes requires accepting compromises in form and title without compromising the substance of one's mission or beliefs.
A leader's willingness to absorb personal indignity for the greater benefit of their community is a mark of profound wisdom and humility.
Formal agreements and clear terms, even difficult ones, lay the foundation for long-term stability and trust between parties.
practical_advice: In negotiations or conflicts, identify which points are essential to your core values and which are matters of form that can be conceded without real loss.
Practice patience when others challenge your identity or credentials; your character and actions will ultimately speak louder than any title.
When facing seemingly unfair terms in a compromise, focus on the long-term outcomes rather than the short-term sense of diminishment.

title: Abu Jandal's Desperate Plea and the Muslims' Deep Sorrow
summary: Abu Jandal, a Muslim prisoner of Quraysh, arrives in chains during the signing of the Hudaybiyyah treaty, screaming for help, but the Prophet is compelled by the treaty terms to return him, deepening the Muslims' anguish.
story: While the Messenger of God (SAAS) was writing the document, up came Abu Jandal, Suhayl's son, dragging fetters, having escaped to the Messenger of God (SAAS). When those with the Messenger of God (SAAS) had gone forth, they had not doubted that there would be victory, because of visions he had seen. Witnessing now the peace being made and their withdrawal being arranged and what the Messenger of God (SAAS) was agreeing to on his own behalf, they were all very disappointed and deathly depressed. When Suhayl saw (his son) Abu Jandal, he got up, went to him and struck him in the face, grabbing him by the collar and saying, 'The matter was agreed to between us before this fellow came to you!' 'You are right,' he agreed. And so Suhayl began dragging his son away to return him to Quraysh custody, while Abu Jandal screamed at the top of his voice, 'Muslims, shall I be returned to the polytheists who will subvert my faith?' This increased their sense of depression. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Be patient and control yourself, Abu Jandal! God is preparing relief and succour for you and the others with you who are powerless. We have made a peace pact with the enemy and we and they have sworn to this in God's name; we cannot act falsely with them.' 'Umar b. al-Khattab jumped up and walked along beside Abu Jandal, saying, 'Be patient! They're only polytheists, their blood worth no more than that of dogs!' He was keeping the hilt of his sword close to Abu Jandal. Umar used to say, 'I was hoping he would take the sword and strike his father with it! But the man spared his father, and the matter was settled.'
lessons: There are times when honoring one's word and commitments takes precedence even over the impulse to relieve immediate suffering, and this requires profound trust in God.
God's relief for those who are oppressed may not come in the moment we expect it, but the promise of that relief is certain.
Witnessing injustice without being able to act is one of the most painful experiences a believer can endure, and this pain itself is a form of worship when borne with patience.
practical_advice: When you are powerless to change a painful situation immediately, remind yourself that relief can come through paths you have not yet imagined, and hold on.
If you feel overwhelmed by a situation that seems deeply unjust, articulate your distress to a trusted person and allow them to counsel you toward patience rather than impulsive action.
In moments of collective grief or disappointment, support those around you by staying calm and grounded, as the Prophet modeled, rather than amplifying the despair.

title: The Sacrifice, Head-Shaving, and the Virtue of Those Who Did Not Doubt
summary: After completing the treaty, the Prophet sacrifices his animals and shaves his head, prompting the Muslims to follow; he then invokes special mercy for those who shaved, explaining their action reflected unshaken certainty.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) had set up his tent in unhallowed territory and he would say his prayers on sacred ground. When he had concluded the peace, he set about sacrificing his animals. He then sat and had his head shaved; the man who shaved it that day was Khirash b. Umayya b. al-Fadl al-Khuza'i. When the Muslims saw that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had made sacrifice and shaved, they hurried to do the same. 'Abd Allah b. Abi Najih related to me, from Mujahid, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'Some men shaved their heads at al-Hudaybiyya, while others cut their hair short. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, "May God have mercy on those who shaved!" They asked, "And on those who cut it short, Messenger of God?" "May God have mercy on those who shaved," he repeated. "And on those who cut it short, Messenger of God?" they again asked. He said, "And on those who cut it short." They then asked him, "Messenger of God, why did you emphasize God's mercy for those who shaved and not for those who cut their hair short?" He replied, "They did not doubt."' 'Abd Allah b. Abi Najih stated, 'Mujahid related to me, from Ibn 'Abbas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sacrificed one camel among the rest that had belonged to Abu Jahl and had a silver ring on its head; he did this to anger the polytheists.'
lessons: Complete certainty in God's promise, expressed through wholehearted action, earns a spiritual station that cautious half-measures do not.
Following the example of righteous leadership, even when circumstances are painful and confusing, is itself an act of faith.
Small distinctions in how we perform acts of worship can reflect deep differences in the level of our trust and certainty in God.
practical_advice: When you commit to a decision or a spiritual practice, do so fully and without holding back out of doubt, trusting that God honors wholehearted dedication.
Look to the actions of those you trust and respect as a guide when you are uncertain what to do in a difficult moment.
Reflect on whether hesitation in your devotional life or daily commitments comes from genuine prudence or from a lack of trust in God's support.

title: God's Word on Rain and Belief: The Morning After the Storm at Hudaybiyyah
summary: After rain fell on the night at Hudaybiyyah, the Prophet relayed a divine message distinguishing those who attributed the rain to God's mercy from those who attributed it to the stars.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, 'Khalid b. Makhlad related to us, quoting Sulayman b. Bilal, who quoted Salih b. Kaysan, from Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah, from Zayd b. Khalid, who said, "We went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS) the year of al-Hudaybiyya, and at night it rained hard. The Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed the al-subh prayer with us. Later he turned to us and said, 'Do you know what your Lord said?' We replied, 'God and His messenger know best!' He said, 'God Almighty said, "Of my servants, some have awakened to belief in me, others to disbelief. Those who said, 'Our rain comes by God's mercy and bounty' are believers in me and disbelievers in the stars. Those who said, 'Our rain comes by this or that star' is a believer in the stars and a disbeliever in me."""' Al-Bukhari related this in several places in his Sahih collection. Muslim gave the tradition in various lines back to al-Zuhri. From al-Zuhri the line of transmission went back to Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah from Abu Hurayra.
lessons: The way we interpret the blessings in our lives reveals the true orientation of our hearts, whether toward God or toward secondary causes.
Gratitude and correct attribution of blessings to God is itself a profound act of faith.
Every moment of natural beauty or good fortune is an opportunity to strengthen one's relationship with God through acknowledgment and thanks.
practical_advice: Practice consciously attributing good things that happen in your life to God's mercy and generosity, even when there is an obvious worldly explanation.
When you receive an unexpected blessing, pause and say a word of gratitude to God before moving on, as this builds a habit of God-consciousness.
Reflect regularly on whether you tend to see goodness in your life as earned, coincidental, or as a gift from God, and work to shift your perspective toward the latter.

title: The Willing Pledge at Hudaybiyyah and the Miracle of the Well
summary: The 1,400 companions at Hudaybiyyah gave a pledge of allegiance under the tree, and when they drained the well dry from thirst, the Prophet's prayer and touch caused the water to flow abundantly for all.
story: Al-Bukhari also stated, 'Ubayd Allah b. Musa related to us, from Isra'il, from Abu Ishaq, from al-Bara', who said, "You use the word al-fath, 'the victory', to apply to the fath of Mecca. That was certainly a victory. We consider al-fath (also) to have been the bay'at al-ridwan, the 'willing pledge', that occurred at al-Hudaybiyya. There were 1,400 of us with the Prophet (SAAS). At al-Hudaybiyya there is a well; we went down to it and drained it to the last drop. When the Prophet (SAAS) heard of this, he came there and sat on its edge and called for a vessel of water, with which he performed the ablution. Then he rinsed out his mouth, prayed, and poured it in the well. We then went a little way off, and later it produced for us (the water that) we and our mounts needed."' Al-Bukhari also stated that Yusuf b. 'Isa related to him, quoting Ibn Fudayl, quoting Husayn from Salim, from Jabir, who said, 'At al-Hudaybiyya the men were thirsty while the Messenger of God (SAAS) had before him a waterskin he would use for ablutions. Eventually the men went over to him and he asked what they wanted. They told him that they had no water for ablutions or for drinking and that all that remained was in his waterskin. The Prophet (SAAS) then placed his hand in the waterskin and the water began spurting out from between his fingers as if it were a spring.' Jabir continued, 'So we drank and performed the ablutions.' We asked Jabir, 'How many were you there that day?' He replied, 'Even if we had been 100,000, there would have been enough for us. We were 1,500 men.'
lessons: Divine provision can appear in the most desperate and seemingly hopeless circumstances, affirming that trust in God is never misplaced.
The bay'at al-ridwan demonstrates that a pledge of loyalty made sincerely in a moment of difficulty carries immense spiritual weight.
Miracles of provision remind us that our limitations are never the final word when God's grace is at work.
practical_advice: When you feel you have exhausted all your resources and see no way forward, turn to prayer before concluding that there is no solution.
In moments of communal stress or shortage, look to trustworthy leadership and collective supplication rather than panic or conflict.
Reflect on past moments in your life when relief came unexpectedly at a point of real desperation, and let those memories strengthen your trust in God during current hardships.

title: Al-Zuhri's Testimony: The Hudaybiyyah Peace as the Greatest Victory in Islam
summary: Al-Zuhri explains that despite appearances, the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah was the greatest victory in Islam's history, as it ended warfare, opened dialogue, and led to a doubling of Muslim numbers within two years.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, regarding the words of God Almighty, 'but He made apart from that a near victory' (Surat al-Fath; XLVIII, v.27), (referring to) 'the Hudaybiyya peace'. Al-Zuhri stated, 'No victory occurred previously in Islam than was greater than it. (Previously) wherever men had met, there had been fighting; when the truce was made, warfare came to an end and all made peace with one another. When they met they would negotiate with one another when they talked. And no one of any intelligence had Islam explained to him without accepting it. In those two years the same number of men entered as before, or even more.' Ibn Hisham stated, 'The proof of what al-Zuhri said is in the fact that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth to al-Hudaybiyya with 1,400 men, as Jabir states, and then two years later attended the conquest of Mecca with 10,000.'
lessons: What appears to be defeat or failure in the moment can contain within it the seeds of the greatest victories, visible only in hindsight.
Peace and open dialogue, even with adversaries, can accomplish what conflict never could, including the spread of truth.
God's plan operates on a timeline and through methods that are often incomprehensible to us in the moment of disappointment.
practical_advice: When a situation does not unfold as you hoped or planned, resist the urge to judge the outcome immediately; give time for the fuller picture to emerge.
Look for ways to open respectful dialogue even with those who oppose you, as honest conversation can achieve what confrontation cannot.
Keep a record or memory of moments when something that initially seemed like a setback turned out to be a turning point for the better, and revisit it when you feel discouraged.

title: The Miracle of Water Flowing from the Prophet's Fingers at al-Hudaybiyya
summary: During the expedition to al-Hudaybiyya, when water was scarce, the Prophet Muhammad placed his hand in a waterskin and water began flowing from between his fingers like a spring, providing enough water for the 1,500 companions to drink and perform ablutions.
story: that remained was in his waterskin. The Prophet (SAAS) then placed his hand in the waterskin and the water began spurting out from between his fingers as if it were a spring." Jahir continued, "So we drank and performed the ablutions." We asked Jiihir, "How many were you there that day?" He replied, "Even if we had been 100,000, there would have been enough for us. We were 1,500 men."
lessons: Miracles occur through divine will at moments of genuine need, reminding believers that scarcity is never a barrier when God wills abundance.
The accounts of eyewitnesses like Jabir preserve the memory of divine grace across generations, teaching us that gratitude must be spoken and shared.
Even when resources appear exhausted, trust in God's provision sustains the believer beyond what is materially visible.
practical_advice: When you feel you have reached the end of your resources — whether emotional, financial, or physical — pause and ask God for help before concluding that nothing remains.
Practice gratitude by narrating moments of unexpected relief to those around you, as Jabir did, reinforcing communal trust in divine provision.
Keep a record of times when what seemed insufficient turned out to be more than enough; revisiting these memories builds resilience in future hardships.

title: The Pledge of al-Hudaybiyya Beneath the Tree
summary: At al-Hudaybiyya, the Prophet's companions gathered beneath a tree and made a solemn pledge of allegiance to him, an act of extraordinary loyalty in the face of potential conflict with Quraysh, later confirmed in the Quran as the 'Pledge of Good Pleasure.'
story: Al-Bukhm stated that 'Ali b. 'Abd All& related to him, quoting Sufyw, that CAmr said, "I heard Jiihir say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to us at al-Hudayhiyya, "You are the best people on earth!" We were 1,400; and were I able today to see, I would show you the place where the nee stood."' Al-Layth b. Sa'd related it similarly, from Ahii al-Zuhayr, from Jabir, who said, "A slave belonging to Hatih came to make a complaint about him. He said, 'Messenger of God, Hatih will surely enter hell-fire!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'You have lied; he will not enter hell; he was present at Badr and al-Hudaybiyya.'" Muslim related this. Muslim also gave, from several lines of transmission, the following from Ihn Jurayj, "Aha al-Zubayr narrated to me that he heard Jahir say, 'Umm Maysar told me that she heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, when he was with Hafsa, "None, if God wills it, will enter hell of those who made the pledge beneath the tree." Hafsa commented, "On the contrary, Messenger of God." He berated her and she then quoted, "All of you are headed there" (smut Maryum; XIX, v.71). The Messenger of God (SAAS) then responded, "Almighty God stated, '... and then We will deliver those who are pious, and We will leave there the evil-doers, prostrate'"'" (szlrat Mayam; XIX, v.72).
lessons: Commitment to God and His messenger in moments of fear and uncertainty carries a weight that transcends the moment itself, with lasting spiritual consequences.
The Prophet's correction of Hafsa's misreading of the Quran teaches that sacred texts require careful, contextual understanding rather than selective citation.
Being present and loyal at critical moments of communal trial — like those who pledged at al-Hudaybiyya — marks a person's character in a way that endures long after the moment passes.
practical_advice: When facing a moment that demands courage and commitment, remind yourself that your choices in difficult times define your character more than your choices in easy times.
When a verse or teaching seems to contradict a hope you hold, seek the full context before concluding it is hopeless; the Quran repeatedly pairs difficulty with relief.
Surround yourself with people who, like those at al-Hudaybiyya, show up when the stakes are high; their loyalty is a sign of genuine connection worth preserving.

title: The Forgetting of the Tree of the Pledge
summary: A year after the Pledge of al-Hudaybiyya, the companions of the Prophet returned to find they could no longer identify the tree beneath which the momentous pledge had been made, a detail that later prompted a pointed remark from Sa'id ibn al-Musayyab about the difference between the companions and later generations.
story: Al-Bukhm stated that Muhammad b. bfi' related to him, quoting Shahaba b. S u w a &Fami, quoting Shu'ba, from Qatada, from Sa'id h. al-Musayyab, from his father, who said, "I saw the tree; then I came to it later hut did not recognize it." (Al-Bukhai went on) "Miisa related to us, quoting Abii 'Awana, quoting Tariq, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyah, from his father, that he was among those who made the pledge beneath the tree. So we returned to it the following year; but it was obscure to us." Al-Bukhari also stated that Mahmiid related to him, quoting Whayd Allah, from IsrPil, from Tariq b. 'Ahd al-Ram%, who said, "I set off as a pilgrim, and passed by some people praying. 'What masjid, place for prayer, is this then?' They replied, 'This is the tree where the Messenger of God (SAAS) called for the baycat al-qiw&z, the "willing pledge".' I then went over to Sacid b. al-Musayyab and related this to him. Sacid then said, 'My father related to me that he was among those who made the pledge to the Messenger of God (SAAS) beneath the tree. He told me, "The following year we had forgotten it and could not fmd it."' Sacid then said, 'The Companions of Muhammad did not know it hut you people do! You know best, then!'"
lessons: The companions of the Prophet were not attached to sites and symbols; their devotion was to the living faith, not to physical monuments, offering a lesson against idolizing places over principles.
Later generations' eagerness to venerate locations the companions themselves forgot reflects how distance from an event can paradoxically produce stronger sentimentality than genuine experience.
What matters most about a transformative moment is not the place where it occurred but the change it produced in the heart.
practical_advice: Be cautious about over-attaching your spiritual identity to physical places, rituals, or symbols at the expense of the inner transformation they are meant to produce.
When you find yourself romanticizing a past moment, ask honestly whether your relationship with its meaning has deepened since, or whether you are substituting nostalgia for genuine growth.
If a later generation seems to know more about a tradition than its original participants, consider whether familiarity with the letter of a tradition has replaced living by its spirit.

title: Ibn Zayd Refuses to Pledge Allegiance Unto Death to Anyone After the Prophet
summary: At the Battle of al-Harra, when people were pledging allegiance unto death to Ibn Hawala, a companion who had been present at al-Hudaybiyya refused to make such a pledge to anyone after the Messenger of God.
story: Al-Bukhm stated that Sacid related to him, quoting his brother, from Sulayma, from 'Amr b. Y&y%,from 'Abhd b. Tamim, who said, "At the battle of al-Harra, people were making a pledge to 'Abd AUnh b. Haqala. Ibn Zayd asked, 'What is the pledge the people are making to Ibn Hawala?' He was told, 'To the death'. He then said, 'I will not pledge that to anyone following the Messenger of God (SAAS).' He had been present at al-Hudaybiyya with him."
lessons: Witnessing a formative moment of devotion to a true leader shapes a person's standards permanently; those who experienced the pledge at al-Hudaybiyya could not lower their bar of allegiance for anyone lesser.
Moral clarity sometimes means standing apart from the crowd, especially when collective emotion drives people toward oaths or commitments that exceed what is spiritually appropriate.
The depth of one's attachment to the Prophet Muhammad is measured not only in love but in the careful preservation of what was uniquely his.
practical_advice: Before making a deep commitment or oath, ask yourself whether it is proportionate and appropriate to the person or cause you are committing to.
When group pressure pushes you toward a decision that feels wrong in your heart, draw on a formative moment of clarity from your own past to ground your refusal.
Reflect on the people and moments in your life that have set your highest standards; returning to those touchstones can protect you from making promises you will later regret.

title: Salama ibn al-Akwa' Pledges Allegiance Unto Death at al-Hudaybiyya
summary: When asked what pledge he had made to the Messenger of God at al-Hudaybiyya, the companion Salama ibn al-Akwa' answered simply: 'To the death.'
story: Al-Bukhwi stated that Qutayba b. Sacid related to him, quoting Hatim, from Yazid b. Aba Wbayd (who said), "I said to Salama h. al-Akwac, 'What pledge was it you made to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at al-Hudaybiyya?' 'To the death,' he replied." Muslim related this from a hadith of Yazid b. Abn Wbayd. In the ~ahihcollection of Muslim, it states that he sought the pledge three times - from a first group, from a middle group and from a final group. In the phih there is a hadith from Ma'qil b. Y a w that he held the tree branches away from the face of the Messenger of God (SAAS) while he was receiving their pledge. (It also states there) that the fust man to give his pledge that day was Aba Sinm, he being Wahh h. Mihsan, brother of W b h a b. Mihsan.
lessons: Pledging unto death is the ultimate expression of commitment; the companions understood that their devotion to truth could not be partial or conditional.
The repetition of the pledge — taken from three successive groups — shows the Prophet's care for ensuring that each person's commitment was freely, consciously, and fully given.
Simple, unelaborated answers like 'To the death' carry more weight than lengthy explanations; integrity speaks with economy.
practical_advice: Examine the commitments you have made in your life and ask whether you have honoured them with the same clarity and completeness with which they were given.
When you make a promise, mean it fully; partial commitment to important relationships and responsibilities causes more harm than an honest acknowledgment of limits.
Practice speaking plainly about what you stand for; clarity about your values protects you from being slowly talked away from them.

title: Abdullah ibn Umar Pledges Before His Father Umar at al-Hudaybiyya
summary: At al-Hudaybiyya, Abdullah ibn Umar independently pledged allegiance to the Prophet before his father Umar arrived, leading later people to mistakenly believe the son had accepted Islam before the father.
story: Al-Bukh~ri stated that it was related to h i by Shujsc h. al-Walid that he heard al-Nadr h. Muhammad (say), that Sakhr b. al-Rabi' quoted Nnfi' as having said, 'People relate that Wmar's son accepted Islam before Wmar, yet it was not so. The day of al-Hudayhiyya Wmar sent (his son) CAhd All& to bring him a mare of his that was with one of the aezr; this was so that he wuld ride it to battle. At the time, the Messenger of God (SAAS) was receiving the pledge beneath the tree, though Wmar did not know this. <Abd Allah pledged his allegiance, then left. Wmar then went with him and made his pledge to the Messenger of God (SAAS). This is why people say that Wmar's son accepted Islam before Wmar did.' "Hish&n b. 'Ammar stated, 'Al-Walid b. Muslim related to us, quoting Wmar b. Muhammad al-Wmari, (who said), "Nafic narrated to me, from Ibn Wrnar, that the people who were with the Prophet (SAAS) at al-Hudaybiyya were dispersed in the shade of the tree. Then they all began looking directly at the Prophet (SAAS). He ('Umar) then said (to his son), 'Abd Allxh, go and find out why everyone is looking directly at the Messenger of God (SAAS).' H e ('Abd Allah) found them to be making their pledge, and he did so himself. H e then returned to Wmar, who went and pledged allegiance."'"
lessons: Rumors and misrememberings about great people often arise from incomplete information; the full story usually reveals a more nuanced and honourable truth.
A son's independent act of conscience — pledging before being instructed to — reflects the quality of the character instilled in him by his upbringing, not a competition with his father.
Good leadership, like Umar's, involves promptly joining a noble cause once you become aware of it, without ego or delay.
practical_advice: Before accepting a story that diminishes someone's honour, seek out the full account; partial information often produces unfair judgments.
When someone you respect acts independently in a way that surprises you, consider that their action may reflect the values you taught them rather than a challenge to your authority.
If you discover that a community effort of great importance is underway and you were simply unaware of it, join it wholeheartedly rather than being embarrassed by your late arrival.

title: The Prophet's Departure for al-Hudaybiyya as a Pilgrim, Not a Warrior
summary: The Prophet departed for al-Hudaybiyya with his companions in a state of ritual pilgrimage, decorating sacrificial camels and sending scouts ahead, until word came that Quraysh had mobilised forces to prevent them from reaching the Ka'ba.
story: Al-Bukhm stated in his military chronicle as follows, "'Abd A k h b. Muhammad related to us, quoting Sufyan, who said, 'I heard al-Zuhri when he related this hadith. I learned part of it by heart and Ma'mar corroborated me. (The hadith ~ quotes) from Wrwa b. d-Zubayr, from al-Miswar b. Makhrama and M a r w b. al-Hakam, each of these adding to the account of the other. They both stated, "The Prophet (SAAS) went forth to al-Hudayhiyya accompanied by some 1,000 men. When he arrived at Dha al-Hulayfa, he put neck decorations and badges on the sacrificial camels, and went out from there as a pilgrim, sending on ahead a scout of his from the Khuzaca. "'"The Prophet (SAAS) travelled on till he arrived at the stream at al-Ashpt, where his scout came to him and said, 'Quraysh have gathered a force against you. They have assembled the Abyssinian warriors to fight you. They intend to do battle with you and to keep you away from the kacba.' He then asked, 'Men, give me your advice. Do you think that I should give way for the children and families of those who wish to keep us from the kacba?(Better) if they do come and join us, (so) God will have cut out one eye of the polytheists; alternatively, we will leave them defeated.' Abii Bakr replied, 'Messenger of God, you have come forth to visit the ka'ba, not intending to do battle or to make war against anyone. Proceed to it, and we will fight anyone who blocks our way.' He responded, 'Then proceed onwards, in God's name.'"'"
lessons: Peaceful intention does not guarantee peaceful reception; the Prophet's pilgrimage was entirely non-aggressive, yet Quraysh mobilised against it, teaching that righteous purpose must sometimes be maintained in the face of unjust opposition.
Abu Bakr's counsel — to stay true to the original intention rather than escalate — reflects the wisdom of not allowing the enemy's hostility to redefine your mission.
Consulting companions in moments of crisis is not weakness but a prophetic practice that honours the intelligence and loyalty of those who travel with you.
practical_advice: When you are blocked from a legitimate and peaceful goal by another's hostility, resist the temptation to reframe your mission as one of conflict; stay anchored to your original, righteous intention.
Seek counsel from those closest to you when facing unexpected opposition; their perspective often clarifies what your anxiety has obscured.
Proceed in God's name — with prayer, intention, and trust — even when the path forward involves risk; the alternative of turning back from a righteous goal carries its own cost.

title: The Prophet's Camel Kneels at al-Hudaybiyya
summary: As the Prophet and his companions approached Mecca for pilgrimage, his camel al-Qaswa' kneeled and refused to move. The Prophet declared this was a divine sign, just as the elephant had been held back from Mecca, and pledged to accept any honorable terms from Quraysh.
story: The Prophet (SAAS) proceeded until he reached the pass from which the way led down to them. There his camel kneeled down; people said, 'Hal! Hal! Get up! Get up!' but it persisted (in kneeling). People said, 'Al-Qaswa' (the camel's name) has become refractory!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded to them, 'No, al-Qaswa' has not; that's not in her nature. She has been held back by Him who held back the elephant.' He then said, 'By Him who holds my soul in His hand, I'll accept any request that they make of me by which the sanctuaries of God are dignified!' He then scolded his camel and it got up.
lessons: Divine intervention can manifest through unexpected circumstances, redirecting us toward paths of peace rather than conflict.
True strength lies in willingness to pursue honorable peace even when you have the power to fight.
Recognizing signs of God's will requires spiritual awareness and the humility to pause rather than force one's way forward.
practical_advice: When plans are suddenly disrupted, pause before reacting — consider whether this unexpected halt may be redirecting you toward something better.
In conflicts, ask yourself: 'Can I accept terms that preserve dignity for all parties?' Peace-seeking is not weakness.
Practice identifying moments in your life where an obstacle later revealed itself as divine guidance, and journal about them to build trust in God's plan.

title: The Miraculous Well at al-Hudaybiyya
summary: When the Muslim camp exhausted the water supply of a small well at al-Hudaybiyya and faced intense thirst, the Prophet placed an arrow in the dry well-hole and it miraculously produced abundant water until the entire army was satisfied.
story: He then veered away from Quraysh and halted beyond al-Hudaybiyya at a well there that had little water. His men kept taking the water from it, little by little, and soon they had bailed it out completely. Complaint of thirst was made to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he drew an arrow from his quiver and told them to put it into the water-hole. And, by God, it continued providing them plenty of water until they left.
lessons: In moments of collective crisis, turning to God rather than despair is the first and most powerful response.
God's provision can come through the simplest, most unexpected means — a single arrow placed with faith.
Leaders who remain composed and spiritually grounded during crises provide a stabilizing force for those around them.
practical_advice: When you feel a resource in your life has run dry — energy, hope, patience — make a sincere supplication before concluding it is gone.
Reflect on past moments when you were provided for in an unexpected way; let those memories strengthen your trust during current hardships.
In group stress, model calmness for others around you — your composure can be as nourishing as water to those who are anxious.

title: Budayl b. Warqa' Brings News of Quraysh's Resolve
summary: Budayl b. Warqa' of Khuzaa came to the Prophet at al-Hudaybiyya warning that Quraysh were encamped nearby with their families, ready to fight and prevent the Muslims from reaching the Ka'ba. The Prophet responded with a message of peace and an offer of negotiation.
story: While this was going on, Budayl b. Warqa' al-Khuza'i arrived, accompanied by others of Khuzaa. They were the trusted confidants of the Messenger of God (SAAS) from the people of Tihama. Budayl said, 'I have just left Ka'b b. Lu'ayy and 'Amir b. Lu'ayy who have encamped at the Hudaybiyya wells; they have with them their milch-camels with their young. They are going to do battle with you and prevent you from reaching the ka'ba.' The Prophet (SAAS) responded, 'We have not come to fight anyone. We have come to make a pilgrimage. Quraysh have been consumed by the warfare! It has ruined them. If they want, I will give them respite for a period. They should then give me access to the people. If it (Islam) prevails, and they wish to join into what the people have entered, they could do so. Otherwise, they will have gained in numbers. If they refuse this, then I swear by Him who holds my soul in His hand, I will battle them in this cause of mine until I perish! Let God's will be done!' Budayl said, 'I will inform them what you say.' He then went off to Quraysh and told them, 'We come to you from that man. We have heard him make certain statements which, if you wish, we will relate to you.' Fools among them said, 'We have no need for you to tell us anything of him.' Those of them who were sensible, said, 'Tell us what you heard him say.' He responded by relating to them what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said.
lessons: Clarity of intention — stating plainly that you come in peace, not for conflict — can defuse tension and open doors that aggression would close.
Wisdom within an opposing group can be allies; not everyone on the other side is an enemy of reason.
Standing firm in peaceful purpose even when surrounded by hostility requires deep conviction in the righteousness of one's cause.
practical_advice: When facing conflict, clearly and calmly state your true intention upfront — it disarms misunderstanding and forces the other party to engage with your actual position.
Seek out the reasonable voices in any opposition rather than engaging only with the most hostile ones.
When you feel caught between a desire for peace and a need to defend what matters to you, write out your bottom line clearly so you can articulate it under pressure.

title: Urwa b. Mas'ud Observes the Companions' Devotion to the Prophet
summary: Quraysh's envoy Urwa b. Mas'ud, after meeting with the Prophet at al-Hudaybiyya and witnessing the extraordinary reverence of the Companions for him, returned to Quraysh astonished, comparing what he had seen to the courts of the greatest kings on earth and urging acceptance of the Prophet's offer.
story: Urwa b. Mas'ud then arose and said, 'People, am I not as your father?' 'Yes, you are,' they told him. 'And are you not as my children?' 'Yes, indeed,' they replied. 'Do you mistrust me?' he then asked. 'No,' they answered. 'Do you not know that I called to the people of 'Ukaz to come forth to war alongside me and that when they put me off I came to you with my family and my son, and all who obeyed me?' 'Yes indeed,' they replied. 'This man', he went on, 'has made an appropriate offer. Accept it, and let me go to see him.' 'Go to him,' they told him. When he went to him, he began talking to the Prophet (SAAS), who said to him approximately what he had told Budayl. Urwa at that point said, 'Hey now, Muhammad, haven't you considered that you are ruining your people? Have you ever heard of any Arab who ruined his people before yourself? And if that doesn't happen, I swear I don't see faces here; I just see a hodge-podge of people who are quite capable of fleeing and abandoning you.' Abu Bakr said to him, 'Go suck al-Lat's tits! Would we flee and abandon him!' Urwa asked, 'Who is that?' 'Abu Bakr,' people told him. Urwa commented, 'Well, by Him who has my soul in His hand, if I weren't obligated to you, I'd give you a reply all right!' He then went on speaking to the Prophet (SAAS) and while doing so would take hold of the latter's beard. Al-Mughira b. Shu'ba, who was standing at the head of the Messenger of God (SAAS), wearing a sword and a helmet, would tap his hand with the hilt of his sword each time Urwa stretched it out to touch the beard of the Messenger of God (SAAS) saying, 'Keep your hands off the beard of the Messenger of God!' Urwa raised his head and asked, 'And who is that?' 'That is al-Mughira b. Shu'ba,' they replied. Urwa then said, 'You wretch! I couldn't match your treachery!' Before Islam, al-Mughira b. Shu'ba had stayed with a family and had murdered them and taken their property. Thereafter, he came and accepted Islam, whereupon the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'As for Islam, do accept it. As for money, I've nothing to do with that.' Then Urwa began watching closely the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He said (later), 'I swear that if the Messenger of God (SAAS) spat, the phlegm would fall into the hand of one of them and that person would rub it on to his face or skin. If he gave them a command, they would carry it out immediately. If he performed the ablutions, they would almost kill one another trying to get to the water he had used. If he spoke, they lowered their voices. And they never looked directly at him, out of their reverence for him.' Urwa returned to his companions and told them, 'Well, I've gone in to kings, had audiences with Caesar, Chosroe and the Negus and I swear by God I've never seen the subjects of any king venerate him the way Muhammad's people venerate him! I swear, if he spits, the phlegm always falls into the palm of one of them and he will then rub it on to his face or skin. If he gives them a command, they hurry to carry it out. If he performs the ablutions, they almost kill one another for the water he used. If he speaks, they lower their voices in his presence. And they never look directly at him, out of their veneration for him. He has made you an appropriate offer; accept it.'
lessons: Sincere devotion to a righteous leader speaks louder than any argument — it is witnessed and recognized even by those who oppose the cause.
True loyalty is not blind; Urwa came as a skeptic and left as a witness to something he could not deny.
The integrity of a community reflects the integrity of what it stands for; the Companions' behavior was itself a form of da'wah.
practical_advice: Your character and the quality of your relationships are often the most powerful testimony to your values — live in a way that others cannot help but notice.
When someone challenges your community or beliefs, respond with dignity rather than defensiveness; let your conduct answer for you.
Reflect on who or what inspires your deepest loyalty and why — understanding the source of your devotion helps you articulate it when questioned.

title: The Man of Banu Kinana and the Sacrificial Camels
summary: A man from Banu Kinana, a tribe known for venerating sacrificial camels, was sent by Quraysh to observe the Muslim camp. When he saw the necklaced and decorated sacrificial animals and heard the pilgrims chanting the talbiyya, he returned to Quraysh declaring that these people had every right to reach the Ka'ba.
story: A man of Banu Kinana then asked to be allowed to go to see him and Quraysh agreed. As he was approaching, the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'This is so-and-so; he is from a tribe who much venerate sacrificial camels. Send them out to him.' They were put out for him and people greeted him chanting labbayk, 'at Your service', ('O Lord'). When he saw this, he said, 'All praise be to God! It is not right for these people to be kept from the ka'ba.' When he returned to his companions, he told them, 'I have seen the sacrificial camels necklaced and decorated with badges, and I don't think they should be kept from the ka'ba.'
lessons: Meeting people through what they already hold sacred is a profound form of communication — the Prophet understood this man's values and spoke to them.
Sometimes the most powerful arguments are not words but visible, lived expressions of sincere faith.
God can place witnesses in unexpected places whose testimony serves the cause of truth.
practical_advice: When trying to reach someone, learn what they value most and address them through that lens rather than forcing your own framework upon them.
Let your spiritual practice be visible and consistent — sincerity witnessed by others carries its own weight.
Pay attention to unexpected advocates in your life — sometimes the people who speak up for you are those you would least expect.

title: Abu Jandal Arrives in Chains and Is Returned to Quraysh
summary: As the treaty was being finalized, Abu Jandal b. Suhayl escaped his captivity and threw himself among the Muslims, showing the marks of torture. His own father Suhayl demanded his return under the new treaty clause, and the Prophet, bound by the terms being negotiated, was unable to protect him, causing deep anguish among the Companions.
story: While this discussion was in progress Abu Jandal b. Suhayl b. 'Amr came along, dragging his chains, having escaped from below Mecca, and threw himself down among the Muslims. Suhayl said, 'This fellow, Muhammad, is the first whom I charge you to return to me.' The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'But we've not completed the agreement yet.' 'In that case,' Suhayl insisted, 'I'll never make a pact with you over anything.' The Prophet (SAAS) then said, 'Release him to my custody.' 'I'll not release him to you,' Suhayl said. 'I insist that you release him!' 'That I'll not do,' he replied. Mikraz then said, 'Very well, we'll release him to you.' Abu Jandal then exclaimed, 'Muslims, shall I be returned to the polytheists? I have come to you as a Muslim. Can't you see how I've been treated?' He had been brutally tortured in God's cause. 'Umar, may God be pleased with him, said, 'So I went to the Messenger of God, God's peace and blessings be upon him, and asked, "Are you not truly the Messenger of God?" "Yes, indeed," he replied. "And are we not in the right and our enemy in error?" "Yes, indeed," he agreed. "Why, then, are we so demeaning our religion?" I asked. He replied, "I am the Messenger of God, and I will not disobey him; He.."'
lessons: Sometimes honoring a commitment means bearing the pain of not being able to help someone who is suffering — this is one of the most difficult forms of integrity.
The anguish felt by the Companions shows that emotional suffering in the face of injustice is not weakness but a sign of living conscience.
Trust in God's plan does not eliminate pain; it gives meaning and purpose to pain that cannot yet be resolved.
practical_advice: When you are unable to help someone you care about due to circumstances beyond your control, acknowledge your grief rather than suppressing it — God sees both your intention and your sorrow.
Reflect on times when honoring a commitment cost you something significant; such experiences build the deepest form of integrity.
When facing what feels like a compromise of your values, seek counsel from trusted guides, as Umar did, rather than acting on emotion alone.

title: Umar's Distress and Abu Bakr's Counsel at Hudaybiyya
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab, troubled by the terms of the Treaty of Hudaybiyya, questioned both the Prophet and Abu Bakr about why Muslims were demeaning their religion, and both reassured him with firm trust in the Prophet's guidance.
story: I have come to you as a Muslim. Can't you see how I've been treated?' He had been brutally tortured in God's cause. "Umar, may God be pleased with him, said, 'So I went to the Messenger of God, God's peace and blessings be upon him, and asked, "Are you not truly the Messenger of God?" 'Yes, indeed,' he replied. "And are we not in the right and our enemy in error?" "Yes, indeed," he agreed. "Why, then, are we so demeaning our religion?" I asked. He replied, "I am the Messenger of God, and I will not disobey him; He is my helper." I asked, "didn't you tell us how we would come to the ka'ba and circumambulate it?" "Indeed yes," he replied. "But did I tell you we would do that this year?" "No," I replied. "Well," he said, "you will go to it and circumambulate it." Umar went on, 'I then went down to Abu Bakr and asked, "Abu Bakr, is this man not in truth God's prophet?" "Yes, indeed," he replied. "But are we not in the right and our enemy in error?" "Yes, indeed," he agreed. "Well", I asked him, "then why are we demeaning our religion?" He replied, "Look, man, he is indeed the Messenger of God and he will not disobey Him; He is his helper. Trust him; he is right." "But", I continued, "didn't he tell us we would go to the ka'ba and circumambulate it?" "Yes indeed," he agreed. "But did he say you would get there this year?" "No," I said. "You will get there and circumambulate it," he said.'
lessons: Anger and frustration in difficult circumstances do not necessarily reflect a lack of faith — even the greatest companions struggled with accepting painful decisions.
Trusting in divinely guided leadership means accepting that the full picture may not yet be visible to us, and that promises may be fulfilled on a timeline different from our expectations.
Seeking counsel from wise and grounded people during moments of emotional turmoil helps restore clarity and patience.
practical_advice: When you feel that life is treating you unjustly or that circumstances seem to contradict what you were promised, pause before reacting and seek the counsel of someone grounded and wise.
Remind yourself that divine promises are real but may unfold on a different timeline than you expect — write down a promise or goal you trust and revisit it during moments of doubt.
When you feel your emotions pulling you toward rash decisions, do as Umar did: voice your concern honestly to a trusted mentor rather than acting impulsively.

title: The Prophet's Sacrifice and the Companions Following His Lead at Hudaybiyya
summary: After the signing of the Treaty of Hudaybiyya, the Prophet's companions hesitated to make their sacrifices and shave their heads until Umm Salama advised the Prophet to act first, after which the companions followed immediately.
story: And when he had completed the document, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told his Companions, 'Make your sacrifices and shave.' And, I swear, not a man of them did so until he repeated this three times. When none of them arose, he went in to Umm Salama and told her how the men were acting. Umm Salama said, 'Prophet of God, do you like that? Go on out without speaking a word to anyone and sacrifice your camel. Then call for your barber and have him shave your head.' He went out and spoke to no one until he had done this. He made his sacrifice and called for his barber who shaved his head. When the men saw this, they arose and made sacrifice. Then some so hurriedly shaved the heads of others that it appeared that they were killing each other in their grief.
lessons: Leadership by example is often more powerful than repeated verbal instruction — actions move people when words cannot.
In moments of collective grief and emotional paralysis, a single decisive act of courage can unlock the capacity of an entire community to move forward.
Seeking wise counsel, even from unexpected sources such as Umm Salama, reflects strength and openness rather than weakness.
practical_advice: When a group you belong to is paralyzed by grief or resistance, consider whether you can be the first to take the needed step rather than waiting for others.
When you are unsure how to help someone move through emotional paralysis, take a small decisive action that models the way forward instead of repeating instructions.
Identify a trusted, grounded person in your life whose advice you can seek when you feel stuck — Umm Salama's practical wisdom in a tense moment changed the course of events.

title: The Revelation Regarding Believing Women Emigrants and Umar's Divorce
summary: After Hudaybiyya, God revealed verses testing the faith of believing women who emigrated, and Umar ibn al-Khattab divorced two wives he had married during his time as a polytheist.
story: Then some believing women went to see him. And so God Almighty revealed the words, 'O you who believe! If believing women should come to you as emigrants, then test them ...' and so on to the words, '... the marriage ties of unbelieving women' (sūrat al-Mumtahana; LX, v.10). That day Umar divorced two women who had been his while he was a polytheist. Mu'awiya b. Abi Sufyan married one of these, while Safwan b. Umayya the other.
lessons: Faith sometimes demands painful personal sacrifices, including the severing of relationships that are no longer aligned with one's beliefs and values.
Divine revelation can provide clarity and decisive guidance during moments of personal and social confusion.
Transitions in faith and identity, though difficult, open new chapters that God ordains for purposes beyond our immediate understanding.
practical_advice: When faith calls you to make a difficult personal change, acknowledge the pain of that transition honestly rather than suppressing it — grief over necessary change is human and valid.
Reflect on whether any relationships or attachments in your life are in tension with your deepest values, and seek guidance on how to navigate these with wisdom and care.
Trust that major life transitions, even painful ones, can be doorways to new blessings and arrangements ordained by God.

title: Abu Basir Kills His Captor and the Formation of the Muslim Coastal Group
summary: Abu Basir, a Muslim handed back to Quraysh per the Hudaybiyya treaty, escaped by killing one of his captors, and eventually formed a group with other escaped Muslims who began intercepting Quraysh caravans until Quraysh themselves asked the Prophet to call the group to Medina.
story: Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned to Medina. There Abu Basir, a Quraysh man, came to him, he being a Muslim. They (Quraysh) sent two men to retrieve him, referring to the pact that had been made. He gave Abu Basir over to them and they left with him and went to Dha al-Hulayfa, where they halted to eat the dates they had. Abu Basir said to one of the two men, 'By God, I can see that that sword of yours is a very fine one.' The other man unsheathed it and said, 'Yes, it is; I swear it is fine. And I've put it to the test often.' Abu Basir asked, 'Do let me look at it.' He then took it from him and struck him until he was dead. The other man fled back to Medina where he ran into a mosque. When he saw him, the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'That man has seen something dreadful.' When the man reached the Prophet (SAAS), the man exclaimed, 'I swear by God, my companion has been killed! And I'm as good as dead!' Then Abu Basir arrived and said, 'Prophet of God, God has fulfilled your obligation! You returned me to them, and then God saved me from them.' The Prophet (SAAS) then commented, 'Woe on his mother! What an inciter to war he would have been if he had had supporters!' When Abu Basir heard this, he knew that he would return him to Quraysh, and so he left for the coast. Abu Jandal b. Suhayl b. Amr escaped from Quraysh and joined Abu Basir. Thereafter, every man who left Quraysh to accept Islam went and joined Abu Basir. Eventually a group of them were formed. Whenever they heard of a Quraysh caravan heading for Syria, they would attack it, killing the men and taking the goods. And so Quraysh sent to the Prophet (SAAS) invoking God and family ties (and saying) that if he were to send for those men they would be safe. And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) did send for them. Thereafter, God Almighty revealed, 'And His it was who kept their hands away from you and yours away from them in the valley of Mecca, after He had given you control over them ...' as far as the words, '... their zeal, the zeal of al-jahiliyya' (sūrat al-Fath; XLVIII v. 24). Their 'zeal' referred to is the fact that they did not agree that he was the Prophet of God, and that they would not accept the words, 'in the name of God, the most Merciful and Compassionate'. And the fact that they denied them access to the ka'ba.
lessons: God's protection of believers can manifest through unexpected and unconventional paths — what appears to be an unjust constraint may contain within it the seeds of divine relief.
