The Prophet's adherence to treaty obligations, even when painful, demonstrated that integrity and divine trust produce outcomes that human strategy alone cannot achieve.
Those who are determined and sincere in their cause will find others drawn to them, and small groups of committed individuals can shift the balance of power significantly.
practical_advice: When you feel trapped by circumstances beyond your control, trust that God's relief can come from directions you have not anticipated — focus on what is within your power to do with integrity.
If you have made a commitment or agreement, honor it even when it is difficult, knowing that principled behavior builds trust and ultimately brings better outcomes.
When facing oppression, seek out communities of people who share your values and struggles — solidarity and mutual support can transform individual vulnerability into collective strength.

title: Umar's Night Conversation with the Prophet and the Revelation of Surat al-Fath
summary: While travelling at night, Umar repeatedly questioned the Prophet but received no reply, feared a revelation was coming about him, and was then told that Surat al-Fath — announcing a clear victory — had just been revealed.
story: It was related to him by Abd Allah b. Yusuf, quoting Malik, from Zayd b. Aslam, from his father, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was travelling one night in the company of Umar b. al-Khattab. Umar asked him a question on some matter, but the Messenger of God (SAAS) made him no reply. Umar asked him again and yet again, receiving no reply from him. Umar b. al-Khattab then exclaimed, 'Umar, your mother is as good as bereaved of you! You persisted three times in asking the Messenger of God (SAAS), and despite that he would not respond to you.' Umar related, 'So I moved my mount away and advanced to the front of the Muslims, fearing that there was going to be a revelation about me. But immediately I heard someone shouting at me. I responded, I was afraid there was about to be a revelation about me.' Then I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and greeted him. He said, 'Tonight a sūra has been revealed to me that is more pleasing to me than anything over which the sun rises.' He then recited, 'We have made for you a clear victory!' (sūrat al-Fath; XLVIII, v.1).
lessons: Silence from those we look to for guidance can provoke fear and self-doubt, but that silence may simply mean that something greater is being prepared for us.
The fear of divine accountability, as Umar felt, reflects a healthy conscience and a sincere desire to remain on the right path.
Moments of personal anxiety can be immediately transformed by divine reassurance — the revelation of victory came precisely during a moment of fear.
practical_advice: When someone you trust does not respond to you, resist the urge to catastrophize or assume the worst — pause, reflect on your own conduct, and wait with patience.
Use moments of silence or rejection from others as an opportunity for self-reflection: ask yourself whether your approach needs adjustment rather than escalating your demands.
Keep a record of moments in your life when anxiety was followed by unexpected good news — this pattern can become a source of hope during future difficult periods.

title: The Expedition of Ukkasha ibn Mihsan to Ghami Marzuq
summary: In 6 AH, the Prophet sent Ukkasha ibn Mihsan with 40 men to Ghami Marzuq; the enemy fled, and Ukkasha captured 200 camels which he brought back to Medina.
story: In Rabi al-Awwal (or Rabi al-Akhir) of that year, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Ukkasha b. Mihsan with a force of 40 men to Ghami Marzuq. They fled from him and he encamped at their wells. He sent some men in pursuit of them and took 200 of their camels which he led into Medina.
lessons: Strategic patience and disciplined positioning can achieve objectives without direct confrontation.
Leadership involves deploying the right people for the right tasks and trusting them to execute with wisdom.
Even missions that appear uneventful require courage, preparation, and commitment from those who undertake them.
practical_advice: When facing a challenging task, prepare thoroughly and position yourself well — sometimes the mere act of showing up with resolve causes obstacles to remove themselves.
Trust the people you delegate important tasks to, and give them the authority and resources they need to succeed.
Recognize and appreciate the quiet, undramatic acts of service that keep communities safe and stable — not every contribution requires a dramatic story to be valuable.

title: The Expedition of Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah to Dhu al-Qassa
summary: Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah was sent with 40 men to Dhu al-Qassa; he marched on foot through the night, arriving before dawn while the enemy fled into the mountains, and he captured one man whom he brought to the Prophet.
story: Also that year was the dispatch of Abu Ubayda b. al-Jarrah to Dhu al-Qassa, also with 40 men. He made for them on foot, reaching them in the morning while it was still dark. They fled from him into the mountain heights. He captured one of their men and brought him to the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Willingness to endure physical hardship — such as marching on foot through the night — demonstrates the depth of one's commitment to a cause greater than personal comfort.
Even when a mission does not end in full victory, partial success achieved with integrity is honorable and valuable.
Courage is often expressed not in dramatic moments but in the quiet, sustained effort of doing what is required regardless of difficulty.
practical_advice: When you face a demanding task, break it into the steps that are immediately before you rather than being overwhelmed by the whole — Abu Ubayda simply walked through the night, one step at a time.
Commit to doing what your values and responsibilities call you to do, even when the conditions are uncomfortable or the outcome uncertain.
Reflect on a time you pushed through difficulty to fulfill a commitment — use that memory as evidence to yourself that you are capable of enduring and succeeding.

title: The Tragic Expedition of Muhammad ibn Maslama
summary: Muhammad ibn Maslama was sent with 10 men on a mission; the enemy hid and ambushed them at night, killing all his companions, while Muhammad himself barely escaped wounded.
story: And there was his dispatch of Muhammad b. Maslama with a force of 10 men. The enemy hid from them until night; all the companions of Muhammad b. Maslama were killed and he escaped, wounded.
lessons: Loss and survival can coexist — being the one who survives a tragedy carries its own burden, and grief for those lost is a natural and sacred response.
Not every mission ends in the outcome we hope for, and acknowledging failure honestly is part of living with integrity and learning.
Those who survive devastating losses carry within them the responsibility to continue, to honor those who were lost, and to persevere.
practical_advice: If you have survived a loss or tragedy that others did not, allow yourself to grieve honestly without guilt — survival is not betrayal, and grief is not weakness.
When facing an outcome that went terribly wrong despite good intentions and preparation, resist the urge to blame yourself excessively — focus on what can be learned and how you can continue.
Reach out for support after experiencing trauma or devastating loss — isolation in grief compounds the pain, and communities are meant to carry one another through such moments.

title: Zayd ibn Haritha's Mission to al-Jamum and the Story of Halima
summary: Zayd ibn Haritha was sent to al-Jamum where a woman named Halima of Muzayna was captured; she guided them to a Banu Salim settlement where cattle and prisoners were taken, but the Prophet generously returned her husband to her and released them both.
story: That year too there was the mission of Zayd b. Haritha to al-Jamum. A woman of Muzayna called Halima was captured and she directed them to one of the places where Banu Salim were. There they captured cattle and sheep and took a number of the polytheists prisoner. Among them was the husband of Halima; the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave him to her and released them both.
lessons: Mercy and generosity toward those who have been captured or are vulnerable reflects the highest character of a just leader.
Even in circumstances of conflict, individual human relationships and bonds deserve recognition and compassionate response.
An act of unexpected kindness — such as reuniting spouses — can transform an adversarial encounter into a moment of profound human dignity.
practical_advice: Look for opportunities in difficult situations to extend unexpected mercy or generosity — small acts of compassion can have an outsized impact on others' sense of dignity and hope.
When you have power or authority over a situation, ask yourself what the most humane and generous response would be, not merely the most technically correct one.
Remember that behind every conflict or difficulty are human beings with bonds, relationships, and vulnerabilities that deserve to be seen and honored.

title: Zayd ibn Haritha's Mission to Banu Tha'laba
summary: Zayd ibn Haritha was sent with 15 men to Banu Tha'laba in Jumada al-Uwla; the Bedouin fled, he captured 20 of their camels, and returned after four nights away.
story: Also that year was the mission of Zayd b. Haritha, in Jumada al-Uwla, to Banu Tha'laba with a company of 15 men. The bedouin fled from him; he captured 20 of their camels, then returned, having been absent for 4 nights.
lessons: Consistency and reliability in fulfilling assigned responsibilities, even in short and uncelebrated missions, is the foundation of trustworthiness.
Not every challenge presents itself as an overwhelming confrontation — sometimes steady, quiet service is what is called for.
Small, disciplined actions taken in service of a larger purpose accumulate into a legacy of faithfulness over time.
practical_advice: When given a task or responsibility, fulfill it with the same diligence whether it is large or small, celebrated or unnoticed.
Measure success not only by dramatic outcomes but by your consistency and reliability in following through on what you committed to.

After completing a demanding period of service or work, take time to rest and restore yourself before the next responsibility — Zayd returned after four nights, and rest is a legitimate part of the cycle of service.

title: The Story of Abu al-As and Zaynab's Refuge in Medina
summary: Abu al-As, the husband of the Prophet's daughter Zaynab, was separated from his property when his caravan companions were killed; he sought refuge with Zaynab in Medina, the Prophet restored his goods, and he eventually accepted Islam and was reunited with his wife.
story: He (al-Waqidi) also said, 'That year the property that had been with Abu al-As b. al-Rabi was taken; he sought refuge with Zaynab, the daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and she took him in.' Ibn Ishaq related this man's story, about when the camels that were with him were taken, his companions were killed and he fled from them and went to Medina. His wife, Zaynab, the daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS), had emigrated after Badr. When he arrived in Medina, he sought refuge with her, and she took him in following the al-subh prayer. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave him over to her custody and told his men to return the camels taken from him. They did return to him everything taken from him, so that he lost nothing. When he returned with his property to Mecca and gave over to those he owed what was due them from their deposits, he accepted Islam. He then left Mecca and returned to Medina. The Messenger of God (SAAS) returned his wife to him by the first marriage, not by remarriage or contract, as was related heretofore.
lessons: Love, loyalty, and familial bonds can transcend religious and political divisions, and God can use these bonds as pathways to eventual guidance and reunion.
Integrity in fulfilling financial obligations — as Abu al-As did by returning deposits before accepting Islam — is a hallmark of true character regardless of one's faith at the time.
The Prophet's justice was consistent and universal: even the husband of his own daughter, who was not yet Muslim, received fair treatment and had his property restored.
practical_advice: If you are separated from someone you love by circumstances beyond your control, maintain integrity in your own conduct and trust that sincere good character can eventually bridge even the widest divides.
Honor your financial and moral obligations to others as a priority — fulfilling what you owe, as Abu al-As did before accepting Islam, reflects a character that God and people can trust.
If you find yourself in desperate circumstances, do not be too proud to seek refuge with those who care for you — accepting help from loved ones in times of crisis is a sign of wisdom, not weakness.

title: Abu Sufyan's Wife Returned and His Acceptance of Islam
summary: Abu Sufyan accepted Islam during the conquest of Mecca in 8 AH, and the Prophet returned his wife to him by the original marriage without requiring a new contract, after a separation of six years.
story: the al-subh prayer. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave him over to her custody and told his men to return the camels taken from him. They did return to him everything taken from him, so that he lost nothing. When he returned with his property to Mecca and gave over to those he owed what was due them from their deposits, he accepted Islam. He then left Mecca and returned to Medina. The Messenger of God (SAAS) returned his wife to him by the fmt marriage, not by remarriage or contract, as was related heretofore. The period between his accepting Islam and her having emigrated was six years; it is also said to have been two years. We have explained that there is no discrepancy between the two accounts, and that his acceptance of Islam came two years after the time when believing women were prohibited to unbelievers. His acceptance of Islam came in 8 AH, in the year of the conquest (of Mecca), not as is told above in the account of al-Waqidi that it took place in 6 AH. But God knows best.
lessons: Sincere acceptance of truth, even late in life, can restore what was lost and bring profound blessing.
God's mercy encompasses those who turn to Him regardless of their past opposition.
Honoring commitments and returning what belongs to others is a mark of true integrity and eases the path to spiritual transformation.
practical_advice: If you have been resistant to a truth for a long time, reflect honestly on whether pride or social pressure is holding you back rather than genuine conviction.
When you make a major life change, begin by making right what you owe to others — settle debts, return trusts, restore relationships.
Trust that sincere change is recognized and honored; do not let the fear of your past define what you can become.

title: Dihya al-Kalbi Robbed by Men of Judhm and the Retaliatory Expedition
summary: Dihya b. Khalifa returned from Caesar bearing gifts and was robbed by men of Judhm at al-Husn, prompting the Prophet to dispatch Zayd b. Haritha against them.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that in that year Dihya b. Khalifa al-Kalhi came hack from Caesar, who had awarded him money and robes of honour. When he was at al-Husn he was waylaid by some men of Judhm. They robbed him and left him with nothing. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent out Zayd b. H%itha, may God be pleased with him, against them.
lessons: Standing up against injustice and protecting those who have been wronged is a responsibility of those in authority.
Material loss, however painful, does not diminish one's dignity or standing before God.
Swift and proportionate response to wrongdoing helps maintain trust and security within a community.
practical_advice: When you are wronged or robbed of something you worked hard for, seek legitimate redress rather than despair — there are people and systems meant to support you.
If someone in your care is harmed, take practical steps to address it rather than letting injustice stand uncontested.
Remind yourself that worldly possessions can be taken, but your character and your mission cannot be stripped away.

title: Ali's Night Expedition to Banu Asad to Prevent Aid to Khaybar
summary: The Prophet sent Ali with 100 men on a covert night expedition to a village of Banu Asad after learning they intended to assist the Jews of Khaybar, capturing a scout who confirmed the plan.
story: Al-Wsqidi stated, "'Abd Allsb b. Ja'far related to me, from Ya'qab b. Wtba, who said, 'Ali, may God be pleased with him, went forth with 100 men to a village of Bana Asad b. Bakr. This was because the Messenger of God (SAAS) had learned that a group of their men wanted to extend help to the Jews of Khaybar. He approached them at night, keeping hidden by day. He ('Ali) captured a scout of theirs who confmed that he had been sent to Khaybar to suggest to them that they hand over to them their dates.'"
lessons: Proactive measures to prevent harm, taken with wisdom and restraint, are a form of responsible leadership.
Patience and strategic concealment — traveling by night and hiding by day — reflect disciplined resolve over impulsive reaction.
Gathering accurate intelligence before acting prevents unnecessary conflict and protects all parties involved.
practical_advice: When you sense a threat or conflict brewing, gather facts carefully before reacting; acting on solid information prevents unnecessary harm.
Sometimes the wisest response to a looming problem is quiet, steady preparation rather than loud confrontation.
Protect your community or family by being attentive to early warning signs and addressing them before they escalate.

title: Expedition of Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf to Dumat al-Jandal and His Marriage
summary: Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf was sent to Dumat al-Jandal, where the people accepted Islam; following the Prophet's instruction, he married Tumadir, the daughter of their king.
story: Al-Wzqidi, may God be pleased with him, said, "In Sha'bzn of 6 AH there was the expedition of 'Abd al-Ralpm b. 'Awf to Damat al-Jandal. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'If they obey, then marry the daughter of their king.' They did accept Islam and 'Abd al-Rabnn married their king's daughter, Tamndur, daughter of al-Isba' al-Kalbiya; she was the mother of Aba Salama b. 'Ahd al-Rabnn b. 'Awf."
lessons: Peaceful outreach, when conducted with wisdom and respect, can open hearts that force never could.
Trusting the guidance of one in authority, even in personal matters, can lead to blessings beyond what one might have chosen alone.
The union of people across different backgrounds, when founded on shared faith and values, produces lasting good for communities.
practical_advice: Approach difficult conversations or outreach efforts with gentleness and respect rather than pressure; people respond to sincerity.
When a trusted mentor or guide offers you direction in an important decision, consider it carefully rather than dismissing it.
Be open to life taking an unexpected positive turn — sometimes obedience to a higher principle leads to personal blessings you did not anticipate.

title: The People of Urayna: Betrayal, Murder, and Punishment
summary: A group from Ukl and Urayna came to the Prophet claiming illness from Medina's climate; he sent them to live with his camels, but they killed the herdsman, stole the herd, and apostasized, and were severely punished upon capture.
story: According to texts given by al-Bukhiiri and Muslim, quoting Sacid b. Aba Wraba, from Qamda, from Anas b. Mdik, what happened was that a group from Wkl and 'Urayna (from Wkl or Wrayna, according to one version) went to the Messenger of God (SAAS). They told him, "Messenger of God, we're really herdsmen, not farmers and we fmd Medina unhealthy." The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered them to leave with a small herd, in the charge of a shepherd, and told them to live by drinking the milk and urine of the animals. They left and when in the vicinity of al-Harra, they killed the herdsman of the Messenger of God (SAAS), drove off the herd and denied Islam after having accepted it earlier. The Prophet (SAAS) sent men in pursuit of them and had their hands and feet cut off and their eyes gouged out; they were left there at al-Harra in that state until they died. Qatnda stated, "We have heard that thereafter, whenever the Messenger of God (SAAS) made an address he would speak in favour of compassion and against exemplary punishment." This hadith is related by one group of scholars from Qatzda, while another group relates it from Anas b. Mnlik. In the account of Muslim, from Mu'zwiya b. Q u m , from Anas, it is stated that a group of Wrayna went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and announced their acceptance of Islam and their allegiance. Then smallpox broke out in Medina, and they told him, "Since this smallpox has broken out, we'd like to go back to the camels." He replied, "Yes; go and be with them." They did then leave, but they killed the herdsmen and left with the camels. According to that account, he sent some 20 of the an@r in pursuit of them, along with an expert tracker. When they were found, their hands and feet were severed and their eyes were gouged out. In the sahib collection of al-Bukhzri, on a line of transmission through Ayyiib, from Abn Qdzba, from Anas, who is quoted as saying, "A group from Wkal arrived and accepted Islam. They loathed Medina and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him so. He then said to them, 'Then go and join the camels; drink their milk and urine.' They then left and lived among them for a time; hut eventually they killed the herdsman and stole the camels. News of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and before the sun next arose they had been brought to him. He had iron rods brought and these were heated and used to cauterize them. He then had their hands and feet cut off and they were dumped out in the heat, where they sought water. But they were given none until they died, without protection. In an account from Anas, he is quoted as saying, 'I saw one of them biting the ground in his thirst.'" Abn Qdaba stated, "That gang murdered, stole and apostatized after having declared their faith. They fought against God and His messenger (SAAS)." Al-Bayhaqi related, through Wthmzn b. Aba Shayba, from 'Abd al-Rahmm b. Sulaymm, from Muhammad b. Wbayd All&, from Abn al-Zubayr, from Jzhir, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent men in pursuit of them, he said, "0 God, make them lose their way and make it narrower than a camel's ankle." He went on, "They did lose their way and were captured and brought back. He had their hands and feet severed and their eyes gouged out." In the rahih collection of Muslim, it states, "The reason he had their eyes gouged was that they had done this to the herdsman."
lessons: Betrayal of trust and kindness is among the gravest of moral failures, and its consequences fall ultimately on those who commit it.
Compassion given in good faith, as the Prophet showed by accommodating their needs, reflects the highest character even when it is abused.
Even in the aftermath of severe justice, the Prophet's instinct was toward compassion — he subsequently counseled mercy in his addresses, showing that justice and mercy must always be held together.
practical_advice: When someone takes advantage of your generosity, resist the urge to harden your heart toward everyone; the failure was theirs, not yours.
If you have ever betrayed someone's trust, reflect honestly on what drove that choice and commit to being a person of integrity going forward.
In situations of conflict or injustice, seek guidance from legitimate authority rather than taking matters into your own hands impulsively.

title: Revelation Forbidding Muslim Women to Marry Polytheists at Hudaybiyya
summary: During the year of Hudaybiyya, God revealed that believing women who emigrated could not be returned to unbelieving husbands, establishing a new boundary that superseded the general terms of the Hudaybiyya truce.
story: It was during this year that Muslim women were forbidden to polytheists. This was an exception to the general rule established by the truce agreed upon at al-Hudaybiyya that year, namely that members of Quraysh were to be returned to them even if wishing to accept Islam. And so the words of the Almighty were revealed, 'Lo you who believe! If believing women should come to you as emigrants, then test them. God knows best about their faith. If you ascertain them to be believers, then do not return them to the unbelievers. These women are not permitted for marriage to those men, nor are those men permitted for marriage to those women' (siirat al-Mumtahana; LX, v.10).
lessons: Divine revelation can refine and correct human agreements when higher principles of justice and dignity are at stake.
Faith creates bonds that transcend social and political arrangements; a person's spiritual identity must be protected even within complex negotiations.
The community has a responsibility to protect vulnerable individuals, especially those who have taken a courageous step by embracing a new faith.
practical_advice: When you feel caught between a prior commitment and a higher moral principle, seek wisdom from trusted guides rather than acting alone.
If you have made a difficult change in your life — in faith, values, or relationships — know that your new identity deserves protection and respect.
Advocate for those in vulnerable transitions; being a safe harbor for someone in a courageous moment of change is a profound act of support.

title: Summary of Major Events of 6 AH
summary: The text enumerates the major events of 6 AH including the expedition to al-Murays', the incident of the false accusation against Aisha, the Hudaybiyya pilgrimage attempt, and the peace treaty with Quraysh.
story: That year also there was the expedition to al-MuraysiC, during which the &at al-zjZ, the calumnious report, arose which ultimately resulted in the Revelation telling of the innocence of the umm al-mu'mmin, the mother of the believers, 'A'isha, as was related above. The 'umra to al-Hudaybiyya occurred that year too, along with the blockade made by the polytheists against the Messenger of God (SAAS). Ultimately there resulted peace between them lasting ten years, during which time people lived in security with one another on the understanding that there should be neither fetters used nor swords drawn. Reference is made at length in the appropriate places heretofore; and to God belong all praise and credit. That year the polytheists had control of the pilgrimage.
lessons: Periods of intense trial — false accusation, blockade, and difficult compromise — can be followed by long seasons of peace and stability.
Trusting in God's plan through humiliating and painful circumstances is the foundation of enduring faith.
Peace agreements, even imperfect ones, can create the breathing room in which communities grow and flourish.
practical_advice: When you are in a season of overlapping hardships, remind yourself that such periods are often followed by significant breakthroughs.
If you have been falsely accused, hold on to your integrity and trust that truth has a way of surfacing in time.
Consider whether a difficult compromise might actually serve a larger good — not all uncomfortable agreements are defeats.

title: The Prophet's Dispatch of Ambassadors to Foreign Rulers
summary: In Dhu al-Hijja of 6 AH, the Prophet dispatched multiple envoys to the rulers of Alexandria, the Ghassanid kingdom, Rome, Persia, Yamama, and Abyssinia, inviting them to Islam.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "In Dhn al-Hijja of that year, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent forth six men in the company of Hafib b. Abn Balta'a to al-Muqawqis, the governor of Alexandria. He also dispatched Shujacb. Wahb b. Asad b. Judhayma, who was present at Badr, to al-H&th b. Aba Shammar al-Ghassmi, the king of the Christian Arabs, that is. Also he sent Dihya b. Khalifa al-Kalbi to Caesar, he being Heraclius, emperor of Rome. And he dispatched 'Abd Allah b. Hudhafa al-Sahmi to Chosroe, king of the Persians. Also he sent Salir b. 'Amr al-'Amiri to Hawdha b. 'Ali al-Hanafi, and 'Amr b. Umayya al-Damri to the Negus, king of the Christians of Abyssinia, his name being Ashama b. al-Hurr."
lessons: Carrying a message of truth to those in power requires immense courage, as these envoys risked their lives before some of the world's mightiest rulers.
The willingness to reach out across vast cultural, political, and religious divides reflects a universal vision that transcends tribalism.
Each envoy trusted both the importance of their mission and God's protection — a model of faith-driven action in the face of great uncertainty.
practical_advice: Do not let fear of rejection or the power of others prevent you from sharing what you genuinely believe to be true and beneficial.
When you feel called to take a bold step — whether in career, relationships, or advocacy — prepare carefully and then act with conviction.
Reflect on the courage it takes to stand before authority with an unpopular message; find that same courage in smaller ways in your own life.

title: The Expedition to Khaybar in 7 AH
summary: After returning from Hudaybiyya, the Prophet remained in Medina for about twenty days before setting out to Khaybar early in 7 AH, fulfilling a divine promise made to the believers.
story: Shu'ba stated, from al-H&im, who quoted 'Abd al-Rahman b. Aba Lay12 as having said that the words, "and He rewarded them with a near victory" (siirat al-Fath; XLVIII, v.18), referred to Khaybar. Mnsa b. Wqba stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned (to Medina) from al-Hudaybiyya, he remained for some 20 days and then went forth to Khaybar; this was what God had promised to h i." Mnsa narrated, from al-Zuhri, that the conquest of Khaybar was in 6 AH; what is correct is that this occurred early in 7 AH, as stated above. Ihn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then remained in Medina after his return from al-Hudayhiyya for Dhii al-Hijja and for part of al-Muharram. He then went to Khaybar for the remainder of al-Muharram." Yanus h. Bukayr stated, quoting Muhammad b. Ishsq, from al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, that Marwm and al-Miswar stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth in the year of al-Hudayhiyya and siirat al-Fath (XLVIII) was revealed to him, while he was between Mecca and Medina. In Dhii al-Hijja, he came back and stayed until he went forth to Khaybar, making camp at al-Rajic. That is a valley between Khaybar and Ghatafsn. He was concerned that Ghatafan might give them assistance. Next morning he attacked them." Al-Bayhaqi stated, "Al-W~qidirelated similarly on the authority of his sheikhs that he went forth (to Khaybar) early in 7 AH." 'Abd All& b. Idris stated that Ishsq said to him, "'Ahd All& h. Aba Bakr related to me as follows, 'Since the conquest of Khaybar took place following al-Mulprram, the Prophet (SAAS) arrived (hack) at the end of Safar.'" Ibn Hishzm stated, "He appointed Numayla b. 'Abd All& al-Laythi in command of Medina."
lessons: God's promises to those who remain steadfast are fulfilled in His timing, not ours — the believers had to wait through Hudaybiyya before receiving the promised victory at Khaybar.
Strategic patience — resting and preparing before a major undertaking — reflects wisdom rather than weakness.
Appointing trustworthy people to guard what you leave behind is a mark of responsible leadership and careful planning.
practical_advice: When you feel like a long season of waiting has been fruitless, look for signs that it was actually preparation for something significant ahead.
Before embarking on a major challenge, take time to rest, consult, and plan — do not rush into difficulty without preparation.
If you carry responsibility for others, ensure someone capable is looking after things while you attend to another challenge; you cannot be everywhere at once.

title: The March to Khaybar: Route, Camp, and Prevention of Ghatafan's Aid
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) marched to Khaybar via Mt. Qsr and al-Sahba', strategically camping between Khaybar and Ghatafan to prevent the latter from aiding the Jews. Ghatafan initially set out to help but turned back upon hearing rumors of danger to their own homes.
story: Ibn Isḥāq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) left Medina for Khaybar, he went by way of Mt. Qsr, where a mosque was built for him. He then passed by al-Ṣaḥbā', advancing with his army and eventually making camp at a wadi by the name of al-Rajīʿ. He made his camp between Khaybar and Ghaṭafān to prevent the latter from giving assistance to the people of Khaybar. They, Ghaṭafān, had declared their support for the Messenger of God (SAAS). I have heard that when Ghaṭafān heard about all this, they met together then went forth to give aid to the Jews against the Messenger of God (SAAS). When they had travelled for a day, however, they heard from home a rumour regarding their property and families. They thought that their enemy had surprised them and so they retraced their steps and stayed with their property and families, leaving the way clear between the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Khaybar."
lessons: Strategic wisdom involves anticipating opposition and positioning oneself to neutralize it before conflict erupts.
Providence can work through unexpected means — even rumor and fear — to protect the faithful without a single blow being struck.
Trust in God does not preclude careful planning; the Prophet's (SAAS) military positioning demonstrates that tawakkul and preparation go hand in hand.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult challenge, think ahead about who or what might obstruct you, and take proactive steps to reduce those obstacles before they materialize.
Recognize that sometimes problems resolve themselves through circumstances beyond your control — this is a reason for gratitude, not complacency.
Combine sincere reliance on God with thorough preparation in your own affairs, whether in work, relationships, or personal goals.

title: Provisions at al-Sahba': Simple Food and the Prophet's Example of Contentment
summary: At al-Ṣaḥbā' below Khaybar, when provisions were called for, only barley gruel was available. The Prophet (SAAS) ordered it diluted, ate from it himself alongside his companions, then performed the maghrib prayer with only a rinse of the mouth.
story: Al-Bukhārī stated that it was related to him by ʿAbd Allāh b. Maslama, from Mālik, from Yaḥyā b. Saʿīd, from Bashīr, that Suwayd b. al-Nuʿmān related to him that he went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the year of the attack on Khaybar, eventually reaching al-Ṣaḥbā', a place below Khaybar. There the Messenger of God (SAAS) led the al-ʿaṣr prayer, then called for provisions to be brought. All that was produced was al-sawīq, barley gruel. He ordered it to be diluted and he ate, as did we. He then arose to perform the al-maghrib prayer, rinsed out his mouth and prayed, without performing the ablutions.
lessons: True leadership means sharing in the hardships of those you lead rather than insulating yourself from difficulty.
Contentment with what is available, however modest, is a mark of strong character and gratitude to God.
Religious obligations such as prayer are maintained with care and integrity even under the stress of a military campaign.
practical_advice: When circumstances are difficult and resources are scarce, resist the urge to complain; focus instead on making the best of what you have.
Model the behavior you wish to see in others — leaders and parents alike earn respect by sharing burdens rather than exempting themselves.
Maintain your core spiritual practices (prayer, reflection, gratitude) even during stressful seasons of life; they are anchors, not luxuries.

title: ʿĀmir b. al-Akwaʿ: The Poet-Warrior, His Supplication, and His Martyrdom at Khaybar
summary: On the night march to Khaybar, the companion ʿĀmir b. al-Akwaʿ recited stirring verses of supplication. The Prophet (SAAS) prayed for mercy upon him, which others interpreted as foretelling his martyrdom. ʿĀmir was later accidentally killed by the rebound of his own sword during the battle, yet the Prophet affirmed he would receive a double reward.
story: Al-Bukhārī stated that ʿAbd Allāh b. Maslama related to him, quoting Ḥātim b. Ismāʿīl, from Yazīd b. Abū ʿUbayd, from Salama b. al-Akwaʿ, who said, "We went forth for Khaybar with the Messenger of God (SAAS). We travelled by night, and one of our men said to ʿĀmir, 'ʿĀmir, would you recite us a little something you have composed?' ʿĀmir was a poet. So he dismounted to recite for our men, saying, 'Allāhumma! O God! Without You we would not have been rightly guided, Nor would we have given alms nor prayed. We beseech You to forgive what we have done (wrong) — may we be sacrificed for You! We beseech you to send down Your serenity upon us To make our feet secure when we meet our enemies. And if they cry out trying to subvert us, we will refuse. Already have they loudly sought others' help against us.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Who is this camel driver?' 'That's ʿĀmir b. al-Akwaʿ,' they replied. 'Yarḥamuhu Allāh! God have mercy on him!' he exclaimed. One of the men then commented, 'Have you then made his martyrdom inevitable, Prophet of God? I wish you had let us enjoy his (company) longer!' "When we reached Khaybar, we besieged them and were afflicted by great hunger. God then helped us conquer them and that evening — the evening of the conquest — the men lit many fires. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Why all these fires? What do you plan to cook?' 'Meat,' they replied. 'What kind of meat?' he asked. 'The meat of domestic donkeys.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Cast away the meat and break the pots!' One man asked, 'Should we not throw away the meat but wash the pots?' 'Either way,' he replied. "When the opposing forces were lined up for battle, ʿĀmir's sword was short, and when he aimed it at a Jew's leg, the sharp blade swung back and injured his own knee, causing his death." Salama went on, "When they returned from battle, the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw me (looking sad) and took me by the hand, asking, 'What is wrong?' I replied, 'May my father and my mother be your ransom, they say that ʿĀmir failed.' The Prophet (SAAS) replied, 'Whoever said that lied; he will receive a double reward.' He raised two fingers, adding, 'He was a warrior whose valour few Arabs have ever matched.'"
lessons: Sincerity of intention and purity of heart matter more than the outward manner of one's death; God judges the soul, not the spectacle.
The Prophet's (SAAS) compassion for a grieving companion — taking Salama by the hand and reassuring him — teaches us how to accompany others in sorrow.
Poetry, song, and uplifting words have a legitimate and honored role in sustaining the spirit of a community under pressure.
practical_advice: When you grieve the loss of someone you admired, seek out trustworthy guidance rather than accepting the harsh or dismissive judgments of others about that person's legacy.
Use art, poetry, or meaningful words to uplift your community or family during difficult times — morale is a form of care.
If you have acted with sincere intention and something still goes wrong, trust that God sees your heart; do not let others' misreading of events diminish your sense of worth.

title: The Prophet's Supplication Upon Overlooking Khaybar
summary: As the Muslim army halted and overlooked Khaybar before the assault, the Prophet (SAAS) led his companions in a comprehensive supplication seeking God's good and protection from evil in the village, its people, and all it contained, before commanding them to advance in God's name.
story: Ibn Isḥāq stated, "A source I do not doubt related to me, from ʿAmr b. Abū Marwān al-Aslamī, from his father, from Abū Muʿtib b. ʿAmr, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached a point overlooking Khaybar, he said, 'Stop!' Then he spoke as follows before his Companions, of whom I was one, 'O God, Lord of the heavens and all they shade, Lord of the earths and what they produce, Lord of the devils and their deceptions, Lord of the winds and what they winnow, we implore of You the good of this village and the good of its people and the good of what it contains. And we seek refuge in You from its evil, from the evil of its people, and the evil of what it contains! Go forward, in God's name!'" The hadith al-Bayhaqī related it from al-Ḥākim, from al-Aṣamm, from al-Wāridī, from Yūnus b. Bukayr, from Ibrāhīm b. Ismāʿīl b. Mujmaʿ, from Ṣāliḥ b. Kaysān, from Abū Marwān al-Aslamī, from his father, from his grandfather, who said, "We went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Khaybar. When we were near it and overlooking it, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told the men to stop and when they had done so, he said, 'O God, Lord of the seven heavens and what they shade, Lord of the seven earths and what they produce, Lord of the devils and their deception, we implore of You the good of this village and the good of its people and the good of what it contains. And we seek refuge in You from the evil of this village, from the evil of its people, and the evil of what it contains! Go forward, in the name of God the most-Merciful and Compassionate!'"
lessons: Before entering any significant challenge, pausing to acknowledge God's sovereignty and seek His protection is a prophetic practice that centers the heart.
Asking God for both good and protection from evil is a balanced form of supplication — hope and caution held together in faith.
Recognizing that good and evil can coexist in any place or community reflects a mature, nuanced understanding of the world.
practical_advice: Before beginning a difficult task, a hard conversation, or a new chapter of life, take a moment to pray or reflect, naming what you hope for and what you fear.
Adopt the habit of seeking blessing in new environments — a new job, city, or relationship — rather than approaching them with either naive optimism or pure anxiety.
Remember that surrendering outcomes to God does not mean passivity; the Prophet (SAAS) prayed and then commanded, 'Go forward.'

title: The Morning Arrival at Khaybar: 'It's a Bad Day for an Enemy We Have Warned'
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) arrived at Khaybar in the early morning. Finding no call to prayer — signaling it was not a Muslim settlement — he ordered the advance. When the Khaybar Jews came out with their tools and saw the Muslim army, they fled in alarm, prompting the Prophet's famous declaration that Khaybar was ruined.
story: Ibn Isḥāq went on, "A source I do not doubt related to me, from Anas b. Mālik, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was about to attack an enemy, he refrained from doing so until it was morning. If then he heard a call to prayer, he would stop. If he did not, he would attack. We made camp at Khaybar overnight and the Messenger of God (SAAS) rested until morning came, at which time he did not hear a call to prayer. And so he mounted up and we all did so along with him. I rode behind Abū Ṭalḥa, with my foot touching that of the Messenger of God (SAAS). On the way, we met the Khaybar labourers leaving the village with their hoes and baskets. When they saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) they exclaimed, "It's Muhammad and his army!" They then turned away in flight. At that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Allāhu Akbar! God is most Great! Khaybar is ruined! It's a bad day for an enemy we have warned when we can dismount in their open square!"'" Al-Bukhārī stated that ʿAbd Allāh b. Yūsuf related to him, quoting Mālik, from Ḥāmid al-Ṭawīl, from Anas b. Mālik, (who said), "The Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived at Khaybar at night. Whenever he reached an enemy at night, he would not attack them until morning. When it was morning, the Jews came out bearing their hoes and baskets and, seeing what they did, they exclaimed, 'It's Muhammad, by God! Muhammad and his army!' At this the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'Khaybar is ruined! It's a bad day for an enemy we have warned when we can dismount in their open square!'" Al-Bukhārī went on to state that Ṣadaqa b. al-Faḍl related to him, quoting Abū ʿUyayna, quoting Ayyūb, from Muḥammad b. Sīrīn, from Anas b. Mālik, who said, "We arrived at Khaybar early in the morning as its people were coming out bearing their hoes. When they saw the Prophet (SAAS) they called out, 'By God, it's Muhammad and his army!' At that the Messenger of God, God's peace and blessings be upon him, commented, 'Khaybar is ruined! It's a bad day for an enemy we have warned when we can dismount in their square!'"
lessons: Principled restraint — waiting for morning and listening for signs before acting — reflects the prophetic ethics of avoiding unnecessary harm.
Those who have been clearly warned and persist in opposition bear responsibility for the consequences that follow.
Gratitude expressed in the moment of divine assistance ('Allāhu Akbar!') trains the heart to recognize that victory comes from God, not merely from human effort.
practical_advice: Before making a consequential decision, give yourself time and space — do not act in the dark, literally or figuratively; wait for clarity.
If you have warned someone repeatedly about the consequences of a path and they continue, release yourself from guilt over the outcome; you fulfilled your responsibility.
When things go unexpectedly well after a period of stress and struggle, pause to express genuine gratitude rather than rushing forward to the next task.

title: Abū Hurayra Joins the Muslim Army at Khaybar and Receives a Share of the Booty
summary: Abū Hurayra arrived in Medina with a group from his people while the Prophet (SAAS) was already at Khaybar. He caught up with the army after the conquest, and the Prophet (SAAS) generously included him and his companions in the distribution of booty despite their late arrival.
story: Imām Aḥmad stated, "ʿAffān related to us, quoting Wuhayb, quoting Khuthaym — Ibn ʿIrāk, that is — who quoted his father (as saying) that Abū Hurayra arrived in Medina with a group of his people while the Prophet (SAAS) was at Khaybar, having left Sibāʿ b. ʿUrfuṭa — of Banū Ghifār, that is — in command of Medina. He, Abū Hurayra stated, 'When I went to him he was saying the al-ṣubḥ prayer, at the first rakʿa reciting "kāf hā ʿayn ṣād"' (sūrat Maryam; XIX), and at the second, the verse, "Woe to those who default" (sūrat al-Muṭaffifīn (or al-Taṭfīf); LXXXIII, v.1). I told myself, "Woe to so-and-so who receives full measure but gives short change!"'' He went on, "When he had prayed, we went back some distance, as far as Khaybar; the Prophet (SAAS) had by then conquered Khaybar. He addressed the Muslims and gave us shares of the booty along with them." Al-Bayhaqī related this from a ḥadīth of Sulaymān b. Ḥarb, from Wuhayb, from Khuthaym b. ʿIrāk, from his father, from men of Banū Ghifār, who said, "Abū Hurayra arrived in Medina and related this."
lessons: The Prophet's (SAAS) generosity in including latecomers in the distribution of reward models a leadership ethic of inclusion over strict technical entitlement.
Reflection during prayer — as Abū Hurayra pondered the verse about giving short measure — shows that worship is meant to engage the conscience, not merely fulfill a ritual.
Arriving late to an effort does not necessarily disqualify one from participating in its fruits, especially when one's absence was not from laziness or cowardice.
practical_advice: If you feel you have 'missed out' on an opportunity or arrived too late to contribute, do not give up — show up anyway; there may still be a place for you.
Allow your spiritual practices to speak to your daily life; when a verse or reflection surfaces during prayer or meditation, take it seriously as guidance.
In leadership, err toward generosity and inclusion when distributing recognition or reward; strict gatekeeping of credit damages community spirit.

title: The Army's Arrival at Khaybar and the Prophet's Declaration
summary: The Prophet Muhammad and his army arrived at Khaybar at dawn, declaring its ruin upon arrival as the inhabitants fled in fear back to their forts.
story: it's Muhammad and his army!' At that the Messenger of God, God's peace and blessings be upon him, commented, 'Khaybar is mined! It's a bad day for an enemy we have warned when we can dismount in their square!' Imam Ahmad stated that CAhd al-Razzaq related to him, quoting Makar, from Qatada, from Anas, who said, 'When the Prophet (SAAS) came to Khaybar, he found them carrying their hoes as they went out to their fields. When they saw him and the army with him, they fled back into their forts. The Prophet (SAAS) then said, "Allahu Akbar! Khaybar is ruined! It's a bad day for those already warned if we can dismount in their open square!"' Al-Bukhari stated that Sulayman b. Harb related to him, quoting Hammad b. Zayd, from Thabit, from Anas b. Malik, who said, 'At Khaybar, the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the pre-dawn prayer close to Khaybar and then he said, "Allahu Akbar! Khaybar is ruined! It's a bad day for those already warned if we can dismount in their open square!" They (the people of Khaybar) took various paths in flight.'
lessons: Preparation and trust in God transforms fear into confidence — the Prophet began even this military campaign with prayer at dawn.
Those who are warned yet persist in opposition bring consequences upon themselves; accountability is a universal principle.
True strength is not in intimidation but in righteousness — the Prophet's declaration was one of confidence grounded in divine support.
practical_advice: When facing a daunting challenge, begin your day with prayer and remembrance of God to ground yourself before taking action.
If you have repeatedly warned someone about a destructive path and they have not listened, release the burden of responsibility — you have fulfilled your duty.
Remind yourself in moments of anxiety that preparation combined with trust in God is the most powerful combination available to you.

title: The Prohibition of Donkey Meat at Khaybar
summary: After some soldiers began eating captured donkey meat at Khaybar, the Prophet's caller announced its prohibition, and the soldiers immediately complied.
story: (Anas went on) 'We captured some donkey meat. Then the crier of the Prophet (SAAS) called out, "God and His prophet forbid you to eat the meat of donkeys; it is unclean!"' Al-Bukhari is unique in giving this; Muslim did not. Both al-Bukhari and Muslim reported the prohibition of eating the meat of domestic donkeys through various chains of transmission in the work Kitab al-Ahkam.
lessons: Revelation and divine guidance can redirect us even mid-action — it is never too late to correct course when truth becomes clear.
The willingness to immediately abandon a permissible-seeming action upon receiving authoritative guidance is a mark of spiritual maturity.
Trust in divine wisdom even when the reasoning is not immediately apparent to us is a foundational act of faith.
practical_advice: When you receive credible guidance that something you are doing is harmful — even if it seemed harmless — stop and reflect rather than defending your previous action.
Practice the discipline of checking your actions against your values and principles regularly, not just when you are caught.
If you find yourself confused about whether something is permissible or beneficial, seek guidance from trusted, knowledgeable sources before proceeding.

title: The Prophet's Humble Mount at Khaybar
summary: Reports describe the Prophet Muhammad riding a simply equipped donkey with a palm-fibre halter and saddle at Khaybar, illustrating his profound humility as a leader.
story: The hadith al-Bayhaqi stated, 'Abu Tahir, the jurist informed us, quoting Khatib b. Ahmad al-Tusi; Muhammad b. Hamid al-Abhari related to us, quoting Muhammad b. al-Fadl, from Muslim al-Acwar al-Maki, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to visit the sick, follow behind funeral processions and respond to the pleas from slaves, and rode on donkeys. At the expeditions to Banu Qurayza and to al-Nadir, he rode a donkey. At the battle of Khaybar he rode a donkey with a halter made of palm fibre, and his saddle was also made of palm fibre."' Al-Tirmidhi related this hadith in its entirety from 'Ali b. Hajr, from 'Ali b. Mushir, and Ibn Maja, from Muhammad b. al-Sabbah, from Sufyan, and from Umar b. Gafi' from Jarir, all of them quoting Muslim.
lessons: True leadership is demonstrated through service and simplicity, not pomp and status symbols.
Visiting the sick, following funeral processions, and responding to those in need are acts of profound human dignity that no leader is too great to perform.
Material simplicity chosen voluntarily is a spiritual strength, not a weakness — the Prophet led armies from the back of a simply saddled donkey.
practical_advice: Challenge yourself to perform one act of humble service this week that you might normally consider beneath your station or too inconvenient.
When you are tempted to use status or position to avoid discomfort, ask yourself what example you are setting for those who look up to you.
Reflect on whether the material comforts you pursue are adding to your character or distracting from it.

title: The Banner of Khaybar: Ali's Eyes Healed and Victory Granted
summary: The Prophet promised to give his banner to one whom God and His messenger love, healed Ali's painful eyes with his saliva and prayer, and sent him forward to conquer Khaybar.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that 'Abd Allah b. Maslama related to him, quoting Hatim, from Yazid b. Abi Ubayd, from Salama b. al-Akwa', who said, "'Ali b. Abi Talib was lagging behind the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Khaybar, suffering from sore eyes. 'Ali commented, 'How should I lag behind the Prophet (SAAS)?' And he joined him. And on the night (before) when Khaybar was conquered, he said, 'I will certainly give the banner tomorrow to someone (or the words were: "may someone tomorrow take the banner") whom God and His messenger love, and it will be conquered by him.' We had hoped for that, but it was said that this referred to 'Ali. So it was given to him, and Khaybar was conquered." Al-Bukhari then stated that Qutayba related to him, quoting Ya'qub b. 'Abd al-Rahman, from Abu Hazim, who said, 'Sahl b. Sa'd narrated to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said at Khaybar, "I will certainly give this banner tomorrow to a man at whose hand God will grant victory, someone who loves God and His messenger and whom God and His messenger love."' The account continued, 'The men spent that night wondering to which of them it would be given. When morning came, they all went to the Prophet (SAAS), each hoping that it would be given to him. He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) then asked, "Where is 'Ali b. Abi Talib?" "Messenger of God," they told him, "his eyes are bothering him." "Send for him," he said. They sent to fetch him and he came. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then spat in his eyes and said a prayer for him. And he was cured, there being pain no longer. He then gave him the banner. 'Ali asked, "Messenger of God, shall I battle them until they become as we are?" He replied, "Continue riding until you reach their main square and dismount there. Then invite them to Islam and inform them of their duties to the Almighty therein. And by God, if God gives guidance through you to just one man among them, that will be better for you than for you to have the humr al-na'am, the choice part of the herd!"'
lessons: Physical limitation and illness do not disqualify a person from being chosen for great responsibility — Ali's eyes were healed at the very moment he was needed.
God's selection of people for honour is not based on self-promotion or eagerness for leadership, but on the quality of one's love for God and His messenger.
Guiding even one person toward truth and goodness is declared by the Prophet to be more valuable than the greatest worldly wealth — a powerful reorientation of what success means.
practical_advice: When you feel sidelined by illness, exhaustion, or personal struggle, remember that your moment of being called upon may be closer than you think — do not abandon your position.
Release ambition for recognition and instead focus on deepening your love for God and your commitment to serving others; honour follows naturally.
Measure your success not by what you have accumulated but by the positive impact you have had on even one other human life.

title: Umar's Rare Desire for Leadership
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab admitted that the night the Prophet promised the banner was the only time in his life he truly desired a position of leadership, reflecting an important lesson about intentions.
story: In the Sahih collection of Muslim, and in al-Bayhaqi, there is given a hadith of Suhayl b. Abi Salih, from his father, from Abu Hurayra, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Tomorrow I shall give the banner to a man who loves God and His messenger and whom God and His messenger love. God will grant victory at his hands." Umar commented, "It was only that day that I loved leadership!" He called for 'Ali and gave him his mission. He then said, "Go and fight on till God gives you victory; do not turn aside." 'Ali asked, "How long should I battle the enemy?" He replied, "Fight them until they bear witness that there is no god but God and that Muhammad is His servant and His messenger. If they do that they will have saved their blood from us, along with their possessions, except for what is otherwise justified, their accounting being with God."'
lessons: Even the most pious and disciplined individuals can feel the pull of ambition — what matters is honest self-awareness about our motivations.
Umar's admission is an act of profound spiritual honesty; acknowledging a desire for honour is not sinful, but pursuing it at the expense of others is.

The purpose of leadership in the Islamic tradition is not self-glorification but service to a higher truth — the mission given to Ali was one of invitation and guidance, not conquest for its own sake.
practical_advice: Practice honest self-examination: when you find yourself hoping for recognition or a prominent role, acknowledge that feeling without judgment, then check whether your motivations are pure.
When you are not chosen for something you hoped for, take the example of Umar — accept the outcome gracefully and find meaning in the role you do have.
Remind yourself regularly that the goal of any responsibility entrusted to you is to serve others and uphold truth, not to elevate your own status.

title: Abu Bakr and Umar's Earlier Attempts at Khaybar Before Ali's Mission
summary: Before Ali was given the banner, both Abu Bakr and Umar were sent to lead at Khaybar but returned without victory, illustrating that success comes in God's time and through God's chosen means.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr quoted Muhammad b. Ishaq as saying, 'Burayda b. Sufyan b. Farwa al-Aslami related to me, from his father, from Salama b. 'Amr b. al-Akwa', may God be pleased with him, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) sent Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, to several of the forts at Khaybar. He engaged in battle, but then returned, there having been no conquest despite his efforts. He then sent 'Umar, may God be pleased with him, and he battled and returned without conquest. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Tomorrow I shall certainly give the banner to a man whom God and His messenger love and who loves God and His messenger. God will grant victory at his hands; and he is not someone who retreats.""' Salama stated, 'And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) called for 'Ali b. Abi Talib, may God be pleased with him, whose eyes were bothering him that day. The Messenger of God (SAAS) spat in his eyes, then said, "Take the banner and carry on with it until God grants you victory." He went forth with it, travelling fast and with confidence, and we followed directly after him. Eventually he planted the banner in a pile of rocks beneath a fort. A Jew looked down from the fort and asked, "And who are you?" He replied, "I am 'Ali, son of Abi Talib!" The Jew then said, "I swear by what was revealed to Moses, you are victorious!" And he did not return until God granted victory at his hands!"'
lessons: Even the greatest companions — Abu Bakr and Umar — faced setbacks and returned without achieving their goal; failure is not a reflection of one's worth.
God's timing is not our timing; what appears to be repeated failure may simply be the unfolding of a plan whose completion belongs to someone or something else entirely.
Confidence, speed, and commitment — as shown by Ali — are qualities that, when combined with divine support, transform a daunting mission into an accomplished one.
practical_advice: When you have given your best effort and still not achieved your goal, do not interpret that as personal failure — release the outcome to God and trust that the right result will come through the right means.
Observe how the greatest people around you also experience setbacks; let their resilience normalise your own struggles rather than making you feel uniquely inadequate.
Move forward in your responsibilities with the same confidence and speed as Ali — hesitation and self-doubt shrink what divine support can accomplish through you.

title: Safiyya bint Huyayy Taken Prisoner and Set Free as Her Dowry
summary: Among the prisoners taken at Khaybar was Safiyya bint Huyayy, who was initially given to a companion but then came to the Prophet, who freed her and made her freedom her dowry.
story: 'They (the people of Khaybar) took various paths in flight. And the Prophet (SAAS) killed their warriors and took their families prisoner. Among the prisoners was Safiyya. She went to the lot of Dihya al-Kalbi, and then to the Prophet (SAAS), who set her free as her dowry. "Abd al-Aziz b. Suhayb said to Thabit, "Abu Muhammad, was it you who asked Anas, 'What was it he gave as her dowry?' Thabit nodded in affirmation to him."'
lessons: In the midst of conflict and upheaval, there are moments of dignity and mercy that stand apart — the freeing of Safiyya was an act of honouring her humanity.
The circumstances of one's beginning do not define the dignity of one's future — Safiyya moved from captivity to a position of profound honour.
Even in the darkest transitions of life, a new chapter defined by dignity and care is possible.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in circumstances that feel degrading or beyond your control, hold on to the truth that your story is not finished and your worth is not determined by your current situation.
Look for ways in your own life to extend dignity to those who are vulnerable or have lost their footing — small acts of genuine respect can transform someone's experience entirely.
If you are navigating a painful life transition, try to identify one element of it that represents a new beginning rather than only a loss.

title: The Banner Given to Ali at Khaybar – Abu Bakr and Umar's Attempts
summary: The Prophet was afflicted with a severe headache at Khaybar and could not lead the battle. Abu Bakr and then Umar each took the banner and fought fiercely but returned without victory, after which the Prophet promised to give the banner the next day to a man whom God and His messenger love.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) might well be affected by a bad headache and spend a day or two without going outside to the people. When he made camp at Khaybar he suffered a severe headache and did not come out to the men. Abu Bakr took up the banner of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and then set out to battle where he fought fiercely. He then returned. Umar next took it, fought a battle even more fierce than the first and then he returned. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was informed of this and he said, 'Tomorrow I shall certainly give it to a man who loves God and His messenger and whom God and His messenger love. He will take it (Khaybar) by force.'
lessons: True leadership is not claimed but given by God's will; patience in waiting for divine appointment is a virtue.
Personal suffering, like the Prophet's headache, does not diminish one's spiritual rank or God's care for a person.
Sincere effort in the face of difficulty, even without immediate success, is honourable before God.
practical_advice: When you strive hard for something and do not succeed, do not lose heart — continue with sincerity and leave the outcome to God.
During times of physical illness or weakness, trust that God's plan is unfolding even when you cannot act.
Avoid measuring your worth by immediate results; your sincere effort is witnessed and valued by God.

title: Ali's Sore Eyes Healed by the Prophet Before the Battle of Khaybar
summary: Ali ibn Abi Talib arrived at Khaybar suffering from a painful eye ailment, and the Prophet miraculously healed him by spitting into his eye, after which Ali felt no pain and was given the banner.
story: 'Ali was not present. The men of Quraysh there had aspirations for it, each one hoping to receive it. Next morning 'Ali b. Abi Talib came riding a camel of his. He made it kneel nearby. He was suffering from an eye ailment and had bound his eye with a piece of striped cloth. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him, 'What's wrong?' He replied, 'My eyes became sore after you left.' He told him, 'Come close to me.' He then spat in 'Ali's eye and he suffered no pain from it till he left.
lessons: Divine healing can come through unexpected and humble means; God's mercy is not bound by our expectations.
Trust in the Prophet's intercession and in God's power to remove hardship at the right moment is a source of strength.
Physical affliction does not disqualify a person from being chosen for a great task; God enables whom He wills.
practical_advice: When you are suffering physically or emotionally, bring your pain to God and trust that relief can come swiftly and miraculously.
Do not exclude yourself from responsibility or service because of your current weakness; present yourself and let God decide.
Remember moments when God removed your hardship unexpectedly — let those memories reinforce your trust in Him during new trials.

title: The Prophet Gives the Banner to Ali and Predicts Victory at Khaybar
summary: The Prophet gave the battle banner to Ali ibn Abi Talib, declaring that God and His messenger love him, and that victory would come at his hands — a prophecy that was fulfilled.
story: He arose and then not one among us with good standing with the Messenger of God (SAAS) did not hope that he would be that man. I myself was presumptuous enough to hold my own head high in expectation, because of my standing with him. He called for 'Ali b. Abi Talib, whose eyes were bothering him. He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) wiped them and then gave him the banner. And victory was granted him.
lessons: God's choice of a person for an honourable role is based on divine wisdom and love, not on human expectations or self-assessment.
Humility before God's decisions — even when we hoped to be chosen ourselves — is a mark of mature faith.
A prophecy fulfilled is a reminder that God's word is certain; trusting His promises brings peace to the heart.
practical_advice: When you are passed over for an honour or opportunity you desired, practise graceful acceptance and trust that God placed it where it belongs.
Reflect on the times God's plan proved wiser than your own wishes — this builds contentment with divine decree.
Speak well of those chosen over you; your character in that moment reflects the depth of your faith.

title: Ali Defeats Marhab the Champion of Khaybar
summary: The Jewish champion Marhab came out boasting of his prowess in battle verse, and Ali ibn Abi Talib met him in single combat, responding with his own verse before splitting Marhab's head and conquering the fort.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then gave him the banner and he took it and left. He was wearing a purple and red outer garment, the nap of which had worn off. When he reached Khaybar, Marhab, the commander of the fort, came out, wearing on his head a Yemeni helmet with a stone with a hole in it the size of an egg. He was reciting the following, 'Khaybar knows that I am Marhab, fully armed, a hero, well tested. When lions advance and rage, their prey retreats from the victors' attack.' 'Ali, may God be pleased with him, responded with the verse, 'I am he whom my mother named "Haydar", "lion", like those of the forests, extremely fierce. I'll give you full measure for measure.' They exchanged two blows and 'Ali then surprised him with a blow that split the stone, and his helmet and his head, right down to the molars. He then conquered the city.
lessons: Courage in the face of an intimidating adversary is strengthened when one acts for a cause greater than oneself.
The boasting of an opponent need not unsettle a believer whose confidence is rooted in God rather than in personal might.
Swift decisive action, when the moment calls for it, can bring about outcomes that prolonged hesitation cannot.
practical_advice: When facing a daunting challenge, remind yourself that you are not relying solely on your own strength but on God's support.
Do not allow the confidence or loud proclamations of others to shake your sense of purpose and self-worth.
Prepare thoroughly, then act decisively — second-guessing yourself at the critical moment drains the energy needed to succeed.

title: Amir ibn al-Akwa's Martyrdom and the Prophet's Consolation
summary: The brave warrior Amir ibn al-Akwa accidentally struck his own ankle in battle against Marhab and died from the wound; some Companions said his effort was in vain, but the Prophet declared he would receive a double reward.
story: 'Amir, may God be pleased with him, went out to challenge him, reciting, 'Khaybar knows that I am 'Amir, fully armed, a hero brave.' They exchanged two blows, then Marhab's sword struck into 'Amir's shield. He charged vigorously at him, and he, 'Amir, retreated and struck his own ankle, causing a fatal wound. Salama stated, 'I then left and came across a group of the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS), saying, "Amir's work was all in vain, he killed himself!" When I approached the Messenger of God (SAAS) I was weeping and he asked, "What's wrong with you?" I replied, "They say that Amir's work was in vain." "And who said that?" he asked. "Some of your Companions," I replied. "They lied," he said, "he shall have a double reward."'
lessons: An accidental or tragic end does not erase the sincerity and courage of a person's effort in God's cause; God judges intention.
The Prophet's immediate correction of false narratives about the deceased teaches us not to rush to judge the misfortune of others.
Grief is natural and honourable; weeping for a fallen companion is not weakness but an expression of genuine love and brotherhood.
practical_advice: When others rush to judge someone's failure or misfortune harshly, stand up for a more compassionate and measured understanding of events.
If you feel that your efforts ended in an embarrassing or painful failure, remember that God sees the sincerity behind the effort, not just the outcome.
Allow yourself to grieve losses properly — suppressing sorrow about a loved one or a painful event delays healing rather than speeding it.

title: The Prophet's Prediction of Amir's Martyrdom Before the Battle
summary: Before the battle, Amir recited verses of supplication and the Prophet asked who was speaking; upon learning it was Amir, he prayed for his forgiveness — a prayer the Prophet never made for a person without that person soon being martyred.
story: 'Amir went out and began to recite, 'By God, had it not been for you, we would not have been rightly guided, nor given alms nor prayed. By your grace, we have not failed; so send down Your serenity upon us and make our feet firm when we meet the enemy.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked who it was speaking. 'That is Amir,' they said. 'May your Lord forgive you!' he then said. And the Messenger of God (SAAS) never made such special mention of any person without him being martyred. 'Umar, mounted on a camel, commented, 'I wish you had let us go on enjoying Amir!'
lessons: A prayer for forgiveness from the Prophet was a sign of a person's nearness to God and impending martyrdom — death in God's cause is an honour, not a punishment.
Umar's wish to keep enjoying Amir's company reflects the Islamic value of cherishing righteous companions while they are with us.
Awareness that our time with those we love is finite should inspire us to honour them, pray for them, and express appreciation while we can.
practical_advice: Tell the people you love and respect that you value them — do not assume there will always be more time.
Recite prayers and words of remembrance before entering any difficulty; placing yourself in God's hands is the greatest preparation.
When someone is taken from your life, find comfort in the knowledge that their sincere deeds were witnessed and valued by God.

title: Mahmud ibn Maslama Killed by a Millstone at the Fort of Naim
summary: During the campaign at Khaybar, the first fort to fall was that of Naim, where Mahmud ibn Maslama was killed when the defenders dropped a millstone upon him from above.
story: The first of the forts at Khaybar to be defeated was that of Naim. There Mahmud b. Maslama was killed; a millstone was dropped from it upon him and it killed him.
lessons: War and its sacrifices are real and painful; the loss of a companion in service of a noble cause is a profound sorrow that must be acknowledged.
Not every person who strives in a righteous cause lives to see its completion, yet their contribution is never lost.
Accepting the unpredictability of life and death is part of trusting God's wisdom, even when loss feels sudden and unjust.
practical_advice: When life cuts short the work of someone good, honour their memory by continuing what they gave their effort to.
Sit with grief when it comes rather than rushing past it; acknowledging the weight of loss is part of processing it in a healthy way.
Reflect on the fragility of life not to become fearful, but to use your own time with greater intention and gratitude.

title: Ali Uses the Gate of the Fort as a Shield at Khaybar
summary: During the assault on Khaybar, a Jew knocked Ali's shield from his hand; Ali seized the gate of the fort and used it as a shield throughout the battle until God granted victory, after which eight men could not turn the gate over.
story: We went forth with 'Ali to Khaybar; the Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent him out with his banner. As he approached the fort, its people came out to him and he battled them. One of them, a Jew, struck him and knocked his shield from his hand. 'Ali picked up the gate of the fort and used it to defend himself. It stayed in his hand as he fought, until God (SAAS) granted him victory. He then threw it aside. And I, with seven others, myself being the eighth, tried hard to turn that door over, but we could not do it!
lessons: When one resource is taken away, God can provide another unexpected means — adaptability in the face of adversity is a gift.
Extraordinary feats in service of a righteous cause are made possible by God's enablement, not solely by human strength.
Victory comes from God; the means through which it is delivered may be surprising, even wondrous.
practical_advice: When you lose a tool or resource you were relying on, pause before panicking — look around for what God has placed nearby that you can use.
Do not become so attached to a specific plan or resource that you cannot pivot when circumstances change suddenly.
After a difficulty has passed, reflect on how God provided for you in ways you did not expect — this builds deeper reliance on Him for future challenges.

title: Muhammad ibn Maslama Kills Marhab in Single Combat
summary: Muhammad ibn Maslama volunteered to fight Marhab to avenge his brother who had been killed the day before; after a fierce duel using a tree as cover, he struck Marhab dead.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Who will do this?' Muhammad b. Maslama responded, 'I will, Messenger of God! It is up to me to seek revenge, for they killed my brother yesterday.' He told him, 'Go at him! O God, give him aid against him!' When they drew near one another a brittle old tree was there between them and each of them began using it to avoid his adversary. Whenever one of them took refuge behind it, the other would cut the wood with his sword to get at the other. Eventually the tree stood branchless, like a man. Marhab then attacked Muhammad b. Maslama and struck him. He met the blow with his shield and Marhab's sword cut into it and remained wedged there. He then withdrew it, and Muhammad struck at him until he had killed him.
lessons: Grief and the desire to honour a fallen loved one can be channelled into courageous and purposeful action rather than paralysing sorrow.
The Prophet's prayer for a fighter before battle is a reminder to seek the prayers of the righteous when entering any difficult undertaking.
Persistence through danger, maintaining focus even when an adversary is strong and the conditions are difficult, is a mark of true determination.
practical_advice: When grief moves you to act, ensure your action is purposeful and directed toward something constructive rather than destructive.
Before entering a major challenge, ask God and trusted people to pray for you — the support of sincere supplication is real.
In prolonged struggles, adapt your approach as circumstances change; rigidly clinging to a plan when the environment shifts can cost you everything.

title: Zubayr ibn al-Awwam Kills Yasir, Brother of Marhab
summary: After Marhab was killed, his brother Yasir came out challenging any Muslim to combat; al-Zubayr fought and killed him, fulfilling the Prophet's reassurance to Zubayr's mother Safiyya that her son would not be killed.
story: Marhab's brother Yasir next came out, asking, 'Who will do combat with me?' Hisham b. Urwa claimed that al-Zubayr went out to fight him and Umm Safiyya, daughter of 'Abd al-Muttalib, asked, 'Messenger of God, will he kill my son?' He replied, 'On the contrary, your son will kill him, if God so wills it.' The two men then fought and al-Zubayr killed him. If anyone thereafter commented to al-Zubayr, 'By God, your sword was really sharp that day!' he would reply, 'I swear by God, it wasn't sharp. I just hit him really hard.'
lessons: A mother's fear for her child is natural; bringing that fear to God and His Prophet and receiving reassurance is a model of trust in divine knowledge.
Zubayr's humble deflection of praise — crediting effort over the quality of his weapon — reflects the Islamic value of not boasting about personal achievement.
Victory in any struggle comes from determination and God's will combined, not from the superiority of one's tools or resources alone.
practical_advice: When you are worried about someone you love facing danger, bring your fear to God in prayer rather than letting anxiety consume you.
When you succeed at something difficult, be humble about it — acknowledge your effort while recognising that God's support was essential.
Do not over-rely on your tools, resources, or circumstances; cultivate the internal strength and determination that makes the most of whatever you have.
