title: Salama's Wound Healed by the Prophet at Khaybar
summary: Salama ibn al-Akwa was wounded at Khaybar and the Prophet blew on his wound three times, after which Salama never suffered from it again.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Makki b. Ibrahim related to him, quoting Yazid b. Abi Ubayd, who said, "I saw a scar from a blow on Salama's leg. I asked him, 'Aba Muslim, what blow caused this?' 'This is a wound I got at Khaybar. People told one another, "Salama was wounded!" Then I went to the Prophet (SAAS), and he blew on it three times. And I've never suffered from it right up till now.'"
lessons: Turning to a trusted spiritual leader or guide during pain can bring unexpected relief and peace.
Physical trials in the path of righteousness carry honour and are remembered with pride, not shame.
The blessings that come through sincere devotion can produce effects that outlast the trial itself.
practical_advice: When experiencing physical or emotional pain, seek support from those you trust spiritually and personally rather than suffering alone.
Keep a record of hardships you have overcome; revisiting them can remind you of your resilience.
Reframe wounds and scars — physical or emotional — as marks of courage rather than sources of shame.

title: The Man Who Fought Fiercely but Killed Himself — Actions vs. Intentions
summary: A seemingly heroic fighter at Khaybar wounded himself to death, leading the Prophet to teach that outward actions do not reveal one's inner state or ultimate destiny.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that 'Abd Allah b. Maslama related to him, quoting Ibn Abi Hazim, from his father, from Sahl, who said, "On one of his expeditions, the Prophet (SAAS) met and battled the polytheists. Then each side returned to their camp. Among the Muslims there was one man who tirelessly chased after all and sundry of the polytheists, striking at them with his sword. Someone said to him, 'None of us has accomplished so much as so-and-so has.' He replied, 'He will go to hell.' They asked him, 'Well, if he's going to hell, which of us will go to heaven?' One of the men said, 'I'm going to follow him. Whether he hurries or slows down, I'll be with him.' Eventually the man was wounded and wanted to die. So he placed the hilt of his sword in the ground and its tip between his nipples and bore down on it, thus killing himself. "So the men (who had followed him) came to the Prophet (SAAS) and exclaimed, 'I do bear witness that you are the Messenger of God!' 'What's up with you?' he asked him and the man told him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'A man may perform actions like those who belong in paradise and seem to people to belong in paradise, yet be headed for hell, or he may perform actions such as those of the inhabitants of hell and be headed for paradise.'"
lessons: External performance and visible accomplishment do not define one's true spiritual state — sincerity of heart is paramount.
We are called to judge our own intentions rather than rank others based on their visible deeds.
Despair severe enough to lead to self-destruction is itself a spiritual failure; hope in God's mercy must be maintained even in suffering.
practical_advice: Before taking pride in your accomplishments or comparing yourself to others, pause and reflect on whether your motivations are genuinely sincere.
When overwhelmed by pain, resist the urge to act rashly; reach out to someone you trust before making any irreversible decision.
Cultivate inner spiritual health, not just outward religious performance — attend to your intentions, not just your actions.

title: The Pretender at Khaybar Who Committed Suicide — God Aids Faith Through an Evil Man
summary: A man who was secretly not a true believer fought fiercely at Khaybar, received many wounds, and then killed himself, fulfilling the Prophet's earlier warning and prompting the proclamation that God aids the faith even through an evil man.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Abu al-Yaman related to him, quoting Shu'ayb, from al-Zuhri (who said), "Sa'id b. al-Musayyab informed me that Abu Hurayra said, 'We took part at Khaybar, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, referring to a man among his force who was pretending allegiance to Islam, "This man belongs in hell." When the fighting commenced, that man fought very fiercely and received many wounds, so that some people began to have their doubts. The man suffered much pain from his wounds and put his hand into his quiver, took out an arrow and cut his throat with it. Some of the Muslims hurried off and said, "Messenger of God, God has fulfilled what you said! So-and-so has committed suicide!" He then said, "Get up, so-and-so and so-and-so and announce, 'No one but a believer will enter paradise; and God aids the faith even through an evil man!"'"'
lessons: The appearance of great effort or sacrifice does not guarantee sincerity; true faith is an internal reality that only God can judge.
God's plan encompasses even those who act with wrong intentions — goodness can emerge from imperfect or even corrupt instruments.
The Prophet's insight into spiritual realities reminds believers to trust divine wisdom over human appearances.
practical_advice: Be cautious about judging others' faith or worth based solely on their outward actions or apparent sacrifices.
When confused by contradictions between someone's behaviour and their outcome, trust that ultimate justice belongs to God alone.
Focus on nurturing genuine sincerity in your own heart rather than performing faith for others' approval.

title: The Black Slave of Khaybar — Faith and Martyrdom Within One Hour
summary: A black Abyssinian slave in Khaybar accepted Islam moments before battle and was killed as a martyr, having never once prostrated in prayer, yet the Prophet testified that God honoured him and saw two houris of paradise at his head.
story: Musa b. Uqba narrated from al-Zuhri the story of the black slave upon whom God bestowed faith and martyrdom within the same hour. Ibn Lahia narrated it similarly from Abu al-Aswad, from Urwa. Both stated, "There was a black Abyssinian slave who lived in Khaybar who cared for a herd belonging to his master. When he saw that the people of Khaybar were taking up their weapons, he asked them, 'What are you going to do?' 'We are going to do battle with that man who claims to be a prophet.' "This reference to a prophet had an impact upon him and he took his herd and approached the Messenger of God (SAAS), asking him, 'To what do you invite?' He replied, 'I invite you to Islam, to bear witness that there is no god but God, that I am the Messenger of God, and that you will not worship any other than God.' The slave then asked, 'What will I get if I bear witness to that and believe in God?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'Paradise, if you die believing that.' "The slave accepted Islam and said, 'Prophet of God, this herd I have is my security.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Drive it out of our camp and throw stones at it. God will discharge your security from you Himself.' He did so, and the herd returned to its Jewish master, who realized that his slave had accepted Islam. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then arose and addressed his men." He went on to relate the hadith of him having given the banner to 'Ali, the approach of the latter to the fort of the Jews, and his killing of Marhab. (The account states) "Also killed (while) with 'Ali was that black slave, who was borne by the Muslims to their camp and brought into the main tent. They claim that the Messenger of God (SAAS) looked inside the tent, then turned to his Companions and commented, 'God has honoured this slave and led him to good. Islam was truly within his heart. At his head I saw two of the houris of paradise.'" The hadith al-Bayhaqi narrated through Ibn Wahb, from Haywa b. Shurayh, from Ibn al-Hadd, from Shurahbil b. Sa'd, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, who said, "We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the expedition to Khaybar. A raiding party went out and they captured a man who was shepherding a herd." He went on to relate roughly the same story of the black slave and said of him, "He died a martyr and had not made a single prayer prostration to God!"
lessons: God's mercy is not constrained by social status, race, or length of practice — sincere faith at any moment is honoured.
True sincerity of heart matters more to God than accumulated religious ritual when the heart is genuine.
The willingness to surrender worldly security for faith is itself an act of profound trust that God does not leave unrewarded.
practical_advice: Do not disqualify yourself from God's mercy because of a past without prayer or religious practice — sincerity in the present moment has immense value.
When facing a moment of spiritual awakening, act on it promptly rather than waiting for a 'better time' to commit.
Let go of material securities you are clinging to when they conflict with your values — trust that God will provide another way.

title: The Man Who Asked for Martyrdom and Received It — Keeping Trust with God
summary: A Bedouin who followed the Prophet seeking martyrdom rather than booty was struck by an arrow exactly where he had pointed and died, and the Prophet testified that he kept trust with God and God kept trust with him.
story: Al-Bayhaqi then narrated through Ibn Jurayj as follows, "'Ikrima b. Khalid informed me, from Ibn Abi 'Amra, from Shaddad b. al-Hadd, that a bedouin came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), expressed his belief in him and followed him, saying, 'I will go forth with you.' The Prophet (SAAS) gave him into the charge of one of his Companions. At Khaybar the Messenger of God (SAAS) received booty and divided it up, including a portion for this man which he gave to the Companions of this man for he had been guarding their rear. When he came to them they gave it to him. He asked, 'What is this?' They replied, 'The portion assigned to you by the Messenger of God (SAAS).' He responded, 'It was not for this that I followed you! I followed you so that I might be shot right here.' And he pointed with an arrow at his throat. 'So I would die and enter heaven!' He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) then said, 'If you keep trust with God, he will keep trust with you.' "They then arose to battle the enemy. Later he was carried in to the Messenger of God (SAAS); he had been struck with an arrow where he had pointed! The Prophet (SAAS) then asked, 'That is really him?' 'Yes,' they replied. He then said, 'He kept trust with God, so God kept trust with him.' "The Prophet (SAAS) then gave his own gown for him to be wrapped in, laid out his body in front and prayed over him. Part of his prayer that is known includes, 'O God, this is your servant; he went forth in your cause and was killed a martyr. I bear witness for him.'" Al-Nasa'i related this from Suwayd b. Nasr, from 'Abd Allah b. al-Mubarak, from Ibn Jurayj, in similar words.
lessons: Sincerity of purpose — seeking no worldly gain — is the highest form of devotion and is honoured by God in kind.
When a person aligns their deepest intention with trust in God, divine fulfillment can come in ways that are precise and unmistakable.
True disinterest in worldly reward, combined with sincere spiritual aspiration, is a quality that earns the highest testimony.
practical_advice: Examine your motivations when pursuing spiritual or charitable actions — ask yourself honestly whether you are seeking worldly recognition or something deeper.
Practice trusting God with your most sincere aspirations; articulate them clearly in prayer and then act with consistency.
When someone close to you acts with pure sincerity, honour and acknowledge that — your recognition of their character is itself a form of gratitude.

title: The Man with No Wealth or Beauty Who Sought Paradise Through Martyrdom
summary: A man who described himself as black, ugly, and owning nothing asked the Prophet if martyrdom would grant him paradise; after he was killed, the Prophet declared that God had made his face handsome, sweetened his smell, and increased his wealth.
story: Al-Bayhaqi then stated that Muhammad b. Muhammad b. Muhammad al-Faqih related to him, quoting Abu Bakr al-Qattan, quoting Abu al-Azhar, quoting Musa b. Isma'il, quoting Hammad, quoting Thabit, from Anas, that a man came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, "Messenger of God, I'm a man who is black in colour, ugly of face and who owns nothing. If I fight those people until I'm killed, will I enter paradise?" "Yes," he replied. (Anas's account continues that) "the man then went forth and battled until he was killed. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was brought to his body, he said, 'God has made your face handsome, sweetened your smell and increased your wealth.' Then he said, 'I saw his two wives, houris, competing to take off his gown; they were entering the space between his skin and his gown.'"
lessons: God's measure of worth is entirely different from society's standards of beauty, wealth, or status — the sincerely devoted soul is honoured beyond all worldly measure.
Those who feel marginalized, overlooked, or without worldly advantage are not excluded from divine dignity and reward.
A single sincere question asked from genuine faith can open a door to the highest spiritual possibility.
practical_advice: If you struggle with feelings of low self-worth due to appearance, poverty, or social standing, remember that these are not God's criteria for your value.
When feelings of hopelessness arise around your circumstances, redirect the energy into purposeful action aligned with your values.
Speak honestly to God about your insecurities and limitations — that honesty itself is a form of prayer.

title: The Conquest of Khaybar Fort by Fort — Death of Mahmad b. Maslama
summary: The Prophet conquered Khaybar's forts one by one; during the conquest of the fort of Na'im, Mahmad b. Maslama was killed when a millstone was thrown down upon him.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then took possession of the properties one by one as he conquered one fort after the next. The first of their forts conquered was Na'im. There Mahmad b. Maslama was killed; a millstone was tossed down on him and it killed him. The next fort was al-Qamus, that of Banu Abu al-Haqiq."
lessons: Even the most righteous and experienced companions are not immune from sudden loss — life's fragility is a constant reminder to live with purpose.
Each step of a long and difficult journey carries its own cost; perseverance in the face of loss is itself a form of courage.
Grief over fallen companions is natural and honourable; it need not be suppressed but channelled into continued commitment.
practical_advice: When a long effort brings unexpected losses alongside its gains, allow yourself to grieve properly rather than pushing past grief in pursuit of the next goal.
Honour the memory of those who gave much in causes you care about — their sacrifice deepens the meaning of your own contribution.
Remind yourself that difficulty in a worthy endeavour does not mean the endeavour is wrong; it may mean the cost is simply real.

title: Safiyya bint Huyayy Taken Captive at Khaybar and Chosen by the Prophet
summary: Among the captives taken at Khaybar was Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy b. Akhtab and wife of Kinana b. al-Rabi'; the Prophet chose her for himself and gave her two cousins to Dihya b. Khalifa.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) acquired captives from them. These captives included Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy b. Akhab. She had been the wife of Kinana b. al-Rabi' b. Abu al-Haqiq. Also captured were two of her female cousins. The Messenger of God (SAAS) chose Safiyya for himself; Dihya b. Khalifa had asked him for her; when the Messenger of God (SAAS) chose her for himself, he gave to Dihya her two cousins. The captives taken at Khaybar were distributed among the Muslims.
lessons: Historical accounts of war and its aftermath — including captivity — must be understood within their own time and context, not anachronistically judged.
The status of individuals can change dramatically through the events of history; what matters most is how one responds to circumstances beyond their control.
Even in radically altered circumstances, dignity and identity are internal qualities that no external event can entirely remove.
practical_advice: When facing drastic and unwanted changes in your life circumstances, focus on what remains within your control — your response, your values, your inner orientation.
Study difficult historical events with their full context before forming judgments; context transforms understanding.
In times of personal upheaval, hold onto your sense of identity and worth as something that does not depend on your external situation.

title: Prohibition of Eating Donkey Meat at Khaybar
summary: At Khaybar the Muslims were eating domestic donkey flesh; the Prophet issued a public proclamation forbidding it, declaring it unclean, while permitting the eating of horsemeat.
story: The men were eating the flesh of donkeys, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) issued a proclamation against their doing this. Al-Bukhari devoted his attention to this and reported the prohibition from excellent sources. Its prohibition has been accepted by most scholars both early and later; all four of the "orthodox Imams" prohibited its consumption. However, some early authorities, including Ibn 'Abbas, maintained that eating it was permissible. These scholars offered varying reasons for discounting the ahadith that have come down prohibiting it. For example, it was suggested that the prohibition came because they were using the donkeys for transport. Another reason given was that the prohibition related to the fact that the donkeys were booty that had not yet been divided up into the (required) fifths. It is also suggested that it related to the donkeys eating excrement, dung that is. The truth is that it was prohibited because of its nature. Authentic evidences relate that the crier of the Messenger of God (SAAS) called out, "God and His prophet prohibit your eating the meat of donkeys; it is unclean." Ibn Ishaq stated, "Sallam b. Karkara related to me, from 'Amr b. Dinar, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah — Jabir himself not having been present at Khaybar — that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) prohibited people from consuming donkey meat, he also allowed them to eat horsemeat." The provenance of this is secure in both Sahih collections, from a hadith of Hammad b. Zayd, from 'Amr b. Dinar, from Muhammad b. 'Ali, from Jabir, may God be pleased with him. He said, "At Khaybar, the Messenger of God (SAAS) prohibited eating the meat of donkeys and gave permission for horses (to be eaten)." This is the phraseology given by al-Bukhari. Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Abd Allah b. Abi Najih related to us, from Makhul, that the Prophet (SAAS) prohibited four things that day: intercourse with pregnant women, eating domestic donkeys, eating carnivorous animals, and selling the spoils of war."
lessons: Religious guidance sometimes comes in moments of need precisely to protect individuals and communities from harm they may not fully recognise.
Scholarly disagreement on secondary matters is a sign of intellectual vitality within a tradition, not a sign of confusion or weakness.
Following established communal guidance — even when personal preference might resist it — is an act of trust in a wisdom larger than one's own.
practical_advice: When you encounter a rule or boundary that feels inconvenient, pause to consider the reasoning behind it rather than immediately resisting it.
In matters where scholars or trusted authorities disagree, seek the most careful and evidence-based position rather than simply choosing the most convenient one.
Recognise that community standards and ethical guidelines often protect us from harms we are too close to see clearly — trust the framework even when you don't fully understand every detail.

title: Prayer for the Conquest of Fort al-Sa'b at Khaybar
summary: The tribe of Banu Sahm of Aslam came to the Prophet saying they had fought hard but had nothing. The Prophet prayed to God to open the wealthiest fortress for them, and the next morning the fort of al-Sa'b b. Mu'adh — the wealthiest in Khaybar — was conquered.
story: 'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr related to me that it was related to him by someone of Aslam that Banu Sahm of Aslam came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, 'Messenger of God, we have fought hard and yet we have nothing.' They had not found the Messenger of God (SAAS) able to give them anything. He then said, 'O God, You well know their state and that they have no strength and that I have nothing to give them. Open up before them the wealthiest of their fortresses and give them sufficiency mainly in fats and food.' Next morning the fort of al-Sa'b b. Mu'adh was conquered for them. And no fort in Khaybar was wealthier or had richer food than it.
lessons: When human means are exhausted, turning sincerely to God in supplication is itself a powerful act of leadership and trust.
Acknowledging one's limitations honestly before God is not weakness — it is the foundation of true reliance upon Him.
God responds to the sincere needs of those who struggle in His path, often in ways that exceed what could have been arranged by human planning.
practical_advice: When you have done everything within your power and still feel helpless, voice your need to God directly and specifically, as the Prophet did.
If you are responsible for others who are struggling, make du'a on their behalf — interceding for those in your care is a form of compassionate leadership.
When relief comes after a period of difficulty, pause and acknowledge that it came from God, reinforcing gratitude and strengthening your trust for future hardships.

title: Abu al-Yusr Catches Two Sheep for the Prophet at Khaybar
summary: The companion Abu al-Yusr Ka'b b. 'Amr volunteered to fetch sheep for the Prophet from a passing Jewish flock during the siege of Khaybar, sprinting to catch two ewes and carrying them back as though weightless. He later wept when recounting this story, saying he wished they had been given his whole life to enjoy him.
story: Burdayda b. Sufyan al-Azdi al-Aslami related to me, from some men of Banu Salama, from Abu al-Yusr Ka'b b. 'Amr, who said, 'I was with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Khaybar one evening when a herd of sheep belonging to a Jew passed by, heading for their forts which we were besieging. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, "Which man will feed us from these sheep?" I replied, "I will, Messenger of God!" "Then do so," he told me. So I went out, racing as hard as I could, like an ostrich. When he saw me do this, and was about to head back, he exclaimed, "O God, let us enjoy him long!" When I had reached the sheep, the first ones had already entered their fort. But I caught two ewes from their rear, tucked them under my arms and sped back, carrying them as if I bore nothing. I threw them down before the Messenger of God (SAAS), and they slaughtered and ate them both.' Abu al-Yusr was one of the last of the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to die. He would weep when telling this hadith, and then say, 'Would they were given my whole life to enjoy; I was the last of them.'
lessons: The grief of outliving those you love most is one of the most profound human experiences — it is not a sign of weakness but of the depth of love.
The Prophet's supplication for Abu al-Yusr — that he be enjoyed long — was a blessing that also carried the weight of being the last survivor, showing that God's gifts are complex.
Cherishing moments of service to those we love is a source of lifelong meaning, even when those people are gone.
practical_advice: When you feel the grief of loss or of being 'left behind', honor the memory of those you loved by recounting the good you shared together, as Abu al-Yusr did even through tears.
Volunteer for acts of service to those around you, no matter how small — these moments become the memories that sustain you across a lifetime.
If you are grieving or lonely after the loss of community or loved ones, know that your longevity can itself be a form of honor and trust placed in you by God.

title: The Prophet Heals Fever-Struck Companions at Khaybar with Cooled Water
summary: When the Muslim army advanced on Khaybar and men ate unripe fruit and fell feverish, the Prophet instructed them to cool water in pots, pour it over themselves at dawn while invoking God's name. They did so and were cured as if their ailment had been suddenly unbound.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) advanced on Khaybar, the fruit was unripe. But the men raced for it, became feverish and complained to him of this. He told them to cool water in pots and then to have this poured over them when dawn came and to repeat the name of God. They did this and it was as though they had been hobbled and then had their cords removed.
lessons: Physical hardship is often an invitation to combine practical remedy with spiritual remembrance — the two are not in opposition.
The Prophet consistently addressed both the physical and spiritual dimensions of his companions' suffering, modeling holistic care.
Invoking God's name during difficulty is not mere ritual — it is a reorientation of the heart toward the source of all healing.
practical_advice: When you are unwell or overwhelmed, combine practical self-care (rest, hydration, physical relief) with intentional remembrance of God — say 'Bismillah' or another dhikr as you take your remedy.
Do not neglect the body when addressing spiritual struggle, nor neglect the spirit when addressing physical struggle — well-being is whole.
If anxiety or stress feels like a fever you cannot shake, try the practice of grounding: cool water, slow breath, and a sincere invocation of God's name at the start of each day.

title: Ibn Mughaffal and the Bag of Lard at Khaybar
summary: During the siege at Khaybar, the companion 'Abd Allah b. Mughaffal seized a leather bag of lard as his share of booty and clutched it possessively, then turned to find the Prophet smiling at him — a moment that filled him with both shame and warmth.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Yahya and Bahz both related to him as follows: Sulayman b. al-Mughira related to us, quoting Humayd b. Hilal, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Mughaffal, who said, 'My share of the booty at Khaybar was a leather bag of lard. I gripped it tight and said, "I'll not give any of this to anyone!" Then I turned and saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) smiling.' Ahmad said, "'Affan related to us, quoting Shu'ba, from Hamid b. Hilal, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Mughaffal, who said, 'While we were besieging the fortress at Khaybar, someone threw down at us a leather bag containing lard. So I went and took it. Then I saw the Prophet (SAAS), and I felt ashamed.'"
lessons: The Prophet's response to human weakness was often a smile rather than rebuke — compassion is the most effective teacher.
Feeling ashamed before a person of noble character is itself a sign of a living conscience and moral awareness.
Our instinct toward self-preservation and acquisition is natural, but awareness of a higher standard invites us to rise above it.
practical_advice: When you catch yourself acting out of pure self-interest or hoarding, do not spiral into harsh self-judgment — let the recognition itself be the turning point, as it was for Ibn Mughaffal.
Practice the gentle self-correction that the Prophet modeled: sometimes a smile at someone's all-too-human behavior is kinder and more effective than a lecture.
If you feel embarrassed about a lapse in generosity or character, use that feeling as fuel to give or share something today — transform the guilt into action.

title: The Leather Bag of Lard at Khaybar
summary: During the siege of Khaybar, Abd Allah ibn Mughaffal seized a leather bag of lard from the fortress and refused to surrender it to the booty-master. The Prophet smiled and laughed at the dispute, then told the booty-master to let him keep it.
story:
at Khaybar was a leather bag of lard. I gripped it tight and said, "I'll not give any of this to any one!" Then I turned and saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) smiling.'"
Ahmad said, "'Affan related to us, quoting Shu'ba, from Hamid b. Hild, quoting 'Abd All* b. Mughaffal, who said, 'While we were besieging the fortress at Khaybar, someone threw down at us a leather bag containing lard. So I went and took it. Then I saw the Prophet (SAAS), and I felt ashamed.'"
Both compilers of the ~ahihcollections gave it from a hadith of Shu'ba. Muslim also related it, from Shaybm b. Fariikh, from Wthmm b. al-Mughira. Ibn Ishaq stated, "Someone whose word I do not doubt related to me from 'Abd Allah b. Mughaffal al-Mazani as follows, 'At Khaybar, I captured a leather bag of lard. I carried it around my neck and went off to my mount and my companions. The man placed in charge of the booty then came up to me and grabbed the side of it, saying, "Come on! Not till it's divided up among the Muslims." I replied, "No, by God, I'll not give it to you!" He then began pulling the hag away from me. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw us while we were behaving this way, and he smiled and laughed. He then told the booty-master, "Let him have it." So he released it and I went off with it to my mount and to my companions and we ate it.'" Most scholars refer to this hadith in relation to Imam Malik's prohibition of the use of the fat from animals sacrificed by Jews and captured by Muslims. This is because God Almighty stated, "and the food of those to whom the Book came is permissible for you" (sarat al-Md'ida; V, v.5). He (Imm Malik) said, 'LThisis not food for them (the Jews)." (His opponents, however,) cite this had& against him. Nevertheless, there are doubts about this hadith. This lard could well have been permitted to them. But God knows best. This hadith was also cited to show that food is not subject to the division into fifths. This is supported by what Iman Aha Da'iid related, as follows, "Muhammad h. al-'A12 related to us, quoting Abn Mu'zwiya, quoting I s h ~ q al-Shaybmi, from Muhammad h. Aba Mujdid, from 'Abd Allah h. Ahti Awf~, who said, 'I asked, "Didn't you divide up (captured) food into the fifths during the time of the Messenger of God (SAAS)?" He (sac) replied, "We did capture food at Khaybar. People would come and take of it what they needed, then leave."'" Aha Da'ad is alone in giving this; the hadith is hasan, "good".
lessons: The Prophet's gentle humor and leniency in matters of human weakness reflects a leadership style rooted in compassion rather than rigid enforcement.
Feeling embarrassment when one's lower impulses are witnessed by a righteous person is itself a sign of a living conscience and moral awareness.
Scholarly differences in interpreting religious law are a natural part of the tradition; uncertainty calls for humility rather than dogmatism.
practical_advice: When you catch yourself acting out of greed or selfishness, use the feeling of embarrassment as a prompt for reflection rather than self-condemnation.
Cultivate a sense of humor about your own human imperfections; the Prophet's smile reminds us that not every mistake requires a harsh response.
In disputes over possessions or fairness, ask yourself whether the thing you are holding onto so tightly is truly worth the conflict it causes.

title: Safiyya's Dream and Her Marriage to the Prophet
summary: Safiyya bint Huyayy, a Jewish woman of noble lineage, saw a prophetic dream before the conquest of Khaybar in which the moon fell into her lap. Her husband struck her for it. After Khaybar fell, her husband was killed, and the Prophet chose her, freed her, and married her, honoring her with his cloak and a wedding banquet.
story:
An Account of the Story of Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy b. Akhtub al-Nadriyya, may God be pleased with her. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) forced the Jews of Bana al-Nadir to vacate Medina, as was related above, most of them went to Khaybar. Their number included Huyayy h. Akhruh and Bana Aha al-Huqayq, who were persons of wealth and prestige among their people. Safiyya at that time was a child, not yet having reached maturity. Thereafter, when she was ready for marriage, it was arranged to her cousin. When she was conducted to him after the wedding, he consummated the marriage. Their married life continued for some time. Then in her sleep she saw the moon descend from the sky in her lap. She related this vision to her cousin, who slapped her face and said, "So you yearn to have the 'king of Yathrih' as your husband, do you?" Soon thereafter the arrival and siege of them by the Messenger of God (SAAS) occurred and Safiyya was among those captured, while her husband was kiied. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) chose her and she became part of his household, to which she now belonged, as will be related. After ascertaining that she was not pregnant and not menstruating he consummated mamage with her and discovered the mark made on her cheek by her husband's blow. He asked her about this, and she related to him what she had seen in those wonderful visions. May God he pleased with her and grant her pleasure.
Al-Bukhai stated that Sulayman h. Harh related to him, quoting Hammsd b. Zayd, from Thshit, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) performed the al-subh, morning prayer, while it was still dark, near Khaybar. He then said, 'Alhhu Akbar! God is most Great! Khaybar is ruined! It's a bad day for an enemy who has been warned when we can dismount in their open square!' "Then the enemy hurried to escape along the alleyways and the Prophet (SAAS) k i e d their warriors and took their children captive. Among the prisoners was Safiyya. She went to Dihya al-Kalbi, and then to the Prophet (SAAS), who gave her her freedom as her dowry." Muslim also related this from a hadith of Hammad h. Zayd which has several lines of transmission from Anas.
Al-Bukhzri stated that Adam related to h i , from Shu'ba, from CAbd al-CAziz b. Suhayb, who said, "I heard Anas b. Malik say, 'The Prophet (SAAS) took Safiyya captive and then gave her her freedom and married her.' Thiibit asked Anas, 'Did he not give her a dowry?' He replied, 'He gave her herself as dowry, rewarding her her freedom.'"
Al-Bukhzri stated that 'Abd al-Ghaffar b. Da'iid related to him, quoting Ya'qiib b. 'Ahd al-Rahmm, as did Ahmad h. %a, quoting Wahh (who said), "Yacqab b. 'Abd al-Ra!mitn al-Zuhri informed me, from CAmr, the freed-man of al-Mugalib, from Anas b. Maik, who said, 'We came to Khaybar. After the Messenger of God (SAAS) had conquered the fortress, mention was made to h i of the beauty of Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy b. Akhcub. Her husband had been killed, she still being a bride. The Prophet (SAAS) then chose her for himself. He left with her, travelling in her company until he reached Sudd al-Sahbal. She was then free of her menses and so the Messenger of God (SAAS) consummated his marriage with her. Thereafter, he made some ,hays, dates with butter, on a small leather mat. Then he told me, "Make an invitation to those around you." And this was the wedding banquet given for Safiyya. We then left for Medina. I saw the Prophet (SAAS) make a cushion behind himself for her with his cloak and would then sit upon his mount. He would bend hi knee and she would put her foot on it, then mount up.'"
Al-Bukhzri stated that Sacid b. Abii Maryam related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Jacfar b. Aba Kathir (who said), "Humayd narrated to me that he heard Anas say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed between Khaybar and al-Madd for three nights, Safiyya being brought to him for consummation of their marriage. I invited the Muslims to his wedding banquet. There was no bread or meat; all he did was order BiM to get some leather dishes in which he threw some dates, sour milk cheese and butter. The Muslims asked, "Is she to be considered one of the ummah~tal-mu'mmin, 'mothers of the believers', or someone taken captive?" They replied, "If he keeps her veiled, then she is one of the former; if not, then of the latter." When he rode on, he placed her behind himself and put a veil over her.'"
Abn Daliid stated that Musaddad related to him, quoting Hammad b. Zayd, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Suhayb, from Anas b. Malik, who said, "Safiyya went to Dihya al-Kalbi, and then to the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Aha DPad also stated, "Ya'qiib b. Ibrahim related to us, saying, 'Ibn 'Aliyya related to us, from (Abd al-'Aziz b. Suhayb, from Anas, who said, "The captives were gathered" -meaning at Khaybar - "and D&ya arrived and said, 'Messenger of God, award me a slave girl from amongst the captives.' He replied, 'Go along and select one.' He selected Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy. Then a man came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, 'Prophet of God, you gave to Dihya' (Ya'qiib added, 'Safiyya, daughter of Huyay, a lady of Qurayza and &Nadir7) 'someone for whom only you are appropriate.' He replied, 'Have her brought.' When the Prophet (SAAS) saw her, he said, 'Take a slave girl from the captives other than her.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then set her free and married her."
Abn Da'ad stated that Muhammad b. Khallad al-B%hili related to him, quoting Bahz h. Asad, quoting Hammad b. Salama, quoting Thabit, from Anas, who said, "A lovely slave girl fell to the lot of Dihya, hut the Messenger of God (SAAS) bought her for a price of seven head of cattle. He then gave her over to Umm Salama to prepare her." H m d (b. Salama) said, "And I believe he said, 'And she was readied in her house, Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy.'"
Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) conquered al-@Us, the fort of Bann Abii al-Huqayq, brought to h i were Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy h. A k h ~ band another girl. Bilal, who had brought them both, had gone past some of the Jews who had been killed. When the woman with Safiyya saw them she screamed, slapped her face and poured dirt over her head. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw her, he said, 'Get that she-devil away from me!' He ordered Safiyya to be brought to him and she was placed behimd him and he threw his cloak over her. And so the Muslims knew that he had chosen her for himself. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told Bild, so I have been informed, after having seen how that Jewess had behaved, 'Have you lost your sense of compassion, Bilal, taking the two women past their dead menfolk!' "While still the bride of Kinana b. al-Rabic b. Abii al-Huqayq, Safiyya had seen a vision in her sleep that a moon dropped into her lap. She told her husband of her vision and he replied, 'That's only because you desire the "king of Hija", Muhammad!' He then gave her a slap on her face that blackened her eye. The mark of this was still upon her when she was brought to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He asked her how it had happened, and she told him."
lessons: Dreams and inner visions can carry profound meaning; what others mock or punish us for may in fact be a pointer toward our true destiny.
Dignity and honor can be restored even after great loss and humiliation — Safiyya moved from captivity and grief to the highest station among the believers.
True compassion considers the emotional suffering of others even in the midst of war, as shown by the Prophet's rebuke of Bilal for exposing the women to the sight of their dead.
practical_advice: When you have experienced humiliation or been punished for your hopes and dreams, hold on to them quietly — time may reveal their worth.
In your darkest moments of loss, remember that your current circumstances do not define your final destination; trust that God's plan for you extends beyond what you can currently see.
When handling situations involving others' grief, pause to consider how your actions might affect their emotional state, and choose the path of compassion even when it is inconvenient.

title: Bilal Rebuked for Lacking Compassion at Khaybar
summary: After the conquest of Khaybar, Bilal led Safiyya and another woman past the bodies of their slain kinsmen. The Prophet rebuked him sharply, asking whether he had lost his sense of compassion.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) conquered al-@Us, the fort of Bann Abii al-Huqayq, brought to h i were Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy h. A k h ~ band another girl. Bilal, who had brought them both, had gone past some of the Jews who had been killed. When the woman with Safiyya saw them she screamed, slapped her face and poured dirt over her head. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw her, he said, 'Get that she-devil away from me!' He ordered Safiyya to be brought to him and she was placed behimd him and he threw his cloak over her. And so the Muslims knew that he had chosen her for himself. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told Bild, so I have been informed, after having seen how that Jewess had behaved, 'Have you lost your sense of compassion, Bilal, taking the two women past their dead menfolk!'"
lessons: Even in the context of military victory, the Prophet insisted on the humane treatment of prisoners and considered their emotional suffering, showing that compassion is not suspended during hardship.
A person of great virtue like Bilal could still make an error in judgment; receiving correction gracefully is part of moral growth.
The grief of those who have lost loved ones deserves respect regardless of the circumstances; witnessing the suffering of others should awaken our empathy, not our indifference.
practical_advice: When you are focused on a task or goal, periodically pause and ask yourself whether you are inadvertently causing unnecessary pain to those around you.
If you receive a rebuke from someone you respect, resist the urge to defend yourself immediately; sit with the correction and allow it to refine your character.
Consider the emotional weight that others carry — especially those in grief — and adjust your behavior accordingly rather than proceeding on autopilot.

title: Kinana ibn al-Rabi and the Hidden Treasury of Banu al-Nadir
summary: After the conquest of Khaybar, the Prophet questioned Kinana ibn al-Rabi about the hidden treasury of Banu al-Nadir. When Kinana denied knowledge but was caught by a witness's testimony, he was executed by Muhammad ibn Maslama in revenge for the killing of his brother.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) had Kinma h. al-Rabi' brought to him; he was in charge of the Bana al-Nadir treasury. When asked about it, Kinana denied knowledge of its whereabouts. Then there was brought to the Messenger of God (SAAS) a Jew who said, 'I saw Kinsna circle around the treasure early every morning.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said to Kinana, 'Do you realize that I'll kill you if we find it with you?' 'Yes,' he replied. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered that their treasure be dug up and some of it was brought out. He then asked Kinma the whereabouts of the rest, but he refused to hand it over. And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered al-Zuhayr b. al-'Awam to go to him, saying, 'Torture him until you root out what he has.' Al-Zubayr then repeatedly struck fire on his chest until he was close to death. Finally, the Messenger of God (SAAS) pushed him over to Muhammad b. Maslama, who cut off his head in revenge for the loss"
lessons: Deception and the concealment of trust carry severe consequences; honesty, even when difficult, is ultimately the wiser and safer path.
The events of history — including those involving violence and harsh judgment — must be understood within their historical and legal context rather than judged by anachronistic standards.
Accountability for one's actions is inescapable; the truth will surface regardless of how carefully one attempts to hide it.
practical_advice: When tempted to conceal the truth to protect a personal interest, weigh the short-term benefit against the long-term cost — deception rarely remains hidden indefinitely.
In moments of confrontation or high stakes, choose transparency and honesty even if the immediate consequences seem frightening.
Reflect on whether there are areas in your own life where you are 'hiding a treasury' — withholding honesty from yourself or others — and take steps to bring those things into the open.

title: Torture and Execution of Kinana ibn al-Rabi for Concealing Khaybar's Treasure
summary: The Prophet ordered the torture of Kinana ibn al-Rabi to extract information about Khaybar's hidden treasure, after which Kinana was executed by Muhammad ibn Maslama in revenge for his brother's death.
story: he replied. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered that their treasure be dug up and some of it was brought out. He then asked Kinma the whereabouts of the rest, but he refused to hand it over. And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered al-Zuhayr b. al-'Awam to go to him, saying, 'Torture him until you root out what he has.' Al-Zubayr then repeatedly struck fire on his chest until he was close to death. Finally, the Messenger of God (SAAS) pushed him over to Muhammad b. Maslama, who cut off his head in revenge for the loss of his brother, Mahmud b. Maslama.
lessons: Breaking trust and concealing truth from those in authority leads to severe consequences.
Accountability for one's actions is inevitable, even when deception seems successful in the short term.
Loyalty and justice, even in grief, are qualities that define righteous character — Muhammad ibn Maslama's act was recognized as just retribution.
practical_advice: When you are tempted to hide wrongdoing, reflect on the eventual cost of deception versus the difficulty of honesty.
If you carry grief or a desire for justice, seek resolution through legitimate means rather than letting resentment fester.
Trust that God sees what others conceal; release the burden of seeking to expose every wrong yourself.

title: The Surrender of Khaybar's Last Forts and the Peace Agreement
summary: After prolonged siege, the people of Khaybar's final forts al-Watih and al-Sulalim surrendered, negotiating to spare their lives in exchange for leaving their possessions, and the people of Fadak followed suit upon hearing of this outcome.
story: Ibn Ishsq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) besieged the people of Khaybar in their two forts al-Waph and al-Sul~lim. When they were sure that all was lost, they asked him to let them leave, and to spare their blood. He did so. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had seized all the wealth of al-Shiqq, d-Nitat and d-Katiba, and all their forts, except for those two. When the people at Fadak heard what had happened to the others, they wanted the same. So they sent to the Messenger of God (SAAS), asking him to let them leave and to spare their blood on the understanding that they would leave their possessions for him. He agreed. One of the intermediaries between the Messenger of God (SAAS) and them in this matter was Muhayyi~ab. Masciid, brother of Bana HGtha."
lessons: Recognizing when resistance is futile and choosing peace is a sign of wisdom, not weakness.
Mercy extended even to adversaries reflects the noble character of just leadership.
Intermediaries and peaceful negotiation can bring about outcomes that save lives on both sides.
practical_advice: When facing an overwhelming challenge, honestly assess your situation and consider whether negotiating or stepping back serves the greater good.
If you find yourself in conflict, look for trusted mediators who can help reach a resolution with dignity.
Accepting a difficult outcome gracefully, rather than prolonging unnecessary struggle, can be an act of wisdom and self-preservation.

title: The Jews of Khaybar Permitted to Farm Their Lands on Half-Share Basis
summary: After surrendering, the Jews of Khaybar requested to remain and farm their lands in exchange for half the produce, and the Prophet agreed, establishing a sharecropping arrangement that benefited both the Muslim community and the remaining inhabitants.
story: "When the people of Khaybar surrendered on this agreement, they asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) to grant them a half share in the properties, saying, 'We know more about it than you do, and we'll make it produce more.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) made peace with them on this basis of half shares, provided that, 'If we wish to expel you, we will do so.' He made a similar arrangement with the people of Fadak."
lessons: Practical wisdom sometimes means working with those who were once adversaries for mutual benefit.
Flexibility and pragmatism in leadership allow for arrangements that serve the community's needs.
Agreements built on clear, stated conditions protect all parties and prevent future misunderstanding.
practical_advice: When resolving a conflict, seek arrangements that allow both parties to move forward constructively rather than prolonging antagonism.
Be clear about the terms of any agreement you enter, including the conditions under which it may change.
Recognize the skills and knowledge others bring, even those with whom you have disagreed, and use them wisely.

title: The Informant 'Azd and the Cutting of Khaybar's Underground Springs
summary: A Jewish man named 'Azd revealed to the Prophet that the besieged people of al-Natat were sustained by hidden underground springs; acting on this intelligence, the Prophet ordered the springs cut off, forcing the defenders into open battle.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "When the Jews from the forts of Nscim and of that of al-Sa'b h. Musdh went to that of al-Zubayr, the Messenger of God (SAAS) besieged them for three days. Then one of the Jews, a man named 'Azd, came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, 'Abii al-@sim, will you give me protection if I direct you to what refreshes the people of al-Napt and then proceed against those of al-Shiqq? Those at al-Shiqq are deathly afraid of you.' He went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) did give this man protection for his family and possessions, and the Jew then told him, "If you were to stay a whole month besieging them, they would not care. They have underground springs to which they can go out at night and drink from and then return to their fortress." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then gave orders that their springs be cut off and the Jews came out and fought most fiercely. A number of the Muslims were killed that day, as were ten of the Jews. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then conquered it."
lessons: Acting on reliable intelligence, even from unexpected sources, can be decisive in resolving a prolonged struggle.
Offering protection and honoring it, even to those who were once enemies, demonstrates integrity and builds trust.
Strategic thinking — addressing the root of a problem rather than its symptoms — leads to more decisive outcomes.
practical_advice: When you are stuck in a prolonged difficulty, look for new information or perspectives you may have overlooked that could change your approach.
If someone comes to you with useful truth, honor the trust they placed in you by fulfilling your commitments to them.
Address the source of a problem directly rather than only managing its surface manifestations.

title: The Battle for the Forts of al-Shiqq: Single Combat and the Fall of Samwu
summary: During the campaign against the forts of al-Shiqq, fierce single combat took place between Muslim and Jewish warriors, culminating in Abu Dujana's victories and the capture of the fortress of Samwu.
story: "The last of these forts (to fall) was al-Natat. He then turned his attention to al-Shiqq, which consisted of several forts. The first one he attacked was Uhayy. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then attacked a castle named Samwu and he fought for it most vigorously. One of the enemy there, a man named CAzul, came forth and made a challenge for single combat. Al-Habsb b. al-Mundhir went out to fight him and cut off half his arm, the man's sword dropping from his hand. The Jew fled, but al-Habsb followed him and severed his tendon. Another of them then emerged for single combat and one of the Muslims went forth to meet him. The Jew killed him. Abii Dujana then went out against him, killing him and seizing his weapons. After that they offered no more challenges for single combat and the Muslims shouted 'Allahu Akbar! God is most Great!' They then mounted an attack on the fortress and entered it, led by Aba Dujana. Inside they found furniture, furnishings, cattle and food. The fighting men who had been inside fled in great haste, like lizards, until they reached the forts al-Bazat, at al-Shiqq. There they put up a strong defence; but the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his men moved slowly towards them and they exchanged arrows. The Messenger of God (SAAS) actually shot at them in person until their arrows wounded his fingertips. He then picked up a handful of stones which he cast at their fort which shook under their impact and collapsed to the ground. The Muslims then defeated them in hand-to-hand combat."
lessons: Courage in the face of danger, even when others have fallen, is a quality that turns the tide for a community.
Leadership that participates alongside followers — even accepting personal injury — inspires extraordinary commitment.
Perseverance through setbacks, including the loss of a companion, does not mean abandoning the mission.
practical_advice: When someone around you stumbles or fails, do not let that discourage you from stepping up with your own best effort.
True leadership means sharing in the hardship of those you lead, not merely directing from a safe distance.
When progress feels slow and the resistance is strong, maintain a steady, measured advance rather than retreating in despair.

title: The Final Siege: Catapult Deployed Against al-Qamus and the Peace of Ibn Abi al-Huqayq
summary: After a fourteen-day siege of the last major Khaybar fortresses, with a catapult deployed against them, Ibn Abi al-Huqayq descended to negotiate peace, agreeing to surrender all wealth and possessions in exchange for their lives.
story: Al-Wsqidi stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then turned his attention to the inhabitants of al-Akhbiyya, and of al-Watih and al-Sulaim, two forts of Aba al-Huqayq. These they defended strenuously, having been joined by all those who had been defeated from al-Natat to al-Shiqq. These joined with the rest in defending al-Qamas and al-Katiba - a very strong fortress - and in al-Waph and al-Suhlim. Ultimately they stopped looking down and the Messenger of God (SAAS) erected a catapult to use against them. When they were sure their defeat was certain, the Messenger of God (SAAS), having by then besieged them for 14 days, Ibn Aba al-Huqayq came down to him and made peace. The conditions were that he would spare their lives and allow them to withdraw, while they would give him free access to their lands, properties, gold, silver, horses, armour and weapons, while they could keep only what was 'alci zahn mnsacin, 'on their backs', their clothing, that is. The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'But the protection of God and of His messenger is lost to you if you conceal anything.' They made peace on that basis."
lessons: There comes a point in every prolonged struggle when accepting a negotiated end with dignity is wiser than total destruction.
Clear and explicit conditions in any agreement prevent ambiguity and protect all parties from future betrayal.
A warning given openly before an agreement is struck is itself an act of fairness and transparency.
practical_advice: When you have exhausted your resources in a struggle, consider what a dignified and honest settlement looks like rather than holding out for an ideal outcome that is no longer possible.
When making agreements, be explicit about the consequences of breaking them, and hold yourself to the same standard.
Accept help or mercy when it is offered with clear conditions; false pride in refusing reasonable terms can lead to worse outcomes.

title: The Hidden Leather Bag: Broken Pact and the Execution of the Sons of Abu al-Huqayq
summary: After peace was made at Khaybar, the people of Banu al-Nadir concealed a leather bag of wealth belonging to Huyayy ibn Akhtab, violating their agreement; when discovered, the Prophet executed the two sons of Abu al-Huqayq and took their families captive.
story: I note that when they did hide things and told lies, concealing that leather bag in which they had much wealth, it became clear that they were not keeping to their pact. And so he killed both sons of Aba al-Huqayq and part of his family because they had broken their pacts and agreements. The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated that Aba al-Hasan 'Ali b. Muhammad al-Muqri al-Asfarayini stated to him, quoting Hasan b. Muhammad b. Ishaq, quoting Yusuf b. Ya'qub, quoting 'Abd al-Wahid b. Ghiyath, quoting Hammad b. Salama, quoting 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Umar - according to Aba Salama - from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) battled against the people of Khaybar until he forced them to take refuge in their fortress. He thus gained control of their land, their produce and their date-palms. They then made peace with him. The conditions were that they would leave their fortress keeping what their mounts would carry. To the Messenger of God (SAAS) would go their gold, and silver. They would move away and not hide or fail to disclose anything; if they did so, they would have no valid pact of agreement. They then concealed a leather bag containing money and jewels belonging to Huyayy b. Akhtab he had carried it with him to Khaybar when Bana al-Nadir was expelled. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked at this time, 'What happened to the leather bag of Huyayy that he brought from al-Nadir?' He replied, 'Various expenses and the warfare have exhausted it.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'Only a little time has passed and there was more money than that.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then pushed him over to al-Zubayr who tortured him. Before this, Huyayy had gone inside a ruin and he reported seeing Huyayy circling it. They then went and searched, ultimately finding the bag in the ruin. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then killed the two sons of Aba al-Huqayq, one of whom was the husband of Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy b. al-Akhtab. He took their women and children captive and divided up their possessions since they had broken their agreement.
lessons: Deception within a covenant is a grave moral failure that undermines peace and leads to lasting consequences.
The truth will emerge even when carefully hidden; attempting to conceal wrongdoing prolongs suffering rather than preventing it.
Breaking a solemn agreement has repercussions not just for the individual but for entire communities who depend on that trust.
practical_advice: When you have given your word, honor it fully — partial honesty that hides key truths is still a form of deception.
If you have broken trust with someone, acknowledge it openly and seek to repair the relationship before the consequences escalate.
Reflect on what you may be concealing from yourself or others that is eroding an important relationship or commitment in your life.

title: The Khaybar Jews Permitted to Remain as Farmers and the Integrity of Abdullah ibn Rawaha
summary: The defeated Jews of Khaybar requested permission to remain and farm the land in exchange for half the produce; Abdullah ibn Rawaha was appointed to fairly assess the harvest, and when they tried to bribe him, he refused with remarkable integrity.
story: "He wanted to expel them, but they said, 'Muhammad, let us stay on this land to care for and improve it.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions had no revenues from farming to live on and they themselves were too occupied otherwise to work on the land. And so he gave Khaybar over to them on condition that they would have an equal part of all the produce, dates and other returns, for so long as the Messenger of God (SAAS) wished. 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha would come to them every year to receive payment for it from them, paying them their half. But they complained to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the zeal with which he performed this task. They had tried to bribe him. He told them, 'You enemies of God! Would you pay me with ill-gotten gains! By God, I come to you on behalf of the person most dear to me of all. You are more hateful in my view than the monkeys and pigs that are your like! But my hatred for you and my love for him will not lead me to fail to treat you fairly.' They replied, 'By this, heaven and earth are set.'"
lessons: True integrity means treating even those you dislike with fairness, refusing to let personal feelings distort your judgment.
A bribe offered is a test of character; the ability to refuse it, especially with principled clarity, is a mark of moral strength.
Love and loyalty to a person or cause should elevate your conduct, not be used as an excuse for injustice toward others.
practical_advice: When you hold responsibility over others, especially those you find difficult, commit to fairness regardless of your personal feelings toward them.
If you are ever offered something that would compromise your integrity, name the compromise clearly to yourself and refuse it without ambiguity.
Let your love for what is right be stronger than your dislike of those you must serve — this is the standard of excellent character.

title: Safiyya's Dream and Her Account of the Prophet's Kindness After Khaybar
summary: Safiyya bint Huyayy recounts a dream she had before Khaybar's fall, the slap she received for it, and how the Prophet's patient explanation gradually transformed her grief and hostility into acceptance.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) noticed some blackness around Safiyya's eye and he asked her, 'Safiyya, what is this blackness?' She replied, 'My head was in the lap of Ibn Aba al-Huqayq while I slept. I seemed to see a moon drop into my lap. When I told him of this, he slapped me, saying, "You yearn for the 'king of Yathrib'!"' She went on, 'I thought of the Messenger of God (SAAS) as the most hateful of men. He killed my husband and my father. But he kept on explaining to me, saying, "Your father incited the Arabs against me and did so and so forth." Eventually all that left my mind.'"
lessons: Grief and resentment, even when deeply felt, can be healed through patient, honest, and compassionate explanation.
Attending to signs of someone's pain — as the Prophet noticed the mark on Safiyya's face — is an act of profound care.
What we imagine we will never overcome can, with time and understanding, lose its grip on our hearts.
practical_advice: If someone you care about carries visible signs of pain or stress, ask about it gently rather than ignoring it.
When you have caused someone grief, even unintentionally through circumstance, take time to explain your actions with honesty and compassion rather than expecting them to simply accept the situation.
Allow yourself space to process difficult emotions without rushing to resolution; grief and resentment do fade when met with truth and kindness.

title: Umar's Division of Khaybar and Expulsion of the Jews
summary: During the Caliphate of Umar, after the Jews of Khaybar injured his son and cheated the Muslims, Umar divided the Khaybar lands among those who had participated in its conquest and ultimately expelled the Jewish community.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to give each of his wives 80 bags of dates and 20 of barley annually. During the Caliphate of 'Umar, they cheated the Muslims, and threw 'Umar's son down from atop a house, breaking his wrists. 'Umar then said, 'Those owning a share of Khaybar should come for us to divide it up.' He did divide it up and their (the Jews') leader said, 'Don't expel us; allow us to remain in it as did the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abu Bakr too.' 'Umar responded, 'Do you consider that what the Messenger of God (SAAS) said does not apply to me! But how would you feel if your mount raced off towards Syria, day after day after day!' 'Umar then divided it up between those who were at al-Hudaybiyya and then at Khaybar."
lessons: Persistent violation of agreements and trust eventually leads to the loss of the arrangements that protected the violators.
Just leadership requires enforcing consequences consistently, even when those affected appeal to past lenience.
Previous mercy does not obligate indefinite tolerance of ongoing harm; agreements exist within a context of mutual good faith.
practical_advice: If someone repeatedly takes advantage of a generous arrangement you have made, it is just — not harsh — to revisit the terms of that arrangement.
When making decisions about ongoing relationships or agreements, consider whether the other party is genuinely upholding their side.
Accountability in leadership means following through on stated consequences; delaying this indefinitely does not serve justice or the community.

title: The Jews of Khaybar Allowed to Remain as Sharecroppers
summary: After the conquest of Khaybar, the Jews requested to remain on their land as sharecroppers, working for half the produce. The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) agreed, allowing them to stay on that condition for as long as he wished.
story: When Khaybar was conquered, the Jews asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) to allow them to remain on condition that they would work for one-half of what it produced. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told them, 'I will allow you to stay in it on that condition for as long as we wish.' And so they did. The dates, of one-half of Khaybar, were divided into shares and the Messenger of God (SAAS) would take a portion of one-fifth. From that fifth, he would provide 100 bags of dates and 20 of barley for each of his wives, annually.
lessons: Even in victory, the Prophet (SAAS) showed pragmatic mercy by allowing the defeated to maintain their livelihoods under fair terms.
Agreements made in good faith must be honored; the arrangement stood until the Jews later broke its conditions.
Leadership involves balancing the needs of many stakeholders — the community, the defeated, and one's own family.
practical_advice: When resolving a conflict, seek solutions that allow all parties a dignified path forward rather than total defeat of one side.
Set clear terms in any agreement and ensure all parties understand the conditions under which it can be revisited.
When you are in a position of authority, be transparent about how shared resources are divided so trust is maintained.

title: Umar Expels the Jews of Khaybar
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab, recalling the Prophet's original condition that the Jews of Khaybar could be expelled at will, offered the Prophet's wives compensation and then expelled the Jews from Khaybar.
story: When Umar wished to evict the Jews, he sent to the wives of the Prophet (SAAS) and told them, 'Whichever of you wishes me to set aside for her (land that would produce) 100 bags, she may have the roots, land and water therefor, along with the plants to produce 20 bags of barley. This I will arrange. Those who prefer us to set aside what she owns of the fifth share as it has been, this too we will do.' Umar stated, 'O people, the Messenger of God (SAAS) made a pact with the Jews of Khaybar on the understanding that he could expel them if he wished. Those with property there should go to it; I am expelling the Jews.' And he did expel them.
lessons: Leadership sometimes requires making difficult decisions that uphold prior agreements, even when those decisions are painful for those affected.
Conditions established by just leaders carry weight and must be honored in both letter and spirit.
When enforcing a difficult decision, those in authority should still seek to provide fair alternatives and compensation for those impacted.
practical_advice: Before entering any agreement, clearly understand all conditions attached to it, including conditions under which it may end.
When you must deliver difficult news or make a hard decision, do so with clarity and fairness, offering alternatives where possible.
Reflect on whether your current situation rests on conditions that could change, and prepare yourself spiritually and practically for that possibility.

title: The Prophet Explains the Unity of Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib
summary: Jubayr ibn Mut'im and Uthman ibn Affan questioned why Banu al-Muttalib received a share of the Khaybar fifth while Banu Abd Shams and Banu Nawfal did not. The Prophet explained that Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib were one united group.
story: Jubayr b. Mut'im informed him as follows, 'I and Uthman b. Affan walked over to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, "You gave to Banu al-Muttalib a part of the fifth share of Khaybar but left us out, even though we and they have the same status with you." He replied, "Well, Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib are one." Jubayr b. Mut'im stated, "The Prophet (SAAS) gave no share to Banu Abd Shams, nor to Banu Nawfal."' There is one version that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Banu Hashim and Banu Abd al-Muttalib are one; they were not separate from us before Islam or thereafter.' Al-Shafi'i stated, 'They went into the defile with them and gave them help after their having accepted Islam just as they had before Islam. Abu Talib expressed displeasure with Banu Abd Shams and Nawfal, saying, "May God punish badly Abd Shams and Nawfal for us, and the sooner the better!"'
lessons: Loyalty and solidarity forged through shared hardship creates bonds that transcend formal kinship categories.
It is human to feel hurt when excluded from what others receive; bringing such concerns directly and respectfully to leadership is the right course.
Justice does not always mean equal treatment — it means treatment proportional to history, sacrifice, and shared struggle.
practical_advice: When you feel you have been treated unfairly, seek clarification from the decision-maker directly and calmly rather than harboring resentment.
Reflect on the history behind decisions that affect you — understanding context can transform feelings of rejection into acceptance.
Ask yourself: have you shared in the sacrifices of those who are receiving recognition? Solidarity must be earned through action, not just claimed through association.

title: Horses Awarded Double Shares at Khaybar
summary: At Khaybar, the Prophet awarded each horse two shares of the spoils and each man on foot one share, resulting in a mounted fighter receiving three shares in total.
story: At Khaybar, the Messenger of God (SAAS) awarded a double share to each horse and a single share to each man on foot. This pleased Nafi', who said, 'So if a man had a horse, he received three shares: two for the horse, one for himself.'
lessons: Recognizing and rewarding those who bring greater resources and effort to a shared endeavor is a principle of just compensation.
Preparation and investment — such as bringing a horse to battle — is itself a form of sacrifice that deserves acknowledgment.
Leaders who establish clear, fair systems of reward build trust and morale within their communities.
practical_advice: Invest in the tools and preparation needed for your responsibilities — that investment will be recognized and rewarded.
When distributing rewards in a team or community setting, consider both effort and the resources each person brought to the table.
Express gratitude for systems of fairness when you encounter them, as Nafi' did — recognizing justice encourages its continuation.

title: Umar's Reflection on Dividing Conquered Lands
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab expressed that he wished he could divide all conquered villages among warriors as the Prophet divided Khaybar, but held back out of concern for future Muslims who would need the revenues.
story: Umar b. al-Khattab said, 'By Him who bears my soul in His hands, were I not afraid that the other Muslims might be left in poverty, I would divide up (among the warriors) each village conquered for me, just as the Prophet (SAAS) divided up Khaybar. However, I will leave it all to them as a (source of) treasury for them to distribute among themselves.'
lessons: True leadership requires thinking beyond the immediate moment and considering the long-term welfare of the entire community.
Sometimes restraining a generous impulse is itself an act of generosity toward future generations.
The ability to hold competing goods in tension — rewarding present sacrifice while protecting future welfare — is a hallmark of wise governance.
practical_advice: Before making a decision that feels right in the moment, pause and consider its long-term impact on those who are not yet present.
Practice deferred gratification: sometimes the most generous act is to preserve resources for when they are needed most.
When you are in a position of stewardship over shared resources, remember that your responsibility extends beyond those immediately in front of you.

title: The Division of Khaybar into Two Halves
summary: The Prophet divided Khaybar into two halves: one for his own responsibilities and delegations, and one distributed among the Muslim fighters in 18 parts, with double shares going to cavalry.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) divided Khaybar into two halves; one-half was for his own responsibilities, and one-half went to the Muslims; he divided it up among them on the basis of 18 parts. He assigned to the half allotted for his needs (responsibilities) al-Watih, al-Katiba, al-Sulalim and their adjoining properties, and gave to the Muslims the half consisting of al-Shiqq and al-Natit and their adjoining properties; the portions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) was what adjoined both of these. The Messenger of God (SAAS) gained victory over Khaybar and divided it into 36 shares, each of which combined 100 shares. To the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims went one-half of this. The second half he put aside for those from the delegations who came to stay with him, and for other matters, along with some portions set aside for those who suffered special misfortune. Khaybar was apportioned to those who had (also) been present at al-Hudaybiyya. He divided it up into 18 parts. The army consisted of 1,500 men, including 300 cavalry. He allotted two shares to each man with a horse, and one share to each man on foot.
lessons: A just leader ensures that both present needs and future contingencies are provided for through careful planning.
Honoring those who were present at earlier trials — such as al-Hudaybiyya — reflects the Islamic principle that sacrifice is never forgotten.
Transparent and systematic distribution of shared resources prevents grievance and fosters unity.
practical_advice: When dividing any shared resource or responsibility, establish clear and transparent criteria so all involved understand the basis of the division.
Remember those who sacrificed alongside you in earlier, harder times — do not let new success cause you to overlook old loyalty.
Set aside a portion of any windfall for those facing hardship and for future needs, rather than distributing everything immediately.

title: 'A'isha and Ibn Umar on Finally Having Enough Dates After Khaybar
summary: After the conquest of Khaybar, the Muslims celebrated the prospect of having enough dates to eat. Ibn Umar noted that they had never had their fill of dates until Khaybar was conquered.
story: A'isha said, 'When Khaybar was conquered, we said, "Well, now we'll have our fill of dates!"' Ibn Umar said, 'We never had our fill' — meaning of dates — 'until we conquered Khaybar.'
lessons: The relief that follows prolonged hardship is one of the sweetest gifts; those who have known scarcity understand abundance with a depth others cannot.
Small comforts — like having enough food — carry profound spiritual and emotional weight when they have long been denied.
The Companions lived with genuine material sacrifice for the sake of their faith, which deepened their gratitude for every blessing.
practical_advice: Reflect on the deprivations you have endured and allow yourself to feel genuine gratitude when relief comes, rather than immediately wanting more.
Do not take daily provisions for granted — practicing mindful gratitude for food, shelter, and safety is a spiritual discipline.
When blessings arrive after hardship, pause and acknowledge them with thankfulness before moving on to the next pursuit.

title: Abd Allah ibn Rawaha Oversees Valuation of Khaybar's Date-Palms
summary: Abd Allah ibn Rawaha was appointed to value the date-palms of Khaybar for two years. After his martyrdom at the Battle of Mu'ta, Jabbar ibn Sakhr took over this responsibility.
story: The men placed in charge of the division and the accounting were Jabbar b. Sakhr b. Umayya b. Khansa', brother of Banu Salama, and Zayd b. Thabit, may God be pleased with them both. The commander who had authority over valuation of the date-palms of Khaybar was Abd Allah b. Rawaha. He did this for two years. Following his death, may God be pleased with him, at the battle of Mu'ta, Jabbar b. Sakhr, may God be pleased with him, took over his work.
lessons: Faithful service in administrative and logistical roles is as honored in Islam as physical bravery — both Ibn Rawaha and Jabbar ibn Sakhr served with distinction.
When a trusted servant of the community falls, others must be ready to step forward and continue the work without allowing it to collapse.
Death does not erase the legacy of honest service; Ibn Rawaha's memory was preserved precisely because of his faithful stewardship.
practical_advice: Take pride and care in administrative and behind-the-scenes responsibilities — they are essential to the functioning of any community.
Prepare others to take over your responsibilities so that important work continues if you are suddenly unable to carry it on.
When a colleague or mentor passes, honor their memory by continuing their work with the same dedication they brought to it.

title: The Prophet's Instruction on Fair Date Exchange at Khaybar
summary: The Prophet Muhammad corrected an unfair bartering practice at Khaybar, instructing that lesser quality dates should be sold for cash first before purchasing high-quality dates, rather than exchanging unequal quantities directly.
story: Al-Bukhāri stated that Ismā'īl related to him, quoting Malik, from 'Abd al-Majid b. Suhayl, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, from Abū Sa'id al-Khudari and Abū Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) placed a man in command of Khaybar who brought him some dates that were janib, 'of very high quality'. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him, 'Are all the dates of Khaybar like this?' He replied, 'No, I swear by God, Messenger of God! We take a sa' weight of this (dates of high quality) for two sa' or three sa' (of lesser quality).' He told him, 'No, don't do that. Sell the lesser quality dates for cash and then with that cash buy the janib dates.' Al-Bukhāri stated that al-Darāwardi said, quoting 'Abd al-Majid, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, that Abū Sa'id and Abū Hurayra both related that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent a brother of Banū 'Adi of the ansār to Khaybar and appointed him in charge of it.
lessons: Fairness in trade and economic transactions is a core Islamic value, and even well-intentioned practices must be corrected if they lead to injustice.
Leadership requires accountability — those placed in authority must be willing to receive correction and guidance with humility.
Islam's concern for economic justice extends to everyday transactions, reminding us that ethical conduct is not limited to acts of worship.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a situation where a common practice seems unfair or exploitative, have the courage to question it and seek a more equitable alternative.
