If you are in a position of authority or responsibility, remain open to correction from those with greater wisdom, treating feedback as a gift rather than a criticism.
Apply principles of fairness in your daily transactions — whether financial, social, or relational — by ensuring that exchanges are balanced and not at another's expense.

title: The Prophet's Management of Khaybar Properties and the Dispute Over Inheritance After His Death
summary: After the conquest of Khaybar and Fadak, the Prophet managed those properties for charitable purposes, and after his death, Fatima and his wives sought inheritance which Abu Bakr denied based on the Prophet's statement that prophets leave no inheritance.
story: The properties of Khaybar gained by the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims and all of Fadak, constituted a large part of the territory of Khaybar. They (the Jewish inhabitants) had emerged and made peace with the Messenger of God (SAAS) because of their great fear of him. To all this were added the acquisitions mentioned above from Banū al-Nadir where the Muslims similarly had no need to use force. These were the personal property of the Messenger of God (SAAS). From them he would subtract the yearly expenses of his family and then treat the remainder as belonging to God. He would use it to pay for the mounts, weapons and other needs of the Muslims. When he died, Fātima and his wives - or most of them - believed that these properties would be inherited from him. They had not been informed of a statement that has been established to have been made by the Messenger of God (SAAS) as follows, 'We prophets do not leave inheritance; what we leave becomes sadaqa, a public charity.' When Fātima and the wives of the Prophet (SAAS), along with al-'Abbās, sought their share of this and asked (Abū Bakr), the Trusting, to pay it to them, he related to them what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said, namely, 'We do not leave inheritance; what we leave becomes a sadaqa.' He told them, 'I will provide for those whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) cared for; I swear by God, the relatives of the Messenger of God (SAAS) are dearer to me than my own.' He spoke the truth, may God be pleased with him and give him pleasure! In that he was faithful, well-guided and adhering to the truth. Al-'Abbās requested, as did 'Ali on behalf of Fātima, since they had failed to receive an inheritance, that they should have access to that charitable fund and use it for those expenses that the Prophet (SAAS) used to meet. But Abū Bakr refused that, considering it his duty to proceed as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had done, and not to deviate from his path and practices. Fātima, may God be pleased with her, was annoyed with him about this and experienced some anger. Yet this was not appropriate for her. She and all the Muslims well knew his position and standing with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his role in assisting him both during his life and after his death. May God reward him well, on behalf of his prophet, Islam and his people. Fātima, may God be pleased with her, died six months thereafter. 'Ali then renewed his oath of allegiance. During the Caliphate of 'Umar b. al-Khattāb, they asked him to give control of this Sadaqa to 'Ali and al-'Abbās. They pressured him through a group of the Companions. And 'Umar, may God be pleased with him, agreed to this. He did so because of the multiplicity of his duties, the extensiveness of his domain and the great spread of his subjects. 'Ali gained advantage over his uncle al-'Abbās in this matter. Then they accompanied each other and quarrelled in front of 'Umar. And they brought along a group of the Companions to him and asked him to divide up the assets between them in such a way that each would be independent of the other. But 'Umar resisted this strongly, fearing that such a division would be similar to the division through inheritance. He said, 'Look after it together. And if you can't, then turn it over to me. By Him at whose command the heavens and earth stand, I'll never reach any other decision about this!' The property continued to be managed in this way, by their descendants, into the 'Abbāside era, the expenditures continuing to be made as they had been by the Messenger of God (SAAS). (This is what became of) the assets of Banū al-Nadir, from Fadak and the share of the Messenger of God (SAAS), from Khaybar.
lessons: Grief and unmet expectations can cloud our judgment even in matters of principle; Fatima's disappointment was human and understandable, yet the truth of what Abu Bakr upheld remained valid.
True leadership sometimes means maintaining an unpopular but just position, as both Abu Bakr and Umar demonstrated in resisting pressure to alter the Prophet's legacy.
The Prophet's example of dedicating surplus wealth to the community rather than to personal inheritance reflects a profound commitment to collective welfare over individual gain.
practical_advice: When you feel wronged in a matter involving inheritance or family assets, take time to investigate whether there may be a principle or prior agreement you were not aware of before allowing resentment to take hold.
If you are in a position of authority and face pressure from people you love and respect, seek clarity on the principle at stake and hold firm to what is right even when it is emotionally difficult.
Channel feelings of loss or disappointment after a loved one's death into continued service to the values they stood for, rather than disputes over material possessions.

title: The Slave 'Umayr Receives a Gift Instead of a Full Share at Khaybar
summary: A freed slave named 'Umayr participated in the Khaybar campaign and was initially awarded a sword as booty, but when the Prophet learned he was a slave, he ordered a more suitable gift be given to him instead.
story: Abū Dā'ūd stated that Ahmad b. Hanbal related to him, quoting Bishr b. al-Mufaddal, from Muhammad b. Zayd, (who said), ''Umayr, freed-man of Abā al-Lab, said, 'I was present at Khaybar with my masters. They talked about me to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he ordered that I be awarded a sword. I was just about to draw it when he was informed that I was a mamlak, a slave. He then ordered I be given something (suitable) I would enjoy.''' Both al-Tirmidhī and al-Nasā'i related this, from Qutayba, from Bishr b. al-Mufaddal. Al-Tirmidhī stated, 'It is hasan sahīh, good and authentic.' Ibn Māja related it from 'Ali b. Muhammad, from Wakī', from Hishām b. Sa'd, from Muhammad b. Zayd b. al-Muhājir, from Munqidh, from 'Umayr.
lessons: Every person deserves to be treated with dignity appropriate to their situation; the Prophet's adjustment of 'Umayr's reward shows sensitivity to individual circumstances rather than rigid uniformity.
Social status or position does not determine a person's worth or the care they deserve from a just leader.
Gratitude for whatever one receives, regardless of its size, reflects a healthy and resilient spirit.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked or given less than others in a situation, remind yourself that fair treatment is not always identical treatment — what matters is that your needs and dignity are respected.
Practice gratitude for the gifts you do receive rather than focusing on what you did not get.
If you are in a position to give or reward others, consider the specific circumstances and needs of each person rather than applying a one-size-fits-all approach.

title: The Young Woman of Banū Ghifār Experiences Her First Period on Campaign to Khaybar
summary: A young woman from Banū Ghifār who accompanied the Prophet to Khaybar experienced her first menstrual period on the journey; the Prophet responded with calm guidance and later gifted her a necklace from the conquered spoils.
story: Muhammad b. Ishāq stated, 'There were women who were present at Khaybar with the Messenger of God (SAAS). He awarded them part of the booty, but he did not allot them shares. Sulaymān b. Suhaym related to me, from Umayya, daughter of Abū al-Salt, from a woman of Banū Ghifār, whose name was given me. She said, "I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), along with some women of Banū Ghifār. We asked him, "Messenger of God, we would like to go forth with you," — he was then on his way to Khaybar — "to take care of the wounded and give whatever help we can to the Muslims." He replied, "May God bless you."" She went on, "So we left with him. I was a young girl then and the Messenger of God (SAAS) placed me on his mount behind himself, on a bag of provisions. When he dismounted to perform the al-subh prayer, I got down from the bag. On it I saw blood of mine; this was my first period. I hurried back to the camel, feeling embarrassed. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw I was upset and noticed the blood, he asked, "What is wrong? You probably menstruated." "Yes," I answered. He then said, "Cleanse yourself and then take a vessel of water, put some salt in it and use it to wash the blood off the bag. Then go back to your seat."" She went on, "When God conquered Khaybar, he awarded us some of the booty. He took this necklace you see around my neck and gave it to me, himself putting it on me. By God, I will never be parted from it." It was around her neck until she died and she left instructions that it be buried with her. She never cleansed herself after her menstruation without putting salt in the water. And she left instructions that it be put in the water to be used to wash her when she died.' Imām Ahmad and Abū Dā'ūd related this similarly, from the hadith of Muhammad b. Ishāq.
lessons: The Prophet's calm, practical, and non-judgmental response to the young woman's embarrassment models how leaders and caregivers should handle moments of vulnerability with compassion.
Moments of embarrassment, when met with kindness and guidance, can become cherished memories rather than sources of lasting shame.
A small act of personal kindness — like placing a necklace around someone's neck — can leave an impression so deep that a person carries it to their grave.
practical_advice: When you feel embarrassed or caught off guard by a bodily or personal matter, remember that these are natural human experiences and that a compassionate person will respond with understanding rather than judgment.
If someone around you is visibly distressed or embarrassed, offer calm, practical assistance before anything else — your composed response can completely change how they experience the moment.
Hold onto the acts of kindness that have touched your heart most deeply; let them remind you of your worth and of the goodness that exists in people.

title: Six Women of the Ansār Accompany the Prophet to Khaybar and Receive a Share
summary: A group of six women, including the narrator's grandmother, accompanied the Prophet to Khaybar to support the wounded; though the Prophet was initially displeased, he allowed them to continue, and they later received a share of the dates as booty.
story: Imām Ahmad stated that Hasan b. Mūsā related to us, quoting Rafī' b. Salama al-Ashja'i (who said), 'Hashraj b. Ziyād related to me, from his grandmother, his father's mother, who said, "We went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the expedition to Khaybar, I being the youngest of six women. When news reached the Prophet (SAAS) that there were women accompanying him, he summoned us. We could see from his face that he was angry. He asked us, "Why have you come, and who gave you permission?" We replied, "We have come to hand out the arrows and to pour the sawīq we have with us medication for wounds and we can spin hair and use it for God's cause." "You may proceed," he told us." She went on, "When God conquered Khaybar for him, he drew arrows (to determine shares) for us, just as he did for the men." I then asked her, "Grandmother, what was allotted to you then?" "Dates," she replied.' I comment that all he gave them was the produce. He certainly did not give to them shares in the land, as he did to the men.
lessons: Genuine intention to serve and contribute can transform initial disapproval into acceptance; the women's clear articulation of their purpose moved the Prophet from displeasure to permission.
Service in times of crisis — tending the wounded, providing supplies — is a noble contribution regardless of one's formal role or status.
Persistence rooted in sincere purpose, rather than stubbornness, is a quality that earns respect even from those in authority.
practical_advice: When your motives are questioned or your presence unwelcome, calmly and clearly explain your genuine intentions — often misunderstanding dissolves when we speak with honesty.
Look for meaningful ways to contribute in difficult situations even when you do not hold a formal role; practical service is always valuable.
When you receive a smaller reward than others for equal effort, focus on the contribution itself rather than the compensation — your sincerity is its own reward.

title: A Pregnant Woman's Complication During the Khaybar Campaign and the Birth of Her Child
summary: During the Khaybar campaign, a companion's pregnant wife suffered a bleeding complication; the Prophet prescribed a remedy of soaked dates, and she recovered. After the conquest, both the mother and her newborn child received a gift from the booty.
story: The hāfiz al-Bayhaqī stated, 'In my book, (it states) from the hāfiz Abū 'Abd Allāh, that 'Abd Allāh al-Isbahānī informed him, as follows, "Al-Husayn b. al-Jahm related to us, quoting al-Husayn b. al-Faraj, quoting al-Wāqidī, (who said) "'Abd al-Salām b. Masā b. Jubayr, from his father, from his grandfather, from 'Abd Allāh b. Anis, who said, 'I went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Khaybar, and my wife, who was pregnant, accompanied me. On the way she had a discharge of blood. I reported this to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and he told me, "Soak some dates for her and when it all turns liquid, tell her to drink it." She did so and saw nothing to alarm her. When we had conquered Khaybar, he gave to the women, but did not allot them shares. He gave both to my wife and to my child who was born.'"" 'Abd al-Salām added, 'I don't know whether it was a boy or a girl.'"
lessons: The Prophet's attention to the health and wellbeing of individuals even in the midst of a military campaign reflects the Islamic value of caring for the vulnerable at all times.
Practical wisdom in health matters — such as the remedy of soaked dates — is part of a holistic approach to human welfare.
Even the most vulnerable — a newborn child — was recognized and included in the community's provisions, reflecting Islam's protection of the weak.
practical_advice: In times of collective stress or crisis, do not neglect the health needs of those who are most vulnerable around you; a small act of care can prevent serious harm.
When faced with a health concern during a difficult situation, seek guidance from those with knowledge and act on it promptly rather than waiting for a more convenient time.
Remember that those who cannot yet advocate for themselves — children, the unborn, the ill — deserve extra consideration and inclusion in any distribution of resources or care.

title: The Arrival of Ja'far ibn Abī Tālib and the Abyssinian Emigrants at Khaybar
summary: While the Prophet was at Khaybar, Ja'far ibn Abī Tālib returned from Abyssinia with the Muslim emigrants who had sought refuge there, bringing great joy to the Prophet who had been separated from them for years.
story: An Account of the arrival to the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he was encamped at Khaybar, of Ja'far b. Abū Tālib, may God be pleased with him, and those who had remained in Abyssinia. These were Muslims who had emigrated there, along with those from the people of Yemen who had joined them. Al-Bukhārī stated that it was related to him by Muhammad b. al-'Alā', quoting Abū Usāma, quoting Burayḍ b. 'Abd Allāh b. Abū Burda, from Abū Burda, from Abū Mūsā, who said, 'We learned of the departure of the Prophet (SAAS) while we were in Yemen and so we left as mutāqi'rīn to join him. I was accompanied by my brothers, myself being'
lessons: Years of patient endurance in exile, maintaining faith through separation from the Muslim community, ultimately led to a joyful reunion — a reminder that patient perseverance is never wasted.
The bonds of brotherhood in faith transcend distance and time; those who sacrificed by emigrating to Abyssinia remained part of the community even after years of separation.
Moments of reunion after long hardship carry a special sweetness that ordinary comfort cannot produce; trials prepare us to truly appreciate blessings.
practical_advice: When you feel separated from your community, your loved ones, or your purpose due to circumstances beyond your control, hold on — the separation is not permanent and the reunion can be profoundly meaningful.
Use periods of exile or isolation, whether literal or emotional, to deepen your faith, your character, and your readiness to contribute when circumstances change.
When someone you care about returns after a long absence or difficulty, greet them with genuine warmth and celebration — the gesture affirms that they were missed and valued throughout their absence.

title: The Arrival of Ja'far and the Abyssinian Emigrants at Khaybar
summary: After years of exile in Abyssinia, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and a group of Muslim emigrants returned to join the Prophet just as he had conquered Khaybar, prompting a joyful reunion.
story: Muhammad h. Ishaq stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent 'Amr b. Umayya al-Damri to the Negus to request him for those of his Companions who had remained in Abyssinia. They arrived in the company of Ja'far, after the Prophet (SAAS) had conquered Khaybar. He went on, "Sufya b. Wyayna related, from al-Ajl*, from d-Sha'bi, that Ja'far b. Aba Tdib came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the day when he conquered Khaybar. The Messenger of God (SAAS) kissed him between his eyes and hugged him, saying, 'I don't know which makes me happier - the conquest of Khaybar, or the arrival of Ja'far!'" Sufya al-Thawri related it thus from al-Ajlah, from al-Sha'bi, only as mursal, with an incomplete chain of transmission. Al-Bayhaqi recorded a line of transmission through Hasan h. Husayn al-'Arzami, from al-Ajlah, from al-Sha'bi, from Jiibir, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) came back from Khaybar, Ja'far arrived from Abyssinia. The Messenger of God (SAAS) kissed him on the forehead when he met him, saying, 'By God, I don't know what makes me happier - the conquest of Khaybar or the arrival of Ja'far!'" Al-Bayhaqi then stated, "The ha& Ahii 'Ahd Allzh related to us, quoting al-Husayn b. Aha Isma'il al-'Alawi, quoting A h a d h. Muhammad al-Baymti, quoting Muhammad b. A h a d h. Aha Tayba (who said), 'Makki h. Ibmhim al-Ru'ayni related to me, quoting Sue- d-Thawri, from Aba al-Zubayr, from Jiibir, who said, "When Ja'far b. Abo Talib arrived from Abyssinia, he was met by the Messenger of God (SAAS). When Ja'far saw hi, he hajala, he hopped on one leg, to show his respect for the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter then kissed him between his eyes."'"
lessons: Reunion after long separation is one of life's most profound joys, and the Prophet's reaction shows that love for people can match joy over great worldly achievements.
Years of patient endurance in hardship do not diminish your worth — the emigrants' long exile was honoured, not forgotten.
Expressing affection openly, as the Prophet did by kissing and hugging Ja'far, is a prophetic practice that strengthens bonds and heals the wounds of separation.
practical_advice: When you are reunited with someone you love after a long separation, allow yourself to fully express joy and gratitude without restraint.
If you feel that your sacrifices have gone unnoticed, remember that God and those who truly care for you are fully aware of what you have endured.
Make a habit of physically and verbally expressing affection to family and close friends — it is a sunnah and a source of emotional healing for both giver and receiver.

title: Asma bint Umays Confronts Umar Over the Honour of the Abyssinian Emigrants
summary: When Umar implied that those who emigrated to Abyssinia had less honour than those who stayed with the Prophet, Asma bint Umays boldly challenged him and brought the matter to the Prophet, who honoured the sea-emigrants with a double hijra.
story: Asmxi, daughter of Wmays - she having come with us, went in to visit Hafsa, the wife of the Prophet (SAAS), she, Asmax, having been one of those who had emigrated to the Negus. Wmar then came in to Hafsa while Asmx1 was with her and when he saw the latter, he asked, 'And who is this?' 'Asmz', daughter of Wmays,' she replied. Wmar then said, 'Is this the "Abyssinian" and the "sailor"?' 'Yes,' Asmax replied. He then commented, 'Well, we emigrated before you did. And so we have now more right to the Messenger of God (SAAS) than you do!' She became angry and replied, 'That's not so, by God! All the time you were with the Messenger of God (SAAS) he was feeding your hungry and instructing your ignorant, while we were in the dar, the domain, of the buCad2 and the bughu~j~? in Abyssinia! And we were there serving God and the Messenger of God (SAAS). I swear by God, I'll not eat nor drink again before I tell the Prophet (SAAS) what you said, and I'll ask hi. And I'll not lie or distort or add anything to it!' When the Prophet (SAAS) arrived, she said to him, 'Prophet of God, Wmar said so-and-such' 'And what did you reply?' he enquired. 'I replied so-and-such,' she told him. He then said, 'No one has greater right to me than you do. He and his associates had one hijra, but you boat people had two!' She later said, 'I then saw Aba Masa and the boat people stream in to me, asking about this conversation. And nothing in the world could have more pleased and impressed them than what the Prophet (SAAS) said about them.' Abn Burda narrated, 'Asmax commented, "When I saw Aba Masa, he asked me to repeat this hadith."'
lessons: Standing up firmly for one's honour and the honour of one's community, while committing to complete honesty, is a mark of moral courage.
The Prophet's justice was swift and clear — he did not allow comparisons that diminished sincere sacrifice, no matter who made them.
Suffering in exile or isolation for the sake of one's faith is not a lesser sacrifice; it may carry greater reward precisely because it is unseen and uncelebrated.
practical_advice: When someone dismisses or belittles your sacrifices, calmly but firmly articulate the truth of what you endured, without exaggeration or bitterness.
If you feel wronged, bring your concern directly to someone in authority or a trusted person rather than letting resentment fester silently.
Remember that God recognises every form of sacrifice — the visible and the invisible — and do not measure your worth by who received more recognition.

title: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari and the Boat People Receive a Share of Khaybar's Booty
summary: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari and his companions, who had sailed from Yemen to Abyssinia and then to Madinah, arrived after the conquest of Khaybar and were nonetheless granted a share of the booty by the Prophet.
story: Al-Bukhbi stated that it was related to h i by Muhammad b. al-LCAla', quoting Abii Us%ma, quoting Burayd b. 'Abd All& b. Aba Burda, from Aba Burda, from Aba Mnsa, who said, "We learned of the departure of the Prophet (SAAS) while we were in Yemen and so we left as mutiqi'rin to join him. I was accompanied by my brothers, myself being the youngest. One was called Abn Burda, the other Aba Ruhm. We were among a number" (or the narrator may have given the figure 53, or 52) "of men of my people. We rode aboard a boat that took us to the Negus in Abyssinia. There we met up with Ja'far h. Ahii Tdib, with whom we stayed before all coming on together. We arrived at the Prophet (SAAS) after he had conquered Khaybar. Some people there said to us - meaning those who had travelled by boat - 'We joined in the hijra before you did!'" Then al-Bukhai stated that Ishzq b. Ihahim related to h i, quoting Hafs b. Ghiyxth, quoting Burayd (b. 'Ahd All&) from Abii Burda, from Abii Miisa, who said, "We came to the Prophet (SAAS) after Khaybar had been conquered. He did give us a share (of the booty), though he gave none to anyone who had not been present at the victory except for us."
lessons: Those who strive sincerely for the right cause, even when delayed by circumstances beyond their control, are not forgotten or penalised by God.
The Prophet's generosity toward latecomers teaches that mercy and inclusion should define leadership, not rigid rules that punish sincere effort.
A long and difficult journey toward good — even one interrupted by detours and delays — still carries immense spiritual worth.
practical_advice: If you feel you have 'missed out' on something important due to circumstances outside your control, trust that sincere intention and effort are recognised and rewarded.
Do not compare your spiritual journey to others' — your path may be longer or harder, but it is no less valid.
When in a position to show generosity or include others, err on the side of inclusion and compassion as the Prophet did.

title: The Prophet Praises the Ash'ari People for Their Qur'an Recitation
summary: The Prophet expressed that he could recognise the houses of the Ash'ari tribe at night simply by hearing their voices reciting the Qur'an, praising their devotion.
story: Abii Burda said, quoting Abii Masa, 'The Prophet (SAAS) said, "I well know the voices of the 'ash'ari people', the reciters of the Qur'iin, when they go home at night. And I know their houses from their voices merely reciting the @?an at night, even without seeing their dwellings during the day. One of them is Hakim h. Yizzm, who, when he meets the enemy" - or he could have said, "cavalry" - "would say to them (the enemy), 'My companions order you to wait for them.""'
lessons: Consistency in worship, especially in the quiet of the night, does not go unnoticed — it becomes the defining mark of a person's character.
The Prophet's intimate knowledge of his companions' habits of devotion shows the deep spiritual attentiveness that true leadership requires.
Night worship and Qur'an recitation are among the most powerful tools for building inner peace and a lasting spiritual identity.
practical_advice: Establish a nightly practice of Qur'an recitation or remembrance of God, even if brief — consistency matters more than quantity.
Let your private devotion be more important to you than your public acts of worship, as it is a truer measure of your sincerity.
Reflect on what sounds and habits define your home — strive to make it a place known for remembrance of God.

title: Abu Hurayra Requests a Share of Booty and Confronts Aban ibn Sa'id
summary: When Abu Hurayra asked the Prophet for a share of Khaybar's booty, a man from Banu Sa'id objected and insulted him, prompting Abu Hurayra to identify the man as the killer of Ibn Qawqal; the Prophet ultimately withheld a share from the latecomers.
story: Al-Bukhw stated that CAli b. 'Abd Allah related to him, quoting Sufyan (who said), "I heard al-Zuhri say, when Isma'il b. Umayya asked him a question, 'Anbasa h. Sacid informed me that Abn Hurayra came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and made a request of him - that is, he asked him for a share in the booty. At this a man from Banii Sacid b. al-'AS said, "No, don't give it to him." Abii Hurayra commented (to the Prophet (SAAS)), "This is the murderer of Ibn Qawqal." The man replied: "That's a strange comment to come from a weasel who has come down from Qadiim al-Da'n!"'" Al-Bukhari went on to state that it was related from al-Zubaydi that al-Zuhri said, "'Anbasa b. Sa'id informed me that he heard Abii Hurayra informing Sa'id b. alLCAs as follows: 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Absn (h. Sacid) on an expedition from Medina towards Najd.' Abn Hurayra stated, 'And Aba and his companions came to the Prophet (SAAS) at Khaybar after he had conquered it; the girdles of their horses were made of palm-fibre rope.' Abn Hurayra said, 'So I said to the Messenger of God (SAAS), "Messenger of God, don't give them a share!" Abm exclaimed, "You're just a weasel that's come down from atop a da'l, a lotus tree!" At this the Prophet (SAAS) said, "Aban, sit down!" And he did not give them a share.' Al-Bukhxri stated that Mnsa h. Ismacil related to h i, quoting 'Amr h. Y*ya h. Sa'id (who said), 'My grandfather, Sacid b. 'Amr h. Sacid h. alLCAsinformed me that AbZn h. Sacid came to the Prophet (SAAS) and greeted him. Aha Hurayra then said, "Messenger of God, this is the murderer of Ihn Qawqal!" Aha retorted to Aha Hurayra, "How strange! For you, a weasel coming down from Qadnm Da'n to complain about me for the death of a man whom God honoured with martyrdom at my hand, a man whom God prevented from overcoming me!"'"
lessons: Disputes over material entitlement can reveal deeper unresolved grievances, and the Prophet's calm authority defused the tension without allowing it to escalate.
Being insulted or belittled in a public setting is painful, but maintaining dignity and bringing the matter to a just authority is the wiser response.
The Prophet's silence and redirection — telling Aban to sit down — shows that sometimes the most powerful response to conflict is measured restraint rather than argument.
practical_advice: When you feel publicly humiliated or dismissed, resist the urge to retaliate with more insults; instead, state your grievance plainly and let a trusted authority decide.
If someone raises a past wrong in a heated moment, reflect honestly on whether your own actions contribute to the tension before dismissing their concern.
Practice the prophetic model of de-escalation: a calm word or a simple redirection can dissolve conflict more effectively than winning the argument.

title: Distribution of Khaybar Booty and the Story of Mid'am's Death
summary: After the conquest of Khaybar, the Prophet's slave Mid'am was killed by a stray arrow and people called him a martyr, but the Prophet warned that a cloak he had taken without permission from the booty would cause him punishment in the hereafter.
story: When we conquered Khaybar, we did not gain any booty of gold or silver. We did take camels, cattle, personal possessions and properties. We then departed with the Messenger of God (SAAS) for Wadi al-Qura. With him was a slave of his called Mid'am, who had been presented to him by some of Banu al-Dahib. While he was mounting the saddle of the Messenger of God (SAAS), a stray arrow came in and struck him. People then said, 'Lucky for him; paradise!' But the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Definitely not; by Him in Whose hands is my soul, the cloak he took at Khaybar that the divider of the booty never received will light the fires (of hell) for him!' A man then approached, after having heard that from the Messenger of God (SAAS), carrying one or two sandal straps and said, 'This is something I captured.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'A sandal strap or two of hellfire!'
lessons: Outward appearances and a seemingly good death do not determine one's fate; only God knows the true state of a person's heart and deeds.
Honesty in all matters, especially regarding shared communal property and trust, is a foundational moral obligation in Islam.
Even small acts of dishonesty or taking what does not belong to us carry serious spiritual consequences that should not be dismissed.
practical_advice: Before acquiring anything, pause and ask yourself whether it is truly yours to take, whether in a workplace, shared home, or communal setting.
Cultivate a habit of scrupulous honesty in financial dealings, even when you believe no one is watching, as integrity in small matters builds integrity in large ones.
If you have taken something that was not rightfully yours, seek to return or account for it rather than rationalizing it away, as rectifying wrongs brings peace of conscience.

title: The Poisoned Sheep Presented to the Prophet at Khaybar
summary: After the conquest of Khaybar, a Jewish woman presented the Prophet with a poisoned roasted sheep to test whether he was truly a prophet; he was miraculously warned, but the poison had lasting effects on his health and killed one of his companions.
story: When Khaybar was conquered, a poisoned sheep was presented to the Prophet (SAAS). The latter then ordered, 'Assemble for me the Jews who were here.' They were brought and the Prophet (SAAS) asked them, 'I am about to ask you something. Will you respond truthfully about it?' 'Yes, we will, Aba Qasim,' they replied. He then asked them, 'Who is your father?' They replied, 'Our father is so-and-so.' He then said, 'You lie; your father is so-and-so!' They responded, 'You correctly speak the truth.' He then asked, 'Are you truthful about something if you are asked about it?' They replied, 'Yes, Aba Qasim. If we were to lie, you would know of it just as you did about our father.' He then asked, 'Who are those in hell?' They replied, 'We will be there for a short while, then you will remain there after us.' He responded, 'By God, we'll never be there after you.' He then asked them, 'Will you be truthful about something if I ask you?' 'Yes indeed, Aba al-Qasim,' they replied. He went on, 'Did you put poison in this sheep?' 'Yes,' they replied. 'What made you do that?' he asked. 'We wanted to be rid of you if you were a liar; and if you were a prophet, it would not have harmed you.' A Jewish woman of Khaybar poisoned a roasted sheep then presented it to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who took a shoulder and ate from it. A number of his Companions ate along with him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told them, 'Take your hands away!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent for the woman and asked her, 'Did you poison this sheep?' The Jewess replied, 'Who told you?' 'This in my hand told me,' he answered, referring to the shoulder of meat. She then said, 'Yes, I did.' He asked, 'What did you intend by that?' She replied, 'I told myself that if you were a prophet, it would not harm you. And if you were not a prophet, we would be rid of you.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then forgave her and did not punish her. Some of his Companions who had eaten of the sheep did die. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had blood drawn from his upper back because of his having eaten from the sheep. Aba Hind, an ansari of Banu Bayzada, drew the blood with the point and blade of a sword. Bishr b. al-Bara b. Ma'rur died and so the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent for the Jewess and asked her, 'What made you do this?' It is probable that he did not execute her initially, but did so when Bishr b. al-Bara died. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) conquered Khaybar, killing some of its inhabitants, the Jewess Zaynab, daughter of al-Harith, who was the niece of Marhab, presented to Safiyya some roast mutton that she had poisoned. She included plenty of shoulder and leg because she had learned that these were the parts of a sheep most liked by the Messenger of God (SAAS). The Messenger of God (SAAS) came in to Safiyya accompanied by Bishr b. al-Bara b. Ma'rur, of Banu Salama. She offered him the roast mutton and the Messenger of God (SAAS) took some shoulder and bit from it. Bishr took a bone and bit from it. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) swallowed his morsel, Bishr b. al-Bara swallowed what was in his mouth. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Take away your hands! The shoulder of this sheep tells me that my death will be announced because of it!' Bishr b. al-Bara then said, 'By Him who honoured you, I found that too in the piece I ate!'
lessons: The Prophet's miraculous awareness of the poison is itself a sign of his prophethood, affirming that truth and divine guidance will manifest even in moments of mortal danger.
Forgiveness in the face of betrayal and attempted murder is among the highest expressions of moral character, demonstrating that justice and mercy need not be mutually exclusive.
The effects of harm, even when survived, may linger — a reminder that the physical toll of trials on believers is real and should be acknowledged with compassion.
practical_advice: When someone wrongs you deeply, consider whether forgiveness or measured justice is the wiser course, seeking guidance through prayer and reflection rather than reacting from anger alone.
In moments of physical or emotional pain caused by another's betrayal, remember that enduring it with patience is itself an act of worship and a source of spiritual elevation.

Trust that truth has a way of revealing itself over time, as the Prophet's prophethood was confirmed even to his enemies through this very incident; let this encourage you to remain steadfast in integrity rather than resorting to deception.

title: Bishr ibn al-Bara's Sacrifice – Choosing Not to Warn the Prophet at the Cost of His Own Life
summary: Bishr ibn al-Bara ate from the poisoned sheep alongside the Prophet and, sensing the danger, chose not to warn the Prophet to stop eating out of deep respect and love, accepting his own death rather than cause the Prophet discomfort.
story: Bishr b. al-Bar2 then said, 'By Him who honoured you, I found that too in the piece I ate! The only thing that prevented me from voicing it was that I respected you too much to want to make you dislike your food! And when you swallowed what you had in your mouth, I could not allow myself to protect my life to the exclusion of you. I had hoped you would not have swallowed it, for in it is my death too.' And before Bishr could get up, his colour turned ashen and his pains were such that he could not move before he died.
lessons: True love sometimes expresses itself through silent sacrifice — Bishr's decision not to speak stemmed from a profound devotion that placed another's wellbeing above his own life.
Loyalty and selflessness, even when they come at great personal cost, are among the highest expressions of human character that Islamic tradition honors.
The grief of losing a companion is real and valid; the sorrow of those who witnessed Bishr's death reminds us that Islam does not ask us to suppress grief but to bear it with faith.
practical_advice: Reflect on the relationships in your life where someone has quietly sacrificed for you without making it known — expressing gratitude for these acts, even belatedly, is a spiritually meaningful practice.

When you are facing a situation where speaking up might cause discomfort to someone you love, seek wisdom in discerning when silence is protective and when it may cause harm — Bishr's case was extraordinary, but in everyday life, speaking truth with gentleness is often the wiser path.

If you are grieving the sudden loss of someone close, remember that the companions of the Prophet also experienced sudden, shocking deaths among their circle and continued their journey of faith — allow yourself to grieve while holding onto the belief that such losses are not without divine meaning.

title: The Soldier Who Stole a Necklace – Deception in the Distribution of Booty at Khaybar
summary: A companion celebrated for his bravery died at Khaybar, but the Prophet refused to declare him a martyr, and upon searching his belongings, a Jewish necklace he had taken without authorization was found — worth only a couple of dirhams.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Yahya b. Sacid related to him, from Muhammad h. Yahya b. Habban, from Abii Wmra, from Zayd h. Khdid al-Jubani, that one of the bravest of the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) died at Khaybar. When this was related to the Prophet (SAAS) he said, 'Pray for your comrade!' The faces of those present showed their surprise at his saying this and he went on, 'Your comrade strayed from God's path.' (The account states) 'So we searched his belongings and discovered one of those Jewish shell necklaces not worth more than a couple of dirhams!'
lessons: Outward bravery and religious standing do not exempt a person from accountability for hidden dishonesty; God's judgment is based on the totality of a person's deeds, including what is concealed.
Small acts of dishonesty — things that seem trivial in worldly terms — can carry heavy spiritual consequences when they involve taking what does not rightfully belong to us.
The companions' surprise at the Prophet's response is a reminder that we should not rush to judge spiritual outcomes based solely on how impressive a person appeared in life.
practical_advice: Take inventory of small dishonest acts in your own life — minor deceptions, things taken without full authorization, corners cut — and make a sincere intention to correct them before they accumulate spiritual weight.

If you are in a position of leadership or community trust, uphold transparency in how shared resources or responsibilities are handled, knowing that accountability before God is more comprehensive than human observation.

When someone you admire or respect is found to have acted wrongly in a hidden matter, resist both the urge to excuse it entirely and the urge to condemn them wholly — hold the complexity with fairness and use it as a reminder of your own accountability.

title: The Tribe of Banu Fazara Flees from the Prophet's Messenger on the Return from Khaybar
summary: On the return journey from Khaybar, the tribe of Banu Fazara assembled intending to fight the Prophet but fled quickly when the Prophet sent word proposing a location for battle.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related that Banii Fazaa wanted to do battle against the Messenger of God (SAAS) on his way back from Khaybar and assembled for this purpose. He then sent a messenger to them suggesting a specific site for the battle. Having ascertained this, they fled as fast as they could, moving far outside his path.
lessons: Those who plan aggression against a cause rooted in truth often lose resolve when confronted directly — the Prophet's calm, confident response to a threat converted it into a non-event.
Proactive, measured communication in the face of conflict can dissolve hostility without bloodshed, as the Prophet's choice to propose a direct engagement caused the aggressors to retreat.
Fear of a just confrontation is itself a form of internal judgment; when we know our cause lacks righteousness, we often flee from accountability.
practical_advice: When you are being threatened or pressured by those whose intentions are hostile, consider responding with calm directness rather than panic — clearly naming the situation and your willingness to address it head-on can defuse aggression.
If you are anxious about a conflict that feels imminent, reflect on whether the anxiety is proportionate to the actual threat — sometimes fears dissolve when brought into the open.

In moments of collective tension or group conflict, ask yourself honestly whether your participation is rooted in principle or in social pressure, and be willing to step back if your cause cannot withstand honest scrutiny.

title: The Prophet's Marriage to Safiyya and Abu Ayyub's Night Watch
summary: After the battle of Khaybar, the Prophet married Safiyya bint Huyayy, who had accepted Islam and was freed. His companion Abu Ayyub al-Ansari kept watch outside the Prophet's tent throughout the night out of protective concern, for which the Prophet prayed for him.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) proceeded further and when Safiyya was free of her period he consummated his marriage with her at a place called Sudd al-Sahbal, on his route to Medina. He served hays for a wedding feast and stayed there with her for three nights. She accepted Islam and he freed her and married her. He gave her freedom as her dowry. She was one of the ummahat al-mdminin, 'mothers of the believers' as his Companions understood when he placed a veil over her when she rode behind him on the same mount. May God be pleased with her. Muhammad b. Ishaq related in his biography of the Prophet (SAAS) as follows, 'When he married Safiyya at Khaybar - on the way back - the woman who combed her hair and prepared her for him was Umm Sulaym, daughter of Milhm, the mother of Anas h. Mdik. The Messenger of God (SAAS) spent the night with her in a pavilion of his, while Aba Ayyiib kept watch over him, wearing his sword, patrolling around the tent until morning. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw him there, he asked him, "What is wrong, Abii Ayytib?" He replied, "I was afraid for you from this woman. She is a woman whose father, husband and people you have killed. And until recently she was a disbeliever. So I feared for you from her." They say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, "O God, preserve Abii Ayyiib, just as he spent the night watching over me."'
lessons: Genuine concern for the safety and wellbeing of those we love, even when expressed imperfectly or unnecessarily, is recognized and honored by God — Abu Ayyub's vigilance, though perhaps unneeded, earned him the Prophet's sincere supplication.

The Prophet's response to Abu Ayyub was not rebuke but prayer, teaching us that those who act out of sincere love deserve appreciation rather than embarrassment, even when their concern turns out to be unfounded.
Safiyya's transition from a context of loss and war to becoming a respected mother of the believers demonstrates that identity, dignity, and belonging can be renewed even after profound upheaval.
practical_advice: If someone in your life expresses worry or concern for you in a way that feels excessive or mistrustful, try to receive it with the understanding that it may come from genuine love — address the concern gently rather than dismissing it.

When you are going through a major life transition — a new relationship, a new role, a new community — allow yourself time to settle in and be known for who you are becoming, not only for where you have come from.

If you find yourself worrying about someone you care about in the night hours, channel that energy into sincere prayer for them rather than rumination — Abu Ayyub turned his anxiety into active, devoted protection.

title: The Overslept Morning Prayer on the Return from Khaybar – Bilal and the Prophet Wake Late
summary: On the journey back from Khaybar, the Prophet and his companions including Bilal overslept and missed the morning prayer at its proper time. Bilal had been tasked with keeping watch but fell asleep. The Prophet responded with calm, moved the camp, performed ablution, and led the prayer.
story: He went on, 'Al-Zuhri related to me, from Sacid h. al-Musayyab, how they had overslept past the morning prayer on their way back from Khaybar. He told how the Messenger of God (SAAS) was the first of them to wake up and that he then asked, "What have you done to us, Bihl!" He replied, "Messenger of God, He who took my soul was He who took yours too!" "You speak the truth," he agreed. He then rode his camel a little way off and dismounted, performed the ablution and the prayer as he had done previously.' Aha Da'ad stated that h a d b. Sdih related to him, quoting Ibn Wahb (who said), 'Yanus informed me, from Ibn Shihah, from Sacidh. al-Musayyab, from Aha Hurayra (who said), the Messenger of God (SAAS), having finished with Khaybar, travelled on by night until we reached al-Kari, and there he had his wedding. He told Bilal, "Keep awake for us tonight." But Bilal's eyes closed in sleep as he rested against his baggage and neither the Prophet (SAAS) nor he, nor any of the others woke up before the sun struck them. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was the first to wake up. In alarm he called out Bilzl's name. Bilal replied, "By my father and mother, Messenger of God, He who took my soul away also took yours!" They rode their camels off a little way and then the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the ablution. He gave orders to Bild who announced the prayer, and then he led the morning prayer for them. When he had finished it, he said, "One who forgets a prayer should perform it when he remembers. For God Almighty says, 'Perform the prayer for remembrance (of Me)'" (sayat TZHZ; XX, v.14).'
lessons: Missing an act of worship due to genuine human limitation — fatigue, illness, forgetfulness — is not a spiritual catastrophe; the proper response is to make it up with sincerity and move forward without excessive guilt.

The Prophet's calm, practical response to oversleeping — moving the camp, performing ablution, leading prayer — models the principle that what matters after a mistake is not self-flagellation but composed corrective action.

Bilal's response, 'He who took my soul also took yours,' is a beautiful expression of how sleep itself is a trust from God, reminding us that we do not control every moment of our lives and that humility in the face of human limitation is itself a form of wisdom.
practical_advice: If you miss a prayer, a commitment, or an important obligation due to genuine oversight or exhaustion, resist the spiral of shame — acknowledge it, make it up as soon as you are able, and continue.
When you are responsible for waking others or keeping watch and you fail, own it directly and honestly as Bilal did, without excessive deflection, and move quickly to remedy the situation.

Build routines and backup systems for your most important daily obligations — the Prophet had appointed Bilal specifically to keep watch, showing that even the most disciplined people benefit from external supports for important commitments.

title: 'Uyayna ibn Hisn's Dreams and al-Harith ibn 'Awf's Account of Jewish Prophecy About the Prophet
summary: 'Uyayna ibn Hisn had dreams urging him to fight the Prophet but arrived at Khaybar too late. Al-Harith ibn 'Awf then recounted how even Abu Rafi' Sallam ibn Abi Huqayq, a Jewish leader, had privately acknowledged the Prophet's prophethood and predicted two devastating defeats for the Jews.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that 'Uyayna b. Hisn, before he accepted Islam, saw visions in his sleep while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was besieging Khaybar. These visions inspired in him a desire to battle against the Messenger of God (SAAS) and to conquer him. When he did go to the Messenger of God (SAAS), at Khaybar, he found he had already taken it. So 'Uyayna said, 'Muhammad, give me some of the booty you have taken from my allies' - meaning the people of Khaybar. But the Messenger of God (SAAS), told him, 'Your visions lied!' And he then told 'Uyayna what he had seen in his dream. (The account went on) ''Uyayna then went home and was met by al-Harith b. 'Awf, who said, "Did I not say you were putting your loyalty in the wrong place? By God, Muhammad will triumph over all between East and West! Jews used to inform me of this. I swear, I heard Aba Rafi' Sallm h. Abti Huqayq say, 'We envy Muhammad for the prophethood, since it has now left Aaron's people. He has been sent, though Jews will not obey me in this. We will suffer two massacres from him - one at Yathrib, the other at Khaybar.'"' Al-Harith went on, 'So I asked Sallam, "Will he govern the earth?" "Yes," he replied, "he will, by the Torah that was revealed to Moses, but I don't want Jews to be told of what I said of him."'
lessons: Truth has a way of being acknowledged even by those who oppose it — the private admission of Abu Rafi' Sallam shows that recognition of the Prophet's truth existed even among his enemies, pointing to the futility of opposing what is divinely ordained.
Misguided visions and aspirations, when pursued without grounding in truth, lead only to wasted effort and embarrassment — 'Uyayna arrived to find his dreams had already been overtaken by reality.

Those who privately know the truth but suppress it out of communal or social loyalty place themselves in a tragic position — Sallam acknowledged the Prophet's truthfulness privately but feared sharing it, a reminder that courage in acknowledging truth is itself a spiritual obligation.
practical_advice: If you find yourself holding beliefs or loyalties that you privately sense are misaligned with truth, give yourself permission to examine them honestly rather than suppressing the discomfort — private acknowledgment is the first step toward alignment.

When you have been proven wrong in a strongly held position, as 'Uyayna was, resist the temptation to demand compensation or to save face through negotiation — accept the outcome with dignity and reflect on what the experience is teaching you.

If you are in a community where speaking the truth carries social risk, find at least one trusted person with whom you can be fully honest, as Sallam spoke privately to al-Harith — the inability to be honest with anyone is a sign that isolation from truth is deepening.

title: Mid'am's False Martyrdom – The Cloak Stolen from the Booty at Khaybar
summary: A man named Mid'am was struck by a stray arrow and killed, and those present assumed he had died as a martyr. But the Prophet declared he was not in paradise because he had taken a cloak from the Khaybar booty before it was properly distributed.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'When he had finished with Khaybar, the Messenger of God (SAAS) left for Wadi alqura. He besieged its inhabitants for some days and then returned to Medina.' He then went on to relate the story of Mid'am and how he was struck and killed by a stray arrow. (He told) how people then said, 'Lucky for him: paradise!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) had responded, 'Definitely not; by Him in whose hands is my soul, the cloak he took at Khaybar that the distributor of the booty did not receive will light fires (of hell) for him!'
lessons: The manner or circumstances of a person's death do not automatically determine their spiritual standing — only God knows the full account of a person's deeds, including what they hid.
Taking what belongs to the community before it is properly distributed is a form of betrayal of trust that carries serious spiritual consequences, regardless of how small the item seems.

We should be careful about making confident spiritual pronouncements about others, whether positive or negative — the companions' assumption of Mid'am's paradise was corrected by the Prophet, reminding us that such judgments belong to God alone.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether you are fully honest in how you handle shared resources, communal funds, or collective goods — small violations of trust in communal settings are easy to rationalize but carry weight.

Resist the habit of assuming that someone who dies in apparently honorable circumstances was necessarily righteous in all things — hold space for the complexity of human lives without rushing to judgments about their ultimate fate.

If you are in a leadership role responsible for distributing shared resources, establish clear, transparent processes — the corruption that surrounded Mid'am's death began with an informal act of taking before official distribution, which proper systems can prevent.

title: Sleeping Through the Prayer and the Miraculous Water Vessel
summary: During one of the Prophet's expeditions, the companions slept through the morning prayer and were later blessed with a miraculous water vessel whose supply did not diminish despite quenching the entire army.
story: called out Bilzl's name. Bilal replied, 'By my father and mother, Messenger of God, He who took my soul away also took yours!' They rode their camels off a little way and then the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the ablution. He gave orders to Bild who announced the prayer, and then he led the morning prayer for them. When he had finished it, he said, 'One who forgets a prayer should perform it when he remembers. For God Almighty says, "Perform the prayer for remembrance (of Me)"' (sayat TZHZ;XX, v.14). Yanus stated, 'Ibn Shihzb used to recite it (the hadith) like that.' Muslim related it the same from Harmala b. Y*y% from 'Abd Allah h. Wahb. Therein is stated that this was on their return from Khaybar. In the hadith of Shu'ba, from J~ic b. Shaddad, from 'Ahd al-Rabm b. Aha 'Alqama, from Ibn Mascad, it is stated that occurred on their return from al-Hudaybiyya. In one report from him (it states) that it was Bild who kept awake for them. And there is a report from him (Bilal) that it was he who used to keep awake for them. The ha.? al-Bayhaqi stated, 'It is likely that this happened twice.' He went on, 'Andin a hadith of Clmrm h. H u~ayn and Aba Qamda (there is reference to) their sleeping through the prayer. Also in it is the reference to the water vessel. It is likely that this makes reference to one of the two occasions in question, or it might be a third.' He went on, 'Al-Wqidi related in a hadith of Aba @&a that that happened on their way hack from the expedition against Tabiik. He stated, "Zafir b. Sulayman narrated, from Shu'ba, from Jamic b. Shaddad, from 'Abd al-Rabman, from Ihn Mascad, that that occurred on their way back from Tahak. But God knows best."' Al-Bayhaqi then proceeded to relate what the author of the sahib collection reported concerning the anecdote of 'Awf al-Acrabi, from Aba Rajs', from qmrm b. Hu~ayn. It tells of their having slept through the prayer and that there was a woman who owned two water skins from which sufficient water was drawn to quench the entire army without diminishing the water in them. He then recounted what Muslim related from the hadith of Thiibit al-Buniini, from 'Abd Allah b. Ribah, from Aba Qatada. It is a long hadith and tells of their sleeping through the prayer and of the great quantity of water taken from that water vessel. 'Ahd al-Razaq related this from Ma'mar, from Qatiida.
lessons: Missing a prayer due to genuine circumstances like sleep or forgetfulness is pardoned in Islam; the obligation is to pray as soon as one remembers, without guilt or despair.
The Prophet's calm and composed response to missing the prayer models how believers should handle unintentional religious lapses — with correction, not shame.
Divine mercy can manifest in unexpected ways, as the miraculous water vessel demonstrated; trust in God's provision even in moments of insufficiency.
practical_advice: If you miss a prayer or religious obligation unintentionally, perform it as soon as you remember rather than letting guilt paralyze you or cause you to abandon it altogether.
When facing communal stress or shared hardship, follow the Prophet's example of immediately re-establishing structure and worship as an anchor for the community.
Reflect on moments in your life where provision came unexpectedly; use these as reminders of God's care to counter feelings of anxiety about future scarcity.

title: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari and the Treasure of Paradise
summary: On the way to or from Khaybar, the Prophet overheard Abu Musa al-Ash'ari reciting 'La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah' and told him it was a phrase from the treasures of paradise.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Mass b. Isma'il related to him, quoting 'Abd al-W%id, from 'A~im, from Abn CU, from Aba Masii al-Ash'ari, about the expedition of the Messenger of God (SAAS) against Khaybar. Abn Mas% stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) moved out towards Khaybar and when his men looked down into a valley, they raised their voices in a shout of "Allahu Akbar! La Zlaha alla Allah! God is most Great! There is no god but God!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) told them, "Restrain yourselves! You are not calling out to someone deaf or far away; you are addressing One who is All-Hearing and near at hand; He is with you!" I was directly behind the mount of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he heard me as I said, "La hamla ma la quwwara allii br Allah, 'There is no strength nor power except with God'." He said, "Ahd Alliih b. Qays." I responded, "At your service, Messenger of God." He then said, "Should I not direct you to a phrase from the treasures of paradise?" "Yes, please do, Messenger of God, may my father and mother be your ransom!" He went on, "La hamla ma Iii quwmata rlla br Alliih."' The other authorities relate this too, along various lines of transmission, from 'Abd al-&an h. Mull, father of CUthmm al-Nahdi, from Abu Mas~ al-Ash'ari. What is correct is that this incident refers to their return from Khaybar. Aba Masii only arrived after the conquest of Khaybar, as is related above.
lessons: The remembrance of God — especially acknowledging that all strength belongs to Him — is a spiritual treasure that costs nothing yet yields immeasurable reward.
The Prophet corrected his companions' loud proclamations not to diminish their enthusiasm but to deepen their understanding of God's nearness, teaching that true devotion is internal and intimate.
Simple phrases of remembrance, if uttered sincerely, carry divine worth that surpasses worldly riches.
practical_advice: Incorporate 'La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah' into your daily routine, especially during moments of difficulty or overwhelm, as a reminder that you do not carry your burdens alone.
When you feel the urge to make grand gestures of devotion, remember the Prophet's guidance: sincerity and awareness of God's nearness matter more than volume or show.
Share uplifting spiritual reminders with those around you, just as the Prophet did with Abu Musa, as an act of kindness that strengthens community and faith.

title: The Martyrs of Khaybar
summary: Ibn Ishaq records the names of the Companions who were martyred during the Battle of Khaybar, honoring their sacrifice for the early Muslim community.
story: An Account of those of the Companions who were martyred at Khaybar, may God be pleased with them, according to what Ibn Ishiiq b. Yasiir, may God have mercy on him, and other authorities on the military engagements reported. Of the best of the muh~jiirnwere Rabi'a b. Aktham b. Sakhbara al-Asadi, freed-man of Bana Umayya, and Thaqif h. 'Amr and Rifn'a b. Masriih, allies of Bana Umayya, 'Abd Allah h. al-Huhayb b. Uhayh b. Snhayrn h. Ghira of Bmii Sa'd b. Layth, ally of Banii Asad, and their nephew. Of the aqZr, there were Bishr b. al-Barn' b. Ma'riir, who ate from the poisoned sheep in the company of the Messenger of God (SAAS), as is told above, and Fudayl b. d-NncmZn, both these men being of Bana Salam. Then there were Masciid h. Sacd b. Qays b. Khdid b. 'Amir b. Zurayq al-Zarqi, M&miid h. Maslama al-Ashhali, Abii Dayy* Htritha b. Thsbit h. d-Nucmnn al-'Amri, d-H~ith b. HSeh, Wrwa b. Murra b. Suraqa, Aws h. d-Fa'id, Unayf b. Habib, Th2hit b. Athila, Talha, W m a a h. Wqha, L i e d by an arrow, 'Amir h. al-Akwac, and then Salama b. 'Amr b. al-Akwac; he was struck in his knee by the edge of his sword and it killed him, as is told above; may God be pleased with hi. Then there was al-Aswad, the shepherd. Ibn Ishnq is alone in telling here the story of the last-mentioned. We already related it early on in the account of the expedition. And to God belong all praise and credit. Ibn Ishnq stated, 'According to Ibn Shihab, those of Bana Zahra who were martyred were Mascad b. Rabi'a, an ally of theirs from al-Qtra. And of the aqsr of Banii 'Amr h. 'Awf there was Aws h. Qamda, may God he pleased with them all.'
lessons: Every name recorded among the martyrs represents a life given in sincere devotion; remembering them teaches us the true cost and value of standing for one's convictions.
Martyrdom in Islamic tradition is not a tragedy without meaning but a transition to divine mercy; grief and gratitude can coexist when loss is understood through a lens of faith.
The breadth of those who fell — freed slaves, allies, Ansar, Muhajirun — shows that sacrifice transcends social rank and origin.
practical_advice: When grieving the loss of loved ones, draw comfort from the Islamic understanding that those who die in sincere service to God are honored, not forgotten.
Honor the memory of those who sacrificed for your community, faith, or family by striving to live with the same sincerity and purpose they demonstrated.
If you feel your struggles go unnoticed, reflect on the fact that every name of every martyr was preserved and honored; God sees and records every act of devotion, however small.

title: Al-Hajjaj b. 'Ilat's Ruse to Retrieve His Wealth from Mecca
summary: After the conquest of Khaybar, a newly Muslim companion Al-Hajjaj b. 'Ilat sought permission from the Prophet to travel to Mecca and use deception to safely retrieve his wealth, spreading false news of a Muslim defeat to protect himself.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'When Khayhar had been conquered, the Messenger of God (SAAS) talked with al-Hajjnj b. 'Ilat al-Sulami, of the sub-tribe of Bahz, who asked him, "Messenger of God, in Mecca I own money in the possession of my wife Umm Shayba, daughter of Aha T&a (she had earlier borne him his son Mu'rid h. al-Hajjaj) and I also have other funds held by various merchants there. Please allow me to go there." He did so. Al-Hajjaj then said, "Messenger of God, I'll he forced to speak lies." "Do so," he told him. Al-Hajjiij stated, "I travelled to Mecca and at the pass al-Baydii' there were men of Quraysh listening for news and asking about the activities of the Messenger of God (SAAS). They had heard that he had gone to Khaybar, which they knew to be the chief village of Hija, productive, well-fortified and well populated. They were seeking information from travellers. When they saw me, they said, "It's al-Hajjaj h. 'Ilii!." (They did not know of my acceptance of Islam.) "He'll have news, by God. Tell us, Aba Muhammad. We have learned that al-Qa.t6, 'the breaker of family ties', has gone off to Khaybar, a town of Jews and the 'farm of the Hija'." I replied, "I heard that too; and I have news that will please you." They crowded at both sides of my camel asking, "Well, what is it, Hajjaj!" I replied, "He has been badly defeated, such as you could never imagine. Very many of his men have been killed and Muhammad has been taken captive. And they (the Jews) have said, 'We'll not kill him; instead, we'll send him in to Mecca where they can kill him among themselves in revenge for those of their men he killed.'" (Hearing this) the Quraysh men went off into Mecca shouting, "News has arrived! You need only wait and Muhammad will he brought in to be killed among you!"' Al-Hajj@ went on, 'I asked them, "Please help me collect the money I own and is owed me in Mecca; I want to go to Khaybar and acquire the spoils from Muhammad and his men before some other merchants precede me there." And they did, gathering my assets faster than I had ever heard before. I then went to my wife and said, "I want my money" (I had left money there with her), "for I want to go to Khayhar to seize opportunities to buy before other merchants precede me there." When alLcAbbiisb. 'Abd al-Muttalibn heard the news and about myself, he came and stood beside me in the tent of a merchant. He asked, "Hajjiij, what news is this you bring?" I replied, "Could you keep safe what I might entrust with you?" "Yes," he answered. "Then wait", I told him, "till I can meet you alone; I'm collecting my funds, as you can see; please leave me until I finish." When I had finished getting together everything I had in Mecca and was ready to leave, I met al-cAbhias and said, "Aha al-Fadl, keep private what I tell you for three days, because I fear pursuit. After that, say whatever you like." "I agree," he said. I then told him, "I left your nephew the bridegroom of their king's daughter" — meaning Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy — "after he had conquered Khaybar and had taken away all it contained for himself and his men." "What are you saying, Hajjaj?" be asked. "It's true, by God," I assured him, "hut keep it confidential about me. I have accepted Islam and only came for my money fearing it might he seized. When three days have elapsed, you can tell what you know as ever you want." On the third day, al-'Ahbas dressed in a fine robe, put on perfume, took his walking stick and went out to the ka'ba, which he perambulated. When people saw him, they said, "Abii al-Fadl, this must imply, by God, that you are remaining steadfast in the face of some great misfortune!" "Not at all, by God, by Whom you swear oaths. Muhammad has conquered Khaybar and has married their king's daughter after seizing all their possessions, whicb now belong to him and his men." "Who brought you this news?" they asked. "The same man who brought news to you! He came to you as a Muslim, took his money and has gone off to join Muhammad and his men!" "O servants of God," they exclaimed, "God's enemy has escaped! If we had known this he would have had trouble with us!" Soon after, news came to them confirming'
lessons: Standing firm in faith sometimes requires navigating hostile environments with wisdom and discretion; concealing one's belief when under genuine threat has been permitted in Islamic jurisprudence.
The story of Al-Hajjaj illustrates that trust between a leader and follower, built on sincere intention, can allow for flexibility in extraordinary circumstances.
Al-Abbas's patient restraint for three days, followed by his joyful public proclamation of the truth, shows that the right moment for speaking truth can multiply its impact.
practical_advice: When you are in an environment hostile to your identity or values, seek wise counsel about how to navigate it safely rather than either recklessly exposing yourself or permanently hiding who you are.
If you carry good news that could cause you harm if shared prematurely, practice the wisdom of timing — truth shared at the right moment is far more powerful than truth shared recklessly.
Reflect on moments when you concealed something important out of fear; consider whether the time has come, as it did for Al-Abbas, to step forward with confidence and share what is true.

title: Settlement of Wadi al-Qura and Tayma' Jews Under Jizya
summary: After the conquest of Khaybar and Fadak, the Prophet spent four days at Wadi al-Qura dividing spoils, while the Jews of Tayma' sought peace and agreed to pay the jizya poll-tax upon hearing of the Muslim victories.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained at Wadi al-Qura for four days. He divided up what he had taken among his men, leaving the land and palm trees in the hands of the Jews, putting them in charge of it. When news reached the Jews of Tayma' how the Messenger of God (SAAS) had defeated Khaybar, Fadak and Wadi al-Qurit, they made peace with him, agreeing to pay the jrzya, the poll-tax. They kept their possessions. Although Wmar had later evicted the Jews from Khaybar and Fadak, he did not force out the people of Tayma' and Wadi d-Qura, because the latter extended into the land of Syria; he considered that what was between Wadi al-Qura and Medina to be part of Hijzz, areas beyond any part of Syria. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left to return to Medina, having now finished with Khaybar and Wadi al-Qura and God, Almighty and Glorious is He, having given him much booty.
lessons: Peace and negotiated settlement can prevent further conflict and loss of life on all sides.
Wisdom in leadership means accepting reasonable terms when offered, rather than insisting on prolonged confrontation.
Divine assistance manifests in ways that extend beyond the battlefield, as news of victories can bring entire communities to peaceful terms.
practical_advice: When facing a conflict, consider whether a peaceful agreement that satisfies core needs might be preferable to continued struggle.
Reflect on how the outcomes of past challenges can open new doors and opportunities you did not anticipate.
Trust that perseverance through difficult periods can lead to resolutions that benefit everyone involved.

title: Warning Against Visiting Wives After Night Prayer and Its Consequence
summary: The Prophet warned his companions not to visit their wives after the late evening prayer, but one man disobeyed and discovered something troubling, yet chose not to divorce his wife out of love for her and their children.
story: Al-Waqidi went on, 'Ya'qiib b. Muhammad related to me, from 'Abd al-R&mm h. 'Ahd Allah h. Abii Sa'sa'a, from al-H&th h. 'Abd Allah h. Ka'b, from Umm 'Imzra, who said, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) at al-Jnrf saying, 'Do not make your way to the women after the Salzr al-'asha', the late evening prayer.' One man from the quarter did make his way to his family, but found what he did not like. But he left her free and did not himself leave her. He was reluctant to give up his wife by separating from her. He had children by her and loved her. So by disobeying the Messenger of God (SAAS) he had seen what he did not like."'
lessons: Prophetic guidance, even when its wisdom is not immediately apparent, is meant to protect us from harm and distress.
Love and commitment to family can be tested by moments of disobedience and their consequences, but forgiveness and patience can preserve bonds.
The pain of a discovered wrong can be the direct result of ignoring sound counsel, reminding us to trust in the guidance given to us.
practical_advice: When you receive trusted advice about a situation, reflect carefully before acting against it, even if your desires push you otherwise.
If you discover something painful in a relationship, resist making impulsive decisions and give yourself time to process before acting.
Consider the full weight of your responsibilities—to your children, your spouse, and your community—before making major relational decisions in moments of distress.

title: The Sharecropping Arrangement with the Jews of Khaybar
summary: After conquering Khaybar, the Prophet allowed its Jewish inhabitants to remain and work the land under a sharecropping agreement, with the proceeds divided equally, and appointed Abdullah ibn Rawaha to oversee fair appraisals.
story: It is established in both Sahih collections that when Khaybar was conquered, the Messenger of God (SAAS) placed the Jews in charge of it in return for a half of its dates and other produce. In some phrases of this hadith it is implied that he was removing it from their ownership, while in others it is stated, 'The Prophet (SAAS) stated to them: "We will keep you in them for as long as we wish."' In the Sunan works it is stated that he would send 'Ahd All& b. Raw@a to appraise it for them when he came to take the harvest, holding them responsible for it. When 'Ahd All& h. Raw*a was killed at Mn'ta, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Jahbiir h. Sakhr to do this. Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, 'I asked Ibn Sh'ihab how it was that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had given the Jews of Khaybar their palm-groves. He informed me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) conquered Khaybar by force and after fighting. Khaybar was part of what God bestowed upon him as war booty; he divided its proceeds into fifths, distributing it among the Muslims. After the fighting, some of its inhabitants came out to leave and the Messenger of God (SAAS) called for them to approach and told them, "If you wish, I will make over these properties to you on condition that you work them; their proceeds will be divided between us. I will let you stay for as long as God lets you stay." They accepted and worked the land in accord with this agreement. The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to send 'Ahd Allah b. Raw@a to divide up the proceeds, treating them fairly in the appraisal.'
lessons: True justice includes ensuring that even those who were opponents are treated with fairness in economic arrangements.
Practical mercy—allowing people to maintain their livelihoods and dignity after conflict—can create more lasting peace than harsh measures.
Trustworthy stewardship of shared resources, as modeled by Abdullah ibn Rawaha, is a form of worship and service to the community.
practical_advice: When resolving a conflict, look for arrangements that allow all parties to maintain their dignity and contribute meaningfully.
Appoint or seek out trustworthy people to oversee fair dealings in shared or contested situations.
Even after a difficult dispute, consider whether a cooperative arrangement might serve everyone's long-term wellbeing better than a complete severing of ties.

title: Umar's Eviction of the Jews of Khaybar Based on the Prophet's Final Instruction
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab, upon learning of the Prophet's deathbed statement that two religions should not coexist in the Arabian Peninsula, investigated its authenticity and ultimately evicted the Jews of Khaybar who had no formal contracts with the Prophet.
story: When God took to Himself His Prophet (SAAS), Abn Bakr affirmed Khaybar in their control on the same basis as that which the Messenger of God (SAAS) had maintained before he died. Wmar b. al-Kha~ab reconfirmed them in this from the beginning of his rule. But then Wmar learned that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said during that illness during which God took hold of him, 'Two religions shall not be together in the Arabian peninsula!' Wmar examined this issue closely until he became utterly convinced of its authenticity, at which point he sent a message to the Jews saying, 'God has granted me permission to evict you. I have learned that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Two religions shall not be together in the Arabian peninsula." Anyone with a contract with the Messenger of God (SAAS) should bring it to me and I will enforce it. Those without such contracts should prepare to leave.' And Wmar did evict those who had no contracts with the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Leadership requires the courage to act on truth once it is verified, even when it means reversing a long-standing policy.
Careful investigation before making a major decision reflects wisdom and justice, protecting both the leader and those affected.
Honoring the final instructions of a trusted guide is a form of loyalty that transcends personal comfort or convenience.
practical_advice: Before making a major life decision that goes against the status quo, take time to verify the principles guiding that decision rather than acting impulsively.
When you discover that a past course of action was not aligned with your core values or guidance, have the courage to make the necessary correction.
Distinguish between obligations rooted in formal commitments and those that are informal, and honor your formal commitments even in difficult circumstances.

title: The Fake Document Claiming the Prophet Exempted Khaybar Jews from Jizya
summary: Ibn Kathir exposes a fraudulent document falsely attributed to the Prophet that claimed to exempt the Jews of Khaybar from the jizya, noting it contained testimony from men who were either dead before the relevant date or had not yet accepted Islam.
story: I comment that the Jews of Khaybar in latter-day times, after 300 (years) claim to have in their possession a document from the Messenger of God (SAAS), in which it states that he exempted them from paying the jrzya, the poll-tax. Some scholars have been so deluded by this document as to advocate exempting them from the jrzya, this being Sheikh Aha 'Ali b. Khayrtin, of the Shg'i school. The document is spurious and fake, devoid of authenticity; I have demonstrated its foolishness from many viewpoints in a separate hook. A number of colleagues have referred to it and to its foolishness in their writings, such as Ibn al-Sibngh, in his work Masrt'rl and Sheikh Abti H%nid in his commentary. Ibn al-Maslama devoted a separate section to refer to and to refute it. Now, 700 years later, they are all excited about this and have brought out a book with a copy not mentioned by the early authorities. I have examined it and found it to be fake. It contains testimony of Sa'd b. Musdh, though he had died before the date of Khaybar. It also gives the testimony of Mu'nwiya b. Abn Sufynn, though he had not yet accepted Islam.
