At its end it states, 'And 'Ali b. Aba Tnlib wrote it.' This is incorrect, a mistake. It refers to the jazya, too, even though this had not yet been legislated. This tax was first introduced and taken from the people of Najmn. They are said to have arrived at the end of 9 AH.
lessons: Critical examination of religious or historical sources is a duty, not an act of doubt—it protects the integrity of the tradition.
False narratives, no matter how old or widely circulated, can and should be challenged through careful scholarship and internal evidence.
Being deceived by a forged document is a reminder that emotional or political desires can cloud our judgment when evaluating claims.
practical_advice: When you encounter a claim that seems to justify an unusual exception or privilege, apply critical thinking and seek multiple verified sources before accepting it.
Do not allow the age or apparent authority of a source to substitute for actual scrutiny of its content and internal consistency.
If you feel confused or misled by conflicting information, take a step back, identify what can be independently verified, and build your understanding from that foundation.

title: The Attack on Ibn Umar at Khaybar and Umar's Eviction of the Jews
summary: While inspecting their properties at Khaybar, Ibn Umar was attacked at night and his wrists were dislocated; Umar, convinced the Jews were responsible, addressed the people and proceeded to evict them entirely.
story: Ibn Ishzq stated, 'Nafi', freed-man of 'Abd Allah b. Wmar related to me, from Ibn Wmar, who said, "I, al-Zubayr h. al-'Amour and al-Miq& b. al-Aswad went out to inspect our properties at Khaybar. When we arrived there, we separated and went to our properties. During the night, while I slept on my bed, an attack was made on me and my wrists were dislocated. When I cried out for my companions, they came and asked me who had done this. I told them I did not know. They administered to my hands and then took me to 'Umar, who declared, 'This is the work of the Jews of Khaybar!' He then stood up before the people to make a speech and said, 'People, the Messenger of God (SAAS) had an agreement with the Jews of Khaybar that we should evict them if we wished. They have attacked 'Abd All& h. Wmar and dislocated his hands, as you have learned, having attacked one of the ansar before him. We have no doubt that it was they who did it; we have no enemies here besides them. Those who have property at Khaybar should proceed to them; I am going to evict the Jews.' And he did evict them."'
lessons: A leader's response to injustice must be proportionate and grounded in prior established agreements, not merely emotional reaction.
Physical harm done to one member of a community can be the tipping point that reveals a deeper, unresolved tension requiring resolution.
Safety and security of the community sometimes requires difficult decisions, especially when trust has been repeatedly broken.
practical_advice: If you experience an unexpected harm, try to gather information calmly before assigning blame, even when suspicion is strong.
When an agreement is broken, revisit what terms were originally set and act in accordance with those terms rather than out of pure anger.
Address conflicts that have been deferred for too long before they escalate into physical harm or irreparable damage.

title: The Expedition of Abu Bakr to Banu Fazara
summary: Abu Bakr led a night expedition against Banu Fazara, during which Salama ibn al-Akwa intercepted fleeing captives including a beautiful young woman, whom the Prophet later requested and used to ransom Muslim prisoners held in Mecca.
story: Ahmad stated that Bahz related to him, quoting 'Ikrima h. 'Amma, quoting Iy% b. Salama (who said), 'My father related to me, saying, "We went out with Abii Bakr b. Aha Quhafa, the Messenger of God (SAAS) having given forth with him command over us. We attacked Banii Fazara, and when we arrived near the well, Aha Bakr gave us orders and we dismounted to rest for the night. When we had performed the al-subh prayer, Ahii Bakr ordered us to attack. We killed at the well those who passed before us." Salama went on, "I then looked over at the mass of the enemy which included children and women moving towards the mountain while I pursued them. I was concerned that they would get to the mountain ahead of me, so I cast an arrow which fell between them and the mountain. I then led them back to Aba Bakr whom I reached at the well. Among the enemy there was a woman of Fazaa wearing a worn-out leather garment. With her she had her daughter, an unusually attractive Arab girl. Aba Bakr presented her daughter to me. I did not sleep with her before we reached Medina, then again I went to bed without sleeping with her. The Messenger of God (SAAS) met me in the market and asked me, 'Salama, give the woman to me!' I replied, 'By God, Messenger of God, she attracts me greatly but I've not slept with her.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained silent and left me. Next day he met me in the market and said, 'Salama, give the woman to me!' I replied, 'By God, Messenger of God, she attracts me greatly, but I've not slept with her!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained silent and left me. Next day the Messenger of God (SAAS) met me in the market and said, 'Salama, give the woman to me, may God preserve your father!' I replied, 'I swear by God, Messenger of God, I've not slept with her, but she is yours.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent her to the people of Qpwa, Mecca who had in their hands some Muslim prisoners. The Messenger of God (SAAS) ransomed them with this woman."'
lessons: Personal sacrifice for the greater good—giving up something of value to free those in need—is among the highest forms of generosity.
The Prophet's gentle persistence in requesting what would serve the community reflects how a great leader prioritizes communal welfare without coercion.
Responding to the Prophet's request with honesty and eventual willingness, despite personal attachment, demonstrates the virtue of selflessness.
practical_advice: When someone you trust asks you for something that would benefit others more than yourself, consider giving it willingly even if it is difficult.
Reflect on whether a personal desire or possession might serve a greater humanitarian purpose if redirected.
Practice honesty in your interactions—Salama's transparency about his feelings before complying made his act of generosity even more sincere and praiseworthy.

title: The Expedition of Umar ibn al-Khattab to Turba in Hawazin Territory
summary: The Prophet sent Umar with thirty riders to battle Hawazin at Turba, but the enemy fled before any engagement; when asked if he would pursue the Khathham tribe instead, Umar refused, citing that his orders were specifically for Hawazin.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related, through al-Waqidi with the lines of tradition given by him, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Wmar b. al-Khaeab, may God be pleased with him, along with 30 riders and a guide of Bano Hihl. They travelled by night and kept concealed by day. When they reached the enemy territory, they fled and so Wmar returned to Medina. He was asked, 'Would you like to do battle with Khathham?' He replied, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered me only to battle Hawain in their lands.'
lessons: Obedience to clear instruction, even when an alternative seems available, reflects discipline and integrity in leadership.
Not every mission results in the outcome we hoped for; returning without conflict can itself be a form of success when the enemy is deterred.
Staying within the boundaries of what you have been authorized to do protects you from overstepping and causing unintended harm.
practical_advice: When you have been given a specific task or responsibility, resist the temptation to expand its scope beyond what was agreed, even with good intentions.
Accept outcomes that fall short of your expectations with patience—sometimes the mission of deterrence is fulfilled even without a direct confrontation.
Before taking initiative in a situation, ask yourself whether you have been authorized or have the wisdom to act in that domain.

title: The Expedition of Abdullah ibn Rawaha to Yusayr ibn Rizam the Jew
summary: The Prophet dispatched Abdullah ibn Rawaha with thirty riders to deal with Yusayr ibn Rizam, a Jewish leader who posed a threat to the Muslim community.
story: He then related, through Ibrahim b. Lahi'a, from Aba al-Aswad, from 'Urwa, and through Miisa b. 'Uqba, from al-Zuhri, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent 'Abd Allah b. Raw*a with 30 riders - inclusive of 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha - to Yusayr h. Rizam, the Jew, whom they
lessons: Measured and deliberate responses to perceived threats reflect the balance between caution and necessary action.
Entrusting a mission to a capable and faithful leader, as the Prophet did with Abdullah ibn Rawaha, reflects confidence built through prior service.
Preparedness and readiness—travelling as a group with clear purpose—is a form of both physical and spiritual protection.
practical_advice: When facing a potential threat or challenge, gather the right people around you and approach it with a clear plan rather than acting alone or impulsively.
Trust that being sent on a difficult task by someone who believes in you is itself an affirmation of your capability.
Approach uncertain or dangerous situations with both resolve and caution, neither underestimating the challenge nor being paralyzed by fear.

title: The Expedition of Abdullah b. Rawaha to Yusayr b. Rizam, the Jew
summary: The Prophet sent Abdullah b. Rawaha with 30 riders to Yusayr b. Rizam at Khaybar after hearing he was conspiring with Ghatafan to attack the Muslims. Yusayr betrayed them and reached for a sword, triggering a fight in which all of Yusayr's men were killed and the Prophet miraculously healed Abdullah's wound by spitting on it.
story: He then related, through Ibrahim b. Lahi'a, from Aba al-Aswad, from 'Urwa, and through Miisa b. 'Uqba, from al-Zuhri, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent 'Abd Allah b. Raw*a with 30 riders - inclusive of 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha -to Yusayr h. Rizam, the Jew, whom they reached at Khaybar. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had heard that he was joining with Ghatafu to attack h i along with them. When they (the Muslims) met with him, they told him, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) has sent us to you to place you in charge of Khaybar." They stayed with him until he eventually followed them with 30 men, each of whom had a co-rider from the Muslims. When they all reached Qarqara Niyzr, a place some 6 miles from Khaybar, Yusayr b. Rk%nregretted what he had agreed and reached for the sword of 'Abd All& b. Raw&a. The latter was aware of this and first held back his mount, then rushed ahead, leading the others. When he could overcome Yusayr, he struck his leg, cutting it off. Yusayr then charged ahead, wielding a cudgel of fu wood and with it struck at the face of 'Ahd Allah h. Raw*a, hitting him on top of his head. Each of the Muslims then turned upon his co-rider and killed h i . (All were killed) except one Jew whom they were unable to catch. None of the Muslims were killed; and the Messenger of God (SAAS) spat upon the wound of 'Abd Alhh b. Raw*a. The wound did not fester and it did not hurt him for as long as he lived.
lessons: Betrayal can come even after apparent agreement and goodwill; remaining alert and aware is a form of wisdom, not paranoia.
Divine blessings and healing can manifest in unexpected, humble forms — the Prophet's saliva on the wound is a reminder that cure comes ultimately from God.
Steadfastness in a moment of sudden danger, as shown by Abdullah b. Rawaha, reflects the fruit of long spiritual and moral preparation.
practical_advice: When entering uncertain or high-stakes situations, maintain mindfulness and situational awareness rather than assuming all is well once an agreement is reached.
When you are injured or harmed — physically or emotionally — trust that God can bring healing through means you would never anticipate; do not despair of recovery.
Practice mental readiness by regularly reflecting on your values and what you would do under pressure, so that in moments of crisis you respond with clarity rather than panic.

title: The Expedition of Bashir b. Sa'd to Banu Murra and the Killing of Mirdas b. Nuhayk
summary: Bashir b. Sa'd was sent to Banu Murra in Fadak territory but his men were killed by the Jews; he alone survived. Subsequently Usama b. Zayd killed Mirdas b. Nuhayk after he declared the shahada, and the Prophet repeatedly questioned Usama about this act until Usama deeply regretted it and vowed never to kill one who professes the testimony of faith.
story: A hadith is related through al-Waqidi, with its chain of transmission, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Bashir h. Sa'd with 30 riders to Banii Murra, in Fadak territory. He drove off their camels and they did battle with him. The Jews killed all the men with him; Bashir displayed great courage that day and fought most fiercely. Eventually he took refuge in Fadak, spending the night with a Jew there. After that he made his way back to Medina. Al-W~qidistated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent forth Ghalib h. 'Abd Allah against them, along with a group of his chief Companions." He then mentioned Usama h. Zayd, Abn Mas'iid al-Badri, and Ka0 h. 'Ujra. Thereafter al-Wxqidi went on to relate how U s h a b. Zayd killed M i r d s b. Nuhayk, an ally of Bann Murra. He told how, when he held his sword over him, Mirdas spoke the words, La Ilaha rllz Allah! "There is no god but God!" and that those with him criticized Usama since the man had gone on saying this until he had brought his sword down. Al-Waqidi said that Usama later regretted what he had done. Ynnus b. Bukayr related this story from Ibn Ishaq, from a sheikh of Bann Salama, from men of his tribe, to the effect that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Ghalih h. 'Abd All& al-Kalhi to the territory of Bann Murra. He captured Mirdas h. Nuhayk, an ally of theirs from al-Hurqa, and Usama killed him. Ihn Ishaq stated, "Muhammad h. U s w a b. Muhammad b. U s w a related to me from his father, from his grandfather U s w a h. Zayd, who said, 'I and one of the an@r captured him' (meaning Mirdas h. Nuhayk). 'And when we drew our swords over him, he said, "I testify that la Ilaha rlla Allah!" But we did not put down our swords and killed him. When we went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and related this to him, he asked, " U s h a who will be for you (regarding such a case where it has been testified) that la Ilaha rlla Allah 'there is no god hut God'?" I replied, "But Messenger of God, he only said it to avoid being killed." He repeated, " U w a , who will be for you (regarding such a case where it has been testified that) la Iaha rlla allah?" And, I swear, he kept on repeating this to me until I wished I had not previously been aMuslim, that I had only hewme one that day and that I had not killed h i . So I told him, "Messenger of God, I give my word that I will never kill any man who says, 'la Ilaha r l l ~Allah'." He then asked me, "And what about after me?" "And after you (your death) too," I replied.'" I m h A b d stated that Hushaym h. Bashir related, quoting Hu~ayn,from Ahii Zuhym, who said, "I heard U s w a b. Zayd relate as follows, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us out to al-Hurqa of Juhayna. We attacked them in the morning. One of their men fought extremely well against us, and when they retreated, it was he who defended their rear. I and an overcame h i and as we did so, he said, "la Ilaha rlla Allah". The an@ri stood back from him, but I killed him. News of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he asked, "Uswa, did you k i him after he said, 'la Ilaha rlla Allah'?" I replied, "But Messenger of God, he only said that to avoid death." But he kept on asking me the same until I wished I had only become a Muslim that day.'" Al-Bukhai and Muslim gave this account from a hadith of Husbaym in similar terms.
lessons: The inviolability of sincere profession of faith is a foundational moral principle in Islam; judging the hidden intentions of the heart is not within a human being's authority.
Genuine remorse and moral accountability — even for leaders and warriors — is a sign of spiritual health and a pathway to growth rather than shame.
A mentor or leader who persistently questions a disciple's action, as the Prophet did with Usama, does so out of love and concern for the disciple's moral development, not to humiliate.
practical_advice: When you have done something you regret, do not flee from the feeling of guilt — sit with it, learn from it, and let it reshape your future conduct as Usama did by making a firm vow.

Resist the temptation to justify a harmful action by focusing on the other person's possible ulterior motives; what you cannot verify with certainty should not be the basis for an irreversible decision.

If someone in your life consistently calls you to account for a mistake, try to receive that correction as care rather than attack — the discomfort of accountability is often the beginning of genuine change.

title: The Expedition of Ghalib b. Abdullah to Banu al-Mulawwah at al-Kadid
summary: Ghalib b. Abdullah led a raid to Banu al-Mulawwah at al-Kadid. A scout lay motionless on a hill despite being shot twice by arrows, and the Muslims successfully seized the tribe's livestock before a miraculous flood sent by God prevented the pursuing enemy from catching them.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Ya'qnb b. Wtba related to me, from Muslim b. 'Abd Allah al-Juhani, from Jundub b. Makith al-Juhani, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Ghnlib h. 'Abd All& al-Kalbi, Kalb of Layth, to Banii al-Mulaww* at al-Kadid with orders to attack them. I was with his expedition and we went out as far as al-Qudayd, where al-Harith h. Midik h. al-Bars3 al-Laythi met us and we took him prisoner. He said, "I came out only to accept Islam." Ghdib b. 'Abd Allah told him, "If you were on your way to accept Islam, then it won't hurt you to be tied up for a day and a night. And if you really had some other purpose, then we'll have you secured." "'So he tied him up and left him under the charge of a young black man we had with us, whom he told, "Stay with him until we come back for you; if he gives you trouble, cut off his head!" So we went on to the al-Kadid valley, where we made a halt in the early evening and my companions sent me on towards al-Kadid. I made my way to a hill that would allow me to overlook the village. I stretched out on the ground. It was before sunset. One of their men came outside and saw me stretched out on the hill. He told his wife, "I can see something dark on this hill that I did not notice earlier in the day. Look and see whether the dogs have dragged out some of your utensils." She did look, then said, "I swear, I'm not missing anything." The man then said, "Hand me my bow and two arrows from my quiver." She did so and he shot an arrow at me that struck me in the side' (or he may have said, 'on my forehead'). 'I pulled it out and put it aside, without moving. He then shot the other arrow at me, piercing the top of my shoulder. I withdrew it too and put it aside without moving. The man then told his wife, "I'm sure my arrows would have hurt him, if he were a scout, he would have moved. In the morning, go out and retrieve my arrows; I don't want the dogs to chew them up."'" He went on, "We left them alone until their herd had been milked and were lying down quietly and a part of the night had passed. We then launched an attack upon them. We killed some and drove off their herd, heading away with them. Their shouts soon aroused others of them nearby. We raced off, eventually passing by al-H&th b. Malik b. al-Barqa' and his companion. We continued on, with h i accompanying us, until we heard the shouts of our pursuers who were too numerous for us to tackle. Soon all that separated us from them was the Qudyd valley. Then God sent down a flood of water from wherever He wished; we had not previously seen any rain, nor conditions for it. It came down so hard tht no one could withstand it. I saw the enemy force standing there staring at us, unable to get at us, while we drove off their animals" - or "led them off' (al-Nufayli was uncertain). "We hurried away with them until we reached the track; when we had proceeded down it, they were unable to retrieve what we had taken."
lessons: Extraordinary self-control under pain, as shown by the scout who lay still despite two arrow wounds, reflects a discipline of purpose that can be cultivated through dedication to a higher goal.

Divine intervention often arrives at the precise moment of human helplessness — the flood came when the Muslims had no other recourse, reminding believers that God's help is not bound by natural expectations.
Caution and careful planning — such as waiting until the herd was settled before acting — are not contradictions of trust in God but expressions of the wisdom God expects believers to exercise.
practical_advice: When you are in a painful or difficult situation and feel the urge to react immediately, practice stillness — sometimes the most powerful response is patient endurance until the right moment.
In moments when you have done everything within your power and are still overwhelmed, consciously surrender the outcome to God; relief may come through means entirely outside your imagination.
Before undertaking any significant challenge, invest time in careful observation and preparation rather than rushing — the scout's reconnaissance, however painful, made the mission possible.

title: The Expedition of Abu Hadrad to al-Ghaba: Seeking Help for a Marriage Dowry
summary: Abu Hadrad came to the Prophet seeking financial help after promising a dowry of two hundred dirhams for his marriage, only to find the Prophet himself had nothing to offer. The Prophet then sent Abu Hadrad and two companions on a military scouting mission to al-Ghaba, providing only a weak old she-camel as transport.
story: Yiinus stated, quoting Muhammad b. Ishaq, "It was Jacfar b. 'Abd AllSh h. Aslam who related to me the story of Ahii Hadrad to al-Ghaba. He quoted Ahii Hadrad as saying, 'I arranged marriage with a woman of my tribe, promising her a dowry of two hundred dirhams. So I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) to seek his help in my wedding. He asked me, "How much dowry did you promise her?" "Two hundred dirhams," I told him. He exclaimed, 'LGoodness gracious, I swear by God, if you were just picking up the money from a valley floor, you couldn't have given more! I swear, I don't have enough to help you." A few days later a man from Jasham b. Mu'awiya approached; his name was Rifz'a b. Qays - or Q y s b. Rifa'a. He was accompanied by a large group of Jasham men, with whom he encamped at al-Ghaba, with the intention of combining with Qays to fight against the Messenger of God (SAAS). He was a man of fine birth and reputation among Jasham. "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) summoned myself and two other Muslims and said, "Go out and get some information about this man." He then gave us a decrepit old she-camel, mounting one of us on it. I swear, it was so weak it could only get up with him, when some men pushed it up from behind. It could scarcely stand up. He told us, "Make do with her." "'So we left bearing arms consisting of swords and arrows.
lessons: The Prophet's honest admission that he had nothing to give Abu Hadrad is a powerful model of integrity; those in leadership or positions of trust should not make false promises simply to spare another's feelings.

Being sent on a difficult mission immediately after a personal disappointment, as Abu Hadrad was, illustrates that God often opens one door of provision through the very path that seems most demanding.
Contentment with humble means — a weak camel, minimal provisions — is a spiritual disposition that allows a person to act effectively even when resources fall far short of desires.
practical_advice: When you approach someone for help and they genuinely cannot assist you, resist the temptation to feel rejected or abandoned; their limitation is not a measure of your worth or of God's care for you.
If you are facing financial stress related to a major life commitment like marriage, take practical steps one at a time rather than being paralyzed by the gap between what you have and what you need.
Practice gratitude for whatever resources you do have, however meager they appear — the companions set out on a difficult mission with a barely functional camel and still fulfilled their duty.

title: The Raid on Rifā'a b. Qays of Jasham
summary: A small Muslim expedition is sent to gather intelligence on Rifā'a b. Qays, a man of fine birth among Jasham who sought to fight against the Prophet. The narrator describes a night ambush in which Rifā'a is killed and the group drives off large numbers of camels and livestock.
story: to fight against the Messenger of God (SAAS). He was a man of fine birth and reputation among Jasham. "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) summoned myself and two other Muslims and said, "Go out and get some information about this man." He then gave us a decrepit old she-camel, mounting one of us on it. I swear, it was so weak it could only get up with him, when some men pushed it up from behind. It could scarcely stand up. He told us, "Make do with her." "'So we left bearing arms consisting of swords and arrows. When we arrived at sunset close to the village, I hid on one side and told my companions to hide on another. I told them, "If you hear me crying Allāhu Akbar! and running to their camp, then you shout the same and follow me." "'We remained waiting to attack them by surprise until night overcame us. The darkest part of the night passed. They had a shepherd who had gone out to graze their flock. He was late in returning and they were worried about him. Their leader, Rifā'a b. Qays eventually got up and took his sword, which he hung around his neck, saying, "By God, I'm going out to find out about our shepherd; something must have happened to him." Some of those with him said, "By God, don't you go; we'll do it for you." "No," he replied. "Only I will go. I don't want any of you to follow me." "'He then came out and passed by me. When he was in my range, I shot an arrow at him, piercing him in the heart. I swear, he did not utter a sound! I jumped out at him and cut off his head, then raced towards their camp, shouting Allāhu Akbar! My companions did the same. I swear, they fled in disorder, taking with them their women, children and those possessions they could. We drove off large numbers of their camels and livestock which we took back to the Messenger of God (SAAS). I went to him carrying the man's head. He gave me 13 baggage camels from those we captured as my dowry. So I concluded my marriage.'"
lessons: Difficult circumstances and limited resources do not prevent the fulfillment of one's duty; the companions accepted a weak camel and persevered.
Patience and strategic planning — waiting through the night and coordinating with companions — are marks of wisdom in adversity.
Trust in divine guidance means proceeding even when the odds seem stacked against you, and remaining steadfast until the task is complete.
practical_advice: When you face a challenge with inadequate resources, focus on making the best use of what you have rather than lamenting what is lacking.
Before acting in a high-stakes situation, take time to plan carefully and communicate clearly with those around you, as the narrator did by positioning his companions and agreeing on a signal.
In moments of fear or uncertainty at night, remain still and patient; premature action often leads to failure, while composed waiting creates the right opening.

title: The Killing of 'Āmir b. al-Adhba' by Muhallim b. Jaththāma and the Revelation of Qur'an 4:94
summary: During an expedition to Idam, a Muslim named Muhallim b. Jaththāma kills a man who greeted the Muslim force with the Islamic greeting, acting out of a pre-Islamic grudge. The incident prompts the revelation of a Qur'anic verse warning believers to verify a person's faith before acting against them.
story: Yazid b. 'Abd Allāh b. Qusayr related to me, from Ibn 'Abd Allāh b. Abī Hadrad, from his father, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us out against Idam. Our group of Muslims included Abū Qatāda al-Hārith b. Rib'ī and Muhallim b. Jaththāma b. Qays. We went on until we were in the Idam valley. There 'Āmir b. al-Adhba' al-Ashja'ī passed us by; he was riding a young camel, carrying a few provisions and a vessel of yoghurt. He saluted us with the Muslim words of greeting and so we refrained from attacking him. But Muhallim b. Jaththāma attacked and killed him because of some earlier unresolved problem and seized his camel and provisions. When we reached the Messenger of God, (SAAS) we told him our news and it was about us that the following verses of the Qur'an were revealed, 'O you who believe! When you go forth in God's cause, be circumspect. Do not say to those who greet you in peace, "You're no Muslim!" and seek for worldly gain. With God there will be many rewards. That is the way you behaved previously, but God has now favoured you. Be aware that God has knowledge of what you do'" (sūrat al-Nisā'; IV, v.94).
lessons: Personal grudges and old enmities must never be allowed to override the sacred boundaries God has set; acting on unresolved anger leads to grave injustice.
The Qur'anic reminder that 'God has knowledge of what you do' calls every believer to deep self-accountability, especially in moments of conflict.
A person's outward declaration of faith must be respected and not dismissed out of suspicion or worldly desire; assumption of guilt without evidence is itself a sin.
practical_advice: Before taking any irreversible action against someone during a dispute, pause and ask yourself whether old grievances are clouding your judgment.

When you are in a position of power over someone — physically, socially, or emotionally — treat their words and gestures of peace at face value unless there is clear, verified evidence to the contrary.
Reflect regularly on the principle that God is aware of your private motivations; use this awareness to audit whether your actions are truly driven by justice or by hidden personal interests.

title: The Demand for Blood-Wit After the Killing of 'Āmir b. al-Adhba' and the Prophet's Refusal to Pardon Muhallim
summary: After the killing of 'Āmir b. al-Adhba', 'Uyayna b. Badr demands blood retribution. The Prophet repeatedly offers blood-money which is eventually accepted. When Muhallim approaches seeking forgiveness, the Prophet publicly refuses to pardon him three times; Muhallim later dies within seven days and the earth reportedly rejects his burial, which the Prophet frames as a divine warning.
story: Ibn Ishāq stated, "Muhammad b. Ja'far related to me, saying, 'I heard Ziyād b. Dumira b. Sa'd al-Damrī narrating a hadith from 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, from his father and from his grandfather, both of whom said - they had both been present at Hunayn - "The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the al-zuhr, noon prayer, then went under the shade of a tree where he sat. 'Uyayna b. Badr went over to him and demanded the blood of 'Āmir b. al-Adbat al-Ashja'ī, chief of (Banū) 'Āmir. 'Would you agree to now accept 50 camels and another fifty when we return to Medina?' the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked. 'Uyayna b. Badr responded, 'By God, I'll not leave him alone till his women experience the same sorrow my women have.' ""'A man from Banū Layth named Ibn Mukayil, a short man, arose. He said, 'Messenger of God, I find nothing comparable to this death since the beginnings of Islam. All I can compare it to is the rear sheep that flee when their leader out in front is shot. Keep to the traditional ways today and bring change tomorrow!' ""'The Messenger of God (SAAS) reiterated, 'Will you agree to accept 50 camels now and 50 when we return to Medina?' He kept on at them till they accepted the blood-wit. The family of Muhallim b. Jaththāma now asked, 'Bring him over so that the Messenger of God (SAAS) may forgive him.' ""'They brought a tall, thin man dressed in a gown in which he was prepared to be executed, and he stood before the Prophet (SAAS), who then spoke, 'O God, do not pardon Muhallim!' He repeated this three times. Muhallim arose, wiping away his tears with the hem of his gown.""' Muhammad b. Ishāq stated, "His people claim that after that he did forgive him." Ibn Ishāq stated, "Sālim Abū al-Nadr related to me that the account states, 'They did not accept the blood-wit until al-Aqra' b. Hābis addressed them privately, saying, "People of Qays, the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked you to give up a dead man to make peace between people thereby, but you refused him this. Are you secure against the possibility that he will be angry at you, and that God will be angry at his anger? Or that he will curse you and that God will curse you because of his curse? Give him over to the Messenger of God (SAAS), or I will bring 50 men of Banū Tamīm who will all swear that the dead man was an unbeliever who never performed the prayer, and that therefore no compensation for his death should be sought." When he said this to them they accepted the blood-money.'" Ibn Ishāq related from a reliable source, from al-Ḥasan al-Baṣrī, that when Muhallim sat before the Messenger of God (SAAS) the latter asked him, "Did you offer him security then murder him?" and that he then cursed him. "Al-Ḥasan said, 'And I swear Muhallim lived only seven days more thereafter before he died. The earth spat him out. Then they buried him again, but the earth again spat him out. When this happened yet again, they covered him over with stones to hide him. When news of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he said, "The earth covers over worse than him, but God wishes to warn you of what conduct is impermissible, by showing you this."'"
lessons: The gravity of betraying a covenant of safety is so severe that even sincere repentance cannot automatically erase its worldly consequences; justice must be fulfilled.
Persistence in seeking a just resolution — as the Prophet repeatedly offered blood-money — demonstrates that leaders must exhaust peaceful options before escalating conflict.
Divine warnings, even when they come through extraordinary or unsettling events, are mercy in disguise; they exist to protect the community from repeating grave errors.
practical_advice: If you have wronged someone, do not assume that an apology alone is sufficient; actively seek to make material restitution and repair the harm you caused.
When caught between tribal loyalties and moral accountability, remember that submitting to a just authority — as al-Aqra' urged the Qays — protects the entire community from greater harm.
Use moments of witnessing consequences — whether in your own life or others' — as opportunities for honest self-reflection on whether your actions align with your stated values.

title: The Expedition of 'Abd Allāh b. Hudhāfa al-Sahmī and the Lesson on Obedience
summary: The Prophet appoints a commander over an expedition, but the commander angers his men and orders them to enter a fire as a test of obedience. The men refuse, and when they return to the Prophet he tells them their refusal was correct because obedience is only owed in what is good.
story: It is established in both Ṣaḥīḥ collections through al-A'mash, from Sa'd b. 'Ubayda, from Abū 'Abd al-Rahmān al-Ḥubulī, from 'Alī b. Abī Ṭālib, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) gave charge of an expedition to one of the anṣār, telling those men he sent with him to listen well and to obey him. For some reason they angered him, so he said, 'Get some firewood!' They did so. He then said, 'Light it!' They did so. He then said, 'Enter the fire!' The men looked at one another and said, 'But the only reason we fled to the Messenger of God (SAAS) was to avoid the fire!' His anger subsided and the fire was extinguished. "When they came back to the Prophet (SAAS) they related this to him and he said, 'If they had entered it, they would never have come out of it, for obedience is only in that which is good.'"
lessons: Obedience to authority has clear moral limits; no leader — however trusted — can command what violates fundamental divine principles, and conscience must be maintained.
The companions' instinctive refusal, rooted in their core reason for embracing Islam, illustrates that spiritual identity should anchor every decision, including decisions to disobey.
The Prophet's validation of their refusal teaches that a good leader acknowledges the boundaries of legitimate authority rather than demanding blind compliance.
practical_advice: When someone in authority over you gives an instruction that feels deeply wrong, take a moment to evaluate whether it crosses a moral or ethical line before complying, even if the relationship is one you respect.
Build clarity about your own core values so that, under pressure, you have an internal compass that tells you when a line is being crossed.

If you are a leader and your team pushes back on an instruction, be willing to reflect on whether your direction was just; the Prophet's calm response after the fire went out modelled gracious self-correction.

title: The 'Umrat al-Qadā' — The Fulfilment Pilgrimage
summary: The text introduces the 'Umrat al-Qadā', also known as the Fulfilment Pilgrimage or the Decision Pilgrimage, explaining its three names and its basis in the agreement made at al-Ḥudaybiyya, whereby the Prophet would return the following year and enter Mecca with his sword sheathed for no more than three days.
story: THE 'UMRAT AL-QADĀ', THE 'FULFILMENT PILGRIMAGE'. It is (also) known as al-qiṣāṣ. Al-Suhayli preferred this. It is otherwise called 'umrat al-qaḍā', "the decision pilgrimage". The first name relates to fulfilment of what was arranged at al-Ḥudaybiyya. The second name is taken from the words of the Almighty, ma al-ḥurumātu qiṣāṣun, "Those things forbidden are subject to retaliation" (sūrat al-Baqara; II, v.194). The third name comes from the decision he made with them based on his retiring from them that year provided that he would return the next, and that he would enter Mecca only with his sword still sheathed and that he not stay longer than three days. This 'Umra is the one referred to in the words of the Almighty in the
lessons: Fulfilling a commitment — even one made under pressure or after a setback, as at al-Ḥudaybiyya — is itself an act of worship and integrity.
The multiple names given to this pilgrimage show that a single event can carry layers of spiritual, legal, and historical meaning; depth of understanding enriches faith.
Accepting temporary constraints (returning after one year, keeping the sword sheathed) as part of a larger agreement reflects the prophetic wisdom of strategic patience over short-term victory.
practical_advice: When you make a promise or agreement, honour it even when circumstances have changed or when fulfilment requires patience; your reliability is a form of character.
Look for the deeper meaning behind the rituals and commitments in your life; understanding why you do something transforms obligation into devotion.
If you are in a conflict, consider proposing clear, time-bound conditions that both sides can accept; structured compromise often opens doors that confrontation keeps closed.

title: The 'Umrat al-Qada': Names and Quranic Basis
summary: The 'Umrat al-Qada' (compensatory Umra of 7 AH) is explained by its three names and connected to the Quranic verse in Surah al-Fath, fulfilling the Prophet's vision of entering the holy mosque.
story: pilgrimage". The first name relates to fulfilment of what was arranged at d-Hudaybiyya. The second name is taken from the words of the Almighty, ma al-hurumatu qtsdsun, "Those things forbidden are subject to retaliation" (siirat a6Baqara; 11, v.194). The third name comes from the decision he made with them based on his retiring from them that year provided that he would return the next, and that he would enter Mecca only with his sword still sheathed and that he not stay longer than three days. This Cumra is the one referred to in the words of the Almighty in the sacred siirat al-Fath: "God will fulfil the true visions of His messenger: you will certainly enter, if God wills it, the holy mosque in safety, with your heads shaved and cut, not fearing" (siirat al-Fath; XLVIII, v.27). We have examined this verse sufficiently in our work, Tafsir (Exegesis). It was this that was promised in the words of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Responding to Wmar b. al-Khanitb's question, "Did we not say that we would come to the house and would circumambulate it?" he replied, "Yes indeed; but did I tell you you would go there this year?" "No," 'Umar replied. "Well," he reiterated, "you will go there and you will circumambulate it."
lessons: Divine promises are always fulfilled, though the timing may differ from our expectations — patience bridges the gap between promise and fulfillment.
When the Prophet confirmed the vision would come true, he demonstrated that trust in God's word is not naive but grounded in certainty.
Sacred agreements and covenants carry deep moral weight in Islam; the very names of this Umra reflect how seriously God takes the fulfillment of commitments.
practical_advice: When you feel a promise or hope has been delayed, remind yourself that delay is not denial — revisit what God has promised and hold firm.
If someone questions your faith in a future good, respond with the Prophet's composure: affirm the promise without overpromising the timeline.
Study the names and contexts of Quranic verses related to your struggles; understanding why a verse was revealed can deepen your trust and calm your anxiety.

title: The Prophet Departs for 'Umrat al-Qada' After Khaybar
summary: After returning from Khaybar and spending months in Medina sending out expeditions, the Prophet called the people to prepare for the compensatory Umra in Dhul-Qa'da of 7 AH, marching toward Mecca with those who had been previously blocked.
story: Mu'tamir b. Sulaymm quoted his father as saying about the maghzzi, the military expeditions, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from Khaybar, he remained in Medina, sendimg out his expeditions until Dha al-Qakla arrived. He then called out to the people, 'Get ready for the 'umra.' They gathered their baggage and left for Mecca." Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Muslims whose way had been blocked went forth with him on that 'umra of his, it being 7 AH. When the Meccans heard of this, they kept out of their way. Quraysh meanwhile told one another that Muhammad was in great difficulty and distress."
lessons: Returning to a place of past rejection with dignity and purpose is one of the most powerful acts of spiritual resilience.
The community that had been turned away together returned together — shared history of hardship can forge unbreakable bonds.
Quraysh's assumption that the Muslims were weak reveals how fear and pride distort the perception of others; never underestimate those who walk with God.
practical_advice: When you face a situation where others expect you to fail or appear weakened, prepare thoroughly and proceed with calm determination rather than anxious haste.
If you have been rejected or turned away from something meaningful, channel that experience into motivation rather than bitterness.
Surround yourself with people who shared your difficult journey — they understand your resilience in ways others cannot.

title: The Prophet Jogs Around the Ka'ba to Demonstrate Muslim Strength
summary: Upon entering Mecca for the 'Umrat al-Qada', the Prophet ordered his Companions to jog the first three circuits of the Ka'ba with their right shoulders exposed, countering Quraysh's claim that the Muslims had been weakened by Yathrib fevers.
story: "A reliable source related to me, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas, who said, 'They lined up at the assembly hall to look at him and those with him. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) went inside the mosque, he tossed the hem of his mantle over his left shoulder, exposing his right arm and said, "May God have mercy upon a man who today demonstrates his strength to them." "'He then saluted the al-rukn, the "corner", and came outside again, jogging, as did his Companions with him. He continued on until the ka'ba concealed him from them; he saluted the rukn al-yaman, the "south" or "Yemen" corner. Then he walked on and saluted the rukn al-aswad, the "black stone" comer. He then jogged three circuits and walked the rest.' Ibn 'Abbns used to say, 'People used to think that this (procedure) was not i n c h b e n t upon them. They thought this on the grounds that the Messenger of God (SAAS) only did this because of that mbe of Quraysh and what he had heard of them, until the hijat al-wad&, the "farewell pilgrimage"; the sunna, the normative Muslim practice, continued it.'" Al-Bukhari stated that Sulaymm b. Harb related to him, quoting Hammad - he being Ibn Zayd - from Ayyiib, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived, the polytheists said, 'Here comes a delegation of people whom the Yathrib fevers have weakened!' And so the Prophet (SAAS) ordered them to jog for the three courses and to walk between the two comers. All that prevented him telling them to jog throughout all the circuits was his wish to spare them (the effort)." Abii 'Abd Allah stated, "Ihn Salama - meaning Hammad b. Salama - added, from Ayycib, from Sa'id, from Ihn 'Abb%, that the latter said, 'When the Prophet (SAAS) came (to Mecca) that year when it was safe, he said, "Jog, so that the polytheists can see your strength. The polytheists will be positioned in front of Mt. Qu'ayqu%.'"'
lessons: The Prophet responded to mockery not with words of retaliation but with dignified, visible demonstration of strength — actions communicated what arguments could not.
His compassion for his Companions was evident even in this moment of pressure; he did not push them beyond what was necessary, sparing them from jogging all seven circuits.
What began as a situational response to slander became an enduring practice in pilgrimage, showing that noble acts done with sincerity can transcend their original context.
practical_advice: When others doubt your strength or resilience, focus your energy on demonstrating it through calm, purposeful action rather than defensive argument.
Even when you are in a difficult or high-pressure situation, remain mindful of the wellbeing of those around you and do not push them beyond their limits.
Recognize that how you carry yourself in moments of adversity can set a lasting example — your dignified response today may inspire others long into the future.

title: Ibn Rawaha Walks Ahead of the Prophet Reciting Verse
summary: As the Prophet entered Mecca for the 'Umrat al-Qada', the poet 'Abdullah ibn Rawaha walked ahead of him, holding the camel's halter and reciting bold verses proclaiming the truth of the Prophet's mission and warning the disbelievers.
story: Ibn ishaq stated, 'Ahd Allah b. Aba Bakr related to me that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca to perform that pilgrimage, 'Abd Allah b. Rawsha kept hold of his camel's halter and recited, "Unbelievers, clear out of his path; clear out, for all good is in His messenger. 0 Lord, I am a believer in his words; I know God's truth in accepting it We fought you about its interpretation, just as we fought you about its revelation, With blows that remove beads from necks and divert friend from friend." Ibn H i s h a stated, "The words, 'We fought you about its interpretation' to the end of the limes are verses of 'Ammar b. Yasir relating to a different occasion -meaning the battle of Siffin." Al-Suhayli stated this. Ibn Hisham further stated, "The proof of this is that Ibn Rawaha was referring only to the polytheists, who never had accepted the (fact of) revelation. And only those who accept the revelation would do battle over the interpretation." Ibn Hishnm's statement is open to dispute. The ha&? al-Bayhaqi related, 'Abd al-Razsq, from Ma'mar, from from a different line of transmission, al-Zuhri, from Anas, who said, "When the Prophet (SAAS) entered Mecca on the 'umrat al-qada', 'Abd Allah h. Raw*a walked in front of him; in one account he-did so while he held his leather stirrup. As he walked, he spoke the lines, 'Unbelievers, dear out of his path; the al-RahmPn, the Most Merciful, has given revelation in revealing it; That the best fight is in His cause; we have fought you over its interpretation.'" In another version, with precisely the same chain of transmission, the words are, "Unbelievers, clear out of his path; today we fight you over its revelation, With blows that will remove beads from necks and divert friend from friend. 0 Lord, I believe in what he says."
lessons: True loyalty is expressed not only in private devotion but in public declaration, even when doing so is bold and countercultural.
Ibn Rawaha's poetry was an act of worship — using one's God-given talents to serve a righteous cause elevates those gifts to something sacred.
Scholarly diligence in tracing historical accounts, as shown by Ibn Hisham and al-Bayhaqi, reminds us that honesty in narration is itself a form of moral integrity.
practical_advice: Consider how you might use your own unique talents — writing, speaking, art, or skill — in service of something that truly matters to you and to God.
Do not shy away from declaring your values clearly in moments that call for it; silence in the face of meaningful truth can be its own kind of loss.
When you encounter conflicting accounts or perspectives, approach the discrepancy with curiosity and fairness rather than dismissing one side — the truth often rewards careful examination.

title: The Prophet Circumambulates Mounted, Guarded by Companions
summary: During the 'Umrat al-Qada', the Prophet circumambulated the Ka'ba mounted on his camel while his Companions shielded him from harm by polytheist youth, and Ibn Rawaha recited verses while walking ahead.
story: Yanus b. Bukayr stated, from Hisham h. Sa'd, from Zayd b. Aslam, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca the year of al-qadiya, the 'umra in question, and circumambulated the kacba mounted on his camel. He saluted the al-rukn, "the corner (with the Black Stone)", using his staff. Regarding this, Ibn Hisham said, "He did so not due to any illness, while the Muslims were crowding around him, and 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha was reciting, 'In the name of Him besides whose religion there is none, in the name of H i whose messenger is Muhammad, Clear out of his path, unbelievers!'" Al-Bukhai stated that 'Ali b. 'Abd All& related to him, quoting Sufym, quoting Isma'il b. Abn Khsid, (who said) that he heard Ibn Aba Awfa say, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the 'umra, we shielded him from the polytheist youngsters and from the polytheists themselves in case they might harm him."
lessons: The community's act of shielding the Prophet shows that protecting what is sacred and beloved is a collective responsibility, not an individual one.
Performing an act of worship with full communal support transforms it into something greater — the Umra was not merely a personal ritual but a communal declaration of faith.
The Prophet's use of a staff to salute the Black Stone while mounted illustrates the Islamic principle of adapting the form of an act without abandoning its spirit.
practical_advice: Identify who in your community needs protection, support, or simply someone to stand beside them in a difficult moment, and offer yourself for that role.
When circumstances prevent you from worshipping or acting in the ideal way, find a sincere alternative rather than abandoning the act altogether.
Surround your most important endeavors with people who will guard your focus and wellbeing — accountability and protection from distraction are gifts.

title: The Companions Lay Down Arms Before Entering Mecca
summary: Before entering Mecca for the 'Umrat al-Qada', the Muslims left their weapons — shields, armour, spears and arrows — at Ya'jij, entering with only sheathed swords as had been agreed at Hudaybiyya.
story: Miisa b. 'Uqha stated, from al-Zuhri as follows, "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth the year following that of al-Hudayhiyya on the 'umra pilgrimage, in Dhii al-Qa'da of 7 AH. This was the same month that the polytheists blocked him from entry to the sacred mosque. When he reached Ya'jij, he laid down all their weapons - leather shields, armour, spears and arrows. They then entered carrying the swords borne by mounted men."
lessons: Honoring the terms of an agreement, even with adversaries, is a mark of moral superiority — the Prophet kept his word precisely and publicly.
Entering a sacred space in a posture of peace, even when surrounded by those who may wish you harm, requires profound trust in God's protection.
Restraint in a moment when one has the power to act aggressively is not weakness — it is the highest form of strength and wisdom.
practical_advice: Before entering a difficult conversation or situation, consider what 'weapons' — defensiveness, harsh words, old grievances — you might need to set down in order for the encounter to be productive.
Honor your commitments to others, even when the other party has wronged you; your integrity is not contingent on theirs.
Practice entering tense environments with a spirit of peace rather than readiness for conflict; your composure can change the atmosphere of a room.

title: The Prophet Proposes to Maymuna During the 'Umrat al-Qada'
summary: During the 'Umrat al-Qada', the Prophet sent Ja'far ibn Abi Talib ahead to propose marriage to Maymuna bint al-Harith, who appointed her brother-in-law al-Abbas to handle the matter, and the marriage was agreed upon.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Jacfar b. Abii Tdih on ahead of himself to Mayrniina, daughter of al-Hsith, the al-cAmiri woman, and proposed marriage to her. She appointed alLcAbbnsin charge of her affairs; his wife, Umm al-Fadl, daughter of al-Hxrith, was her sister. AlLcAhbS then agreed to her marriage to the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Marriage is a noble institution and the Prophet pursued it through proper channels of family consultation and guardianship, modeling respect and deliberateness.
Delegating important personal matters to a trustworthy representative demonstrates wisdom — not every matter must be handled personally to be handled well.
Moments of spiritual significance, like the Umra, can also be moments of personal and communal growth, including the building of family bonds.
practical_advice: When pursuing an important life decision, identify the right people to involve and consult rather than acting entirely alone — seek wise counsel.
If someone trusts you to represent their interests in a significant matter, take that responsibility seriously and handle it with integrity and care.
Do not compartmentalize your spiritual life from your personal life; meaningful personal milestones can happen in the midst of sacred moments.

title: Quraysh Demands the Prophet Leave Mecca After Three Nights
summary: On the morning of the fourth day, Quraysh leaders Suhayl ibn 'Amr and Huwaytib ibn 'Abd al-'Uzza came to enforce the three-day limit of the Hudaybiyya agreement, leading to a tense exchange with Sa'd ibn 'Ubada and the Prophet.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained in Mecca for three nights, at which point the period of safe access agreed upon at al-Hudayhiyya ended. "When morning of the 4th day came, Suhayl h. 'Amr and Huwaytib h. 'Abd al-'Uz% arrived, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was seated with his Companions, conferring with Sacd h. Wbnda. Huwayhb b. 'Ahd al-Wzzn shouted, 'We invoke God and the pact to you, because you have not left our land and the three nights are over!' Sa'd h. 'Ubada replied, 'You lie, may you have no mother! It is not your land, nor that of your forefathers. By God, he will not leave!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then called out to Suhayl and Huwayhb, saying, 'I have arranged marriage with one of your women. It will not harm you for me to stay until I consummate the marriage with her. We can prepare food and you can eat with us.' They replied, 'We will insist on invoking God and the pact unless you leave us.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave orders to Abii B f i Cand he announced the departure. The Messenger of God (SAAS) rode off and halted in the valley of Saraf. The other Muslims had remained behind; the Messenger of God (SAAS) had entrusted Aha mcwith bringing Maymiina. He stayed at Saraf until Maymiina arrived to"
lessons: The Prophet honored the three-day agreement without argument, even when his Companion Sa'd reacted with understandable indignation — self-control in the face of injustice is a form of leadership.
Sa'd's passionate response shows that righteous anger has its place, but leadership requires knowing when to act on it and when to channel it differently.
The Prophet's gracious offer to share a wedding meal with the very people pressuring him to leave demonstrates extraordinary magnanimity — responding to hostility with generosity.
practical_advice: When you feel pressured to leave a situation or give something up that feels rightfully yours, ask yourself: can I honor the terms of what was agreed and still maintain my dignity?
If those around you react angrily on your behalf, gently redirect their energy — their loyalty is precious, but you may see a path they cannot.
Practice generosity toward those who treat you poorly; it disarms hostility in ways that argument never can, and it preserves your own peace.

title: The Marriage of Maymuna and Departure from Mecca After Umrat al-Qada
summary: After the Umrat al-Qada, the polytheists pressured the Prophet to leave Mecca before he could consummate his marriage with Maymuna there, so he departed and consummated the marriage at Saraf, where Maymuna later died.
story: of your women. It will not harm you for me to stay until I consummate the marriage with her. We can prepare food and you can eat with us.' They replied, 'We will insist on invoking God and the pact unless you leave us.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave orders to Abii B f i Cand he announced the departure. The Messenger of God (SAAS) rode off and halted in the valley of Saraf. The other Muslims had remained behind; the Messenger of God (SAAS) had entrusted Aha mcwith bringing Maymiina. He stayed at Saraf until Maymiina arrived to him there. Maymiina had been caused great distress and been harmed by the foolish polytheists and their children. When she arrived to the Messenger of God (SAAS), at Saraf, he consummated his marriage with her. Thereafter, at night, he travelled on and reached Medina. God decreed that Maymiina's death should occur at a later day at S d , she died where the Messenger of God (SAAS) had consummated marriage with her.
lessons: Patience in the face of hostility and harassment is a mark of noble character; the Prophet did not respond to the polytheists' provocation with anger or retaliation.
Divine decree surrounds every moment of life, even those that appear to be shaped by human opposition — Maymuna's death in the very place of her marriage shows that God's plan encompasses all things.
Honoring agreements and covenants, even when the other party behaves unjustly, reflects integrity and builds long-term trust.
practical_advice: When others treat you unfairly or try to diminish your joy, focus on what you can control and find a dignified path forward rather than escalating conflict.
Reflect on how moments that seem disrupted by others may still carry a deeper significance in God's plan for your life.
Honor your commitments even when it is difficult; your reputation for trustworthiness will outlast any short-term inconvenience imposed on you.

title: The Revelation Concerning the Umrat al-Qada and the Holy Month
summary: God revealed the verse 'The holy month for the holy month, and the things forbidden are subject to retaliation' in connection with the Umrat al-Qada, affirming that the Prophet performed the pilgrimage in the same holy month in which the polytheists had previously blocked him.
story: He then went on to relate the story of the daughter of Hamza and concluded by saying, 'God, Almighty and Glorious is He, revealed concerning that 'umra, the words, "The holy month for the holy month, and the things forbidden are subject to retaliation."' The Messenger of God (SAAS) had made the pilgrimage in that same holy month in which they had blocked his path. Ihn Lahih related from Abii d-Aswad, from Wrwa b. al-Zubayr approximately this same text, which is further substantiated by numerous witnesses in many ahadith. In the jahih collection of d-Bukhzi it is stated, through Fulayh b. Sulayrnan, from Nafi', from Ibn Wmar, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went out to make the 'umra pilgrimage, hut the Quraysh unbelievers blocked his path to the kacba. He made sacrifice and shaved his head at d-Hudaybiyya and made a pact with them that they would make the 'umra the following year; the only arms (the Muslims) would bear would be swords and he would remain only so long as they agreed. He did perform the 'umra the following year and entered it in accord with the pact he had made. After he had been there for three days, they told him to leave, and he did so.
lessons: Divine justice operates on its own timeline; what is taken from the believer is not forgotten, and what is due is restored in God's own time.
Fulfilling religious obligations under difficult conditions, even if delayed, carries profound spiritual merit.
Revelation from God provides clarity and vindication for those who endure injustice with patience.
practical_advice: When you feel that an opportunity has been unjustly taken from you, trust that God's justice will restore what is rightfully yours in a way and time that is best.
Do not abandon your spiritual practices simply because obstacles arise; persist in seeking to fulfill them.
Study the Quran for guidance and comfort when you feel confused about why hardship has come your way — divine wisdom is often revealed in hindsight.

title: The Question of Changing Sacrificial Animals After Being Blocked from the Kaaba
summary: A man who was blocked from the Kaaba during the siege of Ibn al-Zubayr asked Ibn Abbas whether he should change his sacrificial animals the following year, and Ibn Abbas confirmed that the Prophet and his Companions had done exactly this during the Umrat al-Qada.
story: Abti Da'ad stated, 'Al-Nufayli related to me, quoting Muhammad h. Salama, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from 'Amr b. Maymiin (who said), "I heard Abo Hadir al-Himyari relate that Maymiin b. Muhran said, 'I went out to make the Cumra pilgrimage the year the Syrians besieged Ihn al-Zuhayr in Mecca. Men of my tribe sent some animals with me for sacrifice. When we encountered the Syrians, they prevented us from going inside the sacred area. So I sacrificed the animals where I was, performed the other pilgrimage rites and then returned home. When I went out the following year to complete the Cumua, I went to see Ibn 'Ahbas and I asked him and he replied, "You should change the animals for sacrifice; the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered his Companions to sacrifice different animals than they had in the year of al-Hudaybiyya on the Cumrat al-qaddl."'"' Aha Dslad was alone in giving this, from a hadith of Abn Hadir Wthmm b. Hsdir al-Himyari, from Ibn CAbbas. The hd& al-Bayhaqi stated, 'Al-H~kimnarrated to us, quoting al-Asamm, quoting Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, quoting Ynnus b. Bukayr, from Ihn Ishaq, who said, "Amr b. Mayman related to me as follows, 'My father used to ask often, "Did the Messenger of God (SAAS) change the animals he sacrificed when the polytheists blocked him from the kacba?" He never got any information on this until I heard him ask Aha Hadk al-Himyari about that and he told him, "You've found an expert! I went on the pilgrimage the first year Ibn al-Zuhayr was engaged in the siege. I was given some animals to sacrifice, but they (the Syrians) came between us and the kacba. So I slaughtered them in the sacred area and returned to Yemen where I told people, 'I have in the Messenger of God an example!' The following year I again went on the Cumra; I encountered Ihn 'Ahbas and asked him whether or not I should change the animals for sacrifice. He replied, 'Yes; do change. The Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions did change the sacrificial animals from those they slaughtered when the polytheists blocked their path to different ones on the Cumrat al-qaddl. They found camels expensive for them, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave them permission to sacrifice cattle.""""'
lessons: The Prophet's example provides practical guidance even for unprecedented situations, and the companions preserved this guidance carefully for future generations.
Flexibility within the framework of religious law is itself a mercy from God; when circumstances change, the means of worship can adapt while the spirit remains.
Seeking knowledge from those who are learned is the correct response to religious uncertainty — ignorance is not an excuse when scholars are accessible.
practical_advice: When you face an unexpected obstacle in fulfilling a religious or personal commitment, look to established precedent and consult knowledgeable people rather than abandoning the effort altogether.
Understand that God's religion is not meant to cause hardship; when genuine obstacles arise, there are often permissible alternatives that preserve the spirit of the obligation.
Keep a habit of learning from those with more experience; their answers to seemingly simple questions can carry profound wisdom.

title: The Sacrificial Animals and Preparations for the Umrat al-Qada
summary: The Prophet appointed Najiyya ibn Jundab al-Aslami to lead the sacrificial animals ahead during the Umrat al-Qada, and the Muslims set out reciting Labbayka with sixty head of animals prepared for sacrifice.
story: Al-Wsqidi stated, 'Ghanim b. Abn Ghanim related to me, from 'Ahd All& b. Dinar, from Ihn Wmar, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed Najiyya h. Jundab al-Aslami in charge of the animals to be sacrificed. He would go on ahead searching for fodder among the trees, accompanied by four young men of Aslam. The Messenger of God (SAAS) took out 60 head on the Cumrat al-qadsyya."' Muhammad b. Nucaym al-Mujammir related to me, from his father, from Abn Hurayra, who said, 'I was with the man in charge of the sacrificial animals, driving them.' Al-Waqidi stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth reciting Labbayka! "I am at Your service!" and the Muslims with him recited the same.'
lessons: Collective worship and the shared declaration of devotion creates a powerful bond among believers and magnifies the spiritual experience.
Careful preparation and organization in acts of worship reflects both respect for God and care for the community.
Every role in a communal act of worship, from the one leading to the one driving the animals, carries dignity and worth.
practical_advice: Approach significant moments in your life with deliberate preparation, as the Prophet organized every aspect of the pilgrimage carefully.
Find ways to participate in communal acts of worship or service, even in a supporting role, as each contribution matters.
Use phrases of remembrance and gratitude — your own equivalent of Labbayka — to center yourself spiritually as you move through your day.

title: Quraysh's Fear at the Approach of the Muslims During the Umrat al-Qada
summary: As the Prophet approached Mecca for the Umrat al-Qada with cavalry and weapons stored nearby, the Quraysh grew frightened and confused, sending out Mikraz ibn Hafs to confront him, who affirmed the Prophet's piety and trustworthiness after the Prophet clarified he would not enter with arms.
story: Muhammad h. Maslama went on with the cavalry to Marr al-Zahrm, where he encountered a few men of Quraysh. They asked Muhammad h. Maslama some questions and he replied, 'This is the Messenger of God (SAAS); he will arrive at this place tomorrow morning, if God wills it.' They (the Quraysh) noticed that Bashir b. Sa'd had many weapons with him and so they left in a hurry and went to Quraysh whom they informed of the weapons and cavalry they had seen. Quraysh were scared by this. They told one another that they had not stirred up any trouble and they were maintaining their pact of a truce, and they wondered why the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his men were attacking them. The Messenger of God (SAAS) made a halt at Marr al-Zahran. He sent on the weapons to the Yaljij valley, from where they could see the stones marking the limits of the sacred territory. Quraysh sent out Mikraz b. H a f ~b. al-Ahnaf with a party of Quraysh and they encountered h i at the valley of Ya'jij; the Messenger of God (SAAS) was there with his men having met up with the animals to be slaughtered and the weapons. The Quraysh asked, 'Muhammad, you've never been known to break your word for any excuse, whether the issue is major or minor; are you now entering the sacred territory with your men bearing arms, even though you agreed to enter only with weapons such as travellers carry, swords in sheaths?' The Prophet (SAAS) replied, 'I will not bring in weapons against them.' Mikraz b. Hafs commented, 'This is what you are known for - piety and trustworthiness.' He then hurried back to Mecca with his Companions. When Mikraz b. H a f ~brought the news of the Prophet (SAAS), Quraysh left Mecca for the mountain heights. As they left Mecca, they told one another that they would not look at him or his Companions.
lessons: A reputation for trustworthiness and integrity is one of the most powerful forces available to a person; it can disarm hostility and transform adversaries into witnesses of one's character.
Honoring the letter and spirit of agreements, even under pressure, is a mark of prophetic character and is ultimately protective.
Fear often arises from misunderstanding; when the truth is clearly communicated, it can dissolve tension and restore peace.
practical_advice: Invest consistently in your reputation for honesty and reliability — it will speak for you in moments when words alone might not be believed.
When entering a tense situation, make your peaceful intentions unmistakably clear through both words and actions.
If you feel anxious about how others perceive you or your intentions, focus on behaving with integrity; your actions will eventually vindicate you.

title: The Prophet's Entry into Mecca and the Jogging of Circumambulation During Umrat al-Qada
summary: The Prophet entered Mecca on the fourth of Dhu al-Qa'da for the Umrat al-Qada, and ordered his Companions to jog during the first three circuits of the Kaaba to display their physical strength to the Quraysh who had claimed they were weakened by fever.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS), ordered the animals for slaughter to be driven ahead and they were corralled at Dha Tuwa. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went on with his men; he was riding his camel at-Qaswal and they kept their eyes on him, calling out Labbayka! 'At Your service, 0 Lord!' and brandishing their swords. When they arrived at Dha Tuwa, he stood up on his mount al-Qaswal, while Ibn Rawaha took its reins and spoke lines of impromptu poetry, saying, 'Unbelievers, clear out of his path.' In both jahih collections there is a hadirh from Ibn CAbbas in which he stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) and his men arrived in the morning of the 4th - meaning of Dha al-Qa'da, 7 AH - and the polytheists said, "A delegation is approaching you who have been rendered weak by the fevers of Yathrib." So the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered his men to jog for the three circuits and to walk between the two comers. All that prevented him having them jog throughout all the circuits was his wish to spare them (the effort).'
lessons: Dignified courage in the face of mockery and underestimation is far more powerful than angry retaliation; the Prophet responded to slander not with words but with a display of strength and devotion.
Community solidarity — moving together, calling out together, supporting one another — multiplies individual resilience into collective strength.
Leadership involves both inspiring others and sparing them unnecessary hardship; wisdom knows when to push and when to ease the burden.
practical_advice: When others underestimate or mock you, let your composed, capable actions speak louder than any defense you could offer verbally.
In difficult communal moments, focus on what you are doing together rather than on what is being said against you — unity of purpose is its own answer to criticism.
As a leader or supporter in any group, be attentive to when people need to be pushed forward and when they need to be given relief; both require wisdom.

title: The Prophet Gathering Provisions at Marr al-Zahran to Show Strength Before Quraysh
summary: When the Prophet's Companions learned that Quraysh were mocking their physical weakness, they suggested slaughtering their mounts for strength, but the Prophet instead had them pool their provisions and eat well, then entered the mosque with confidence.
story: Imam Ahmad stated, 'Muhammad h. al-Sabah related to us, quoting Ismacil b. Zakariyyal, from 'Abd AUgh b. Wthman, from Aba al-Tufayl, from Ibn 'Abbas, (who said) that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) halted at Marr al-Zahran on his 'urnra, his men learned that Quraysh were saying, "They'll never recover from their emaciation." His Companions told him, "If we were to butcher our mounts and eat their meat and sip soup made from them, we'd gain some strength for when we go out among people tomorrow." He replied, "No; don't do that; gather up all the provisions you have." They did so, spread out the scraps of food and ate until satisfied; each of them stuffed the remainder in their bags. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went out and entered the mosque, while Quraysh squatted over towards the Black Stone. He gathered up the left sleeve of his gown, then said, "Do not let the enemy see any insufficiency in you." He saluted the rukn, the corner, ran until hidden behind the south corner, then walked to the rukn al-aswad, the corner with the Black Stone. Quraysh commented, "They're not satisfied with walking; they race around like gazelles!" He did this for three circuits and it became sunna, orthodox practice.'
lessons: Calm, practical wisdom is more valuable than impulsive sacrifices made out of anxiety; the Prophet's solution of pooling and sharing preserved both resources and dignity.
Presenting strength and confidence outwardly, even in difficult circumstances, is not deception — it is a form of protection for the community.
What begins as a situational response to a challenge can, through consistent practice, become a lasting tradition that benefits generations.
practical_advice: When anxiety tempts you toward drastic or impulsive actions, pause and look for a simpler, more sustainable solution — often the wiser path is close at hand.
Before entering a difficult situation, prepare yourself mentally and physically so that you project calm and competence rather than desperation.
Do not allow what others say about your weaknesses to define how you see yourself; enter every space with the dignity you know you carry.

title: The Debate on Whether Jogging at the Kaaba Is Sunna
summary: Ibn Abbas clarified a nuanced distinction about the jogging during circumambulation, explaining that while the Prophet did run at the Kaaba, this originated as a situational response to Quraysh's mockery rather than a general religious ordinance, though most scholars consider it sunna.
story: Abii Dz'ad stated that Aha Salama Miisa related to him, quoting Hammzd - meaning Ibn Salam - quoting Abii 'Asim al-Ghanawi, from Abii al-Tufayl, who said, 'I commented to Ibn 'Abbs, "So your people say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) ran at the ka'ba, and that that practice is sunna, 'customary orthodox practice'." He replied, "They both spoke the truth and lied." "What was true and what lies?" I asked. He replied, "They did speak the truth when they said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) ran. They also lied; it is not sunna. At the time of al-Hudaybiyya, Quraysh said, 'Let Muhammad and his Companions alone until they die of worms!' Then they made a truce on the understanding that he would return the following year and stay in Mecca for three days. When he came, the polytheists went over towards Mt. Qu'ayqi%. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told his Companions, 'Run three times at the ka'ba. But that is not sunna.'"' Muslim related this from a hadith of Sacid al-Jariri, 'Abd Alhh b. 'Abd al-Rahmiin b. Abii Husayn and 'Abd al-Malik b. Sa'id b. Abjar, all three of them quoting Abii Tufayl 'Amir b. Wxthila, from Ibn 'Abbs, in much the same words. The act of running while circumambulating is considered sunna by most scholars. The Messenger of God (SAAS) did run during the kmrat al-qada', and also at the 'umrat al-3rCrZna, as Aba Dii'iid and Ibn Mzja related, from a hadith of 'Ahd Allzh b. Wthmzn b. Khuthaym, from Aba al-Tufayl, from Ibn 'Abbns, who so stated. It is clearly established in the hadith of Jxbir given by Muslim and others, that the Messenger
lessons: Seeking precision in understanding religious knowledge is a virtue; not every action reported of the Prophet carries the same legal weight, and scholars must distinguish carefully between them.
The origins of a practice can illuminate its meaning; knowing why something was done adds depth to how it is observed.
Intellectual honesty — acknowledging both what is true and what is inaccurate in a popular claim — is a mark of genuine scholarship and integrity.
practical_advice: When you encounter conflicting opinions about an important matter, seek out knowledgeable people who can explain the nuances rather than accepting the simplest version of the story.
Understand the context behind the practices and habits you follow; this deepens your commitment and helps you apply them wisely.
Be willing to say 'it is more complicated than that' when a simple answer would be misleading — honesty about complexity builds real trust.

title: Bilal's Call to Prayer from atop the Ka'ba during Umrat al-Qada
summary: After the Messenger of God completed his duties at Umrat al-Qada, he entered the Ka'ba and ordered Bilal to make the call to noon prayer from atop the Ka'ba's roof. The Quraysh leaders reacted with bitterness and grief, while God later honored most of them with Islam.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had finished his duties at the 'umrat al-qada', he went inside the ka'ba. He remained inside until Bilal, from atop the ka'ba's roof, made the call to the al-zuhr, noon, prayer; the Messenger of God (SAAS) had given him orders to do this. 'Ikrima b. Abi Jahl by not having him hear this slave commented, 'God honoured Aba al-Hakam by not having him hear this slave say what he is saying!' Safwan b. Umayya said, 'Praise be to God who took away my father before he saw this!' Khalid b. Usayd agreed, 'Praise be to God who took the life of my father so that he did not witness this day when Bilal climbs on top of the ka'ba and brays!' Suhayl b.
