Wmar and the men with him covered their faces when they heard this." The hadith of al-Bayhaqi stated, "God honoured most of them with Islam."
lessons: Even those who seem most hostile to truth can eventually be transformed by God's guidance, reminding us never to despair of anyone's capacity for change.
Acts of worship performed openly and with dignity, like Bilal's call to prayer from the Ka'ba, carry profound symbolic power and can shake the hearts of even the most hardened opponents.
Those who are consumed by pride and attachment to worldly status often struggle most painfully when they witness the rise of what they once opposed.
practical_advice: When you face mockery or dismissal for your beliefs or values, draw strength from the knowledge that sincere devotion often outlasts opposition.
Reflect on moments in your own life when something once painful or humiliating turned out to be a source of eventual blessing or transformation.
Practice releasing attachment to how others perceive your acts of worship or devotion; your sincerity matters more than the approval of those around you.

title: The Marriage of the Prophet to Maymuna during Umrat al-Qada
summary: During the journey of Umrat al-Qada, the Messenger of God married Maymuna, daughter of al-Harith, with her sister Umm al-Fadl and her husband al-Abbas facilitating the marriage. Maymuna herself responded to the proposal with immediate and complete devotion, offering herself and all she possessed to the Prophet.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Abu Salih and 'Abd Allah b. Abi Najih related to me, from 'Ata' and Mujahid, from Ibn 'Abbas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) married Maymuna, daughter of al-Harith, on that journey of his when he was haram, in a state of ritual purity. It was al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib who married him to her." Ibn Hisham stated, "She had appointed her sister, Umm al-Fadl, to represent her and she in turn entrusted the task to her husband al-'Abbas. It was he who married her to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and gave her from him a dowry of 400 dirhams." Al-Suhayli reported that when the proposal of the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached her, she was mounted on a camel. She said, "Both the camel and what is on it belong to the Messenger of God (SAAS)." He also stated, "It was about her that the verse was revealed, '(We have made lawful to you) a believing woman if she gives herself to the Prophet, if the Prophet wished to marry her. This provision is only for you, apart from all the believers.'" (surat al-Ahzab; XXXIII, v.50).
lessons: Maymuna's immediate response — offering her camel and herself entirely — illustrates how true devotion and love of God expresses itself through wholehearted giving without hesitation.
Family ties and trusted intermediaries can play a beautiful and honourable role in facilitating important life decisions, as al-Abbas did in representing his nephew.
The Qur'an's acknowledgment of Maymuna's gift to the Prophet reminds us that sincere gestures of devotion are seen and honoured by God.
practical_advice: When an opportunity for something meaningful presents itself, respond with openness and generosity rather than hesitation or excessive calculation.
Lean on trusted family members or mentors when navigating important life transitions; their wisdom and support can be a great blessing.
Reflect on what it means to offer yourself fully to your commitments and relationships, and consider whether you hold back out of fear or pride.

title: The Prophet's Departure from Mecca after Umrat al-Qada and the Dispute over Hamza's Daughter
summary: After completing his 'umra pilgrimage and being asked by the Quraysh to leave, the Prophet departed Mecca. As he left, Hamza's orphaned daughter called out to him, leading to a tender dispute among Ali, Ja'far, and Zayd over her guardianship, which the Prophet resolved with wisdom and profound words of affection for each man.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Ubayd Allah b. Musa related to him, from Isra'il, from Abu Ishaq, from al-Bara', who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) made the 'umra pilgrimage in Dhu al-Qa'da. The people of Mecca refused to invite him to enter Mecca until he compacted with them that he would stay there three days. When they drew up the document, they wrote, 'This is what Muhammad, the Messenger of God, has agreed.' They (the Meccans) commented, 'We don't agree to this. If we knew you to be God's messenger, we would not have opposed you at all. You are, however, Muhammad, son of 'Abd Allah.' He stated, 'I am the Messenger of God, and I am Muhammad, the son of 'Abd Allah.' He then told 'Ali b. Abi Talib, 'Erase the words "Messenger of God".' He replied, 'No, by God, I'll not erase you, ever!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then took the document - he could not write well - and wrote, 'This is what Muhammad, son of 'Abd Allah, has agreed to. No weapons will enter Mecca except swords in sheaths. And he will not take away any one of its people who wishes to follow him. Also, he will not prevent any of his men from staying if they wish to do so.' "When he did enter and the period for the stay was over, they came to 'Ali and said, 'Tell your master to leave us, since the time is over.' The Prophet (SAAS) therefore went to leave, but he was followed by Hamza's daughter calling out, 'Uncle! Uncle!' 'Ali took her by the hand and told Fatima, 'Take care of your cousin.' She carried her away. 'Ali, Zayd and Ja'far then began quarrelling over her. 'Ali said, 'I should take her; she is my uncle's daughter.' Ja'far said, 'She's the daughter of my uncle too, and her aunt is my wife.' Zayd said, 'She's my brother's daughter.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) decided that she should go with her aunt, saying, 'An aunt has the same standing as a mother.' He then said to 'Ali, 'You are from me, and I am from you.' To Ja'far, he said, 'You resemble me in appearance and character.' To Zayd, he said, 'You are our brother and our companion.' 'Ali then asked, 'Will you not marry Hamza's daughter?' He replied, 'She is the daughter of my foster-brother.'" Al-Waqidi narrated the story of Hamza's daughter. He stated, "Ibn Umm Habiba related to me, from Da'ud b. al-Husayn, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, that 'Umara, daughter of Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib and her mother Salma, daughter of 'Umays, were at Mecca. "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived 'Ali b. Abi Talib spoke with him, saying, 'How can we abandon the daughter of our uncle, an orphan, amidst the polytheists?' The Prophet (SAAS) did not forbid bringing her out, and he ('Ali) did so. Zayd b. Haritha, the wasi, the executor, of Hamza, now spoke up; the Prophet (SAAS) had made Zayd and Hamza brothers when he had established brotherly relationships between the emigrants."
lessons: The Prophet's compassionate resolution of the dispute over Hamza's orphaned daughter shows that the welfare of a vulnerable child takes priority over adult claims of right or kinship.

His personal words to each of Ali, Ja'far, and Zayd — affirming each man's unique bond with him — demonstrate that wise leadership acknowledges and honours individuals rather than offering blanket treatment.

Even in the midst of political tension and a forced departure from his homeland, the Prophet's heart remained open to the needs of an orphaned child calling out for him, teaching us that no pressure excuses us from care for the vulnerable.
practical_advice: When disputes arise over responsibilities for a child or vulnerable person, shift the focus from who has the strongest claim to what arrangement truly serves that person's wellbeing.
Make it a practice to affirm the unique value of the people close to you; specific, sincere words of recognition — as the Prophet gave to Ali, Ja'far, and Zayd — strengthen bonds and heal hearts.
If you are forced to leave or lose access to something you love, as the Prophet was forced to leave Mecca, channel your grief into care for those around you who may also be hurting.

title: Dispute Over Umama, Daughter of Hamza, and the Prophet's Judgment
summary: Ali, Zayd, and Ja'far each claimed guardianship of Hamza's orphaned daughter after the Conquest of Mecca. The Prophet resolved the dispute by awarding her to Ja'far due to his wife being her maternal aunt.
story: b. al-Husayn, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Ahhas, that Wmwa, daughter of Hamza h. 'Ahd al-Muttalib and her mother Salma, daughter of Wmays, were at Mecca. "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived 'Ali h. Aba Tdib spoke with him, saying, 'How can we abandon the daughter of our uncle, an orphan, amidst the polytheists?' The Prophet (SAAS) did not forbid bringing her out, and he ('Ali) did so. Zayd b. H%itha, the wag, the executor, of Hamza, now spoke up; the Prophet (SAAS) had made Zayd and Hamza brothers when he had established brotherly relationships between the emigrants (and the anjar). Zayd said, 'I have the greater right to her; she is my brother's daughter.' When Ja'far heard that, he said, 'An aunt is (as) a mother. I have more right to her because her aunt, Asma', daughter of Wmays, is with me.' 'Ali commented, 'Do I really see you fighting? She is my uncle's daughter and I brought her out from among the polytheists. You have no claim better than mine; I have more right to her than you do.' The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'I will judge between you. As for you, Zayd, you are the agent of God and of His messenger. You, Ja'far, are similar to me as my features and my disposition. You, Ja'far, have most right to her; your wife is her maternal aunt, and a woman cannot be wed along with her maternal or her paternal aunt.' And he judged that she should go to Ja'far." Al-Waqidi stated, "When he awarded her to Jacfar, Jacfar arose and skipped around the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter asked, 'What's this then, Jaqfar?' He replied, 'Messenger of God, whenever the Negus was pleased with someone, he would get up and skip around hi.'Then he said to the Prophet (SAAS), 'You marry her.' He replied, 'She's the daughter of my foster-brother.' So the Messenger of God (SAAS) married her to Salama h. Aba Salama. The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to say, 'Did I reward Abii Salama?'"
lessons: Just and wise leadership resolves even emotionally charged disputes without bitterness, showing that clear principles bring peace where passion causes conflict.
An orphan's welfare and dignity must be protected; those in power have a responsibility to ensure the vulnerable are cared for, not abandoned.
Competing claims can coexist among well-meaning people; the resolution of conflict requires a fair arbiter and willingness to accept a just outcome.
practical_advice: When you are in a dispute with people you love, seek a trusted and impartial mediator rather than letting the conflict escalate or fester.
Protect and advocate for the vulnerable people in your life — orphans, the lonely, the forgotten — even when it requires personal inconvenience.
When a decision goes against you in a fair process, practice accepting it gracefully, as Zayd and Ali did, trusting that justice has been served.

title: The Revelation Confirming the Vision of Entering the Sacred Mosque
summary: After the Prophet returned from the 'Umrat al-Qada, a Quranic verse was revealed confirming that his vision of entering the sacred mosque in safety would indeed be fulfilled, and connecting this promise to the nearby victory.
story: Ibn Hisham stated, "It was regarding that Cumra, so Abn Wbayda related to me, that the words of the Almighty were revealed, 'God will fulfil the true visions of His messenger: you will certainly enter, if God will it, the holy mosque in safety, with your heads shaven or shorn, not fearing. He knew what you did not know and vouchsafed for you a victory close at hand'" (serat al-Fath; XLVIII, v.27).
lessons: Divine promises are fulfilled in their own time and in ways we may not expect; patience and trust in God's plan is always vindicated.
Apparent setbacks and delays are not signs of abandonment; God often prepares a greater opening after a period of waiting.
The Quran itself served as a source of emotional reassurance for the early Muslim community, and recitation of scripture remains a powerful tool for hope.
practical_advice: When you feel a long-held hope has been delayed or denied, revisit the sources of your faith for reassurance that God's promises are real and on their way.
Keep a record of moments when things worked out after a period of difficulty; this becomes evidence for your heart that trust in God is well-placed.
Share reminders of divine promise with your community when they are anxious or discouraged, as the Prophet's companions were comforted by revelation in moments of uncertainty.

title: The Expedition of Ibn Abi al-Awja' al-Sulami to Banu Sulaym
summary: The Prophet sent Ibn Abi al-Awja' with fifty horsemen to Banu Sulaym, but the tribe was forewarned by a scout, gathered a large force, and attacked the Muslim mission from all sides, killing most of them before the wounded leader returned to Medina.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related here the expedition of Ihn Abn alLCAwja'al-Sularni to Bmn Sulaym. He then traced the line of transmission from al-Waqidi, "Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah b. Muslim related to me, from al-Zuhri, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from the 'urnrat al-gad~ain Dhn al-Hijja, 7, AH. He then sent forth Ihn Abn alLCAwja'al-Sulami along with 50 horsemen. But the scout sent out by that tribe warned them and gave them information. They gathered a large force and so when Ihn Aha alLCAwja'came to them, they were well prepared. When the men of the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw their force, they invited them to accept Islam. The enemy pelted them with arrows, refusing to listen to them, saying, 'We don't need what you're offering.' They shot at them for a while then called up reinforcements that overlooked them (the Muslims) from all sides. The (Muslim) force fought fiercely until most of them had been killed. Ihn Abii alLCAwja' suffered numerous wounds. He withstood his pain and returned to Medina with his remaining men, arriving there on the 1st of Safar, 8 AH.'"
lessons: Those who carry a message of good will sometimes face violent rejection; this does not mean the mission was wrong or that God has abandoned them.
Enduring physical and emotional pain with perseverance, as Ibn Abi al-Awja' did in bearing his wounds back home, is itself a form of courage and faithfulness.
Losses in service of a righteous cause are not failures; they are part of the larger story of truth establishing itself in the world.
practical_advice: When your sincere efforts to help or guide others are rejected harshly, do not conclude that you were wrong to try; grieve the outcome but do not abandon the mission.
In times of overwhelming difficulty, focus on the one next step — like making it back home — rather than being paralyzed by the full weight of the situation.
Honor those in your community who have suffered loss in service of others; their sacrifice deserves acknowledgment and remembrance, not silence.

title: The Conversion of 'Amr ibn al-'As at the Court of the Negus
summary: 'Amr ibn al-'As, a fierce opponent of Islam, traveled to Abyssinia intending to have the Prophet's emissary killed, but a powerful rebuke from the Negus shook him spiritually and led to his embracing Islam and pledging allegiance.
story: He narrated through al-W~qidi, "'Abd al-Uamid b. Jacfar informed us, from his father (who said) that 'Amr b. al-'AS stated, 'I was stubbornly opposed to Islam. I was present at Radr with the polytheists and escaped. Then I was present at Uhud and escaped. Finally I was present at al-khandaq and escaped. "'I told myself, "What suffering! I swear by God, may Muhammad prevail over Quraysh!" So I took my assets and moved to al-Raht. And I diminished my contacts with people. When the al-Hudaybiyya pact was agreed and the Messenger of God (SAAS) left in peace and Quraysh returned to Mecca, I began saying, "So Muhammad is going to come to Mecca with his men; Mecca is no place to be in, nor is al-Ta'if. The best thing to do is to leave." I was then still hostile to Islam. I think that if all Quraysh accepted Islam, I would not do so. "'I went to Mecca and gathered together some men of my tribe who saw things as I did, repeated my views and sought my advice in matters concerning them. I asked them, "How am I in your view?" They replied, "As our adviser and our protector, a man of fine character and wise decision."' "He went on, 'I then told them, "You well know, by God, that I consider this matter of Muhammad as serious and reprehensible; I have reached a decision." "What is that?" they asked. "We should go and join the Negus", I told them, "and live with him. If Muhammad prevails, we will be there with the Negus; and we would be far better off under his control than under that of Muhammad. And if Quraysh prevail, they already know us well." ""'This is the right decision (they responded)." I told them, "Then gather up presents for him." The gift he most appreciated from our land was leather. So we gathered much leather for him, then left and went to the Negus. While we were there, who should arrive but 'Amr b. Umayya al-Damri. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent him with a document he had prepared marrying him to Umm Habiha, daughter of Abii Sufyan. He went in to see the Negus and then came out again. I told my companions, "That is 'Amr b. Umayya al-Dam. If I had gone in to the Negus and asked him for him, and he had given h i to me and I had cut off his head, then Quraysh would have been delighted and I would have been rewarded for killing Muhammad's emissary." "'I did go in to the Negus and bowed down before him as I was accustomed to do. He said, "Welcome to my friend! Have you brought something as a gift for me from your country?" "Yes, 0 king," I told him. "I have much better leather to give to you." I then presented it to him and he was delighted. Some of it he distributed to his generals and the rest he had stored away and had a record of it written and kept. "'When I saw he was in a good mood, I asked, "Your majesty, I just noticed a man leaving your presence. He is the messenger from an enemy of ours. He has caused us much trouble and has killed some of our leaders and best men. Please hand h i over to me, so that I may kill him." He became angry at this, raised his fist and brought it down so hard on my nose that I thought he had broken it. My nostril soon flowed with blood that dripped on to my clothing. I felt so humiliated I wished the earth would open up and swallow me. "'I eventually said, "Your majesty, if I had known you would be upset at what I said, I would not have asked you."' "He went on, 'He then became embarrassed and said, "'Amr, how could you ask me to hand over to you for execution the messenger of the man to whom the al-naniis aal-akbar, the Archangel Gabriel, comes, just as he did to Moses and to Jesus?"' "'Amr went on, 'God then changed my attitude from the way it had been, and I asked myself, "When the Arabs and non-Arabs alike recognize this truth, how can you oppose it?" I then said to the Negus, "Your majesty, do you really testify to that?" "I so testify to God, 'Amr," he replied. "Obey me and follow him. I swear by God that he is right and that he will definitely prevail over those who oppose him, just as Moses prevailed over Pharoah and his armies." "'I asked him, "Will you accept my allegiance to him in Islam?" "Yes," he replied. He then stretched forth his hands and accepted my allegiance. He called for someone to bring a wash basin and the blood was washed from me and he presented me with garments in which to dress. My own clothing was soaked in blood and I cast it aside. Then I went out to my companions and when they saw the garments from the Negus they were delighted. They asked, "Did you receive what you wanted from your friend?" I told them, "I was reluctant to talk about it with him on the first occasion; I told him I would come back." ""We agree with that," they said."' "'Amr went on, 'So I left them, as if I had something specific to do and made my way to where the boats were moored. I found one ship that was loaded and about to leave. I embarked with them and they sailed it away to al-Shu'ba, where I went ashore, carrying my money. I bought a camel and headed for Medina, passing by Marr al-Zabriin. I then went on until I reached al-Hadda, where I met two men who had preceded me there shortly before. They were looking for accommodation, one man entering a tent, while the other held their mounts. Then I saw that one of them was Khdid b. al-Walid! I asked him, "Where are you headed?" "To Muhammad," he replied. "The people are all joining Islam and there's no one left with any power. I swear by God, if I were to oppose, he'd have us by the neck, like a hyena's neck is caught in his cave." ""'I swear by God," I told him, I too am headed for Muhammad and am accepting Islam." Then Wthman h. Talha emerged and welcomed me. We"
lessons: Even the most stubborn opponent of truth can be transformed in a single moment of humiliation and honest reflection; no heart is beyond the reach of God's guidance.
Pride and the fear of social shame are among the greatest barriers to accepting truth; when 'Amr's pride was broken, his heart opened.
The courage of righteous leaders — like the Negus who rebuked a powerful visitor without fear — can be the instrument through which God guides people to truth.
practical_advice: If you have been resisting a truth you privately sense is right out of stubbornness or pride, ask yourself honestly: what are you actually protecting, and is it worth it?
Do not wait for perfect conditions or for everyone around you to change before you make a sincere internal decision to grow; 'Amr made his decision alone, quietly, and then acted on it.
When you feel humiliated by a failure or a rebuke, pause before reacting — that moment of shame can be a turning point toward something much better if you let it be.

title: Khalid ibn al-Walid, 'Amr ibn al-'As, and 'Uthman ibn Talha Accept Islam Together
summary: On the road to Medina, 'Amr ibn al-'As encountered Khalid ibn al-Walid and 'Uthman ibn Talha, all independently making their way to accept Islam, signaling a historic turning point as key adversaries of the Muslim community embraced the faith.
story: I then went on until I reached al-Hadda, where I met two men who had preceded me there shortly before. They were looking for accommodation, one man entering a tent, while the other held their mounts. Then I saw that one of them was Khdid b. al-Walid! I asked him, "Where are you headed?" "To Muhammad," he replied. "The people are all joining Islam and there's no one left with any power. I swear by God, if I were to oppose, he'd have us by the neck, like a hyena's neck is caught in his cave." ""'I swear by God," I told him, I too am headed for Muhammad and am accepting Islam." Then Wthman h. Talha emerged and welcomed me. We
lessons: Sincere turning points in a person's life often happen when they find others on the same path; community and shared direction strengthen resolve.
When the tide of truth becomes undeniable, the wise person joins it rather than being swept away in opposition to it.
Former adversaries can become the greatest assets to a cause when they genuinely embrace it; transformation is always possible.
practical_advice: When you are making a difficult but right decision, pay attention to who else around you is moving in the same direction — shared journeys lighten the burden.
If you have been in conflict or competition with someone, remain open to the possibility that you may one day stand on the same side; do not build walls that cannot be crossed.
Recognize the signs in your own life when circumstances are clearly pointing you in a new direction, and have the courage to follow that direction even when it means a complete change.

title: Conversion of 'Amr ibn al-'As, Khalid ibn al-Walid, and 'Uthman ibn Talha to Islam
summary: The story of how 'Amr ibn al-'As, Khalid ibn al-Walid, and 'Uthman ibn Talha journeyed together to Medina and accepted Islam, each arriving at their decision through inner conflict and reflection. Upon meeting the Prophet (SAAS), they were warmly received and given his forgiveness and honor.
story: tent, while the other held their mounts. Then I saw that one of them was Khdid b. al-Walid! I asked him, "Where are you headed?" "To Muhammad," he replied. "The people are all joining Islam and there's no one left with any power. I swear by God, if I were to oppose, he'd have us by the neck, like a hyena's neck is caught in his cave." "I swear by God," I told him, "I too am headed for Muhammad and am accepting Islam." Then Wthman h. Talha emerged and welcomed me. We all three lodged together. "We then reached an agreement and went to Medina. I'll never forget what a man said whom we met at the Aha Wtha well who shouted out, 'YLZrub&! YE rubah! YE rabah!' We were much cheered by this and proceeded further. Then the man looked closely at us and I heard him say, 'After (losing) these two, Mecca has given up the leadership!' I assumed he was referring to myself and to Khalid h. al-Walid. He then turned and hurried off into the mosque; I assumed that he was going in to tell the Messenger of God (SAAS) of our arrival and it was as I thought. "We dismounted at al-Harra and dressed in our best clothes. Then the call was made for the 'asr prayer and we went off to see him. His face was exultant, and the Muslims around him were delighted at our acceptance of Islam. Khalid b. al-Walid went forward and gave his allegiance. Then Wthman h. Talha advanced and gave his. I then went forward but, I swear by God, no sooner had I sat down before him than I could not raise my gaze towards him, I felt so ashamed. I then expressed my allegiance to him, provided that I be forgiven my prior sins and that he would not bring up the past. He said, 'Islam cuts off what preceded it and the hijra cuts off what preceded it.' "And I swear by God, the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave to none of his Companions consideration equal to that he gave myself and Khalid h. al-Walid after we had accepted Islam. We had the same status with Aba Bakr and I enjoyed the same with Wmar; Wmar was somewhat critical towards Khalid."
lessons: Guilt and shame over past actions need not be permanent barriers; the path to renewal is always open to those who sincerely seek it.
True intelligence eventually leads one toward truth, as the Prophet (SAAS) recognized in 'Amr and Khalid, believing their minds would guide them to good.
A sincere turning point, even when delayed, is honored fully — the Prophet (SAAS) did not diminish the newcomers but gave them the highest regard among his companions.
practical_advice: If you carry shame about your past, remind yourself that sincere change is always accepted; do not let guilt become a reason to avoid seeking forgiveness or making a fresh start.
When feeling lost between conflicting paths, seek out trusted companions who are also moving toward what is right — the three companions found strength in traveling together.
Once you have made a sincere commitment to change, embrace it fully and dress your best, both inwardly and outwardly, as the companions did when they approached the Prophet (SAAS).

title: Khalid ibn al-Walid's Inner Journey to Islam
summary: Khalid ibn al-Walid narrates his gradual inner conviction that led him to Islam, including witnessing the Prophet's composure at Hudaybiyya, receiving a letter from his brother al-Walid, and a dream that foretold his spiritual transformation. He eventually traveled to Medina and accepted Islam, receiving the Prophet's forgiveness and warm reception.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that Yahya h. al-Mughira h. 'Abd al-Rahman b. al-Harith h. Hisham related to me, "I heard my father quote Khalid b. al-Walid as saying, 'When God wished for good for me, he instilled Islam into my heart and gave me good sense. I then told myself, "I have witnessed how all these lands are against Muhammad (SAAS); yet whatever I witness in these places makes me want to leave them, feeling that I am out of place in them and that Muhammad will prevail." "'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) left for al-Hudaybiyya, I went forth with some of the polytheist cavalry and encountered the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his men at Usfan. I positioned myself directly facing and opposing him. He and his men performed the al-zuhr, the noon prayer, before us and we thought we should attack him then, but could not make up our minds to do so; which was a very good thing! He saw what we were about to do and he and his men performed the al-'asr, late afternoon prayer, as a salat al-khawf, prayer in fear of attack. This made an impression on us and I said, "The fellow is denied us!" We moved back and he avoided our horsemen by taking the route to the right. "'When Quraysh made peace with him at al-Hudaybiyya and put him off till the following year, I asked myself, "What is left? Where can I go? To the Negus? He follows Muhammad, whose men are safe with him. Shall I go to Heraclius and abandon my faith for Christianity or Judaism? Should I go and live with foreigners, or stay at home with those who remain?" "'While I was in this quandary, the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca to perform the 'umrat al-qadiyya. I pretended to be absent and did not witness his entry. My brother, al-Walid h. al-Walid, had come in with the Prophet (SAAS) on the 'umrat al-qadiyya and he asked after me but could not locate me. He wrote me a letter in which he said, "In the name of God the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate. I know of nothing more strange than your antipathy for Islam, being as intelligent as you are. Could anyone disregard something like Islam? The Messenger of God (SAAS) has asked me about you. He said, 'Where is Khalid?' I replied, 'God will bring him.' He then asked, 'Could someone like him disregard Islam? If he were to put his energy and bravery to work with the Muslims it would be better for him. We would certainly give him precedence before others.' Take note, brother, of what good things you are missing." "'When his letter reached me, I got ready to come out and experienced a strong desire for Islam. The question the Messenger of God (SAAS) had asked about me pleased me greatly. I dreamt of being in a constrained and barren land and emerging into one that was spacious and fertile. I told myself that this was a vision. And when I went to Medina, I decided to relate it to Abu Bakr. He said, "Your exiting to which God led you was to Islam. The constrained land where you had been was polytheism." "'And so when I made up my mind to come out to the Messenger of God (SAAS), I wondered in whose company I would go to him. Then I met up with Safwan b. Umayya and asked him, "Abu Wahb, don't you see what we are doing? We're (as useless) as molars! Muhammad may defeat the Arabs and the non-Arabs alike. If we joined and followed Muhammad, then honour gained by him would be honour for us all." But Safwan rejected this vigorously, saying, "If I were the very last, I'd never follow him!" "'We parted and I told myself that this was someone whose brother and father had been killed at Badr. Then I met 'Ikrima b. Abu Jahl to whom I said the same as I had to Safwan b. Umayya. He responded just as Safwan had done. I asked him to keep confidential what I had said, and he agreed. "'I went off home, had my camel readied and left on it. On the way I met 'Uthman b. Talha; knowing him to be a friend, I thought I would tell him my plan. I then recalled the names of some of his elders who had been killed and I thus became reluctant to mention this to him. But I said to myself, "What does it matter to me now that I'm about to leave?" So I told him what had transpired and said, "We're like a fox in a hole that would be sure to come up if doused with buckets of water." I said to him what I had earlier told my other two friends and he responded positively at once. I told him, "I got up this morning ready to proceed forth; over there at the road, kneeling down, is my mount." We then agreed to meet at Ya'jij. If he got there first, he would wait; if I arrived there before him, I would wait there. "'We set off at night, before dawn, and we met at Ya'jij. We proceeded on together to al-Hadda, where we found 'Amr h. al-'As. He welcomed us warmly, as we did him. He asked us where we were headed and we asked him why he had left. He wanted to know our purpose in leaving, and we replied, "To enter Islam and follow Muhammad (SAAS)." He said that that was his purpose too. "'So we travelled on together into Medina and dismounted at the back of al-Harra. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was informed of our arrival, and he was delighted with us. I dressed in my best clothes and headed for the Messenger of God (SAAS). My brother met me and said, "Hurry along; the Messenger of God (SAAS) has been told about you, is delighted at your arrival and is awaiting you." "'We stepped up our pace and when I looked at him I saw him smiling all the time until we stopped before him. I addressed him as "Prophet" and he returned a greeting, his face beaming. I said, "I bear witness that there is no god but God and that you are the Messenger of God." He then said, "Come here." And then he said, "Praise be to God who guided you. I considered you to have intelligence that I hoped would only lead you to good." I replied, "Messenger of God, I used to agree with those others I have seen to be stubbornly opposed to the truth; please pray to God that He will forgive me this." He replied, "Islam cuts off what preceded it." I asked, "Messenger of God, and so hence ..." He then said, "O God, forgive every action Khalid b. al-Walid took to block God's cause." "'Khalid went on, "Uthman and 'Amr then came up and expressed their allegiance to the Messenger of God (SAAS). Our arrival was in Safar of 8 AH. And, I swear by God, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave precedence to none of his Companions over myself."'
lessons: Spiritual transformation often begins with a quiet, growing sense of inner displacement — feeling out of place in one's current life can be a sign pointing toward a better path.
The care and curiosity shown by others, even from a distance, can be the spark that breaks through a person's resistance; the Prophet's asking after Khalid moved him deeply.
Dreams and symbols can carry meaning that helps us process confusion; Khalid's dream of leaving a barren land for a fertile one was a reflection of his soul's readiness for change.
practical_advice: If you feel stuck or out of place in your current circumstances, take that inner restlessness seriously rather than suppressing it — it may be guiding you toward necessary change.
When you are unsure about a major decision, try speaking honestly with even one trusted companion; Khalid found his path clarified when he finally shared his thoughts with 'Uthman ibn Talha.
If guilt over past actions is holding you back from moving forward spiritually or personally, remember that sincere repentance wipes the slate clean — do not let your history define your future.

title: The Expedition of Shuja' ibn Wahb al-Asadi Against Hawazin
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) dispatched Shuja' ibn Wahb with 24 men on a night raid against a group of Hawazin, instructing them to travel covertly. They surprised their target, captured many camels and goats, and returned to Medina where each man received 15 camels as his share of the booty.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that Ibn Abi Sabra related to him, from Ishaq b. 'Abd Allah b. Abi Farwa, from Umar b. al-Hakam, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Shuja' b. Wahb with 24 men out against a group of Hawazin with orders to attack them. He left, travelling by night and remaining hidden by day. When he came to them, they were taken by surprise. He, Shuja', had told his men not to pursue them vigorously. They seized many camels and goats. They drove them off to Medina, where each man received 15 camels as booty."
lessons: Disciplined obedience to clear instructions — such as Shuja's order not to pursue vigorously — prevents harm and ensures a mission's objectives are met safely.
Strategic patience, such as traveling by night and resting by day, is a form of wisdom that protects those under one's care from unnecessary risk.
Even small groups, when acting with focused purpose and coordination, can accomplish significant tasks.
practical_advice: When facing a challenging task, break it into careful stages rather than rushing headlong; the expedition's success depended on deliberate, disciplined movement.
If you are leading others, give clear and specific guidance so that your team knows not only what to do but what to avoid doing.
After a difficult effort, take time to acknowledge the contributions of everyone involved and ensure that the rewards of shared labor are distributed fairly.

title: The Expedition of Shuja' b. Wahb against Hawazin
summary: The Prophet dispatched Shuja' b. Wahb with 24 men against a group of Hawazin, who were surprised by night raids. The Muslims seized camels and goats, and the Hawazin later accepted Islam.
story: Al-Wnqidi stated that Ibn Abii Sabra rclatcd to him, from Ishnq b. 'Abd Allah b. Abn Farwa, from Wmar b. al-Hakam, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Shuja' b. Wahh with 24 men out against a group of Hawazin with orders to attack them. He left, travelling by night and remaining hidden by day. When he came to them, they were taken by surprise. He, Shujn', had told his men not to pursue them vigorously. They seized many camels and goats. They drove them off to Media, where each man received 15 camels as booty." Others maintain that they also took many captives and that the leader (Shujac) chose from them one modest girl and that their people then accepted Islam. The Prophet (SAAS) advised their leader to return the women to them, and he agreed. He gave a choice to the girl he had with him, and she chose to stay. This expedition may be the one to which al-Sh2fi'i referred, quoting Mzlik, from Nafi: from Ihn Wmar, to the effect that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent an expedition towards Najd, 'Abd All& h. Wmar being among them. The latter said, "We captured many camels and our share of the booty was 12 camels apiece, which the Messenger of God (SAAS) awarded us camel by camel." This is included in both sahib collections in a hadith from Malik. Muslim also related it in a hadith of al-Layth, and from a hadith of 'Abd Allah. AU of these quoted Nafi', from Ibn Wmar, in similar words. Abii Da'nd stated that Hanad related to him, quoting 'Abda, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from Nnfi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent an expedition out to Najd and I took part in it. We captured many camels and our leader divided them up to each of us, camel by camel. Then we went on to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and be divided up our booty between us, so that each of us acquired 12 camels, after the khumus (the one-fifth for the bayt al-mdl, the treasury) had been set aside. The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not include in our counts the camels our leader had given us, nor did he criticize him for what he had done. Each one of us received 13 camels, after he had made his distribution."
lessons: Strategic patience and careful planning, such as travelling by night and hiding by day, can lead to success even with small numbers.
The Prophet's compassionate leadership is evident in his advice to return captured women, showing that mercy is a core value even in times of conflict.
Fairness in distributing shared gains, as modeled by the Prophet, strengthens trust and unity within a community.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult task with limited resources, plan methodically and use what advantages you have rather than lamenting what you lack.
In disputes or difficult negotiations, look for solutions that honor the dignity of all parties involved, as the Prophet did by giving the captive girl a choice.
Ensure that whatever you share or distribute among those who depend on you is done transparently and equitably, as this builds lasting trust.

title: The Expedition of Ka'b b. 'Umayr to Banu Quda'a in Syria
summary: The Prophet sent Ka'b b. 'Umayr al-Ghifari with 15 men to Dhat Adah in Syria, where they were ambushed by a vastly larger enemy force. All but one were killed; the sole survivor returned wounded to inform the Prophet.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah related to him, from al-Zubri, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Ka'b b. Wmayr al-Ghif~riwith 15 men out as far as Dhat Adah, in Syria. There they found a very large body of men. The Muslims invited them to accept Islam, but they did not agree and showered them with arrows. When the men of the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw this, they fought the enemy fiercely until they (the Muslims) were killed. One of their men was wounded badly and lay among the dead. In the cool of the night he managed to make his way back to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was about to send out an expedition against them when news reached him that they had gone elsewhere."
lessons: Fulfilling one's duty faithfully, even in the face of overwhelming odds, is itself a form of courage and honour.
A single survivor carrying vital news can change the course of events, reminding us that no individual effort is ever truly insignificant.
Grief and loss in service of a noble cause, while deeply painful, can be a source of lasting spiritual meaning.
practical_advice: When you are outnumbered or overwhelmed in life's challenges, focus on doing what is right in the moment rather than on guaranteeing a particular outcome.
If you survive a traumatic or devastating experience, understand that your role as a witness and bearer of truth has its own profound importance.
Allow yourself to grieve losses fully, but also look for how the experience can inform and guide those who come after you.

title: The Expedition of Mu'ta: Appointment of Commanders and Farewell
summary: The Prophet appointed Zayd b. Haritha, Ja'far b. Abi Talib, and 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha as successive commanders for the expedition to Mu'ta. A Jewish man predicted they would all be killed, and an emotional farewell took place before the army departed.
story: This is the expedition of Zayd b. Haritha with approximately 3,000 men to al-Balqa' territory in Syria. Muhammad b. Ish%qstated, following his account of the 'urnrat al-qadzyya, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained in Medina for the duration of Dhii al-Hijja - the polytheists having controlled that pilgrimage - throughout al-Muharram, Safar and both Rabical-Awwal and al-Thai. In Jumzda al-Uwla he sent out into Syria those who were afflicted at Mu'ta. "Muhammad b. JaYar b. al-Zubayr related to me, from Wrwa b. al-Zubayr, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out his expedition to Mu'ta in Jumada al-UwB, 8 AH. He appointed as their leader Zayd b. Hwitha, saying, "If Zayd should be killed, then Jacfar b. Abii Tzlib will take command. If he should fall, then 'Abd All%hb. Raw*a will lead."' "The army, consisting of 3,000 men, then prepared to leave." Al-Waqidi stated that Rabi'a b. 'Uthrnzn related to him, from 'Amr b. al-Hakam, from his father, who said, "Al-Nueman b. Fanhas, the Jew, came and stood with the men looking over at the Messenger of God (SAAS), when he said, 'Zayd b. H&ha will be commander and if Zayd should be killed, then it will be Jacfar h. Abii Tzlib. If Ja'far is killed, then 'Abd A k h h. Rawaha will be commander. If he should be killed, then the Muslims should choose one from among themselves to appoint over them.' "Al-Nucmzn then said, 'Abii al-Q%im, if you are a prophet and were to so name them, few or many in number, they would all be killed. When the prophets of Banii Isr2il named a commander and then went on to name his successor if he should be killed, then they would all be killed, even if they named 100.' He told Zayd, 'Attend to your affairs. For you will never return, if Muhammad is a prophet.' "Zayd replied, 'I do testify that he is a prophet, a man of truth and piety; may God's peace and blessings be upon him.'" Al-Bayhaqi related this.
lessons: True faith means affirming one's convictions even when facing direct predictions of death, as Zayd demonstrated with his calm testimony.
The Prophet's systematic appointment of successive commanders shows that good leadership always plans for contingency, ensuring the mission does not collapse when tragedy strikes.
Prophecy and foreknowledge, whether seen as ominous or reassuring, do not diminish human responsibility to act with courage and integrity.
practical_advice: When others try to intimidate or discourage you by predicting failure, respond as Zayd did: reaffirm what you know to be true and proceed with dignity.
In your own responsibilities, always have a contingency plan so that the work continues even if you are no longer able to carry it forward.
Confront fear of the future by grounding yourself in your deepest convictions rather than trying to argue away the uncertainty.

title: 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha Weeps at the Farewell of Mu'ta
summary: 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha wept during the farewell before the Mu'ta expedition, explaining his tears came not from love of life but from reflection on a Quranic verse about the certainty of passing through hellfire. He then recited moving poetry expressing his wish for martyrdom.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "When time came for their departure, the men said farewell to the commanders of the Messenger of God (SAAS). When 'Abd All& b. Raw?&a said goodbye with the rest, he wept. When people asked him why, he replied, 'I swear by God, it's not because of my love for this life or my affection for you; I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) reciting a verse from God's Book which mentions hell-fire and says, "All of you will go to it; this is determined and decreed by your Lord" (sarat Maryam; XIX, v.71). And I don't know how I'll be able to escape from it after I've gone into it!' "The Muslims then said to him, 'May God accompany you, protect you and return you all to us in safety.' "'Abd All& b. Raw2ha spoke the following limes, 'But I ask the All-Merciful for forgiveness, and a large wound that bleeds profusely Or a spear from a warrior armed with one that goes through my intestines and my liver. So that it will be said when people pass by my grave, "God gave him guidance as a warrior, and he behaved well."' Ibn Ishaq continued, "The men then were about to leave when 'Abd All& b. Raw&a came over to the Messenger of God (SAAS), said farewell to him and recited the following verses, 'May God affirm the good He presented you, as he gave affirmationto Moses, and a victory like they won. I sensed the goodness in you to be a gift; and God knows me to have tine sight. You are the Messenger and whoever is deprived of your gifts and sight of you has been diminished by fate.'" Ibn Ishaq went on, "The army then set forth and the Messenger of God (SAAS) went out with them to bid them farewell. When he had said goodbye to them and turned away, 'Abd All* b. Rawaha spoke the following verse, 'May peace persist for a man to whom I have said farewell amidst the palm trees, the best friend and escort.'"
lessons: Tears and emotional honesty in the face of mortality are not signs of weakness but of profound spiritual depth and sincerity.
Reflecting on sacred texts can awaken a healthy awareness of one's accountability before God, motivating righteous action rather than despair.
Poetry and expressive language can serve as a powerful vehicle for processing fear, love, and longing, allowing the soul to find peace before a great trial.
practical_advice: When you are overwhelmed by fear or grief, allow yourself to feel it openly rather than suppressing it; honest emotion is a starting point for genuine courage.
Turn to the sacred texts or spiritual writings that ground you when facing something frightening; let them reorient your perspective rather than increase your anxiety.
Try expressing your deepest feelings through writing, poetry, or prayer before a major challenge; this can help you release tension and clarify your intentions.

title: 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha Stays Behind for Friday Prayer Before Mu'ta
summary: 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha delayed his departure for the Mu'ta expedition to perform the Friday prayer with the Prophet, who told him that even spending all the earth's wealth could not equal the blessing of his companions' early departure.
story: Ahmad stated that Abn Mulawiya related to him, quoting al-Hajjaj, from ai-Hakam, from Miqsam, from Ihn 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out CAbd All& b. Rawxha on an expedition, and that day coincided with a Friday. His companions went on ahead, while 'Abd Abdullah h. Raw&a said, '1'11 stay hack and perform the Friday prayer with the Messenger of God (SAAS), then rejoin them.' When the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the prayer, he saw 'Abd All& and asked him, 'What prevented you leaving with your comrades?' He replied, 'I wanted to perform the Friday prayer with you, then catch them up.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Were you to spend all there is on earth you would not attain their departure.'"
lessons: Even acts of sincere worship, if they cause us to delay what God has commanded, cannot substitute for prompt obedience and commitment.
The Prophet's gentle correction of Ibn Rawaha shows that good intentions are valued but must be aligned with one's actual obligations.
Every moment of righteous action has a weight that cannot be compensated for later; timeliness in fulfilling our duties is itself a spiritual virtue.
practical_advice: When you find yourself postponing a commitment for something that feels spiritually beneficial, ask honestly whether it is truly the right priority at that moment.
Accept gentle correction from those wiser than you with grace, as Ibn Rawaha did, without becoming defensive or disheartened.
Remind yourself that the value of acting at the right time often cannot be recovered; develop the habit of prompt, wholehearted commitment to what you have taken on.

title: The Muslim Army at Ma'an Confronts Overwhelming Byzantine Forces
summary: The Muslim army of 3,000 at Mu'ta learned they faced over 200,000 Byzantine and Arab forces. Some wished to send for reinforcements, but 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha rallied them with the call to seek martyrdom and fight with faith rather than numbers.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "They then proceeded and encamped at Ma% in Syria. There news reached them that Heraclius had encamped at Ma'ah, in al-Balqa1 temtory, with a force of 100,000 e m , 'Byzantines'. T o them were joined another 100,000 men of Lakhm, Judhzm, alQayn, Bahra' and Bali, commanded by a man from Bali tribe and the Irzsha clan named Malik h. Zafila." In an account of Ynnus, from Ibn Ishaq, it is stated, "News reached them that Heraclius had encamped at Ma1ah with 100,000 Byzantines and 100,000 must'ribasi AIabs." It is also stated that the Byzantines numbered 200,000, along with 50,000 others. The smallest figure given is that the Byzantines numbered 100,000, while there were also 50,000 Arabs (allied to them). Al-Suhayli related this. (Ibn Ishaq went on) "When this news reached the Muslims, they remained at Ma'an for two nights to discuss the matter. Some said, 'We should send a message to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and tell him of the size of our enemy force. He will then either reinforce us or give us some order we can carry out.' But CAbd All& b. Raw&a encouraged the troops, saying, 'Men, what you dislike is what you have come forth to seek - martyrdom! We are not fighting by means of our numbers or strength; we are combating them only with this religion with which God has honoured us. Go forth; it will only result in one of two good outcomes - either victory or martyrdom!' "The others said, 'By God, Ihn Raw*a is right.' And so the m y went forth. "(Abd Allah h. Rawaha spoke the following lines regarding this interruption of theirs, 'We brought our horses forward from Mt. Aja' and Mt. Far', their bellies distended with grass. The shoes with which we shod them were of stones as smooth as leather. They remained for two nights at Ma% and after their period of rest they were fully restored. When we went ahead our horses had free rein, the veins in their nostrils pulsing hard. I swear by my father, we will go on to Ma'ab, even though both Byzantines and Arabs are there. We gripped their reins and they galloped ahead vigorously, their dust swirling up, Their noise'"
lessons: When facing seemingly impossible odds, reframing the situation in terms of higher purpose rather than mere survival can transform fear into resolve.
The willingness to accept either victory or a noble death removes the paralyzing grip of fear and allows a person to act with full commitment.
Collective courage can be ignited by a single voice of clarity and faith; one person's conviction can turn a wavering group into a determined community.
practical_advice: When confronted with an overwhelming challenge, identify the deeper purpose behind your struggle; this clarity will give you strength that numbers and resources alone cannot provide.
Reframe the possible outcomes of a difficult situation in terms of what you can genuinely accept, so that fear of a particular result no longer controls your decisions.
If you are in a leadership or peer role and see others losing heart, speak your conviction calmly and clearly; authentic faith is contagious and can shift the mood of an entire group.

title: The Army's Advance and Poetry of 'Abd Allah ibn Rawaha
summary: As the Muslim army advanced toward Byzantine territory, commander 'Abd Allah ibn Rawaha recited moving poetry expressing his longing for martyrdom, bringing his young ward Zayd ibn Arqam to tears.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated further, "'Abd Allah b. Abii Bakr related to me that it was related to him from Zayd h. Arqam, who said, 'I was an orphan living in the care of 'Abd Allgh b. Raw*a. He took me forth on that journey, riding behind him on his saddle bag. I swear that by night I heard him recite the following lines, "Since you (my mount) have brought me near and borne my baggage for four nights' distance from the marshes, Then you deserve pleasure and freedom from blame; and I will not return hereafter to my people. The Muslims having come will leave me in Syria where I long to stay And those with whom I am joined in relationship to the Most Merciful, though not my brothers, will take you back. There I will not worry about plants sprouting, nor about palm trees whose roots need water." "'When I heard these words from him I cried and he rapped me with his whip and said, "Why be sad, silly boy, if God grants me martyrdom and you ride home on a saddle?" ' "(Abd Allgh h. Raw*a also spoke the following line of verse on one of his journeys, 'Zayd, Zayd of the lean, fast camels, you have been led forward throughout a long night, so dismount now."'
lessons: Accepting mortality with courage and serenity is a profound form of spiritual strength.
True devotion to God can transform fear of death into peaceful anticipation of meeting Him.
A leader's composure and faith can steady those who depend on him, even in the face of grief.
practical_advice: When facing a daunting challenge, try expressing your fears through writing or journaling, as Ibn Rawaha did through poetry, to process and release anxiety.
If you feel overwhelmed by the possibility of loss, redirect your focus to your ultimate purpose and values rather than the outcome alone.
When someone younger or more vulnerable looks to you for strength, remember that your composure is itself a gift you can give them.

title: The Battle of Mu'ta Begins: The Army Confronts the Byzantine Forces
summary: The Muslim army of 3,000 reached the outskirts of al-Balqa and faced the vastly superior Roman and Arab forces of Heraclius, with Abu Hurayra overwhelmed by the enemy's numbers and equipment.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The army then advanced to the outskirts of al-Balqal. There they were met by the Roman and Arab troops of Heraclius, at one of the villages of al-Balqal called Mashaif. The enemy advanced and the Muslims withdrew to a village called Mu'ta. There the forces clashed. The Muslims arranged their forces to meet the enemy, putting a man of Banii Wdhra named Qutha h. Qatada in command of the right flank and an ansari named Wbaya b. Mdik in charge of the left flank." Al-Waqidi stated, "Rabi'a b. Wthman related to me, from al-Maqburi, who quoted Aba Hurayra as saying, 'I was present at the Battle of Mu'ta. When the polytheists approached, we saw such equipment, weapons, horses, brocade, silk and gold as no one ever before witnessed! I was dazzled. Thahit h. Arqam said to me, "Aha Hurayra, don't you think that's a huge force?" "Yes," I replied. "But you weren't present at Badr with us. Superiority in numbers won't defeat us.""'
lessons: Being overwhelmed by a seemingly insurmountable obstacle is a natural human reaction; what matters is how we respond to that feeling.
Experienced mentors can reframe a frightening situation with wisdom drawn from lived experience.
Faith and a track record of God's help in the past can be the most stabilizing force when fear threatens to paralyze us.
practical_advice: When you feel outnumbered or outmatched in life, seek counsel from someone who has faced similar odds and come through them.
Remind yourself of past moments when things worked out despite the odds — these memories are anchors against present fear.
Practice reframing overwhelming situations by asking: 'What would someone with calm, seasoned experience say about this right now?'

title: Zayd ibn Haritha Falls: The First Commander is Martyred
summary: Zayd ibn Haritha, the first commander appointed by the Prophet, fought bravely bearing the Prophet's banner at Mu'ta until he fell amidst enemy spears, fulfilling the Prophet's premonition.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "The forces then engaged and battle commenced. Zayd b. H%itha fought, bearing the banner of the Messenger of God (SAAS), until he perished amidst the enemy spears. Ja'far then picked it up and fought on until he was killed. Ja'far was the first Muslim to hamstring his mount in battle."
lessons: Fulfilling one's duty faithfully to the very end, regardless of personal cost, is among the highest expressions of integrity.
Leadership in the face of certain danger is a form of spiritual courage that transcends ordinary bravery.
Every soul eventually returns to God; what endures is how faithfully one carried out their responsibilities while alive.
practical_advice: When you are in a role of responsibility, focus on carrying out your duty with full commitment rather than calculating personal cost.
Reflect on what it means to be fully present in your purpose — Zayd did not abandon his post even under mortal threat.
In moments of grief over a loss, honor the person by continuing the work they cared about, just as Ja'far picked up what Zayd left behind.

title: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib's Heroic Stand and Martyrdom
summary: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib fought with extraordinary bravery at Mu'ta, hamstringing his horse, having both arms severed while holding the banner, and was ultimately killed at age 33, after which God honored him with two wings in paradise.
story: He continued, "Yahya h. 'Abbad b. 'Abd All& b. al-Zubayr quoted his father 'Abbad as saying, 'My foster-father, of Bana Murra b. 'Awf, who was present at the Battle of Mu'ta, related to me, "I swear I can see Ja'far jumping off his sorrel mare, hamstringing her and fighting on until he was killed, reciting the lines, 'Hail to paradise approaching, its waters fine and cool The punishment of the Byzantines is near, unbelievers of doubtful descent. It's up to me to strike them when we clash.""' Aba Dalad related this from a hadith of Abn Ishaq, but he did not quote the poetry. Those who permit the slaughter of animals if there is concern that the enemy may make use of them make reference to this hadith. Similarly, Abn Hanifa states, concerning al-aghnrhz, sheep or goats, that if they cannot keep up with the pace and it is feared that the enemy will capture and use them, they may be slaughtered and burned to prevent this. But God knows best. Al-Suhayli stated, "No one criticizes Ja'far for this. This action is permissible unless (the animals) are safe from seizure by the enemy. This has nothing to do with the prohibition of killing animals for amusement." Ibn Hisham stated, "A reliable source, a scholar, related to me that Ja'far took the banner in his right hand which was cut off. He then took it in his left hand, and it was cut off. He then hugged it to himself with his upper arms until he was killed. He was 33 years of age. God rewarded him for his valour by giving him two wings in heaven with which he could fly wherever he wished. It is said that one of the Byzantines gave him a blow that day that cut him in two."
lessons: The body can be destroyed but the spirit of sacrifice lives forever; Ja'far's legacy of courage outlasted his mortal life by centuries.
Embracing one's purpose so completely that even physical incapacitation does not halt one's effort is the pinnacle of commitment.
Divine compensation for earthly suffering is a core Islamic belief that reframes loss not as an end but as a transformation.
practical_advice: When you feel that your resources or capacities have been stripped away, ask what you can still do with what remains — Ja'far held the banner with his arms when his hands were gone.
Meditate on the idea that hardship endured with faith is not wasted; it carries a weight of meaning and reward beyond what is immediately visible.
Let the memory of those who gave everything for what they believed in inspire you to give your best in the struggles you face today.

title: 'Abd Allah ibn Rawaha's Inner Struggle Before Martyrdom
summary: After Zayd and Ja'far fell, 'Abd Allah ibn Rawaha took up the banner but momentarily hesitated, wrestling with his soul through poetry before steeling himself and charging forward to martyrdom.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Yahya b. 'Abbad b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr related to me, quoting his father 'Abbad, as follows, 'My foster-father, a man of Banti Murra b. 'Awf, related to me, saying, "When Ja'far was killed, 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha took up the banner and advanced with it, on his horse. Then he began to waver and to hesitate somewhat and so he spoke the following verses, 'I swear, my soul, that you will indeed do battle or be forced to do battle Even though the men are shouting and screaming; why is it I see you hating paradise? For long you have been at ease; are you anything but a drop of liquid inside a skin bag?' "'He also said, 'My soul, even if you're not killed you'll die anyway; this is the fate of death you are exposed to. What you hoped for has been given to you; if you do what they both did, you will have been well directed.' "'The reference here is to his two companions, Zayd and Ja'far. Then he dismounted. When he did so a cousin of his came up carrying a meat bone and said, 'Take strength from this; you have suffered much recently.' He took it from his hand and took a bite out of it. Then he heard a great commotion among the men. He said (to himself), 'And are you still alive!' He threw away the bone, took up his sword and advanced, fighting until he was killed. May God be pleased with him!""'
lessons: Hesitation before a frightening commitment is not weakness; it is the honest experience of a soul confronting its own limits.
Speaking honestly to oneself — even through internal argument — is a form of moral self-accountability that leads to growth.
The decision to act despite fear, made consciously and deliberately, carries a dignity that impulsive bravery does not.
practical_advice: When you find yourself wavering before a difficult but right decision, try articulating your hesitation honestly, as Ibn Rawaha did, rather than suppressing it.
Remind yourself that avoidance does not eliminate the challenge — death comes regardless, and so does the consequence of not acting on what you know is right.
Use your awareness of life's brevity not as a source of dread but as a clarifying lens: what truly matters in the time you have?

title: Khalid ibn al-Walid Takes Command and Withdraws the Army
summary: After the three appointed commanders were martyred, Thabit ibn Arqam urged the Muslims to rally around a leader, and Khalid ibn al-Walid took the banner, skillfully protecting the army and conducting a tactical withdrawal.
story: "Th-ahlt' b. Aqram, brother of Bano al-'Ajlan, then took the banner. He said, 'Muslims, form around one of you!' 'Let it be around you,' they shouted. 'No, I will not do it,' he replied. So they formed around Khzlid h. al-Walid. When he (Khzlid) took the banner he defended his men, keeping them safe, then withdrew and the enemy distanced from him so that he could leave with the men."
lessons: True leadership is sometimes about knowing when to protect lives through strategic retreat rather than pressing forward at all costs.
Humility in refusing a role one is not suited for, and deferring to someone better qualified, is itself a form of leadership.
A community's survival can depend on its willingness to reorganize quickly under capable leadership when circumstances demand it.
practical_advice: When a situation demands urgent leadership and you are not the right person for it, have the courage to say so and point toward someone better suited.
Recognize that protecting what remains after loss is a meaningful and courageous act — not all victories look like advances.
In overwhelming situations, focus on the one actionable step available to you right now, as Khalid did in organizing a safe withdrawal.

title: The Prophet Announces the Deaths of the Three Commanders from Madinah
summary: Before news reached Madinah, the Prophet miraculously announced the martyrdom of Zayd, Ja'far, and Ibn Rawaha, his eyes filling with tears, and described a vision of their elevated stations in paradise.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the army suffered great loss, the Messenger of God (SAAS) - so I have been told - said, 'Zayd b. Haritha took the banner and fought with it until he was martyred. Ja'far then took it and fought, bearing it until he was martyred.' After that the Messenger of God (SAAS) became silent and the faces of the ansar changed as they wondered whether there was something terrible about 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha. Then he said, 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha has taken it now and fought with it until martyred.' "He then said, 'They have been raised up to heaven - I saw in a vision - upon couches of gold. I noticed that the couch of 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha was slanting away from those of his comrades and I asked, "Why is this?" I was told, "They went straight ahead, whereas 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha hesitated somewhat before proceeding."' This is related thus by Ibn Ishaq munqata', with an incomplete chain of transmission. Al-Bukhari stated that Ahmad h. Waqid related to him, quoting Hammad h. Zayd, from Ayyah, from Hamid b. Hilal, from Anas b. Malik, (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) announced to the people the deaths of Zayd, Ja'far and Ibn Rawaha before they received the news. He said, 'Zayd took the banner, then was struck down. Ja'far took it and was struck down. Ibn Rawaha took it and was struck down.' His eyes then brimmed with tears. He went on, 'And then one of the Lord's swords' (Khalid h. al-Walid, that is) 'took it until God gave them victory.' Al-Bukhari is alone in giving this. He also related it elsewhere saying that he (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) was on the minbar when he said, 'And it would not please them to be with us.'"
lessons: Grief is not incompatible with faith; even the Prophet's eyes filled with tears at news of beloved companions' deaths.
God sees every moment of our struggle, including our hesitations, and His judgment is both just and merciful.
Those who lead with love and connection feel the losses of those they care for deeply — this is a mark of compassion, not weakness.
practical_advice: Allow yourself to grieve losses fully and without shame, as the Prophet himself wept openly for those he loved.
Reflect on the idea that even your moments of hesitation or imperfection do not disqualify you from God's mercy and reward.
When mourning someone, honor their specific story and struggles rather than generalizing their memory — the Prophet distinguished each companion's journey individually.

title: The Wounds of Ja'far ibn Abi Talib: Testimony of Ibn Umar
summary: Ibn Umar and others who examined Ja'far's body after the Battle of Mu'ta counted dozens of sword and spear wounds on his front, none on his back, testifying to his unwavering courage in facing the enemy.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Ahmad b. Aba Bakr related to him, quoting Mughira h. 'Abd al-Rahman al-Makhzumi, not al-Hazami, from 'Abd Allah b. Sa'id, from Nafi', from 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed Zayd b. Haritha in command of the expedition to al-Mu'ta. He also said, 'If Zayd should be killed, then Ja'far (will take over); if Ja'far is killed, then 'Abd Allah h. Rawaha.' 'Abd Allah said, 'I was among those at Mu'ta. We searched for (the body of) Ja'far b. Aba Talib and found him among those slain. On his body we found some 90 sword wounds or arrow holes.'" Al-Bukhari is alone also in giving this hadith. Al-Bukhari went on, "Ahmad related to us, quoting Ibn Wahb, from 'Amr, from Ibn Aba Hid, he being Sa'id b. Aba Hild al-Laythi, who said, 'Nafi' informed me that Ibn 'Umar informed him that he stood over Ja'far b. Aba Talib on that day, he having been killed, (and that Ibn 'Umar said), "I counted 50 wounds made by spears or swords, and none were in his back."'" This too is a hadith only given by al-Bukhari. This account may be reconciled with the previous one; Ibn 'Umar saw that number of wounds, whereas others saw more than that. Or that those he saw on his front were inflicted before his death and that when he fell to the ground, they also gave him blows in his back. Ibn 'Umar counted those in his front suffered while he faced the enemy, before he was killed. May God be pleased with him!
lessons: The physical evidence of one's courage can outlast one's life, becoming testimony that inspires generations.
Facing hardship head-on, without retreating or turning away, leaves a mark of integrity that others can witness and learn from.
The care taken to search for, examine, and honor the fallen reflects the deep bonds of brotherhood that Islam cultivates.
practical_advice: Reflect on how you face your own challenges — are you turning toward them or away from them? Ja'far's wounds bore witness to his direction.
Honor the memory of those who gave their all by studying their example with the same attention and care that Ibn Umar gave to examining Ja'far's wounds.
Let the courage of those who faced hardship without retreat be a standard you carry internally, not as guilt but as inspiration.

title: Khalid ibn al-Walid: Nine Swords Broken at Mu'ta
summary: Khalid ibn al-Walid reported that nine swords were broken in his hand during the fierce fighting at Mu'ta, and he was left holding only a Yemeni broad-sword by the end of the battle.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Aba Nu'aym related to him, quoting Sufyan, from Isma'il, from Qays b. Abii Hazim, who said, "I heard Khalid b. al-Walid say, 'At the battle of Mu'ta, nine swords were broken in my hand; I finally held only a Yemeni broad-sword.'" He then narrated it from Muhammad b. al-Muthanna, from Yahya, from Isma'il, who quoted Qays as having said, "I heard Khalid b. al-Walid say, 'At the battle of Mu'ta, nine swords were broken in my hand; a Yemeni broad-sword was'"
lessons: Perseverance through resource depletion — continuing to fight with whatever remains — is a testament to unyielding resolve.
True capacity is tested not when resources are abundant but when they are exhausted and one must continue with almost nothing.
The tools we rely on will fail; what does not fail is the will and creativity to keep going.
practical_advice: When your usual tools, strategies, or supports fail you one by one, do not interpret each failure as a signal to stop — adapt and continue with what remains.
Take inventory of what you still have available when you feel depleted, rather than focusing on what has been lost or broken.
Khalid's account is a reminder that those who endure the hardest battles often have the most broken along the way — perseverance through loss is itself the achievement.

title: Khalid ibn al-Walid at the Battle of Mu'ta: Nine Swords Broken
summary: At the Battle of Mu'ta, Khalid ibn al-Walid fought so fiercely that nine swords were broken in his hand, leaving only a Yemeni broad-sword.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Abu Nu'aym related to him, quoting Sufyan, from Isma'il, from Qays b. Abi Hazim, who said, 'I heard Khalid b. al-Walid say, "At the battle of Mu'ta, nine swords were broken in my hand; I finally held only a Yemeni broad-sword."' He then narrated it from Muhammad b. al-Muthanna, from Yahya, from Isma'il, who quoted Qays as having said, 'I heard Khalid b. al-Walid say, "At the battle of Mu'ta, nine swords were broken in my hand; a Yemeni broad-sword was left in my hand."' Al-Bukhari is alone in giving this hadith.
lessons: Perseverance through exhaustion and adversity is a mark of true commitment to one's duty.
When physical resources fail, inner resolve can carry a person forward.
Even when tools and means are stripped away one by one, the determined soul finds a way to continue.
practical_advice: When you feel you have exhausted every resource, pause and assess what remains available to you rather than giving up entirely.
Train yourself mentally to adapt when your primary plan fails, as Khalid adapted from sword to sword.
Reflect on a past moment when you kept going despite setbacks, and let that memory strengthen you in current struggles.

title: The Prophet Announces the Martyrdom of the Three Commanders from the Minbar
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) mounted the minbar and announced to the gathered Muslims the successive martyrdoms of Zayd ibn Haritha, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, and Abd Allah ibn Rawaha at Mu'ta, praying for each and revealing that Khalid ibn al-Walid had then taken command.
story: 'Abd Allah b. Rabah al-Ansari came to us; the ansar themselves would ask him for legal opinions. The people swamped him; I was among them as they did so. He said, 'Abu Qatada, the horseman of the Messenger of God (SAAS), sent out the jaysh al-'umara', the "army of the commanders", saying, "Zayd b. Haritha will command. If he should be struck down, then Ja'far will command. If Ja'far is struck down, then 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha will command." Ja'far jumped up and said, "Messenger of God, I didn't want you to appoint Zayd over me!" He replied, "Go forth; you do not know which would be better." "They did leave, staying only for as long as God wished. The Messenger of God (SAAS) (later) mounted the minbar, gave an order and the summons was announced, "A full prayer assembly!" People gathered around the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he then said, "I will give you news of this army of yours. They have gone forth and encountered the enemy. Zayd has been martyred." He then prayed for forgiveness for Zayd. He went on, "Then Ja'far took the banner and he charged the enemy and was eventually martyred." He then bore witness that Ja'far had attained martyrdom and sought forgiveness for him. He went on, "'Abd Allah b. Rawaha then took the banner and planted his feet firmly until he was martyred." He prayed for forgiveness for 'Abd Allah. He went on, "Then Khalid b. al-Walid took the banner; he was not one of the (appointed) commanders, but took command himself." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, "O God, he is truly one of Your swords; may You help him." And from that day on Khalid was known as "God's sword."'
lessons: Leadership in crisis often falls to those who are ready and willing, even when not initially designated.
Acknowledging the sacrifice of others with prayer and remembrance honours their contribution and comforts the living.
A community united in grief and prayer finds strength to continue what those who fell began.
practical_advice: When you receive difficult news, gather your community around you rather than processing it in isolation.
Honour those who have sacrificed for you by continuing their work with dedication, not by abandoning it in grief.
When facing a role you did not expect, step forward with trust in God as Khalid did, knowing that divine support accompanies sincere effort.

title: Ja'far's Objection to Being Placed Under Zayd's Command
summary: Before the army departed for Mu'ta, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib objected to the Prophet that Zayd had been appointed commander over him, to which the Prophet replied that one cannot know which arrangement would be better.
story: Ja'far jumped up and said, "Messenger of God, I didn't want you to appoint Zayd over me!" He replied, "Go forth; you do not know which would be better."
lessons: Accepting God's decree, even when it contradicts our personal preferences, is an act of profound faith.
Human judgement about rank and order is limited; divine wisdom encompasses what we cannot see.
Subordinating the ego to a greater mission requires spiritual maturity and humility.
practical_advice: When you are placed in a position lower than you feel you deserve, reflect on whether your resistance is ego-driven or genuinely principle-based before voicing objection.
Remind yourself in moments of frustration that outcomes are not always visible in advance, and trust in the wisdom of those in authority when their integrity is sound.
Practice releasing the need for recognition by focusing on the quality of your contribution rather than your title or rank.

title: The Prophet Describes Zayd, Ja'far, and Abd Allah ibn Rawaha Facing Satan's Temptation Before Martyrdom
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) described how Satan tried to make each of the three commanders love life and fear death before they were martyred, and how each one rejected Satan's temptation and advanced until they were killed.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that 'Abd al-Jabbar b. 'Amra b. Ghaziyya related to me, from 'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr, from 'Amr b. Hazm, who said, 'When the army joined in battle at Mu'ta, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sat on the minbar and God revealed to him what lay between him and Syria so that he could observe their conflict. He said, "Zayd b. Haritha took the banner and Satan came to him and made life seem good to him and death hateful, making life on earth attractive to him. He (Zayd) said, "Now that the faith is firmly planted in the hearts of the believers, would you make life on earth seem good to me?" He then advanced until he was martyred." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then performed the prayer for him and said, "Ask forgiveness for him; he has entered paradise as a martyr."' Al-Waqidi stated that Muhammad b. Salih related to him, from 'Asim b. 'Amr b. Qatada, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'When Zayd was killed, Ja'far b. Abi Talib took the banner. Satan then came to him and made life seem good to him, death hateful, and he made life attractive to him. But Ja'far said, "Now, when faith has become firm in the hearts of the believers, would you have me wish for life?" He then advanced until he was martyred.
