The Messenger of God (SAAS) then spoke the prayer for him. He said, "Seek forgiveness for your brother; he is a martyr and has entered paradise. He is flying in heaven with wings of sapphire, going wherever he wishes there."' He went on, "'Abd Allah b. Rawaha then took the banner and was martyred. He then entered heaven mu'tarid, 'indirectly'. This upset the ansar and someone asked, 'Messenger of God, why "indirectly"?' He replied, 'When he suffered wounds, he drew back. But he criticized himself, regained his courage, was martyred and entered paradise.' 'His people were much relieved by this.'"
lessons: The temptation to cling to worldly life is a universal human struggle, and recognising it for what it is is the first step to overcoming it.
Self-criticism and returning to one's principles after a moment of weakness is itself a form of spiritual courage.
Moments of doubt followed by renewed resolve do not erase one's honour; God judges the whole of a person's striving.
practical_advice: When fear or worldly attachment pulls you back from a difficult but necessary course, ask yourself what truly matters to you at the deepest level, as each commander asked himself.
If you have retreated from something important out of fear, do not let shame paralyse you further; criticise yourself honestly, then re-engage as Abd Allah ibn Rawaha did.
Identify the specific temptations that make you cling to comfort and rehearse the values that counter them, so that when pressure comes you have a ready answer.

title: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib Flying with Wings of Sapphire in Paradise
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) informed the companions that Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, after his martyrdom at Mu'ta, was given wings of sapphire and flies in paradise wherever he wishes.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then spoke the prayer for him. He said, 'Seek forgiveness for your brother; he is a martyr and has entered paradise. He is flying in heaven with wings of sapphire, going wherever he wishes there.' Musa b. Uqba stated, 'And they claimed that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Ja'far passed me by amidst the angels, flying as they did, and he had two wings."'
lessons: The sacrifice of one's life for a noble cause is met with a dignity and freedom that surpasses anything the world can offer.
Grief at the loss of a beloved person can be tempered by the knowledge that their station with God is an exalted one.
The promises of God regarding the afterlife give meaning to suffering that would otherwise seem purposeless.
practical_advice: When mourning a loss, allow yourself to grieve while also holding space for the belief that what is unseen may far exceed what was taken.
Share stories of the honour given to those who sacrificed greatly, as these narratives comfort the living and keep the memory of the departed alive.
Reflect on what your own legacy of service and sacrifice might look like, and let that vision motivate present action.

title: Khalid ibn al-Walid's Tactical Reversal That Defeated the Byzantines
summary: After the three appointed commanders were martyred, Khalid ibn al-Walid spent the night regrouping, then at dawn reversed the formations of the Muslim army, causing the Byzantines to believe reinforcements had arrived and to retreat in defeat.
story: Al-Waqidi also stated, 'Al-'Igf b. Khalid related to me as follows, "Ibn Rawaha having been killed in the evening, Khalid b. al-Walid spent that night (without engaging the enemy). Next morning, he attacked, having placed his vanguard at the rear and exchanged the forces of the left and right flanks. The enemy were no longer familiar with their banners and disposition and, believing them to have been reinforced, were frightened and retreated in disarray. And so they suffered a defeat as no enemy had inflicted upon them before."' This accords with the account given by Musa b. Uqba, may God be pleased with him, in his work on the military campaigns. He stated, after recounting the 'umrat al-Hudaybiyya, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went to Medina and remained there for six months. He then despatched an army to Mu'ta, appointing Zayd b. Haritha as their commander. He ordered, "If he is struck down, then command is to go to Ja'far b. Abi Talib. If Ja'far is struck down, then 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha is to be their commander." They travelled on until they encountered Ibn Abi Sabra al-Ghassani at Mu'ta, where there was a large force of Christian Arabs and Byzantines, including men of Tanakh and Bahra'. Ibn Abi Sabra closed the gate of the fortress against the Muslims for three days. The armies then clashed on a red field, where the fighting was intense. Zayd b. Haritha took the banner and was killed; Ja'far then took it and he was killed. 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha took it next and was killed. Then the Muslims chose Khalid b. al-Walid al-Makhzumi to succeed those three commanders chosen by the Messenger of God (SAAS). God then defeated the enemy and gave victory to the Muslims.'
lessons: Creative thinking and adaptability in a crisis can turn near-defeat into victory.
Leadership requires the courage to make bold decisions when the situation is desperate and conventional approaches have failed.
Trusting in God while also using every available means of intellect and strategy reflects a complete and mature faith.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult situation, pause overnight if possible before making a major decision, as Khalid paused before his dawn attack.
In a crisis, consider rearranging the resources and approaches you already have before assuming you need new ones.
Reflect on a challenge you face and ask whether a simple reordering of your existing strengths might change how others perceive and respond to you.

title: The Prophet Sees the Battlefield of Mu'ta Revealed to Him from the Minbar
summary: While sitting on the minbar in Medina, the Prophet (SAAS) was shown the battlefield of Mu'ta by God, allowing him to witness the events in Syria in real time and relay them to the companions.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that 'Abd al-Jabbar b. 'Amra b. Ghaziyya related to me, from 'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr, from 'Amr b. Hazm, who said, 'When the army joined in battle at Mu'ta, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sat on the minbar and God revealed to him what lay between him and Syria so that he could observe their conflict.' Musa b. Uqba stated: 'They also claim — but God knows best — that Ya'la b. Umayya came with news of the men at Mu'ta to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who asked, "If you wish, you can tell me or, if you prefer, I will tell you." "You tell me, Messenger of God," he asked. So the Messenger of God (SAAS) told them all the news of them and described it all to them. He (Ya'la) then commented, "By Him who sent you with the truth, you have not left out a word — what happened to them is exactly as you stated!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, "God raised the earth up before me so that I could see their battlefield."'
lessons: God can grant those close to Him knowledge and awareness that transcends ordinary human perception.
A leader who shares difficult truths with compassion and clarity helps the community process grief with dignity.
Bearing witness to the sacrifice of others, even from afar, is a form of honouring their struggle.
practical_advice: When you cannot be present with someone going through a trial, find meaningful ways to acknowledge their suffering and affirm that you see and value what they are enduring.
Trust that God is aware of every detail of your struggle even when no human witness is present.
Be willing to be the bearer of difficult news to your community when it is necessary, and do so with the care and honesty the Prophet modelled.

title: The Returning Soldiers Called Runaways and the Prophet's Defence of Them
summary: When survivors of the Battle of Mu'ta returned to Medina, some people threw dirt at them and called them runaways who had fled from God's path, but the Prophet (SAAS) defended them, saying they were men who would fight again.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'Muhammad b. Ja'far related to me, from 'Urwa, who said, "When those who had fought at Mu'ta approached, the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims met them. The boys rushed out to meet them while the Messenger of God (SAAS) went out on his mount with the other people. He said, 'Take the boys away and give me Ja'far's son.' 'Abd Allah was brought to him and he picked him up and carried him. The people then began tossing dirt at the army, saying, 'You runaways! You fled from God's path!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'They are not runaways but men who will fight again, if God, Almighty and Glorious is He, wills it.'"'
lessons: Public shaming of those who have already suffered greatly compounds their wound and reflects a failure of empathy in the community.
A true leader protects the dignity of those under his care, especially when others are quick to condemn.
The judgement of the crowd is often premature and incomplete; full understanding of a person's actions requires knowing the full context.
practical_advice: Before criticising someone who has withdrawn from a difficult situation, make the effort to understand what they actually faced.
If you have been publicly shamed for a failure, seek out the voice of someone who knows your full story and can speak truthfully on your behalf.
When you lead others, make it a practice to publicly defend those in your care from unjust criticism, as the Prophet did for the returning soldiers.

title: Abd Allah ibn Umar and the Soldiers Who Fled: Returning to the Prophet for Forgiveness
summary: Abd Allah ibn Umar and other soldiers who had fled during a military expedition debated whether to return to Medina, fearing they might be killed for their flight, but ultimately presented themselves before the Prophet, who forgave them and called them men who would fight again.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Hasan related to him, quoting Zuhayr, quoting Yazid b. Abi Ziyad, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abi Layla, from 'Abd Allah b. Umar, who said, 'I took part in one of the expeditions of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and some of the men fled, myself among them. We wondered what we should do, having fled and caused anger. We thought we might be killed if we returned to Medina. Then we decided we should present ourselves before the Messenger of God (SAAS), in which case we would either be forgiven or we would leave. We went to him before the pre-dawn prayer. He came out and asked, "Who are you?" "We are the runaways," we replied. "No", he replied, "you're the ones who will fight again. I'm one of you, and I'm one of the believers." We then went forward and kissed his hand.'
lessons: Returning to face accountability, even when the outcome is uncertain, is an act of courage and integrity.
True leadership meets confession with mercy rather than punishment, transforming shame into renewed purpose.
Guilt, when it leads to honest reckoning rather than paralysis, can become the first step toward restoration.
practical_advice: When you have failed or let others down, resist the urge to hide or disappear; presenting yourself honestly and seeking reconciliation is the path to healing.
If you are in a position of authority, practise responding to the admissions of failure from those under you with the mercy and redirection the Prophet showed.
Name your failure plainly to yourself and to those you have wronged, as the soldiers named themselves 'the runaways', and then allow the possibility of a new identity to take hold.

title: The Runaways Who Will Fight Again - Soldiers Return to the Prophet After Battle of Mu'ta
summary: Muslim soldiers who fled during the Battle of Mu'ta returned to the Prophet Muhammad in shame, calling themselves 'runaways,' but he reassured them they were not runaways but those who would fight again, and declared himself to be of their party.
story: case we would either he forgiven or we would leave. We went to h i before the pre-dawn prayer. He came out and asked, 'Who are you?' 'We are the runaways,' we replied. 'No', he replied, 'you're the ones who will fight again. I'm one of you, and I'm one of the believers.' We then went forward and kissed his hand. He then went on to relate it from Ghundar, from Shu'ba, from Yazid b. Aha Ziysd, from Ibn Aba Layla, from Ibn Wmar, who said, 'We were on a military expedition, but fled. We wondered whether we should go overseas. Then we went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, "Messenger of God, we are the runaways." He replied, "No; you're the ones who will fight again."' Aba Da'nd related it, as did al-Timidhi and Ibn Maja, from a hadith of Yazid b. Aba Ziyad. Al-Timidhi stated, 'It is hasan, "good"; we know it only from his hadith.' Ahmad stated, 'Ishaq b. Ciss and Aswad b. Amir related to us as follows, "Shuravk related to us, from Yazid b. Aba Ziyad, from Abd al-Rahman b. Abi Layla, from Ibn Umar, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us out on a military expedition. When we encountered the enemy we fled in the first clash and went back to Medina by night, as a group, and went into hiding. Then we wondered if we should go to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and apologize to him." "We did go to him, and when he met us we told him, "We are the runaways, Messenger of God." He replied, "No; you are those who will fight again, and I'm of your party.""' Al-Aswad said (that the words used were) 'I am of the party of every Muslim.'
lessons: Shame and guilt after failure do not have to be permanent; seeking forgiveness and returning with humility is the path forward.
True leadership responds to failure with compassion and re-empowerment rather than condemnation.
One's identity is not permanently defined by their worst moment; redemption and renewal are always possible.
practical_advice: When overwhelmed by guilt after a mistake, resist the urge to hide or disappear; approach those you have wronged or your support system with honesty.
Replace self-labeling of failure ('I am a runaway', 'I am a coward') with a forward-looking identity ('I am someone who will try again').
Seek out a mentor or trusted person after a setback; their reassurance and perspective can restore your sense of purpose and belonging.

title: Salama b. Hisham Confined to His Home After Being Shamed as a Runaway
summary: Salama b. Hisham, a companion who had been present at the Battle of Mu'ta, was publicly shamed as a 'runaway from God's path' and confined himself to his home, unable to attend prayers with the Prophet and the Muslim community.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'Abd Allah b. Aba Bakr b. Amr b. Hazm related to me, from Amir b. Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, that Umm Salama, the wife of the Messenger of God (SAAS), asked the wife of Salama b. Hisham b. al-Mughira, "Why don't I see Salama attend the prayer with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims?" "She replied, "He can't go outside. Whenever he does, people cry out, "Runaway! You ran away from God's path!" So he sits at home and never goes out; he was present at the battle of Mu'ta."'
lessons: Public shame and social rejection can be as crippling as physical injury; it is important to distinguish between guilt that leads to growth and shame that leads to isolation.
Community members have a responsibility to show mercy and avoid shaming those who have already suffered and failed; cruelty in the name of moral judgment causes lasting harm.
Withdrawal from community and worship due to fear of judgment can deepen suffering; finding safe spaces to reconnect spiritually is vital.
practical_advice: If you find yourself withdrawing from community, prayer, or daily life due to fear of judgment, identify at least one safe person or space where you can reconnect without shame.
Speak openly with a trusted friend or confidant about the social pressure you are facing, as Umm Salama's concern for Salama shows that compassionate people notice and care.
Challenge the narrative that one failure defines your worth; remind yourself that being present and trying again is itself a form of courage.

title: The Yemeni Auxiliary's Bravery and the Dispute Over Spoils at Mu'ta
summary: A poorly equipped Yemeni auxiliary fighter showed exceptional bravery at the Battle of Mu'ta by killing a well-armed Byzantine officer, but Khalid b. al-Walid seized the man's rightful spoils, leading to a dispute that the Prophet resolved by rebuking the complainant for undermining his commander's authority.
story: 'Awf b. Malik al-Ashja'i said, 'I was among those who accompanied Zayd b. Haritha and the other Muslims to the battle of Mu'ta. I went with a madadi, an "auxiliary", from Yemen whose only equipment was his sword. One of the Muslims slaughtered a camel and the auxiliary asked him for a piece of its leather. He gave it to him and the madadi used it as a shield. We advanced and encountered a force of the Byzantines, among whom was a man riding a sorrel equipped with a gilded saddle and a gilded sword. The Roman began attacking the Muslims, and the auxiliary crouched down behind a rock. When the Roman went by, the madadi hamstrung his horse and when its rider fell, he jumped on him and killed him. And so he seized his horse and weapon. When God gave victory to the Muslims, Khalid b. al-Walid sent to him and took the booty.' Awf went on, 'So I went to Khalid and said, "Khalid, haven't you heard that the Messenger of God (SAAS) has decreed that the spoils (from a dead enemy) belong to the person who killed him?" "Yes", he replied, "but I thought this excessive." I told him, "If you don't give it back to him, then I will report you to the Messenger of God (SAAS)." But he refused to return it.' Awf continued, 'When we had a meeting with the Messenger of God (SAAS), I related to him the story of the madadi and what Khalid had done. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, "Khalid, return to him what you took." I then said, "So there, Khalid! Didn't I tell you I would!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, "What's all this?" So I related it to him and he got angry. He said, "Khalid, don't give it back to him! Would you disobey one of my commanders? You expect only what in their commands is trouble-free, while assigning them the troublesome!"'
lessons: Justice must be balanced with institutional order; correcting a wrong through proper channels is noble, but undermining authority with a spirit of self-righteousness can itself become a fault.
Bravery and contribution are not measured by one's resources or equipment; the Yemeni auxiliary with only a sword and a leather shield exemplified extraordinary courage.
Leaders bear responsibility not to exploit those under their command, and those who witness injustice should speak up through proper means while remaining humble.
practical_advice: When you witness an injustice, raise the concern through appropriate channels rather than public confrontation, and check your own motives to ensure you are acting for the right reasons.
Recognize that those with fewer resources or lower status often display remarkable strength; do not underestimate or overlook their contributions.

After advocating for a just cause, accept the outcome gracefully even if the resolution is unexpected; the Prophet's response shows that righteousness in method matters as much as righteousness in aim.

title: The Prophet Weeps Upon Announcing the Death of Ja'far at Mu'ta
summary: The Prophet Muhammad visited Ja'far's wife Asma' bint Umays after the Battle of Mu'ta to personally announce the death of her husband, wept when he embraced her children, and then instructed the community to prepare food for the grieving family.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr related to me, from Umm Isa al-Khuzaliyya, from Umm Ja'far, daughter of Muhammad b. Ja'far b. Abi Talib, from their grandmother Asma', daughter of Umays, who said, "When Ja'far and his men had suffered casualties, the Messenger of God (SAAS) came in to see me just after I had tanned 40 manna weight of skins, kneaded my dough and washed, oiled and cleaned off my children. He said to me, "Bring Ja'far's sons to me." I brought them to him, he smelt them and his eyes welled with tears. I asked, "Messenger of God, by my father and my mother, do tell me what makes you cry! Do you have news of Ja'far and his men?" "Yes", he replied, "they have been struck down today." I arose screaming, and the women gathered around me. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went outside to his people and said, "Don't neglect Ja'far's family; make food for them. They are preoccupied by the fate of their master.""' Imam Ahmad stated that Sufyan related to him, quoting Ja'far b. Khalid from his father, from Abd Allah b. Ja'far, who said, "When the news came of the death of Ja'far, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Prepare food for Ja'far's family. A matter has come to them that preoccupies them.'" Or the words were, "something that occupies them has come". This is similarly related by Abi Da'ud, al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Maja from a hadith of Sufyan b. Uyayna, from Ja'far b. Khalid b. Sitrat al-Makhzumi al-Makki, from his father, from Abd Allah b. Ja'far. Al-Tirmidhi categorized this hadith as hasan, "good".
lessons: Grief is a shared human experience that even the greatest of people are not exempt from; the Prophet's tears in the presence of Ja'far's children validate the naturalness of mourning.

In times of bereavement, the most powerful support a community can offer is practical care; the Prophet's instruction to prepare food for the grieving family is a timeless model of compassionate community response.
Leadership includes being present with people in their pain; the Prophet did not send a messenger but went himself to deliver difficult news and offer comfort.
practical_advice: When someone you know is grieving, show up in person if possible and offer concrete, practical help such as bringing food, rather than only expressing condolences from a distance.
Allow yourself to grieve openly when loss comes; suppressing tears and sorrow in the name of strength can delay healing.
If you are leading a group or community, model emotional presence by personally acknowledging the losses of those in your care and mobilizing collective support around them.

title: 'A'isha Witnesses the Prophet's Visible Sadness Upon the Death of Ja'far
summary: The Prophet's wife 'A'isha observed visible sadness on the Prophet's face when news of Ja'far's death arrived, and described how he dealt with the distress of mourning women by repeatedly sending a companion to calm them.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq went on, 'Abd al-Rahman b. al-Qasim related to me, from his father, from A'isha, wife of the Prophet (SAAS), who said, "When news of the death of Ja'far arrived, we could see the sadness in the face of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." She commented, "A man went in to him and said, "Messenger of God, the women are distressing and annoying us." He told him, "Go back in to them and quieten them down."" She said, "He left, then he came back and said the same to him."'
lessons: Even the greatest of leaders and human beings carry grief visibly; acknowledging sadness rather than concealing it is a sign of emotional authenticity, not weakness.
Managing communal grief requires patience and repeated, gentle redirection rather than harsh suppression of emotion.
Observing and naming another person's grief, as 'A'isha did, is itself an act of emotional intelligence and empathy that honors the experience of loss.
practical_advice: When you are grieving, give yourself permission to show it; you do not need to perform strength for those around you.
If you are supporting a grieving community or household, expect that emotions may need to be tended to repeatedly and gently rather than resolved in a single interaction.
Pay attention to the emotional state of those you love; noticing and naming their grief, as 'A'isha did with the Prophet, creates space for honest emotional expression and connection.

title: The Prophet's Grief Upon Receiving News of Ja'far's Death
summary: When news arrived of the death of Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and his companions at the Battle of Mu'tah, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) was visibly overcome with sorrow, which was witnessed by 'A'isha through a chink in the door.
story: When news of the death of Ja'far arrived, we could see the sadness in the face of the Messenger of God (SAAS). A man went in to him and said, 'Messenger of God, the women are distressing and annoying us.' He told him, 'Go back in to them and quieten them down.' He left, then he came back and said the same to him. And (as the saying goes) 'Persistence often harms', that is, those who engage in it. He then told the man, 'Go off and quieten them; and if they keep it up, then toss dirt in their mouths!' I said to myself, 'May God remove you, fellow! You neither relieved yourself, nor will you be able to obey the Messenger of God!' I well knew he could not toss dirt in their mouths.
lessons: Even the greatest among people experience profound grief when losing loved ones — grief is a natural and human response, not a sign of weakness.
The Prophet's visible sorrow teaches us that expressing sadness is not contrary to faith or strength of character.
Patience in the face of loss does not mean suppressing all emotion; it means enduring the pain while maintaining trust in God's wisdom.
practical_advice: Allow yourself to grieve when you lose someone dear — suppressing grief entirely is neither healthy nor required by faith.
When you are overwhelmed by sorrow, seek a quiet moment of reflection rather than forcing yourself to appear unaffected.
If others around you are grieving, be gentle and patient with them rather than expecting them to recover immediately.

title: The Prophet Announces the Martyrs of Mu'tah and Praises Khalid ibn al-Walid
summary: After the Battle of Mu'tah, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) went out to the people, praised God, and announced the sequential deaths of Zayd, Ja'far, and 'Abd Allah ibn Rawaha, before noting that Khalid ibn al-Walid — 'one of God's swords' — took the banner and was granted victory.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out an army under the command of Zayd b. Haritha, saying, 'If Zayd should be killed or martyred, then your leader will be Ja'far; if he should be killed or martyred, then your leader will be 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha.' When they clashed with the enemy, Zayd took the banner and fought until he was killed. Then Ja'far took the banner and fought until he was killed. 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha then took the banner and fought until he was killed. Khalid b. al-Walid then took the banner and God gave him victory. When their news reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), he went out to the people and gave thanks and praise to God. He then said, 'Your brothers have met the enemy. Zayd took the banner and fought until he was killed or martyred. After him, Ja'far b. Abi Talib took the banner and fought until he was killed or martyred. Then 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha took the banner and fought until he was killed or martyred. Then one of God's swords, Khalid b. al-Walid, took the banner, and God gave him victory.'
lessons: Leadership in times of crisis requires courage and the willingness to step forward even when those before you have fallen.
Recognizing and praising the contributions of those who sacrifice for a noble cause is an act of justice and gratitude.
Divine assistance can come through unexpected people — Khalid ibn al-Walid had only recently embraced Islam, yet he was called 'one of God's swords'.
practical_advice: When facing a chain of setbacks, do not lose heart — each effort, even if it ends in apparent failure, contributes to an ultimate breakthrough.
Acknowledge and celebrate the contributions of others openly, as the Prophet did by naming each martyr and their valor.
Trust that God can bring victory and relief through means and people you may not have anticipated.

title: The Prophet Visits Ja'far's Family and Comforts His Orphaned Children
summary: Three days after the death of Ja'far, the Prophet (SAAS) visited his family, had the children's heads shaved, prayed for them, and personally assured their mother that he would be their protector in this world and the next.
story: He then gave Ja'far's family a respite of three days before going to them. Then he went to them and said, 'Do not grieve for my brother after today. Summon to me all my brother's family.' We were then brought there as if we were a flock of birds. He said, 'Summon the barber to me.' The barber was brought and he shaved our heads. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Muhammad is just like our uncle Abu Talib. As for 'Abd Allah, he is just like myself in character and appearance.' He then took my hand and raised it, saying, 'O God, let Ja'far live on in his family; and bless 'Abd Allah in his affairs.' He said this three times. Then our mother came and spoke of those of us who were orphaned and began arousing his sympathy. Then he said, 'How can you worry about your family when I am their protector in this world and the next?'
lessons: The Prophet's personal attention to the orphaned children of Ja'far demonstrates that caring for the vulnerable is among the highest expressions of compassion.
A period of mourning followed by a deliberate gesture of comfort and renewal can help those grieving begin to move forward.
Prayers made sincerely for others — especially children who have lost a parent — carry immense spiritual weight and offer real solace.
practical_advice: When someone loses a loved one, give them space to grieve initially, then make a deliberate, personal effort to visit and offer comfort.
If you know children who have lost a parent, make special efforts to affirm their worth, pray for them, and let them know they are not alone.
When you feel overwhelmed by the responsibility of caring for dependents, remind yourself that God is the ultimate protector and provider.

title: Asma' bint 'Umays Mourns Ja'far and Later Remarries
summary: Asma' bint 'Umays eulogized her martyred husband Ja'far with a heartfelt poem of grief, observed her waiting period, and was later married to Abu Bakr, reflecting both the depth of her sorrow and the continuation of life after loss.
story: I note that Asma', daughter of 'Umays, eulogized her husband in an ode in which she stated, 'I swear, my soul will never cease to be sad over you, and my skin will always wear dust. No one ever saw the like of him, a hero who persisted so valiantly in the fray.' Eventually her 'idda, waiting period, ended and Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, became engaged to her. He then married her and gave a feast to which people came. 'Ali b. Abi Talib was one of those attending and when the others had gone he asked permission of Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him, to speak with Asma' through the curtain. He gave him leave to do so. When 'Ali approached the curtain, the scent of her perfume reached him and he asked her — in general terms — who was the person speaking in the above verse, 'I swear, my soul will never cease to be sad over you, and my skin will always wear dust.' She replied, 'Away with you, Abu al-Hasan, you're always joking!' She bore Muhammad to Abu Bakr, giving birth at a tree between Mecca and Medina, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was on his way to the hijjat al-wada', the 'farewell pilgrimage'. He told her to wash herself and recite prayers. When later Abu Bakr died, 'Ali b. Abi Talib married her and she bore him children.
lessons: Grief expressed through poetry or other creative means is a healthy and honored way of processing the pain of loss.
Life continues after devastating loss — Asma's story shows that honoring a lost loved one does not prevent one from embracing new chapters.
The observance of the waiting period ('idda) provides a structured, spiritually grounded space for a widow to heal before moving forward.
practical_advice: Consider expressing your grief creatively — through writing, poetry, or art — as a healthy outlet for the depth of your loss.
Allow yourself to honor the memory of those you have lost while also remaining open to the healing and new beginnings that life may bring.
Trust the wisdom in the structured practices of your faith during periods of grief; they exist to protect your emotional and spiritual wellbeing.

title: The Army Returns from Mu'tah — The Prophet Defends Them Against Accusations of Cowardice
summary: When the surviving Muslim army returned from Mu'tah, the people of Madinah threw dirt at them and called them runaways, but the Prophet (SAAS) defended them publicly, declaring they would fight again.
story: When they drew near Medina, the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the people came out to meet them. The boys were racing around, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) came forward on a mule. He said, 'Take away the children; carry them off and bring me Ja'far's son.' 'Abd Allah, Ja'far's son was brought and he (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) carried him in his own hands. The crowd began tossing dirt at the army, shouting, 'You runaways! You ran away from God's path!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) was calling out, 'They're not runaways! They'll fight again, if God wills it!'
lessons: A true leader defends those under his care from unjust criticism, especially when they have sacrificed greatly.
Strategic retreat is not cowardice — wisdom in battle sometimes requires withdrawing to fight another day.
The judgment of crowds is often hasty and uninformed; seeking the truth before condemning others is an obligation of fairness.
practical_advice: When you are unfairly criticized or called a failure after a genuine effort, remember that those with true understanding will defend and vindicate you.
Do not let the harsh words of others define your self-worth, especially when you know you acted with sincerity and courage.
If you see others being unjustly shamed for their struggles or setbacks, speak up in their defense as the Prophet did.

title: Zayd b. Haritha: His Origins, Enslavement, and Life with the Prophet
summary: Zayd b. Haritha was taken from his mother during a raid and sold into slavery, eventually coming into the possession of Khadija who gave him to the Prophet. When his father found him, Zayd chose to remain with the Prophet, who freed him and raised him.
story: The genealogy of Zayd b. Haitha is as follows: son of Shurail b. Ka'b b. 'Abd al-'Uzza b. Imr al-Qays b. 'Amir b. al-Nu'man b. 'Amir b. 'Abd Wadd b. 'Awf b. Kinwa b. Bakr b. 'Awf b. Wdhra b. Zayd al-Lat b. Rufayda b. Thawr b. Kalb b. Barra b. Tha'Lab b. Haww b. 'Imm b. al-Qaf b. Quda'a-Kalbi al-Quda'i. He was the freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS). This came about because Zayd's mother went to visit her family and they were attacked by horsemen who took him away. Hakim b. Hizam purchased him for his aunt Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid; it is also said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) purchased him for her. She in turn presented him to the Prophet (SAAS), before he received the Prophethood. His father discovered his whereabouts, but he chose to stay with the Messenger of God (SAAS), who manumitted him and brought him up. He was known as Zayd b. Muhammad; the Messenger of God (SAAS) loved him greatly. He was the first of the freed-men to accept Islam.
lessons: Difficult circumstances beyond our control, like Zayd's enslavement, can become the path to unexpected blessings and elevated status.
True belonging and love can transcend blood ties and social status, as Zayd chose loyalty to the Prophet over reunion with his family.
God's plan often works through seemingly unfortunate events to place people exactly where they need to be.
practical_advice: When facing circumstances that feel unfair or beyond your control, reflect on how apparent setbacks in your life may have opened doors you could not have opened yourself.
Consider the relationships in your life that feel like family even without blood ties — nurture those bonds of chosen loyalty.
When feeling lost or displaced, focus on where you feel genuine love and spiritual nourishment rather than solely on where you came from.

title: Quranic Verses Revealed About Zayd b. Haritha
summary: God revealed verses in the Quran naming Zayd b. Haritha directly, addressing the pre-Islamic practice of adoption and the circumstances of his marriage and divorce from Zaynab bint Jahsh.
story: Verses of the Quran were revealed about him. These include: 'And He has not made to be your sons those you merely claim to be your sons' (surat al-Ahzab; XXXIII, v.4). 'Affirm their relationship to their fathers; that is more just in God's view' (Ibidem; XXXIII, v.5). 'Muhammad is not the father of any of your men' (Ibidem; XXXIII, v.40). 'And when you said to him whom both God and you had favoured, "Retain your wife and fear God," you concealed in yourself what God was about to reveal. And you feared men, but God has greater right to be feared. (But) when Zayd had finished his union with her, We married her to you' (Ibidem; XXXIII, v.37). There is a consensus that these verses were revealed about him. The words an'ama Allaha 'alayhi 'him God had favoured' refer to his accepting Islam. And the words, an'amta 'alayhi, 'you had favoured' refers to his having manumitted him. We have discussed these verses in our Tafsir (Exegesis). The point is that God Almighty named in the Quran no other Companion (of the Prophet (SAAS)) except him.
lessons: Being the subject of divine revelation is the highest honor a human being can receive, reflecting that God notices and cares about the specific circumstances of individuals.
Social norms and human fears must yield to divine guidance, even when what God commands challenges prevailing customs.
The distinction between spiritual brotherhood and biological fatherhood carries profound implications for how we understand family and obligation.
practical_advice: When social pressure makes you feel you must conform to customs that conflict with your conscience or your faith, reflect on how the Quran itself broke longstanding customs to establish justice.
If you feel anxiety about what others will think of decisions guided by your values, remember that fearing God's judgment is more worthy than fearing human disapproval.
Study the context of Quranic verses to understand how divine guidance addresses real human situations — this deepens your connection to the text.

title: The Prophet's Deep Love for Zayd and His Honor Above All Companions
summary: The Prophet Muhammad showed extraordinary love and honor toward Zayd b. Haritha, marrying him to prominent women, establishing brotherhood between him and Hamza, and repeatedly appointing him as commander of expeditions.
story: God Almighty named in the Quran no other Companion (of the Prophet (SAAS)) except him. He led him to Islam, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) freed him and married him to his freed-woman Umm Ayman, whose name was Baraka, and she bore him Usama, son of Zayd. He used to be known as al-hibb ibn al-hibb, 'the dearest of all'. He then married him to his maternal aunt's daughter, Zaynab, daughter of Jahsh. He established him as a brother to his uncle Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib and gave him precedence as commander over his nephew Ja'far b. Abi Talib at the battle of Mu'ta, as we related above. Imam Ahmad and the Imam and hafiz Abu Bakr b. Abi Shayba — whose words these are — stated, 'Muhammad b. 'Ubayd related to us, from Wa'il b. Da'ud, "I heard al-Bahi relate that 'A'isha used to say, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed Zayd as commander of every expedition, without exception, to which he dispatched him; and if he were still alive, he would have left him in command.""'
lessons: True honor in the sight of God and His Prophet is not determined by lineage or worldly status but by sincere faith and trustworthy character.
When those in positions of authority recognize and consistently elevate people of proven merit, it builds a culture of trust and excellence.
The love the Prophet showed for Zayd teaches us that emotional bonds formed through shared faith and loyalty are among the most sacred relationships.
practical_advice: Reflect on the people in your community who are overlooked due to social status or background — consider how you can honor their contributions more openly.
If you feel unrecognized for your efforts, take comfort in the example of Zayd, whose worth was so evident that the Prophet never once passed him over for leadership.
Cultivate loyalty and trustworthiness in your relationships, as these are the qualities that earn enduring love and respect.

title: The Prophet Defends Usama b. Zayd's Appointment as Commander
summary: When some companions complained about Usama b. Zayd being appointed as military commander, the Prophet rebuked them by defending both Usama and his father Zayd, declaring his deep love for them both.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Sulayman related to him, quoting Isma'il, quoting Ibn Dinar, from Ibn 'Umar, may God be pleased with him, that the latter sent out an expedition in command of which he placed Usama b. Zayd, but some people complained about his appointment. The Messenger of God (SAAS) arose and said, 'You complain about his being made commander; you used to complain earlier about his father being made commander, even though, by God, he was certainly qualified to be commander, and moreover was extremely dear to me. Well, this man too is extremely dear to me.' This hadith is included in the sahih collections, from Qutayba, from Isma'il — he being the son of Ja'far b. Abi Kathir al-Madani — from 'Abd Allah b. Dinar, from Ibn 'Umar.
lessons: Those who are beloved to God and His Prophet may still face criticism and social rejection, but divine endorsement is worth more than human approval.
Leaders must be willing to publicly stand by their decisions and the people they trust, even in the face of complaint and pressure.
Prejudice against people due to their origins or family background is a recurring human failing that must be actively resisted.
practical_advice: When you face criticism for a decision you made with sincere judgment, stand by it calmly and explain your reasoning rather than capitulating to social pressure.
If you feel rejected or looked down upon by others, remember that the Prophet's explicit love and endorsement of both Zayd and Usama was his answer to all such complaints.
Challenge yourself to examine whether your own judgments about who deserves leadership or recognition are influenced by bias rather than genuine assessment of character and capability.

title: The Prophet's Grief Upon Seeing Usama After Zayd's Death
summary: After Zayd b. Haritha was killed at Mu'ta, when his son Usama was brought before the Prophet, the Prophet's eyes welled with tears and he could not face him, overcome with grief at the reminder of his beloved companion.
story: The hafiz Abu Bakr al-Bazzar stated that 'Umar b. Isma'il related to him, from Mujalid, from al-Sha'bi, from Masruq, from 'A'isha, who said, 'When Zayd b. Haritha was killed and Usama, Zayd's son, was brought to him and placed before him, the eyes of the Messenger of God (SAAS) welled with tears and he put off the meeting. Next day, when Usama was brought back before him, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "You make me feel today just as you did yesterday."'
lessons: Grief is a natural and even prophetic response to losing those we love; the Prophet's tears show that emotional pain at loss is not a sign of weakness.
The face of a beloved person's child can reopen the wound of grief in profound ways, reminding us how deeply love connects us across generations.
Even the greatest of men are not immune to sorrow; acknowledging grief honestly is part of being fully human.
practical_advice: Allow yourself to grieve fully when you lose someone dear — suppressing grief does not honor the love you shared.
If certain people, places, or memories unexpectedly trigger overwhelming sadness, recognize this as a reflection of the depth of your love rather than a sign of instability.
Be gentle with others who are grieving; sometimes simply being present with them, even in silence, is the most compassionate thing you can do.

title: The Prophet Announces the Deaths of the Three Commanders from the Minbar
summary: Standing on the minbar, the Prophet announced the sequential deaths of Zayd, Ja'far, and Ibn Rawaha at the Battle of Mu'ta, his eyes welling with tears, and bore witness that they were martyrs assured of paradise.
story: It has been related above how it is recorded in both sahih collections that when, upon the minbar, the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke of their deaths, he said, 'Zayd took the banner and was struck down; then Ja'far took it and was struck down. Then 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha took it and was struck down. Then one of God's swords took it and God gave him victory.' And it stated, 'His eyes welled with tears.' Then he said, 'They would not be pleased with us.' In another hadith it is said that he bore witness that they had been martyred and were assured of paradise.
lessons: The Prophet's public acknowledgment of the fallen commanders affirms that honoring those who sacrifice for a righteous cause is a communal duty.
Tears in grief do not contradict gratitude and faith; the Prophet wept and simultaneously testified to their paradise, holding both sorrow and hope together.
Those who give everything in service of a just cause are remembered and honored not just by people but by God Himself.
practical_advice: When you lose someone important, find a way to publicly acknowledge their contribution — speaking their name and their deeds keeps their memory alive and honors their sacrifice.
Practice holding grief and gratitude simultaneously: you can mourn a loss deeply while also being thankful for what that person gave to your life.
When facing a seemingly hopeless situation, remember that 'one of God's swords' appeared at Mu'ta when three commanders had fallen — divine support can come when human effort has reached its limit.

title: Hassan b. Thabit's Elegy for Zayd and Ibn Rawaha
summary: The poet Hassan b. Thabit composed a moving elegy mourning the deaths of Zayd b. Haritha and Abd Allah b. Rawaha at Mu'ta, lamenting their nobility and the sorrow left behind.
story: Hassan b. Thabit spoke the following verses eulogizing Zayd b. Haritha and Ibn Rawaha, 'O eyes, weep generously, to your last tear, and recall, in your ease, those in the grave. Remember Mu'ta and what happened there, that day they went off to defeat in battle. When they left, abandoning Zayd there, in a resting place fit for the destitute or powerless. That dear friend of the very best of mankind, that lord of men, beloved by all. That Ahmad who knows no equal, that man who has both my pleasure and my pain. The status Zayd had among us was not that of a man who tells lies and is conceited. And weep tears generously, eyes, for the Khazraji ('Abd Allah b. Rawaha); a lord who was not niggardly there. We have suffered much by their deaths; we pass our night in sorrow, not pleasure.'
lessons: Poetry and art are powerful means of processing communal grief, giving form and language to sorrow that might otherwise remain wordless.
Honoring the dead through remembrance is an act of love and a source of healing for those left behind.
The pain of losing noble, generous people is intensely felt precisely because their qualities made the world a better place — such loss is a measure of their worth.
practical_advice: Consider writing, poetry, or other creative expression as a tool for processing grief — giving form to your feelings can bring relief and clarity.
When mourning, allow yourself to speak the names and recall the specific qualities of those you have lost rather than keeping grief vague and unspoken.
Surround yourself with others who share your loss; collective mourning — as the Muslim community mourned at Mu'ta — can transform isolating grief into shared strength.

title: Ja'far b. Abi Talib: His Early Islam, Emigration to Abyssinia, and Return
summary: Ja'far b. Abi Talib was among the earliest Muslims, emigrated to Abyssinia where he distinguished himself with eloquent responses, and returned at the time of the conquest of Khaybar to an emotional reunion with the Prophet.
story: The genealogy of Ja'far b. Abi Talib is as follows. He was the son of the paternal uncle of the Messenger of God (SAAS), being older than his brother 'Ali by ten years. (Another brother) 'Aqil was also ten years older than Ja'far; another, Talib, was ten years older than 'Aqil. Ja'far was early in accepting Islam. He emigrated to Abyssinia and performed many famous and praiseworthy actions and functions there, and gave fine responses to questions. We have dealt with all these previously, in the section on the emigration to Abyssinia; and to God be all praise. At the battle of Khaybar he arrived back to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who said to him then, 'I don't know what pleases me more — Ja'far's arrival or the conquest of Khaybar!' He went up to Ja'far, embraced him and kissed him on the forehead.
lessons: When you are far from your community for the sake of God, your absence is noticed and your return is a source of joy as great as any worldly victory.
Standing firm in your faith in a foreign land, as Ja'far did in Abyssinia, produces qualities of eloquence and courage that benefit the entire community upon your return.
The Prophet's words about Ja'far's return teach us that the reunion of believers can match the joy of great achievements.
practical_advice: If you are living far from your faith community or family, remember that your perseverance in difficult circumstances is building qualities that will serve others when you return.
Make a point of warmly welcoming back those who have been away — a genuine, joyful reception, as the Prophet gave Ja'far, can restore a returning person's sense of belonging.
When two good things happen at once, practice recognizing and verbalizing your gratitude for both rather than taking either for granted.

title: The Prophet Tells Ja'far He Resembles Him in Appearance and Character
summary: On the day of the compensatory Umrah, the Prophet told Ja'far b. Abi Talib that he resembled him in both appearance and character, causing Ja'far both embarrassment and delight.
story: And the day they went forth to the 'umrat al-qadiyya, he said to him, 'You, Ja'far, resemble me in both appearance and character.' It is said that he was embarrassed and delighted to hear this, as was related in the correct sequence above.
lessons: Being told you resemble someone you deeply admire is one of the most moving compliments a person can receive.
The Prophet's words to Ja'far show his attentiveness and affection for those close to him — he noticed and named Ja'far's qualities specifically.
Moments of personal recognition from a trusted mentor or leader can shape a person's sense of identity and inspire them to live up to those qualities.
practical_advice: Take time to offer sincere, specific compliments to people you admire — naming what you genuinely see in someone has a lasting impact.
When you feel embarrassed by a compliment, sit with both the embarrassment and the delight rather than deflecting; receiving recognition gracefully is itself a form of humility.
Identify someone in your life whose character you admire and whose example you wish to emulate, and let that admiration actively shape your choices.

title: Ja'far b. Abi Talib's Heroic Death at Mu'ta and His Status as Martyr
summary: At the Battle of Mu'ta, after Zayd fell, Ja'far took the banner and fought until both his arms were severed. He held the banner to his body until he was killed, and 90 wounds were found on him, all on the front of his body.
story: When he dispatched him to Mu'ta, he appointed him as deputy commander to Zayd b. Haritha. When the latter was killed, they discovered 90 wounds upon him, some from swords, others from spears and arrows. All of these were on his front, not his back. His right arm was severed, then his left and he held the banner. When he lost both, he held it to himself, until he was killed, still hugging it. It is said that a Roman split him into two with his sword. May God be pleased with Ja'far and curse his killer! The Messenger of God (SAAS) told of him that he was a martyr and one of those destined for heaven. There are ahadith in which he referred to him as having two wings. Al-Bukhari related, from Ibn 'Umar, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) greeted Ja'far's son 'Abd Allah, he would say, 'Peace be upon you, son of him with the two wings!' Some authorities quote this from 'Umar b. al-Khattab himself, but the authentic tradition comes from Ibn 'Umar. They say that it is as compensation for his two arms that God awarded him wings in paradise.
lessons: Ultimate courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to continue even when everything is being taken from you — Ja'far held the banner with his chest after losing both arms.
God compensates those who sacrifice their physical capacities in His cause in ways that transcend worldly imagination — Ja'far's severed arms became wings in paradise.
The 90 wounds all on his front are a testament to the principle that those who stand firm never turn their backs on what they believe in.
practical_advice: When you feel depleted and think you have nothing left to give, remember Ja'far, who continued to serve his cause even after losing both arms — there is often more capacity within us than we realize.
Reflect on a sacrifice you have made or are being asked to make: trust that God does not overlook what is given up sincerely in His path.
When grief or difficulty strips away things you relied upon, focus on what you are still holding — like Ja'far clutching the banner — and hold it close.

title: The Prophet's Vision of Ja'far Flying in Paradise
summary: The Prophet reported seeing Ja'far b. Abi Talib flying among the angels in paradise, and Ja'far was thereafter known as al-Tayyar, 'the flyer', with God having given him wings in paradise as compensation for his severed arms.
story: The hafiz Abu 'Isa al-Tirmidhi stated that 'Ali b. Hijr related to him, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far, from al-'Ala' b. 'Abd al-Rahman, from his father, who quoted Abu Hurayra as saying, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I saw Ja'far flying in paradise with the angels."' It is related above in a hadith that he was killed at the age of 33. Ibn al-Athir stated in al-Ghaba (The Forest), 'His age when he was killed was 41. Though some state otherwise.' I note that regarding his being said to have been 10 years senior to 'Ali would require his age to have been 39 at his death. This is because 'Ali accepted Islam when he was 8 years old, as is well known, and remained in Mecca for 13 years thereafter. When he emigrated he was 21. The battle of Mu'ta took place in 8 AH. But God knows best. Ja'far was referred to after his death as al-Tayyar, 'the flyer', for the reason given above.
lessons: God's compensation for sincere sacrifice transcends what we can imagine in this world — Ja'far's wings are a symbol of how God elevates those who give everything.
The Prophet's spiritual vision of Ja'far at peace and honored in paradise brought comfort to a grieving community, reminding them that death is not the end of the story.
A person's legacy and title — like al-Tayyar — can carry their spirit forward across generations and inspire faith in those who hear it.
practical_advice: When you grieve a loss, hold onto the conviction that God honors and rewards every sincere sacrifice — even what the world cannot see or measure.
Learn the stories of those who gave everything for their faith; their examples can be a source of strength when your own sacrifices feel too costly.
Consider how you want to be remembered — what title or quality would you want associated with your name — and let that aspiration guide your choices now.

title: Ja'far b. Abi Talib Known as 'Father of the Poor' for His Generosity
summary: Ja'far b. Abi Talib was renowned for his extraordinary generosity toward the poor, earning him the title 'abu al-masakin' — father of the poor — and was praised for his noble presence and bearing.
story: He was a man who was noble, generous and much praised. For his charity he was known as abu al-masakin, 'father of the poor', because of his generosity to them. Imam Ahmad stated that 'Affan b. Wahib related to him, quoting Khalid, from 'Ikrima, from Abu Hurayra, who said, 'No one wore shoes or sandals, rode his mount or wore his clothes, apart from the Messenger of God (SAAS), more impressively than did Ja'far b. Abi Talib.' This hadith has a fine chain of transmission back to Abu Hurayra. It seems that he finds him superior only in his noble qualities. Regarding superiority in his religious qualities, it is well known that Abu Bakr, 'Umar and 'Uthman were better than him. As for his brother 'Ali and himself, may God be pleased with both, it is clear that they were equal or that 'Ali was superior.
lessons: True nobility is expressed not only in bearing and presence but in how generously one gives to those who have nothing — Ja'far united both.
Being known for generosity to the poor is among the most enduring and meaningful legacies a person can leave.
Even among those with extraordinary virtues, humility requires acknowledging where others surpass us — the scholar's careful distinctions here model intellectual honesty.
practical_advice: Examine your relationship with generosity: are you giving to the poor in ways that are consistent, personal, and sacrificial, or only when convenient?
Consider taking on a title of service — not literally but in practice — by committing to a specific community or group of people who need consistent support.
Practice carrying yourself with dignity and care in how you present yourself: Ja'far's noble bearing was not vanity but a reflection of his gratitude for God's blessings.

title: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib's Noble Character and Generosity to the Poor
summary: Abu Hurayra praises Ja'far ibn Abi Talib as the most noble and generous of men, recounting how Ja'far would take the poor into his home and feed them whatever he had, even scraping out empty leather butter vessels for them.
story: Hurayra, who said, 'Noone wore shoes or sandals, rode his mount or wore his clothes, apart from the Messenger of God (SAAS), more impressively than did Ja'far b. Abi Talib.' This hadith has a fine chain of transmission back to Abu Hurayra. It seems that he finds him superior only in his noble qualities. Regarding superiority in his religious qualities, it is well known that Abu Bakr, Umar and Uthman were better than him. As for his brother 'Ali and himself, may God be pleased with both, it is clear that they were equal or that 'Ali was superior to him. Abu Hurayra only wished to establish his superiority in noble qualities. This is proven by what al-Bukhari related, as follows, 'Ahmad b. Abu Bakr related to us, quoting Muhammad b. Ibrahim b. Dinar Abu 'Abd Allah al-Juhani, from Ibn Abi Dhi'b, from Sa'id al-Maqburi, from Abu Hurayra (who said) that, "The people used to say, "Abu Hurayra does too much."" I used to keep close to the Messenger of God (SAAS), content to fill my stomach with (unleavened) bread. I never ate leavened bread, never wore silk and never had men or women serve me. I would press my belly against the pebbles in my hunger. I would teach people about a verse of the Qur'an I already knew to get him to take me home to feed me. The person who was best to the poor was Ja'far, son of Abu Talib. He would take us home and feed us whatever he had there. He would even bring out to us an empty leather butter vessel we would split open and lick out its contents.' Al-Bukhari is alone in giving this.
lessons: True nobility is measured by generosity and compassion toward the poor and needy, not by wealth or status.
Hardship and hunger do not diminish a person's dignity; Abu Hurayra endured poverty while still seeking knowledge and serving the Prophet.
Even small acts of generosity — sharing whatever one has, even an empty vessel — leave a lasting impact and are remembered by those who benefited.
practical_advice: When you are going through financial hardship, look for small ways to give or share with others; generosity even in scarcity brings spiritual relief and builds community.
If you feel overlooked or undervalued, reflect on the example of Abu Hurayra, who found contentment in proximity to goodness rather than material comfort.
Identify one person in your life who is struggling and offer them something concrete this week — a meal, your time, or simply your presence.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit's Elegy for Ja'far ibn Abi Talib
summary: The poet Hassan ibn Thabit mourns the death of Ja'far ibn Abi Talib at the Battle of Mu'ta, eulogizing him as the best of men in lineage, generosity, and nobility, second only to the Prophet Muhammad himself.
story: Hassan b. Thabit spoke the following verses eulogizing Ja'far, 'I wept, for the death of Ja'far, the dear friend of the Prophet, was hard on all mankind. I was very upset, and when your death was told me, I called out, "Who is for fighting in the shade of the eagle (banner)?" With swords which, when drawn from their sheaths striking and spears continually piercing, After Faza'a's blessed son, Ja'far, the best of men, the worst To lose; the most noble of all in lineage, the most active when wronged and most submissive To the right when it occurs, unaffected by falsehood, most generous and least Immoderate, most open-handed when appropriate, most evident in Generosity, except for Muhammad, the like of whom does not exist in all mankind.'
lessons: Grief is a natural and honorable response to the loss of those who embodied goodness; expressing sorrow is not weakness but a recognition of what truly matters.
Commemorating the virtues of those who have passed preserves their legacy and inspires the living to emulate their noble qualities.
True greatness is recognized by the community — when a person of genuine character is lost, the whole community feels the absence.
practical_advice: When grieving the loss of someone admirable, try to write down or verbally share one quality they had that you wish to carry forward in your own life.
Honor the memory of those you have lost by performing an act of generosity in their name, just as Ja'far was remembered for his generosity.
Allow yourself to feel the weight of loss without rushing to suppress it; grief processed honestly leads to healing and renewed purpose.

title: The Life, Piety, and Martyrdom of Abdullah ibn Rawaha
summary: Abdullah ibn Rawaha, a companion of the Prophet and commander at Mu'ta, is remembered for his deep faith, his habit of gathering companions for remembrance of God, his steadfast fasting on journeys, and his ultimate martyrdom at Mu'ta.
story: Ibn Rawaha's genealogy is as follows. His full name was 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha b. Tha'laba b. Imru al-Qays b. 'Amr b. Imru al-Qays al-Akbar b. Malik b. al-Agharr b. Tha'laba b. Ka'b b. al-Khazraj b. al-Harith b. al-Khazraj, Abu Muhammad, known as Abu Rawaha and as Abu 'Amr, al-Ansari, al-Khazraji. He was the maternal uncle of al-Nu'man b. Bashir, and his sister was 'Amra, daughter of Rawaha. He was early in accepting Islam and was present at al-'Aqaba. That evening he was one of the leaders of Banu al-Harith b. al-Khazraj. He participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud, al-Khandaq, al-Hudaybiyya and Khaybar. He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) would send him out to negotiate these, as is told above. He was present on the 'Umrat al-qada' pilgrimage and on that occasion went in holding the reins of the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Some say it was its leather stirrup he held. As he did so, he called out, 'Clear the unbelievers from his path.' The rest of his verses are reported above. He was one of the commanders martyred at the battle of Mu'ta, as told above. He urged on the Muslims to battle the Byzantines when they discussed that issue. He also encouraged himself and dismounted (to take the banner) after his two comrades were killed. The Messenger of God (SAAS) testified to his martyrdom and he was one of those promised entry to paradise. He is also related to have addressed the Prophet (SAAS) in verse when he said farewell to him, as follows, 'May God return the good He presents you, as he gave affirmation to Moses, and a victory they won.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'May God keep you firm.' Hisham b. 'Urwa commented, 'And God did keep him firm so that he was martyred and entered paradise.' Hammad b. Zayd related, from Thabit, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abi Layla, that 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) while he was making an address and heard him tell everyone to sit down. So he did sit down, right where he was, outside the mosque, until people had finished listening to his address. Word of 'Abd Allah's action reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he said, 'May God increase your zeal in obedience to God and to His messenger!' In the Sahih collection of al-Bukhari, Abu al-Darda' is quoted as saying, 'We were out on a journey with the Messenger of God (SAAS), on a very hot day. The only ones of us fasting were the Messenger of God (SAAS) and 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha, may God be pleased with him.' He was one of the Companions of the Prophet (SAAS), who was well known for his poem. His verses on the Messenger of God (SAAS), quoted by al-Bukhari, include the lines, 'With us there is the Messenger of God; we recite his document, when it emerges clear and shining from the dawn. He spends the night keeping his body outside his bed, when the polytheists lie heavily upon theirs. He brought us guidance after blindness, and our hearts are certain that what he spoke is true.' Al-Bukhari stated that 'Imran b. Maysara related to him, quoting Muhammad b. Fudayl, from Husayn, from 'Amir b. al-Nu'man b. Bashir, who said, ''Abd Allah b. Rawaha once fainted, and his sister 'Amra began crying and wailing and lamenting him. When he came to, he said, "Whenever you said something (about me) I was asked, 'Are you really like that?"' Qutayba related to us, quoting Khaythama, from Husayn, from al-Sha'bi, from al-Nu'man b. Bashir, who said, 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha fainted once as above. But when he died she did not weep for him.'
lessons: Consistent obedience to God in small matters — sitting where one is told, fasting on a difficult journey — builds the character that holds firm in life's greatest trials.
A person who lives by their convictions earns a death consistent with those convictions; Ibn Rawaha's life of faith culminated in a death of martyrdom that the Prophet himself affirmed.
The Prophet's supplication 'May God keep you firm' was answered fully — reminding us that sincere du'a and divine support are real and powerful forces in a believer's life.
practical_advice: Practice small acts of consistent obedience and discipline today — fasting, punctual prayer, or keeping your word — as these build the inner strength needed in times of real hardship.

When facing a difficult decision that requires courage, recall those who came before you who dismounted from safety and picked up the banner; ask yourself what your values demand of you in this moment.
Ask someone you trust to pray for you to remain firm in your faith, and reciprocate that du'a for them; this mutual spiritual support was a practice of the early Muslim community.

title: Abdullah ibn Rawaha's Sessions of Remembrance of God
summary: Abdullah ibn Rawaha would invite his companions to sit together for remembrance of God, which was misunderstood by some as encouraging abandonment of faith, but the Prophet praised these gatherings as sessions where angels competed with one another.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Samad related to him, from 'Amara, from Ziyad al-Nahwi, from Anas, who said, ''Abd Allah b. Rawaha used to say when he met one of his comrades, "Come, let's sit and express faith to our Lord sa'atan, 'for a while'." One day he said this to a man who got angry and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, "Messenger of God, don't you know about Ibn Rawaha? He's encouraging people to abandon your faith for that of Satan!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "May God have mercy on Ibn Rawaha; he loves those sessions when the angels try to outdo one another."' This hadith is very strange. Al-Bayhaqi stated that al-Hakim related to him, quoting Abu Bakr, quoting Muhammad b. Ayyub, quoting Ahmad b. Yunus, quoting a sheikh of Medina, from Safwan b. Salim, from 'Ata' b. Yasar, (who said) that 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha said to a comrade of his, 'Come; let's express faith for sa'atan.' He replied, 'But aren't we believers?' 'Why yes,' he replied, 'but by mentioning God we increase our faith.' The hafiz Abu al-Qasim al-Lakki related, from a hadith of Abu al-Yaman, from Safwan b. Salim, from Shurayh b. 'Ubayd, that 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha used to take one of his comrades by the hand and say, 'Let's go and express faith for a while; we'll sit in a dhikr session.'
lessons: Gathering for the remembrance of God increases faith and attracts divine blessings, including the presence of angels — a reminder that spiritual community is not optional but essential.
Good intentions and righteous actions can be misunderstood by others; the test is not whether we are misunderstood but whether we continue doing what is right.
Increasing one's faith is an active, ongoing practice, not a one-time event; Ibn Rawaha understood that believers need regular renewal of their connection to God.
practical_advice: Set aside a regular time — even five minutes — each day to sit quietly and engage in dhikr or remembrance of God; invite a trusted friend to join you in this practice.
When your sincere actions are misunderstood or criticized, seek clarification calmly and trust that what the Prophet affirmed as praiseworthy is worth continuing.
Recognize that faith needs to be nourished and refreshed; if you are feeling spiritually flat or disconnected, seek out a gathering of remembrance rather than withdrawing further into isolation.

title: The Miracle of Mu'ta: Three Thousand Against Two Hundred Thousand
summary: At the Battle of Mu'ta, a Muslim army of three thousand faced a Byzantine and Arab Christian force of two hundred thousand, yet only twelve Muslims were killed while many of the enemy fell, a result described as truly wonderful and a sign of divine assistance.
story: That two armies of opposing faiths, one consisting of those fighting for God's cause and numbering three thousand men, and the other of disbelievers totalling two hundred thousand, one hundred thousand Byzantines and one hundred thousand Christian Arabs, could battle together with the resulting deaths of only twelve Muslims is truly wonderful! And a large number of unbelievers were killed. Khalid alone stated, 'Nine swords were broken in my hand; I finally held only a Yemeni broad-sword.' How many must he have killed with all those swords, not to mention all the other brave bearers of the Qur'an like him, who sentenced to death those crucifer worshippers, upon whom be the curses of the Most Merciful at that time and for ever! This relates to the words of the Almighty, 'There was a sign for you in the two sides that clashed. One side fought for God's cause, while the others were unbelievers whom they saw to be twice as numerous as themselves. Yet God assists with victory whomever He pleases. There is surely a lesson in this for those who have sight' (surat Al 'Imran; XIII, v.12).
lessons: Divine assistance is not limited by numerical or material odds; when one stands for a righteous cause, God's support can manifest in ways that defy human calculation.
The Qur'an explicitly draws our attention to such events so that we develop 'sight' — the spiritual insight to recognize God's hand in history and in our own lives.
Courage in the face of overwhelming opposition, as shown by Khalid and the companions at Mu'ta, is itself a form of trust in God that opens the door to extraordinary outcomes.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by a challenge that seems impossibly large, reflect on Mu'ta: the disparity between your resources and your obstacles does not determine the outcome when God is with you.
Cultivate spiritual 'sight' by studying moments in history — sacred and personal — where apparent weakness led to unexpected strength through reliance on God.
Before facing a daunting situation, remind yourself of a time when you or someone you know overcame seemingly impossible odds through faith and perseverance.

title: The Martyrs of Mu'ta: Eight to Twelve Companions Who Gave Their Lives
summary: The names of the Muslim martyrs at the Battle of Mu'ta are recorded — eight according to Ibn Ishaq and twelve according to Ibn Shibab al-Zuhri — including commanders Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, Zayd ibn Haritha, and Abdullah ibn Rawaha, as well as lesser-known companions from among the Muhajirun and Ansar.
story: THOSE MUSLIMS MARTYRED AT MU'TA. From among the muhajirun there were Ja'far b. Abi Talib, their freed-man Zayd b. Haritha al-Kalbi, Mas'ud b. al-Aswad b. Haritha b. Nadla al-'Adawi and Wahb b. Sa'd b. Abi Sarh; these total four. From among the ansar there were 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha and 'Abbad b. Qays, both of Khazraj, al-Harith b. al-Nu'man b. Isaf b. Nadla al-Najjari, and Suraqa b. 'Amr b. 'Atiya b. Khansa al-Ma'ini; these numbered four. The total of Muslims killed that day was these eight men, according to the account of Ibn Ishaq. However, Ibn Hisham stated, 'According to Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri, those killed at Mu'ta included Abu Kulayb and Jabir, both sons of 'Amr b. Zayd b. 'Awf b. Mabdhul, both of Ma'in, both full brothers, along with 'Amr and 'Amir, sons of Sa'd b. al-Harith b. 'Abbad b. Sa'd b. 'Amir b. Tha'laba b. Malik b. Afsa.' These four were also ansar and so according to these two authorities the total killed should be twelve men.
lessons: Every martyr is remembered by name — a reminder that no sacrifice made for a righteous cause is anonymous or forgotten before God.
The sacrifice of a small number of faithful people can have an impact far beyond what their numbers suggest, as the Battle of Mu'ta demonstrated.
Remembering those who gave everything for their beliefs helps us hold our own commitments more seriously and gratefully.
practical_advice: Take time to learn the names and stories of people who sacrificed greatly for causes you benefit from today; this deepens gratitude and strengthens your sense of purpose.
When you feel that your contribution is too small to matter, remember that the twelve martyrs of Mu'ta faced two hundred thousand — their individual choices shaped history.
Honor sacrifice by living with greater intentionality; ask yourself regularly whether you are living in a way that justifies the blessings you have inherited from those who came before you.

title: A Returning Soldier's Lament for the Martyrs of Mu'ta
summary: A Muslim soldier who survived the Battle of Mu'ta expresses grief at returning alive while his three commanders — Ja'far, Zayd, and Abdullah ibn Rawaha — were buried in their graves, lamenting the misery of survival when noble companions have fallen.
story: A Muslim poet who returned with others from Mu'ta — may God be pleased with them all — spoke the following verses, 'Sad enough it is that I returned, while Ja'far, Zayd and 'Abd Allah are buried in graves. Their end came as they passed away on their path, leaving me to misery and with all that changes.'
lessons: Survivor's grief is a real and human experience; feeling sorrow at outliving those who were better or braver than oneself reflects love and humility, not weakness.
Those who fall on the path of righteousness are on their path — their end was consistent with how they lived, which is itself a form of grace.
Grief and continued living are not contradictions; the survivor's task is to carry forward the meaning of those who are gone.
practical_advice: If you carry guilt or grief for surviving a loss that took others — whether literal or figurative — recognize this as a form of love, and let that love motivate you to live more purposefully.
When grief leaves you feeling stranded and miserable, name what you have lost specifically, as this poet did, rather than letting sorrow remain vague and overwhelming.
Find a way to honor those you have lost by continuing the work or values they embodied; transforming grief into purposeful action is one of the most healing responses to loss.

title: The Prophet's Dream: Vision of Hell, Martyrs, and Prophets
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) recounts a vivid dream in which two men take him on a journey showing him the torments of hell, the blessed state of the martyrs of Mu'ta, and a meeting with Abraham, Moses, and Jesus.
story: The srnrhl, scholar and Ed.& Abn Zur'a 'Abd All?ih b. 'Abd al-Karim a l - h i , may God make his visage glow, stated in his fine book Dalz'il al-Nubuwwa as follows, "Both Safwm b. Salih al-Dimasbqi, quoting al-Walid, quoting Ibn Jarir, and 'Abd al-lblpnm b. Ibrahim al-Dimashqi, quoting al-Walid and 'Arnr, that is, Ibn 'Abd al-W?&id, related to him that Ibn Jarir stated that he heard Salim b. 'Amir al-Khaba'iri say, 'Abo U m b a al-Bahili informed me, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, 'While I was sleeping two men came to me, gripped me by the arm and took me to a desolate mountain. They told me, "Climb!" "I can't," I replied. "We'll make it easy for you," they said. I then climbed on up until I was at its centre and there I heard loud noises. "What are those noises?" I asked. "That is the groaning of those in hell," they replied. Then they took me on to where there were people suspended by their tendons, their mouths split open and streaming blood. "Who are these?" I asked. "These", they replied, "are those who eat before their fast has ended." Then he said, "The Jews and Christians will fail."'" Salim (b. 'Amir) asked, "Did he hear it from the Messenger of God (SAAS), or was this his opinion?" "'The two men then took me on to where there were people exceedingly distended and exuding a foul odour like that of a toilet. "Who are these?" I asked. They replied, "These are unbelievers who have been killed." They then took me further where there were others even more distended and even more foul in smell, their odour also toilet-like. "Who are these?" I asked. "These are men and women who engage in prostitution," they replied. They then took me further and I saw women whose nipples were being bitten by snakes. "What did these people do?" I asked. They replied, "They are women who denied milk to their children." They then took me further off to where there were children playing between two lakes. "Who are these?" I asked. "These are the children of believers," they told me. ""Then they took me up to a high place where there were three men drinking wine. "Who are these?" I asked. "These are Ja'far h. Ahti Tdib, Zayd b. Hzritha and 'Abd Allah h. Rawaa," they replied. Then they took me to yet another high point where I found myself with three other men. The two men said, 'These are Abraham, Moses and Jesus, upon all of whom be peace. They await you.'"""
lessons: Dreams and visions can carry profound spiritual meaning, reminding us that our actions in this world have real consequences in the next.
The elevated station of the martyrs of Mu'ta — Ja'far, Zayd, and Ibn Rawaha — is affirmed even in the realm of the unseen, showing that sacrifice for God's cause is never forgotten.
