The vision of the prophets awaiting Muhammad (SAAS) conveys that the righteous are never truly alone; they are part of a great spiritual community across time.
practical_advice: When feeling anxious about the weight of your choices, reflect on how your daily actions align with your values and the lasting impact they may have.
Draw comfort from the knowledge that those who suffer for doing what is right are honoured and remembered, both in this life and beyond.
In moments of fear or uncertainty, recall that you are connected to a long tradition of faithful people who faced their trials with courage and trust in God.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit's Elegy for the Martyrs of Mu'ta
summary: The poet Hassan ibn Thabit composes a mournful elegy mourning the loss of Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, Zayd ibn Haritha, and Abd Allah ibn Rawaha, praising their courage and noble lineage.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The following verses of Hassnn (h. Thabit) are among those spoken in mourning for those killed at Mu'ta, 'A dificult night I had in Yathrib, worry that kept me sleepless while others slept deep. Remembering a friend, my tears flowed copiously, and memory often causes weeping. Indeed, the loss of a friend is a tragedy, and what a lot of noble men suffer yet persist! I saw the finest Muslims go off in groups, whiie others hehiid them delayed. May God not keep distant those dead men who followed on at Mu'ta, including Ja'far who has wings And Zayd and 'Abd M%h,all of whom followed on, when the ropes of death swung. That morning they went forth with the believers, led on to death by that happy man of splendid character, Fine as the light of the moon, of Hsshim's line, proud before injustice, valiant. He thrust on until he lay down without a cushion on a battlefield, a broken spear in him. His reward is with that of the martyrs - a paradise of gardens, under a canopy of green. In Ja'far we saw someone faithful to Muhammad, a man of decision when he gives orders. May there never cease to be in Islam men of H%hi's line, perpetual pillars of glory and the source of pride. They are Islam's mountain, and those around them are large rocks rising up to splendid, towering heights. Fine leaders, who include Ja'far and his brother 'Ali, as well as m a d , the one chosen. Hamza and al-'Abbas are of them too, as is 'Aqll, the sap of the tree from which he was pressed. Through them all hardships are alleviated at every dismal and difficult time when people suffer. They are God's friends to whom He revealed his wisdom, including that of absolute purity.'"
lessons: Grief expressed through art and poetry is a legitimate and healing outlet; the early Muslim community mourned openly and honoured their fallen without shame.
Remembering and praising the sacrifices of those who came before us keeps their legacy alive and strengthens our own resolve in difficult times.
The companions of the Prophet (SAAS) understood that true nobility lies not in wealth or status but in courage, faithfulness, and service to a cause greater than oneself.
practical_advice: When you lose someone dear, allow yourself to grieve fully rather than suppressing your sorrow; acknowledging loss is the first step toward healing.
Honour the memory of those you have lost by speaking well of them, preserving their stories, and embodying the values they lived by.
In times of communal hardship, seek solidarity through shared remembrance and expressions of gratitude for those who sacrificed on your behalf.

title: Ka'b ibn Malik's Lament for the Fallen at Mu'ta
summary: The companion-poet Ka'b ibn Malik composes a heartfelt lament for the Muslim warriors who fell at Mu'ta, praising their endurance, bravery, and devotion to God.
story: "Ka'b b. Malik, may God be pleased with him, spoke the following verses, 'While others slept, your eyes shed like water dripping from the seam of a waterskin. That night when cares overwhelmed me so that sometimes I moaned, at others tossed and turned, Sorrow kept returning, and I spent the night as though guardian of Ursa and Pisces. It felt as though a flame burned inside me, between my ribs and intestines In pain for those who followed one another at Mu'ta and lay piled high, unmoved. May God bless them, heroes all, and may fine rain water their bones. They endured at Mu'ta for God's sake, ignoring death and fearful of cowardice. They went on ahead of the Muslims, as though they were young stallions accoutred with full armour, Being led ahead by Ja'far and his banner; he was first and how fine a leader! Until the lines were breached and Ja'far lay stretched out where the ranks had clashed. The light-giving moon was changed then, and the sun was obscured and almost set. A leader of mighty stature, of Hshim's line, most honourable in his power, unmovable. Men by whom God protected his servants, to whom the revealed Book came down. They were superior to other tribes in their honour and dignity, their fine qualities overwhelming the ignorant. They would not expend their efforts on trivial matters, and their spokesman would be seen to decide with justice. Noble of visage, their hands would be open and flow with generosity, even when bad times excused them. For their leadership, God was pleased at His creation, and because of their bravery the God-sent Prophet was made victorious.'"
lessons: True courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to act rightly despite it; the martyrs of Mu'ta feared cowardice more than death itself.
Generosity and justice are marks of true leadership, and these qualities inspire others long after the leader is gone.
When the community suffers a great loss, communal mourning through shared expression can transform personal grief into collective strength and renewed purpose.
practical_advice: When overwhelmed by sorrow that keeps you awake at night, try giving your feelings a voice — through writing, poetry, or conversation — rather than keeping them locked inside.
Reflect on the qualities of those you admire and ask yourself how you can embody even one of those qualities more fully in your own daily life.
When facing a difficult task, remind yourself that fearing failure or dishonour can be a powerful motivator; channel that energy into doing what is right rather than what is easy.

title: The Prophet Dispatches Letters to Foreign Rulers Inviting Them to Islam
summary: Following the Treaty of Hudaybiyya, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) sends letters to rulers including Chosroe, Caesar, and the Negus, inviting them to accept Islam.
story: Al-Wzqidi related that this happened at the end of year 6 AH, in Dhii al-Hijja, after the Cumra to al-Hudaybiyya. Al-Bayhaqi gme this section in this position, following his account of the expedition to Mu'ta. But God knows best. There is no dispute among scholars that this activity began before the conquest of Mecca, and following al-Hudaybiyya. This accords with Aba Sufyw's response to Heraclius. When asked by hi, "Would he betray?" Aha Sufym replied, "No; we are into a period of time with him during which we do not know what he will do." In al-Bukhari's phrase, "That was the period in which Aha Sufyan had agreed to a truce with the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Muhammad h. Ishaq stated, "(This activity) took place in the period between al-Hudayhiyya and the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." We note here that comment, even though the statement of al-Wzqidi is preferable. But God knows best. Muslim related, from Yasuf b. Hammsd al-Ma'ni, from 'Ahd al-A'la, from Sa'id b. Aha 'U~ba, from Qaada, from Anas b. M a l i (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote, before the battle of Mu'ta, to Chosroe, to Caesar and to the Negus and to every tyrant, inviting them to God, Almighty and Glorious is He; this does not apply to the Negus, for whom he had prayed.
lessons: The Prophet's willingness to reach out to the most powerful rulers of his time with a message of peace and invitation demonstrates that da'wah requires courage and vision beyond one's immediate circle.
True conviction in one's beliefs compels one to share them even with those who hold great power, without resorting to force or coercion.
The Peace of Hudaybiyya created breathing room that the Prophet (SAAS) used not for rest but for expansion of outreach, teaching us to use periods of calm productively.
practical_advice: Use times of relative stability and truce in your personal life — lulls between conflicts or crises — to expand your positive efforts and reach out to others you have been meaning to connect with.
When you believe in something important, do not let fear of rejection from powerful or intimidating people stop you from sharing your convictions respectfully and clearly.
Consider how you can use diplomacy and written communication to build bridges with people who are very different from you, as the Prophet (SAAS) did with foreign rulers.

title: Abu Sufyan's Account of Caesar and the Arab Messenger
summary: Abu Sufyan recounts how Caesar, after defeating the Persians, received news of the Prophet Muhammad's emergence in Arabia and became deeply interested after discovering that the Arab he questioned was circumcised.
story: Yanus b. Bukayr quoted Muhammad b. Ishnq as having said, "At-Zuhri related to me, from Wbayd Allzh h. 'Abd AUXh b. Wtba, from 'Ahd Allzh b. 'AhbB, who said, 'Aba Sufym related to me personally as follows, "We were a mercantile people. The warfare had so blocked us in that our merchandise was spoiled. When the truce came - that of al-Hudaybiyya - between us and the Messenger of God (SAAS), we were stiU not sure that we were safe. I went away to Syria on a trading expedition with a group of Quraysh. I swear, I didn't know anyone in Mecca, man or woman, who did not consign goods to me. Our objective upon leaving Syria was to proceed to Gaza in Palestine. ""'We left and reached there. That was at the time when Caesar, commander of the Byzantines, defeated the Persians in his country and expelled them, retrieving his great cross that they had stolen from him. News of this reached him when he was in his palace at Hims, in Syria. He left there on a march of thanks to Jerusalem, to pray there. Carpets were laid out to welcome him, and these were spread with aromatic plants. Eventually he reached Aelia Uerusalem) and prayed there. ""'One morning he got up depressed and turned his sight up to the sky. His patriarchs asked him, '0King, are you worried this morning?' He replied, 'Yes, I am.' 'Why is that?' they enquired. He asked them, 'Did you notice that last night the mulk al-khitan, the "king of the circumcised" was ascendant?' They swore in response that the only nation they knew who practiced circumcision was that of the Jews. They pointed out that they were under his power and sovereignty and that if this vision had come to him about them, then he should send throughout his kingdom and kill all the Jews and so relieve himself of this concern. ""'While they were discussing this a messenger arrived from the governor of B u ~ r bringing a in an Arab whom they had captured. The messenger told Caesar, 'Your majesty, this is an Arab, one of those people of sheep and camels. He will tell you about an event in his land if you would care to question him.' When the man was before him, Caesar told his interpreter, 'Ask him for news of what is going on in his country.' He did ask him and the man told him that there was news of an Arab of Quraysh who had come forth claiming to be a prophet and that some groups were supporting, others opposing hi. He said that battle had occurred in several places and that all this had been in progress when he had left home. ""'When he had related this, Caesar said, 'Strip him!' And he was found to be circumcised! Caesar commented, 'I swear by God, it was this I saw, not what you said. Give him back his clothes.' The Arab then left, free. ""'Caesar then summoned his chief of police and told him, 'Turn Syria upside down until you bring me someone from that man's tribe I can question about him.'""'"
lessons: God's plan for the spread of His message works through unexpected and unlikely channels; a chance encounter between a captured Arab and the Byzantine emperor became a pivotal moment in Islamic history.
Even those outside the faith can recognize signs of divine truth when they are sincere in their search, as Caesar's vision and subsequent inquiry demonstrate.
The period of the Hudaybiyya truce, though uncertain and uncomfortable for the Muslims, was quietly arranged by God to open doors to the greatest powers of the time.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a period of uncertainty and transition — not knowing what comes next — trust that the calm may be preparing you for something larger than you can yet see.
Be open to receiving important guidance or insight from unexpected sources and in unexpected circumstances; do not dismiss encounters that seem coincidental.

When powerful forces in your life seem to be moving against each other, remember that larger events are often unfolding in ways that ultimately serve a greater good beyond what any individual can control.

title: Abu Sufyan's Encounter with Heraclius: The Circumcision Sign
summary: Caesar (Heraclius) discovers that a mysterious man whose kingdom he had seen in a vision was circumcised, matching the description of the Prophet Muhammad, and orders his police to find someone from that tribe to question.
story: He said that battle had occurred in several places and that all this had been in progress when he had left home. "'"When he had related this, Caesar said, 'Strip him!' And he was found to be circumcised! Caesar commented, 'I swear by God, it was this I saw, not what you said. Give him back his clothes.' The Arab then left, free. ""'Caesar then summoned his chief of police and told him, 'Turn Syria upside down until you bring me someone from that man's tribe I can question about him.'""' Abu Sufyan went on, "I and my companions were there in Gaza when he burst in on us demanding which tribe we were from. We told him and he took us all in to him."
lessons: Signs and evidence of truth can appear in unexpected ways, reminding us that God's plan unfolds through seemingly ordinary moments.
Honest inquiry and genuine seeking of truth are marks of wisdom, as even a non-Muslim ruler pursued the truth about the Prophet with sincerity.
Even those who are adversaries of truth may be compelled by God's will to become instruments through which the truth is confirmed.
practical_advice: When you encounter confusing or unexpected signs in life, pause and reflect rather than dismissing them — they may carry deeper meaning.
Pursue truth with the same diligence Heraclius showed, even when the answers challenge your assumptions.
Trust that God's truth will be made manifest through many different channels; remain open and observant in your daily life.

title: Abu Sufyan Before Heraclius: The Interrogation of the Prophet's Character
summary: Abu Sufyan, a leading opponent of the Prophet, is brought before Emperor Heraclius and questioned about Muhammad's lineage, followers, and conduct, and despite his desire to speak ill of the Prophet, his honesty compels him to confirm the Prophet's noble character.
story: "Eventually there we were before him. And I swear I never saw a man more astute than him," -he was referring to Heraclius. He went on, "Caesar asked us, 'Which of you is most closely related to that man?' I told him that I was and he asked me to approach closer. He then seated me before him and told my companions also to sit behind me. He told them, 'If he lies, then refute him.' I knew that they would not refute me even if I did lie, but I was a man of dignity and honour and would have been ashamed to lie. I also knew that if I did it was likely that they would report what I had said to others and that people would talk about me in Mecca. So I did not lie to him. "Caesar asked, 'Tell me about this man who has come forth among you.' I responded by telling him a little about him, minimizing his importance. He was not impressed by this and told me to answer his questions about him. I said I would. "He then asked, 'How do you view his lineage amongst you?' "'To be pure and of our finest lineage,' I replied. "'Tell me', he asked, 'has any other member of his family made claims similar to his and is he imitating that person?' "'No,' I replied. "'Tell me', he went on, 'is there any property involved that you have taken from him which might make him say these things so that you will return them to him?' "'No,' I replied. "'Tell me about his followers,' he asked. 'Who are they?' "'They are the young, the powerless and the poor; his followers include no chiefs of high birth.' "'Tell me about his companions; do they love and honour him, or (do they come to) despise and abandon him?' "'No man who has accompanied him has ever abandoned him,' I replied. "'Tell me about the warfare between yourselves and him,' he asked. "'Well', I replied, 'it varies; sometimes it goes in our favour, sometimes in theirs.' "'Tell me, would he betray?' This was the only thing he had asked that I could deceive him about, so I said, 'No; we now have a truce with him, yet we don't feel secure against some treachery by him.' But, I swear, Caesar took no account of this remark of mine."
lessons: A person of genuine honour and dignity is compelled toward truthfulness even when telling the truth works against their own interests, as Abu Sufyan's testimony illustrates.
The truth of a righteous person's character can be confirmed even by their enemies, for God ensures that integrity leaves undeniable traces.
Our conscience is one of God's greatest gifts — even those who oppose truth can be made instruments of its testimony when they listen to their inner sense of honour.
practical_advice: When facing a situation where dishonesty might seem advantageous, remember that the long-term cost to your dignity and reputation far outweighs any short-term benefit.
Reflect on the qualities of those you admire or even those you find difficult — often honest reflection reveals more goodness than pride allows us to admit.
Use moments of honest self-examination to assess whether you are living up to the values you claim to hold.

title: Heraclius Interprets Abu Sufyan's Testimony: A Non-Muslim King Recognises Prophethood
summary: After carefully questioning Abu Sufyan, Emperor Heraclius systematically analyses every answer and concludes with certainty that Muhammad is a true prophet, declaring he wishes he could be with him.
story: "He went on to talk further. He commented, 'You say he is of your purest lineage. God selects prophets that way; He only takes men from the purest lines in their people. I asked you whether any others of his family members were saying similar things that he is imitating; you said no. I asked whether he had some property you might have seized and suggested he might be saying what he is to get you to return it to him. But you said no. I asked you about his followers and you maintain that they are young, powerless and poor. That's how the followers of prophets are in every age. I asked you whether those who follow him like and respect him or despise and abandon him. You claim that only rarely does someone follow him then abandon him. In such circumstances the sweetness of faith does not enter a person's heart then leave again. "'I asked you about the warfare between you. You replied that sometimes it favours you, sometimes him. That is how warfare is for prophets, yet they win in the end. I asked you if he betrayed, and you said he did not. "'If what you have said is the truth, then he will conquer the land beneath these feet of mine. I wish I were with him so I could wash his feet!' "He then told me to go on about my business. So I arose, clapping my hands together and commented, 'This affair of Ibn Abi Kabsha has got to the point where the kings of those of pale skin fear him within their own domain!'"
lessons: The evidence for truth is often most powerful when it comes from those who have no worldly reason to affirm it, as Heraclius's testimony demonstrates.
God places recognition of the truth in the hearts of sincere seekers, regardless of their background or religion.
The companions of the Prophet — poor, young, and powerless — were a sign of genuine prophethood, reminding us that truth is not measured by worldly status.
practical_advice: When evaluating what to believe or trust, look for consistent patterns of integrity over time rather than relying solely on first impressions.
Do not underestimate the wisdom that can come from those outside your own community — truth resonates across boundaries.
Reflect on the people around those you follow or admire: their loyalty and character are often the truest measure of a leader's worth.

title: Al-Bukhari's Account of Abu Sufyan's Meeting with Heraclius: The Full Sahih Narration
summary: Al-Bukhari's detailed and authenticated narration of Abu Sufyan's interrogation by Heraclius covers the Prophet's lineage, the nature of his followers, his commands regarding worship and morality, and Heraclius's conclusion that Muhammad is a true messenger.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, prior to the section of his sahih collection that deals with faith, as follows, "Abu al-Yaman al-Hakam b. Nafi' related to us, quoting Shu'ayb, from al-Zuhri, (who said), 'Ubayd Allah b. Abd Allah b. Utba b. Mas'ud informed me, that Abd Allah b. Abbas informed him that Abu Sufyan informed him that Heraclius sent for him while he was with a group of companions of Quraysh. They were merchants and in Syria and it was during the period in which he, the Messenger of God (SAAS), had made a truce with Abu Sufyan and the Quraysh unbelievers. They (Caesar's men) went to them in Aelia (Jerusalem) and he (Caesar) invited them to a meeting with him. Around him were the Byzantine dignitaries. He invited them (the Quraysh merchants) to come forward and summoned his translator. Caesar then asked, "Which of you is closest in relationship to this man claiming to be a prophet?" "'Abu Sufyan went on, 'I am closest in relationship to him.' "'Caesar said, "Bring him close to me and move his companions forward to sit at his back." "'He then told his interpreter, "Tell them I want to ask him about this man. If he lies to me, then they should contradict him." And I swear by God, were it not for my embarrassment at having them attribute lies to me, I would have lied about him! "'The first thing he asked was, "How do you regard his lineage among yourselves?" I replied, "He is of good lineage." He next asked, "Has any one of your people made such claims as he makes prior to him?" "No," I replied. He then enquired, "Was any of his forebears king?" "No," I replied. ""'Is it your chiefs who are his followers, or your powerless?" "It is our powerless." "Are they increasing in number or decreasing?" he asked. "They are increasing," I told him. "Do any of his followers apostatize in anger at his faith after adopting it?" "No," I replied. ""'"Did you accuse him of telling lies prior to his making his claim?" "No," I replied. "Does he betray?" he enquired. "No," I told him. "We are in a truce and we don't know what he is doing now." This comment was the only one in which I could offer inference of anything (negative). ""'Have you battled with him?" he asked. "Yes," I answered. "And how did the fighting between you turn out?" "The warfare between us has sometimes favoured him, sometimes us," I replied. "What is it that he commands you to do?" he asked. "He says", I responded, "as follows: 'Worship God alone. Do not associate anything with Him. And abandon what your forefathers used to say.' He orders us to pray, to be truthful, chaste, and to maintain our family ties." "'He then told the translator, "Tell him, 'I asked you about his lineage, and you claimed that he is of high lineage. Messengers are sent among their people's nobility."' He went on, "I asked you whether any one else among you made such claims before him. You said not. If anyone had said the same before him, I would have said he was imitating him. ""'I asked you whether any of his forebears was king. You said not; if one had ruled, I would have said, 'He is a man seeking his father's kingdom.' ""'I asked you whether you accused him of lying before he made his claims. You said not. I then knew that he was not someone who would tell lies to people or lie about God. ""'I asked you whether it is his people's leaders or the powerless who follow him. You said it was the powerless; they are the ones who do follow prophets. ""'I asked you whether his supporters were increasing or decreasing. You said they were increasing. That is the way it is with a faith until it comes to fruition. ""'I asked you whether any of his supporters later renege after becoming displeased with his religion. You said not. That is how it is with a faith, when joy with it pervades the people's hearts. ""'I asked you whether he betrays. You said not. Similarly, prophets do not betray."
lessons: The Prophet's message of worshipping God alone, being truthful, chaste, and maintaining family ties represents a universal moral foundation that resonates across cultures and religions.
Faith that brings genuine joy to its people's hearts does not produce mass apostasy — the stability and loyalty of the early Muslim community was itself a powerful testimony to the truth of Islam.
Even an enemy's honest testimony can become one of the most powerful proofs of truth; God's wisdom sometimes works through those who oppose His message.
practical_advice: Reflect on the core commands attributed to the Prophet in this passage — to pray, be truthful, be chaste, and maintain family ties — and assess how consistently you are living by these principles.

When your faith is tested, remember that the early Muslims' steadfastness and joy in their religion is a model: seek to cultivate that same deep-rooted contentment rather than a surface-level practice.
If someone challenges your beliefs, respond as Abu Sufyan was compelled to respond — with honesty — and trust that the truth of your faith can speak for itself.

title: Abu Sufyan's Testimony Before Heraclius: The Prophet's Character Confirmed by an Enemy
summary: Abu Sufyan, an enemy of the Prophet, is questioned by the Byzantine Emperor Heraclius about Muhammad's character and lineage, and despite his animosity, his honest answers lead Heraclius to conclude that Muhammad is a true prophet.
story: Abu Sufyan stated, "When he had said this and had finished reading the letter, there was an uproar in the court. Voices were raised and we were expelled. After we had left, I told my companions, 'This Ibn Aba Kabsha affair has got to the point where he even scares the king of those of pale skin!' Thereafter I was convinced that he would prevail; and eventually God drew me into Islam."
lessons: Truth has a way of making itself known even through the mouths of enemies; when someone is genuinely righteous, those who know them best cannot deny it even under pressure.
Conviction in one's faith can grow gradually through witnessing its effects on others — Abu Sufyan's eventual Islam was preceded by honest reflection forced upon him by Heraclius's questioning.
Personal pride and tribal loyalty can delay one's recognition of truth, but sincere examination of evidence ultimately guides hearts toward what is right.
practical_advice: When you find yourself defending a position out of habit or pride rather than conviction, pause and ask yourself whether your reasoning is honest — truth-seeking requires courage.
If you are struggling to accept something that challenges your worldview, try examining the evidence as if you were an impartial observer, the way Heraclius cross-examined Abu Sufyan.
Reflect on moments when your own opponents or critics were forced to acknowledge your good qualities — this can be a source of quiet confidence and reassurance.

title: Heraclius Reads the Prophet's Letter and Recognizes His Truthfulness
summary: Emperor Heraclius reads the letter of the Prophet Muhammad sent through Dihya al-Kalbi, questions Abu Sufyan about the Prophet's character, and privately acknowledges Muhammad as a genuine prophet, though he fears the reaction of his people.
story: He then called for the letter dispatched by the Messenger of God (SAAS) with Dihya to the governor of Busra which the latter had sent on to Heraclius. Its contents were as follows, "In the name of God the Most Compassionate, the most Merciful. From Muhammad, the servant of God and His messenger to Heraclius, the leader of the Byzantines. Peace be upon him who follows the guidance. I invite you to Islam; accept it and you will be safe and God will give you double reward. If you turn away, then yours will be the sin of the peasants. Moreover, O people of the scriptures, agree to the al-kalima the joint statement with us: that we will worship only God and will associate nothing with Him. That some of us will not take others as masters besides God. And if they turn away, say, 'Bear witness that we are Muslims'" (Surat Al-'Imran; III, v.64).
lessons: A message of truth delivered with clarity and sincerity reaches even the most powerful hearts, even when worldly circumstances prevent those hearts from acting on it.
The fear of social consequences and the fear of losing power are among the greatest barriers to accepting truth — recognizing this in ourselves can help us overcome similar obstacles.
Divine guidance is extended to all people regardless of their background or religion; the invitation to truth is universal and respectful in its tone.
practical_advice: When communicating something important and true to someone in a position of power or influence over you, follow the Prophet's example: be direct, respectful, and clear about consequences without being threatening.

If you feel you know the right path but fear the reaction of those around you, identify specifically what you fear losing and ask yourself whether that loss outweighs the peace of living in alignment with your convictions.
Study the diplomatic language of the Prophet's letters — they model how to deliver difficult truths to people of different faiths and backgrounds with dignity and without hostility.

title: Heraclius's Vision of the Circumcised Kingdom and His Recognition of Islam's Rise
summary: Heraclius wakes in a bad mood after reading celestial signs indicating the rise of a kingdom of the circumcised, and upon learning that Arabs practice circumcision and that a prophet has emerged among them, he privately concludes that Islam is ascendant.
story: Al-Bukhari continued, "Ibn Natir, governor of Aelia when Heraclius had been appointed leader of the Christians of Syria, would relate that after Heraclius had arrived in Aelia, he awoke one morning in a bad mood. Some of his patriarchs asked him why and expressed concern. Heraclius, Ibn Natir related, was a soothsayer who interpreted the stars. When they asked him about his mood, he replied, 'When I looked up at the stars, I saw that the "kingdom of the circumcised" was ascendant. Who is it of this nation who are circumcised?' They replied, 'Only the Jews practise circumcision; and you should definitely not fear them. Issue orders to your provincial town to kill all the Jews there.' "While they were discussing this, in came to Heraclius a man sent by the king of Ghassan and he told them of the activities of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Heraclius questioned him further and gave orders for them to go and discover whether or not he (the messenger from Ghassan) was circumcised. They went and looked and reported that he was. Heraclius then asked whether or not the Arabs practised circumcision and was told that they did. Thereupon he said, 'The kingdom of this nation is ascendant!' He wrote to a colleague in Rome who was as knowledgeable as himself."
lessons: Even those outside the faith, using the tools of their own traditions, could perceive signs of the truth of Islam's message — divine truth leaves marks that transcend cultural and religious boundaries.
The emergence of a new era of guidance can be recognized by those who are sincere seekers of truth, even before they fully understand or accept it.
History turns on pivotal moments of recognition — those who pay attention to signs and reflect deeply are better positioned to make life-changing decisions at the right time.
practical_advice: When you sense that something significant is changing in your life or the world around you, do not dismiss the feeling — take time to reflect and seek knowledge rather than reacting with fear or denial.
Like Heraclius seeking confirmation from a colleague in Rome, consult knowledgeable and trustworthy people when making important decisions rather than relying solely on your own interpretation.
Pay attention to patterns and convergences in your life; sometimes multiple signs pointing in the same direction are a form of guidance worth taking seriously.

title: Heraclius Tests His Dignitaries and Despairs of Their Accepting the Faith
summary: Heraclius gathers his Byzantine dignitaries in a locked chamber and proposes allegiance to the Prophet, but they rush for the doors in rejection, causing him to despair of their ever accepting the faith, and he retreats by claiming he was only testing them.
story: Then Heraclius went to Hims, where he had scarcely arrived before a letter came from his colleague agreeing with his own conclusion that the prophet who had emerged was indeed genuine. Heraclius had his dignitaries taken into a chamber in Hims, the doors of which were locked. Looking down at them, he said, 'Byzantines! Is it your desire for success and right guidance and to have stability in your country? If so, then you will give allegiance to this prophet.' "The dignitaries raced for the doors like wild onagers, but found them locked. When Heraclius saw their negative reaction, he despaired of them (accepting) the faith and ordered for them to be reassembled before him. He told them, 'I only spoke as I did before to test the strength of your allegiance to your faith. Now I have seen.' "They bowed low before him and expressed their satisfaction with him. And this brought the (involvement) of Heraclius to an end."
lessons: Knowing the truth and acting on it are two very different things — Heraclius knew Muhammad was a genuine prophet but allowed fear of his people to keep him from following what he knew to be right.
Social belonging and the fear of rejection from one's community can be among the most powerful forces preventing people from embracing truth and making meaningful change.
There are moments in life when one must choose between the approval of people and the approval of God — postponing that choice indefinitely is itself a choice with consequences.
practical_advice: If you find yourself believing one thing privately but acting differently in public due to fear of others' reactions, identify one small, honest step you can take toward aligning your actions with your convictions.

When the people around you reject something true or good, do not let their reaction define your own decision — seek out communities and individuals who support your growth rather than your stagnation.

Reflect on whether there are areas of your life where you know what is right but have been making excuses — acknowledging this honestly to yourself, as Heraclius could not, is the first step toward change.

title: Abu Sufyan's Second Account: Defending the Prophet's Honor Before Heraclius
summary: In a second narration, Abu Sufyan is brought before Heraclius and despite his hostility toward the Prophet, his honest answers about Muhammad's lineage, intelligence, and trustworthiness confirm the Prophet's authenticity to the emperor.
story: Ibn Lahi'a stated, from al-Aswad, from 'Urwa, "Abu Sufyan b. Harb left on a mercantile expedition to Syria, accompanied by some colleagues. News of the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached Heraclius and he wished to ascertain what was known about him. So he sent word to the leader of the Arab community in Syria within his domain and ordered him to dispatch to him some Arabs so that he could question them about the Messenger of God (SAAS). He therefore sent to Heraclius 30 Arabs, including Abu Sufyan b. Harb. They went in to him in the church in the centre of Aelia. Heraclius said, 'I sent for you to give me information about the activities of this man of Mecca.' They replied, 'He is a magician and a liar and no prophet.' "Heraclius asked them, 'Then tell me, which of you knows him best and is closest to him in relationship?' They replied that that would be Abu Sufyan, his uncle, and that he had fought against him. "When they told him that, he had them removed. Then he seated Abu Sufyan and questioned him. He asked, 'Now tell me, Abu Sufyan.' He replied, 'He is a magician and a liar.' "Heraclius went on, 'I don't just want you to slander him; tell me about his lineage among you.' 'He is of the house of Quraysh,' he replied. "'How is his intelligence, his reasoning?' 'We never found fault with his views.' "'Was he deceitful, lying and wont to swear oaths in his business dealings?' 'No, I swear by God; he was not like that.' "'Perhaps he is seeking some kingship or high honour that had previously been held by a forebear of his?' 'No,' Abu Sufyan replied. "'Do any of those (who follow him) come back to you?' 'No,' he replied. "'And did he renege after making an agreement?' Heraclius asked. 'No', he replied, 'except that he might renege on this truce,' Abu Sufyan replied. "'And what are your fears about this truce?' 'My people and their allies have given a truce to him and his allies.' Heraclius commented, 'If you begin (the fighting) then it is you who will be the ones betraying.' "Abu Sufyan became angry at this and said, 'He only ever beat us once, and I was absent that day! That was at Badr. Then I attacked him twice while they were in their homes, slitting open stomachs, cutting off their ears and private parts!' "'And do you consider him a liar, or truthful?' Heraclius enquired. 'Oh, he's a liar,' he replied. "'Well, if there is a prophet among you, you should not kill him. The Jews are the ones best at doing that!' Heraclius commented. "Abu Sufyan then returned home."
lessons: A person's true character is revealed under careful, impartial questioning — the Prophet's enemies could not honestly deny his noble character even when given every opportunity to do so.

Anger and wounded pride often cause people to contradict themselves, as Abu Sufyan did when he raged about military defeats while claiming Muhammad was a liar — emotions can cloud our perception of reality.
The Prophet's consistency in honesty, trustworthiness, and noble lineage were recognized even by his fiercest opponents, demonstrating that authentic character speaks for itself over time.
practical_advice: When someone challenges you or questions your credibility, focus on demonstrating your character through consistent actions rather than through forceful argument — let your track record speak.

If you find yourself growing angry during a discussion and saying things that contradict your own position, pause and recognize that your emotions may be controlling your reasoning rather than the other way around.
Ask yourself periodically: if my harshest critic were questioned about my character, what would they honestly say? Use that question as a mirror for your own self-improvement.

title: Dihya al-Kalbi Brings the Prophet's Letter to Heraclius and Meets the Bishop
summary: Dihya al-Kalbi delivers the Prophet's letter to Heraclius, who privately acknowledges the Prophet's truth but fears his people, and then refers Dihya to Bishop Saghacir, who boldly declares his faith in the Prophet before the Byzantines and is killed for it.
story: Ibn Jarir stated in his work on history as follows, "Ibn Hamid related to us, quoting Salama, quoting Muhammad b. Ishaq, from a certain scholar, who said, 'Heraclius said to Dihya b. Khalifa al-Kalbi, when the latter brought him the letter from the Messenger of God (SAAS), "I swear by God, I know well that your leader is a prophet sent by God and that he is the one whom we have been awaiting and whom we find mentioned in our scripture. But I fear for myself from the Byzantines; if I did not, I would follow him. Go and see Bishop Saghacir, and tell him about your leader. The Byzantines actually consider him more highly and more persuasive than myself. See what he tells you." "'Dihya went and told him what he had brought to Heraclius from the Messenger of God (SAAS), and informed him of what was the latter's message. Saghacir responded, "I swear by God, your leader is a prophet; we know him from his qualities and we find him with his own name in our scriptures." "'He then went and exchanged black clothing he had been wearing for white. He took his staff and went out to address the Byzantines in the church. He said, "Byzantines, a letter has reached us from Ahmad inviting us to God. I bear witness that there is no god but God, and that Ahmad is His servant and messenger." They attacked him immediately in unison, hitting him until they had killed him. "'When Dihya returned and informed Heraclius what had happened, the latter commented, "I told you! We fear for ourselves from them. And Saghacir was more respected and effective in speaking with them than myself.""'
lessons: Bishop Saghacir's willingness to publicly declare his faith knowing it would cost him his life stands as one of the most powerful examples of choosing truth over self-preservation.

The symbolic act of changing from black to white clothing before his declaration shows that sincere conviction calls for outward expression — faith is not merely internal but manifests in our actions and choices.

Some truths, once recognized, demand to be spoken even at great personal cost — the bishop's martyrdom was the ultimate expression of integrity, and it was recognized as such even by those who disagreed.
practical_advice: Reflect on a truth you know but have been afraid to speak or act upon — what is the cost of continued silence, and what would it mean for your integrity to finally act on it?

When you decide to take a courageous stand for something you believe in, prepare yourself mentally as the bishop did by making a deliberate, symbolic transition — marking the moment with intention can strengthen your resolve.
Consider the people in your life who have modeled moral courage at personal cost — draw on their example when you face your own moments of difficulty and need inspiration to do what is right.

title: Caesar's Bishop Affirms the Prophet's Truth and Advises Following Him
summary: When Dihya al-Kalbi presents the Prophet's letter to Caesar, a bishop — the most prominent religious figure present — declares upon reading it that Muhammad is the prophet foretold by Moses, and advises Caesar to follow him, though Caesar acknowledges the truth but feels unable to act.
story: Al-Tabarani related, through Yahya b. Salama b. Kuhayl, from his father, from 'Abd Allah b. Shaddad, from Dihya al-Kalbi, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent me with a letter to Caesar, leader of the Byzantines." (The account continues) "Caesar was informed that there was a man at his gate claiming to be an envoy from the Messenger of God (SAAS). Caesar was disturbed at this and ordered that the man be brought in. This was done; he had his bishops present with him." (Dihya continued) "I gave him the letter which stated, 'In the name of God, the Most Merciful and Beneficent. From Muhammad, Messenger of God, to Caesar, leader of the Byzantines.' A nephew of Caesar's, tall, red-haired and blue-eyed, snorted in anger at this and said, 'Don't read out this letter today! He began it with himself and then wrote, "leader of the Byzantines" instead of "king of the Byzantines".' "The letter was read to its end and Caesar ordered everyone out and sent for me. I went in and he asked me questions to which I answered. He then sent for his bishop, their most prominent figure, a man whose opinion and statements were influential, and when he read the letter, he said, 'By God, he is the prophet Moses and Caesar foresaw and whom we have awaited!' 'What do you advise me?' Caesar asked. The bishop replied, 'As for myself, I believe and follow him.' 'I too know', Caesar commented, 'that he is genuine, but I can do nothing. If I did,'"
lessons: Recognizing the truth intellectually is not the same as submitting to it — Caesar and his bishop both knew Muhammad was a genuine prophet, yet only the bishop chose to act on that knowledge.
The objection raised by Caesar's nephew over the letter's address — a matter of protocol and pride — shows how trivial concerns and wounded ego can become barriers to receiving important truths.
Those in positions of religious and scholarly authority bear a heightened responsibility to guide others toward truth, as the bishop did, even when secular leaders fail to lead the way.
practical_advice: When you encounter a truth that challenges your existing framework or identity, ask yourself honestly: am I resisting this because of a logical problem with it, or because accepting it would require me to change something I am attached to?
Do not let protocol, pride, or the formalities of how a message is delivered cause you to dismiss its substance — focus on what is being said rather than on grievances about how it was said.
If you are in a position of influence — as a parent, teacher, mentor, or leader — recognize that your willingness to acknowledge and act on truth sets the tone for those who look to you for guidance.

title: Caesar's Bishop Recognizes the Prophet and Caesar's Dilemma
summary: Caesar consults his bishop upon receiving the Prophet's letter; the bishop affirms the Prophet's truth, but Caesar refuses to embrace Islam fearing the loss of his kingdom.
story: On this subject, Muhammad b. Ishaq quoted Khālid b. Yasar as relating that a Syrian elder said, "From what news he had heard of the Prophet (SAAS), and having decided to leave Syria for Constantinople, Heraclius assembled the Byzantines and addressed them as follows, 'Byzantines, I have certain issues I wish to present to you. Tell me what are your wishes regarding them.' "They asked what they were and he went on, 'You will know, by God, that this man certainly is a prophet who has been sent. We find him to have those qualities by which he has been described to us (before); let us proceed and follow him and thus find peace in this world and the next.' "They replied, 'But then we would be under the control of the Arabs; and we are more numerous, powerful and have a more extensive domain than they do!' "He continued, 'Let us pay him the jizya, the poll-tax, every year. That will relieve pressure on me from him and by what I give him I will be spared possible warfare.' "They responded, 'Why should we give to the despised and powerless Arabs tax money they would take from us, when we are the more numerous, have a more powerful nation and a larger domain? No, by God, we'll absolutely never do that!' "'Well then,' Caesar suggested, 'let's make peace with him by my giving him Sariyya, while he leaves me the land of al-Sham.' He explained that Sariyya consisted of Palestine, Jordan, Damascus and Hims, up to al-darb, while the lands beyond al-darb constituted al-Sham. "They objected, 'Why should we give him Syria when you know well that it is the very navel of al-Sham! We'll never do that!' "When they refused, he asked them, 'Do you think, by God, that you'll have defeated him if you defend yourselves from him in your towns?' "He then rode away on his mule, eventually looking down upon al-darb and approaching the land of al-Sham. He then said, 'Peace be upon you, land of Sariyya; this is my farewell greeting.' He then hurriedly returned to Constantinople. But God knows best."
lessons: Truth can be recognized even by those who ultimately reject it — knowing what is right and acting on it are two different things, and we must strive to align our actions with our convictions.
Fear of losing worldly status and power can be a major barrier to embracing truth; we should examine whether our attachments to status are preventing us from making better choices.
Peer pressure and collective opinion can prevent individuals from doing what they know to be right; standing firm in one's convictions despite social pressure is a mark of true courage.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether fear of losing status, relationships, or comfort is preventing you from making a decision you know to be right — journaling about these fears can bring clarity.
When you face a decision that seems costly, ask yourself: 'What will I regret more in the long run — the loss of this worldly thing, or failing to do what I know is right?'
If you find yourself swayed by the opinions of those around you rather than your own conscience, take time in solitude to reconnect with your deeper values before making important decisions.

title: The Prophet Sends Shuja' ibn Wahb to the King of the Christian Arabs in Syria
summary: The Prophet dispatches Shuja' ibn Wahb with a letter to al-Mundhir ibn al-Harith al-Ghassani, governor of Damascus, inviting him to Islam, but the king responds defiantly.
story: Ibn Ishāq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent Shujā' b. Wahb, brother of Banū Asad b. Khuzayma to al-Mundhir b. al-Hārith b. Abī Shimr al-Ghassānī, the governor of Damascus." Al-Wāqidī stated, "He wrote a letter to take with him that said, 'Peace be upon him who follows the true path and believes in Him. I invite you to believe in God alone, and without associate who will preserve your kingdom for you.' "When Shujā' b. Wahb arrived, he read it (to the king) who responded, 'Who is it who would take my kingdom away from me? I will go to him.'"
lessons: Delivering a message of truth with wisdom and sincerity is the responsibility of the messenger — the response of the recipient is beyond their control.
Arrogance and attachment to worldly power can blind a person to the greater benefit being offered to them.
Fulfilling one's duty to convey what is right, even in the face of a hostile reception, is itself a form of spiritual success.
practical_advice: When you have done your part in a situation — offered good advice, shared the truth, or fulfilled your responsibility — release yourself from the burden of the other person's response.
Recognize when pride or fear of losing control is shaping your reactions, and ask whether that reaction is truly serving your best interests.
Practice focusing on what is within your power — your sincerity and effort — rather than fixating on outcomes you cannot control.

title: The Prophet Sends a Message to Chosroe, King of Persia, Who Tears It Up
summary: The Prophet dispatches envoys to Chosroe, King of Persia, with a letter inviting him to Islam; Chosroe tears the letter in rage, and the Prophet prays that his kingdom be torn apart likewise.
story: Al-Bukhāri related, from a hadith of al-Layth, from Yūnus, from al-Zuhrī, from 'Ubayd Allāh b. 'Abd Allāh b. 'Utba, from Ibn 'Abbās (who said), "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent his message with an envoy to Chosroe with orders that he deliver it to the governor of al-Babayn. The governor forwarded it to Chosroe who tore it into shreds after he had read it. "And I believe that Ibn al-Musayyab said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) cursed them, saying, "May they be completely torn into shreds!"'" 'Abd Allāh b. Wahb quoted al-Zuhrī, from Yūnus, as saying, "'Abd al-Rahmān b. al-Hāri related to me, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) climbed the minbar one day to make an address and gave praise and thanks to God, spoke the shahāda, then said, 'I wish to send some of you forth to foreign kings; do not dispute among yourselves about me as Banū Isrā'īl did about Jesus, son of Mary.' "The muhājirīn said, 'O Messenger of God, we would never disagree about you over anything; order us to go forth.' "He then dispatched Shujā' b. Wahb to Chosroe who had his palace decorated and assembled the dignitaries of Persia. He then brought in Shujā' b. Wahb. When he entered, Chosroe ordered that the letter be taken from him, but Shujā' b. Wahb told him, 'No; I will hand it only to you, as the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered me.' Chosroe had him brought forward and Shujā' then gave him the letter. "Chosroe called for one of his scribes, a man from al-Hīra, who read it to him. It said, 'From Muhammad, servant and Messenger of God, to Chosroe, leader of Persia.' It angered Chosroe that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had begun the letter with a reference to himself, and he shouted in anger and tore up the letter before he learned what it said. He then had Shujā' b. Wahb taken outside. "Having seen what he had, Shujā' mounted his camel and left, saying, 'Well, I swear, I don't care which of the two routes I am on, since I did deliver the letter from the Messenger of God (SAAS).' "When the force of his anger had abated, Chosroe sent for Shujā' to go in to him. They searched but he could not be found. He was sought for as far as al-Hīra, but he had gone on ahead. "When Shujā' went in to tell how Chosroe had behaved and how he had torn up the letter, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'May Chosroe tear up his kingdom!'"
lessons: The courage to stand firm in one's mission, even in the face of rage and hostility from the powerful, is a sign of true faith and integrity.
How we respond to a message of truth reveals the state of our character — arrogance and pride can cause a person to destroy what could have saved them.
Trust in God's justice means that we do not need to personally avenge every wrong done to us or to the truth — consequences follow naturally from one's choices.
practical_advice: When someone dismisses your sincere effort with contempt, remind yourself of Shuja's words: 'I did deliver the letter.' Your responsibility is to act with integrity, not to control the reception.
Reflect on moments when pride caused you to reject good advice — consider whether a similar pattern is operating in your life right now and what it is costing you.
When you witness injustice or contempt toward what you hold sacred, channel your reaction into supplication and trust in God rather than destructive anger.

title: Chosroe Dispatches Men to Bring the Prophet to Him — and Receives News of His Own Son's Plot
summary: After Chosroe tears up the Prophet's letter, he orders his Yemeni governor Badhām to send men to bring the Prophet to him; the Prophet receives revelation that Chosroe's own son has killed him, and relays this to Chosroe's envoys.
story: Muhammad b. Ishāq narrated, from 'Abd Allāh b. Abī Bakr, from Abī Salama, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out 'Abd Allāh b. Hudhāfa with his message to Chosroe. When he read it, he tore it into shreds. When news of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he said, "May he tear up his kingdom!" Ibn Jarīr stated that Ibn Hamīd related to him, quoting Salama, quoting Ibn Ishāq, from Yazīd b. Abī Habīb, who said, "He dispatched 'Abd Allāh b. Hudhāfa b. Qays b. 'Adī b. Sa'īd b. Sahm to Chosroe, son of Hurmuz, the king of Persia with a message saying, 'In the name of God, the Most Merciful and Beneficent. From Muhammad, Messenger of God, to Chosroe, leader of Persia. Peace be upon those who follow the guidance, believe in God and His messenger and bear witness that there is no god but God who is unique and without associate, and that Muhammad is His servant and His messenger. I call upon you with God's invitation. I am God's messenger to all people, to warn all who live that the word (of God) bears witness against all unbelievers. If you accept Islam, you will be secure; if you refuse, then the sin of al-Majūs will be upon you.' "When he had read this, Chosroe ripped it up, saying, 'How dare he write this to me when he is my slave!' "Chosroe then wrote to Badhām, his governor over Yemen, saying, 'Dispatch two strong men of yours to this man in Hijāz to bring him to me.' "Badhām then sent off Qahraman, a scribe adept in Persian writing, along with a Persian named Kharkhara, carrying a message to the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordering him to accompany them to Chosroe. And he told Abadhaway, 'Go into this man's country, talk to him and bring me news of him.' "The two men left and when they reached Tā'if, they found there a man of Quraysh whom they questioned about the Messenger of God (SAAS). He told them, 'He is in Medina.' The people of Tā'if — and Quraysh too — welcomed the two men joyfully. They told one another, 'Rejoice! Chosroe, the King of Kings, is going after him! We'll be rid of him!' "They left and when they reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) Abadhaway told him, 'The Shāhinshāh and King of Kings Chosroe wrote to King Badhām ordering him to send to you those who would conduct you to him. He has sent me to you for you to leave with me. If you do so, a message will be sent on your behalf to the King of Kings that will be to your advantage and will protect you from him. If you refuse, then you know his reputation. He will destroy you and your people and lay waste to your country.' "The two men then entered to see the Messenger of God (SAAS). They had shaved off their beards but left their moustaches intact. He hated the sight of them and asked, 'Poor fellows! Who told you to do that?' They replied, 'Our Lord' — meaning Chosroe. The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'Well, my Lord ordered me to spare my beard but cut off my moustache!' He then told them, 'Leave now and come back to me tomorrow.' "Then news came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) from heaven that God had empowered Chosroe's son Shirawayh to kill his father. And on such-and-such a night of such-and-such a month Shirawayh did attack and kill his father. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) called in the two men and told them of this. They responded, 'Do you know what you are saying? We don't hate you so much as that! Shall we write to King Badhām about you regarding this?' 'Yes,' he replied. 'Do tell him this from me. Say to him, "My faith and my power will attain that reached by Chosroe and will ultimately encompass all creatures." Tell him, "If you accept Islam, I will award you the land beneath your feet and make you and your sons kings over your people."' "He then presented to Kharkhara a belt decorated with gold and silver that a king had given to him. The men left, returned to Badhām and told him their news. He told them, 'I swear by God, those are not the words of a king; I see the man to be a prophet as he claims. Let it be as he said, for if it turns out as he predicted, he is indeed a prophet sent by God; otherwise we will reconsider what to do.' "Soon thereafter, a letter arrived from Shirawayh which said, in part, 'I have killed Chosroe. I did so because of the anger in'"
lessons: Calm, dignified confidence in the face of threats from the powerful is a fruit of genuine trust in God — the Prophet was neither rattled by Chosroe's demands nor intimidated by the envoys.
Worldly power, no matter how vast, is subject to God's decree; the mightiest empires can be undone from within by their own conflicts.
Treating even hostile visitors with dignity and wisdom, as the Prophet did with Chosroe's envoys, can open hearts that intimidation never could.
practical_advice: When you face threats or pressure from those more powerful than you, ground yourself in the awareness that ultimate outcomes are in God's hands, and focus on responding with composure and integrity.

Reflect on how the Prophet used the occasion of the envoys' visit not just to defend himself but to extend an invitation — consider how you can turn adversarial encounters into opportunities for constructive dialogue.
When you receive news that seems almost unbelievable in how it vindicates your position, remain humble and focused on the larger mission rather than using it merely to prove a point to others.

title: The Assassination of Chosroe by His Son Shirawayh
summary: Chosroe, the Persian emperor who had torn up the Prophet's letter, was killed by his own son Shirawayh on the night of 13th Jumada al-Akhira, 7 AH, fulfilling the Prophet's prophecy.
story: Al-Waqidi, may God have mercy on him, stated, "The assassination of Chosroe by his son Shirawayh took place during the night of the 13th of Jumada al-kkhira, 7 AH at 6 o'clock." I note that there is poeay saying that his death occurred in a harzm, a sacrosanct, month, as follows, "They killed Chosroe during the night of the sacrosanct month; he passed away and was accorded no shroud." And an Arab poetw spoke the verses, "And there was Chosroe, sliced up by his sons with swords as if he were meat. On one day fate brought him to term; is not there a term for every pregnant mother?"
lessons: No worldly power, however great, is exempt from the decree of God — Chosroe's empire and life ended in disgrace despite his might and authority.
The Prophet's prophecy was fulfilled exactly as spoken, reminding us that the words of those connected to divine revelation carry a certainty that transcends ordinary human knowledge.
Pride and arrogance — such as tearing up a letter of guidance — can lead a person down a path of destruction they cannot foresee.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by the power or influence of those who oppose what is right, remember that every earthly authority has a term appointed by God — your role is to remain steadfast, not to despair.
Reflect on the moments in your life when arrogance or dismissiveness led to poor outcomes, and use that reflection to cultivate greater humility in how you receive counsel and guidance.
Use the transience of worldly power as a reminder to invest in what endures: your character, your faith, and your relationships with God and people.

title: The Prophet Tells Chosroe's Envoys of Their King's Death That Same Night
summary: The Prophet Muhammad told the envoys of Chosroe sent by the governor of Yemen that God had killed their lord that very night, and this was found to be exactly true.
story: Al-Bayhaqi narrated through more than one line, from al-Zuhri, as follows, "Aba Salama b. 'Abd al-Rahman informed me that it had reached h i that while Chosroe was in his palace reception chamber a petitioner was brought - or sent - to him, seeking his rights and to his great surprise a man walked up bearing a staff, saying, 'Chosroe, will you accept Islam before I break this staff?' Chosroe answered, 'Yes; don't break it!' The man turned and left. When he had gone, Chosroe sent to his chamberlain and asked, 'Who permitted this man access to me?' The replied, 'But no one went in to see you.' 'You lie!' he insisted. He was angry and threatened them, then left them. "When the new year came, the man returned, carrying the staff. He said, 'Chosroe, will you accept Islam before I break this staff?' "'Yes; don't break it,' he agreed. "When the man had left, he called for his chamberlain and questioned him as he had the first time. "Next year back came that man carrying the staff. He asked Chosroe, 'Will you accept Islam before I hreak this staff?' 'Don't break it! Don't break it!' Chosroe asked. But the man did hreak it. And thereupon God destroyed Chosroe."
lessons: God's invitation to guidance is extended even to the most powerful — repeated chances were given to Chosroe, demonstrating divine mercy before divine justice.
Procrastination in responding to the call of truth is a spiritual danger; Chosroe kept saying 'don't break it' but never actually accepted the call, and eventually the opportunity was lost.

The unseen workings of God operate beyond what human gatekeepers can control — the mysterious man who reached Chosroe despite all guards is a reminder that divine will cannot be blocked by worldly barriers.
practical_advice: If you have been putting off a spiritual commitment or a meaningful change in your life, treat each day as a renewed invitation — do not wait for one more sign before you act.
When you notice a pattern in your life where a call to growth keeps returning, pay attention: repeated nudges often carry a message worth heeding before the window closes.
Reflect on the ways you may be saying 'yes, but not yet' to what you know is right — identify one small step you can take today to begin moving in that direction.

title: Sa'd Brings News of Chosroe's Death to the Prophet
summary: Sa'd came to the Prophet Muhammad with the news that Chosroe had died, and the Prophet, having already read the news in Sa'd's expression, responded with a prophecy about the Persians and Arabs.
story: Then al-Bayhaqi narrated through Aba Bakr b. ' A y y ~ h ,from Da'iid b. Abti Hiid, from his father, from Abti Hurayra, who said, "Sa'd went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who said, 'I see some news from Said's face!' Sa'd then said, 'Messenger of God, Chosroe is dead!' He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) commented, 'God curse Chosroe! The first people destroyed will be the Persians, and then the Arabs!'"
lessons: The Prophet's perceptiveness — reading news in a companion's face before it was spoken — illustrates the deep attentiveness of a leader who is truly present with those around him.
The destruction of entire peoples can follow when they collectively reject divine guidance, a sobering reminder of the collective consequences of communal choices.
Even in moments of apparent victory or fulfilled prophecy, the Prophet's response carried a note of gravity, not celebration — a lesson in sobriety and awareness of God's larger plans.
practical_advice: Practice being truly present with the people in your life — notice their expressions, their energy, and what they may be carrying before they speak.
When something you predicted or hoped for comes to pass, resist the urge to gloat or celebrate excessively; instead, reflect with gratitude and awareness of what the moment means spiritually.

Consider how the choices of communities and institutions around you affect your own life, and think about what you personally can do to contribute to collective moral health rather than collective decline.

title: The Prophecy That the Treasures of Chosroe Would Be Spent in God's Cause
summary: The Prophet Muhammad foretold that the treasures of Chosroe and Caesar would be spent in God's cause, and this prophecy was fulfilled when Muslims later conquered the white fortress of the Persians.
story: Imam al-Shzfifi'i stated that Ibn Wyayna informed him, from al-Zuhri, from Sacid b. al-Musayyab, from Abn Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "When Chosroe perishes, there will he no Chosroe after him; and when Caesar perishes, there will be no Caesar after him. And, I swear by him who holds my soul in His hands, thiir treasures will be spent in God's cause." Muslim gave this from a hadith of Abii Wyayna, and both of these quoted it as a hadith from al-Zuhri. Al-Shafici stated, "(It is said that) When the letter from the Messenger of God (SAAS) was brought to Chosroe, he tore it up and the former commented, 'May his kingdom be tom up!' But we maintain that Chosroe honoured the letter from the Messenger of God (SAAS) and placed it in musk and that what the Messenger of God (SAAS) said was, 'May his kingdom be preserved!'" Al-Shzfi'i and other scholars say that when Arabs, some of whom having accepted Islam, went out on merchant ventures to Syria and Iraq, they would complain to the Messenger of God (SAAS) of their fear of the kings of those two countries. And he would respond, "When Chosroe perishes, there will he no Chosroe after him; and when Caesar perishes, there will be no Caesar after him." He (al-Shafici) stated, "And the kingdom of the Chosroes did indeed die out completely, and that of Caesar and Syria also ended completely, even though in general their reign persisted. This was grace to the prayer spoken for them by the Messenger of God (SAAS) when they honoured his letter. But God knows best." Muslim related, from Qutayha and others, from Aha 'Awma, from Sim&, from J ~ h i h. r Samra, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'A group of Muslims will discover the treasures of Chosroe in the white fortress.'" Asbat narrated, from Sim&, from Jahir h. Samara, a hadith similar to that and added, "I and my father were among them, and we acquired 1,000 dirhams from that."
lessons: The Prophet's prophecies about the fall of the great empires of his time were fulfilled in detail, affirming that trust in divine promise is not wishful thinking but a rational foundation for faith.

Treating a message of guidance with honour, even when one does not fully accept it, carries its own form of mercy — as seen in the possibility that Chosroe's respectful treatment of the letter earned a degree of divine grace for his kingdom.
The redistribution of worldly wealth to serve a higher cause reminds us that resources accumulated through injustice are ultimately temporary and will be redirected by divine decree.
practical_advice: When you face fears about powerful forces or overwhelming circumstances in your life, recall the Prophet's assurance to his companions that the feared powers would fall — and trust that God's promise is more reliable than any earthly threat.

When you receive a message of guidance — from a teacher, a book, or your conscience — treat it with respect even if you are not yet ready to act on it fully; that openness itself can be a form of mercy.
Reflect on how you use material resources: are they being directed toward what is good and lasting, or toward what is temporary and self-serving?

title: The Embassy of Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a to al-Muqawqis, Ruler of Alexandria
summary: The Prophet Muhammad sent Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a with a letter to al-Muqawqis, ruler of Alexandria, who received him with honour, engaged in thoughtful dialogue, and sent gifts back including the slave girl Mariya who later became the mother of the Prophet's son Ibrahim.
story: Ytinus h. Bukayr quoted Ihn Ishaq as stating, "Al-Zubri related to me, from 'Ahd Al-Fblpna h. 'Ahd al-Qari, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Hahb b. Aba Balta'a to the Muqawqis, ruler of Alexandria. When Hatih delivered to h i a letter from the Messenger of God (SAAS) he kissed it, honoured Hahb and entertained him well. He then sent him hack to the Messenger of God (SAAS), dispatching to him with Hatih a ceremonial gown, a mule and saddle and two slave girls, one of whom became Ibmhim's mother. The other girl was presented by the Messenger of God (SAAS) to M u w a d h. Qays alLCAhdi." Al-Bayhaqi related this also. He then narrated through 'Abd al-R&miin b. Zayd h. Aslam, from his father (who said) "Yahya h. 'Ahd al-R&man b. Ijaub related to him, from his father, from his grandfather, Haub h. Aha Balta'a, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent me to the Muqawqis, the ruler of Alexandria. I took h i a letter from the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he gave me accommodation in his home and there I stayed with him. He then sent for me, having assembled his patriarchs. He said, "I have questions for you and would like you to help me reach understanding." I replied, "Please ask." He said, "Tell me about your master; is he not a prophet?" "He is indeed," I replied. "He is the Messenger of God." "Then why", he asked, "if he is so, why did he not curse his people, since they evicted him from his country to another?" I replied, "Take Jesus, son of Mary; do you not bear witness that he was the Messenger of God?" "Yes, indeed I do," he replied. "Well", I asked, "what about when his people took h i and wanted to crucify him; could he not have cursed them and had God destroy them when God raised him up to the earthly heaven?" He replied, "You are a wise man who has come from a wise man. These are presents I am dispatching with you to Muhammad. And I am sending with you a guard who will conduct you to your safe haven." "'He presented to the Messenger of God (SAAS) three slave girls, including one who was the mother of Ibr&im, son of the latter. One girl the Messenger of God (SAAS) presented to Hassan b. Thahit, the aqzri. He also sent some of his treasures to him.'" Ihn Ishaq recounted that he presented four girls to the Messenger of God (SAAS), one of whom was Mariya, Ihmhim's mother. Another was Sirin, whom he gave to Hassan h. Thahit; to him she bore 'Ahd d-Rahmm h. Hassw. I note that among these presents was a hlack eunuch named Malhnr, a pair of plain hlack slippers, and a white female mule named al-Daldal.
lessons: Diplomatic wisdom lies in finding common ground across different traditions — Hatib's response to al-Muqawqis's question about the Prophet's forbearance was grounded in a reference that al-Muqawqis himself held sacred, demonstrating the power of meeting others within their own frame of understanding.

The Prophet's mercy toward those who wronged him — never cursing his people even when he had cause — was itself an argument for the truth of his prophethood that even a non-Muslim ruler could recognize.
Respectful engagement with a letter or message of guidance, even when one does not convert, reflects a quality of sincere intellect that opens doors of goodwill between peoples.
practical_advice: When trying to explain your values or convictions to someone from a different background, look for bridges in their own tradition or experience rather than simply asserting your perspective — this approach builds understanding rather than walls.

Study the example of the Prophet's forbearance: when people wrong you, consider whether responding with patience and mercy might ultimately be more persuasive and impactful than responding with anger or retaliation.

If you are in a position of representing your community, your family, or your faith to others, reflect on how Hatib carried himself — with dignity, wisdom, and the ability to think on his feet — and invest in developing those qualities.

title: The Expedition to Dhū al-Salāsil: Command Dispute Between 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ and Abū 'Ubayda
summary: The Prophet sent 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ to Dhū al-Salāsil for a military expedition, and when he sought reinforcements, Abū 'Ubayda was sent with the first muhājirīn. A dispute over command arose, but Abū 'Ubayda, in his gentle and easy-going manner, deferred to 'Amr to avoid conflict, as the Prophet had instructed him not to quarrel.
story: The hadīth al-Bayhaqī related it here, before the account of the conquest (of Mecca). His line of transmission leads through Mūsā b. 'Uqba and 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, both of whom said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ to Dhū al-Salāsil in the Syrian uplands, in territory of Balī and 'Abd Allāh and those of Quḍā'a allied to them." 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr stated, "Banū Balī were uncles of al-'Āṣ b. Wā'il. When he travelled there, he was afraid at the large numbers of the enemy. So he sent word to the Messenger of God (SAAS) asking for reinforcements. The Messenger of God (SAAS) assigned the first muhājirīn; Abū Bakr and 'Umar volunteered, along with a group of muhājirīn leaders, may God be pleased with them all. The Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed Abū 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrāḥ as their leader." Mūsā b. 'Uqba stated, "When they reached 'Amr, he told them, 'I am your leader; it was I who sent to the Messenger of God (SAAS) to seek reinforcements.' The muhājirīn replied, 'No; you are leader of your men, while Abū 'Ubayda is in command of the muhājirīn.' 'Amr insisted, 'No; you're just reinforcements provided to me.' "When Abū 'Ubayda, who was a man of fine disposition and easy-going nature, said, 'You know, 'Amr, that the last instruction he gave me was to say, "When you reach your comrade, obey him." If you disobey me, then I will obey you.' Abū 'Ubayda gave the leadership over to 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ." Muhammad b. Isḥāq stated, "Muhammad b. 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. 'Abd Allāh b. al-Ḥusayn al-Tamīmī related to me, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ to bring the Arabs into Islām.' This was because the mother of al-'Āṣ b. Wā'il was from Banū Balī. And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him to them, for he would therefore be at ease with them. When he arrived at a well in Judhām territory known as al-Salāsil - this expedition being known by that name - he became concerned. And so he sent to the Messenger of God (SAAS) for reinforcements. The latter sent him Abū 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrāḥ along with the first muhājirīn, among whom were Abū Bakr and 'Umar. When he dispatched Abū 'Ubayda, he told him, 'Do not quarrel.' "Abū 'Ubayda then set forth and when he arrived, 'Amr said to him, 'You have merely come to reinforce me.' Abū 'Ubayda said, 'No; but I will do my duty, as you will.' "Abū 'Ubayda was a mild and easy-going man. 'Amr told him, 'I insist; you are my reinforcements.' Abū 'Ubayda replied, ''Amr, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told me, "Do not quarrel." So even if you disobey me, I will obey you.' 'Amr told him, 'Well, I am your commander, and you are my reinforcement.' 'So be it,' he replied. "And 'Amr went and led the prayers with the men."
lessons: Humility and yielding in moments of conflict can preserve unity and serve a greater purpose than insisting on one's rights.
Following the direct guidance of a trusted leader, even when it feels personally costly, demonstrates mature faith and wisdom.
Gentle, easy-going character is a form of strength, not weakness — Abū 'Ubayda's disposition was praised by the Prophet himself.
practical_advice: When facing a disagreement at work or in a group setting, ask yourself: is winning this argument worth fracturing the team? Sometimes stepping back is the wisest move.
Before entering a potentially contentious situation, reflect on the clearest guidance you have been given and commit to following it regardless of provocation.
Practice letting go of titles and recognition; focus instead on whether the mission is being accomplished and the people around you are being served.

title: 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ Performs Ablution with Sand Due to Extreme Cold
summary: 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ, on the expedition to Dhū al-Salāsil, suffered an involuntary discharge on an extremely cold night and feared that washing with water would kill him, so he performed dry ablution (tayammum) and led the morning prayer. When he later explained this to the Prophet, the Prophet laughed and made no reprimand.
story: Abū Dā'ūd said that Ibn al-Muthannā related to him, quoting Wahb b. Jarīr, quoting his father, (who said), "I heard Yaḥyā b. Ayyūb relate from Yazīd b. Abī Ḥabīb, from 'Imrān b. Abī Anas, from 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Jubayr, from 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ, who said, 'I involuntarily discharged semen on a very cold night during the expedition to Dhū al-Salāsil. I was afraid that if I were to wash, I would perish. So I performed the ablutions with sand, then performed the morning prayers with my men. They later reported that to the Messenger of God (SAAS). God asked, "'Amr, did you really pray with your men while in a state of junub, ritual impurity?" I then told him what had prevented me from washing myself and said, "I heard God say, 'Do not kill yourselves; God is compassionate towards you'" (sūrat al-Nisā' IV, v.29). The Messenger of God (SAAS) laughed and said no more.'" Al-Wāqidī stated, Aflah b. Sa'īd related to me, from Abū 'Abd al-Raḥmān b. Raqīsh, from Abū Bakr b. Ḥazm, who said, "While away on an expedition, 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ involuntarily discharged semen during a night of exceeding cold. He asked his men, 'What do you think? I had a discharge, but if I wash, I'll die!' He had water brought and did perform the ablutions, washing his private parts and cleaning himself with sand.
