He then went on and performed the prayer with the rest. 'Awf b. Mālik was sent on ahead to report. He said, 'I reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the pre-dawn, while he was praying in his home. I greeted him, al-salāmu 'alayka, "peace be upon you", Messenger of God!' He responded, "You're 'Awf b. Mālik?" "Yes, indeed I am, I swear." "The one who does the butchering?" he asked, but made no further comment. He then asked, "Well, tell me the news." I made a report to him on our expedition and of the disagreement between Abū 'Ubayda and 'Amr, and how Abū 'Ubayda had submitted to 'Amr. The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "May God be pleased with Abū 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrāḥ!" "'I then told him that 'Amr had prayed with the men while he was in a state of junub, and that even though he had water, he had only washed his private parts in performing the ablutions. The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained silent. "'When 'Amr returned, the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him about his performance of the prayer and he related it to him, commenting, "I swear by Him who sent you with the truth, that if I had washed completely, I would have died; I never experienced cold like that. And the Almighty had said, 'Do not kill yourselves; God is compassionate towards you.'" "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) laughed; and we have not heard that he made any comment.'"
lessons: Islam is a religion of ease; when a religious obligation genuinely threatens a person's wellbeing, Allah provides alternative accommodations out of His mercy.
Being honest about one's mistakes and the genuine reasons behind them, rather than hiding them, leads to understanding rather than punishment.
The Prophet's gentle response — laughing rather than reprimanding — teaches leaders to respond to human vulnerability with compassion, not harshness.
practical_advice: When you are overwhelmed or physically unable to meet an obligation perfectly, remember that God's mercy is built into the religion itself — do what you can and trust in His compassion.
If you have made a decision under pressure that you worry others will judge, be transparent about your reasoning; honesty usually disarms criticism far better than defensiveness.
When someone in your care makes an imperfect but well-intentioned decision, consider responding with understanding rather than correction — sometimes a knowing smile is worth more than a lecture.

title: 'Awf b. Mālik's Butchering Incident and the Reaction of Abū Bakr and 'Umar
summary: During the expedition to Dhū al-Salāsil, 'Awf b. Mālik butchered camels for a group in exchange for a share of the meat, then fed it to his companions Abū Bakr and 'Umar, who upon learning how it was obtained were so troubled that they made themselves vomit the food.
story: Ibn Isḥāq stated, "Yazīd b. Abī Ḥabīb quoted 'Awf b. Mālik al-Ashja'ī as saying, 'I was present on the expedition to Dhū al-Salāsil sent out by the Messenger of God (SAAS) under 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ. I went in the company of Abū Bakr and 'Umar. We passed by some people who had slaughtered some camels but did not know how to cut them up properly. I was a proficient butcher, so I told them, "If you will give me a tenth share, I'll divide it up between you." They agreed and so I took a sharp knife and cut it up, right then and there. I took a portion for myself and carried it off to my companions and we cooked and ate it. Abū Bakr and 'Umar then asked, "Where did you get this meat, 'Awf?" I told them and they said, "By God, you did not do well to feed us this!" Then they arose and brought back up the food from their stomachs. "'When we set off for home from that trip, I was the first to reach the Messenger of God (SAAS), as he was praying in his house. I greeted him, al-salāmu 'alayka, "peace be upon you", Messenger of God!' He responded, "You're 'Awf b. Mālik?" "Yes, indeed I am, I swear." "The one who did the butchering?" he asked, but made no further comment.'"
lessons: The companions of the Prophet held themselves to the highest standards of scrupulousness in matters of what they consumed and earned, even in trying circumstances.
True accountability sometimes involves a personal cost — Abū Bakr and 'Umar did not simply express disapproval but took action consistent with their convictions.
The Prophet's quiet acknowledgment — asking the question without elaborating — shows that sometimes the most powerful form of moral guidance is a pointed question rather than a lengthy rebuke.
practical_advice: Examine the sources of what you consume — not just physically, but in terms of how you earn your income and what you allow into your life — and be willing to make adjustments when something feels off.
If you realize you have participated in something questionable without full awareness, take whatever corrective action you can rather than rationalizing it as being already done.
When you are in a leadership or mentoring role, sometimes the most effective correction is a brief, pointed question that prompts the other person to reflect rather than a long lecture.

title: 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ Asks the Prophet Who Is Most Beloved to Him
summary: After being given command over senior companions including Abū Bakr and 'Umar during the Dhū al-Salāsil expedition, 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ assumed this reflected his high standing with the Prophet and asked him directly who was most beloved to him, only to receive a humbling answer that caused him to stop asking.
story: The ḥāfiẓ al-Bayhaqī stated that Abū 'Abd Allāh al-Ḥāfiẓ and Abū Sa'īd b. Abī 'Amr informed him as follows, "Abū al-'Abbās Muḥammad b. Ya'qūb al-Aṣamm related to us, quoting Yaḥyā b. Abī Ṭālib, quoting 'Alī b. 'Āṣim, quoting Khālid al-Ḥadhdḥā', from Abū 'Uthmān al-Nahdī (who said), 'I heard 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ say, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent me forth in command of the army sent to Dhū al-Salāsil. Among us were Abū Bakr and 'Umar. I told myself that he would only have given me command over Abū Bakr and 'Umar because of some very high regard he had for me. So once I went to him, sat down before him and asked, 'Messenger of God, who is your favourite person?' "'A'isha,' he replied. 'I wasn't asking you about your family,' I told him. 'Then it would be her father,' he replied. 'Who after that,' I enquired. ''Umar,' he replied. I went on asking until he had mentioned a number of men. Then I told myself that I had better stop asking!"'"
lessons: Human beings are prone to measuring their worth through external recognition or position, but true closeness to God and His Messenger is built on faith, deeds, and character — not rank or appointment.
The Prophet answered honestly and directly even when the answer was humbling to the questioner, demonstrating that kindness does not require flattery.
Self-awareness is a gift — 'Amr's decision to stop asking before he heard something worse reflects a wisdom that protected his own peace of mind.
practical_advice: Resist the temptation to interpret a promotion, recognition, or opportunity as a definitive measure of your worth compared to others; such readings often set you up for disappointment.

When you find yourself fishing for reassurance about your standing, pause and ask whether the reassurance you seek would actually bring lasting peace, or whether it is better to simply do good work and trust the process.
Practice gratitude for the position and role you have been given rather than comparing yourself to others; every role has its own honour and responsibility.

title: The Prophet's Favourite People
summary: Amr ibn al-As asks the Prophet Muhammad about his favourite people and receives an answer that includes Aisha, her father Abu Bakr, and Umar, with Amr himself mentioned further down the list.
story: down before h i and asked, 'Messenger of God, who is your favourite person?' "A'isha,' he replied. 'I wasn't asking you about your family,' I told hi.'Then it would be her father,' he replied. 'Who after that,' I enquired. 'Wmar,' he replied. I went on asking until he had mentioned a number of men. Then I told myself that I had better stop asking!'" This haditla is given in both sahih collections through Khzlid b. Mahran al-Hadhdha', from Ahii Wthman d-Nahdi, his name being 'Ahd al-R*mm h. Mul. (The latter said), "'Amr b. alLCAs related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him out in command of the army to Dhii al-Salasil. ('Amr said), 'I went to h i and asked, "Which person do you like best?" "'A'isha," he replied. "And from the men?" I enquired. "Her father," he said. "Who then?" I asked. "Then 'Amr b. alLCAs," he answered. He went on to enumerate various other men.'" This is the text given by al-Bukhm. In one account 'Amr is quoted as saying, "I fell silent, fearing that he would place me last!"
lessons: Love and affection are natural human qualities even for the greatest of people, and the Prophet openly expressed his feelings without shame.
Knowing where you stand in someone's regard can be a source of motivation rather than insecurity — Amr ibn al-As found reassurance in being mentioned at all.
Genuine relationships with righteous people are among the greatest blessings one can have in life.
practical_advice: When you feel uncertain about your worth in a relationship, remember that being valued at all — even if not first — is itself a gift to be grateful for.
Practice expressing appreciation for the people you love, as the Prophet did openly and without hesitation.
If you find yourself anxious about comparisons with others, redirect that energy toward strengthening your own relationship with God and those around you.

title: The Expedition of Abu Ubayda to the Sea Coast and the Whale
summary: A force of 300 men under Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah is sent toward the coast on a military expedition, endures extreme hunger and food rationing, and then miraculously finds a giant beached sperm whale that sustains the entire army for weeks.
story: Imam Malik quoted Wahb h. Kayszn as stating that Jabir said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out an expedition towards the coast; in command he appointed Abii 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah. The force consisted of 300 men. And I was one of them. "Having travelled part of the way, our food supply became depleted. Aha Whayda ordered all the remaining food to be gathered together. The food consisted of dates. He would distribute a very little of it to us each day until eventually all we received was one date per day. I asked him, 'What good is one date?' He replied, 'You'll find out when it has run out!' "At last we arrived at the sea where we found a whale as big as a hillock. The army ate from it for 18 days; then Abii 'Ubayda ordered that two of its ribs be set up. He then had his mount saddled and he passed beneath them without touching them." This anecdote is given in both sahib collections from a hadith of Malik in similar form. It is also included in both sahih collections through Sufyah b. Wyayna, from 'Amr b. Din%, from Jabir, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us out with a force of 300 mounted men, our commander being Abii Wbayda b. al-Jarr@. We were to look for caravans belonging to Quraysh. We were overcome by intense hunger, so that we were forced to eat al-khabat, 'tree leaves'. The army therefore became known as the khabag army. Then a man slaughtered 3 camels then 3 more and yet 3 more. Ultimately Abu 'Ubayda forbade him from doing this. Then the sea cast up a creature called an 'anbar, a sperm whale. We ate of it for half a month and we grew fat and our bodies became healthy." He then went on to relate about the ribs. The reference here to looking for caravans belonging to Quraysh indicates that this expedition occurred before the truce of al-Hudaybiyya. But God knows best. The man in the anecdote who butchered the camels was Qays b. Sa'd b. 'Ibada, may God be pleased with him. The hafiz al-Bayhaqi stated that Aba Bakr h. Isbaq narrated to him, quoting Isma'il b. Qutayba, quoting Yahya b. Yahya, quoting Abi Khaythama — he being Zuhayr b. Mu'awiya — from Aba al-Zubayr, who quoted Jabir as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us out on an expedition under the command of Aba Wbayda to seek caravans belonging to Quraysh. He provisioned us with leather bags full of dates; that was all we had. Abu Wbayda would ration them out one by one. "I (Aba al-Zubayr) asked Jabir, 'What did you do with them?' He replied, 'We would chew on them like the children do, then we would drink water with them, and that would last us all day until the night. We also would knock down leaves with our sticks, pour water over them and eat them. "'We went to the sea coast and there on the beach something as big as a sand dune was cast up. When we approached it, we found it to be a creature called an 'anbar, a sperm whale. Aba Wbayda first said that it was dead. But he then said, "No; after all, we are messengers on behalf of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and pursuing God's cause. You are forced to do so; eat it." So we ate off it for a month, all 300 of us, and put on weight again. We would ladle the fat out of its eye sockets with leather bottles and cut meat off it the size of a bull. Aba Wbayda took 13 of us and sat them in its eye. He also took one of its ribs, stood it upright, saddled the biggest of his camels and passed under it. We provisioned ourselves with strips of its meat dried, and when we returned to Medina and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him this, he said, "It was bounty that God brought forth for you; do you have any of its meat you could give us to eat?" And we did send some to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he ate it.'"
lessons: In moments of extreme scarcity and hardship, patience and trust in God's provision are rewarded in unexpected and extraordinary ways.
Leadership in crisis requires wisdom and restraint — Abu Ubayda's careful rationing, though painful, preserved the army's survival until divine provision arrived.
Gratitude must follow relief: when the companions returned and shared their provisions with the Prophet, they completed the cycle of blessing by giving back.
practical_advice: When you are in the depths of a difficult situation and resources feel impossibly thin, hold on — relief often comes from a direction you never anticipated.
Practice disciplined restraint during scarcity rather than consuming everything at once out of anxiety; measured patience protects you for the long term.
When hardship ends, mark the moment with gratitude and share your blessings with others, as the companions shared the whale meat with the Prophet.

title: Usama ibn Zayd Kills a Man Who Declared the Shahada
summary: During a military raid against al-Haraqa, Usama ibn Zayd kills an enemy combatant who declared La ilaha illa Allah moments before being struck, and the Prophet repeatedly questions him about this act, leaving Usama deeply regretful.
story: Al-Bukhari related that of Usama b. Zayd to al-Haraqat of (Bano) Juhayna. He stated that 'Amr b. Muhammad related to him, quoting Hushaym, quoting Husayn b. Junduh, quoting Aba Zubyan, who said, "I heard Usama b. Zayd say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us out against al-Haraqa; we attacked them in the morning and defeated them. I and an ansari chased after one of their men and when we caught up with him, he said La Ilaha illa Allah, "there is no god but God". At this the ansari stopped, but I stabbed at him with my spear until I had killed him. When we returned home, this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) and he asked me, "Usama, did you really kill him after he had said, La Ilaha illa Allah?" "He was only trying to save himself," I replied. But he kept on asking me the same question until I wished I had not accepted Islam before that incident.'"
lessons: The sanctity of human life and sincere declarations of faith must never be overridden by assumptions about a person's inner motives, which only God can know.
Accountability for our actions, especially the gravest ones, is something we must face honestly rather than deflect with justifications.
Deep regret and moral conscience are signs of a living heart — Usama's anguish shows that recognising a wrong is itself the beginning of moral growth.
practical_advice: When you have done something you regret, resist the urge to justify it with after-the-fact reasoning; honest acknowledgment is the first step toward healing.
Before acting in anger or urgency, pause to consider whether the assumptions driving your action are actually within your knowledge to make.
If guilt is weighing heavily on you, know that sincere remorse is itself a form of moral repair — turn toward God in repentance and commit to doing better.

title: Salama ibn al-Akwa Lists His Military Service with the Prophet
summary: Salama ibn al-Akwa testifies that he participated in seven military expeditions alongside the Prophet and nine others on which the Prophet sent him, naming Abu Bakr and Usama ibn Zayd as two of his commanders.
story: Al-Bukhari then went on to narrate the hadith of Yazid b. Aba Wbayd, from Salama b. al-Akwac, who said, "I participated in seven military expeditions with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and fought also in nine others on which he sent us. On one we were commanded by Aba Bakr, and on another by Usama b. Zayd, may God be pleased with both."
lessons: A life dedicated to service in God's cause is a source of deep personal honour and lasting gratitude.
Remembering and bearing witness to one's experiences of sacrifice and devotion keeps the legacy of righteousness alive for future generations.
Service under righteous leaders is itself a blessing — Salama recognised and named his commanders with respect and gratitude.
practical_advice: Reflect regularly on the meaningful ways you have contributed to causes greater than yourself, and let that reflection renew your sense of purpose.
Express gratitude for the leaders and mentors who have guided you, as Salama honoured Abu Bakr and Usama by name.
When you feel your efforts go unrecognised, remember that a sincere account of your service, even simply told, carries its own dignity.

title: The Death of the Negus and the Prophet's Prayer for Him
summary: The Prophet Muhammad announces the death of the Negus of Abyssinia on the very day he dies, leads the companions in a funeral prayer for him in the mosque, and later confirms that the Negus died as a Muslim.
story: At this point the hafiz al-Bayhaqi recounted the death of the Negus, the ruler of Abyssinia, in a state of being Muslim, the announcement of his death to the Muslims being made by the Messenger of God (SAAS), and the latter's prayer for him. It was narrated through Malik, from al-Zuhri, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyah, from Aba Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) announced the death of the Negus on the day he died, led the people to the mosque where he lined them up and that he then pronounced Allahu Akbar! "God is Most Great", four times. Both these authorities derive their accounts from a hadith of Malik and also from a hadith of al-Layth, from Wqayl, from al-Zuhri, from Sacid and Abi Salama, from Aba Hurayra in similar terms. They both also gave it from a hadith of Ibn Jurayj, from 'Ata', from Jabir, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Today a fine man died; pray for Ashama.'" These accounts have also been quoted and discussed heretofore; and all praise be to God! I note that it seems that the death of the Negus occurred a substantial time before the conquest (of Mecca). In the sahih collection of Muslim, it is said that when he wrote to the foreign rulers, he also wrote to the Negus and that he was not a Muslim. Others, like al-Waqidi, claim that he was. But God knows best. The hafiz al-Bayhaqi narrated through Muslim h. Khalid al-Zanji, from Musa b. Wqba, from his father, from Umm Kulthum, (who said), "When the Prophet (SAAS) married Umm Salama, he told her, 'I have sent to the Negus some ounces of musk and a copper pot as presents, but I believe that he has died. I expect the presents to be returned to me. If that happens' — I believe he said this — 'then I will divide them between you.' Or he may have said, 'and they will be yours'. "And it was as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said. The Negus did die and the presents were returned. He then gave an ounce (of the perfume) to one of his wives and all the rest to Umm Salama."
lessons: Righteousness transcends nationality and religion of birth — the Negus, a Christian king, earned the Prophet's prayers and grief through his goodness and fairness to the early Muslims.
The Prophet's concern extended to all sincere souls regardless of their origin, teaching us that compassion has no borders.
Honouring the dead through prayer and remembrance is a profound act of community solidarity and love.
practical_advice: When someone who supported you or showed you kindness passes away, honour their memory actively — through prayer, words of tribute, or acts of charity in their name.
Let the example of the Negus remind you that goodness lived privately before God is never invisible — it is seen, valued, and rewarded.
In grief, follow the Prophet's example of gathering the community together, because shared mourning lightens individual sorrow.

title: The Breaking of the Hudaybiyya Truce and the Cause of the Conquest of Mecca
summary: Banu Bakr, aided secretly by Quraysh with weapons and fighters, attacks the allied tribe of Khuza'a at night near Mecca, violating the Hudaybiyya truce and triggering Amr ibn Salim's urgent ride to the Prophet to report the aggression.
story: The cause of the conquest, following the truce of al-Hudaybiyya, was as Muhammad b. Ishaq stated. He said, "Al-Zuhri related to me, from Wrwa h. al-Zuhayr, who said that al-Miswar b. Makhrama and Marwan b. al-Hakam both told him as follows, 'It was stipulated in the truce of al-Hudaybiyya that whoever wished to enter into an alliance with Muhammad could do so, and that those wishing to ally with Quraysh could also do that. Thereafter Khuza'a stated that they wished to be allied with the Messenger of God (SAAS) while Banu Bakr joined with Quraysh. "'The truce remained in effect for some 17 or 18 months. But then Banu Bakr attacked Khuza'a at night at a well called al-Watir, close to Mecca. Quraysh, thinking that because it was night and that they would not be observed, assisted Banu Bakr by providing horses and weapons, and they fought along with them in order to express their hatred for the Messenger of God (SAAS). "'When the conflict between Khuza'a and Banu Bakr became intense, Amr b. Salim rode off to the Messenger of God (SAAS) to tell him the news. He had composed some verses on the subject and spoke these to the Messenger of God (SAAS) when he arrived, "O Lord, I address Muhammad and..."'"
lessons: Injustice committed under the cover of darkness or secrecy is never truly hidden from God, and its consequences eventually come into the light.
When covenants are broken through treachery, the wronged party's appeal to righteous authority is itself an act of courage and dignity.
Events that appear to be setbacks — like a broken truce — can become turning points that lead to much greater outcomes, as the conquest of Mecca would soon demonstrate.
practical_advice: When you experience betrayal after a period of peace and trust, resist the urge to retaliate impulsively; seek counsel and bring your grievance to those with the wisdom and authority to respond justly.
If you are suffering an injustice, do not suffer in silence — speak up clearly, as Amr ibn Salim did, even composing his words carefully to convey the urgency of his people's plight.
Trust that broken covenants and oppression rarely go unanswered by God's justice, even when the timing is not in your hands.

title: The Attack on Khuza'a at al-Watir: Banu Bakr and Quraysh Break the Treaty
summary: Banu Bakr, aided by Quraysh with weapons and fighters under cover of night, attacked the Khuza'a tribe at the well of al-Watir, killing them even as they prayed, thus violating the Treaty of Hudaybiyya. This treacherous assault prompted 'Amr b. Salim to ride to the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) to seek his aid.
story: night at a well called al-Watir, close to Mecca. Quraysh, thinking that because it was night and that they would not he observed, assisted Bann Bakr by providing horses and weapons, and they fought along with them in order to express their hatred for the Messenger of God (SAAS). "'When the conflict between Khuza'a and Bann Bakr became intense, (Amr b. Snlim rode off to the Messenger of God (SAAS) to tell him the news. He had composed some verses on the subject and spoke these to the Messenger of God (SAAS) when he arrived, "0Lord, I address Muhammad and invoke hi father's pact with our ancient forebears. You were as children and we as parents and then we accepted Islam and never held back. So Help the Messenger of God with a h a 1 victory, and call for God's servants to come up as reinforcements Including the Messenger of God, accoutred for war; when angry, his face becomes stern. With an army broad as the sea and foaming; @raysh have broken their pacts. They have destroyed their clear treaty with you and sent guards for me up on Mt. Ka& They claim that I will not inspire (to help) yet they are more lowly and fewer in number. They attacked us at night while we slept at al-Watir, and killed us as we bowed in prayer." "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) told h i , "You have been helped, 'Amr h. Sdim!" "'Soon a cloud passed overhead in the sky and the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "This cloud is bringing aid to Bana Ka'b." "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told his men to prepare for battle, but he hid from them his destination. He asked God to blind Quraysh to news of him so that he could surprise them in their own territory."'
lessons: When oppressed people call upon their rightful protectors, help can arrive swiftly and from unexpected signs — a passing cloud became a sign of divine reassurance.
Expressing pain and injustice through sincere appeal, as 'Amr b. Salim did through verse and direct address, is a dignified and effective way to seek support.
A leader who prepares carefully and conceals his strategy demonstrates that trust in God does not contradict wise planning; the Prophet combined prayer with decisive action.
practical_advice: When you feel wronged or overwhelmed by injustice, articulate your pain clearly and bring it to those with the capacity to help — silence rarely resolves oppression.
Look for small signs of hope and divine support in difficult moments, as the companions found reassurance even in a passing cloud.
When facing a serious challenge, combine sincere supplication with careful, strategic preparation rather than waiting passively for relief.

title: The Historical Roots of the Khuza'a–Banu Bakr Conflict and the Violation of the Sacred Territory
summary: Ibn Ishaq traces the deep historical origins of the enmity between Khuza'a and Banu Bakr through successive acts of killing and revenge, culminating in Nawfal b. Mu'awiya's attack on Khuza'a at al-Watir and the shocking violation of the sacred Meccan territory during pursuit.
story: Ibn Ishnq stated, "The reason for the trouble was that a man named M a l i h (Ahbad, of Bana al-Hadrami, allied to al-Aswad h. Rizn, went out on a trading trip. When he was deep in Khuzn'a territory, they attacked h i , killed him and robbed him. Bann Bakr then attacked a man of Bana Khuza'a and killed him. Khuzn'a, just before accepting Islam, had attacked the sons of al-Aswad h. Rizn d-Dili, Salmz, K u l t h m and Dhu'ayb, leaders who were the pride of Bann Kinma, and killed them at 'Arafa at the boundary rocks marking the sacred area. "A man of al-Dil related to me that Banii al-Aswad b. Rizn, due to their high prestige, were paid double blood-wit in the pre-Islamic period, while Banii d-Dil received single blood-wit. "While Banii Bakr and Khuzz'a were engaged in this conflict, Islam came between them. When the agreement at d-Hudaybiyya was made, Bana Bakr made a pact with Quraysh, as did Khuzz% with the Messenger of God (SAAS). Then the truce went into effect. But Banii al-Dil, (a clan) of Banii Bakr, used it to gain revenge from K h u s ' a for the men killed. Nawfal b. Mu'zwiya d-Dili went out at the command of their men; not all of BanG Bakr followed him, however. H e attacked Khuzz'a, killing one of their man, at al-Watir, a well of theirs. The sides then disengaged, then battled on. Quraysh supplied Bann Bakr with weapons and some of them participated in the fighting, using the dark of night inside the sacred to conceal their identity, until eventually they forced K h u z ~ % territory. Once inside it, Bann Bakr pointed out to Nawfal, 'We have entered the sacred territory! (Think of) your God! Your God!' H e (Nawfal) then said an awful thing, 'There is no God today! Bann Bakr, gain your revenge! I swear by my life that you steal within the sacred area, so can you not also gain your revenge?' "Khuz~%took refuge in the house of Budayl b. Warq~'in Mecca and in that Al-Akhzar b. Lu'r al-Dili spoke the of a freed-man of theirs named &ti'. following verses on this subject, 'Have the people of furthest Abyssinia learned that we defeated Banii Ka'b with our arrow heads? We imprisoned them in the home of the slave kfi', and also with Budayl we kept them, inactive. In the home of a base and passive man, once we had satisfied our anger by the use of our swords, we confined them to their great distress, having descended upon them Fie a rain torrent from a mountain gully Slaughtering them like goats, as if we were lions, slashing our swords into them. They had wronged us and acted aggressively and were the first to murder at the sacred boundary, So scared when attacked that they were like ostrich chicks, their flight blocked by Mt. Thawr.' "Budayl b. 'Abd Manat b. Sdama b. 'Amr b. al-Ajabb, known as Budayl b. Umm Asram, answered him back with the following verses: 'A boastful people made a pact, but we have left them only Nzfii as their chief to assemble them. Was it fear of the prime people, whom you disdain, that you went beyond al-Watir, panicking, not to reurn? Every day we favour those we protect by paying the blood-wit, but no one needs to favour us. We attacked your homes at al-TalP'a with our swords that forestalled any complainer's blame. We defended against the tribe's attack from all the way from between Bayd and 'Atnd to the slopes of Mt. RadwB. At the battle at al-Ghamlm W a y s retreated in haste; we terrified him by our brave chief. Perhaps it was because the mother of one of you had diarrhoea that you fled and we did not fight? By God's house, you lie! You did not do battle; but we left your affairs in confusion'":
lessons: Cycles of revenge and retaliation between communities, when left unresolved by justice, spiral into greater harm — this account shows how unresolved historical grievances can erode even formal peace agreements.
The desecration of the sacred territory by Nawfal, who declared 'There is no God today,' illustrates how unchecked anger can cause a person to abandon the very values that define their humanity.
Poetry and public speech in these accounts served as both a record of injustice and a means of processing communal grief and anger — giving voice to pain has always been a human need.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether any ongoing conflicts in your life are being fuelled by unresolved historical grievances; sometimes peace requires confronting and formally closing old wounds rather than waiting for the other side to act first.

Notice when anger or a desire for revenge is pushing you toward actions that violate your own principles — Nawfal's words are a stark warning of how rage can make a person abandon their deepest values.
Find healthy outlets, such as writing, journaling, or speaking with a trusted person, to process feelings of injustice rather than allowing them to accumulate and eventually explode.

title: Budayl b. Warqa' Brings News to the Prophet and Abu Sufyan's Mission to Madinah
summary: After the attack on Khuza'a, Budayl b. Warqa' travelled to the Prophet (SAAS) to report the treachery. Meanwhile, Quraysh, alarmed by what they had done, dispatched Abu Sufyan to Madinah to renew the treaty — but he was rebuffed by the Prophet, Abu Bakr, and Umar, and given only hollow advice by 'Ali.
story: Ibn Ishiiq stated that 'Ahd All& h. Aha Salama related to him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "You can expect to see Abn S u f y a come to you to re-establish the pact and extend its duration." Ihn Ishiiq stated, "Then Budayl h. Warqii' went with a group of Khuza'a to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told how they had been attacked and how Quraysh had joined with Bana Bakr against them. They then left and on their way back, at W s f a , encountered Aba S u f y a . Quraysh had dispatched him to the Messenger of God (SAAS) to reaffirm and extend the pact, since they were now worried by what they had done. "When Aha S u f y a met Budayl he asked him whence he had come, thinking that he had come from the Messenger of God (SAAS). Budayl replied that he had been travelling in that valley and along the coast with Bana Khuza'a. Aba Sufyzn thereafter went to where Budayl's camel had knelt and examined its droppings; he found it to contain date stones. He said, 'I swear, Budayl has been to Muhammad!' "Abii Sufyzn then made his way to Medina to the Messenger of God (SAAS), where he went in to see his own daughter, Umm Habiba. When he was about to sit on the rug of the Messenger of God (SAAS), she roiled it up. He then commented to her, 'I wonder whether you consider this rug undesirable for me or me for it!' She replied, 'It is the mg of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and you are a dirty polytheist! I won't have you sit on his rug!' He responded, 'Well, daughter, you've certainly turned out badly since you left me!' "He then went to see Abii Bakr whom he asked to speak on his behalf with the Messenger of God (SAAS), hut he refused. He next visited Wmar h. al-Khagiih and spoke with h i , but Wmar replied, 'Me intercede for you with the Messenger of God (SAAS)! I swear, if ants were all I had to fight you with, I'd do it!' "Aha Sufy- then made his way to (the home of) 'Ali b. Abii Talib, where , daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS). She was he also found F a ~ a the sitting playing with her son Hasan. Abii S u f y a said, "Ali, you are the man closest in relationship to me. I have come with a request and I don't want to leave disappointed. Please intercede with the Messenger of God (SAAS) on my behalf.' He replied, 'I sympathize, Abii Sufyan, but once the Messenger of God (SAAS) has determined something, we just can't talk to him about it.' Abii Sufyan then turned to Fahma and said, 'Muhammad's daughter, won't you have this son of yours bring protection between our men so that he may then become the lord of all the Arabs till the end of time?' She replied, 'My son is not old enough to bring such protection, and no one could provide protection against the Prophet (SAAS).' "Abii Sufyan then addressed 'Ali, 'I'm really in desperate circumstances; what advice can you give me?' 'Ali replied, 'I swear, I don't know what would help you. But you are chief of Banii Kinma. Why don't you arise in public, guarantee protection against them, then return home?' 'You think that might well do me some good?' be asked. 'Ali replied, 'Well, I really don't, but that's all I think you can do.' "So Abn Sufym stood up in the mosque and said, 'I guarantee protection between our men.' He then mounted his camel and rode off. When he reached Quraysh, they asked what he had accomplished. He reported how he had gone and visited Muhammad and spoken with him but had received no response, gone on to Ibn Abii Quhafa without avail, and then to Wmar whom be had found very antagonistic. He commented that he had found 'Ali the most sympathetic and reported that be had given him advice which he had followed, though without any expectation that it would prove useful. They asked what he had advised. Abii S u f y a reported bow 'Ali had advised offering protection, which he had done. They enquired whether the Prophet (SAAS) had approved that, and Abii Sufym said no. They commented that 'Ali had been toying with him and that Abn Sufyan had gained nothing. H e replied that he had not known what else to do."
lessons: Actions have consequences that cannot be undone by diplomatic appeals alone — Quraysh broke the treaty and then discovered that no amount of negotiation could undo what had been set in motion.
Umm Habiba's firm refusal to let her polytheist father sit on the Prophet's rug illustrates that sincere faith can produce a clarity of conviction that transcends even the deepest family bonds.
Umar's fierce response and 'Ali's honest but gentle acknowledgement that he could do little both reflect different faces of integrity — neither deceived Abu Sufyan with false hope.
practical_advice: When you have wronged someone, understand that rushing to smooth things over diplomatically without genuine accountability is unlikely to succeed — true repair requires honest acknowledgement of the harm caused.

If you find yourself in a position like Abu Sufyan's — going from door to door seeking help after a serious mistake — use that humbling experience as an opportunity for genuine self-reflection rather than frustration.

When people around you are firm in their principles and will not compromise them on your behalf, respect that firmness rather than interpreting it as personal rejection; it can be a sign of their integrity, not their cruelty.

title: Abu Sufyan's Failed Mission to Renew the Treaty
summary: After Banu Bakr violated the Treaty of Hudaybiyya by attacking Banu Ka'b with Quraysh's support, Abu Sufyan traveled to Madinah to seek renewal of the pact, but was turned away by the Prophet, Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Fatima, and Ali in succession, returning to Mecca empty-handed.
story: Miisa h. Wqha said with regard to the conquest of Mecca, "Then Banii Nufatha, of Bann al-Dil, attacked Banii Ka'b during the truce between the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Quraysh. Bans Ka'b were at peace with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and Banii Nufatha were at peace with Quraysh. Banii Bakr gave aid to Bana Nufatha whom Quraysh were aiding with weapons and men. Bana Mudlij kept withdrawn from them and honoured the pact they had made with the Messenger of God (SAAS). In Bann al-Dil there were two men who were their chiefs, SalmZ h. al-Aswad and Kulthiim b. al-Aswad. It is said that those who helped them were Safwsn h. Umayya, Shayba b. Wthman and Suhayl h. 'Amr. "Banii al-Dil made a raid against Banii 'Amr and their dependants, including their women, children and the weak. They fought them and forced them to seek refuge, ultimately making them withdraw to the home of Budayl b. Warqit' in Mecca. "Riders from Bana Ka'b then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him what they had suffered and the part that Quraysh had played in it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told them to go hack home and then to disperse into the land. "Abii Sufyan then left Mecca for the Messenger of God (SAAS), worried at what had happened. He said, 'Muhammad, strengthen the pact and extend it for us.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'That is why you have come? Has something had happened?' 'God forbid! We are keeping to our pact and to the truce of d-Hudaybiyya; we are not changing anything.' "He then left the Messenger of God (SAAS) and went to Abii Bakr, to whom he said, 'Review the pact and extend it for us.' Aha Bakr replied, 'My protection is with the Messenger of God (SAAS). I swear by God, if all I had was ants to fight you with, I'd take their help against you!' "He then went to Wmar h. d-Kha~Zband spoke to him. CUmar replied, 'Nothing new ever happened without God having created it and nothing was ever fundy set that God could not destroy, and nothing was ever cut asunder that God could not join together.' Abii Sufyan commented, 'May you be punished badly by a close relative!' "He then went to see Wthmzn and when he had spoken to him, Wthmm told him, 'My protection rests with that of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' Abn Sufyan then pursued Quraysh nobles and spoke with them, hut all said that their pact was made with the Messenger of God (SAAS). "When he despaired of their help, he went in to see Fatima, daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and spoke with her. She replied, 'I'm just a woman. That is up to the Messenger of God (SAAS).' He told her, 'Then give orders to one of your sons.' She replied, 'But they are just children; they couldn't grant protection.' 'Then speak to 'Ali,' he asked her. She replied, 'You talk to him.' "He did speak to 'Ali, who said to him, LAbnSufym, none of the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) would ever act contrary to his wishes by granting protection. You are the leader of Quraysh, their most important and powerful figure. Give protection on behalf of your tribe.' 'Yes,' Aba Sufysn replied. LYouspeak the truth; I am as you say.' He then left and called out, 'I grant protection. And, by God, I do not believe that anyone will betray me.' "He then went in to see the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, 'Muhammad, I have declared protection between the people and I don't think anyone will betray me by revoking my protection.' 'As ever you say, Abn Hamala,' he told him, and at that Aba Sufym left. "They claim, but God knows best, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said when Abn Sufysn had left, '0God, close over their hearing and their seeing so that they only see us suddenly and only hear us too late!' "Abn Sufyw returned to Mecca and there Quraysh asked him, 'What happened? Did you bring some letter or pact from Muhammad?' 'No, by God,' he replied. 'He refused me that. I watched his men closely and I never saw any people more obedient to a king who ruled them than they were to h i . However, 'Ali b. Abn Tnlib told me to grant them protection without claiming it for myself or my people. He told me I could do this without having it revoked by anyone because of my high status and leadership. I got up and made the announcement, then went in to tell Muhammad that I had done so; I told him that I did not think it would be revoked. He merely said it was I who was saying so.' Quraysh responded, 'You have no reason to be pleased. You return with nothing of any use to you or us. 'Ali was merely toying with you! Your offer of protection was invalid and useless.' "He then went in to see his wife and she told him the same. She exclaimed, 'What a terrible envoy you are! You gained nothing at all.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) saw a cloud and commented, 'These clouds are gently raining down victory for Bann Ka'b!'"
lessons: When those who have wronged others seek to escape accountability through negotiation, justice still takes its course — our duty is to stand firm in principle rather than yield to pressure.
The unity of the believers around righteous leadership is itself a form of strength; when everyone defers to what is right, manipulation finds no foothold.
Sometimes what feels like a failure or dead end is actually God's way of redirecting events toward a greater, divinely ordained outcome.
practical_advice: When someone tries to pressure you into compromising your values or loyalties through social manipulation, recall how every Companion simply redirected Abu Sufyan back to the source of authority — identify your own 'anchor point' and return to it consistently.
If you feel dismissed or rejected after seeking help, reflect honestly on whether your request was just before assuming wrongdoing on others' part.
When you feel powerless against injustice done to you or your community, channel that energy into prayer and preparation rather than panic, trusting that God sees what is hidden.

title: The Prophet Secretly Prepares the Army for the Conquest of Makkah
summary: After the Hudaybiyya truce was broken, the Prophet secretly began preparing his army for the march on Mecca, instructing Aisha to prepare food in secret and praying that Allah blind Quraysh to their movements until the Muslims could arrive by surprise.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed there for that period that God wished after the departure of Abii Sufynn. He began equipping for battle. He told 'A'isha that she should prepare his food, and to hide that fact. "He then went out to the mosque, or for some other purpose, and Aba Bakr came in to see 'A'isha. He found her busy pounding and sifting wheat. Abn Bakr asked her why she was preparing the food but she made no reply. 'Is the Messenger of God (SAAS) preparing for battle?' he asked. She was still silent. He then asked, 'Is he going to attack the "yellow skins",' meaning the Byzantines. She said nothing. He then said, 'Perhaps he is heading for Najd?' She was silent. 'Maybe he is heading for Quraysh then?' She said nothing. "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned, Aba Bakr asked h i , 'Messenger of God, are you leaving on a campaign?' 'Yes,' he replied. LAreyou perhaps heading for the "yellow skins"?' 'No,' he replied. 'Then to Najd?' 'No,' he answered. 'So maybe you are going after Quraysh?' he suggested. 'Yes,' he replied. Ahii Bakr then said, 'But, Messenger of God, don't you have a valid truce with them?' 'Haven't you heard what they did to Banii Ka'b?' he asked. "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) announced to everyone that there was to be a campaign. At that Hacib b. Abii Balta'a wrote a message to Quraysh, but God made His messenger (SAAS) aware of the letter." Muhammad b. Ishaq said that Muhammad b. Jacfar related to him, from Wrwa, from 'A'isha, who said that Abii Bakr came in to see her while she was sifting wheat. Abii Bakr asked her, "What's this? Has the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked you to prepare supplies for a campaign?" ''Yes'', she replied, "to prepare for a campaign." "Where to?" he asked. "He did not tell us anything, except to make preparations." Ihn Ishaq went on to say that the Messenger of God (SAAS) then made an announcement to the people that he was going to Mecca. He ordered them to act seriously and to make preparations. He said, "0 God, take away sight and information from Quraysh so that we may take them by surprise in their land." And so the people made their preparations.
lessons: Strategic wisdom sometimes requires discretion; not every plan needs to be announced prematurely, and protecting a righteous endeavor from sabotage is itself an act of trust in God.
Loyalty and discretion, as shown by Aisha in keeping the secret, are among the highest expressions of trust in a relationship.
When we act with sincerity and preparation while entrusting the outcome to God, we fulfill our part of the covenant with Him.
practical_advice: When working toward an important goal, be thoughtful about what you share and with whom — not out of deception, but to protect what is sacred and still unfolding.
If you are in a position of support to someone on an important mission, your quiet, faithful contribution — like Aisha's silent preparation of food — can be just as valuable as any public act.
Before undertaking any significant effort, follow the Prophet's example of combining thorough preparation with sincere du'a, asking God to clear the path ahead.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit's Poetry Rousing the Muslims Before the Conquest
summary: The poet Hassan ibn Thabit composed stirring verses referencing the killing of Banu Ka'b men in Mecca's valley, condemning the Qurayshi leaders who enabled it and calling the Muslim community to the reality that war had now been made inevitable.
story: (Ibn Ishaq went on) "Hassm b. T h ~ b i spoke the following verses to arouse the people and to refer to the men killed by Khuza'a, 'It hurt me greatly, even though I did not witness, as men of Banii Ka'b were having their heads cut off in the Mecca valley By men who did not unsheathe their swords; and there were many men killed who were unburied. How I wish that my (poetry's) help, with its heat and hurt could harm Suhayl h. 'Amr! And S a f w a , an old fellow who grunts from his arse; this is the time when war had been saddled for battle. Do not feel secure, Ibn Umm Mujalid when war is fully prepared for and about to break out. But don't worry about it, for our swords will have their impact and death's door will open wide!'"
lessons: Righteous anger at injustice, when channeled constructively — into words, action, or mobilization — can serve the greater good rather than leading to destructive rage.
The arts, including poetry, have historically served as a legitimate means of giving voice to communal grief and galvanizing moral courage.
Acknowledging pain openly, as Hassan did in saying 'it hurt me greatly even though I did not witness,' validates collective suffering and is the first step toward healing and action.
practical_advice: When you feel angry about an injustice done to yourself or others, find a constructive outlet — writing, speaking, organizing — rather than letting that anger fester inwardly or erupt destructively.
Allow yourself to grieve losses even when you were not a direct witness; empathy for others' pain is spiritually valuable and does not need to be earned by personal experience.
If you feel the urge to speak up about a wrong, consider how your words can move others toward something better rather than simply expressing bitterness.

title: Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a Sends a Secret Letter to Quraysh
summary: On the eve of the march to Mecca, the companion Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a secretly sent a letter to Quraysh warning them of the Prophet's plans, motivated by fear for his family living among them, but Allah revealed the act to the Prophet who sent Ali and Zubayr to intercept the letter-bearer.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated that it was related to h i by Muhammad h. Jacfar, from Wrwa b. al-Zuhayr and others, from various of our scholars, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) had decided to attack Mecca, HaGh b. Abii Balta'a wrote to Quraysh informing them of how the Messenger of God (SAAS) was about to move against them. He gave the letter to a woman; Muhammad b. Ja'far claimed her to have been of Muzayna, while others maintained that she was Sara, a freed-woman of someone from Banii 'Abd al-Mu~alih. He paid her to take it to Quraysh and she placed it on her head, tied it up in her hair and left with it. Ibn Ishaq went on, "News came from heaven to the Messenger of God (SAAS) of what H a ~ i bhad done and he sent 'Ali b. Aha Talib and al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwa in pursuit, telling them, 'Seize a woman with whom Hatih h. Aha Balta'a has sent a letter to Quraysh warning them of what we have decided to do about them.' "The two men caught up with her at al-Halifa of Bana Aba Ahmad. They made her dismount and searched her baggage but found nothing. 'Mi then told her, 'I swear by God, the Messenger of God (SAAS) has not been misinformed, nor has he lied to us. Either you produce this letter for us, or we will search you for it!' When she saw how serious he was, she told him to turn away and untied her hair, took out the letter and gave it to him. He took it to the Messenger of God (SAAS). "The Messenger of God (SAAS) summoned Hauh and asked h i , 'Haub, what made you do this?' He replied, 'I really am, I swear, a true believer in God and in His messenger. I have not changed at all. But I'm not a man with any claims to nobility or tribal influence and there, living among Quraysh, I have my son and family and I wanted to protect them.' "Wmar h. al-Khat@b said, 'Messenger of God, let me cut off his head! The man has turned hypocrite!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him, 'What makes you so sure, Wmar? Perhaps God looked down upon those who fought at Badr and said, "Do as you wish; I forgive you!"' "It was about Hatib that God revealed, '0YOU who believe: do not consider...'"
lessons: Fear for those we love can lead us to make choices that compromise our integrity, but honest accountability before God and community — not concealment — is the path back to wholeness.

The Prophet's response to Hatib's betrayal, citing his presence at Badr rather than calling for punishment, teaches us that a person's full history and sincerity of faith must be weighed before passing judgment.
Divine knowledge encompasses what is hidden; those who place their ultimate trust in God rather than in secret schemes will find their affairs better protected.
practical_advice: When fear for your loved ones tempts you toward a compromise of your values, pause and ask whether the action you are considering will truly protect them or simply create a new and greater danger.
If you have made a mistake out of fear or misguided love rather than malice, own it fully and honestly — as Hatib did — rather than compounding the error with denial.

When you are tempted to judge another person's faith or character based on a single failure, remember the Prophet's example with Hatib: consider the full person, their history, and the sincerity behind their actions.

title: Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a's Letter to Quraysh and the Prophet's Forgiveness
summary: Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a secretly sent a letter to the Quraysh warning them of the Prophet's planned march on Mecca. The Prophet intercepted the letter, confronted Hatib, and ultimately forgave him due to his participation at Badr, with a Quranic verse revealed about the incident.
story: messenger. I have not changed at all. But I'm not a man with any claims to nobility or tribal influence and there, living among Quraysh, I have my son and family and I wanted to protect them.' "Wmar h. al-Khat@b said, 'Messenger of God, let me cut off his head! The man has turned hypocrite!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him, 'What makes you so sure, Wmar? Perhaps God looked down upon those who fought at Badr and said, "Do as you wish; I forgive you!"' "It was about Hatib that God revealed, '0YOU who believe: do not consider My enemies and yours to be allies to whom you give friendship"' @rat al-Mumtahana; LX, v.1). Ibn Ishaq related this story as being mursal, with an incomplete line of authorities. Al-Suhayli narrated that in Hatib's letter it said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) may well send forth an army against you that will descend upon you like nightfall. And I swear by God, that even if he were to attack you alone, then God would give him victory over you. He fulfils what He promised him." Al-Suhayli went on, "And in the Tafsir (Exegesis) of Ibn S a l l a it is stated that Hatib wrote, 'Muhammad has assembled an army for war; he is either heading for you or for others. You should beware!'" Al-Bukhzri stated that Quraysh related to him, quoting Sufya, from 'Amr b. Dinzr, who said that al-Hasan b. Muhammad told him that he heard Wbayd Allah h. Abii &fit say that he heard 'Ali relate, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched me, al-Zubayr and al-Miqdad saying, 'Go to the well at Khakh. There you will find a woman in a howdah who has a letter. Take it from her.' So we left, our horses racing us along, and we arrived at the well, where we found the woman in the howdah. We told her to produce the letter, hut she denied having it. We then told her that if she did not produce it, we would strip her clothes off her. "She then brought it forth from her hair wrappings and we took it to the Messenger of God (SAAS). The letter said, 'From Hacib b. Balta'a to the polytheists of Mecca, informing them of some of the actions of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) asked Hahb about this and he replied, 'Messenger of God, don't act harshly with me! I was just associated with Quraysh, allied with them but not of them. Some of your muhajzrin have relatives among Quraysh who can look after their families and possessions. What I wanted was to fmd someone among them who would protect my relatives. I did not do this to apostatize from my faith, nor to express my acceptance of disbelief after Islam.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Well, he has spoken the truth to you.' Wmar asked, 'Messenger of God, let me shike off the head of this hypocrite!' (He replied) LButhe fought at Badr. And what makes you so sure; perhaps God looked down upon those who fought at Badr and said, "Do as you wish; I forgive you."' "God then sent down, '0 YOU who believe, do not consider My enemies' up to the words, 'has strayed from the right path.'" (siirat al-Mumtahana; LX, v.1). The other compilers of the tradition also gave it, except for Ibn Maja, from a hadith of Sufym b. Wyayna. Al-Tirmidhi stated that it was hasan, sahih, "good and authentic". Imzm Ahmad said that it was related to him by Hujayn and Ynnus, both of whom said that Layth h. Sa'd related to him, from Abn al-Zubayr, from Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, that Harib b. Abn Balta'a wrote to the people of Mecca telling them that the Messenger of God (SAAS) intended to attack them. However, the Messenger of God (SAAS) was provided information about the woman who had the letter and so he sent after her and retrieved the letter from her head. He then asked HaGb whether he really did this and he confessed. He explained, "I did not do it to betray the Messenger of God (SAAS), nor am I a hypocrite. I knew that God would make His messenger prevail and allow him to complete his mission. However, I've always been a stranger amidst them (Quraysh) and my mother is there with them. And I wanted to have some favour with them." Wmar asked, "Should I cut off this fellow's head?" But the Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "Would you really kill someone who fought at Badr? What makes you sure; perhaps God looked down at those who fought at Badr and said, 'Do as you wish.'" Only Imam &mad gives this tradition from this line of transmission; its chain of authorities meets the criteria of Muslim. And all praise be to God!
lessons: Past righteous deeds and sincere service to God carry great weight; Hatib's participation at Badr earned him mercy even after a serious error, reminding us that God considers the totality of a person's life and faith.

Human weakness — such as fear for one's family — does not necessarily nullify sincere faith; the Prophet recognised Hatib's true motive and judged accordingly, teaching us to look for the deeper context before judging others harshly.

Divine forgiveness is vast and not to be underestimated; the Prophet's rhetorical question about God's mercy to the fighters of Badr invites believers to remain hopeful in God's pardon even after grave mistakes.
practical_advice: When you have made a mistake driven by fear or love for your family, honestly explain your true motivation rather than hiding it; sincerity in acknowledging a lapse is often what opens the door to forgiveness.

Before rushing to judge someone who has erred, pause and ask yourself whether you know their full situation and history; channel the energy of righteous anger into seeking clarity rather than immediate punishment.

If you feel overwhelmed by guilt over a past wrongdoing, remind yourself of your sincere deeds and ongoing commitment to faith; use that foundation as a reason to return to God rather than as a comparison that breeds complacency.

title: The Prophet's Departure for the Conquest of Mecca and the Rulings on Fasting While Travelling
summary: The Prophet set out from Medina in Ramadan of 8 AH with a large army of 10,000 Muslims toward Mecca, fasting initially before breaking his fast at al-Kadid, establishing a flexible precedent for fasting during travel that was witnessed by his companions.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that Muhammad b. Muslim b. Shihab al-Zuhri related to him, from Wbayd All& b. 'Abd Allah b. Wtba, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then proceeded on his journey, having appointed Aha Ruhm Kulthiim h. Huvayn h. Wtha h. Khalaf al-Ghifm in command of Mediua. He departed on the 10th of Ramadan and fasted, as did those with him. Having arrived at al-Kadid, between Wsfan and Amaj, he hroke his fast and thereafter travelled on to Marr al-Zahm, where he and 10,000 Muslims made camp." Wrwa h. al-Zuhayr stated, "He had 12,000 men with him." Al-Zuhri and Miisa b. 'Uqha said the same. Sulaym totalled over 700 men; some say they were over 1,000 strong. Muzayna were over 1,000 strong, and each tribe was represented in both force and faith in Islam. The muhZjrriin and the an@ accompanied the Messenger of God (SAAS) en masse, none of them remaining behind. Al-Bukhzri narrated a similar account from M a h i i d , from 'Ahd al-Razaq, from Macmar, from al-Zuhri. Al-Bayhaqi narrated, from a hadith of 'A~imb. 'Ah, from al-Layth h. Sacd, from Wqayl, from al-Zuhri (who said), "'Uhayd Allah h. 'Ahd Allah informed me, from Ibn 'Ahbas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth on the expedition that led to the conquest in Ramadin." Al-Zuhri went on, "I heard Sa'id b. al-Musayyah make a similar statement, that he did not know whether he started out with some days left of Sha'bin and that then Ramadan arrived, or whether he left in Ramadin, after it had begun. However, 'Uhayd Allah h. 'Ahd Allah informed me that Ihn 'Ahhas said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) fasted until he reached al-Kadid, the well between Qudayd and Wsfin, where he hroke the fast. And he continued to eat regularly (without fasting) throughout the month.'" Ai-Bukhi%ri related this from 'Ahd All& b. Ytisuf, from al-Layth. He, however, did not mention the confusion between Sha'ban and Ramadin. Al-Bukhari stated, that 'Ali b. 'Ahd Allah related to us, quoting Jarir, from Mausnr, from Mujahid, from Tawus, that Ibn 'Abhas said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) set out in Ramad%, fasting until he reached Wsfin. Then he called for a vessel and drank from it, allowing the people to see this. He continued to eat normally until he reached Mecca." He went on, "Ibn 'Ahhas used to say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) both fasted and ate normally while travelling. Those who wished, fasted; those who wished, ate normally.'" Yiinus quoted Ibn Ish%q, from al-Zuhri, from 'Ubayd Allah h. 'Ahd Allah as stating that Ibn 'Ahhas said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) set forth on the conquest of Mecca, he placed Ahii Ruhm Kulthiim b. al-Husayn al-Ghifgri in command of Medina. He left on the 10th of Ramadin, fasting, as did those accompanying him. When he arrived at al-Kadid, between 'Usfun and Amaj, he hroke his fast. When he entered Mecca, he was eating normally and people saw that this was his latter practice, having abrogated his former, his fasting." Al-Bayhaqi stated, "His statement, 'he left on the 10th of Ramadan' is interpolated in the hadirh, as is his reference to 'Uhayd Allah h. Idris, quoting Ibn Ishaq." He then related through Ya'qab b. Sufym, from Jabir, from Y&ya, from Sadaqa, that Ibn Ishaq said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) left on the 10th of Ramadm, 8 AH." Al-Bayhaqi narrated from the hadith of Aba Ishaq d-Fazw, from Muhammad b. Aba Hafva, from d-Zuhri, from Wbayd All& b. 'Abd Allah, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The conquest occurred 13 days into Ramad&." Al-Bayhaqi then commented, "This dating is fanciful; it relies only on al-Zuhri's statement." He went on to narrate through Ibn Wahb, from Yanus, from al-Zuhri, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went out from Medina in Ramadan on the campaign for the conquest - the conquest of Mecca. He was accompanied by 10,000 Muslims. This coincided with the beginning of 8 and one-half years following his arrival at Media. Mecca was conquered with 13 days remaining of Ramadm." Al-Bayhaqi also narrated through 'Abd al-Razzaq, from Ma'mar, from al-Zubri, from Wbayd AH& b. 'Abd Allah, from Ibn 'Abbs, (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) left in Ramad&, accompanied by 10,000 Muslims. He fasted until he reached al-Kadid, then he broke his fast. Al-Zuhri stated, "Only the most recent being acceptable, then that would he what follows." He said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went to Mecca on the morning of the 13th of Ramadan." He then recounted the conquest in accord with both rahih collections, through 'Abd Al-Razzaq. But God knows best. Al-Bayhaqi narrated through Sacid b. 'Abd d-'Aziz al-Tantimi, from 'Ayiyya h. Qays, from Aba Sa'id al-Khudari, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) told us to set forth in the year of the conquest on the 2nd of Ramadan. We fasted en route until we reached d-Kadid, where the Messenger of God (SAAS) advised us to break the fast, being pleased with both those who continued fasting and those who did not. When we reached the first staging post where we encountered the enemy, he told us to break the fast, and we all did so." Imam A b a d related it from Abii al-Mughira, from Sa'id b. 'Abd al-'Aziz (who said) that 'Auyya b. Qays related to him, from someone who had told it to him, who quoted Aha Sa'id al-Khudari, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) told us to set forth in the year of the conquest on the 2nd of Ramadm. We fasted en route until we reached al-Kadid, where the Messenger of God (SAAS) told us to break the fast. Thereafter some continued fasting, while others did not. When we reached the first staging post where we encountered the enemy, he told us to break the fast, and we all did so." I note from al-Zuhri's account that the conquest was on the 13th of Ramad&, and from that of Aba Sa'id that they left Medina on the 2nd of Ramad&; their trip between Mecca and Medina must therefore have taken 11 days. However, d-Bayhaqi related, from Abii al-Husayn h. d-Fadl, from 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far, from Ya'qab b. Sufy&, from al-Hasan h. d-Rabi', from Ibn Idris, from Muhammad h. Ishaq, from al-Zuhri, and Muhammad b. 'Ali b. al-Husayn, and 'Asim b. Wmar b. Qatada and 'Amr b. Sbu'ayb, and 'Abd All& b. Abn Bakr and others, all said that the conquest of Mecca took place with 10 days remaining of Ramadm in 8 AH. Ahn Da'ttd al-Taydisi stated that Wahib related to him, from Ja'far b. Muhammad, from his father, from Jabir, from 'Abd Alhb, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth in the year of the conquest and was fasting until he reached Kuracd-Ghamim. Some of the men accompanying h i were on foot, whie others rode; this"
lessons: Islam's religious obligations are designed with human capacity in mind; the Prophet's flexibility in fasting during the journey to Mecca demonstrates that God does not intend hardship for believers, especially in times of physical and strategic exertion.

Leadership requires practical wisdom alongside spiritual devotion; by publicly breaking his fast and signalling that companions could choose either way, the Prophet modelled how to balance religious observance with practical necessity without imposing unnecessary burden on others.

Great historical undertakings — even those with the highest spiritual significance — require meticulous preparation and collective effort; the united march of the Muhajirun and Ansar with none remaining behind shows how solidarity amplifies both strength and blessing.
practical_advice: When facing a physically or emotionally demanding period, remember that Islam permits concessions during hardship; give yourself permission to take the easier lawful option without guilt, just as the Prophet allowed companions to break their fast during the march.

In group settings where people have different capacities, follow the Prophet's example of being pleased with both those who push through and those who ease up, and resist pressuring others to match your level of endurance.

Before embarking on a major life challenge, ensure you have delegated responsibilities at home adequately, just as the Prophet appointed a trusted leader over Medina before departing, so that your absence does not create chaos for those who depend on you.

title: Breaking the Fast During the Journey to the Conquest of Mecca
summary: During the march to conquer Mecca in Ramadan, the Prophet broke his fast to signal permission for the men, and called those who continued fasting 'disobedient.'
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth in the year of the conquest and was fasting until he reached Kura'd-Ghamim. Some of the men accompanying him were on foot, while others rode; this journey was during Ramadan. He was told, 'Messenger of God, the men are suffering from fasting, but are watching to see what you will do.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then called for a vessel of water and drank, while the men watched. Some of the men then broke the fast, while others continued it. When he was told that some men were still fasting, he commented, 'They're disobedient.' Muslim narrated it from a hadith of al-Thaqafi and al-Darawardi, from Ja'far b. Muhammad. Imam Ahmad narrated from a hadith of Muhammad b. Ishaq, who said that Bashir b. Yasa related to him, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) left in the year of the conquest in Ramadan. He fasted, as did the Muslims with him, until he reached al-Kadid. There he called for water in a wooden cup, while he was mounted. He drank while the men looked on. This was to inform them that he had broken his fast. The Muslims then broke their fast.' Ahmad was alone in giving this hadith.
lessons: Following the example of the Prophet is a form of obedience; clinging to personal piety while ignoring prophetic guidance can become a form of stubbornness.
Flexibility and mercy toward oneself and others is part of the prophetic example, especially in times of hardship.
Leaders communicate guidance through their actions, not just their words, and followers should remain attentive and responsive.
practical_advice: When you are struggling to keep up with a rigorous spiritual or physical commitment during a difficult period, give yourself permission to ease the burden — this is not weakness but wisdom.
Pay attention to the example set by those you trust and respect, and do not hold onto a practice out of pride when a wiser path is being shown.
If you find yourself justifying continued hardship as devotion, ask whether your intention is sincerely for God or subtly for self-image.

title: The Rabbit at Marr al-Zahran
summary: While camped at Marr al-Zahran, the companions flushed out a rabbit which Abu Talha slaughtered and sent its thighs and legs to the Prophet, who accepted them.
story: In both sahih collections Anas is quoted as saying, 'We disturbed a rabbit while we were at Marr al-Zahran and the men took it to Abu Talha who slaughtered it. He sent its thighs and legs to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he accepted them.'
lessons: Generosity flows naturally from a heart of gratitude — even a small, spontaneous gift offered sincerely is worthy of acceptance and appreciation.
The Prophet's acceptance of simple, ordinary gifts from his companions showed his humility and the value he placed on his community's affection.
Even in the midst of great historical events, the small and human moments of daily life carry their own spiritual significance.
practical_advice: Do not hold back from offering a small, genuine gift or act of kindness out of fear that it is too insignificant — sincerity makes every gift meaningful.
Practice gratitude for the simple provisions that come your way, especially during stressful or demanding periods of life.
Look for opportunities to share what you have, even unexpectedly — spontaneous generosity strengthens bonds and lifts spirits.

title: Abu Sufyan's Conversion and the Night Before the Conquest of Mecca
summary: Abu Sufyan, accompanied by Hakim ibn Hizam and Budayl ibn Warqa, encounters the Prophet's forces at Marr al-Zahra before the Conquest of Mecca. He is brought before the Prophet, spends the night under al-Abbas's protection, and ultimately accepts Islam after witnessing the discipline and devotion of the Muslim army.
story: in to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who talked with them in the course of the night and then invited them to wimess that there is no god but God. They did so and he then asked them to bear wimess that "Muhammad is the Messenger of God". Hakim and Budayl testified to this, but Aba Sufysn said, "I do not know this." After morning came, he did accept Islam. They then asked him (the Prophet (SAAS)) to grant shall Quraysh safety. He replied, "Those who enter the house of Aha S u f y ~ be safe," his house being in the heights of Mecca. He went on, "Whoever enters the house of Hakim h. H i z m shall be safe," his house being in the lower part of Mecca. And he added, "Whoever locks his own door shall he safe." AlLCAbbns's account states, "Then Wmar hurried off towards the Messenger of God (SAAS), while I rode the mule and anived before him, but only by the amount that a slow-moving mule can outpace a slow-moving man. I jumped off the mule and went in to see the Messenger of God (SAAS). Wmar did the same and said, 'Messenger of God, this is Aha Sufyan whom God has delivered without any pact or agreement! Let me cut off his head!' I said, 'Messenger of God, I have given him my protection.' I then sat down with the Messenger of God (SAAS), holding his (Ahii Sufyan's) head and said, 'I swear, no man apart from myself is williig to protect him tonight!' "When Wmar kept on complaining about him, I told him, 'Take it easy, 'Umar! I swear, if he were from Bann 'Adi h. Ka'b you'd not he saying this! But you well know that he is from Bann 'Ahd Manaf.' 'Take it easy yourself,' (Abhas,' he replied. 'Your accepting Islam the day you did was more welcome to me than if al-Khapb' (his father) 'had been the one to do so! And this was only because I knew that your accepting Islam was more welcome to the Messenger of God (SAAS) than that of al-Khagab would have been.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Take him away to your tent, 'Abbas, and bring him to me when morning comes.'" AILCAhbas continued, "So I went off with him to my tent where he spent the night with me. Next morning I hurried him over to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who said, when he saw him, 'Woe on you, Abii Sufyan! Is it not time for you to know that there is no god but God?' He replied, 'How wise, nohle and kind you are! Had there been any other besides God he would have already given me some aid!' "'Well, Abii Sufym,' continued the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'is it not time for you to know that I am the Messenger of God?' 'How wise, noble and kind you are', AbO Sufym replied, 'bur I'm still having some trouble with that.'" Al-'Ahhas then told him, "Woe on you, accept Islam! Bear wimess that there is no god hut God and that Muhammad is the Messenger of God, before your head is cut offl" He then gave true testimony, accepting Islam. AILCAhhas went on, "So I said, 'Messenger of God, Aha Sufym is a man to whom prestige is important; do something for him.' "He then stated, 'Yes; whoever enters Aba Sufyan's house shall he safe.'" Wrwa added that he went on to say, "Whoever enters the house of Hakim h. H i z a shall be safe." And Miisa h. Wqha stated similarly, from al-Zuhri, that he said, "And whoever locks his own door shall be safe, as will be anyone who goes into the mosque." As he was about to depart, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to al-'Ahb%, "Keep him at where the valley narrows, and the mountain projects, until God's forces pass him by, so that he sees them." Mas3 h. Wqha narrated, from al-Zuhri, that Ahii Sufym, Budayl and Hakim h. H i z m stood along with al-'Ahha at the mountain projectory, and that when Sa'd h. Ubada spoke the verse, "Today is that of the great battle; today sanctuary is disallowed" to Aba Sufyzn, he complained to the Messenger of God (SAAS). The latter then took back the banner of the away from Sa'd and gave it to al-Zubayr b. al-'AwwSm. He took it in to the upper pan of Mecca and planted it in al-Hajiin. Khelid entered from the lower part of Mecca, where Bana Bakr and Hudhayl confronted him. He killed 20 men of Banii Bakr and 3 or 4 of Hudhayl. They were defeated and killed at al-Hazwara, the fighting there reaching the door of the mosque. Al-'Abb% went on, "I proceeded out with Aba Sufyan, keeping him in the narrow part of the valley to which the Messenger of God (SAAS) had asked me to restrict him. "The tribes went past flying their banners. As one went by, Abii Sufyxn asked, 'Who are those, 'Abbns?' I replied, 'Sulaym,' and he commented, 'Well, Sulaym don't concern me.' When another passed, he asked who they were and I told him they were Muzayna. He commented, 'Muzayna don't concern me.' This went on until all the tribes had gone by; he asked me about each one, and he always replied that they were of no concern to him. Eventually the Messenger of God (SAAS) went past with his 'green squadron' that consisted of muhajirin and ansar, their armour allowed only their eyes to be seen. He then exclaimed, 'Glory be to God, 'Abbas! Who are those?' I replied, 'That is the Messenger of God (SAAS) with his muhajrin and his ansar.' He commented, 'No one could ever withstand those men! By God, Ahii al-Fadl, your brother's domain this morning is immense!' "I replied, 'Abii Sufyan, it is that of the prophethood.' "'I agree,' he said. I told him, 'You should hurry to your people.'" (AILCAbbas continued) "When he reached them he shouted at the top of his voice, 'Quraysh, this is Muhammad who brings a force against you that you cannot withstand. Whoever goes into the house of Abti Sufyan will be secure.' "Hind, daughter of 'Utba, stood up to him and gripped him by the mustache and said, 'Kill this overweight bag of fat! He is too disgraceful to lead our people!' "Abii Sufyan replied, 'Woe upon you, do not allow this woman to deceive you. You cannot withstand the force against you. Whoever enters the house of Abii Sufym will be secure.' "People shouted, 'God kill you! What good will your house do us?' "He replied, 'Those who lock their doors will be secure. Those who enter the mosque will he secure.' "The people then dispersed to their homes or into the mosque." Wrwa b. al-Zubayr related that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by, Abii Sufyzn asked him, "I see many faces I do not recognize. Are all these faces against me?" The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "It is you and your people who have done this; these people believed me when you called me a liar. They assisted me when you exiled me." Ahn Sufyan then complained to him how Sa'd b. Wbsda had spoken the following line as he passed him, "Today is that of the great battle; today sanctua.ry is disallowed." At this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "Sa'd lied; this is a day when God is glorifying the ka'ba, a day by which the ka'ba will be adorned." Wrwa related that in the early morning after that night he spent with al-'Abbas, Abii Sufym saw the people gathering for prayer, spreading forth to perform their ablutions and he felt afraid. He asked al-'Ahbas, "What are they doing?" He replied, "They have heard the call to prayer and are spreading out to perform the prayer." When the prayer began and he saw them prostrating and bowing along with him (the Prophet (SAAS)), Abn Sufym asked, "Would they do anything he asked of them?" "Yes, they would," he replied. "If he ordered them to give up food and drink, they would obey him." Mnsa b. Wqba related, from al-Zuhri, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed his ablutions, the men scrambled for the leftover water with which he had washed and Abii Sufym commented, "'Abb&, I've never seen anything like tonight, not even in the kingdoms of Chosroe or Caesar!" The hndfiz al-Bayhaqi related from al-H&im and others, from al-A~amm, from Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, from Yiinus b. Bukayr, from Ibn Is@q, who said that al-Husayn b. 'Abd All& b. Whayd All& b. 'Abbns related to him, from Ibn 'Abhs (a certain hadirh).
