The last-mentioned then proceeded to narrate this story in its entirety, just as Ziyad d-Bakb'i related it, from Ibn Is@q, munqatican, with an incomplete line of transmission. But God knows best. However, al-Bayhaqi narrated through Abii Bild d-Ashcari, from Ziyad al-Bakka'i, from Muhammad b. Ishsq, from d-Zuhri, from Whayd All&, who quoted Ibn 'Abbs as saying that al-'Abbss brought Abii Sufyan to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and so on. However, in this account he is quoted as saying that Abii Sufyan accepted Islam that night, before he came next morning to the Messenger of God (SAAS). This account also says that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, "Whoever enters Abn Sufyan's house will he secure," Aba Sufym asked, "But what if my house is not large enough?" The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, "And whoever enters the kacba will be secure." "And what if the kacba is not large enough?" Abii Sufym enquired. He replied, "And whoever enters the mosque shall be secure." "What if the mosque is not large enough?" He replied, "Whoever locks his door will be secure." Abn Sufysn then said, "There will he sufficient space." Al-Bukhm stated that Whayd b. Ismncil related to him, quoting Abii Usama, from Hishw, from his father, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) set forth in the year of the conquest of Mecca and this news reached Quraysh, Abii Sufyan h. Harb, Hakim b. Hizsm and Budayl b. Warqa' came out to look for the Messenger of God (SAAS). They travelled as far as Marr al-Zahra where they saw so many camp fires that they seemed like those on Mt. 'Arafxt (in the pilgrimage season). Abii Sufyan commented on this and asked whose fires they could he and Budayl h. Warqa' responded that they must he the fires of Bann 'Amr. Abn Sufyan objected that they were fewer in number. The two men were seen by the guards of the Messenger of God (SAAS), who seized them and took them to him. Aba Sufym then accepted Islam. "As Aba Sufym was returning home, he (the Prophet (SAAS)) said to al-'Abh%, 'Keep Aba Sufym where the mountain projects so that he sees the Muslims.' AILCAbbn s did restrain him there while the tribes went past, squadron by squadron, with the Messenger of God (SAAS). As one squadron went by, Aba Sufym asked al-'Abbas who they were. He replied, 'These are Ghifar.' 'Well, what are Ghifzr to me?' Aba Sufym commented. Then Juhayna passed by and he said the same. Sa'd b. Hudhaym and Sulaym then passed and he made the same comment. Then another squadron passed that was unlike the others and he asked their identity. Al-'Abbas replied, 'They are the anszr under the command of Sa'd h. Wbnda with the banner.' Sa'd called out, 'Hey, Abii Sufya, today's the day of the great battle; today the ka'ha will he no sanctuary!' Aba Sufym commented, "Abbas, would that this were a day of honour!' "Then there passed a squadron smaller than the rest;"
lessons: Even the most hardened opponents of truth can find their way to faith when they witness sincerity, mercy, and overwhelming evidence of God's guidance. Abu Sufyan's gradual conversion shows that hearts can change at any stage of life.

The Prophet's response to his former enemy — granting safety to Abu Sufyan's household and honoring him publicly — demonstrates that mercy and strategic wisdom are not opposites but deeply complementary virtues.
Witnessing the unity, discipline, and devotion of the Muslim community had more impact on Abu Sufyan than any argument. A community that lives its values is itself a form of dawah.
practical_advice: When you feel your faith wavering or are slow to accept a truth you sense is right, reflect on the signs around you — the lives of sincere believers, the coherence of their community, and the moral clarity of their practice — rather than waiting for certainty to arrive on its own.

If someone you care about is resistant to positive change, practice the patience of al-Abbas: protect them, give them time and space, and trust that guided exposure to truth is more powerful than confrontation.

When you find yourself in a position of power or advantage over someone who once wronged you, consider the Prophetic model: respond with magnanimity rather than revenge. This is both spiritually elevated and practically effective in winning hearts.

title: The Army Enters Mecca: Sa'd b. 'Ubada's Proclamation and the Prophet's Response
summary: As the Muslim army entered Mecca, Sa'd b. 'Ubada proclaimed it a day of great battle and that the Ka'ba would be no sanctuary, but the Prophet corrected him, declaring it a day of honour and glory for the Ka'ba.
story: are Ghifzr to me?' Aba Sufym commented. Then Juhayna passed by and he said the same. Sa'd b. Hudhaym and Sulaym then passed and he made the same comment. Then another squadron passed that was unlike the others and he asked their identity. Al-'Abbas replied, 'They are the anszr under the command of Sa'd h. Wbnda with the banner.' Sa'd called out, 'Hey, Abii Sufya, today's the day of the great battle; today the ka'ha will he no sanctuary!' Aba Sufym commented, "Abbas, would that this were a day of honour!' "Then there passed a squadron smaller than the rest; the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions were with them, his banner being borne by al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwb. As the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by Abn Sufym, the latter called out, 'Do you know what Sa'd b. W n d a said?' 'What did he say?' asked the Messenger of God (SAAS). Aba Sufyan repeated it and the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Sa'd lied; this is a day when God will assert the glory of the ka'ha, a day when the ka'ha will be adorned.' He then ordered that his banner he planted at al-Hajan."
lessons: True leadership requires correcting misguided zeal even among one's own allies, prioritising mercy and honour over vengeance.
Victory granted by God calls for gratitude and reverence, not arrogance or aggression.
The purpose of divine support is to honour what is sacred, not to satisfy personal or tribal grievances.
practical_advice: When you feel a surge of anger or triumph, pause and ask whether your intended action honours God or merely satisfies your ego.
If someone in your circle voices hostility during a moment of power, gently but firmly correct them rather than staying silent.
Channel feelings of strength and success into gratitude and constructive action, not into intimidation of others.

title: Khalid b. al-Walid Enters the Upper Part of Mecca; Two Companions Martyred
summary: The Prophet ordered Khalid b. al-Walid to enter Mecca from the upper part while he himself entered from below; during Khalid's advance, two cavalrymen, Hubaysh b. al-Ash'ar and Kurz b. Jabir al-Fihri, were killed.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered Khdid h. al-Walid to enter the upper part of Mecca, at Kadx', while the Messenger of God (SAAS) went in from Kada. That day two of the cavalry under Khalid h. al-Walid, Hubaysh h. al-Ash% and Kurz b. Jabir al-Fihri were killed.
lessons: Even in moments of great victory, loss and sacrifice remind us that every blessing comes at a price paid by others.
Those who give their lives in service of a just cause are honoured and remembered, even when their names are few.
Trusting in God's plan does not mean being shielded from all hardship; faith sustains us through loss as much as through triumph.
practical_advice: When you experience success, take a moment to remember those who sacrificed so that moment could come.
Do not let grief over loss overshadow gratitude for what has been achieved; hold both emotions at once with honesty.
In moments of stress or danger, focus on your role and trust that each person's contribution matters, even when outcomes are painful.

title: Abu Sufyan Accepts Islam and the Prophet Grants Sanctuary to His House
summary: Al-'Abbas brought Abu Sufyan to the Prophet at Marr al-Zahran where he accepted Islam, and the Prophet, at al-'Abbas's request, granted him dignity by declaring that whoever entered Abu Sufyan's house would be safe.
story: Ibn 'Abbs who said that his father al-'Abb% b. 'Abd al-Mufplih brought Aha Sufym b. Harb to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at M a n al-Zamn and he accepted Islam. AlLCAbb2stold him, "Messenger of God, Aha Sufym is a man who likes to enjoy prestige; perhaps you could do something for h i ." "Yes," he replied. "Whoever goes into Abii Sufym's house will be secure. And whoever locks his door will be secure."
lessons: A leader's greatness is shown in how they treat a former enemy who humbles himself and seeks peace.
Interceding on behalf of someone, even a former adversary, reflects the highest qualities of compassion and social intelligence.
Granting dignity to those who surrender is a means of healing communities and preventing future conflict.
practical_advice: When someone who has wronged you sincerely changes course, consider how you can acknowledge that change without completely forgetting the past.
Advocate for those in your community who are vulnerable or in need of protection, even if they are not your closest allies.
If you are the one who has erred, know that genuine repentance and humility can open doors that seemed permanently closed.

title: The Prophet Enters Mecca Wearing a Helmet and Orders the Execution of Ibn Khatal
summary: The Prophet entered Mecca wearing a helmet rather than the pilgrim's ihram, and when told that Ibn Khatal was clinging to the hangings of the Ka'ba seeking sanctuary, he ordered his execution.
story: It is established in both jahih collections from a hadith of Malik, from al-Zuhri, from Anas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca wearing a helmet. When he took it off a man came who said, "Ibn Khawl is clinging to the hangings of the ka'ba." "Kill him," he ordered. Malik stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was not, we believe, in a state of Ihr%n, though God knows best."
lessons: Justice must sometimes be carried out even in sacred spaces when the gravity of a transgression demands it.
Leadership in difficult times requires making hard, unambiguous decisions without wavering.
The sanctity of a place does not nullify accountability for grave crimes; no external refuge replaces inner repentance.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult decision that feels contradictory to your comfort zone, seek guidance from principle rather than convenience.
Recognise that true sanctuary and safety come from one's own conscience and accountability, not from hiding behind symbols or rituals.
Do not delay justice or necessary action out of discomfort; unresolved wrongs compound over time.

title: The Prophet Enters Mecca Wearing a Black Turban
summary: Multiple narrations confirm that the Prophet entered Mecca on the day of its conquest wearing a black turban, a detail recorded from several companions including Jabir and 'Amr b. Hurayth.
story: Amad stated that 'Affxn related to him, quoting Hammad, quoting Aha al-Zubayr, from Jxbir, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca following its conquest he was wearing a black turban. The a~hzb al-s~nan relate this from a hadith of Hammad h. Salama. AlTirmidhi said that it was hasan szhih, "good and authentic". Muslim related it from Qutayba, as did Yahyx b. Y&y from Mu'xwiya b. 'Amma al-Duhni, from Abii al-Zubayr, from Jzbir, who stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca wearing a black turban and that he was not in a state of zhrzm. Muslim narrated from a hadith of Abii U w a , from Musawir al-Warraq, from Jacfar b. 'Amr b. Hurayth, from his father, who said, "It's as if I can still see the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the day Mecca was conquered; he was wearing an ash-black turban, its end hanging down between his shoulders."
lessons: The details companions preserved about the Prophet reflect the depth of their love and the significance they attached to even ordinary aspects of his appearance.
In moments of great achievement, simplicity and humility in outward appearance speak more powerfully than grandeur.
Attention to authentic narration and careful transmission of knowledge is itself an act of devotion and trust.
practical_advice: In your greatest moments of success, resist the urge to dress them up with unnecessary display; let the substance speak for itself.
Cultivate gratitude by paying attention to meaningful details in the people and moments you value.
When recounting important events, prioritise accuracy and honesty over embellishment.

title: The Prophet Recites Surah al-Fath While Mounted on His Camel Entering Mecca
summary: 'Abd Allah b. Mughaffal narrated that on the day of the conquest of Mecca he saw the Prophet mounted on his camel reciting Surah al-Fath in a vibrant, quivering tone, and wished he could have recited the same way.
story: Al-Bukhm stated that Aho d-Walid related to him, quoting Shu'ba, from 'Abd All& h. Qurra, who said, "I heard 'Abd All& b. Mughaffal say, 'On the day Mecca was conquered, I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) mounted on his camel reciting the sitrat al-Fath (XLVIII) in a vibrant, quivering tone. And if it had not been for the people crowding around me, I would have recited in the same tone as he did.'"
lessons: Connecting to God through recitation and remembrance, even at the height of worldly triumph, reflects the truest sense of priority.
Emotional resonance in worship is not weakness but a sign of sincere presence before God.
Witnessing another person's depth of devotion can inspire longing for the same closeness to God.
practical_advice: In your most joyful or triumphant moments, turn to prayer, recitation, or remembrance of God to ground yourself in gratitude.
Allow yourself to be moved emotionally during worship; do not suppress the feeling of awe or joy in those moments.
When you witness someone else's devotion and feel inspired, let that inspire your own practice rather than comparison or envy.

title: The Prophet Bows His Head in Deep Humility Upon Entering Mecca
summary: As the Prophet entered Mecca at the head of a vast army, he bowed his head so low in submission and gratitude to God that his beard almost touched the middle of his saddle, demonstrating profound humility at the moment of his greatest worldly triumph.
story: Muhammad b. Ishxq stated that it was related to him by 'Abd All& b. Abii Bakr he halted who said that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached Dhn TUWS, on his mount; he was wearing a turban made of striped red cloth from Yemen. He bowed his head so low in submission to God, having seen how He had honoured him with the conquest, that his heard almost touched the middle of his saddle. The hzfi~ al-Bayhaqi stated that Aha 'Abd All& al-Hafiz told him that Daclij b. A m a d related to him, quoting Ahmad b. CAli al-Abba, quoting CAbd Allah b. Aba Bakr al-Maqdisi, quoting Ja'far b. Sulaymm, from Thahit, from Anas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca the day of the conquest of Mecca with his chin resting in submission (to God) on his saddle."
lessons: True greatness is measured not by the power one holds but by the humility one maintains when holding it.
Every blessing and achievement is from God; the appropriate response to honour is not pride but prostration.
The Prophet's example shows that the greater the gift, the deeper the need for gratitude and lowliness before God.
practical_advice: When you achieve something significant, resist the pull toward pride; instead, pause and genuinely acknowledge the role of God, others, and circumstances in your success.
Practice gratitude physically as well as mentally: bowing your head, pausing, or taking a moment of stillness can help embody humility.
Use moments of success as opportunities to renew your commitment to your values rather than to elevate your self-image.

title: The Prophet Trembles When Addressed and Describes Himself as the Son of a Woman Who Ate Cheap Meat
summary: When a man addressed the Prophet on the day of the conquest and the Prophet began to tremble, he humbly reminded the man that he was simply the son of a Qurayshi woman who ate cheap dried meat, demonstrating extraordinary modesty despite commanding a massive army.
story: Al-Bayhaqi went on to say that Abn 'Abd Alkh al-Hnfiz informed him, quoting Abii Bakr b. Balawayh, quoting A m a d b. Sacid, quoting Ismacil b. d-H%rith, quoting Jacfar b. 'Awn, quoting Ismxcilb. Abii Khdid, from Qays, from Ibn Mascad, who said that a man spoke to the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the day of the conquest and he began to tremble. Then he said, "Tread softly! I'm the son of a woman of Quraysh who ate cheap desiccated meat!" This modesty in this place on the part of the Messenger of God (SAAS) as he entered with such a huge army contrasts completely with those fools of Banii Isra'il, the "ancient Israelites", who were ordered to enter the temple (at Jerusalem) bowing down - that is prostrating - while saying "Wipe out (our past sins)". However, instead they entered creeping forwards on their behinds saying, "Wheat in barley!"
lessons: Genuine humility means remembering your ordinary human origins even when the world around you elevates you.
Anxiety and trembling in a position of great responsibility can reflect not weakness but awareness of the weight of one's role before God.
The contrast between the Prophet's humility and the Israelites' defiance shows that the form of obedience matters as much as its performance.
practical_advice: When others treat you with excessive reverence or deference, gently redirect attention by grounding the conversation in your shared humanity.
If you feel anxious or overwhelmed in a position of responsibility, remind yourself of your origins and the ordinariness that keeps you connected to others.
Resist the temptation to let praise inflate your sense of self; use it instead as a reminder to be worthy of trust.

title: The Prophet Enters Mecca from Kada' in the Lower Part of the City
summary: Scholarly narrations establish that the Prophet entered Mecca from Kada' in the lower part of the city, while Khalid b. al-Walid entered from the upper part, with Ibn Kathir examining variant transmissions to determine the more authentic account.
story: It is stated above that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Khdid b. al-Walid in from the upper part of Mecca, while he himself entered from below, at Kuda; that is given in the ~ahih collection of al-Bukhari. Al-Bayhaqi stated, "Aba al-Husayn b. CAbdan informed us, quoting A b a d b. Wbayd al-Saff~r, quoting 'Abd All& b. Ibr&im b. al-Mundhir al-Khuzami, quoting Macn, quoting 'Abd All& b. Wmar b. H a f~ from , Ni%fifi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said, 'When, in the year of the conquest, the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca, he saw the women striking at the heads of the horses with their veils. He smiled at Abii Bakr and said, "And what was it, Abo Bakr, tbat Hasszn said?" Abii Bakr, may God be pleased with him, then recited the lines, "May I be deprived of my 'young daughters' (i.e. horses) if you do not see them making the dust rise at both sides of Kad2' As they race ahead, pulling at their reins, fully saddled, as the women strike at them with their veils." "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "Add those verses to those spoken by Hassnn!"'"
lessons: Even in a moment of historic triumph the Prophet found joy in poetry and the beauty of what his companions observed around him.
Attention to scholarly precision in preserving historical detail reflects the Islamic tradition's deep respect for truth.
Joy and lightness of heart are part of a believer's character even in the midst of serious events.
practical_advice: Allow yourself moments of genuine delight and shared joy with those around you, even in the middle of serious or stressful times.
Appreciate art, poetry, and beauty as legitimate expressions of gratitude and communal celebration.
When studying or learning, value careful discernment of sources and take time to understand why details matter.

title: Abu Quhafa Watches the Muslim Army Enter Mecca from Mount Abu Qubays
summary: Abu Quhafa, father of Abu Bakr, nearly blind, was led up Mount Abu Qubays by his daughter to watch the Muslim army enter Mecca; his granddaughter's silver necklace was stolen during the commotion, and he barely made it home before the cavalry arrived.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated that Y a h y b. (Ahhad h. 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr related to him, from his father, from his grandmother Asma', daughter of Aba Bakr, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) halted at Dha Tawa, Aba Quhafa said to a daughter, one of his youngest children, 'Daughter, lead me up on Mt. Aba Qubays.' Asma' commented that his sight was very weak." She went on (quoting the daughter in question), "She said, 'I took him up there and he asked me, "Well, what do you see?" "I can see a black mass." "That must be horses," he said. I then said, "Now I see a man hurrying to and fro in front of that mass, going back and forth." "That", he said, "must be the mi~zi!" - meaning the man who goes out to and gives orders to the cavalry. "'I then said, "The black is now spreading out!" Aba Quhafa exclaimed, "The cavalry has been dispatched, then! Hurry me back home!" I led him down, but the cavalry met him before he reached home.'" Asms' went on, "The girl was wearing a silver necklace and a man confronted her and cut it off her neck."
lessons: Great historical events touch ordinary people in immediate, personal ways; no one is simply a spectator when history moves through their streets.
Even the vulnerable and elderly are caught up in the currents of major events; care for them in times of upheaval is a moral obligation.
Theft and opportunism during moments of chaos reveal the character of individuals regardless of the broader righteousness of a cause.
practical_advice: During times of uncertainty or upheaval in your community, check on elderly relatives and those with limited mobility who may need help.
Do not let the chaos of a major event cause you to lower your ethical standards; how you act when no one is watching defines your character.
If you feel anxious watching large events unfold around you, focus on what is immediately in your control: your safety, your loved ones, and your next step.

title: Abu Bakr Brings His Father Abu Quhafa to Accept Islam Before the Prophet
summary: Abu Bakr brought his elderly, nearly blind father Abu Quhafa into the mosque to accept Islam before the Prophet, who expressed his wish to have gone to the old man himself, but praised Abu Bakr's intent and warmly welcomed Abu Quhafa.
story: "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca and went into the mosque, Abii Bakr came in, guiding his father. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw him, he said, 'Why did you not leave the sheikh at home for me to attend him there?' Abii Bakr replied, 'Messenger of God, it is more fitting for him to come to you rather than you to him.' The account went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then had Abii Quhafa sit down before him and rubbed his chest, saying, "Accept..."'"
lessons: Bringing a loved one to faith is one of the most profound gifts one person can offer another, and it is worth every effort.
The Prophet's willingness to go to the elderly rather than require them to come to him reflects Islam's teaching of honouring age and infirmity.
Conversion and acceptance of truth, even late in life, is always possible and always welcomed with open arms.
practical_advice: If you have a loved one who is elderly or unwell, consider how you can bring goodness, care, and spiritual nourishment directly to them rather than waiting for them to seek it out.
Do not give up hope that those close to you will find their way to faith and peace, even if it takes a long time.
When welcoming someone who is new to your community, faith, or cause, greet them with warmth and physical reassurance, not formality or distance.

title: Abu Bakr Brings His Father Abu Quhafa to Accept Islam
summary: Abu Bakr brings his elderly father Abu Quhafa to the Prophet on the day of the conquest of Mecca, where the Prophet accepts his Islam and instructs that his white hair be changed in color.
story: guiding his father. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw him, he said, 'Why did you not leave the sheikh at home for me to attend him there?' Abii Bakr replied, 'Messenger of God, it is more fitting for him to come to you rather than you to him.' The account went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then had Abii Quhafa sit down before him and rubbed his chest, saying, 'Accept Islam!' Abii Quhafa did so. Abii Bakr then guided him back home. Abii Quhafa's hair was as white as a thaghama plant. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Change his hair (colour).' Abii Bakr then arose and, taking his sister's hand, he said, 'I invoke God and Islam; (who has) my sister's necklace?' No one replied. He then said, 'Forget your necklace; I swear by God, there's little trust in people today!' Abii Bakr meant that specific day, because the army was so numerous that almost no one concerned himself with anyone else, and there were people scattered everywhere. Perhaps the person who took it considered it spoils of war. God knows best.
lessons: Family ties are a vehicle for guiding loved ones to goodness; Abu Bakr's care for his father demonstrates that we should never give up on those closest to us.
The Prophet's compassion was so great that he wished to visit the elderly rather than burden them with travel, reminding us that true leadership is rooted in humility and mercy.
Even in moments of great joy and victory, loss and disappointment can occur; Abu Bakr's equanimity over his sister's necklace models graceful acceptance of what we cannot control.
practical_advice: If a family member has not yet found faith or purpose, continue to invite them gently and be present for them rather than forcing or giving up.
When something valuable is lost or taken from you unjustly, practice releasing attachment to it and trust that God is aware of every injustice.
In chaotic or overwhelming situations, focus on what you can do rather than lamenting what is beyond your control.

title: Sa'd ibn Ubada's Proclamation and the Prophet's Correction at the Conquest
summary: Sa'd ibn Ubada carries the banner into Mecca declaring it a day of great battle and the loss of the sanctuary's sanctity, prompting the Prophet to correct him and transfer the banner, affirming instead that it is a day of glorification of the Ka'ba.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that 'Abd Allah b. Abi Najih related to him that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent forth the various parts of his army from Dhu Tawa he ordered al-Zubayr b. al-'Awam to enter with some of the men from Kada'; al-Zubayr was in command of the left flank. He ordered Sa'd b. Ubada to take some of the others in from Kuda. He went on, 'Some scholars claim that when Sa'd proceeded inside, he said, 'Today is the day of the great battle; today the sanctuary will lose its sanctity.' A man heard this (according to Ibn Hisham that man was Umar b. al-Khattab) and he asked, 'Messenger of God, do you hear what Sa'd b. Ubada says? We can't be sure he won't assault Quraysh.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told 'Ali, 'Go after him and take away the banner from him, you enter with it.' Sources other than Muhammad b. Ishaq recount that when Abu Sufyan complained to him about what Sa'd b. Ubada had said when he passed him by. The words Sa'd had used were, 'Abu Sufyan: today's the day of the great battle. The hurma, the sanctuary - by which he meant the ka'ba - will lose its sanctity.' The Prophet (SAAS) then said, 'No; this is a day on which the ka'ba will be glorified.' He then ordered that the banner - that of the ansar - be taken away from Sa'd b. Ubada as a way to teach him a lesson. It is said that it was given over to his son, Qays.
lessons: Even the most devoted companions can allow personal zeal or old grievances to cloud their judgment; true leadership means correcting errors with wisdom, not harshness.
Victory and power are tests of character; the Prophet's redirection of the conquest from vengeance to glorification of God shows that strength should always serve mercy.
How we frame pivotal moments shapes their outcome; calling it a day of glorification rather than battle transformed the entire spirit of the conquest.
practical_advice: When you feel righteous anger pushing you toward an extreme action, pause and ask whether your intention is truly for God's sake or for personal satisfaction.
If someone in a position of influence acts in a way that could cause harm, address it promptly but privately and respectfully, as the Prophet did by sending 'Ali.
In moments of personal or collective triumph, consciously choose a narrative of gratitude and humility rather than triumph over others.

title: The Battle of al-Khandama and Himas's Defeat
summary: Safwan, Ikrimah, Suhayl, and Himas gather men at al-Khandama to resist the Muslim army but are decisively defeated; Himas returns home in shame, his earlier boasts to his wife proven hollow.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that 'Abd Allah b. Abi Najih and 'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr related to him, that Safwan b. Umayya, 'Ikrima b. Abi Jahl and Suhayl b. 'Amr had gathered some men at al-Khandama to do battle. Himas b. Qays b. Khalid, brother of Banu Bakr, had been sharpening a sword prior to the arrival of the Messenger of God (SAAS). His wife asked him why and he replied that it was for use against Muhammad and his men. She commented, 'I swear by God, I don't think it will be of any use against them, Muhammad and his men!' He replied, 'Well, I swear by God, I hope to give you one of them as a servant!' He then spoke the verses, 'If they advance today, I'll have no excuse. This set of weapons is complete. With sharp blades and easy to manipulate.' He then took part in the fighting at al-Khandama, along with Safwan, 'Ikrima and Suhayl. There they were engaged by the Muslims accompanying Khalid. Kirz b. Jabir, Banu Muharib b. Fihr and Hubaysh b. Khalid b. Rabi'a b. Asram, ally of Banu Munqidh, both of Khalid's troops, were killed. They had become separated from Khalid and taken a different route; they were killed together, Kirz prior to Hubaysh. Both sources quoted by Ibn Ishaq went on to say that Salama b. al-Mayla' al-Juhanni, also of Khalid's cavalry, was killed there, while some 12 or 13 of the polytheists were killed. When they had lost the battle, Himas left in defeat and went home. He told his wife, 'Lock the door on me.' She asked, 'And what about all that you said?' He replied with the following verses: 'If you had witnessed the battle at al-Khandama when Safwan and 'Ikrima fled, With Abu Yazid erect like a widowed mother receiving condolences, And the Muslims greeted them with their swords, Cutting and striking arms and skulls with nothing but their battle cries being heard, While we emitted sobs and groans, then you would not speak the slightest word of blame.'
lessons: Arrogance and boastfulness before a trial often give way to humiliation; Himas's story is a timeless warning against overconfidence in one's own strength.
The wife's quiet skepticism proved wiser than her husband's bravado, reminding us that those who counsel caution are worth listening to even when their words are unwelcome.
Defeat, though painful, can be the beginning of self-knowledge; Himas's poetic admission of failure is itself a kind of honest reckoning.
practical_advice: Before making bold claims or commitments driven by pride, take a quiet moment to honestly assess your readiness and whether your confidence is grounded in reality.
When someone close to you expresses doubt about a risky course of action, treat their concern as valuable information rather than a challenge to your ego.
After a failure or humiliation, resist the urge to hide or deflect blame; acknowledging what happened honestly, as Himas did in verse, is the first step toward healing and growth.

title: The Prophet's Declaration of Mecca's Sanctity and the Incident with Khalid's Fighting
summary: The Prophet declares Mecca's eternal sanctity as established since creation, instructs his commanders to avoid unnecessary killing, but an incident occurs in which a messenger apparently conveys the opposite order to Khalid ibn al-Walid, resulting in deaths.
story: Someone told the Messenger of God (SAAS) that Khalid b. al-Walid was killing people. He then told a man to go to Khalid and order him to stop the killing. When the man reached him, he told him, 'The Prophet (SAAS) says, 'Kill those you are able to!' He killed 70 people. This fact was reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who sent Khalid a message that asked, 'Did I not forbid you killing?' Khalid replied, 'So-and-so came to me and ordered me to kill those I was able to.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) again sent him a message asking, 'Did I not give you an order?' Khalid responded, 'You wanted one thing, but God wanted another! God's order superseded yours; I could not do anything other than what I did.' At this the Prophet (SAAS) remained silent and gave him no response. Ibn Ishaq stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) had instructed his commanders to refrain from doing battle against any except those who fought them.' It was also related that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'God made this land sacrosanct the day He created the heavens and the earth. He forged it the day he forged the sun and the moon. That which stems from heaven is haram, sacrosanct. It was not made halal, desanctified, for anyone before me (to do battle). It was only made halal for me for a brief period of the day, and it then reverted to its former state.'
lessons: Miscommunication in critical moments can have grave consequences; leaders and followers alike bear responsibility for ensuring instructions are clearly understood and verified.
Khalid's response — 'God's order superseded yours' — reflects a deep theological conviction, yet it also illustrates the danger of rationalizing an outcome rather than following clear directives.
The sanctity the Prophet declared over Mecca reminds us that some boundaries are divinely established and must be approached with the greatest reverence and restraint.
practical_advice: In high-stakes situations, always verify important instructions directly rather than relying on intermediaries, especially when the stakes involve harm to others.
When you have acted in a way that caused unintended harm due to miscommunication, acknowledge the breakdown honestly and put better systems in place going forward.
Reflect regularly on what you treat as truly sacred and off-limits in your own life, and be honest about whether your actions consistently honor those boundaries.

title: Khalid's Defiance and the Prophet's Silence
summary: Khalid ibn al-Walid defied the Prophet's order to avoid battle during the Conquest of Mecca, claiming God's will superseded the command. The Prophet remained silent and gave no response.
story: message asking, 'Did I not give you an order?' Khalid responded, 'You wanted one thing, but God wanted another! God's order superseded yours; I could not do anything other than what I did.' At this the Prophet (SAAS) remained silent and gave him no response.
lessons: Sometimes silence from a leader carries its own weight and meaning; not every action requires an immediate verbal rebuke.
Even those closest to God can make decisions they believe are divinely guided while still being held accountable by legitimate authority.
Humility requires us to acknowledge when we have overstepped, even if our intentions were sincere.
practical_advice: When you have acted outside of instructions, own your decision honestly rather than deflecting — explain your reasoning calmly and accept the response.
If you are in a position of leadership and someone defies your guidance, take time to reflect before responding rather than reacting in anger.
Reflect on the difference between genuine divine guidance and personal rationalization — seek counsel from trusted mentors to distinguish the two.

title: Abd Allah ibn Sa'd ibn Abi Sarh: The Apostate Spared by Intercession
summary: Abd Allah ibn Sa'd, who had accepted Islam, written Revelation, then apostatized, was condemned to death at the Conquest of Mecca. His foster-brother Uthman interceded for him, and after a long silence the Prophet accepted his allegiance, later lamenting no one had acted during his silence.
story: Among these persons was 'Abd Allāh b. Sa'd b. Abū Sarh, who had accepted Islam, written down the Revelations and then apostatized. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca, having sentenced him to death, this 'Abd Allah fled to 'Uthmān who was his foster-brother. When the latter brought him to ask that he be spared, the Messenger of God (SAAS) made no reply for a long time, but then said, 'Yes.' When 'Abd Allāh departed with 'Uthmān, the Messenger of God (SAAS) commented to those around him, 'Is there no wise man among you who could have got up and killed him when you saw I was remaining silent?' They replied, 'But Messenger of God, could you not have given us some signal?' He replied, 'Prophets do not kill by making signals.' In a different account, the wording is, 'It is not proper for a prophet to betray by eye signals.' Ibn Hishām stated that thereafter he was a good Muslim and that 'Umar appointed him as a governor, and that 'Uthmān did the same thereafter. I note that he died while prostrated in performing the morning prayer, or after having finished it in his house.
lessons: Intercession and advocacy for others can open doors of mercy that seem permanently closed.
Genuine repentance and return to faith, even after grave betrayal, can lead to complete restoration — Ibn Sa'd later became a trusted governor.
The Prophet's reluctance to use deceptive signals even to achieve a just outcome reveals that integrity in method matters as much as the outcome itself.
practical_advice: If you have strayed from your values or commitments, do not let shame prevent you from returning — sincere recommitment can lead to full rehabilitation.
When advocating for someone in trouble, choose the right moment and the right person to approach, as Uthman did with patience and relationship.
Do not look for shortcuts or hidden signals in your moral decisions; act on clear principles and honest communication.

title: Abd Allah ibn Khatal Killed Clinging to the Kaaba
summary: Abd Allah ibn Khatal, who had accepted Islam then murdered a companion and apostatized, was condemned to death and killed while clinging to the hangings of the Kaaba during the Conquest of Mecca.
story: Ibn Ishāq stated (that the Prophet (SAAS)) also condemned to death 'Abd Allāh b. Khatal, a man of Banū Taym b. Ghalib. I note that it is also said that this man was named 'Abd al-'Uzzā, 'worshipper of al-'Uzzā', b. Khatal. It is likely that that had been his former name and that after he accepted Islam he was called 'Abd Allāh, 'worshipper of God'. (Ibn Ishāq continued) 'After this man had accepted Islam, the Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent him out to collect charity contributions, in the company of one of the Ansār. With him also he took a freed-man he had. Later he became angry with the freed-man, killed him and apostatized and became a polytheist again. He had two female singers, Fartana and a friend, who would sing songs mocking the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the Muslims. This was why the Messenger of God (SAAS) condemned him and the two singers to death. He was killed while he clutched the hangings of the ka'ba. Abū Barza al-Aslamī and Sa'id b. Hurayth al-Makhzūmī participated in executing him. One of his women singers was killed, while the other was granted clemency.' Al-Suhayli related that Fartana also accepted Islam.
lessons: Unchecked anger can lead to catastrophic and irreversible consequences — Ibn Khatal's rage at his servant set off a chain of events that led to his death.
Mocking those who are sincere in their faith and purpose is a spiritually destructive path.
Even sacred spaces cannot provide refuge for those who have committed grave wrongs and refused to repent.
practical_advice: When you feel intense anger rising, remove yourself from the situation before acting — the consequences of acting on unchecked rage can be permanent.
Be careful about the content you consume, share, or create; media that demeans and mocks can harden hearts and damage communities.
If you find yourself far from your values, do not wait for a crisis to return — seek reconciliation with your principles and your community before circumstances force the issue.

title: Al-Huwayrith Who Harmed Fatima and Umm Kulthum During the Hijra
summary: Al-Huwayrith ibn Nuqaydh had goaded the camel carrying Fatima and Umm Kulthum during their migration, causing them to fall. He was condemned to death at the Conquest of Mecca and executed by Ali ibn Abi Talib.
story: Ibn Ishāq went on, 'Also (he condemned) al-Huwayrith b. Nuqaydh b. Wahb b. 'Abd Qusayy. He had been one of those who had insulted the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Mecca. When al-'Abbās had mounted up Fātima and Umm Kulthūm to conduct them to Medina to join the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the start of the Hijra, this al-Huwayrith had goaded a camel on which they were mounted, and they had fallen to the ground. When he had been condemned to death, 'Ali b. Abū Tālib executed him.'
lessons: Deliberately harming the innocent, especially the vulnerable, carries lasting moral and worldly consequences.
Cruelty and mockery motivated by enmity toward the righteous is a path that leads to ruin.
Justice, though sometimes delayed, is ultimately served — those who act unjustly should not assume their actions are forgotten.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether your opposition to someone is driven by principle or by personal animosity — animosity-driven harm harms the one who inflicts it most.
Stand up for those who are vulnerable and being harmed, as the companions did by ensuring justice was served for the Prophet's daughters.
Do not let grievances fester into cruelty; seek resolution through legitimate means before they escalate beyond repair.

title: Miqyas ibn Subaba: Betrayal, Murder, and Apostasy
summary: Miqyas ibn Subaba feigned acceptance of Islam to claim blood-money for his accidentally killed brother, then murdered the Muslim who had killed him and returned to polytheism. He was condemned to death and killed at the Conquest of Mecca.
story: Ibn Ishāq stated, 'Also condemned was Miqyas b. Subāba because he had murdered the man who had by accident killed his brother, and he did this after he had accepted the blood-wit. He later apostatized and became a polytheist. It was a man of his own tribe, named Numayla b. 'Abd Allāh, who executed him.' Muhammad b. Ishāq narrated from 'Abd Allāh b. Abī Bakr b. Muhammad b. 'Amr b. Hazm, that Hishām, the brother of Miqyas b. Subāba, was killed at the expedition against Banū al-Mustaliq by a Muslim who thought him to be a polytheist. Miqyas then came and made a pretence of accepting Islam to seek the blood-money for his brother. When he received it he attacked and murdered the man who had killed his brother and returned to Mecca as a polytheist. After the Messenger of God (SAAS) condemned him to death, he was killed between al-Safa and al-Marwa. Ibn Ishāq and al-Bayhaqi quoted as follows the verses he composed when he murdered the man who killed his brother: 'It assuaged me that he was felled in the valley, his clothing stained by the blood of his jugular. My mind was troubled before I killed him; my worries kept me from enjoying my bed. Through him I avenged Fihr and charged his blood-wit to the chiefs of Banū al-Najjār, the Lords of Fair castle. By killing him I eased my tension and got my revenge; and I was first to return to the idols.'
lessons: Grief and the desire for revenge, when left unchecked by moral boundaries, can corrupt even a person's stated faith and lead them to catastrophic choices.
Accepting compensation and then taking revenge anyway is a profound betrayal of trust and agreement — integrity demands we honor our commitments even in pain.
The verses Miqyas composed reveal that he was aware of his own spiritual deterioration; self-awareness without repentance and action changes nothing.
practical_advice: When processing grief over a loss — especially a sudden or violent one — seek community support and spiritual grounding before making any major decisions, especially those involving confrontation.
If you have agreed to a resolution or settlement of a conflict, honor it fully; reopening wounds after accepting restitution prolongs suffering for everyone.
Notice when your emotions are driving you toward actions that conflict with your stated values — this is the moment to pause, seek counsel, and reflect deeply.

title: Umm Sara: From Poverty to Pardon to Condemnation
summary: Umm Sara, a freed-woman who had received charity from the Prophet but then carried a treacherous message to the Meccans, was condemned to death at the Conquest of Mecca, though she later received immunity and lived until the time of Umar.
story: Then there was Sara (who was also executed), a freed-woman of Banū 'Abd al-Mutallib and of 'Ikrima b. Abū Jahl, because she used to insult the Messenger of God (SAAS) while he was in Mecca. I observe that it is narrated above that it was she who had carried the message from Hātib b. Abī Balta'a and seems to have been forgiven or to have escaped. Thereafter he condemned her to death. But God knows best. She fled until she was granted immunity by the Messenger of God (SAAS). She lived on into the era of 'Umar, and died when trodden underfoot by a man on a horse. Al-Bayhaqi went on: 'Umm Sāra was a freed-woman of Quraysh who had gone to the Prophet (SAAS) and complained to him of her poverty. He had then given to her. But thereafter a man sent her off with a message for the Meccans.'
lessons: Receiving kindness and generosity from someone and then betraying them is among the gravest moral failures a person can commit.
Even those who have committed serious wrongs can find mercy and a path to continued life — Umm Sara received immunity despite her condemnation.
One's station in life — poverty, social marginalization — does not excuse betrayal, but understanding context helps us pursue justice with compassion.
practical_advice: If someone has shown you generosity and kindness, honor that relationship by being trustworthy — betrayal of a benefactor damages your own character deeply.
When you feel desperate due to poverty or hardship, seek help through honest means and from trustworthy sources rather than being drawn into harmful schemes.
If you have done something wrong, do not flee indefinitely — seek to make amends and pursue reconciliation, as prolonged avoidance only deepens the breach.

title: Ikrima ibn Abi Jahl: From Fleeing Enemy to Sincere Convert
summary: Ikrima ibn Abi Jahl, son of the Prophet's fierce enemy, fled to Yemen after the Conquest of Mecca. Caught in a storm at sea, he made a sincere vow to God, returned, and accepted Islam. His wife had already secured his pardon from the Prophet.
story: Ibn Ishāq stated, ''Ikrima b. Abū Jahl fled to Yemen. His wife, Umm Hakīm, daughter of al-Hārith b. Hishām, accepted Islam and sought immunity for him from the Messenger of God (SAAS), who granted that. She set off and located 'Ikrima, brought him back to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and he accepted Islam. Al-Bayhaqi stated that Abū Tāhir Muhammad b. Muhammad b. Muhammis al-Faqīh informed him, quoting Abū Bakr Muhammad b. al-Husayn al-Qattān, quoting Ahmad b. Yūsuf al-Salmī, quoting Ahmad b. al-Mufaddal, quoting Asbat b. Nasr al-Hamdānī, who said, 'Al-Suddī maintained, on the authority of Mus'ab b. Sa'd, that the father of the latter said, "On the day of the conquest of Mecca, the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave immunity to all except four men and two women, saying, 'Execute them even if you find them clinging to the hangings of the ka'ba.' These persons were 'Ikrima b. Abū Jahl, 'Abd Allāh b. Khatal, Miqyas b. Subāba and 'Abd Allāh b. Sa'd b. Abū Sarh.' 'Ikrima went to sea. A storm blew up and other people on the boat told one another, 'Worship God sincerely, for your gods will do you no good here!' And so 'Ikrima said, 'Well, I swear by God, if the only thing that succeeds at sea is sincere faith, the fact is that nothing but that succeeds on land too! O God, I make a pact with you that if you save me from my present plight, I will go to Muhammad, place my hand in his and receive generous forgiveness.' So he did go to him and accepted Islam.'
lessons: Crisis and near-death experiences can strip away pride and pretense, revealing the truth that sincere faith is the only real anchor in life's storms.
A devoted spouse's love, loyalty, and intercession can be the bridge that brings a lost person back to truth and safety.
No matter how far a person has strayed or how long they have been an enemy of what is right, the door to return remains open as long as they are alive.
practical_advice: When you face a crisis that strips away your usual defenses and certainties, use that moment of vulnerability as an opportunity for genuine reflection about what you truly believe.
If someone you love is lost, separated from faith or community, do not give up on them — patient, loving advocacy, as Umm Hakim showed, can bring them home.
Make your commitments to God in moments of clarity and then honor them when the crisis passes — following through on sincere vows builds integrity and deepens faith.

title: Umm Hani Shelters Her Brothers-in-Law and Seeks the Prophet's Protection
summary: Umm Hani, daughter of Abu Talib, sheltered two of her brothers-in-law from the Banu Makhzum who had fled to her home during the Conquest of Mecca. When her brother Ali came to kill them, she locked her door and went to the Prophet to seek their protection.
story: Ibn Ishāq stated, Sa'id b. Abū Hind related to me, from Abū Murra, the freed-man of 'Uqayl b. Abū Tālib, that Umm Hāni', the daughter of Abū Tālib, said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) made a halt in the heights of Mecca, two of my brothers-in-law of Banū Makhzūm fled to me.' Ibn Hishām stated, 'The two men were al-Hārith b. Hishām and Zuhayr b. Abū Umayya b. al-Mughīra.' Ibn Ishāq went on to quote her as saying that while she was in the home of Hubayra b. Abū Wahb al-Makhzūmī, 'My brother 'Ali b. Abū Tālib came in and said, "I swear by God, I'll kill them both!" So I locked the door of my house on both men and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) who was in the heights of Mecca. I found him engaged in washing himself from a basin that had in it some traces of dough; his daughter Fātima was shielding him.'
lessons: Courage in protecting the vulnerable, even when facing pressure from those we love and respect, is a mark of moral strength.
The Prophet's accessibility — being found in an ordinary, human moment of washing — reflects the approachability of true leadership.
Family bonds can create genuine moral tensions; navigating them requires both love and principled clarity.
practical_advice: When someone vulnerable seeks your protection, act decisively even if it puts you at odds with others you care about — protection of the innocent takes precedence.
Do not let fear of a powerful person's reaction prevent you from seeking justice or mercy from the right authority — go directly to those who can help.
In moments of conflict between loyalty to family and doing what is right, pause before acting irreversibly, as Umm Hani did by placing a door between harm and its target.

title: The Prophet Circumambulates the Ka'ba on His Mount and Calls for Its Key
summary: After entering Mecca on the day of conquest, the Prophet circumambulated the Ka'ba seven times on his mount, then called for the key to the Ka'ba from Uthman ibn Talha, entered it, and broke a wooden dove idol found inside.
story:
Ibn Ishsq stated that Muhammad b. Jacfar b. al-Zubayr related to him, from Wbayd A b h b. 'Abd Alhh h. Abii Thawr, from Safiyya, daughter of Shayba, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived at Mecca and had reassured the people, he went to the kacba and circumambulated it seven times on his mount, saluting the d n , the corner with the Black Stone, with a staff he carried in his hand. When he had fmished his circumambulations, he called for 'Uthmm b. Talha and took from h i the key to the kacba. When it was opened for him, he went inside, where he found a dove made of wood. He broke it in his hand and threw it away. He then stood at the door to the kacba, the people having assembled for him in the mosque.

Miia b. Wqba stated, 'He then performed two rakacatand went to zamzam and looked down into it. He called for water, drank it and performed the ablutions, people hastening to collect the leftovers of the water he had used. The polytheists watched him with great interest, saying, 'We never saw or heard of any king being treated this way!' He pushed back the al-maq~m where it is located today, whereas it was previously close to the kacba.'
lessons: Returning to sacred spaces and purifying them of corruption is an act of spiritual restoration that benefits entire communities.
Genuine leadership inspires awe not through pomp but through sincere devotion and righteous action, as even the polytheists marveled at how people responded to the Prophet.
Small acts of ritual — drinking zamzam, performing ablutions, returning the maqam to its rightful place — demonstrate that spiritual discipline continues even in moments of historic triumph.
practical_advice: After achieving a major goal, take a moment to express gratitude and reconnect with your spiritual foundation before moving on to the next task.
Lead with humility and purpose rather than seeking admiration; people are drawn to those whose authority is rooted in sincerity.
Honor the rights of others even in positions of power, as the Prophet immediately restored the key of the Ka'ba to its rightful custodian.

title: The Prophet's Speech at the Door of the Ka'ba: 'You Are Free'
summary: Standing at the door of the Ka'ba on the day of conquest, the Prophet proclaimed the end of tribal pride and ancestry-based privilege, then offered a general amnesty to the Quraysh, declaring, 'You may go; you are free.'
story:
Muhammad b. Ishzq went on, 'A scholar related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stood at the door of the kacba and said, 'There is no god but God alone! He has no associate. He has kept His promise, has helped His servant and has defeated the opposing parties alone. Every advantage of ancestry or money that might he claimed is herewith placed beneath these feet of mine, except for the guardianship of the kacba and the provision of water to the pilgrims. Compensation for those who are killed by mistake or incomplete intent by whip or cudgel shall be compensated following a binding oath of 100 camels, 40 of which shall be pregnant. 0 Quraysh, God has removed from you the false pride of thejahd~yyaperiod and the veneration of ancestry. All men stem from Adam, and Adam stemmed from dust.' He then recited the following verse (from the Qur'm), '0 people, We created you from male and from female' (swat al-Hujurat; XLIX, v.13). He then said, '0 Quraysh, what do you think I am going to do with you?' They replied, LGood!(You are) a noble brother, and the son of a noble brother.' He then said, 'You may go; you are free.'

'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sat down in the mosque and 'Ali b. Aha Talih went up to him, holding the key of the kacba in his hand. He said, '0 Messenger of God, join together for us the right of guardianship of the ka'ba with that of provision of the water for the pilgrims, may God bless you!' He responded, 'Where is Wthmm h. Talha?' He was summoned for him and then he told Wthman, 'Here is your key, Wthman; this is a day of piety and good faith.'

Imzm A h a d stated, 'Sufym related to us, from Ibn Jad%n, from al-Q%im b. Rabi'a, from Ibn Wmar, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, while standing on the steps of the kacba on the day of the conquest of Mecca, "Praise be to God who fulfilled His promise and helped His servant and has defeated the opposing parties alone. (Compensation) for those killed by quasi-intent by whip or cudgel shall be compensated by 100 camels." On another occasion he said, "by a binding oath promising payment of 40 pregnant camels. All privilege of ancestry and pride that existed in thej~hzlzyya (are finished)." Another time, he said, "Both these factors are beneath these feet of mine; however, the right to provide water for the pilgrims and to have guardianship of the ka'ba will continue for those in charge of them, as before."'
lessons: True victory is demonstrated not through retribution but through magnanimity; the Prophet's general amnesty transformed former enemies into devoted followers.
Human equality before God is a foundational principle; no person's lineage, wealth, or tribal status grants them superiority over another.
Fulfilling trusts and restoring rights — such as returning the key to its rightful keeper — is a mark of prophetic character and moral excellence.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a position of power over someone who has wronged you, consider whether forgiveness might accomplish more than punishment — both for them and for your own inner peace.
Reflect on any sense of superiority you may carry based on background, status, or achievement, and consciously release it in recognition of the fundamental equality of all people.
Honor commitments and return what belongs to others promptly and graciously, especially when it would be easy to claim it for yourself.

title: The Prophet Condemns Images of Angels and Prophets Inside the Ka'ba
summary: Upon entering the Ka'ba, the Prophet found representations of angels and prophets, including an image of Abraham holding divining arrows. He condemned the depictions and ordered all images obliterated.
story:
Ibn Hishzm stated that a scholar related to him that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered the ka'ba the day of the conquest, he saw inside it representations of angels and others. He saw Abraham pictured holding divining arrows in his hand. He said, 'May God kill them! They have depicted our elder using divining arrows; what would Abraham have to do with divining arrows? Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian. But he was a hang and a Muslim; he was no polytheist.' He then gave orders and all those images were obliterated.

Imam A b a d stated that Sulayman related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Rahmm, from M a a h. Wqba, from Aba al-Zubayr, from Jabir, who said, 'There were pictures inside the kacba and the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered Wmar b. al-Khattab to wipe them away. And so Wmar moistened a piece of cloth and used it to erase them. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) went inside nothing of them remained.'
lessons: Correcting distortions of religious truth — even longstanding ones enshrined in sacred spaces — requires both courage and clarity of conviction.
The Prophet's anger was not personal but principled; righteous indignation in the face of falsehood is a form of moral integrity.
Purifying one's spiritual environment of misleading influences allows for a cleaner, more honest relationship with God.
practical_advice: Examine whether any misconceptions or inherited distortions have quietly shaped your understanding of faith, and seek to correct them with knowledge and humility.
When you feel righteous anger at an injustice or falsehood, channel it into constructive action rather than letting it fester or express itself destructively.
Take practical steps to remove from your environment things that mislead you spiritually or pull you toward what is false, just as the images were erased before the Prophet entered.

title: The Prophet Destroys 360 Idols Around the Ka'ba
summary: On the day of the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet struck the 360 idols surrounding the Ka'ba with a staff, declaring 'The truth has come and error has gone,' and every idol he gestured toward fell.
story:
Al-Bukhai stated that Sadaqa b. al-Fad1 related to h i , quoting Ibn 'Uyayna, from Ibn Aba Najih, from Mujahid, from Aba Ma'mar, from 'Ahd Allah - he being Ibn Masciid - who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca on the day of the conquest, around the ka'ba there were 360 idols. He set about striking them with a stick he carried, saying, 'The truth has come and error has gone. The truth has come and error is powerless.'' Muslim related this from a hadith of Ibn Wyayna.

Al-Bayhaqi related, from Ibn Ishzq, from %bd Allzh b. Aba Bakr, from 'Ali h. 'Ahd All& h. 'AhbS, from his father, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca on the day of its conquest, in the ka'ba there were 300 idols. He took a staff and set about striking the idols with it until he had hit them all.'

He then related, through Suwayd h. Sa'id from al-@sim b. 'Abd All&, from 'Ahd M h h. Din=, from Ihn 'Umar, (who said) that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca he discovered there 360 idols. He gestured towards each one with a stick and said, 'The truth has come and error has gone; error is bound to disappear' (s~ra Bana Isrz'ik XVII, v.81). And every idol towards which he pointed fell over, without him touching them with his stick.

Hanhal b. I s b q stated that he was informed by Aha d-Rahic, from Ya'qiih d-qammi, quoting Jacfar b. Abn al-Mughira, from Ihn Abza, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) conquered Mecca, an old, grey-haired black woman appeared, clawing at her face and complaining loudly. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'That is Na'ila; she is despairing that she will ever again be worshipped in your country.'

Ihn Hish- stated, 'A narrator of traditions in whom I have trust narrated, on a line of authorities from Ihn Shihzh, from TJhayd M z h b. 'Ahd All& h. Wtha, from Ihn 'Abb%s, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca on the day of the conquest on his mount and circumambulated the kacba on it. Around the kacba there were idols made with lead reinforcing. He began pointing at the idols with a stick he held, saying, 'The truth has come, and error is gone. Error is bound to disappear.' Every idol at whose face he gestured fell over on'
lessons: The triumph of truth over falsehood may be long delayed, but when it arrives it can be total and decisive, as symbolized by every idol falling before a single gesture.
Physical idols represent internal attachments to falsehood; dismantling them begins with a firm declaration of truth spoken with conviction.
Even the opponents of truth — represented by the despairing idol Na'ila — ultimately recognize their own defeat; staying firm on the path of truth eventually makes falsehood powerless.
practical_advice: Identify the 'idols' in your own life — habits, fears, or false beliefs that you have placed above God — and begin the work of dismantling them one by one.
Speak truth clearly and without hesitation in the face of what is false, trusting that sincerity itself carries a power that arguments alone cannot match.

When you witness the collapse of something you once feared or felt oppressed by, respond with gratitude and humility rather than gloating, as the Prophet's words focused on God's truth rather than personal triumph.

title: Destruction of Idols Around the Ka'ba During the Conquest of Mecca
summary: On the day of the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) circumambulated the Ka'ba on his mount and gestured toward each idol with a stick, causing every single one to fall over, declaring 'The truth has come, and error is gone.'
story: A narrator of traditions in whom I have trust narrated, on a line of authorities from Ihn Shihzh, from TJhayd M z h b. 'Ahd All& h. Wtha, from Ihn 'Abb%s, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca on the day of the conquest on his mount and circumambulated the kacba on it. Around the kacba there were idols made with lead reinforcing. He began pointing at the idols with a stick he held, saying, "The truth has come, and error is gone. Error is bound to disappear." Every idol at whose face he gestured fell over on its hack, while every one at whose hack he pointed fell over on its face. Eventually every single one had fallen over. Tamim b. Asad al-KhuzSi therefore spoke the verse, "There is a lesson and learning in the idols for those expecting reward or punishment."'
lessons: Truth ultimately prevails over falsehood, no matter how long falsehood may appear to dominate.
False idols — whether literal or metaphorical beliefs and habits — will eventually fall when confronted with divine truth.
Every moment of triumph over wrongdoing carries a lesson for those who reflect deeply.
practical_advice: When you feel surrounded by falsehood or injustice, remind yourself that truth has an enduring power that falsehood cannot withstand.
Identify the 'idols' in your own life — harmful habits, false beliefs, or toxic attachments — and take deliberate steps to dismantle them.
Reflect on moments of positive change in your life as signs of divine guidance, and use them to strengthen your faith and resolve.

title: The Prophet Salutes the Black Stone and Confronts the Idol at the Ka'ba's Side
summary: Upon reaching the Black Stone, the Prophet (SAAS) saluted it and circumambulated the Ka'ba; arriving at an idol worshipped beside the Ka'ba, he poked it in the eye with the frame of his bow, declaring the truth and the vanishing of error, then climbed Mt. Safa and praised God.
story: In the ~ahihcollection of Muslim, (there is a hadith) from Shayha h. Farrakh, from Sulayma b. al-Mughira, from Thzhit, from 'Abd All& h. Rah*, from Aha Hurayra, who said in his account of the conquest of Mecca, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forward and when he reached the (Black) Stone he saluted it and circumambulated the ka'ba. Amving at an idol at the side of the ka'ba to which they had made worship, the Messenger of God (SAAS) grasped a how he was carrying by its bent frame and began poking it in the eye, saying 'The truth has come and error has gone. The truth has come and error is bound to disappear.' When he had fmished his circumambulation, he went out and climbed Mt. Safn and, looking down at the kacba, raised up his hands, gave praise to God and spoke various prayers."
lessons: Acts of worship, like the Prophet's salutation of the Black Stone and his supplication on Mt. Safa, are powerful expressions of gratitude and connection to God.
Confronting falsehood directly and without hesitation is a mark of spiritual courage and conviction.
After achieving a great victory, the immediate turning toward God in praise and prayer reflects true humility and gratitude.
practical_advice: After any personal victory or relief, pause to offer sincere gratitude to God before moving on to the next task.
Develop a habit of returning to sacred rituals and prayers as anchors that ground you after emotional or life-changing events.
Practice standing firm in truth even when falsehood is deeply embedded in your environment, trusting that God supports those who uphold what is right.

title: The Prophet Refuses to Enter the Ka'ba Until Idols and Offensive Images Are Removed
summary: The Prophet (SAAS) refused to enter the Ka'ba while it contained idols, and upon seeing a picture of Abraham and Ismail holding divining arrows, he expressed strong disapproval before ordering all such images removed.
story: Al-Bukhm- stated that I s h ~ qb. Mamjrrr related to him, quoting 'Abd al-Samad, who quoted his father as quoting A m b , from qkrima, from Ibn 'Abb%, who said that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) reached Mecca, he refused to enter the kacba while it contained idols. He therefore ordered them to be removed. One picture showed Abraham and Ismcil holding dinning arrows, and he said, 'May God kill them! They knew very well that they never used them to cast lots!' "He then went inside the ka'ba, and said, Allahu Akbar! God is most Great! in various parts of it, then came out without saying prayers there."
lessons: Sacred spaces should be kept free of anything that distorts or corrupts the truth of God's message.
Maintaining the integrity of worship requires the courage to remove that which has been wrongfully introduced, even when it is long-established.
Righteous indignation at the distortion of sacred figures and truths is a natural and appropriate response.
practical_advice: Evaluate the environments in which you worship or seek spiritual peace — remove distractions or influences that compromise the quality of your connection with God.
When you encounter misrepresentations of sacred teachings, speak up with knowledge and clarity rather than remaining silent.
Declare God's greatness (Allahu Akbar) as a conscious act of reorienting your heart toward what truly matters, especially in moments of spiritual renewal.

title: The Prophet Prays Inside the Ka'ba
summary: After entering the Ka'ba, the Prophet (SAAS) prayed inside it, positioning himself between two columns with the western wall close before him, in a moment of profound spiritual intimacy recorded through multiple narrations.
story: I m a Ahmad stated that it was related to him by 'Abd al-Samad, quoting H a m m a , quoting 'A@, from Ibn 'Abbs, (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to the kacba in which there were six columns. He stood at each column and prayed, but he did not perform the ~ a l z hprayers. b. Yahynal-gdhi, Muslim related this from Shayhm b. Farfikh, from Hamfrom 'Ata". Iman &mad stated that it was related to him by H%rm b. MaCriif who quoted Ibn Wahb as having been informed by 'Amr b. a l - H ~ t hthat Bukayr related to him, from Kurayb, from Ibn 'Abb%, who said that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered the kacba he found a picture of Abraham and another one of Mary. He then said, "They are well aware that angels will not enter any building containing a picture; yet this is Abraham depicted! Why ever would he cast lots?" Al-Bukhai and al-Nasn'i related this from a hadith of Ibn Wahb. Imnm Ahmad stated that it was related to h i by 'Abd a l - m q , quoting Ma'mar, who said that Wthmzn al-Khazraji had related to h i that he heard Miqsam relate that Ibn 'Abh% had said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went into the ka'ba and spoke prayers at its side and then went out and performed two rakca of the ~ a l a hprayers." Ahmad alone gives this. I m a Ahmad stated, "Ismncil related to us, quoting Layth, from Mujnhid, from Ibn Wmar, (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) perfonned two rak'as of the ~ a l a hprayers in the Ra'ba. Al-Bukhm stated that al-Layth said, quoting Yiinus, quoting Nafi: from 'Abd All& b. Wmar (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca on the day of its conquest from its heights and that riding with him on his mount was U s m a b. Zayd. From al-Hajaba he was accompanied by Wthmm b. Talha He dismounted at the mosque and ordered that the key to the kacba be brought to him. He then went in with U s w a h. Zayd, Bib1 and W t h n b. Talha and stayed there for a long time. Eventually he came out again and the people hurried there. 'Abd All* b. Wmar was the fust to go inside and he found Bilzl standing behind the door. He asked h i , 'Where was it that the Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed?' Bilal pointed to the spot. 'Ahd All& commented, 'I forgot to ask him how many prayer prostrations he had made.'" I note that it is established in the ~ahihcollection of al-Bukhari and elsewhere that the Prophet (SAAS) prayed inside the kacba with its door behind his hack, positioning himself with two columns on his right, one on his left and three to his rear. At that time the ka'ba had six columns. There was a distance of some three arms-lengths between himself and the western wall.
lessons: Prayer is the most fitting response to moments of great triumph — the Prophet's first act inside the Ka'ba was to pray, not to celebrate.
The companions' eagerness to know exactly where and how the Prophet prayed reflects the deep importance of following the example of the righteous.
Sacred moments of worship, even small details of position and manner, carry spiritual significance worth preserving and passing on.
practical_advice: In your most significant life moments — whether of achievement or relief — prioritize prayer before anything else as an act of gratitude and humility.
Learn the details of how acts of worship are performed correctly; precision in worship reflects reverence for God.
When entering any sacred or meaningful space, be fully present and intentional, letting the environment draw your heart closer to God.

title: Bilal Makes the Call to Prayer from Atop the Ka'ba
summary: On the day of the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet (SAAS) ordered Bilal to make the call to prayer from the roof of the Ka'ba, a moment that filled believers with joy but provoked resentment among some Quraysh, whose dismissive remarks about Bilal's race revealed their lingering arrogance.
story: Ihn H i s h m stated, "And a certain scholar related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered the kacba at the conquest of Mecca accompanied by Bihl, whom he ordered to make the call to prayer. Abii Sufym b. Harh, 'Atmh h. Usayd and d-Harith b. Hisham were seated in the courtyard of the kacba. 'Attxb said, 'God honoured (my father) Usayd by not having him hear this; had he done so, it would have enraged h i .' Al-Haith h. H i s h m commented, 'I swear by God, if I had known he was right, I would have followed him!' Ahii Sufym added, 'I'll say nothing. If I were to speak, these stones would report on me!' "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) came outside and said, 'I know what you said,' and he then related their comments to them. Al-Harith and 'Attab declared, 'We testify that you are the Messenger of God! There was no one with us who overheard this for us to say he repeated it to you.'" Yiinus b. Bukayr quoted Ibn Ishaq as having related from his father, who said, "A member of the family of Jubayr h. Muc'im related to me that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca, he gave orders to Bilal, who climbed on to the roof of the ka'ba and there made the call to prayer. One of the sons of Sacid b. al-'A$ said, 'God honoured Sa'id by taking him away before he heard this black man on the roof of the ka'ba.'" 'Abd d-Razzaq stated, from Ma'mar, from Ayyiib that Ibn Ahii Mulayka said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave orders to Bilal who made the call to prayer on the day of the conquest of Mecca on top of the ka'ba and that one of the Quraysh men said to al-Hwith h. Hishm, 'Do you see where that black man has climbed?' He (al-Hwith) replied, 'Leave him alone; if God dislikes him, He will change him.'" Yiinus h. Bukayr and others quoted Hisham h. Wrwa as quoting his father as saying that the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave orders to Bild on the day of the conquest of Mecca, and that he made the call to prayer from atop the kdba, to anger the polytheists.
lessons: Islam elevated the status of those who had been oppressed and marginalized — Bilal, once a slave tortured for his faith, now called humanity to God from the highest point of the most sacred place on earth.
Racism and social arrogance have no place in a faith built on the equality of all human beings before God.
Divine knowledge encompasses all things — the Prophet's awareness of what was whispered in secret is a reminder that nothing is hidden from God.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked, undervalued, or dismissed because of your background or circumstances, remember that God's measure of honor is piety and sincerity, not social status.
Resist the temptation to measure people's worth by worldly standards such as wealth, race, or lineage — these are not the measures God uses.
Use moments of personal triumph or public recognition not for self-glorification but as an opportunity to call others toward what is good and true.

title: Abu Sufyan's Private Doubts Exposed by the Prophet
summary: After the conquest, Abu Sufyan privately contemplated resistance against the Prophet (SAAS), but the Prophet approached him, struck him on the shoulder, and repeated his secret thoughts back to him, causing Abu Sufyan to declare his belief in the Prophet's prophethood.
story: Muhammad b. Sacd stated, from al-Waqidi, from Muhammad b. Harb, from Ismacilh. Aha Khdid, from Aba Ishq, as saying that after the conquest of Mecca, Aba Sufyan b. Harb was sitting saying to himself, "What if I had gathered a force against Muhammad!" While he was asking himself this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) slapped h i between the shoulders and said, "Then God would have put you to shame!" Aba Sufyzn then raised his head and, when he saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) standing above him, said, "I was never sure before that you were indeed a prophet!" Al-Bayhaqi stated that Abn 'Abd Allzh al-Hafu related to us, qnzatan, with permission (for me to quote him), quoting Abn Hamid A h d h. al-Hasan al-Muqri, quoting Ahmad b. Yiisuf al-Sulami, quoting Muhammad b. Yasuf d-Firyzbi, quoting Yanus b. Aba Ishzq from Aba al-Safar, from Ibn 'Abb%, who said, "When Abii Sufyan saw that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) walked that the people would tread in his footsteps, he said to himself, 'What if I had fought this man again!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) went up, struck him on the chest and said, 'Then God would have put you to shame!' "He (Aba Sufyzn) said, 'I turn to God and seek His forgiveness for what I have spoken!'"
lessons: God's knowledge is total and complete — no thought, however private, is beyond His awareness.
Even the most stubborn and long-resistant hearts can be opened to truth when confronted with undeniable signs of prophethood.
True repentance begins with acknowledging one's wrong thoughts and words before God, as Abu Sufyan demonstrated by immediately seeking forgiveness.
practical_advice: Be mindful of your private thoughts — cultivate the awareness that God knows what no one else can see, and let that awareness guide your inner life toward integrity.
When you find yourself resistant to a truth you have long opposed, have the courage to examine your resistance honestly and follow the evidence wherever it leads.
The moment you recognize a wrong thought or intention, seek forgiveness immediately without delay, as Abu Sufyan did upon being confronted with his hidden thoughts.

title: Abu Sufyan's Night Conversation with Hind and His Declaration of Faith
summary: On the night of the conquest, while the people celebrated with takbeer around the Ka'ba, Abu Sufyan privately asked his wife Hind whether the victory was from God; the Prophet (SAAS) later revealed this private exchange to Abu Sufyan, prompting him to testify to the Prophet's prophethood.
story: Al-Bayhaqi then related, through Ibn Khuzayma and others, from Aba H h d b. al-Sharqi, from Aba M u m a d h. Yahyz al-Dhuhli, quoting Mosa b. A'yan d-Juzari, who quoted his father, who quoted Ishaq h. &hid as quoting Sacid b. d-Musayyab as saying, "That night of the conquest of Mecca the people who entered kept on calling out AllZhu Akbar! God is most Great! rejoicing and circumambulating the kacba until the next morning. Abii Sufym asked H i d , 'Do you think this is from God?' She replied, 'Yes; this is from God.' "Next morning Aba Sufyzn went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and the latter told him, 'You said to Hind, "Do you think that this is from God?" "Yes", she replied, "this is from God."' "At this Aba Sufyzn exclaimed, 'I testify that you are the servant and Messenger of God! By Him who is invoked in oaths, no one but Hind alone heard my saying this.'"
lessons: When honest questions about truth are asked sincerely — even in private — God has ways of guiding a person toward the answer.
