The celebration of God's greatness (takbeer) is a communal act of gratitude that draws even reluctant hearts toward reflection.
A sincere declaration of faith, when it comes after years of opposition, is a powerful testament to the persistence of God's mercy.
practical_advice: Allow yourself to ask honest questions about your faith, even in moments of doubt — sincere questioning, when paired with openness, is the beginning of deeper understanding.
Participate in communal acts of worship and gratitude, as their collective energy can move even resistant hearts.
If you have long delayed an honest reckoning with your beliefs or values, use moments of undeniable clarity as the opportunity to finally align your outward life with your inner convictions.

title: The Prophet Declares the Sanctity of Mecca and Its Sacred Protections
summary: Following the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet (SAAS) declared that God had made Mecca a sanctuary since the creation of the heavens and the earth, forbidding hunting, cutting of trees, uprooting of vegetation, and misappropriation of lost property within it.
story: Al-Bukhzri stated, "Ishzq related to us, quoting Abii %vim, from Ibn Jurayj who said that Hasan h. Muslim informed him, from MujZhid, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'God made Mecca a sanctuary the day He created the heavens and the earth. It is a sanctuary by God's having made it so and will remain such until Judgement Day. (Conflict in it) was not made permissible for anyone before myself, nor for anyone after me; and this only for a short time. In it game may not be hunted, its trees may not be cut down, nor may its vegetation be uprooted. And items found in it belong to the finder only after announcement of the find.' AlLcAbh%b. 'Ahd al-Muffalib suggested, 'Except for idhkhir grass, O Messenger of God, for it is used by our smiths and in our homes.'"
lessons: Some sacred boundaries are established by God Himself and exist beyond human authority to alter — respecting them is an act of submission to divine wisdom.
Environmental stewardship and the protection of natural life are not incidental to Islamic ethics but are explicitly commanded by prophetic teaching.
The sanctity of Mecca is a reminder that certain spaces are set apart for spiritual elevation, and their preservation serves the wellbeing of all humanity.
practical_advice: Identify the sacred boundaries in your own life — whether in your faith, your relationships, or your personal ethics — and commit to upholding them regardless of convenience.
Practice mindful stewardship of the natural world around you as an act of worship, recognizing that God has made the earth a trust in human hands.
When entering sacred spaces or moments of worship, consciously leave behind the distractions and conflicts of daily life and allow yourself to be fully present before God.

title: The Sanctity of Mecca Proclaimed After the Conquest
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) proclaimed the eternal sanctity of Mecca, forbidding the shedding of blood, cutting of trees, and uprooting of vegetation, while making an exception for the idhkhar plant at the suggestion of Al-Abbas.
story: a sanctuary by God's having made it so and will remain such until Judgement Day. (Conflict in it) was not made permissible for anyone before myself, nor for anyone after me; and this only for a short time. In it game may not be hunted, its trees may not be cut down, nor may its vegetation be uprooted. And items found in it belong to the finder only after announcement of the find.' Al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib suggested, 'Except for the idhkhar plant, Messenger of God, its use is essential in burials and inside homes.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) was silent a moment, then agreed, 'Except for the al-idhkhar; harvesting it is allowed.'
lessons: Sacred boundaries exist to protect communities and preserve harmony; respecting them is an act of faith and civilizational maturity.
Wisdom in leadership includes the ability to pause, listen to counsel, and adapt rulings to genuine human needs without compromising core principles.
Divine law is not rigid to the point of causing unnecessary hardship; there is always room for compassionate exception grounded in reason.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by rules or restrictions in your life, look for the spirit behind them rather than resisting them outright — understanding the 'why' brings peace.
If you have a concern or a differing perspective, express it respectfully and at the right moment, as Al-Abbas did, rather than staying silent or erupting in frustration.
Practice pausing before making final decisions, especially under pressure — even a moment of silence, as the Prophet modeled, can lead to wiser outcomes.

title: Abu Shurayh Reminds Amr ibn Said of Mecca's Sanctity
summary: Abu Shurayh al-Khuza'i recounts to commander Amr ibn Said the Prophet's declaration of Mecca's eternal sanctity and the prohibition of bloodshed within it, but Amr dismisses him, insisting the sanctuary does not protect wrongdoers.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that it was related to him by Sa'id b. Shurahbil, quoting al-Layth, from al-Maqburi, from Abu Shurayh al-Khuza'i, who said that he asked 'Amr b. Sa'id, who was about to dispatch groups of fighters into Mecca, "O amir, 'Commander', please permit me to report a statement made by the Messenger of God (SAAS) the day following the conquest of Mecca. My ears heard, my heart knew and my eyes saw him as he spoke it. He praised and thanked God, then said, 'It was God, not men who made Mecca a sanctuary. It is not lawful for anyone who believes in God and the last day to shed blood nor to cut down trees within it. And should anyone seek permission to fight therein because the Messenger of God (SAAS) fought there, then tell him, "God gave permission to His messenger but gave none to you." And He only allowed this to me for a short period. Its sanctity has reverted today to what it was before. Let those here present inform of this those absent.'" Abu Shurayh was then asked, "What did 'Amr reply to you?" Abu Shurayh responded, "He said, 'I know this better than you do. The sanctuary does not provide refuge to anyone who has sinned, nor to a murderer in flight, nor to anyone seeking to escape payment of the jizya', the poll-tax.'"
lessons: Speaking truth to authority, even when dismissed, is a moral obligation — the value of truth does not depend on whether it is accepted.
Those who witnessed sacred moments have a responsibility to convey what they saw and heard, regardless of how it is received.
Power does not grant the right to override divine law; those in authority will be accountable for how they respond to sincere counsel.
practical_advice: When you have done the right thing and been dismissed or ignored, remind yourself that your responsibility is to convey the truth, not to control its reception — release the outcome and find peace in your integrity.
If someone in authority rejects your honest advice, document your position calmly and clearly, as Abu Shurayh did, so there is no ambiguity about where you stood.
Do not let the fear of rejection silence you when something important is at stake; speak with evidence and personal witness, then leave the matter to God.

title: Khuza'a Kills a Man of Hudhayl and the Prophet Pays Compensation
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, members of Banu Khuza'a killed a polytheist man of Hudhayl in retaliation for past grievances. The Prophet (SAAS) publicly reaffirmed Mecca's sanctity, commanded them to stop killing, and personally paid blood-money compensation for the slain man.
story: Ibn Ishaq related that during the jahiliyya period a man called Ibn al-Athwa' killed a man of Khuza'a known as "Ihmarra Ba'san", "he who turned red in rage". On the day of the conquest of Mecca Banu Khuza'a killed Ibn al-Athwa' while he was in Mecca; the man who killed him was Khirzsh b. Umayya. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, "People of Khuza'a, kill no more; there has been too much killing, even if to some purpose. I will pay compensation for the man killed." Ibn Ishaq went on, "'Abd al-Rahman b. Harmala al-Aslami related to me, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) learned what Khirzsh b. Umayya had done, he said, "Khirzsh kills to excess!"'" Ibn Ishaq also stated that Sa'id b. Abu Sa'id al-Maqburi related to him, from Abu Shurayh al-Khuza'i, who said, "When 'Amr b. al-Zubayr advanced on Mecca to battle his brother 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, I went to him and said, 'We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS) when Mecca was conquered. The day following the conquest Khuza'a attacked and killed a man of Hudhayl who was a polytheist. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then rose to address us and said, "God made Mecca a sanctuary when He created the heavens and the earth. It will remain one of God's sanctuaries until the day of Resurrection. It will not be lawful for anyone who believes in God and the last day to shed blood therein, nor to cut down a tree there. This was not made lawful for anyone who came before me, nor will it be lawful for anyone who comes after me. It was only made lawful for me for this short period because of (God's) anger against its inhabitants. Now its sanctity has reverted to as it was previously. Let those of you present so inform those absent. Respond to anyone who tells you that the Messenger of God (SAAS) fought in it by saying, 'God made this lawful for His messenger, but He has not done so for you.' O Khuza'a, cease killing; there has been too much, even if to some purpose. You have killed someone and for him I will pay compensation. If someone should be killed after my stay here, then his family has two options: to demand the blood of his killer, or to seek blood-money for him." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then paid compensation for the man Khuza'a had killed.'"
lessons: Cycles of revenge perpetuate suffering; a truly just leader breaks the cycle by absorbing the cost personally rather than allowing retribution to escalate.
Old grievances, no matter how legitimate, cannot justify violations of sacred law — the conquest of a new era requires letting go of the wounds of the old one.
Personal accountability in leadership — the Prophet paying compensation himself — is one of the most powerful expressions of moral authority.
practical_advice: When you feel consumed by the urge to retaliate for a past wrong, ask yourself whether your action will close the wound or deepen it — sometimes the bravest choice is to stop the cycle.
If you have caused harm even with justification, acknowledge it openly and make amends; this is not weakness but the highest form of integrity.

When anger from old pain resurfaces, name it honestly — 'there has been too much killing, even if to some purpose' — and then deliberately choose a path that leads to healing rather than continuation of the conflict.

title: The Prophet Declares Those Most Disliked by God on the Day of Conquest
summary: On the day of the conquest, after a man of Khuza'a killed a man of Banu Kalb the next day at al-Muzdalifa despite the cessation of arms being declared, the Prophet (SAAS) arose and declared that the people most disliked by God are those who kill in the sanctuary, kill non-combatants, and kill for the blood-money practices of the jahiliyya period.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Yahya, from Husayn, from 'Amr b. Shu'ayb, from his father, from his grandfather, who said, "When Mecca was conquered by the Messenger of God (SAAS) he said, 'Cease using weapons; except for Khuza'a against Banu Bakr.' He gave them permission (to fight on) until the al-'asr, late afternoon, prayer. He then announced, 'Cease using your weapons!' A man of Khuza'a encountered and killed another from Banu Kalb next day at al-Muzdalifa. When news of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he arose and said, (the narrator added that he saw him and that he was resting his back on the ka'ba), 'The people God dislikes most are those who kill others in the sanctuary, those who kill those not fighting them, and those who kill for the blood-money of the jahiliyya period.'"
lessons: There are moral red lines that no circumstance, political victory, or tribal loyalty can justify crossing; clarity about these lines is itself a form of spiritual protection.
The prohibition of killing non-combatants is not a modern legal invention but a divine command proclaimed at the height of the Prophet's greatest military triumph.
Victory can be a moment of tremendous temptation toward excess; the truly righteous are those who show the most restraint precisely when they have the most power.
practical_advice: In moments when you feel justified in lashing out — at a person, a group, or a situation — pause and ask whether your action targets someone who is actually harming you, or whether you are simply releasing pent-up anger onto whoever is nearby.
Identify the 'jahiliyya patterns' in your own life — inherited habits of retaliation, score-keeping, and grudge-holding — and consciously work to replace them with principled responses.
Use this hadith as a reminder when stressed or angry: the measure of a person's character is not how they act when they are weak, but how they act when they finally have power.

title: The Ansar Fear the Prophet Will Stay in Mecca After Its Conquest
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, the Ansar of Madinah feared among themselves that the Prophet (SAAS) might choose to remain in his hometown. When he learned of their concern, he immediately reassured them that he would live and die among them.
story: Ibn Hisham stated that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca following its conquest and went up to al-Safa to pray, the ansar watched him and asked one another, "Do you think that since God has conquered for the Messenger of God (SAAS) his own land and city that he will remain there?" When he had finished his prayer he asked them what they had been saying and they replied that it was nothing. He kept on at them until they told him. He then said, 'God forbid! I shall live where you live, die where you die!'" This comment given by Ibn Hisham is substantiated by Imam Ahmad b. Hanbal in his hadith compendium.
lessons: True loyalty is reciprocal; the Prophet's immediate and unconditional reassurance to the Ansar shows that those who give their love and support deserve to have their fears addressed with warmth and clarity.
Anxiety about abandonment is a deeply human fear; it does not reflect weakness but rather the depth of one's attachment and love.
A leader who notices the unspoken fears of those around them and addresses those fears proactively builds a community of unshakeable trust.
practical_advice: If you are carrying a quiet fear of being left behind or forgotten by someone important to you, find a gentle way to voice it — as the Ansar eventually did when pressed — because named fears lose much of their power.

If someone in your life seems anxious or withdrawn, gently pursue the conversation as the Prophet did; sometimes people need to be asked more than once before they feel safe enough to share what is truly bothering them.
When you make a commitment to someone — a friend, a spouse, a community — reaffirm it verbally and specifically, especially at moments of transition, so they do not have to wonder in silence.

title: Abu Hurayra Recounts the Entry into Mecca During a Gathering with Muawiyah
summary: During a gathering in Ramadan with delegations visiting Muawiyah, Abu Hurayra recounts the hadith of the conquest of Mecca, describing how the Prophet (SAAS) organized his forces, dispatching al-Zubayr, Khalid, and Abu Ubayda to enter the city from different directions.
story: He stated that the following was related to him by Bahz and Hashim, both quoting Sulayman b. al-Mughira, from Thabit. Hashim said, "Thabit b. Bunani related to me, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Rabah, who said, 'Delegations came in to see Mu'awiya and I was among them, as was Abu Hurayra. This was in Ramadan. We prepared food for one another. Abu Hurayra would invite us very often. So I asked myself whether I should prepare some food and invite them to my tent. I ordered food to be prepared and met Abu Hurayra that evening. I said to him, "Abu Hurayra, the invitation tonight is at my place." "So you're beating me to it then?" "Yes," I replied, "I've invited some people who are there." "Abu Hurayra said, 'Well, you ansari, should I recount a hadith of yours?' He went on to relate the conquest of Mecca. He said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived, he entered Mecca. He dispatched al-Zubayr in command of one of the flanks and Khalid in charge of the other. He sent Abu Ubayda over the bridge and they took the centre of the valley. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was in his squadron. Quraysh had gathered their forces."'"
lessons: The act of sharing a meal and narrating sacred history are both forms of community-building; the companions understood that remembering together is itself an act of worship.
Generosity in hospitality — each companion trying to host the other — reflects a culture of giving that strengthens the bonds of brotherhood far beyond what any formal institution can achieve.
Preserving and transmitting the stories of the Prophet is not merely scholarship; it is a living act of love and loyalty to his legacy.
practical_advice: Make a habit of gathering with friends or family around a meal and intentionally sharing a meaningful story, memory, or lesson — communal storytelling heals loneliness and reinforces shared identity.
When someone consistently shows generosity toward you, look for genuine opportunities to reciprocate rather than simply accepting — the give-and-take of care is what sustains deep friendships.

In your own moments of stress or transition, recall the stories of those who navigated far greater upheavals with faith and community; let their example orient you when your own circumstances feel overwhelming.

title: The Conquest of Mecca: Entry of the Prophet and Dispatching of Commanders
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) entered Mecca during the conquest, dispatching al-Zubayr, Khalid, and Abu Ubayda to lead different flanks while the Quraysh gathered their forces and awaited the outcome.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived, he entered Mecca. He dispatched al-Zubayr in command of one of the flanks and Khalid in charge of the other. He sent Abu Ubayda over the bridge and they took the centre of the valley. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was in his squadron. Quraysh had gathered their forces. They (the army commanders?) said, 'We'll send those ahead; and if they make progress, we'll be with them. If they are attacked, we'll give him whatever he asks.'
lessons: Leadership requires wisdom in delegation — trusting capable people with responsibilities is a sign of strength, not weakness.
When facing overwhelming forces, strategic positioning and calm resolve matter more than reactive emotion.
True authority is exercised with composure; the Prophet entered his hometown not with vengeance but with purpose and order.
practical_advice: When overwhelmed by a major challenge, break it into parts and assign each part appropriate attention rather than trying to handle everything alone.
Practice calm, deliberate decision-making in high-stakes moments by pausing before acting, as the Prophet did upon entering Mecca.
Surround yourself with trustworthy, capable people so that when difficulties arise, you are not carrying every burden alone.

title: The Prophet Circumambulates the Kaaba and Destroys the Idols
summary: After entering Mecca, the Prophet advanced to the Black Stone, saluted it, circumambulated the Kaaba, and struck the idols surrounding it, declaring the truth had come and falsehood had vanished.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS), then advanced to the (Black) Stone, saluted it and circumambulated the ka'ba. In his hand he carried a bow. He grasped it by its bent frame and arriving, on his way round, at an idol beside the ka'ba they used to worship, he struck it in the eyes, saying, 'The truth has come and error is gone. Error is completely gone!' He then climbed al-Safa from where he could look down upon the ka'ba, and raised his hands, invoking God and praying to Him.
lessons: The moment of greatest victory is best met with gratitude to God rather than personal pride — the Prophet's first act was prayer, not celebration.
Falsehood, no matter how long it has stood, cannot withstand the arrival of truth; patience in the face of falsehood is rewarded.
Returning to one's spiritual centre — as the Prophet returned to the Kaaba — is a powerful act of healing and recommitment after a long struggle.
practical_advice: When you achieve a long-sought goal, pause before celebrating and offer gratitude to God first, acknowledging that success comes from Him.
Identify the 'idols' in your own life — habits, attachments, or beliefs that are false — and resolve to displace them with what is true and beneficial.
Climb your own 'al-Safa' — find a quiet elevated place to reflect, pray, and give thanks after a major life transition or achievement.

title: Safwan ibn Umayya Flees and Is Granted Four Months of Immunity
summary: Safwan ibn Umayya fled Mecca in fear after the conquest, but the Prophet granted him immunity through a trusted intermediary and generously extended the period of safe passage to four months.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that Muhammad b. Ja'far b. al-Zubayr related to him, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, who said, 'Safwan b. Umayya left to go to Jidda to take a boat from there to Yemen. 'Umayr b. Wahb told the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Prophet of God, Safwan b. Umayya, his people's chief, has fled from you intending to cast himself into the sea. Do you give him immunity, may God bless you?' He replied, 'He is given immunity.' 'Umayr then asked him, 'Messenger of God, please give me some token by which he will know your immunity for him.' So he gave him the turban he had been wearing when he had entered Mecca. 'Umayr left with it and caught up with Safwan as he was about to go to sea. He told him, 'Safwan, may my father and mother be your ransom; if you plan to kill yourself, this is a guarantee of immunity from the Messenger of God (SAAS) that I have brought you.' 'Clear off! Don't talk to me!' Safwan told him. 'Umayr insisted, 'But I swear, he is the most trustworthy, compassionate and best of all men and your cousin. His fame, honour and possessions are your own.' Safwan replied, 'Yet I fear for myself.' 'Umayr argued, 'But he is too compassionate and noble for that.' 'Umayr did bring him back and when he stood before the Messenger of God (SAAS) Safwan said, 'This man claims that you grant me immunity?' 'He spoke the truth,' he replied. 'Let me have two months to make my choice,' Safwan asked. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'You may have four months to decide.'
lessons: Generosity toward a fearful enemy — offering more than they asked for — reflects the highest moral character and is more transformative than any punishment.
Fear can drive people to desperate, self-destructive decisions; a compassionate intermediary who speaks truth can be the difference between life and death.
Giving people time and space to come to truth on their own terms, without compulsion, respects human dignity and produces genuine change.
practical_advice: If someone who has wronged you is now afraid of your reaction, consider extending more grace than they expect — this often breaks down walls that confrontation cannot.
When you are overwhelmed by fear of consequences, seek a trusted person to represent you and speak on your behalf before acting rashly.
In your own life, when you need to make a major decision, ask for the time you need; and when others ask you for time, give them more than they requested where possible.

title: Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl Follows His Wife Back to Islam
summary: Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl fled to Yemen after the conquest but was followed by his wife Umm Hakim, who persuaded him to return and accept Islam, after which the Prophet affirmed the validity of their marriage.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on to relate from al-Zuhri that Fakhita, daughter of al-Walid, the wife of Safwan, and Umm Hakim, daughter of al-Harith b. Hisham, the wife of 'Ikrima b. Abi Jahl, had followed 'Ikrima to Yemen and asked him to come back, and he did so and accepted Islam. When the two men (Safwan and 'Ikrima) accepted Islam, the Messenger of God (SAAS) affirmed the validity of their first marriages.
lessons: A devoted spouse can be the most powerful guide back to truth; love and loyalty can reach where argument alone cannot.
Islam came with mercy — the Prophet affirmed existing marriages rather than imposing new burdens on those returning to the faith.
No one is beyond redemption; even the son of one of Islam's greatest enemies found his way back through human connection and divine mercy.
practical_advice: If someone you love is walking away from what is good, do not give up on them — follow them with patience and gentle persistence as Umm Hakim did.
Avoid placing unnecessary extra burdens on people who are returning from a difficult place; welcome them back and affirm what is still valid in their life.
Reflect on who in your life might be waiting for someone to come after them — sometimes people need to see that they are worth the effort.

title: Ibn al-Ziba'ra Comes to the Prophet and Accepts Islam, Reciting Verses of Repentance
summary: Abd Allah ibn al-Ziba'ra, a chief poet-enemy of Islam, came to the Prophet after the conquest and accepted Islam, reciting eloquent verses of repentance and acknowledging his past errors.
story: Ibn Ishaq quoted Sa'id b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. Hassan b. Thabit as having said, 'Hassan delivered against Ibn al-Ziba'ra while the latter was in Najran the following single verse, adding to it no other, "May you never be deprived of a man hatred for whom has made you settle in Najran where your life is solitary and mean!"' 'When this reached Ibn al-Ziba'ra, he came out to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and accepted Islam. When he did so, he spoke the verses, "O Messenger of al-Malik, the Sovereign Lord, my tongue is now mending what I tore when I was unproductive, When I followed Satan in the ways of error; and those who turn along his course are deceived. My flesh and bones believe in my Lord; and my heart is witness that you are the one who warns. I shall drive away from you there the clan of Lu'ayy; all of them are deceived."' Ibn Ishaq also quoted Ibn al-Ziba'ra as reciting, when he accepted Islam, 'Worries and cares held back sleep from me and the night pitched above me was disturbed and impenetrable When I had heard that Ahmad had criticized me, I passed that night as though in fever. O best man that ever a sure-footed, fast-paced camel bore upon its body, I apologize to you for what I did; I have been wandering in error During a time when Sahm and Makhzum ordered me to pursue false paths. I gave aid to evil policies, led on by the orders of those in error, accursed men. Today my heart believes in the Prophet Muhammad and whoever mistakes this is deprived (of truth). Enmity is over, its causes gone; the ties and understanding between us call out. Forgive my mistakes - may my parents be your ransom - for you are forgiving and are yourself forgiven. Upon you there is the sign of the knowledge of the Sovereign Lord, a strong light and a seal impressed After friendship, He honoured you with His proof, and proof from God is powerful indeed. I have testified that your religion is true, and that you will be mighty in the hereafter. God testifies that Ahmad is the one chosen, noble and pre-eminent among the righteous, A prince, his high house being from Hashim, the branch of a tree firm at its apex and in its roots.'
lessons: Even those who have spent years actively opposing truth can find their way back; it is never too late to repent and reclaim one's moral integrity.
The talent and gifts you once used for wrong can be redirected entirely toward what is good — Ibn al-Ziba'ra's poetry, once a weapon against Islam, became a vehicle of repentance.
Exile and loneliness, though painful, can strip away the social pressures that kept a person from truth and allow the heart to finally hear its own conviction.
practical_advice: If you have used your abilities — speech, writing, influence — in ways you now regret, consider how you can redirect those same gifts toward healing and truth.
Write or speak your own 'verse of repentance': articulate clearly what you did wrong, why it was wrong, and what you now believe — this act of honest expression can be deeply liberating.
Do not let past enmity define your present identity; the moment you turn sincerely toward what is right, that is who you are becoming.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit Recites Poetry on the Day of the Conquest of Mecca
summary: Hassan ibn Thabit, the Prophet's poet, recited verses on the day of the conquest celebrating the Muslim army's victory, the pilgrimage, and the divine support of Gabriel and the Holy Spirit.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that the following lines of Hassan b. Thabit were among those delivered on the day of the conquest of Mecca: 'Traces of her campgrounds, now empty, at Dhat al-Asa bi' and al-Jiwa' and on to 'Adhrit have vanished, Homes of Banu al-Hashas now deserted and obliterated by wind and rain Always was the company pleasant there and in its pastures were fine camels and sheep. No more of that! But who can deal with a spectre that keeps me awake when evening is past? Sha'tha: when she appears before one, the heart knows no consolation. She is like a fine wine from Bayt al-Ra's, one to be mixed with honey and water; Such drinks are fine to recall, the best of wines for which to make sacrifice. It we blame if we behave badly, getting into fights or quarrels When we drink it, it makes us feel like kings or lions so that nothing can deter us from conflict. May we be deprived of our horses if you do not see them raising the dust, their objective Ka'ba. They battle their reins, turning aside the thirsty spears above their shoulders. Our horses continue charging ahead, the women flapping their veils at them. Either you run away from us and we perform the pilgrimage, and then the conquest will occur and the cover (over the ka'ba) be removed, Or you should bear up against a battle in which God will give victory to whomever he wishes. Gabriel, the Messenger of God, is on our side, and the Holy Spirit is without peer. God said, "I have sent a servant who speaks the truth if visitation can do good. I swear by him; so stand, and acknowledge his truth." You replied,'
lessons: Poetry, art, and creative expression have always been legitimate and powerful vehicles for processing momentous events and giving voice to collective emotion.
Remembering where you came from — the empty campgrounds, the long journey — deepens gratitude for where God has brought you.
The certainty of divine support, represented by Gabriel and the Holy Spirit in the verse, is a source of profound courage and steadiness in the face of adversity.
practical_advice: Use creative expression — writing, poetry, journaling — to process significant turning points in your life, as the companions did on the day of conquest.
When celebrating an achievement, take time to remember the long road that led there; gratitude is deepened by honest memory of the struggle.
Remind yourself in times of difficulty that divine help is real and near; connect to this certainty through prayer, recitation, and reflection.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit's Poetry Defending the Prophet Before the Conquest
summary: Hassan ibn Thabit composed verses defending the Prophet Muhammad and responding to those who satirized him, warning Abu Sufyan and affirming loyalty to the Prophet before the conquest of Mecca.
story: their shoulders. Our horses continue charging ahead, the women flapping their veils at them. Either you run away from us and we perform the pilgrimage, and then the conquest will occur and the cover (over the ka'ba) be removed, Or you should bear up against a battle in which God will give victory to whomever he wishes. Gabriel, the Messenger of God, is on our side, and the Holy Spirit is without peer. God said, 'I have sent a servant who speaks the truth if visitation can do good. I swear by him; so stand, and acknowledge his truth.' You replied, 'No; we'll not arise. We don't wish to.' And so God said, 'I have sent my troops; they are the ansar and their intent is to fight.' Every day we receive from Ma'add insults, conflict or mockery. We will confuse with our rhymes those who mock us; and we will strike when blood mixes with blood! Give Abu Sufyan from me an important message - for now is plain what had been hidden Consider our swords having left you a slave, and household slaves have bondwomen rule them! You satirized Muhammad, and I answered for him; with God will be your requital. Is whoever of you satirizes the Messenger of God, the equal of those who praise and aid him? You satirized someone blessed, a pious man, a companion of God, a man characterized by loyalty. My father, his father and my honour, all protect against you the honour of Muhammad. My tongue is a faultless sword and my verse a water source no buckets could turn cloudy. Ibn Hisham commented that Hassan spoke these verses before the conquest.
lessons: Standing up for truth and righteousness through one's God-given talents is a form of worship and service.
Loyalty to those who guide us toward goodness is a sign of strong moral character.
Words used in defense of truth carry weight and leave a lasting legacy.
practical_advice: When you feel others are being unjustly attacked, consider how your own unique skills — writing, speaking, or creating — can be used constructively in their defense.
Channel feelings of anger at injustice into productive, principled expression rather than destructive reaction.
Reflect on who in your life has guided you toward goodness and express gratitude and loyalty to them openly.

title: The Smile of the Prophet at the Women Flapping Their Veils
summary: As the Muslim army advanced toward Mecca with women waving their veils at horses to urge them forward, the Prophet smiled at Abu Bakr, reflecting a moment of quiet joy and trust in God's promise.
story: Ibn Hisham commented that he had heard that al-Zuhri had stated, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw the women slapping the horses with their veils, he smiled at Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with him.'
lessons: Even in the midst of momentous and weighty events, gratitude and joy can be found in small, human moments.
A leader who smiles with his companions in moments of triumph demonstrates humility and shared humanity.
Trust in God's promise, when fulfilled, brings a deep and quiet happiness that transcends outward celebration.
practical_advice: In your own life, pause during significant milestones to notice and cherish small moments of connection and joy with those close to you.
When you have been patient through difficulty and see relief arrive, let yourself feel and express genuine gratitude rather than rushing past the moment.
Cultivate the habit of sharing joy with others — a smile or small gesture of warmth can be profoundly meaningful to those around you.

title: Bujayr ibn Zuhayr's Poem on the Conquest of Mecca
summary: Bujayr ibn Zuhayr composed verses celebrating the Muslim victory at the conquest of Mecca, describing the strength and resolve of the Muslim forces and their pledge to the Prophet.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that Bujayr b. Zuhayr b. Abu Salma spoke the following verses on the conquest of Mecca: 'That morning Muzayna and Banu Khufaf drove the people of al-Haballaq from every ravine. We struck them with our sharp swords at Mecca the day of the conquest of the good Prophet We came at them with 700 of Sulaym and a full 1,000 of Banu 'Uthman. We crushed their backs, striking and thrusting and pelted them with fine arrows Which you could hear making a crackling sound like arrowshafts breaking at their point fastenings. We charged, our steeds wheeling among them, the shafts of our lances straight ahead. We came back taking plunder as we wished, while they retreated regretting their conflict. We gave the Messenger of God our pledge and goodwill. They heard our speech and set about retreating from us on that morning of terror.'
lessons: Commitment and solidarity within a community multiply individual strength and bring about what seemed impossible.
Pledging one's loyalty to righteous leadership is a defining act that shapes one's legacy.
Decisive, united action in service of truth can bring resolution to long-standing conflict.
practical_advice: When facing a significant challenge, remind yourself of the strength that comes from standing together with those who share your values.
Reflect on the commitments and pledges you have made in your life — renewing your dedication to them can restore a sense of purpose and direction.
Celebrate moments of collective achievement with gratitude, recognizing that success is rarely the work of one person alone.

title: Abbas ibn Mirdas and the Destruction of the Idol Dimar
summary: Abbas ibn Mirdas abandoned idol worship after hearing a supernatural voice from within his father's idol Dimar declaring it destroyed and proclaiming the truth of Prophet Muhammad's prophethood, after which he accepted Islam.
story: Ibn Hisham gave the explanation for the acceptance of Islam by 'Abbas b. Mirdas. His father used to worship a stone idol called Dimar which he entrusted to 'Abbas when his death was near. One day, while 'Abbas was worshipping it, he heard a voice from inside it say, 'Tell all the tribes of Sulaym that Dimar has perished, while the people of the mosque live on. He of Quraysh who has inherited prophethood and right-guidance after Mary's son is a true guide. Dimar has perished, though he was long worshipped, from before the scripture up to the Prophet Muhammad.' At that 'Abbas set Dimar afire, joined the Messenger of God (SAAS) and accepted Islam.
lessons: Truth has a way of breaking through even the most deeply held false beliefs when a person's heart is open to it.
The courage to abandon what one has inherited when confronted with clear truth is a mark of genuine intellectual and spiritual honesty.
Guidance can come from unexpected places and moments — remaining spiritually attentive allows us to recognize and respond to it.
practical_advice: When you feel a deep inner dissonance between what you have been taught and what your heart senses to be true, take time to reflect and investigate with sincerity rather than dismissing the feeling.
Be willing to let go of habits, beliefs, or attachments that no longer serve your spiritual growth, even when they have been part of your life for a long time.
When you experience a moment of clarity or spiritual awakening, act on it — like Abbas, decisive action in response to truth leads to transformation.

title: Khalid ibn al-Walid Sent to Banu Jadhima as a Missionary
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet sent Khalid ibn al-Walid to Banu Jadhima as a missionary, not a warrior, but when the tribe put down their weapons as instructed, Khalid bound and executed some of them, an act the Prophet publicly disavowed.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that it was related to him by Hakim b. Hakim b. 'Abbad b. Hanif, from Abu Ja'far Muhammad b. 'Ali, who said, 'When Mecca was conquered, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent forth Khalid b. al-Walid as a missionary and not as a warrior. With him were Arab tribes, along with Sulaym b. Mansur and Mudlij b. Murra. When Banu Khudhayma b. 'Amir b. 'Abd Manaf b. Kinana saw them approach, they went for their weapons, but Khalid told them, 'Put down your arms; people have accepted Islam.' Ibn Ishaq continued that a companion of his, a scholar from Banu Jadhima, told him, 'When Khalid asked us to put down our arms one of us, a man named Jahdam, said, 'Take care, Banu Jadhima - it's Khalid! I swear by God, all there will be after laying down your arms will be captivity; and after that there will be execution! By God, I'll never lay down my arms!' He was seized by some of his people, who asked, 'Jahdam, do you want to spill our blood? People have (all) accepted Islam now and warfare is over. People are safe now.' They kept on at him until they had taken away his weapons. They disarmed him because of what Khalid had said.' Ibn Ishaq went on to report that Hakim b. Hakim quoted Abu Ja'far as saying further, 'When they had put down their weapons, Khalid ordered that they be bound and he then put a number of them to the sword. When news of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he raised his hands to heaven and said, 'O God, I declare to You my innocence of what Khalid b. al-Walid has done.''
lessons: Even within righteous movements, individuals in authority can make grave errors of judgment, and accountability must follow swiftly.
A leader's public disavowal of wrongdoing done in his name demonstrates that justice and integrity matter more than loyalty to individuals.
Warnings from those with foresight, even when initially dismissed, should not be ignored without careful consideration.
practical_advice: When you are in a position of authority or responsibility, remain vigilant against allowing those acting on your behalf to cause harm — and be willing to take accountability publicly when harm occurs.
If you witness wrongdoing, do not remain silent out of fear of conflict; those who spoke up against Khalid's actions demonstrated moral courage.
When you make or observe a serious mistake, respond as the Prophet did — with immediate, sincere acknowledgment and a genuine effort to make things right.

title: The Prophet's Public Disavowal and Ali's Mission to Compensate the Victims of Khalid's Error
summary: After Khalid ibn al-Walid unlawfully killed members of Banu Jadhima, the Prophet sent Ali ibn Abi Talib with money to pay blood compensation for every loss, including minor ones, publicly declaring his innocence of Khalid's actions three times.
story: Ibn Hisham stated that a scholar told him that one man from that tribe escaped and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and when the latter had been told what had happened, He asked, 'Did anyone argue with him?' The man replied that a man of pale complexion and medium stature had argued with him, but Khalid rebuked him and shut him up. Another man, tall and excitable, had also argued with him and they disputed fiercely. 'Umar b. al-Khattab said, 'The first man, Messenger of God, was my son 'Abd Allah, the other was Salim, the freed-man of Abu Hudhayfa.' Ibn Ishaq went on to say that Hakim b. Hakim further quoted Abu Ja'far as saying, 'Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) called for 'Ali b. Abi Talib and told him, 'Ali, go out to those people and make enquiries. Put an end to the practices of the jahiliyya.' 'Ali went out to them carrying money the Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent with him. He paid them compensation for the deaths and for their material losses, even paying them for a dog's howl! Having met all these obligations, he still had some money left. He asked them whether there was anyone else to be paid for lost blood or damages and they said not. He then gave them the remaining money on behalf of the Messenger of God (SAAS), in case any other demands that neither he nor they were aware of should be made later. Having done this, he returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and reported to him. 'You acted correctly and very well,' he told him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then arose and, facing the qibla and stretching his arms out wide so that his armpits were visible, he said, 'O God, I declare to You that I am innocent of what Khalid b. al-Walid did.' He spoke these words three times.'
lessons: True justice requires going beyond the minimum — Ali's payment even for a dog's howl reflects the depth of moral seriousness the Prophet demanded in making amends.
A leader who publicly and repeatedly disavows wrongdoing sends an unmistakable message that no individual is above accountability.
Repairing harm done to others, even when one was not personally responsible, is an act of profound moral integrity and mercy.
practical_advice: When someone is harmed through you or your community — even unintentionally — respond with thorough, generous restitution rather than the bare minimum required.
Do not allow guilt or defensiveness to prevent you from making wrongs right; the Prophet's example shows that acknowledgment and repair are acts of strength, not weakness.
In your own life, when you identify harm that has been caused, act swiftly and thoroughly to address it, and leave no stone unturned in ensuring justice is done.

title: Ibn Umar's Refusal to Kill His Prisoner and the Dispute Over Khalid's Orders
summary: When Khalid ibn al-Walid ordered each Muslim soldier to kill his prisoner from Banu Jadhima the morning after their capture, Ibn Umar and his men refused, demonstrating individual moral conscience in the face of unjust command.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Razzaq related to him, quoting Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, from Salim b. 'Abd Allah b. Umar, from Ibn Umar, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Khalid b. al-Walid to Banu — I think he said this — Jadhima, and he invited them to enter Islam. But they did not respond properly by saying aslamna, 'we accept Islam', but instead began saying 'saba'na! saba'na!' Khalid then took some prisoner and killed others. He gave to each of our men a prisoner and next morning Khalid ordered that each man of us should kill his prisoner.' Ibn Umar went on, 'I said, 'I swear by God, I'll not kill my prisoner! Nor will any of my men kill theirs either!''
lessons: Moral courage means refusing to participate in injustice even when ordered by someone in authority over you.
The ability to distinguish between a lawful command and an unlawful one is a mark of mature conscience and genuine faith.
Standing firm in what is right, even when surrounded by compliance, protects one's integrity and one's relationship with God.
practical_advice: When you are pressured to act in a way that violates your conscience or moral values, take a clear and firm stand as Ibn Umar did — say 'I will not do this' without equivocation.
Reflect regularly on the difference between loyalty to people and loyalty to principle; true loyalty to a leader does not mean abandoning your moral compass.
If you find yourself in a situation where a group is moving toward harm, you do not have to go along — your individual refusal matters and can influence others.

title: Khalid ibn al-Walid's Killing of Banu Jadhima and the Prophet's Disavowal
summary: Khalid ibn al-Walid mistakenly killed members of Banu Jadhima after they failed to properly declare their acceptance of Islam. The Prophet publicly disavowed Khalid's actions and paid blood money for those killed.
story: Bana - I think he said this -Jadhima, and he invited them to enter Islam. But they did not respond properly by saying aslamnd, 'we accept Islam', but instead began saying '~aPnz! ~aba'na!' Khalid then took some prisoner and killed others. He gave to each of our men a prisoner and next morning Khalid ordered that each man of us should kill his prisoner. Ibu Wmar went on, 'I said, "I swear by God, I'll not kill my prisoner! Nor will any of my men kill theirs either!"' The account continues that they then went to the Prophet (SAAS) and told him what Khalid had done. He responded by raising his hands and saying, 'O God, I declare to You that I am innocent of what Khalid has done!' He said this twice. Al-Bukhari and al-Nasa'i related this from a hadith of 'Abd al-Razzaq in similar terms. Ibn Ishaq stated, 'Jaljdam said to them, when he saw what Khalid was doing, "O Banu Jadhima, the die is cast and lost; I warned you of what had happened to you."' Ibn Ishaq went on, 'I have heard that there were angry words between Khalid and 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf about this. 'Abd al-Rahman told him, "You have behaved in Islam as though in the jahiliyya!" "But I only took revenge for your father," Khalid replied. 'Abd al-Rahman responded, "You lie! I have already killed my father's murderer. It's your uncle, al-Fakih b. al-Mughira, whom you have avenged!" Ill feeling arose between them over this. "When news of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he said, 'Take it easy, Khalid! And leave my Companions alone! I swear, that if you had a mountain like Uhud of gold and then spent it in God's cause, you'd not attain a morning and evening's worth of what my Companions have done!'"' Ibn Ishaq then went on to relate the story of al-Fakih b. al-Mughira b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar b. Makhzum, the uncle of Khalid b. al-Walid. He had left on a business trip to Yemen in the company of 'Awf b. 'Abd 'Awf b. 'Abd al-Harith b. Zahra, this man's son 'Abd al-Rahman, along with 'Affan b. Abi al-'As b. Umayya b. 'Abd Shams and his son 'Uthman. When they returned they brought with them some possessions of a man of Banu Jadhima who had died in Yemen. They brought these effects to his heirs. One of their men, a man named Khalid b. Hisham, claimed them and met them on Banu Jadhima territory, asking for the dead man's possessions before they reached the family of the latter. When they refused to hand them over he attacked them and in the fighting 'Awf and Fakih were killed and their goods were seized. 'Abd al-Rahman killed the man, Khalid b. Hisham, who had slain his father, while 'Affan and his son 'Uthman fled to Mecca. Quraysh were about to attack Banu Jadhima when the latter sent apology to them insisting that the attack had not been their fault. They returned the two dead men and their property and so warfare was averted. This, then, is why Khalid told 'Abd al-Rahman that he had avenged his father by killing Banu Jadhima and why 'Abd al-Rahman responded by saying that he had killed his father's murderer. And so he went on to charge that Khalid was merely avenging his uncle al-Fakih b. al-Mughira when they killed him and took his possessions. What was really thought by both men was that he, 'Abd al-Rahman, had not intended any of this and that the charge had only arisen due to their conflict. Khalid had only sought to render Islam and its side victorious, even though he was in error in thinking that they had been demeaning Islam by saying, saba'na! saba'na! He did not realize that they had accepted Islam, and so he killed a large number of them and took the rest captive. Most of the captives were killed as well. The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not take his command away but left him in charge. He did, however, declare his own innocence of what Khalid had done and paid compensation for the loss of life and property. In this there is substantiation of a statement made by scholars that the mistake of the Imam rests with his treasury, not with his own funds! But God knows best. This is why the Caliph Abu Bakr did not dismiss him when he killed Malik b. Nuwayra during the ridda wars, first executing him, then choosing for himself Malik's wife Umm Tamim. 'Umar b. al-Khattab then advised, 'Dismiss him; there's a fault in his sword!' Abu Bakr replied, 'I'll not sheathe a sword that God drew against the polytheists.'
lessons: Leaders bear moral responsibility for publicly disavowing wrongdoing committed by those under their command, even without dismissing them from duty.
Misunderstanding someone's sincere declaration of faith or intention can lead to grave injustice — we must err on the side of mercy when there is ambiguity.
Personal vendettas and old tribal grievances can corrupt even well-intentioned actions; Islam demands that we separate justice from revenge.
practical_advice: When you are in a position of authority and someone in your care makes a serious error, publicly acknowledge what went wrong without letting it define the entire mission or relationship.
Before acting on an assumption about someone's intentions — especially in conflict — pause and seek clarification, as misreading sincerity can cause irreversible harm.
Examine your motivations carefully before making a major decision: ask yourself honestly whether you are acting for principle or for personal grievance.

title: Ibn 'Umar Refuses to Kill His Prisoner from Banu Jadhima
summary: When Khalid ibn al-Walid ordered each soldier to kill his prisoner from Banu Jadhima, Ibn 'Umar refused outright and ensured none of his men complied, then reported the incident directly to the Prophet.
story: He gave to each of our men a prisoner and next morning Khalid ordered that each man of us should kill his prisoner. Ibn 'Umar went on, 'I said, "I swear by God, I'll not kill my prisoner! Nor will any of my men kill theirs either!"' The account continues that they then went to the Prophet (SAAS) and told him what Khalid had done. He responded by raising his hands and saying, 'O God, I declare to You that I am innocent of what Khalid has done!' He said this twice.
lessons: Moral courage means refusing an unjust order even when it comes from a commander, and bearing whatever consequences follow.
Speaking truth to authority — going directly to the highest level of leadership to report wrongdoing — is an act of integrity, not disloyalty.
One person's principled stand can set an example that protects others and shapes the course of justice.
practical_advice: If you are ever instructed to do something you believe is clearly wrong, state your refusal clearly and calmly, then seek accountability through legitimate channels rather than staying silent.
Don't let peer pressure or group momentum pull you into actions that violate your conscience — as Ibn 'Umar showed, you can hold your ground and bring others with you.
When you witness an injustice, bring it to the attention of someone with the authority to address it, rather than assuming someone else will speak up.

title: The Prophet Defends the Honor of His Early Companions Against Khalid's Slight
summary: After Khalid ibn al-Walid's conflict with 'Abd al-Rahman ibn 'Awf, the Prophet firmly defended the irreplaceable status of his early Companions, declaring that even a mountain of gold spent in God's cause could not equal a single day of what they had endured.
story: 'When news of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he said, "Take it easy, Khalid! And leave my Companions alone! I swear, that if you had a mountain like Uhud of gold and then spent it in God's cause, you'd not attain a morning and evening's worth of what my Companions have done!"'
lessons: Those who sacrifice in the earliest and hardest stages of any righteous cause hold a unique and irreplaceable honor that later success cannot replicate.
Material wealth and generous giving, however vast, cannot substitute for the patient endurance and sacrifice of those who stood firm when it was most difficult.
True leadership includes protecting the dignity of those who gave the most when they had the least.
practical_advice: Reflect on the people in your life who supported you before you had anything to offer in return — honor them, protect their reputation, and never let new success diminish their standing.
When you feel frustrated with someone older or more experienced than you, pause before criticizing and consider what they may have sacrificed that you never witnessed.
Measure your gratitude not only by what people give you materially, but by the courage and loyalty they showed in difficult times.

title: The Tragic Love Story of the Young Man of Banu Jadhima and Hubaysh
summary: A young prisoner of Banu Jadhima asked to be led to his beloved Hubaysh one last time before his execution, recited verses of his undying love to her, and was beheaded — after which Hubaysh threw herself upon him and died of grief.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that Ya'qub b. 'Utba b. al-Mughira b. al-Akhnas related to him, from al-Zuhri, from Ibn Abi Hadrad al-Aslami, who said, 'That day I was with the cavalry of Khalid b. al-Walid and a young man of Banu Jadhima, of about my own age, whose hands had been tied up to his neck with a rope, spoke to me. The women were all gathered together a little way off. He asked me, "Take me by the rope and lead me over to where the women are so that I can say something, and then bring me back and you can do with me whatever you will." I replied that what he had asked was easily granted and took his rope and led him over to stand near them. He then said, "Hubaysh, accept that life is at an end." He then spoke the following verses, "Don't you agree that when I sought and found you at Hilya or came to you at al-Khawaniq was it not right to reward a lover who had travelled hard through long nights and hot days? There was no blame in my saying, when our peoples were together, 'Grant me love, before some tragedy occurs.' 'Grant me love before distance intervenes and the chief leads my lover away.' I never betrayed our secret pact and, after meeting you, was never attracted by another. And even when distracted from love by tribal matters, my passion persisted." She replied, "May you live on for ten, or nine odd numbered years and then eight more in succession!" I then led him back and he was beheaded.' Ibn Ishaq went on to relate, quoting Abu Firas b. Abi Sanbala al-Aslami who quoted some of their sheikhs, from others who were present there, to the effect that the girl, Hubaysh, went over to him when he was beheaded and bent down over, kissing him until she herself died right there with him!
lessons: Human love and loyalty can endure to the very edge of death — this capacity for devotion is a reminder of the depth of what God has placed in the human heart.
Even in the gravest moments, preserving one's dignity and expressing what is true in the heart is a deeply human act that transcends circumstances.
The tragic consequences of unjust killing ripple far beyond the immediate victim, claiming more lives through grief and despair.
practical_advice: When facing something painful and final, find a way to express what is most important to you — leaving words of love, truth, or meaning behind is not weakness but courage.
Allow yourself to grieve losses fully and genuinely; unexpressed grief does not disappear but grows heavier over time.
If you are witnessing someone else's pain or final moments, as Ibn Abi Hadrad did, extending small acts of compassion — even just granting a simple request — carries profound weight.

title: The Prophet's Rebuke Upon Hearing That No Mercy Was Shown to the Lovers
summary: When soldiers returned and reported that a captured man had declared his love for a woman and was then beheaded while she died of grief beside him, the Prophet rebuked them, asking whether there was not even one merciful person among them.
story: Al-Bayhaqi proceeded to relate, through Abu 'Abd al-Rahman al-Nasa'i, who quoted Muhammad b. 'Ali b. Harb al-Marwazi, quoting 'Ali b. al-Husayn b. Waqid, from his father, from Yazid al-Nahwi, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent forth an expedition that captured booty, along with a man. He told the raiders that he did not belong there and was in love with a woman he had been pursuing. He asked to see her one more time, and that then they could do with him whatever they wished. A tall woman of dark complexion was there and he said to her, 'Accept, Hubaysh, before life comes to an end.' He then spoke two verses. She responded, 'Yes, I would sacrifice myself for you!' The narration continued 'When they led him away and beheaded him, she came and threw herself upon him, sighed deeply once or twice and died. When they arrived back and reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS) he exclaimed, "Was there not one man of mercy among you?"'
lessons: The Prophet's standard for his followers was not merely avoiding prohibited acts but actively embodying mercy wherever possible.
Mercy is not a sign of weakness in difficult situations — it is a divine quality that God's Messenger expected to find expressed even on the battlefield.
When we fail to show compassion in moments where it was possible, we must honestly reckon with that failure rather than justify it.
practical_advice: Ask yourself regularly: in the situations I face today, am I choosing the merciful option when it is available to me, or defaulting to harshness out of habit or fear?
The Prophet's question — 'Was there not one man of mercy among you?' — is worth internalizing as a personal standard: could that question be asked of me in any situation I am navigating?
If you have acted without mercy in a past situation and regret it, acknowledge it honestly, make amends where possible, and commit to responding differently in the future.

title: Khalid ibn al-Walid Destroys the Idol al-'Uzza at al-Nakhla
summary: The Prophet dispatched Khalid ibn al-Walid to destroy the idol al-'Uzza, a shrine revered by Quraysh and other Arab tribes. Khalid initially returned without completing the mission and was sent back, where he encountered and defeated a demonic apparition before fully demolishing the shrine.
story: Ibn Jarir stated that its destruction occurred five days before the end of Ramadan that year. Ibn Ishaq stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then dispatched Khalid b. al-Walid to al-'Uzza. There was a building at a palm-grove that Quraysh, Kinana and Mudar revered. Its custodians and guardians were of Banu Shayban, of Banu Sulaym, allies of Banu Hashim. When the Sulami who was its guardian heard that Khalid was coming, he hung his sword upon the Goddess and hurried away up the mountain where she stood, saying, "O 'Azz, make an all-out attack on Khalid; throw aside your veil and roll up your sleeves! O 'Azz, if you don't kill that man, Khalid, then you will meet swift punishment or convert to Islam!" When Khalid reached there he destroyed it, then returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS).' Al-Waqidi and others related that when he arrived there, it was five days prior to the end of Ramadan. Having smashed it, he returned and reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who asked him, 'And what was it you saw?' 'I saw nothing,' he replied. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent him back and when he arrived there he saw a black female emerge from the temple, her hair dishevelled and wailing. He then put her to the sword and spoke the verse, 'O 'Uzza, and such is for disbelief, and not glorifying God; I see that God has indeed disgraced you.' He then went on to lay waste to that temple in which she had been. He also confiscated the valuables inside it, may God be pleased with him and give him pleasure. He then returned and informed the Messenger of God (SAAS), who said, 'That was al-'Uzza; she'll not be worshipped again.' Al-Bayhaqi stated that the jurist Muhammad b. Abi Bakr informed him, quoting Muhammad b. Abi Ja'far, quoting Ahmad b. 'Ali, quoting Abu Kurayb, from Ibn Fudayl, from al-Wali b. Jami', from Abu al-Tufayl, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had conquered Mecca, he sent Khalid b. al-Walid out to al-Nakhla, where al-'Uzza was. It consisted of a shrine positioned above three acacia trees. He cut down the trees and destroyed the shrine and returned and reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS), but he told him, "Go back; you have done nothing." Khalid did so and when the temple guardians saw him they hurried away.'
lessons: Incomplete obedience — doing part of what is asked but not fully — requires us to return and finish what was entrusted to us, without pride or defensiveness.
False powers and idols, no matter how long they have been venerated or how fearsome they appear, hold no ultimate authority in the face of sincere faith and God's command.
The Conquest of Makkah represented not just a military victory but a spiritual liberation — the systematic dismantling of everything that had kept people from worshipping God alone.
practical_advice: When a trusted mentor or guide tells you that a task is not yet complete, resist the urge to defend your effort and instead return with fresh eyes to finish the work properly.
Identify the 'idols' in your own life — habits, fears, attachments, or false certainties — that you have been worshipping in place of what truly matters, and take concrete steps to dismantle them.
Approach spiritual and personal challenges with the same decisiveness Khalid showed: name what you are confronting, act clearly, and report back honestly on what you found.

title: Destruction of al-'Uzza at al-Nakhla
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet sent Khalid ibn al-Walid to destroy the idol al-'Uzza at al-Nakhla. After cutting down the trees and destroying the shrine, the Prophet sent him back, where he encountered and slew a demonic manifestation of the idol.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had conquered Mecca, he sent Khalid b. al-Walid out to al-Nakhla, where al-'Uzza was. It consisted of a shrine positioned above three acacia trees. He cut down the trees and destroyed the shrine and returned and reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS), but he told him, 'Go back; you have done nothing.' Khalid did so and when the temple guardians saw him they hurried off up into the mountain, saying, 'O 'Uzza, cut him down! O 'Uzza, disgrace him! If you don't, you'll have to die!' When Khalid reached there he found a naked female with dishevelled hair piling dirt over her head and face. He put her to the sword and killed her, then returned and reported to the Prophet (SAAS), who commented, 'Yes, that was al-'Uzza.'
lessons: Obedience to prophetic guidance is essential even when one believes a task is already complete — thoroughness in fulfilling a mission matters.
The forces of falsehood may present themselves in frightening or disturbing forms, but truth and divine support are stronger.
Removing the roots of corruption and idolatry requires courage and perseverance, not just surface-level action.
practical_advice: When you feel you have resolved a problem, seek a second opinion or reflect more deeply — sometimes what appears finished still has unresolved elements beneath the surface.
When you face intimidating or spiritually daunting challenges, remember that courage combined with reliance on God is more powerful than fear of the unknown.
Do not be deterred by opposition or threats when pursuing what is right; steady resolve in the face of adversity is a mark of true character.

title: The Prophet's Stay in Mecca After the Conquest and Shortening of Prayers
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet and his companions remained in the city for a period of days during which they shortened their prayers, as travelers do, prompting scholarly discussion on the rules governing prayer shortening during extended stays.
story: There is no dispute over his having remained for the rest of Ramadan, shortening the prayers and breaking the fast. This is used by some scholars to substantiate their view that travellers who do not resolve upon remaining may shorten the prayers and fast for 18 days, according to one view. According to the other, it is determined by the place where he is. Al-Bukhari stated that Abu Nu'aym related to him, quoting Sufyan, quoting Qabisa, quoting Sufyan, from Yahya b. Abi Ishaq, from Anas b. Malik, who said, 'We stayed 10 days with the Messenger of God (SAAS), shortening the prayers.' The other scholars related this from numerous lines, from Yahya b. Abi Ishaq al-Hadrami al-Basri, from Anas, in similar words. Al-Bukhari went on to state that it was related to him by 'Abdan, quoting 'Abd Allah, quoting 'Asim, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained there for 19 days, performing prayers consisting of two rak'as.' In the text of Abu Da'ud the period of the stay was 17 days. Ahmad b. Yunus related to us, quoting Ahmad b. Shihab, from 'Asim, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'We stayed with the Messenger of God (SAAS), while travelling for 19 days, shortening the prayers.' Ibn 'Abbas stated, 'We therefore shorten the prayer if we stop over for less than 19 days. If our stay is longer, however, we complete the prayer normally.' Abu Da'ud stated that Ibrahim b. Musa related to him, quoting Ibn 'Aliyya, quoting 'Ali b. Zayd, from Abu Nadra, from 'Imran b. Husayn as follows, 'I went on military expeditions with the Messenger of God (SAAS) and was present with him at the conquest. He remained (there) for 18 nights performing prayers consisting of only two rak'as, saying, "You residents, pray four rak'as; we are travellers."' Abu Da'ud then related from a hadith of Muhammad b. Ishaq, from al-Zuhri, from 'Ubayd Allah b. 'Abd Allah, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained there 15 nights performing shortened prayers.' Ibn Idris stated, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from al-Zuhri and Muhammad b. 'Ali b. al-Husayn, and 'Asim b. 'Amr b. Qatada, and 'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr, and 'Amr b. Shu'ayb and others, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained at Mecca for 15 nights.'
lessons: Islamic law accommodates human circumstances — the concession of shortened prayer during travel reflects God's mercy and understanding of human limitation.
Scholarly differences in narration and interpretation are a natural part of the living tradition of knowledge, and should be approached with humility.
Even in victory and celebration, the Prophet maintained his religious obligations consistently, setting an example of devotion regardless of circumstances.
practical_advice: When you are going through a period of transition or travel — whether physical or emotional — do not abandon your spiritual practices; adapt them to your situation as the Prophet did.
When you encounter differing opinions or uncertainty, approach the question with patience and seek guidance from trustworthy sources rather than abandoning the practice altogether.
Let the Prophet's consistency in worship during extraordinary circumstances inspire you to maintain your own routines of prayer and reflection even during busy or unsettled times.

title: The Ruling on the Son of Zam'a's Slave Girl During the Conquest
summary: During the conquest of Mecca, a dispute arose over the lineage of the son of Zam'a's slave girl, claimed by Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas as his brother's son and by 'Abd ibn Zam'a as his brother. The Prophet ruled on the basis of established lineage and then commanded his wife Sawda to veil before the boy.
story: 'Utba b. Abi Waqqas promised his brother Sa'd that he would gain custody of the son of Zam'a's slave girl. 'Utba had said that the boy was his own son. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Mecca during its conquest, Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas took the boy to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and 'Abd b. Zam'a went with him. Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas stated, 'This is the son of my brother; he has sworn to me that he is his son.' 'Abd b. Zam'a said, 'Messenger of God, this is my brother; he is Zam'a's son and was born on his bed.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) looked at the son of Zam'a's slave girl and found him to be very like 'Utba b. Abi Waqqas. He then said, 'He is your brother, 'Abd b. Zam'a, because he was born on his (your father's) bed.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said (to his wife), 'And you shall put on your veil before him, Sawda!' This was because of the resemblance he saw in him to 'Utba b. Abi Waqqas. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'A child relates to a bed; for a fornicator there is a stone.' Ibn Shihab stated, 'Abu Hurayra used to affirm this hadith in public.'
lessons: Justice requires looking at established facts and principles rather than outward appearances or personal claims alone.
The Prophet's rulings demonstrate how to navigate complex family disputes with both fairness and wisdom, even when the truth is apparent but legally superseded by principle.
Prophetic wisdom balances legal rulings with practical caution — acknowledging apparent reality while also protecting social order and dignity.
practical_advice: In family disputes or conflicts involving competing claims, seek guidance from established principles and trusted authorities rather than making decisions based solely on emotion or appearances.

When a situation contains moral complexity, do not dismiss the nuance — the Prophet himself acknowledged the resemblance while still ruling by principle, showing that recognizing complexity and acting rightly are not mutually exclusive.
Protect the dignity of all involved in difficult situations; the Prophet's instruction to Sawda shows his care for her comfort even within a legal ruling.

title: The Prophet's Address on Equal Application of the Law — The Case of the Noblewoman Who Stole
summary: During the conquest of Mecca, a woman of noble birth committed theft and her family sought intercession through Usama ibn Zayd. The Prophet rejected the intercession and delivered a powerful address affirming that the law of God applies equally to all, before ordering the woman's hand to be cut off.
story: While the Messenger of God (SAAS) was active on the conquest of Mecca, a woman committed theft and her family asked Usama b. Zayd to intercede (and seek clemency) for her. When Usama brought the matter up with him, the face of the Messenger of God (SAAS) changed colour and he demanded, 'Are you talking to me about a punishment legally prescribed by God?' Usama replied, 'Please seek God's forgiveness for me, Messenger of God!' That evening the Messenger of God (SAAS) arose to make an address. He gave praise to God and went on to say, 'Now then, peoples before you were ruined because if one of them of noble birth were to steal, he would be excused, whereas if a person without power were to do so the penalties of the law would be applied against him. By Him in whose hand rests the soul of Muhammad, if Fatima, the daughter of Muhammad were to steal, I would cut off her hand!' He then ordered that the hand of the woman be amputated. Thereafter she was truly repentant and married. 'A'isha said, 'After that she would sometimes come and visit and I would pass on requests from her to the Messenger of God (SAAS).'
lessons: Justice must be blind to privilege and status — the law of God applies equally to the powerful and the powerless, the noble and the ordinary.
True repentance and personal transformation are possible even after serious wrongdoing; the woman's story did not end at punishment but continued in redemption.
Interceding to circumvent divine law, even with good intentions, is itself a serious error — compassion must not compromise justice.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether you hold yourself and those close to you to the same standards you expect of others — genuine integrity means applying the same principles to yourself as to anyone else.

If you have made a serious mistake, know that repentance, accountability, and a changed life are always possible — the woman in this story went on to live a good life after facing the consequences of her actions.
When you feel tempted to seek special treatment or to use your connections to escape accountability, consider whether doing so would undermine the fairness and trust that hold communities together.

title: Prohibition of Mut'a (Temporary Marriage) at the Conquest of Mecca
summary: At the time of the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet permitted temporary marriage briefly but then definitively forbade it, declaring it prohibited until the Day of Resurrection, though scholars differ on the precise history and number of times the practice was permitted and then forbidden.
story: In the Sahih collection of Muslim there is a hadith of Sabra b. Ma'bad al-Juhani which says, 'At the time of the conquest of Mecca, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told us to engage in al-mut'a, temporary marriage, when he entered (the town) but by the time he left it he forbade it (the practice).' In one account the Prophet (SAAS) is quoted as saying, 'It is certainly haram, forbidden; it is haram from this day and on to the day of resurrection.' In an account in the hadith collection of Ahmad (b. Hanbal), the musnad, as well as in the sunan, it is stated that this (the prohibition of mut'a) came at the hajjat al-wada', the 'farewell pilgrimage'. But God knows best. In the Sahih collection of Muslim, it is stated, on a line of transmission from Abu Bakr b. Abi Shayba, from Yunus b. Muhammad, from 'Abd al-Wahid b. Ziyad, from Abu al-'Umays, from Iyas b. Salama b. Salama b. al-Akwa', from his father, who said, 'In the year of awtas, the Messenger of God (SAAS) condoned temporary marriage with women of three nights duration, but he then forbade it.' Al-Bayhaqi stated, 'The "year of awtas" means the year of the conquest.' This and the hadith of Sabra are identical. I note that those who establish its prohibition in the expedition against Khaybar say that it was twice made permissible and twice prohibited. Al-Shafi'i and others give texts to this effect. The practice is also said to have been both declared permissible and prohibited on more than two occasions. But God knows best. It is also said to have been prohibited only once, on this occasion under discussion, at the conquest of Mecca. It is also said to have been declared permissible only in case of al-darara, 'necessity'; on the basis of this, if necessity exists, it, al-mut'a, is made permissible. This view is attributed to Imam Ahmad. It is also said that the practice was not absolutely forbidden, but should be considered 'ala al-ibaha, 'permissible'. This is well-known as the position of Ibn 'Abbas and his companions, as well as that of a group of the Companions (of the Messenger of God (SAAS)). Discussion of this occurs in the work al-Ahkam.
lessons: Islamic law developed progressively, with certain rulings evolving over time in response to circumstances, and then being definitively settled — this reflects divine wisdom in guiding a community through transformation.
Scholarly differences on legal questions should be engaged with seriousness, humility, and a recognition that the tradition of careful inquiry is itself a form of reverence for divine guidance.

The Prophet's decisive prohibition reflects that what may have been temporarily accommodated during hardship is not the final or ideal standard — growth and moral elevation are the direction of divine guidance.
practical_advice: When you encounter differing scholarly opinions on religious matters, approach them with curiosity and respect rather than confusion or dismissiveness — seek reliable guidance from knowledgeable scholars.
Recognize that moral and spiritual maturity involves accepting refinements to your understanding and practice over time, just as the early Muslim community grew into fuller observance.
Do not use historical flexibility or scholarly disagreement as justification for ignoring clear guidance — the spirit of the prophetic example is always toward greater integrity and discipline.

title: The Prophet Accepts Pledges of Allegiance at Mt. al-Safa After the Conquest
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, the Prophet sat at Mt. al-Safa and received pledges of allegiance from both men and women of Mecca, including the repentant Hind bint 'Utba, who came veiled and fearful of punishment for her past actions.
story: Imam Ahmad stated, ''Abd al-Razzaq related to us, quoting Ibn Jurayj, quoting 'Abd Allah b. 'Uthman b. Khuthaym, (who said) that Muhammad b. al-Aswad b. Khalaf related to him that his father, al-Aswad, saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) accepting pledges of allegiance on the day of the conquest.
