He said, "He was seated with Mt. al-Qarn facing him and was accepting the people for their Islam and their shahada." I asked, "What is meant by shahada?" He replied, "Muhammad b. al-Aswad b. Khalaf related that he received their pledge of allegiance (on their expression of) faith in God and their testimony that 'there is no god but God, and that Muhammad is His servant and His messenger."' Al-Bayhaqi stated, 'People high and low came to him, men and women both, and he accepted their (affirmation of) Islam and their (recitation of the) shahada.' Ibn Jarir stated, 'People then assembled in Mecca to pledge allegiance in Islam to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He was seated before them, so I have been told, on Mt. al-Safa, with 'Umar b. al-Khattab below where he sat. He accepted (their statements) of total obedience to the best of their ability to God and to His messenger. When he had finished receiving the pledges of the men, he accepted those of the women. Among them was Hind, daughter of 'Utba, fully veiled and apologetic in her speech for what she had done to Hamza. She feared that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would punish her for that. When the women came up to the Messenger of God (SAAS), for him to accept their allegiance...'
lessons: The door of repentance and entering into God's mercy is open to all, regardless of past sins — even those who had committed grave wrongs against the Prophet and his family came forward and were received.
True leadership is marked by accessibility and inclusion — the Prophet sat and received every person, high and low, man and woman, affirming their dignity and worth.
The shahada — the testimony of faith — is the foundation upon which community, forgiveness, and new beginnings are built.
practical_advice: No matter what you have done in your past, the example of Hind and others at Mt. al-Safa shows that turning toward God and seeking a new beginning is always possible — take that step.
When you have wronged others, approach reconciliation with humility and genuine remorse, as Hind did when she came veiled and apologetic; sincere contrition opens doors that pride keeps shut.
Reflect on the Prophet's example of receiving all people without discrimination — practice making those around you feel genuinely welcomed and heard, regardless of their background or past.

title: Hind bint Utba's Pledge of Allegiance at the Conquest of Makkah
summary: Hind bint Utba, formerly an enemy of Islam who had mutilated Hamza's body, came veiled and apologetic to give her pledge of allegiance to the Prophet, engaging in a remarkable exchange about the conditions of the pledge.
story: When he had finished receiving the pledges of the men, he accepted those of the women. Among them was Hind, daughter of Utba, fully veiled and apologetic in her speech for what she had done to Hamza. She feared that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would punish her for that. When the women came up to the Messenger of God (SAAS), for him to accept their allegiance, he told them 'Pledge to me that you will not associate anything else with God.' Hind commented, 'Well, I swear, you're making demands of us different from those applied to the men!' He went on, 'And do not steal!' She responded, 'I swear by God, I did occasionally steal a little money from Abu Sufyan; I really didn't know whether that was permissible for us or not.' Abu Sufyan, who was a witness to what she was saying, said, 'You are absolved for whatever you took in the past.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, 'You must be Hind, daughter of Utba?' 'Yes, I am,' she replied. 'Please forgive what is now past, may God forgive you.' 'And you women must swear not to commit adultery,' the Messenger of God (SAAS) went on. 'Does a free woman commit adultery?' Hind asked. 'And do not kill your children,' he continued. She responded, 'Should we raise them from birth, then kill them when grown? You and they know best about this!' At this Umar b. al-Khattab laughed uncontrollably. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'And you shall not make up slanderous and disgraceful stories.' 'Well', Hind replied, 'inventing slander is certainly ugly; a little exaggeration is better!' 'And you must not disobey me,' he went on. Hind responded, 'To do good!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said to Umar, 'Accept their pledge. And may God forgive them. For God is forgiving, merciful.' Umar then accepted their pledges, for the Messenger of God (SAAS) never shook hands with women.
lessons: Even those who have committed grave wrongs against others can find forgiveness and redemption when they sincerely turn toward what is right — no past is too dark for a new beginning.
The Prophet's gentle, dignified manner of receiving even a former enemy demonstrates that true strength lies in mercy and composure, not in retribution.
Accountability before God begins with honesty; Hind's candid admissions during the pledge reflect that genuine repentance requires acknowledging one's wrongs rather than hiding them.
practical_advice: If you carry guilt about past wrongs you have committed against others, reflect on Hind's example: acknowledge what happened with honesty, seek forgiveness sincerely, and then commit to a better path rather than remaining paralyzed by shame.
When you feel anxious that someone you have wronged will not forgive you, take courage and approach them with humility — you may be surprised by the mercy you receive.

Practice self-examination before making commitments or pledges: ask yourself honestly whether you are prepared to fulfill what you are promising, as genuine commitment begins with clear-eyed self-awareness.

title: Hind bint Utba Expresses Her Changed Heart After Accepting Islam
summary: After accepting Islam, Hind bint Utba told the Prophet that there was nothing she had previously wanted more than the humiliation of his supporters, but now there was nothing she wanted more than their success.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related through Yahya b. Bukayr, from al-Layth, from Yunus, from Ibn Shihab, from Urwa, from A'isha, that Hind, daughter of Utba, said, 'Messenger of God, there used to be nothing hidden on the face of the earth that I wanted more than that your supporters be humiliated. But now there's nothing hidden in the back of the earth that I want more than that your supporters be successful.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'And what else, by Him who holds Muhammad's soul in His hand?' She said, 'Messenger of God, Abu Sufyan is a stingy man. Would it be wrong for me to provide food from what he owns?' 'No, but do so bil-ma'ruf, fairly.'
lessons: A sincere change of heart can be so complete that one's deepest desires are reversed — what was once hatred can transform into genuine love and hope for others' wellbeing.
The Prophet's response, 'And what else,' suggests that spiritual transformation opens the heart to continued growth; one moment of genuine turning toward good is only the beginning.
Hind's transformation illustrates that enmity and hostility are not permanent states of the soul — with guidance and conviction, even the hardest hearts can soften.
practical_advice: If you find yourself consumed by resentment or ill-will toward someone or a group, reflect on whether that feeling is truly serving you — genuine peace of heart often requires a willingness to let the resentment go and wish well for others.
When you notice a meaningful change in your own values or attitudes, name it and acknowledge it to yourself; conscious recognition of growth reinforces and deepens it.
Use Hind's example as encouragement that it is never too late to change: no matter how long you have held onto negative attitudes or destructive patterns, transformation is possible.

title: Hind bint Utba Asks the Prophet About Taking Money from Her Husband
summary: Hind bint Utba came to the Prophet complaining that her husband Abu Sufyan was too stingy to provide adequately for her and their children, and asked if it was permissible to take from his wealth without his knowledge.
story: In both Sahih collections it is reported from A'isha that Hind, daughter of Utba, the wife of Abu Sufyan, came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, 'O Messenger of God, Abu Sufyan is a stingy man. He does not give me enough to cover expenses for myself and for my children. Would it be wrong for me to take some of his money without his knowledge?' He replied, 'You may fairly take from his funds enough for yourself and your children.'
lessons: Islam recognizes that individuals, especially those with dependents, have a right to adequate provision, and that this right does not simply disappear due to another person's stinginess or negligence.

The Prophet's practical and compassionate ruling shows that religious guidance is meant to address the real difficulties of daily life, not to leave people helpless in situations of financial hardship.
Seeking guidance from a trustworthy source when facing a difficult ethical dilemma — rather than either suffering in silence or acting rashly — is itself a form of wisdom and responsibility.
practical_advice: If you are in a situation where someone who is obligated to provide for you is failing to do so, seek guidance from appropriate authorities or trusted advisors rather than feeling you must either silently endure or act without clarity.

When financial stress affects your household, address it directly and practically rather than letting resentment build — Hind's approach of bringing the matter to a clear resolution is a model of healthy problem-solving.
Recognize that advocating for your own needs and the needs of those in your care is not a moral failing; doing so with clarity and through proper channels is an act of responsibility.

title: The Prophet's Declaration That Hijra Is Over After the Conquest of Makkah
summary: After the conquest of Makkah, the Prophet declared that the obligation of hijra (migration to join the Muslim community) had ended, though jihad and sincere intention remained, clarifying a significant shift in the nature of Islamic practice.
story: Abu Da'ud stated that Uthman b. Abu Shayba related to him, quoting Jarir, from Mansur, from Mujahid, from Tawus, from Ibn Abbas, who said, 'On the day of the conquest of Mecca, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "(Now) there is no hijra, but there is jihad, striving for God's cause, and niyya, intentions. If you are fought, then fight back."' Al-Bukhari related it from Uthman b. Abu Shayba, and Muslim did so from Yahya b. Yahya, from Jarir. Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Affan, quoting Wahb, quoting Ibn Tawus, from his father, from Safwan b. Umayya, who said that he was told, 'that only those who migrated will enter paradise'. So I said, 'I'll not go home until I ask the Messenger of God (SAAS) what it was he was asked. So I went to him, told him of this and he responded, "There is no hijra after the conquest of Mecca. However, there is jihad and niyya; and if you are fought, then fight back."' These accounts and anecdotes show that the hijra, whether already completed or in general, came to an end after the conquest of Mecca. This was because people had entered into God's religion in large numbers and Islam had become victorious and firmly based; and so there was no hijra any more. Moreover, both jihad and infaq, sacrifice, for God's cause are appropriate and desirable right up to Judgement Day, though not like that jihad and infaq that pertained to the conquest, that is the conquest of Mecca. God, Almighty is He, stated, 'Those of you who made sacrifices and fought prior to the conquest are not equivalent; those are higher in rank than those who made sacrifices and fought thereafter. But for both God has promised reward' (serat al-Hadid; LVII, v. 10).
lessons: Religious obligations evolve in response to changed circumstances — what was once required may no longer be so, while the underlying values of sincere intention and striving for good remain constant.
The end of a particular form of worship or practice does not mean the end of spiritual opportunity; when one door closes, the values it embodied continue to find expression in new ways.

Seeking clarity when you hear something confusing or alarming — as Safwan b. Umayya did by going directly to the Prophet — is a sign of both faith and intellectual integrity; do not let misunderstandings fester.
practical_advice: When you feel confused or unsettled by religious information you have heard, trace it back to reliable sources before acting on it or letting it cause you unnecessary distress.

Recognize that the spirit behind an obligation — sincerity, striving, sacrifice for what is right — often outlasts the specific form that obligation once took; ask yourself how those values are calling you to act in your own life today.

If you are grieving the passing of a particular chapter of life or spiritual practice that gave you meaning, reflect on the enduring values it represented and find ways to embody those values in your present circumstances.

title: Mujashi' Brings His Brother for the Pledge of Hijra and Receives Guidance on What Remains
summary: After the conquest of Makkah, Mujashi' brought his brother to the Prophet to pledge the hijra, and the Prophet explained that the time for that specific pledge had passed, accepting instead a pledge of Islam, faith, and jihad.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Muhammad b. Bashshar related to him, quoting Ghundar, quoting Shu'ba, from Abu Bishr, from Mujahid, who said: After the conquest of Mecca, I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) with my brother and said, 'Messenger of God, I've brought you my brother for you to accept his pledge for the hijra.' He replied, 'Those (credited with) performing the hijra, with all its rewards, have gone.' 'For what will you accept his pledge, then?' I asked. 'I will accept his pledge of allegiance to Islam, to faith and to the jihad.' I later met with Abu Ma'bad, who was the elder of them, and asked him about it. He replied, 'Mujashi' spoke the truth.'
lessons: When one specific path of righteousness closes, God in His wisdom opens others; what matters is that one continues to seek and commit to what is good with sincerity.

The Prophet's compassionate redirection — accepting a pledge of Islam, faith, and striving rather than simply turning the man away — teaches us to meet people where they are and affirm what remains available to them.
Honest acknowledgment of changed realities, rather than clinging to what is no longer possible, allows a person to move forward with clarity and purpose.
practical_advice: If you discover that an opportunity you were counting on is no longer available to you, resist the urge to despair; instead, ask what genuine commitment and striving look like within the circumstances that actually exist.

When guiding someone who is confused or disappointed about what they can or cannot do, follow the Prophet's example: acknowledge what has passed, and clearly affirm the meaningful path that remains open.

Reflect regularly on whether the spiritual commitments you have made — to faith, integrity, and striving for what is right — are being actively honored in your daily life, not just held as abstract ideals.

title: A'isha Explains the Meaning and End of Hijra
summary: A'isha explained to Ubayd b. Umayr that hijra had ended after the conquest of Makkah because Islam was now victorious, but that jihad and sincere intention remained as ongoing obligations.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Ishaq b. Yazid related to him, quoting Yahya b. Hamza, quoting al-Awza'i, from Ata' b. Abu Rabah, who said: I visited A'isha with Ubayd b. Umayr who asked her about the hijra. She replied, 'There is no hijra now. The believers used to flee, each one singly with his religion, to God, Almighty and Glorious is He, and to His messenger, fearing his faith might be subverted. Now God has rendered Islam victorious and the believer worships God wherever he wishes. However, there is jihad and niyya.'
lessons: The original hijra was born out of necessity and fear — a flight to preserve one's faith under conditions of persecution. When those conditions change, the form of the obligation changes with them, even as its spirit endures.

A'isha's articulation that 'the believer worships God wherever he wishes' captures a profound spiritual reality: the conquest of Makkah represented not just a military or political victory but a liberation of spiritual life.
Understanding the wisdom behind a religious obligation — not just its outward form — allows one to honor its spirit even when the specific circumstances that gave rise to it no longer exist.
practical_advice: Reflect on the freedoms you currently have to practice your faith and live according to your values — freedoms that earlier generations may have had to sacrifice greatly to secure — and do not take them for granted.

When trying to understand an Islamic ruling or practice that seems confusing, try to understand the historical and spiritual context that gave rise to it; this will help you grasp both its letter and its spirit.
Ask yourself honestly whether your spiritual striving — your jihad and niyya — is alive and active in your daily life, even if the specific forms it takes look different from those of earlier times.

title: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri Defends the Hadith on Hijra Against Marwan's Intimidation
summary: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri recounted a hadith of the Prophet about the end of hijra, but when Marwan challenged him, he was vindicated by the very companions Marwan cited, who only confirmed the truth once they saw the raised whip.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Muhammad b. Ja'far, quoting Shu'ba, from Amr b. Murra, from Abu al-Bakhtari al-Ta'i, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: When the verse, 'when God's victory and the conquest came' (serat al-Nasr; CX, v.1) was revealed, the Messenger of God (SAAS) recited it to its end and then he said, 'The people are good and I and my Companions are good.' He then said, 'There is no hijra after the conquest, but there is jihad and niyya.' Marwan said to him (Abu Sa'id), 'You have lied! With him there were b. Khadij and Zayd b. Thabit, seated with him on the bed.' Abu Sa'id responded, 'If these two wanted, they would tell you it was so. But this one is afraid that you will withdraw his prestige with his people, while this other one is afraid you will withdraw the stipend he receives!' At this Marwan raised his whip to strike him and when the two men saw this, they said, 'He spoke the truth!'
lessons: Standing firm in truthful testimony, even under threat and intimidation, is one of the highest forms of moral courage — and Abu Sa'id's composure in the face of Marwan's raised whip is a timeless example of that courage.

The willingness to identify clearly why others may be holding back the truth — fear of losing status or material benefit — is itself an act of intellectual and moral clarity, not an attack on persons.
Truth has a way of ultimately vindicating itself; even those who initially stayed silent were eventually compelled to confirm what they knew to be accurate.
practical_advice: When you face pressure to stay silent about something you know to be true, ask yourself what you fear losing — and then ask whether that fear is worth the cost of your integrity.
If you observe that others around you are avoiding the truth out of self-interest, do not simply go along with the silence; speak clearly and let the record stand, as Abu Sa'id did.

Reflect on the times in your own life when social pressure or fear of consequences led you to hold back what you knew; use those reflections not for self-condemnation but as material for growing in courage.

title: Ibn Abbas Interprets the Verse of Victory as a Sign of the Prophet's Approaching Death
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab gathered senior companions of Badr and asked them to interpret the verse 'When God's victory and the conquest come.' Ibn Abbas, a young boy, interpreted it as a divine announcement of the Prophet's approaching death, which Umar confirmed as the correct understanding.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that it was related to him by Musa b. Isma'il, quoting Abu 'Awana, from Abu Bishr, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "Umar would take me in with the sheikhs who had been at Badr. One of them seemed annoyed and said, 'Why do you let him come in with us, when we have sons like him?' Umar replied, 'But you know who he is.' One day Umar invited them and I went in with them; I did not realize that he had only included me to show them something. Umar asked, 'What do you have to say about the statement of God, Almighty and Glorious, "When God's victory and the conquest come"?' One of the sheikhs replied, 'We are ordered to praise God and to seek His forgiveness if we are aided and given victory.' Others of them were silent, saying nothing. Umar asked, 'Is that what you say, Ibn 'Abbas?' 'No,' I replied. 'Well, what do you say?' he asked. I replied, 'This refers to the approach of death for the Messenger of God (SAAS), of which God was informing him. (The phrase) "When God's victory and the conquest come" was a sign of your (the Prophet's) death. And so, "give praise to your Lord and ask his forgiveness. He is ever willing to forgive".' At that Umar said (to the sheikhs) 'I do not know anything about it other than what he knows.'" Al-Bukhari was alone in recounting this. This is related similarly on more than one line of transmission from Ibn 'Abbas, all to the effect that he interpreted this verse as a reference to the approaching death of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Mujahid, Abu al-'Aliyya, al-Dahhak and others, including Ibn 'Abbas and Umar b. al-Khattab, may God be pleased with them both, also subscribed to this interpretation.
lessons: True knowledge and insight are gifts from God that transcend age or social standing — Ibn Abbas's wisdom surpassed that of senior companions despite his youth.
The Quran carries layered meanings; sincere reflection and spiritual depth are required to perceive its deeper guidance, especially in moments of great significance.
Acknowledging the reality of death, even the death of the most beloved, is an act of spiritual maturity and prepares the believer to live purposefully.
practical_advice: When facing an uncertain or emotionally heavy truth, seek understanding through reflection on your faith's core texts rather than avoiding the difficult reality.
Be open to wisdom regardless of its source — younger people, newer acquaintances, or unexpected voices may carry insights you have not considered.
Use the awareness of life's impermanence as a motivator to express gratitude and seek forgiveness daily, rather than postponing spiritual preparation.

title: The Young Boy 'Amr b. Salama Leads His Tribe in Prayer After the Conquest of Mecca
summary: After the conquest of Mecca prompted widespread acceptance of Islam, a young boy named 'Amr b. Salama, who had memorized Quranic verses from passing caravans, was appointed to lead his tribe in prayer despite being only six or seven years old, and his joy at receiving a proper shirt to cover himself remains one of his most vivid memories.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Sulayman b. Harb related to him, quoting Hammad b. Zayd, from Ayyub, from Abu Qilaba, from 'Amr b. Salama (who said) that Abu Qilaba asked him, "Won't you meet him and ask him?" So I did meet him and did ask him. He replied, "We were at a water-source by which people passed and we would ask them, 'What is wrong with the people? What is that man doing?' They would reply, 'He claims that God has sent him and revealed to him such-and-such.' I would learn those words by heart and would feel as though they were implanted inside me. These Arabs were delaying their acceptance of Islam until the conquest. They would say, 'Leave him for his own people to take care of him! If he does overcome them, then he will be a prophet speaking the truth.' "When the combat at the conquest occurred, each tribe hurried to accept Islam. My father accepted Islam before my tribe did. When he arrived back, he said, 'I swear by God that I have come to you from the Prophet, in truth!' He, (the Prophet (SAAS)) said, 'Perform such-and-such a prayer at such-and-such a time. And when the time for prayer comes, then one of you should call the adhan, the summons to prayer. And then whichever one of you knows the Qur'an best should lead the prayer.' So they enquired but found no one who knew more Qur'an than I did, because of what I had been learning from the passing caravans. "And so they appointed me to lead them in prayer; I was six or seven years old at the time. I used to be dressed in a burda, a short, squared garment, and when I prostrated it would ride up (and expose me). A woman of the tribe asked, 'Couldn't you cover over for us the anus of your reciter?' So they bought cloth and made a shirt for me. And nothing had ever made me so happy as that shirt did!"
lessons: Sincere and consistent effort to seek knowledge — even informally, as 'Amr memorized verses from passing travelers — can lead to being entrusted with great responsibility.
God's guidance reaches people through unexpected means and at unexpected times; the conquest of Mecca became the turning point that opened hearts across Arabia.
Simple acts of dignity and care for others, like making a shirt for a child, carry profound emotional significance and reflect the community spirit Islam fosters.
practical_advice: Do not underestimate the value of knowledge you accumulate quietly over time — it may one day serve a purpose far greater than you imagined.
When you feel that events around you are uncertain or that others are 'waiting to see,' stay consistent in your own spiritual development rather than delaying.
Look for small ways to show consideration for others in your community; a simple gesture of care can become the most cherished memory in someone's life.

title: The Battle of Hunayn: The Muslims' Overconfidence and Their Initial Defeat
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, the tribal confederation of Hawazin under Malik b. 'Awf prepared to fight the Muslims at Hunayn. Despite wise counsel from the elderly veteran Durayd b. al-Simma urging Malik not to bring the tribe's women, children, and possessions into battle, Malik ignored the advice, and the Muslims' initial confidence in their large numbers led to an early devastating retreat.
story: God Almighty stated, "God aided you on many battlefields and at the battle of Hunayn, when your large numbers made you confident, but they were of no use to you and the earth was constrained all about you, spacious though it was! Then you turned back, retreating. Thereafter God sent down his sense of calm upon His messenger and upon the believers and He sent down forces which you did not see and He punished those who disbelieved; that is the reward of unbelievers. Then, after that, God will turn towards whomever He wishes; and God is Forgiving, Merciful" (surat al-Bara'a; IX, v.25-7). Muhammad b. Ishaq b. Yasar related in his book that the departure of the Messenger of God (SAAS) against Hawazin came after the conquest, on the 5th of Shawwal, 8 AH. He also claimed that the conquest took place ten days before the end of Ramadan, fifteen days before his departure against Hawazin. This is related similarly from Ibn Mas'ud. 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr stated this, and Ahmad preferred it, as did Ibn Jarir in his history. Al-Waqidi stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth against Hawazin on the 6th day of Shawwal and he reached Hunayn on the 10th of that month. Abu Bakr, 'the Trusting', stated, 'We'll not be defeated today because of size.'" Then they were defeated; the first defeated were Banu Sulaym, then the Meccans, then the remainder of the force. Ibn Ishaq stated that when Hawazin heard about how God had conquered Mecca for the Messenger of God (SAAS), their leader, Malik b. 'Awf al-Nasri, gathered them together. All of Thaqif assembled with Hawazin and they were joined by Nasr and all Jusham, along with Sa'd b. Bakr and a few men of Banu Hilal. Of Qays 'Ayla, only these were present. Of Hawazin, (the clans of) Ka'b and Kilab did not attend and no noteworthy person of them was there. Among Banu Jusham there was Durayd b. al-Simma, an elderly sheikh who was capable of speaking advice and giving his knowledge of warfare; he was a man of very great experience. Thaqif had two leaders; in the Ahlaf there was Qarib b. al-Aswad b. Mas'ud b. Mu'attib. In Banu Malik there were Dhu al-Khimar Subay' b. al-Harith and his brother Ahmar b. al-Harith. The overall command was under Malik b. 'Awf al-Nasri. Ibn Ishaq's account continued, "When he had made the decision to attack the Messenger of God (SAAS), he had the people bring their possessions along with his force their women and their children with them. When they halted at Awtas, gathered around him; among them was Durayd b. al-Simma in a kind of howdah in which he was borne along. When they halted, Durayd asked, 'In which valley are you?' 'In Awtas,' they replied. He commented, 'A fine place for cavalry! It's not hilly with rocks, nor is it soft ground. Why is it I hear the grunting of camels and the braying of donkeys, the crying of babies and the bleating of sheep?' They replied that Malik b. 'Awf had had the force bring their possessions, women and children with them. 'And where is Malik?' asked Durayd. 'He is here,' they told him and summoned him. "Durayd then said to him, 'Malik, you have become your people's leader and this is a day that will greatly influence the future. Why is it I hear the grunting of camels, the braying of donkeys, the crying of babies and the bleating of sheep?' He replied, 'I had the people bring their children and possessions with them.' 'Why was this?' Durayd asked. Malik replied, 'I wanted every man to have his family and possessions behind him so that he would fight for them.' "On hearing this, Durayd made a sound of disapproval and commented, 'You cowardly sheep grazer! Would anything ever turn around someone in retreat? If the battle is to go well, it will be only men with swords and lances who do you good. If the battle goes badly, you'll be disgraced with regards to your family and possessions anyway.' "Durayd then asked, 'What have Ka'b and Kilab done?' 'No one from them is present,' he replied. 'So then skill and courage are absent! If it were to be a day of fine, noble deeds, Ka'b and Kilab would not miss it. I wish you had done the same as them! Who is attending with you?' They told him that 'Amr b. 'Amir and 'Awf b. 'Amir were present. He responded, 'Well, those two branches of 'Amir will do neither good nor harm.' He then said, 'Malik, you've done nothing (good) by sending ahead the main force of Hawazin to face up to the cavalry.' "Durayd then told Malik b. 'Awf, 'Send them up high to some inaccessible area of their people's territory, then meet the turncoat apostates on horse. If the battle goes in your favour, then your people behind you can join you. If it goes against you, then you will have saved your families and possessions.' "Malik replied, 'By God, I'll not do that! You've grown old and so has your mind!' He went on, 'Hawazin, either you obey me, or I'll press myself down on this sword until it comes out through my back!' He hated Durayd to have any say or credit. They said they would obey him. "Durayd then said, 'This is a battle in which I did not participate, nor did I miss it.' He then spoke the following verses, 'How I wish I were a youth once more! I would speed forth into the battle. I would lead on long-maned horses as though they were young donkeys.' "Malik then told everyone, 'When you see them, then break your swords from their sheaths and'"
lessons: Overconfidence in numbers, resources, or worldly strength is a spiritual trap — true victory belongs to God alone, not to size or preparation.
Wise counsel from experienced voices should never be dismissed out of pride or insecurity; Malik's rejection of Durayd's advice led to the endangerment of his entire people.
Setbacks and initial defeats are part of the believer's journey; what matters is returning to reliance on God, as the Muslims ultimately did at Hunayn through divine tranquility sent upon them.
practical_advice: When you face a significant challenge, guard against complacency born from past successes — each situation requires renewed humility and dependence on God.
Seek counsel from those with experience and wisdom before making major decisions, and resist the urge to dismiss advice that challenges your assumptions or authority.
When you experience an unexpected failure or setback, resist despair; reflect on whether pride or overconfidence played a role, and use that awareness to realign yourself spiritually.

title: Scouts of Hawazin Afflicted with Fear
summary: Malik ibn Awf's scouts returned with dislocated joints after witnessing white-robed riders on piebald horses, a sign of divine intervention, yet even this did not deter Malik from his plan.
story: Umayya b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Uthman related to me that he was told that Malik b. 'Awf sent out some of his men as scouts. When they came back their joints had become dislocated. 'What happened to you?' he asked them. They replied, 'We saw some white men mounted on piebald horses. And all of a sudden, we were afflicted as you see us now.' And, by God, even that did not deter him from his plan.
lessons: Divine signs and warnings are given as mercy, but those whose hearts are hardened may still refuse to heed them.
When God wills to protect His servants, He deploys means beyond human comprehension.
Persisting in a wrong course of action despite clear warnings only deepens one's accountability.
practical_advice: Pay attention to signs and circumstances that seem to warn you away from a harmful path; do not let stubbornness override your better judgment.
When you feel an inexplicable unease before a major decision, take time to pause, reflect, and consult trusted counsel.
Practice the habit of acknowledging when circumstances seem to be working against your plans, and consider whether God may be redirecting you.

title: Abdullah ibn Abi Hadrad Infiltrates Hawazin
summary: The Prophet sent Abdullah ibn Abi Hadrad al-Aslami to infiltrate the Hawazin tribe and gather intelligence on their war plans, which he successfully accomplished and reported back.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) heard about them, he sent out 'Abd Allah b. Abii Hadrad al-Aslami with orders to infiltrate them and report back. He did so, and when he had learned of their plan to wage war against the Messenger of God (SAAS) and heard (of the role of) Malik and Hawazin and their intentions, he came back to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and so informed him.
lessons: Careful preparation and gathering of knowledge before any major undertaking reflects sound leadership and trust in God alongside taking practical means.
Obedience to the Prophet's command, even when the mission is dangerous, is an expression of deep faith.
A believer does not act on emotion alone but gathers information and plans carefully before acting.
practical_advice: Before facing a major challenge, gather as much reliable information as possible rather than acting impulsively.
Trust in God does not mean abandoning practical preparation; combine sincere dua with concrete action.
When given a difficult responsibility, commit to it fully and report back honestly, as Abdullah ibn Abi Hadrad did.

title: Umar Questions Ibn Abi Hadrad's Report
summary: When Ibn Abi Hadrad reported the Hawazin threat, Umar ibn al-Khattab questioned his truthfulness, prompting Ibn Abi Hadrad to defend himself firmly, and the Prophet gently corrected Umar.
story: When Ibn Abii Hadrad returned and reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS) about Hawazin, 'Umar b. al-Khattab questioned his veracity. Ibn Abi Hadrad responded, 'If you call my honesty into question, 'Umar, you'd probably call truth itself into question!' 'Umar asked, 'Do you hear what he is saying, Messenger of God?' He replied, 'You were going astray, but God gave you guidance.'
lessons: Honesty and the courage to defend one's truthfulness, even before a powerful person, is a virtue rooted in integrity.
The Prophet's correction of Umar shows that even the most devoted companions could err, and true leaders correct with wisdom rather than harshness.
A believer should be careful not to let skepticism become an obstacle to receiving important and truthful information.
practical_advice: When someone brings you difficult or unwelcome news, resist the urge to immediately doubt them — evaluate the content fairly before questioning the messenger.
If you are unjustly doubted or accused, calmly and firmly assert your honesty without becoming defensive or aggressive.
Reflect regularly on moments when your assumptions led you astray, and be grateful for those who redirected you with wisdom.

title: Safwan Embraces Islam After the Battle of Hunayn
summary: After the battle, some of Safwan's borrowed armour was lost and the Prophet offered compensation, but Safwan declined, saying that Islam had now entered his heart.
story: When the polytheists were defeated, Safwan's armour was collected and some was found to be missing. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked Safwan, 'We have lost some of your armour. Shall we pay you compensation?' 'No, Messenger of God,' he replied. 'There is now in my heart what was not present that day.'
lessons: Witnessing the character and integrity of the Prophet was itself a path to faith for many people; good character is one of the most powerful forms of invitation to Islam.
Material loss can sometimes be the occasion for a greater spiritual gain.
Faith can enter a heart gradually, and even someone who began as an opponent of Islam could become a sincere believer through continued honest dealings.
practical_advice: Reflect on how your personal integrity and honesty in dealings may be the very thing that opens someone's heart to goodness.
Do not write off people who seem unlikely to change; hearts can transform in ways we do not anticipate.
When something of material value is lost, consider whether what you have gained in character, relationship, or faith may be worth far more.

title: The Muslim Army Departs Mecca for Hunayn
summary: The Prophet led an army of 12,000 men — including 2,000 newly converted Meccans — out of Mecca on the 5th of Shawwal to confront the Hawazin at Hunayn, appointing Attab ibn Usayd, a young man of about 20, as governor of Mecca.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went forth accompanied by 2,000 Meccans and 10,000 of his men who had come out with him and through whom God had conquered Mecca. They totalled 12,000 men. Ibn Ishaq related that he went out from Mecca on the 5th of Shawwal, and that he appointed 'Attab b. Usayd b. Abi al-'As b. Umayya b. 'Abd Shams al-Umawi in command of Mecca. I note that 'Attab was at that time approximately 20 years of age.
lessons: The Prophet's trust in a young leader like Attab demonstrates that competence and character matter more than age or seniority.
Great responsibilities can be entrusted to the young when they are worthy; youth is not a barrier to meaningful service.
Preparation, delegation, and trusting others are essential qualities of effective and faith-driven leadership.
practical_advice: Do not underestimate young people's capacity for responsibility; give them meaningful roles when they have shown character and commitment.
When stepping into a major challenge, ensure that the responsibilities you leave behind are in reliable hands.
Approach large undertakings with careful organization and clear delegation, as the Prophet consistently modeled.

title: Hawazin Ambushes the Muslims in the Valley of Hunayn
summary: Malik ibn Awf and the Hawazin forces arrived at Hunayn before the Muslims and positioned themselves in ambush in the narrow passes of the valley, catching the Muslim army off guard as they descended in the half-light of dawn.
story: Malik b. 'Awf went forth with others to Hunayn, where he arrived before the Messenger of God (SAAS). They prepared and positioned themselves for battle in the narrows and twists of the valley. The Messenger of God (SAAS) and his force arrived in the valley in the half-light of dawn. As the men descended
lessons: Even a large and victorious army can be caught off guard when overconfidence replaces vigilance; humility and preparation must always accompany strength.
Adversity and ambush can come when least expected; what matters most is the response of faith and steadfastness in that moment.
God sometimes allows His servants to face moments of crisis so that they rediscover their dependence on Him rather than on their own numbers or strength.
practical_advice: After periods of success, be especially vigilant against overconfidence — it is often in times of ease that we become most vulnerable.
When you find yourself suddenly overwhelmed or ambushed by a crisis in life, return immediately to prayer and remembrance of God as your first response.
Prepare for challenges in advance rather than assuming that past victories guarantee future safety.

title: The Battle of Hunayn: Initial Rout and the Prophet's Stand
summary: At the Battle of Hunayn, the Muslim army was ambushed in the valley at dawn by Hawazin forces and broke ranks in panic, while the Prophet (SAAS) stood firm calling out to his fleeing men.
story: Malik b. 'Awf went forth with others to Hunayn, where he arrived before the Messenger of God (SAAS). They prepared and positioned themselves for battle in the narrows and twists of the valley. The Messenger of God (SAAS) and his force arrived in the valley in the half-light of dawn. As the men descended the enemy cavalry appeared before them and bore down on them. Our men broke ranks and fled, no one caring about the others. The Messenger of God (SAAS) moved away to the right, calling out, 'Where are you going, men? Come to me; I am the Messenger of God! I am Muhammad, son of 'Abd Allah!' But it did no good. The camels were all over one another.
lessons: In moments of collective panic and chaos, those with strong faith and leadership remain anchored while others flee — steadfastness is a spiritual quality, not just a physical one.
Calling people back to truth and purpose during a crisis is itself an act of profound courage, even when no one initially responds.
Difficult trials can strike even large, well-resourced communities; no group is immune to moments of vulnerability and fear.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by a situation where everyone around you seems to be in panic, pause and anchor yourself to what you know is true and right before reacting.
If you are in a leadership or support role and others are losing composure, speak clearly and calmly — your steadiness can be the turning point even if it takes time to take effect.
Prepare mentally for the possibility that difficult moments may come suddenly and without warning, so that your response is rooted in principle rather than impulse.

title: The Remaining Companions Who Stood Firm at Hunayn
summary: A small group of the Prophet's family and close Companions, including Ali, Abu Bakr, Umar, and al-Abbas, remained steadfast beside the Prophet (SAAS) when the bulk of the army fled at Hunayn.
story: With him a group of men of his family: 'Ali b. Abii Talib, Abu Sufyan b. al-Harith, 'Abd al-Muttalib and his brother Rabi'a b. al-Harith b. 'Abd al-Muttalib and al-Fadl b. al-'Abbas. And, it is said, al-Fadl b. Abu Sufyan, Ayman b. Umm Ayman, Usama b. Zayd, al-'Abbas, along with a group of muhajirin, some include with them Qutham b. al-'Abbas, including Abu Bakr and 'Umar. Al-'Abbas was gripping the mouth-ring of his white donkey that he was riding, having struck it to open its mouth.
lessons: True loyalty is revealed in moments of crisis, not comfort — those who remain when others flee demonstrate the deepest bonds of faith and love.
A small number of sincere and committed people can hold a situation together when the majority falters, illustrating that quality of commitment surpasses quantity.
Being present for someone in their hardest moment — physically, emotionally, or spiritually — is one of the highest forms of human support.
practical_advice: Reflect on who in your life has stood by you during your hardest moments, and make an effort to express gratitude to them.
When those around you are abandoning a just cause or a struggling person, ask yourself whether you have the courage to be among those who stay.
In your own community or family, identify ways to be a stabilising presence during collective stress rather than adding to the anxiety.

title: Ali ibn Abi Talib Strikes Down the Hawazin Standard-Bearer
summary: During the Battle of Hunayn, a Hawazin warrior on a red camel carrying a black banner was wreaking havoc until Ali ibn Abi Talib and an Ansari companion brought him down together.
story: There was one man of Hawazin mounted on a red camel who carried a black banner on the end of a long spear; he was ahead of and leading Hawazin who were behind him. When he overtook someone, he would thrust his spear at him; if he missed the man, then he would raise his spear as a sign to those behind him and they would follow the man. While he was engaged in this, 'Ali b. Abii Talib fell upon him, accompanied by an ansari. 'Ali approached him from behind and struck the hocks of the camel and it fell on its rump. The ansari then leapt on the man and gave him a blow that severed his foot, along with half his leg, so that he fell from his saddle.
lessons: Coordinated effort between companions, each contributing their unique strengths, can overcome even the most formidable threat.
Targeting the source of an enemy's momentum — their symbol, their leader, their rallying point — can shift the tide of a conflict.
Courage in the face of a devastating aggressor requires both decisiveness and strategic thinking, not just brute force.
practical_advice: When facing a seemingly overwhelming problem, try to identify its root cause or driving force rather than only reacting to its symptoms.
Collaborate with others whose strengths complement yours — teamwork that divides the task appropriately achieves what individuals cannot do alone.
Do not allow the actions of one destructive person or force to paralyse you into inaction; measured, strategic responses are often more effective than panic.

title: Abu Sufyan ibn Harb's Bitter Reaction to the Defeat of Hawazin
summary: After the defeat of Hawazin at Hunayn, Abu Sufyan ibn Harb — whose Islam was still fresh — revealed lingering ill-will, while Safwan ibn Umayya, still a polytheist, paradoxically defended the Prophet's leadership over his tribal rivals.
story: When the enemy had been defeated, some of the Arab toughs began to reveal their ill-will. Abu Sufyan Sakhr b. Harb (whose acceptance of Islam was still fresh and who was still that day carrying divining arrows) said, 'Their defeat won't end before the sea!' Kalada (or Jahala) b. al-Hanbal, who was there with his brother — on his mother's side — Safwan b. Umayya (he still being a polytheist, during the respite granted him by the Messenger of God (SAAS)) cried out, 'Magic is definitely useless now!' Safwan responded, 'Be silent, may God close your mouth! I swear, I'd sooner have a man of Quraysh be my leader than any of Hawazin!'
lessons: Outward acceptance of truth does not always immediately transform deeply held prejudices and tribal loyalties — spiritual growth is often a gradual, uneven process.
Sometimes those who have not yet formally accepted a faith can demonstrate more fairness and honesty than those who have nominally joined it — sincerity matters more than outward affiliation.
Crisis moments reveal what lies beneath the surface of people's stated beliefs and commitments, which is why ongoing self-examination is essential for genuine spiritual development.
practical_advice: Be patient with yourself and others as you undergo internal change — deep-seated biases and habits do not disappear overnight simply because you have committed to a new path.
When you notice that your reactions in a difficult moment are driven by old resentments or tribal thinking rather than principle, take it as a sign that more inner work is needed.
Regularly examine whether your loyalty is to truth and justice or to group identity and personal advantage, and be honest about the difference.

title: Umm Sulaym and Her Dagger at the Battle of Hunayn
summary: At the Battle of Hunayn, Umm Sulaym carried a dagger and told her husband Abu Talha she was prepared to use it against any polytheist who came near, prompting the Prophet (SAAS) to laugh at her determined resolve.
story: Abu Talha met Umm Sulaym who had a dagger with her. Abu Talha asked, 'What is that?' She replied, 'If one of the polytheists comes near me, I'll stick him in the stomach!' Abu Talha commented, 'Did you hear what Umm Sulaym said?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) laughed and said, 'God has already done well and enough, Umm Sulaym.'
lessons: Courage in defence of one's faith and family is honourable regardless of one's station, and the Prophet's laughter showed respect and warmth toward sincere, spirited devotion.
Proactive preparation for difficulty, rather than passive resignation, reflects a healthy and empowered response to threat.
Even in the gravest situations, moments of lightness and humanity — like the Prophet's laughter — remind us that hope and warmth coexist with hardship.
practical_advice: When facing stressful or threatening circumstances, channel your energy into preparation and practical action rather than paralysis or excessive worry.
Do not underestimate the courage and contribution of those who may seem unlikely warriors — every person has something to offer in a time of need.
Allow yourself to find moments of genuine warmth and even humour within difficulty; this is not a denial of hardship but a sign of resilience.

title: Abu Talha Kills Twenty Men and the Dispute Over Booty
summary: During Hunayn, Abu Talha killed twenty men and took their booty, while Abu Qatada's rightful booty was taken by another man, leading Umar to defend the Companion's right and the Prophet (SAAS) to affirm him.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated that day, 'Whoever kills an unbeliever shall own the booty from him.' Abu Talha killed that day 20 men and took booty from them. Abu Qatada stated, 'O Messenger of God, I struck a man on his shoulder vein; he was wearing armour, but I was forced away from him. Would you see who took it?' At that a man arose and said, 'I'm the one who took it. You should compensate him for it and award it to me.' Whenever a request was made to the Messenger of God (SAAS) he either agreed to it or remained silent. This time he remained silent and Umar spoke up, 'I swear, God would not compensate one of God's lions and give it to you!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Umar has spoken the truth.'
lessons: Speaking up for someone who has been wronged or whose legitimate rights are being usurped is a noble act, and silence in the face of injustice can itself be a failure.
The Prophet's method of deliberate silence before affirming a correct statement models careful, considered judgment rather than reactive decision-making.
Recognising and honouring people's contributions and sacrifices — rather than allowing opportunists to claim what is not theirs — builds trust and justice within a community.
practical_advice: When you witness someone being denied what they legitimately earned or deserve, find the courage to speak up clearly and constructively on their behalf.
If you are in a position of authority and face a difficult judgment, take the time to reflect before responding — not every question requires an immediate answer.
Ensure that in your family, workplace, or community, credit and reward go to those who genuinely did the work, rather than those who are simply most vocal or opportunistic.

title: Umm Sulaym's Request to Execute the Tulaqa and the Prophet's Response
summary: After the battle, Umm Sulaym urged the Prophet (SAAS) to execute the Tulaqa — the recently converted Meccans who had deserted — but he declined, saying God had already done enough.
story: Umm Sulaym asked, 'Messenger of God, execute those Tulaqa', Meccan converts, at our rear who deserted you!' He replied, 'God has already done well and enough, Umm Sulaym.'
lessons: Anger following betrayal and hardship is a natural human response, but wisdom lies in knowing when justice has already been served without requiring further punishment.
The Prophet's restraint in the face of legitimate grievance teaches that mercy and sufficiency can be more powerful responses to betrayal than retribution.
Trust in divine justice — the belief that God's reckoning is sufficient — is a profound source of inner peace that frees us from the burden of seeking revenge.
practical_advice: When you feel a strong urge to punish or retaliate against someone who has let you down, pause and ask whether justice has already been served in some form, and whether further action is truly necessary.
Practice releasing the need for personal retribution by reminding yourself that not every wrong demands your direct response — some things can be left to God or to natural consequences.
Channel righteous anger into constructive action rather than destruction; your energy is better spent rebuilding than tearing down.

title: Anas ibn Malik Describes the Prophet's Age and Character
summary: Anas ibn Malik recalled that the Prophet (SAAS) received his mission at age 40, spent ten years each in Mecca and Medina, and was the most youthful, handsome, and well-built of men at the time of his passing.
story: Anas b. Malik was asked, by al-'Ala b. Ziyad al-'Adawi, 'Abu Hamza, what age was the Messenger of God (SAAS) when he received his mission?' He replied that he was 40 years of age. 'And what happened then?' he was asked. He replied, 'Thereafter he was 10 years in Mecca and 10 in Medina. When he had completed 60 years of age, God took him to Himself.' He was asked, 'At that time, what was he like, relative to the age of others?' 'He was the most youthful of men, handsome and well-built,' he replied.
lessons: The Prophet's blessed appearance, even at the end of his life, was a sign of divine grace and a reminder that true beauty is nurtured by virtue and closeness to God.
A life of purpose and mission — begun at 40 and completed with profound impact — shows that it is never too late to answer one's calling and that quality of life surpasses its length.
Preserving the memories and descriptions of those we love and admire is itself an act of devotion and a gift to future generations.
practical_advice: Reflect on the idea that meaningful purpose can be taken up at any stage of life — do not allow your age to discourage you from pursuing what matters most.
Seek to cultivate inner virtues such as sincerity, patience, and compassion, knowing that these qualities leave a lasting impression on those around you far beyond physical appearance.
Take time to record and share your memories of people who have inspired you, so that their example continues to benefit others after they are gone.

title: The Prophet's Prayer at Hunayn: 'O God, If You Wish It, You Will Not Be Worshipped on Earth After Today'
summary: At a critical moment during the Battle of Hunayn, the Prophet (SAAS) prayed with profound urgency, asking God that if He willed it, He would not be worshipped on earth after that day — expressing the existential stakes of the battle.
story: One of the prayers spoken by the Prophet (SAAS) at the battle of Hunayn was, 'O God, if You wish it, You will not be worshipped on earth after today!'
lessons: The Prophet's prayer reveals that even the greatest of believers can reach moments of such intense vulnerability that they lay everything before God with complete rawness and honesty.
Turning to God in moments of existential fear — rather than pretending to have everything under control — is a sign of profound trust and spiritual maturity, not weakness.
This prayer reflects a heart that cares not for personal survival but for the continuation of God's worship on earth — a reminder to examine what we truly value at the deepest level.
practical_advice: In your most overwhelming moments, allow yourself to pray or speak to God with complete honesty about your fears, even if they feel too dark or desperate to express — God already knows what is in your heart.
Reflect on what truly matters to you at the deepest level, and let that clarity guide your priorities, decisions, and responses when life becomes most difficult.

When facing a situation that feels potentially catastrophic, ground yourself in the understanding that ultimate outcomes rest with God, and that your role is to do your best while trusting in His wisdom.

title: The Prophet's Stand at Hunayn – Dismounting and Calling for Help
summary: During the Battle of Hunayn, the Prophet dismounted his mule, called out his lineage, and prayed to God for help as the battle reached its most intense point. Al-Bara' noted that the bravest would seek protection with the Prophet.
story: Zakariyya' b. Aba Za'ida, from Abii Ishnq, from d-Bar%", who said that he then dismounted, calling for help and saying, "I am the Prophet, undoubtedly; I am the son of 'Abd d-MutpJib. 0 God, send down Your help!" AI-Bar9 went on, "When the fighting became most intense, we would seek protection with the Messenger of God (SAAS); those who were brave would imitate him." Al-Bayhaqi related from various lines that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said that day, "I am al-'Aw~tik's'" son." Al-Tabrw stated that it was related to him by 'Abba b. al-Fad1 al-Asfqi, quoting 'Amr h. 'Awf d-Wasiti, quoting Hushaym, quoting Y*y% b. Sacid, from 'Amr b. Sacid h. al-'As, from Shabhaba, from Ibn 'Avim d-Sulami (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said at the battle of al-Hunayn, "I am al-'Awatik's son!"
lessons: In moments of extreme danger, turning to God with complete reliance is the mark of true faith.
Leadership means standing firm when others flee; the Prophet's courage was itself a source of strength for his companions.
Acknowledging one's lineage and identity in hardship is not pride but a reminder of the trust God has placed in a person.
practical_advice: When you face an overwhelming crisis, pause and call upon God sincerely before taking your next step.
In moments when anxiety peaks, ground yourself by remembering who you are and the values you stand for.
Look to the example of those who remain steadfast under pressure and consciously choose to emulate their composure.

title: Abu Qatada's Valor and the Dispute Over War Spoils at Hunayn
summary: Abu Qatada fought bravely at Hunayn, saving a Muslim's life and killing a polytheist, but struggled to find a witness to claim the slain man's possessions. Abu Bakr defended his right passionately, and the Prophet upheld justice by awarding Abu Qatada his rightful spoils.
story: Al-Bukhai stated that 'Abd All& b. Ynsuf related to him, quoting Malik, from Y*ya b. Sacid, from 'Amr h. Kathir h. An*, from Abii Muhammad, freed-man of Abii Qatxda, from Aba @tada, who said, "We accompanied the Messenger of God (SAAS) into battle at Hunayn. When we clashed, the Muslims retreated. I saw one of the polytheists overpowering one of the Muslims, so I strnck him with my sword from behind on his shoulder vein, cutting through his armour. But he charged at me and pinned me down so hard I felt near death. However, he died and so released me. 1 caught up with Wmar and asked, 'What's wrong with everyone?' 'It is God's decree,' he replied. "Later they (the Muslims) returned (to the battle) and the Messenger of God (SAAS) sat and stated, 'Whoever killed one of the enemy and bas a wimess to it can claim the dead man's possessions.' So I arose and asked, 'Who will he my witness?' I then sat down. The Messenger of God (SAAS) repeated what he had said, and again I arose, asking for someone to be my witness; and then I sat down. The Messenger of God (SAAS) repeated his statement, and again I arose. He now asked, 'What is wrong, Aha Qat~da?' I related what I had done and a man then got up and said, 'He speaks the truth. I have the dead man's possessions, so compensate Aba Qatada for me.' Aba Bakr then said, 'Absolutely not, by God! You cannot expect a lion of God to fight for God and His messenger, then for him to give you the booty he won!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'He (Aha Bakr) spoke the truth; give it back to him!' The man did so and I used it to purchase a piece of land from Bana Salama; it was the &st money I earned after accepting Islam." The rest of the scholarly community, except al-Nasz'i, related this from a hadith of Yahya h. Sa'id. Al-Bukhai also stated that al-Layth b. Sa'd related, quoting Yabya h. Sa'id, from 'Amr h. Kathir h. Aflah, from Aba Muhammad, the freed-man of Aha Qaada, who quoted Aba Qatada as saying, "At the battle of Hunayn, I noticed one of the Muslims fighting a polytheist, while another of the polytheists was sneaking up behind the Muslims to kill him. So I charged at the second polytheist, who raised his hand to hit me, but I shuck it and cut it off. However, he seized me and pulled me to himself so hard I feared (I would die). Then he went down, loosening his grip, and I pushed him away and killed him. After that the Muslims retreated, as did I myself. Then I saw Wmar h. al-Kha~ah amid the army and asked him, 'What's wrong with everyone?' He replied, 'It is God's decree.' "After that the men returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS) (after defeating the enemy) and he announced, 'Whoever can provide proof of hi having killed an enemy shall receive his (the dead man's) possessions.' So I stood up to look for some proof of my having killed an enemy, hut since I could not see anyone who could testify for me, I Sat down again. Then it seemed to me I should tell to the Messenger of God (SAAS) what had happened (and did so). One of the men sitting there said, 'I have the weapons belonging to the man slain to whom he is referring: give him compensation for me.' Abii Bakr said, 'Indeed not; he will not give anything to some little hyena of G r a y s h what belongs to one of God's lions who fought for God and His messenger.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then arose and awarded it to me and I used it to buy a piece of land. It was the first property I earned."
lessons: Persisting in seeking your rightful due — even when initially unrecognized — is an act of dignity, not stubbornness.
True leadership ensures that those who sacrifice are honoured and not exploited by those who did not.
Justice is not always immediate, but it eventually prevails when leaders are committed to fairness.
practical_advice: If you feel your contribution has gone unrecognized, document what you did and advocate for yourself calmly and persistently.
When someone around you is being treated unjustly, speak up on their behalf as Abu Bakr did, without hesitation.
After a hard-won achievement, invest what you earn wisely as a foundation for your future — as Abu Qatada did with his first property.

title: Abbas Calls the Companions Back at Hunayn
summary: As the Muslim forces scattered at Hunayn, the Prophet instructed his uncle Abbas to call out to the Companions — particularly the Ansar and people of the Hudaybiyya truce — and they responded immediately, rallying around the Prophet and turning the tide of battle.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated that al-H&im informed him, quoting al-Asamm, quoting Ahmad b. 'Ahd al-Jahhar, from Yanus b. Bukayr, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, who said that it was related to him by 'A~imb. Wmar, from 'Ahd al-R&mm b. Jabii, from his fatherJahir b. 'Abd All&, who quoted the Messenger of God (SAAS), when he saw how people were behaving at the battle of Hunayn, as saying, "'Abbas! Call out: '0 aqzr! 0 people of the tree!'" (When he did so) they responded, "Labbayka! Labbayka! At Your service! At Your service!" The men then tried to turn their mounts, and when they failed to do so, they threw off their armour from around their necks, took their swords and shields and headed in the direction of his voice. Eventually some 100 of them were gathered about h i and they made a stand and fought on. The first call he made was to the an@?, then another was made to (Bana) Khazraj. They were steadfast in the battle. The Messenger of God (SAAS) looked out at his mounted men and the fierce battle waging and commented, "The fires of war are really hot now!" Al-Bayhaqi went on, "By the time all the force had returned around him, the prisoners were already bound, and there with them the Messenger of God (SAAS). God destroyed many of them and caused many to flee. He awarded to His messenger their possessions and children."
lessons: A single voice of rallying at the right moment can reverse a collective loss of will.
Community bonds forged in prior sacrifice — like the Hudaybiyya truce — become a reservoir of loyalty in times of crisis.
Even in near-defeat, the faithful who remain steadfast become the turning point.
practical_advice: When your community or team is in disarray, identify the strongest bonds of shared experience and call upon them to restore unity.
In your darkest moment, respond to the call of those who need you — sometimes showing up is all that is required.
Build deep relationships during times of peace so that when crisis comes, the bonds hold firm.

title: The March to Hunayn – Women of Mecca Accompany the Army
summary: When the Prophet marched from Mecca to face the Hawazin tribe at Hunayn, many people of Mecca — including women — joined, some motivated not by faith but by curiosity and hope for booty. Even Abu Sufyan and Safwan ibn Umayya accompanied the army despite not yet being sincere Muslims.
story: Ibn Lahi'a stated, from Aha al-Aswad, from Wrwa, and Masa b. Wqbanarrated in his military chronicle, from al-Zuhri, that after God had conquered Mecca for the Messenger of God (SAAS) and he had rested, he went out against Hawszin, accompanied by the people of Mecca, some mounted, others on foot. Even the women left too, walking; they did so not for religious reasons but to watch and to bring back the booty. And they would not have minded if the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his men had lost. They also stated that he was accompanied by Abn Snfym b. Harb and Safw2n b. Umayya. The wife of the latter was a Muslim, while he was still a polytheist. But they (the couple) had not been separated.
lessons: Not everyone who accompanies a righteous cause is sincerely committed to it — discernment is a necessary quality in leadership.
Faith and unbelief can coexist in complex social realities, as shown by Safwan and his Muslim wife remaining together.
True intention is what distinguishes sincere participation from opportunistic presence.
practical_advice: When building a team or community effort, seek to understand the motivations of those who join, and cultivate sincerity over mere numbers.
Do not be discouraged if not everyone around you shares your values — focus on strengthening your own sincerity.
Reflect regularly on your own intentions: are you participating in good works for God's sake, or for personal gain or spectacle?

title: Malik ibn Awf's Strategy and the Muslim Retreat at Hunayn
summary: The Hawazin leader Malik ibn Awf devised a coordinated dawn assault at Hunayn, positioning livestock and families behind the fighters. The polytheists attacked as one force and the Muslims retreated — with only around 100 men remaining near the Prophet.
story: Wrwa stated, "The leader of the polytheists that day was Mslik b. 'Awf al-Na~ri. With him was Durayd h. al-Simma, much suffering from his advanced age. Also accompanying them were their womenfolk, children and livestock. The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent out ahead 'Abd All& b. Aha Hadrad as a scout. He spent the night with the enemy force and heard Mdik b. 'Awf say to his companions, 'When morning comes, attack them as one man. Break the scabbards of your swords and position your livestock and women in separate lines.' "Next morning Abn Sufym, Safwsn and Hakim h. H i z a positioned themselves apart and to the rear to observe which side would prevail. The Muslim forces were ranged into lines and the Messenger of God (SAAS) mounted his grey mule, went out to meet each l i e and urged them to fight hard, encouraging them that they would be victorious if they remained steadfast. "While they were so engaged, the polytheists attacked the Muslims as one man. The Muslims engaged in one round of fighting, then turned around and retreated. Haitha b. al-Nn'mm commented, 'I estimated the number remaining with the Messenger of God (SAAS) after the retreat to be 100 men.'"
lessons: Prior intelligence and reconnaissance are essential to preparedness — the Prophet sent a scout ahead to understand the enemy's plan.
Numbers and initial momentum do not guarantee victory; resolve and leadership matter far more.
Setbacks are a part of every great struggle; what defines the outcome is how quickly one regroups.
practical_advice: Before entering any significant challenge, gather information carefully so you are not caught off guard.
When things go wrong quickly, resist the urge to panic — pause, assess who is still with you, and begin again from there.
Surround yourself with people who will stay with you in the most difficult moments, not only in easy ones.

title: Safwan ibn Umayya's Loyalty to Quraysh Over the Bedouin
summary: As the Muslims initially retreated at Hunayn, a Qurayshi man told Safwan that Muhammad had been defeated. Safwan responded that he would rather be ruled by a Qurayshi than a bedouin — revealing his tribal loyalties — and then sent a slave to listen to the war cries, which confirmed the Prophet's victory.
story: "People say that one of the Quraysh men passed by Safwan b. Umayya and said, 'I bring good news of the defeat of Muhammad and his men. I swear, they will never recover.' Safwzn asked, 'Are yon telling me it's good news for the bedouin to win? I swear, I much prefer a man of Quraysh than a bedouin as my master.' Safwm was angry for this reason." Wrwa went on, "Safwan sent a slave of his ahead, telling him, 'Listen for who is making the war cry.' When he came back, he said, 'I heard them saying, "0 Banii 'Abd al-Rahmzn! 0 Bana 'Abd Allzh! 0 Bano Wbayd All&!"' He (Safwzn) said, 'Muhammad has won! Those were their war cries.'"
lessons: Tribal and personal loyalties can linger even in those on the threshold of faith — change of heart is often gradual.
Even those who opposed the Prophet ultimately had to acknowledge his victory when the evidence became undeniable.
Recognizing truth, even reluctantly, is a step toward eventual acceptance.
practical_advice: Examine the hidden loyalties and biases that might be influencing your judgments without your awareness.
Be willing to acknowledge when the truth contradicts your hopes or expectations — intellectual honesty is a form of courage.
Do not assume that those who seem hostile to good are entirely beyond reaching; circumstances and evidence can soften hearts over time.

title: The Prophet Prays and Throws Pebbles at the Enemy, Turning the Battle
summary: At the height of the Battle of Hunayn, the Prophet stood in his stirrups, raised his hands in prayer to God, then cast a handful of pebbles at the enemy, declaring God's promise would be fulfilled. The tide of battle turned and the Muslims pursued the fleeing polytheists to victory.
story: The sources stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was in the thick of the battle, he stood up in his stirrups while he rode the mnle and raised his hands praying to God, saying, '0 God, I appeal to Yon to fulfd Your promise to me. 0 God, it must not be that they defeat us!' He called for his men and urged them on, '0 men who were at the Hudaybiyya truce! God! God! Things are turning against Your prophet.' It is said that he incited them by saying, '0 ansiiy of God and of His messenger! 0 Banii al-Khazraj! 0 people of swat al-Baqara!' And he assigned some of his Companions to call this out." They went on, "He also picked up a handful of pebbles and cast them into the faces and on to the heads of the polytheists, saying, 'May their faces be deformed!' "His men then hurriedly gathered around him. They claim that the Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Now the f ~ e s of war are heating up!' God then defeated His enemies wherever He had cast His pebbles at them. The Muslims pursued them, k i i n g them. And God gave them their women and offspring as booty. Mzlik b. 'Awf fled and entered the fort of al-Tz'if, along with some of his people's leaders. "Many of the people of Mecca accepted Islam when they saw God's victory for His messenger (SAAS) and the way He had honoured His religion."
lessons: Combining sincere prayer with decisive action is the complete model of trusting in God while doing one's part.
Calling people back to a shared identity and covenant reinvigorates commitment when morale has collapsed.
Divine support often comes at the moment when human effort alone seems insufficient.
practical_advice: In your most overwhelming moments, stop and pray before taking your next action — let supplication precede strategy.
When a group loses its direction, remind them of the values and commitments that originally united them.
Trust that when you have done everything in your power and placed your reliance on God, the outcome is in capable hands.

title: Abbas and Abu Sufyan Hold the Prophet's Mule as He Charges the Enemy
summary: As Muslims fled at Hunayn, the Prophet urged his mule forward toward the enemy lines. Abbas held the bridle to slow it while Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith gripped the saddle, and the Prophet called on Abbas to rally the Companions with a specific call.
story: Ibn Wahb stated that it was told to him by Ynnus, from al-Zuhri, who said that Kathir h. alLCAbbash. 'Abd al-Muccalib quoted al-'Abbns as stating, "I was present with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the battle of Hnnayn; I and Abii Snfyn h. al-Harith kept close to him throughout. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was riding a white mule presented to him by Farwa b. Nnfztha al-Jadh~mi. When the forces met, the Muslims turned back in flight. The Messenger of God (SAAS) suddenly urged his mnle on towards the polytheists. I held tight to its bridle, trying to slow it down, while Aba Sufym gripped the saddle of the Messenger of God (SAAS). "The Messenger of God (SAAS) cried out, 'Hey, 'Abbs, call for the men of the acacia tree!'"
lessons: Those closest to the Prophet instinctively moved to protect him — proximity to goodness engenders devotion.
True courage is not recklessness; those who loved the Prophet tried to protect him even as he pressed forward.
Leadership at its highest means moving toward the danger, not away from it, which inspires others to follow.
practical_advice: When someone you respect and care for is in danger, act to protect them — loyalty is shown in action, not only words.
Do not confuse boldness with foolishness; the goal is to face the challenge, not to court unnecessary harm.
Stay close to those whose character you admire; their example will shape your own conduct when it matters most.

title: The Battle of Hunayn: The Muslims' Initial Retreat and the Prophet's Steadfastness
summary: At the Battle of Hunayn, the Muslim forces initially fled in panic when confronted by the enemy, but the Prophet remained steadfast on his mule, urging it forward while Abbas called the fighters back.
story: of God (SAAS) at the battle of Hnnayn; I and Abii Snfym h. al-Harith kept close to him throughout. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was riding a white mule presented to him by Farwa b. Nnfztha al-Jadh~mi. When the forces met, the Muslims turned back in flight. The Messenger of God (SAAS) suddenly urged his mnle on towards the polytheists. I held tight to its bridle, trying to slow it down, while Aba Sufym gripped the saddle of the Messenger of God (SAAS). "The Messenger of God (SAAS) cried out, 'Hey, 'Abbs, call for the men of the acacia tree!' I swear, it was as though, on hearing my voice, I had gained their attention as does a cow her young! They responded, ' Yii labbaykiih! Yic labbayk~h! At Your service! At Your service!' "They and the polytheists then engaged in battle, while the awiir criers shouted, '0 aqdr!' Then the cry was repeated directly to Bana al-H&th b. al-Khazraj. "The Messenger of God (SAAS), on his mule, stared over at them, as though stretching forward to battle them himself, and said, 'This is when the fires of war will heat up!' Then he picked up gravel which he threw into the faces of the unbelievers and said, 'May you be defeated, by Muhammad's Lord!' I had been watching, and as far as I could see the fighting was progressing as before. But no sooner had he thrown the gravel than the enemy's enthusiasm dulled and they retreated."
lessons: True leadership means standing firm when others flee — the Prophet advanced toward danger while his army retreated, demonstrating that courage is not the absence of fear but action in spite of it.
A single rallying voice at the right moment can reverse the course of an entire battle; never underestimate the power of calling people back to their convictions.
Divine assistance often comes through seemingly small, ordinary means — a handful of gravel — reminding us that God's help does not always appear the way we expect.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed and want to retreat from a difficult situation, pause before fleeing and ask yourself what a composed, principled response would look like instead.
In moments of group panic or chaos, be the steady voice that calls others back to purpose — your calm can be contagious just as fear can be.

When facing a seemingly insurmountable challenge, take the smallest possible action you can toward it rather than away from it; momentum toward a problem often dissolves it faster than avoidance does.

title: Salama ibn al-Akwa's Account: The Prophet Throws Dust and Defeats the Enemy
summary: Salama ibn al-Akwa describes how at Hunayn, when the enemy overwhelmed the Prophet, he dismounted, threw a handful of dirt into their faces, and God filled every enemy's eyes with it, causing them to flee.
story: Muslim also related, from a hadith of 'Ikrima b. 'Ammar, from I y y s b. Salama b. al-Akwa: from his father, who said, "We fought the battle of Hnnayn with the Messenger of God (SAAS). When we confronted the enemy, I advanced up a mountain path where I found a polytheist, at whom I shot an arrow. He hid from me and I did not know what became of him. I then saw the enemy coming up another mountain path. They clashed with the men of the Messenger of God (SAAS), who turned hack. I retreated too. I had two striped garments, in one of which I was dressed, the other draped over me. I untied my waist-wrapper and joined both striped garments together. Then I retreated, passing by the Prophet (SAAS), who was mounted on his grey mule. He commented, 'Ihn d-Akwac has seen something frightening.' "As the enemy was overwhelming the Messenger of God (SAAS), he dismounted from his mule, picked up a handful of din from the ground and threw it into their faces, saying, 'May their faces he deformed!' And every single one of them had his eyes fill with that din from that handful and they turned and fled. God defeated them and thereafter the Messenger of God (SAAS) divided up their possessions among the Muslims."
