lessons: God's power can work through the humblest of materials — dirt from the ground — teaching us that divine help is not bound by what seems powerful or impressive in human eyes.

Even when an experienced warrior like Salama retreated in fear, the Prophet's composure and trust in God turned defeat into victory, showing that one person's unwavering faith can change collective outcomes.
The spoils being distributed after victory signals justice and community care — those who endure hardship together share in the fruits of perseverance together.
practical_advice: When you have done everything you can and still feel overwhelmed, recognize that the outcome is ultimately not yours alone to control — release the need to force results and trust that your sincere effort is seen.
Notice how the Prophet read Salama's fear without judgment, simply naming it — practice acknowledging others' fear with empathy rather than criticism when they struggle under pressure.
After a difficult trial ends, make it a habit to share your relief or success with those around you rather than hoarding it; generosity after hardship builds lasting community bonds.

title: Abu Abd al-Rahman al-Fihri's Account: Bilal Saddles Up and the Prophet Calls Out to God's Servants
summary: Abu Abd al-Rahman al-Fihri recounts riding out with the Prophet at Hunayn, the Muslim retreat, and the Prophet leaping from his horse to throw dirt at the enemy while declaring himself God's servant and messenger.
story: Aba Ds'ad d-Tayalisi stated in his Musnad hadirh collection that it was related to him by Hammad h. Salama, from Ya'la b. 'Ato', from 'Abd Allah b. Yasar, from Abn 'Abd a l - R a h a d-Fihri, who said, "We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Hnnayn. We set out on an extremely hot day. We halted in the shade of some acacia trees, and when the sun had set, I put on my cuirass and mounted my horse. I then went over to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was in his tent. I addressed him, 'Peace be upon you, Messenger of God and may His mercy and blessings he yours. Is it time to go yet, Messenger of God?' 'Yes, it is,' he replied. He then called out the name of Bild, who emerged quickly from beneath an acacia, his shadow like that of a bird. He said, 'At your service and pleasure, may I he ransom for you!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'Saddle up my horse.' He was brought two palm fronds that were without holes or indentations. "He mounted his horse and we spent the day riding. The two cavalries came face to face and we did battle with them. Then the Muslims turned and retreated, as God Almighty stated. The Messenger of God (SAAS) began saying, '0 worshippers of God, I am the servant and Messenger of God!' He then jumped from his horse and someone close to him later told me that he took a handful of dirt and tossed it into the faces of the enemy, saying, 'May their faces be deformed!'" Ya'lx b. 'A@ stated, "Their sons quoted their fathers as saying, 'All of them, without exception, had their eyes and mouths filled with din. And we heard a clanging sound from the sky like iron striking an iron pot and God, Almighty and Glorious is He, defeated them.'"
lessons: The Prophet's declaration 'I am the servant and Messenger of God' in the midst of battle chaos was itself an act of grounding — in crisis, returning to your core identity and purpose is a stabilizing force.
Bilal's instant, eager readiness — emerging swiftly like a bird's shadow — illustrates how a life of devoted service cultivates a kind of joyful alertness that cannot be manufactured on the spot.
The supernatural clanging sound described by witnesses reminds us that there are dimensions of reality beyond our physical senses involved in the outcomes of our struggles.
practical_advice: When anxiety spirals during a crisis, ground yourself by naming who you are and what you stand for — identity is an anchor when circumstances are chaotic.
Prepare for difficulty during calm times: the army rested in the shade, ate, and armed themselves before the battle. Use periods of ease to build the capacity you will need in hard times.
Cultivate a spirit of readiness and willingness in your daily duties so that when something urgent demands you, you can respond with enthusiasm rather than reluctance or resentment.

title: Ibn Mas'ud's Account: Eighty Companions Hold Firm and the Prophet Is Lifted Up
summary: Ibn Mas'ud recounts that only eighty companions remained with the Prophet at Hunayn, that the Prophet slipped from his mule and called for dirt to throw at the enemy, and that the remaining believers rallied with swords shining like stars.
story: Imam A h a d stated that it was related to him by <Affan, quoting 'Ahd al-Wabd b. Ziyad, quoting al-Harith b. Husayn, quoting al-Q%im b. 'Abd d-Rabnzn h. 'Abd All* b. Mascud, from his father, who quoted 'Abd Allah b. Mas'i~das stating, "I was present with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the battle of Hunayn. The men retreated from him, and 80 of the an@r and the muhiijirin remained there with him. We drew back some 80 feet, but did not turn our backs to the enemy. These men were the ones upon whom God sent down al-~akina. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was meanwhile still going forward on his mule, which veered to one side so that he slipped off his saddle. I called out to hi,'Up you get! May God raise you up!' He replied, 'Get me a handful of dirt!' With this he struck them in their faces and filled their mouths. Then he asked, 'Where are the muhiijirin and the anjar?' 'They are close by,' I replied. 'Call for them,' he asked. I did this and they came, their swords in their right hands shining l i e stars, and the polytheists then turned their backs in retreat."
lessons: God's tranquility — al-sakina — descends upon those who do not break and run; holding your ground even partially, even imperfectly, is enough to receive divine steadiness.

Ibn Mas'ud's instinct to encourage the Prophet the moment he slipped — 'May God raise you up!' — shows us that even the greatest leaders need companions who speak encouragement in their lowest moments.

The image of companions arriving with swords shining like stars conveys that when people are called back to a righteous cause after a moment of fear, they can return more luminous and committed than before.
practical_advice: When everyone around you seems to be giving up on a worthy cause, ask yourself honestly whether you are among the eighty who stayed — and if not, what it would take to step back into that circle.
Practice speaking words of encouragement to leaders and people you respect when you witness them struggle; your voice in their difficult moment may matter more than you realize.
After a failure or stumble — even a literal one — do not dwell; ask for what you need immediately and keep moving. The Prophet asked for dirt and moved on. Practical resilience is often that simple.

title: The Fleeing Polytheist's Testimony: Imagining Every Rock as a Pursuing Knight
summary: Two polytheist witnesses describe how after the Prophet threw pebbles at them at Hunayn, a divine terror caused them to flee and imagine every rock and tree as an enemy knight in pursuit.
story: Yacqiib b. Sufyzn stated that it was related to him by Abii Sufym, quoting Sacid CAbd a l - R h n n b. Ibrahim, quoting al-Walid b. Muslim, quoting Muhammad b. CAbd All" d-Sha'bi, from d-Hnrith b. Bad1 al-Nasri, from a man of his tribe who had been present on that day at the battle of Hunayn, along with 'Amr b. Sufyzn al-Thaqafi, both of whom said, "When the Muslims were being defeated at Hunayn, there only remained with the Messenger of God (SAAS) CAbbas and Aba Sufyan b. d-Hzrith. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then picked up a handful of pebbles and threw it into their faces. We fled, imagining every rock or tree to be some knight chasing us." Al-Thaqafi added, "I raced away on my horse until I entered d-Ta'if."
lessons: Fear uncoupled from truth transforms the entire world into a threat — every rock becomes a knight. This is a profound portrait of how anxiety distorts perception.
The testimony of opponents can carry a unique kind of credibility; sometimes the most powerful witness to righteous strength is someone who stood against it.
Divine intervention does not always look miraculous from the outside — to the enemy it felt like terror and confusion, not an angel descending with a sword.
practical_advice: When you notice that your fear is making everything around you seem threatening — people's neutral faces seem hostile, ordinary events seem ominous — recognize this as a signal that your nervous system needs grounding, not that the world is actually closing in.
Seek out honest accounts even from those who opposed what you believe in; their perspective can reveal dimensions of truth that admiring accounts miss.
If you find yourself fleeing from a situation, pause to ask whether you are running from actual danger or from a fear-distorted perception of it — the distinction can save you from unnecessary loss.

title: Shayba's Conversion: The Prophet's Touch and the Birth of Love
summary: Shayba, who joined the battle not for Islam but out of tribal pride, experienced the Prophet striking his chest three times and praying for his guidance, after which he loved the Prophet more than anyone else in creation.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated that he was informed by Abii 'Abd Allah al-H&, quoting d b.Tamim alQw@ri, quoting Abn W b a , Aba d-Husayn Muhammad h. b quoting Abn ' A s i , quoting 'Abd All* b. 'Abd d-R&man al-Tx>ifi, quoting 'Abd A E h b. 'Iyyad b. al-Haith al-An~xri,from his father (who said) that the Messenger of God (SAAS) attacked Bana Hawain with a force of 12,000 men and that at Hunayn a like number of the people of al-TPif were killed as those who died at Badr. From Musabb b. Shayba, from his father, who said, 'I went forth with the Messenger of God (SAAS) to the battle of Hunayn, though it was not Islam nor any knowledge of it that made me do so; I just refused to have Hawiizin defeat Quraysh! As I stood with him, I said, "Messenger of God, I can see piebald horses!" He replied, "No one but a polytheist would see them!" Then he struck me in the chest and said, "0 God, guide Shayba aright!" He then hit it again and said, "0 God, guide Shayba aright!" and then he did and said the same a third time. And I swear by God, no sooner had he raised his hand from me the third time than there was none of God's creation whom I loved more than I did him!' He went on to relate the encounter between the armies, how the Muslims had been retreating, how d-'AbbZs had called out and the Messenger of God (SAAS) had asked God for His help, and so on until God had defeated the polytheists.
lessons: Hearts can be transformed not through argument but through sincere prayer and direct, personal attention — the Prophet did not lecture Shayba, he touched him and prayed for him three times.

Shayba's honesty about his mixed motives for joining the battle — tribal pride rather than faith — is itself a form of integrity; acknowledging the true state of your heart is the beginning of its purification.

Love for righteousness often arrives not gradually but in a single moment when something genuine breaks through — Shayba's transformation was instant, complete, and total upon the third raising of the Prophet's hand.
practical_advice: When trying to help someone whose values or motives you question, resist the urge to correct them with words alone; sincere, repeated prayer for their wellbeing may reach them in ways argument never could.
Be honest with yourself about why you are really doing something — whether it is for God, for pride, for fear, or for love. That honesty, however uncomfortable, is where genuine growth begins.
If you feel a sudden, unexpected opening of your heart toward goodness — a moment where love or faith floods in unexpectedly — do not dismiss it as emotion. Receive it, and let it reorient you.

title: Shayba ibn Uthman's Attempt to Kill the Prophet at Hunayn
summary: Shayba ibn Uthman, seeking revenge for his father killed at Uhud, attempted to assassinate the Prophet during the Battle of Hunayn but was stopped by a divine flame, and his heart was transformed with love for the Prophet.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated that Aba 'Ahd Allah d-H&z informed him, quoting Abii Muhammad Ahmad h. 'Abd Allah al-Muzani, quoting Yiisuf b. Miisa, quoting H i s h h. Khdid, quotingal-Walid b. Muslim, quoting 'Ahd All& h. al-Mubiirak, from Abii Bakr al-Hudhali, from 'Ikrima, the freed-man of Ibn 'Abbas, from Shayba h. Wthmzn, who said, "When I saw at the battle of Hunayn that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was exposed, 1remembered my father and my uncle and how 'Ali and Hamza had killed them. So I said (to myself) today 1'11 get my revenge on the Messenger of God (SAAS). "I went to approach him from his right, but there was al-'Abb% h. 'Abd al-Munalib standing dressed in white metal armour, looking l i e silver with its tarnish wiped away. I said, 'He's his uncle - he'll not desert him.' Then I went at him from the left and came upon Abii Sufyw b. d-Haith h. 'Ahd al-Mu@lih. I told myself, 'That's his c o u s i n he'll not desert him.' Then I came at him from behind. All I had left to do was attack him fiercely with my sword when a great flame like lightning arose heween myself and h i . I was afraid it would hum me, so I put my hand over my eyes and retreated. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then turned and said, 'Shayba, come closer to me. 0 God, take Satan away from him!' I then lifted my gaze to h i and found him to be dearer to me than my own sight or hearing. He then said, 'Shayb, fight the unbelievers!'" Ihn Ishaq quoted Shayba b. Wthmzn b. Abii Talha, a brother of Banii 'Abd al-DZr, as saying, "I said, 'Today I will get my revenge'" (his father had been killed at Uhud). 'Today I shall kill Muhammad!' I went around the Messenger of God (SAAS) to kill him, but something overwhelmed me so that I could not do that; I knew that it was forbidden for me."
lessons: Divine protection operates in ways beyond human comprehension — when Allah wills something, no power can overcome it.
Hatred born from personal loss can blind a person to truth, but the heart is always capable of transformation when Allah wills it.
The Prophet's response to his would-be assassin — praying for him and inviting him closer — demonstrates that responding to enmity with compassion is among the highest forms of character.
practical_advice: When you feel consumed by anger or the desire for revenge, pause and reflect on whether that feeling is truly guiding you toward what is right or simply toward what feels satisfying in the moment.

If you have harbored resentment toward someone, consider that a change of heart is possible — for Shayba, the transformation was instantaneous; for others it may come gradually through reflection and prayer.

Ask Allah sincerely to remove hatred and bitterness from your heart, just as the Prophet asked Allah to remove Satan from Shayba — healing from resentment often begins with this kind of intentional supplication.

title: Jubayr ibn Mut'im Witnesses the Angels at Hunayn
summary: During the Battle of Hunayn, Jubayr ibn Mut'im witnessed what appeared to be a black coverlet of ants descending from the sky and filling the valley, immediately routing the enemy, and those present had no doubt it was the angels.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq also quoted his father, Ishaq b. Yasar, from another man, who quoted Jubayr b. Mutcim as saying, "I was with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the battle of Hunayn and while the battle was going on, I saw something like a black coverlet falling from the sky that descended between us and the enemy. We saw it to be ants that had spread all over and were filling the valley. Immediately the enemy were defeated. We had no doubt that it was the angels."
lessons: Allah's help can arrive in forms that are unexpected and beyond the ordinary — victory is ultimately in His hands, not in numerical superiority or military strength.
Bearing witness to divine intervention strengthens faith and serves as a reminder that the unseen world actively participates in the affairs of the believers.
Certainty of faith is not always the result of formal argument; sometimes it comes from direct experience that leaves no room for doubt.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by circumstances that seem beyond your control, remind yourself of historical accounts like this one that demonstrate divine intervention arrives precisely when human means are exhausted.
Reflect regularly on moments in your own life where outcomes seemed impossible yet resolved — cultivating gratitude for these moments strengthens your trust in Allah.

Share accounts of divine help with others who are struggling; just as the companions narrated what they witnessed to strengthen the community's faith, your testimony of Allah's mercy can be a source of hope for someone else.

title: The Defeat and Death of Thaqif's Banner Bearers at Hunayn
summary: During the Battle of Hunayn, seventy men of Thaqif from Banu Malik were killed beneath their banner, with successive banner bearers fighting until slain, and the Prophet commented on one of the fallen.
story: Ibn Ishxq went on, "When Hawzzin were defeated, widespread execution of Thaqif of Bana Malik took place. Seventy of their men were killed beneath their banner, which was under the charge of Dhn al-Khimzr. When he was killed, W t h m a b. 'Abd Allah b. Rabicab. d-Hitrith b. Habib took it and fought on with it until killed. 'Arnir b. Wahb b. al-Aswad told me that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) learned of his death, he said, 'God damn him! He hated Quraysh!'" Ibn Ishaq went on to quote Yacqabb. Wtba as having said that along with this man Wthmnn, a Christian slave of his was also killed. One of the an@r came to remove the youth's possessions and found h i to be uncircumcised. So he called out at the top of his voice, ''Hey, Arabs, Thaqif are uncircumcised!' "Al-Mughira b. Shu'ba al-Thaqafi said that upon hearing this, 'I took him by the hand, fearing that this calumny would spread among the Arabs and told him, "Don't say that, may my father and mother be your ransom! He was just a Christian slave of ours." I then uncovered the dead for him, asking, "Can't you see that they are indeed circumcised?"'"
lessons: Protecting the honor and reputation of a community, even in the chaos of battle, reflects a deep sense of responsibility toward one's people.
Misinformation spread in moments of heightened emotion can cause lasting damage — the instinct to correct a false claim quickly, as al-Mughira did, is a form of integrity.
Courage is not only displayed on the battlefield but also in standing up to correct a harmful narrative even when it may be personally costly.
practical_advice: When you witness someone making a damaging claim about a group of people based on incomplete information, speak up calmly and with evidence to correct it, as al-Mughira did.
In moments of group conflict or tension, be cautious about how quickly you attribute wrongdoing to an entire community — seek to verify before amplifying.
Reflect on how protecting communal honor is an act of service; small interventions in the spread of harmful narratives can prevent larger social harm.

title: The Flight of Qarib ibn al-Aswad and the Death of Dhu al-Khimar
summary: At Hunayn, the banner of Banu al-Ahlaf was abandoned as its bearer Qarib ibn al-Aswad fled with his family while Dhu al-Khimar led his men to their deaths, and the Prophet commented on the fallen leader of Thaqif.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "The banner of (Bann) al-mnf was with @rib b. al-Aswad. When the enemy were defeated, he leaned it against a tree and he, his relatives and his people all fled. Only two of the al-Ahlaf were killed - one man of Bana Ghiyara named Wahb and a man of Kubba named al-Jullah. When he heard of the death of d-Jullxh, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'The leading young man of Thaqif, except for him descended from Ibn Hunayda, has been killed today.'" By this latter man he was referring to al-Harith b. Uways.
lessons: Leadership carries with it the weight of responsibility for those who follow — those who abandon their people in crisis leave behind a legacy of shame while those who stand firm are remembered.
The Prophet's acknowledgment of al-Jullah's status even in death shows the importance of recognizing worth and dignity in every person regardless of which side they fought on.
Courage under pressure distinguishes leaders; when circumstances become most difficult, character is revealed most clearly.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult situation, ask yourself whether the choice you are about to make is one you will be able to stand behind with honor afterward — the moment of pressure is when character is truly tested.
Avoid the temptation to abandon commitments or responsibilities when things become hard; the consequences of desertion — whether in relationships, work, or community — can be long-lasting.
Acknowledge and honor those who showed integrity and strength, even if they were on the opposing side of a conflict; doing so reflects a commitment to truth over tribalism.

title: Al-Abbas ibn Mirdas's Poem Reflecting on Hunayn and the Call to Islam
summary: Al-Abbas ibn Mirdas composed verses after the Battle of Hunayn reflecting on the defeat of Hawazin, the contrast between those who fled and those who stood firm, and calling the remaining opponents to accept Islam as brothers.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated further, "Al-cAhb% h. Mirdas spoke the following verses, referring to Qtrib b. al-Aswad and his having fled from his brothers, and to Dho al-Khima's having constrained his people and himself to death: 'Who is it will inform Ghaylw for me and, I Imagme, someone knowing will go to him, And Wrwa too; I have only to lead you to a response and a statement that suggests the contrary to what you both say: That Muhammad is a servant and a messenger for a Lord who does not lead astray nor does He do wrong. We find him to be a prophet l i e Moses, and whatever man thinks himself better than him is deluded. Terrible was the state of Bane Qasi in Wajj, when the matters were decreed. They mined their situation; and for every people there is a leader, and fortunes may well change. We attacked them like forest lions; the forces of God move in plain view. We attacked the force of Bann Qasi enraged, almost flying at them I swear that had they stayed, we woold have brought troops against them and they would not have escaped. We were (like) lions of L~ya' there until we revealed them and al-Nosiir were made to surrender. And (there was) a day before that of Honayn that has now gone by when blood streamed. You'll hear of no day like today; a people of long memory has not heard of such. We killed Banii H u @ n on the dust, over their banners, while the cavalry shifted away. Dho al-Khimsr was no leader for a people who had intelligence who could be punished or disavowed. H e took them on the roads to death, matters being clear for those who could see them Some on the point of death escaped and survived, while many of them were killed. A feeble man would not help such a situation, nor would someone lacking modesty or over-cautious. H e destroyed them and perished; they gave him leadership over their affairs and the chiefs left. Bano 'Awf have horses that trot along and are given hay and barley to eat. Were it not for Qtrib and his father's sons, the pastures and forts woold be divided up But they occupied the leadership by luck to which they were directed. They obeyed Qtrib, and good fortune and aspirations lead to glory. If they are led to Islam, they will be found to be leaders of men for so long as day and night persist. If they do not accept Islam, then they call for war against God and shall have no helper. Just as war erased Banii Saki and fate brought evil to the clan of Banii Ghaziyya. As though Bana Mu'gwiya b. Bakr came to Islam like sheep, bleating. We said, 'Accept Islam! We are your brothers; our hearts are innocent of old hatred.' It was as though when the people came to us, they were, after the peace, devoid of hate.'"
lessons: Poetry and eloquent speech have always been powerful tools for calling people to truth and reflecting on the meaning of historical events.

The invitation to Islam extended in this poem — 'we are your brothers; our hearts are innocent of old hatred' — models how reconciliation after conflict should be approached with sincerity, not condescension.
Every leader and community faces a reckoning with their choices; al-Abbas's verses show that acknowledging past errors while extending a hand forward is a mark of wisdom.
practical_advice: When a conflict has ended, approach reconciliation by genuinely releasing old grudges rather than maintaining internal resentment while performing external peace — true brotherhood requires clean hearts.
Use creative expression — writing, poetry, journaling — to process difficult events, make sense of what happened, and articulate what you believe to be true.

When inviting someone toward a better path, lead with the assurance that your heart holds no old hatred toward them; removing the fear of judgment makes it far easier for others to take the first step.

title: Malik ibn Awf at the Pass After the Defeat of Hawazin
summary: After Hawazin's defeat at Hunayn, their leader Malik ibn Awf regrouped at a mountain pass, observing the approaching Muslim cavalry squadrons and assessing the threat each group posed to his remaining forces.
story: Chapter. When Hawsin had been defeated, their leader Malik h. 'Awf al-Nasri halted at a pass with a group of his men and told them to stay there until their weaker comrades and those at their rear caught up with them. Ibn Ishaq stated that he had been informed that some horsemen came up while Mdik and his companions were at the pass. He asked his men, "What do you see?" "We can see men who place their lances between the ears of their horses that have long flanks." Malik commented, "Those are Banii Salim; you should not fear them." When they arrived there they followed a route in the middle of the valley. Then other riders appeared, following the first group. Malik asked his men, "What do you see?" They replied, "We see men who rest their lances carelessly across their horses." "Those", M s l i said, "are al-Aws and d-Khazraj. You should not fear them." When these men arrived at the base of the pass, they took the same route as Banii Salim. Then another horseman appeared,
lessons: Even in defeat, a skilled leader remains observant, calm, and analytical — Malik's ability to assess each approaching group rather than panic reflects the value of composure under pressure.
Not every threat is equally dangerous; discernment in the face of difficulty allows you to allocate your energy wisely rather than being overwhelmed by everything at once.
Looking after those who are weaker and slower — Malik's instruction to wait for his rear companions — reflects the principle that a true leader does not abandon the vulnerable in a crisis.
practical_advice: When you are in a difficult situation, resist the urge to react to every development at once — practice the discipline of observation and assessment before acting, as Malik did from the pass.
Identify which of your current challenges are truly urgent and which only feel urgent; not every difficulty requires the same level of response.
In times of personal crisis, make sure you are not leaving behind those who depend on you — check in on the people in your life who may be struggling to keep up.

title: Al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam Dislodges the Enemy from the Pass
summary: During the aftermath of the Battle of Hunayn, al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam single-handedly charged the enemy at the mountain pass, thrusting at them repeatedly until he dislodged them from their position.
story: Then another horseman appeared, and Malik asked his men, "What do you see?" They told him, "We see a rider on a long-flanked horse holding his lance on his shoulder and with his head covered in a red kerchief." "That is al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwam," Malik observed, "I swear by al-Lat, he will attack you, so fight back hard." When al-Zubayr reached the base of the pass, he saw them, made for them and kept thrusting at them until he had dislodged them from it.
lessons: Courage in the face of overwhelming odds is a form of reliance on God, not recklessness.
A single determined individual acting with conviction can shift the course of events entirely.
True bravery is recognized even by one's opponents, as Malik himself acknowledged al-Zubayr's ferocity.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult challenge, take the first decisive step rather than waiting for perfect conditions.
Draw strength from your convictions and values when fear tries to paralyze you.
Reflect on figures of courage from Islamic history when you need motivation to act despite fear.

title: The Prophet Forbids the Killing of Women and Hired Labourers
summary: After the Battle of Hunayn, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) came upon a slain woman killed by Khalid ibn al-Walid's vanguard and immediately issued a prohibition against killing women, children, and hired labourers in battle.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that he was informed by a colleague that on that day the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by a woman whom Khalid b. al-Walid had killed; a crowd had gathered around her. He told one of his companions, "Go to Khalid and tell him that God's messenger forbids you from killing children, women or hired labourers." Ibn Ishaq related it thus without a complete line of transmission. Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Abu 'Amir 'Abd al-Malik b. 'Amr, quoting al-Mughira b. 'Abd al-Rahman, from Abu al-Zinad, who said that al-Muraqqa' b. Sayfi related to him, from his grandfather Rabi'a b. Rabi', brother of Banu Hawazin al-Ghifa, who stated that on one occasion the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from a military expedition he had waged in which Khalid b. al-Walid was in the vanguard. Rabi'a and the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by a dead woman who had been struck down by the vanguard. They had halted and were looking down admiringly at her when the Messenger of God (SAAS), riding his mount, caught up with them. They drew away from her and the Messenger of God (SAAS) halted over her and said, "This woman should not have been killed!" He then told one of his men, "Go to Khalid and tell him, 'No women nor hired labourers must be killed!'"
lessons: Ethical boundaries in conflict are non-negotiable; mercy and justice must govern even the most intense circumstances.
Leaders have the responsibility to correct wrongs immediately, regardless of the heat of the moment.
The sanctity of human life is a foundational principle in Islam that supersedes military objectives.
practical_advice: When you have acted wrongly, even in the heat of anger or pressure, acknowledge it and correct course immediately.
Hold yourself to ethical standards even when those around you do not; your conscience is a guide.
Reflect on how the Prophet's immediate reaction to injustice can inspire you to speak up against wrongdoing in your own life.

title: The Expedition to Awtas: Defeat of the Remnants of Hawazin
summary: After the Battle of Hunayn, the Prophet dispatched Abu 'Amir al-Ash'ari to pursue the remnant Hawazin forces camped at Awtas, and after fierce fighting the Muslims achieved victory, though Abu 'Amir was mortally wounded.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the polytheists were defeated at the battle of Hunayn, they went to al-Ta'if, in the company of Malik b. 'Awf. Some of them encamped at Awtas, while others, consisting only of men of Banu Ghira of Thaqif, went towards Nakhla. The cavalry of the Messenger of God (SAAS) followed those who took the path through the pass." Ibn Ishaq stated further, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent after those of the enemy who had headed for Awtas a force under Abu 'Amir al-Ash'ari. He caught up with some of those who had fled and they engaged in battle. An arrow was shot at and killed Abu 'Amir and Abu Musa al-Ash'ari took the banner, he being the cousin of Abu 'Amir, battled them and God, Almighty and Glorious, gave him victory over them. They claim that it was Salama b. Durayd who shot Abu 'Amir al-Ash'ari with an arrow, striking him in the knee and killing him."
lessons: Leadership must be passed on with trust when circumstances demand it; Abu Musa stepped forward when Abu 'Amir fell.
Victory comes from God, and human effort is only the means through which His decree is fulfilled.
Every sacrifice made in service of a righteous cause carries weight and meaning beyond what we can see.
practical_advice: When a responsibility is suddenly placed on your shoulders due to another's incapacitation, trust in God and step forward with what you know.
Do not let unexpected setbacks cause you to abandon the mission; adapt and continue.
Remember that outcomes are in God's hands; your duty is to act with sincerity and effort.

title: The Capture and Death of Durayd ibn al-Simma
summary: During the pursuit after Hunayn, the young warrior Rabi'a ibn Rafi' al-Sulami captured and killed the aged tribal leader Durayd ibn al-Simma, who calmly guided his own killer on how to strike him properly.
story: "Rafi'a b. Rafi' b. Ahran al-Sulami - known by his mother's name as 'son of al-Daghina' - captured Durayd b. al-Simma. He did so by taking the reins of his camel, thinking that a woman and not he, was in the howdah upon it. When he made the camel kneel, he discovered an old man inside, Durayd b. al-Simma, whom the young man, Rabi'a, did not recognize. Durayd asked him, 'What do you want of me?' He replied, 'I am going to kill you.' 'Who are you?' Durayd asked. 'I am Rabi'a b. Rafi' al-Sulami.' Rabi'a then struck Durayd with his sword, but the blow was ineffective. Durayd commented, 'Well, your mother did not give you a good weapon! Here, take my sword from the back of my saddle in the howdah, then use it to strike me; hit above the backbone and below the skull. Now that's how I would strike men! And when you go to your mother, tell her that you killed Durayd b. al-Simma. I swear that many were the times I defended your women!' Banu Sulaym claim that Rabi'a said, 'When I struck him and he fell down, he was exposed and the skin high up between his thighs was like parchment from his having so often ridden bareback.' When Rabi'a returned to his mother and told her of having killed him, she said, 'I swear, he set free three of your mothers!'"
lessons: Even at the end of life, a person's character and dignity can remain intact; how we face hardship reveals who we truly are.
The consequences of our choices in battle and in leadership can echo across generations.
Death comes for all; what matters is the legacy of service and protection we leave behind.
practical_advice: In moments of loss or defeat, maintain your dignity and face circumstances with composure, as Durayd modeled even at the end.
Reflect on the lives of those who served others selflessly, and let their example inspire you to acts of protection and care in your own community.
When life does not go as planned, focus on what you can still do with integrity in the time that remains.

title: Abu 'Amir's Final Moments and the Prophet's Prayer for His Forgiveness
summary: Mortally wounded in the Battle of Awtas, Abu 'Amir al-Ash'ari asked his nephew Abu Musa to convey his greetings to the Prophet and request a prayer for his forgiveness; the Prophet raised his hands and prayed earnestly for him.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that Muhammad b. al-'Ala' related to him, quoting Abu Usama, from Burayid b. 'Abd Allah, from Abu Burda, from Abu Musa, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had finished at Hunayn, he sent Abu 'Amir in command of an army to Awtas. There he encountered Durayd b. al-Simma who was killed, while his companions were defeated." Abu Musa went on, "He sent me forth with Abu 'Amir, who was shot in the knee, the arrow, shot by a man of Banu Jusham, lodging in it. When I reached him, I asked, 'Who shot you, uncle?' He pointed over at the man, saying, 'There, that's the man who shot and killed me!' I made for him, but he withdrew when he saw me coming. I followed on, calling out, 'Aren't you ashamed not to stand and fight?' he stopped retreating and we exchanged a few sword strokes then I killed him. I went and told Abu 'Amir, 'God has killed your enemy!' He asked me, 'Take out this arrow.' I did so and water seeped from the wound. He then requested, 'Nephew, deliver my greeting to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and ask him to pray for my forgiveness.' Abu 'Amir then appointed me in command of our men and after a short while he died. I returned and went in to see the Messenger of God (SAAS) inside his house. He was lying on a rope bed of date-palms and stalks with a coverlet over him; the ropes and strands of the bed had made marks on his back and sides. I told him our news and about Abu 'Amir and how he had told me, 'Ask him to seek forgiveness for me.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) called for water to be brought, performed the ablution, then raised his arms praying, 'O God, forgive Ubayd, Abu 'Amir!' I saw the whiteness of his underarms. He then said, 'O God, place him on the Judgement Day of Resurrection above many of Your creation' - or 'of many people'. I said, 'Will you also ask forgiveness for me?' He then said, 'O God, forgive 'Abd Allah b. Qays his sins and on Resurrection Day grant him a favourable entry (into paradise).'" Abu Burda noted, "One of the prayers was for Abu 'Amir, the other for Abu Musa, may God be pleased with them both."
lessons: The intercession of the righteous for one another reflects the deep bonds of brotherhood that Islam cultivates.
Even the Prophet, the most honoured of creation, lived simply on a rope bed; humility is a mark of true greatness.
Asking for forgiveness and prayers from loved ones as one faces death is a beautiful and prophetically endorsed act.
practical_advice: When you are struggling or facing a crisis, do not hesitate to ask those you trust to pray for you; this is a Sunnah.
Reflect on the simplicity of the Prophet's life as a remedy when materialism or worldly comparison causes you stress or dissatisfaction.
Hold on to hope for God's mercy even in your darkest moments; the Prophet prayed for Abu 'Amir's elevation even after his death.

title: Abu 'Amir Invites Nine Enemies to Islam Before Killing Them
summary: During the Battle of Awtas, Abu 'Amir al-Ash'ari called each enemy fighter to accept Islam before engaging him, killed nine of them, and the tenth, after refusing and then relenting, eventually accepted Islam and became a good Muslim.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated further, "An authority on poetry and its transmission whom I trust told me that Abu 'Amir al-Ash'ari confronted the polytheist brothers. One of them attacked him and Abu 'Amir fought back, calling him to accept Islam, saying, 'O God, bear witness against him!' Then Abu 'Amir killed him. A second polytheist attacked him and Abu 'Amir fought back, inviting him into Islam, saying, 'O God, bear witness against him.' Abu 'Amir then killed him. At that they all charged at him while he spoke as before. Eventually he had killed nine of them and only the tenth remained. He attacked Abu 'Amir, who fought back, calling upon him to accept Islam, saying, 'O God, bear witness against him.' Then the man called out, 'O God, do not bear witness against me!' At that Abu 'Amir stopped fighting him and the man withdrew. He thereafter accepted Islam and was a good Muslim. When he saw him, the Prophet (SAAS) would comment, 'That's the fugitive from Abu 'Amir.'"
lessons: Calling people to goodness, even in the most extreme circumstances, reflects the mercy that Islam demands of its adherents.
God's guidance can reach anyone at any moment; the tenth man's plea changed the entire trajectory of his life.
Consistency in principle, as Abu 'Amir demonstrated by calling to Islam every single time, is a form of spiritual integrity.
practical_advice: Even when you feel unheard or your efforts seem futile, continue your good conduct; you do not know who will be moved by it.
If you have been running away from God or from goodness, know that one sincere turning back is enough to transform your life entirely.
Let your interactions with others, even in conflict, be guided by the hope for their wellbeing, not merely by your own interests.

title: Martyrs of the Battle of Hunayn and the Expedition to Awtas
summary: Four companions were martyred at the Battle of Hunayn and the Expedition to Awtas, including Ayman ibn Umm Ayman and Abu Amir al-Ash'ari, the commander of the Awtas expedition.
story: Chapter: On those who were martyred at the battle of Hunuyn and on the Expedition to Awtas. (These men were) Ayman b. Umm Ayman, freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS), he being Ayman b. 'Ubayd; Zayd b. Zam'a b. al-Aswad b. al-Muttalib b. Asad, whose horse, named Jandal, bolted and so he was killed; Suraqa b. Malik b. al-Harith b. 'Adi al-Ansari, of Banu al-'Ajlan; Abu 'Amir al-Ash'ari, the commander of the expedition to Awtas. These total four men; may God be pleased with them.
lessons: The sacrifice of companions who gave their lives for the faith serves as a reminder that true devotion often demands the ultimate price.
Remembering those who were martyred cultivates gratitude for the freedoms and blessings we enjoy as a result of their sacrifice.
Every soul lost in a noble cause is honoured by God, and their memory deserves to be preserved with reverence and love.
practical_advice: When grieving the loss of loved ones or facing personal loss, reflect on the legacy they leave behind and how their sacrifice continues to benefit others.
Channel feelings of sadness into gratitude by acknowledging the contributions others have made to causes greater than themselves.
Keep a practice of remembering those who have passed by invoking blessings upon them, which brings comfort to the heart and maintains spiritual connection.

title: Poetry of Bujayr ibn Zuhayr at the Expedition Against Hawazin
summary: Bujayr ibn Zuhayr composed verses celebrating God's honour bestowed upon the believers at the Battle of Hunayn, describing how God destroyed and humiliated the enemies who worshipped Satan.
story: Among these verses are those of Bujayr b. Zuhayr b. Abi Salma, 'Had it not been for God and His servant, you would have retreated when terror overcame every coward At the winding valley that day our enemies faced us, the swift horses stumbled on to their chins. Some men went running, their garments held up in their hands, others were knocked to the side by hooves and flanks. God honoured us and gave victory to our faith, dignifying us through worship of the Most Merciful. And God destroyed them, dispersing and humiliating them for worshipping Satan.' Ibn Hisham stated that some transmitters of the poetry include the verses, 'When Your Prophet's uncle and his ally arose, calling, O squadron of the faith! Where are they who responded to their Lord, at the battle of Badr and at the 'willing allegiance.''
lessons: True victory belongs only to God, and those who place their trust in Him are never truly defeated even when circumstances appear dire.
Expressing gratitude through poetry, remembrance, and artistic creation is a healthy and spiritually enriching way to process triumph and relief.
Community rallying around a shared noble cause produces a sense of belonging and collective strength that sustains individuals through hardship.
practical_advice: When you experience relief or victory after a period of hardship, pause to sincerely attribute your success to God rather than solely to your own efforts.
Find creative outlets such as writing, journaling, or poetry to process and articulate your emotions during both difficult and joyful times.
Surround yourself with a community that shares your values so that in moments of fear or retreat you have companions who call you back to your purpose.

title: The Battle Poetry of 'Abbas ibn Mirdas al-Sulami at Hunayn
summary: 'Abbas ibn Mirdas al-Sulami composed multiple poems recounting the steadfastness of Banu Sulaym at Hunayn, their pledge of allegiance at Mecca, and their unwavering obedience to the Prophet despite the chaos and ferocity of battle.
story: (Ibn Ishaq stated) ''Abbas b. Mirdas al-Sulami spoke the following, 'By the swift horses on assembly day and by the Book that the Messenger recited, I was pleased at Thaqif's painful fate yesterday beside the defile They are the enemy's leaders from Najd, and killing them is sweeter than drinking. We defeated their group, that group of Banu Qasi, and fierce was the fighting for Banu Ri'ab. The tents of Banu Hilal were left at Awtas begrimed with dust. If (our forces) had clashed with Banu Kilab, their women would have risen with dust upon them. We raced our horses among them between Buss and al-Awral, breathing hard in pursuit of the booty Amidst a clamorous army that included the squadron of the Messenger of God that charged into the battle.' "'Abbas b. Mirdas also spoke the following, 'O seal of the prophets, you are sent with the truth; all guidance on the right path being yours. God built upon you love in His creation, and Muhammad he named you. Then those who were true to the pact you gave them became an army, over whom you appointed al-Dahhak A man with a sharp sword who, when surrounded by the enemy, saw only you. He would even battle those near in blood, seeking to please the All-Compassionate and yourself too. I tell you I witnessed his repeated attacks in the turmoil of war, braining the polytheists, Sometimes strangling them with his hands, sometimes slashing their heads with his sharp sword, Using it to smash the warriors' heads; if you saw what I did, it pleased you well, While Banu Sulaym charged before him, striking and thrusting constantly at the enemy. They walked beneath his banner as though lions of a thicket determined to battle there. Not wanting closeness from their relatives, seeking only that they obey their Lord and to please you. These were our actions for which we were renowned, our ally being your Lord.' "'Abbas b. Mirdas also spoke the following, 'Mijdal is empty of its people now, as is Mutali'; and the plains of Arak are bare, as are its cisterns. Homes of ours, O Juml, when our lives were fine and easy, and time's changes would unite our family with A lover whom great distance has diverted; can life now past ever return? If she seeks after the unbelievers, she is not to be blamed; for I am the assistant and follower of the Prophet. The best of envoys I have known, Khuzayma, brought us, and al-Mirrar is of them, and Wabi' too. And so we came, along with 1,000 men of Sulaym, upon whom were splendid coats of mail made by Da'ud. We pledged allegiance to him at al-Akhshabayn, though it was the hand of God to whom we really pledged there. We went into Mecca with the mahdi, the one well-led, by force, our swords in the rising dust, glistening, openly, The bodies of our horses were covered with sweat, groaning, their blood within them boiling, At the battle of Hunayn when Hawazin came at us, and our tight ribs restricted our breathing We kept firm with al-Dahhak, undeterred by the blows and strikes of our enemies Before the Messenger of God, while a banner flew above us, shining like lightning above clouds. That evening Dahhak b. Sufyan struck with the sword of the Messenger of God, and death was near at hand. We defended our brother from our brother, and if we had seen an option, we would have followed our relatives. But the religion of God is that of Muhammad; we are pleased with it. It has guidance and laws. Through it he set us aright after our mistaken course; and no matter decided by God can be prevented.' "'Abbas b. Mirdas also spoke the following, 'The remaining connection with Umm Mu'ammal has been ended, for she changed her intention, against her word. She had sworn by God not to sever the link; she did not speak true in that, and was not true to her word. She is from Banu Khufaf who spent the summer in the al-'Aqiq valley, living in the desert at Wajra and al-Waris. Even if Umm Mu'ammal follows the unbelievers, she has stocked my heart with passion despite her distance. Someone will tell her that we refused; the only ally we seek is our Lord, And that we are with the guide, the Prophet, Muhammad and that 1,000 are with us, matched by no other tribe, With trustworthy heroes of Sulaym, men who obey and do not disobey by even a syllable. Khufaf, Dhakwan and 'Awf, whose path you would imagine strewn with (disuniting) difficulties, (But) our glossy armour and helmets were as though worn by frowning lions slashing in their lairs. Through us God's religion gained strength; we added a like number to the group already with him At Mecca when we came; it was as if our banner was an eagle that, having soared, was ready to swoop down. Upon mounts staring fixedly ahead, their forcing against their bits you would think inspired by a genie's hum, That morning we trod the polytheists underfoot, finding no deviation or change from the orders of the Messenger of God, At a battle amidst which the men heard from us no urgings except to fight and to crush heads Sent flying from their supports by our swords which sliced right through the warriors' necks. Many's the man we left struck down, and many the widow weeping bitterly over her husband. It is God's pleasure we seek, not that of man; and to God belongs all that is apparent and hidden.' "'Abbas b. Mirdas, may God be pleased with him, also spoke the following verses, 'Why are your eyes sleepless, as though bothered by irritants, husks beneath their lids? Eyes made sleepless from their pain, welling and streaming their tears, Which look like pearls scattered when the man stringing them breaks their thread. How far away is the home of her for whose love you hope, between you and whom al-Samman and al-Hafar stand! Abandon your youth now gone, long past for your hair is grey and thin. And recall the distress of Sulaym in their lands; yet Sulaym have reason to have pride. They are a people who aided the Most Merciful and followed the religion of the Messenger while people were in conflict. They no longer plant palm shoots in their land, nor do cattle low in their wintering grounds Where only fast steeds, like eagles, are kept at hand amidst lands filled with herds of camels. Khufaf and 'Awf were brought to their flanks, and the clan of Dhakwan did not turn away in annoyance. They struck openly at the polytheist armies in Mecca's valley and their souls were whisked away Until we left, their bodies like palm trees uprooted on the plain's surface. At the battle of Hunayn our witness was to the glory of the religion and it is recorded with God. When we rode death, dingy-hued its inner flanks, while the horses emitted a dusky glow Beneath the banner, with al-Dahhak leading us, like a lurking lion walking within his forest, In dire straits due to the burdens of warfare that almost caused sun and moon to fade. We remained steadfast at Awtas; our lances dedicated to God, we helped those we wished, and won victory. And finally people returned to their homes who, were it not for the Sovereign and ourselves, would not have emerged. You will not see any tribe, small in number or large, without our having had some influence upon them.' "'Abbas also spoke the following, 'O man who is borne upon a young, strong-boned, swift-footed camel If you should go to the Prophet, then say to him as it is your duty'"
lessons: Remaining steadfast in one's commitments even when tribal bonds and personal relationships pull in the opposite direction is one of the highest expressions of faith and integrity.
True allegiance is ultimately to God and His guidance, and when personal desires conflict with divine direction, the believer chooses the path of God without resentment.
Reflecting on past hardships and victories, as 'Abbas did through poetry, is a meaningful way to consolidate one's identity, find meaning in suffering, and inspire future generations.
practical_advice: When you face divided loyalties between family, community, or personal desires and your values, seek clarity by returning to your core principles and asking what God would have you choose.
Use journaling, storytelling, or creative expression to process the emotional weight of difficult experiences, transforming pain and tension into something meaningful and enduring.
Cultivate pride in your community's positive contributions without letting it slide into arrogance, recognising that any good accomplished was made possible only through God's aid.

title: Poetry of Abbas ibn Mirdas al-Sulami After Hunayn
summary: Abbas ibn Mirdas al-Sulami recited several odes commemorating the Battle of Hunayn, praising the Prophet and the tribe of Sulaym for their loyalty and valor, and lamenting that Hawazin reclaimed their herds.
story: sun and moon to fade. We remained steadfast at Aww; our lances dedicated to God, we helped those we wished, and won victory. And fmally people returned to their homes who, were it not for the Sovereign and ourselves, would not have emerged. You will not see any tribe, small in number or large, without our having had some influence upon them.' 'LcAbbasalso spoke the following, '0 man who is home upon a young, strong-honed, swift-footed camel If you should go to the Prophet, then say to him as it is your duty to do, if the group assembled are at ease, "0 finest man to ride a mount or ever to walk upon the earth, if souls be counted, We were true to our pact with you when the cavalry were overpowered and wounded by the warriors When there streamed a force from all parts of the Buhtha clan that shook the mountain trails Until we attacked the people of Mecca, in a squadron, their armour shining, being led by proud men. From every powerful clan of Sulaym, wearing close-knitted chain-mail upon them Moistening their lances when they charged into the fray, so that you would imagine them as frowning lions. Overwhelming the squadron, their insignia showing, hearing in their hands slashing swords and quick-thrusting spears. At Hunayn there were a thousand in our force by which the Messenger was supported, as by fierce Lions, They were a vanguard ahead of the believers; and that day the sun upon them seemed more intense. We went forward, God guarding us in his care; and God never loses those He guards. We were blockaded at al-Mansqih. But God took pleasure in that; and that makes our blockade worthwhile. The morning of Afl% we made a fierce attack and the enemy drew hack, calling out to us, "Hold off?" Hawain appealed to the brotherhood between us; but the breast Hawazin used to offer is very dry! Finally we left their force like wild asses that lions have tom apart, one after the other.' "He also, may God be pleased with him, spoke the following, 'Who is it will tell the peoples that Muhammad is God's messenger, right-guided wherever he moves? He called out to God, seeking His help alone and He was true and kind to him. We travelled forth, keeping our appointment with Muhammad at Qudayd so that he could have us fulfil a matter that God had decreed. They scouted us at dawn and as dawn broke they perceived us to be young warriors with lances set, Upon horses, and our armour finnly attached, and our numerous infantry rushing forward like a torrent. The best of the tribe - if ask you must -are Sulaym and they include some who claim to be Sulaym And a force of aqzr who would not desert him, obedient to him and they do not disobey him in what he says. Since you have appointed and ~mmotedKhdid commander of the anny, he has indeed advanced With an army, guided by God, of which you are leader. With it you rightfully strike those who are evil. I have sworn a genuine oath to M & m a d and have fulfilled it with a thousand horses, fully bridled. The Prophet of the believers called, 'Advance!' And it pleased us to he the vanguard. We spent the night at Mustadir pool. We had no fear only enthusiasm and determination. We obeyed you until all the enemy accepted Islam, attacking their force in the morning, those people at Yalamlama. (A force so large) even a piebald red stallion could go astray amidst it, and an old man be insecure unless well marked. We surprised them like a covey of grouse startled at dawn, each of which you see to be uncaring for its brother, That morning; until finally we left them that evening at Hunayn, its streams flowing with blood. If you wished, you could everywhere see file race horses, their riders stretched out besides broken lances. Hawain have regained from us their herds; and for us to he disappointed and deprived of these has pleased them."' Thus did Imam Muhammad b. I s h ~ qgive the poems of 'AbbZs b. Mirdas al-Sulami, may God be pleased with him. We have omitted some of the odes he included for fear of being long-winded and boring. Ibn Ishaq also gives the poetry of others; but enough on the subject has been given above. Though God knows best.
lessons: Fulfilling one's covenant with the Prophet and with God, even at great personal cost, is itself a form of worship and a source of lasting honor.
True victory is defined by steadfastness and loyalty, not merely by material gains; the tribe of Sulaym felt pride in their commitment even when the enemy recovered some spoils.
Gratitude for God's protection should be expressed openly and passed on to future generations through remembrance and testimony.
practical_advice: When you have kept a difficult promise or remained loyal through hardship, take time to acknowledge and reflect on that faithfulness — it is a source of inner strength.
If you feel unappreciated or overlooked after contributing greatly to a shared effort, remind yourself that God witnesses every act of loyalty and nothing sincere is lost.
Use storytelling, journaling, or poetry to process and preserve your experiences of overcoming adversity, as doing so can reinforce resilience and inspire others.

title: The Expedition Against al-Ta'if: The Prophet's March and Siege
summary: After the Battle of Hunayn, the routed Thaqif army retreated to al-Ta'if and fortified themselves; the Prophet marched to besiege the city, reciting the route's landmarks and events along the way.
story: THE EXPEDITION AGAINST AL-TA'IF. Wrwa and Miisa b. 'Uqba quoted al-Zuhri as stating that the Messenger of God (SAAS) fought at Hunayn and besieged al-Ta'if in Shawwal of 8 AH. Muhammad h. Ishxq stated, "The routed Thaqif army reached al-Tg'if, shut themselves behind the gate of the city and fortified themselves in preparation for conflict. "Neither Wrwa h. Masciid nor Ghaylm h. Salama were present at Hunayn or the siege of al-Ta'if. They were at Jurash learning how to construct testudos for use at a distance and close by the walls. "Having finished at Hunayn, the Messenger of God (SAAS) went on to al-Ta'if."
lessons: Persistent effort in the face of a fortified opposition requires patience and strategic thinking — not reckless haste.
Preparation and knowledge, such as the two men learning siege tactics at Jurash, are themselves a form of fulfilling one's duty to God.
Even after a great victory like Hunayn, the work of establishing justice and truth may require further effort and sacrifice.
practical_advice: After achieving one success, resist the temptation to stop; identify what still needs to be addressed and make a clear plan to move forward.
Invest in learning skills and knowledge relevant to your challenges — preparation is not a delay of action but part of the action itself.
When facing a situation where others have 'fortified themselves' against you — whether in a conflict, a relationship, or a negotiation — approach with measured resolve rather than reactive emotion.

title: Ka'b ibn Malik's Poem Concerning the March to al-Ta'if
summary: The companion Ka'b ibn Malik composed verses warning the people of Thaqif of the advancing Muslim army and calling them to accept Islam or face determined resistance.
story: On that subject Ka'b b. M a i k spoke the following verses, 'In the lowlands and at Khaybar we ended all doubt, then rested our swords For them to choose (next). Had they spoken, their blades would have said, "Attack Daws or Thaqif!" May I have no mother if you do not see them at your courtyards, with us in our thousands. We will tear down the pavilions in the Wajj valley, and your homes will he devoid of you. The most swift of our cavalry will attack you, leaving behind them a dense mass When they descend on your areas, you'll hear from them enough to make your camels kneel in alarm. In their hands will be sharp, slim swords, visiting death upon those opposing them, Flashing like lightning, fashioned by Indian blacksmiths, not beaten out flat. You might have thought the blood of heroes in them that battle morning was mixed with saffron. As for their most diligent, had they no adviser in their peoples who knew of us To tell them that we had gathered the most choice and well-bred of horses And had brought against them a force to line up around the walls of their fort? Their leader was the Prophet, resolute, pure of heart, steadfast and abstemious, Mature, wise, knowledgeable, astute, never rash or frivolous. We obey our Prophet and a Lord who is the Most Merciful and has been most kind to us. If you offer us peace we will accept and will make you our allies, and If you refuse, we will fight you steadfastly, and conflict with ui will not be gentle or easy. We will fight on as long as we survive or you turn in repentance to Islam, submissively. We will battle on, careless whom we confront, nor whether we deshoy the old or the new. How numerous were the tribes allied against us, some the central trihes, some their allies. They attacked us thinking themselves unequalled, but we severed nose and ears With our Indian swords, supple and gleaming, violently forcing them forward To the business of God and Islam until the religion is established in justice and devotion And al-L%t, al-Wzzii and Wudd are forgotten and we remove their necklaces and earrings. Now they have become acquiescent and secure; and those who do not refrain (from idolatry) become disgraced."'
lessons: Speaking truth clearly and offering peace before confrontation is a reflection of prophetic character — firmness and mercy are not opposites.
Leadership that is 'resolute, pure of heart, steadfast and abstemious' is the most enduring kind, and following such leadership brings dignity to those who follow.
The ultimate goal of struggle in God's cause is the establishment of justice and sincere devotion — not domination for its own sake.
practical_advice: Before entering any serious confrontation, clearly articulate your terms and leave the door open for peaceful resolution, as the Prophet consistently did.
Reflect on the qualities you most admire in those you look up to — resolve, wisdom, purity of heart — and identify one such quality you can consciously develop this month.
When you feel outnumbered or overwhelmed by opposition, remember that past difficulties you overcame are evidence that God has supported you before and can do so again.

title: The Response Poem of Kinana ibn Abd Yalil al-Thaqafi
summary: Kinana ibn Abd Yalil of Thaqif composed a defiant response to Ka'b ibn Malik's poem, asserting his tribe's intention to defend their homeland, though he later accepted Islam with the Thaqif delegation.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "Kinma b. 'Abd Yalil b. 'Amr b. Wmayr al-Thaqafi answered him in the following verse." (I note that he had later come over to the Messenger of God (SAAS), along with the Thaqif delegation; he accepted Islam along with them. This was so stated by Masa b. Wqba, Aba Ishaq, Aba Wmar b. 'Abd al-Barr, Ibn al-Athir and others. Al-Mads'ini, however, claimed that he did not accept Islam, but went into Byzantine territory, where he accepted Christianity and died.) 'To those seeking us to fight us: we're at a well-known place we will not leave. We know our forefathers were here, where are its terraces and its vineyards. 'Amr b. 'Amir previously put us to the test, and their men of reason and wisdom advised them. They knew - if truth they tell - that we set straight cheeks turned aside in disdain. We reform them; those who are wild we tame and those who do wrong acknowledge the evident truth. We dress in light, supple chain-mail, from a longed-for legacy, sky-coloured its sheen, decorated with stars. We force them off us with our sharp swords which, when once drawn in conflict we do not again sheathe."'
lessons: Even those who initially resist truth and defend their position strongly may eventually open their hearts — never despair of anyone's potential for guidance.
Pride in one's heritage and homeland is a natural human feeling, but it must ultimately be balanced against the greater truth when that truth becomes clear.
Historical accounts remind us that people's final choices — not their initial resistance — are what define their legacy.
practical_advice: When someone seems firmly opposed to a good thing you are trying to share, plant the seed with sincerity and leave the outcome to God — change often comes later and quietly.
Reflect on areas of your own life where pride or attachment to the familiar has slowed your growth, and consider whether openness might lead to something better.
Do not judge others by their worst moments of resistance; give people space to evolve, just as many companions of the Prophet evolved over time.

title: Shaddad ibn Arid al-Jushami's Poem on the Prophet's Arrival at al-Ta'if
summary: The poet Shaddad ibn Arid al-Jushami composed verses warning the people of Thaqif that their idol al-Lat was destroyed and that the Prophet's arrival would leave none of their old ways remaining.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "Shaddad b. 'Arid al-Jushami spoke the following verses concerning the passage of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to al-Tz'if, 'Do not assist al-Lnt whom God destroys; how can aid be given to one who cannot be assisted? She who was burned, flaming in black smoke, while at her stone monument not even the worthless do battle. Once the Messenger descends on your lands and has left, none of its people will remain there."'
lessons: Attachment to false supports — whether idols, harmful habits, or misplaced loyalties — ultimately leaves a person with nothing when those supports are removed.
The arrival of truth in a person's life, even when disruptive, ultimately clears away what could not endure and makes room for what is real and lasting.
Warnings delivered with clarity and brevity can carry great weight; sometimes the most powerful truth is the simplest one.
practical_advice: Identify any 'al-Lat' in your own life — something you rely on for comfort or identity that is actually hollow — and begin consciously redirecting that reliance toward God.
When a major change arrives in your life that feels threatening, ask yourself honestly: what in my current situation truly cannot withstand scrutiny, and what is actually worth holding onto?
Brevity and directness in sharing wisdom or advice is often more effective than lengthy argument; say what is true clearly and let it settle.

title: The Prophet Renames the Difficult Path 'al-Yusra' (The Easy Road)
summary: While marching to al-Ta'if, the Prophet was told the name of a narrow difficult path; he renamed it 'al-Yusra' (the easy road), reflecting his characteristic optimism and trust in God's easing of hardship.
story: 'Amr b. Shu'ayh related to me that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) stopped that day at B u l p t al-Rugha' he allowed retaliation in kind for blood spilled, that being the first occasion in Islam that such permission was given. A man of Banii Layth had killed someone of Hudhayl and he executed him for it. While at Liyya he ordered the destruction of the fort of Malik b. 'Awf. "He then proceeded along a path known as al-Dayyrqa, 'the narrow, difficult path'. As he was setting out along it he asked what it was called and it was so named to him. He commented, 'No; it's al-Yusra, "the easy road"."'
lessons: The Prophet's practice of renaming things with positive meaning reflects a deep spiritual truth: how we speak about our circumstances shapes how we experience them.
Trust in God transforms the perception of difficulty — what appears narrow and hard can be walked with ease when one's heart is anchored in faith.
Small acts of reframing — choosing hopeful language over defeatist language — are a prophetic habit that can be consciously adopted.
practical_advice: The next time you face a daunting path or situation, consciously choose to name it in a way that reflects trust rather than dread — 'a learning curve' rather than 'an impossible obstacle.'
Practice the Prophetic sunnah of choosing good names and good words in your daily speech; language has a real effect on your inner state and on those around you.
When you are anxious about what lies ahead, pause and recall a past difficulty that God brought you through — this builds the conviction that the current 'narrow path' will also open up.

title: The First Permission for Retaliation in Kind and the Destruction of Malik ibn Awf's Fort
summary: On the march to al-Ta'if, the Prophet granted the first formal permission in Islam for retaliation in kind for blood wrongfully spilled, and ordered the destruction of the fort belonging to Malik ibn Awf.
story: 'Amr b. Shu'ayh related to me that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) stopped that day at B u l p t al-Rugha' he allowed retaliation in kind for blood spilled, that being the first occasion in Islam that such permission was given. A man of Banii Layth had killed someone of Hudhayl and he executed him for it. While at Liyya he ordered the destruction of the fort of Malik b. 'Awf.
lessons: Justice requires clear and established principles; the Prophet's careful governance ensured that even in times of military expedition, legal rights were upheld.
Accountability for wrongdoing is a mercy to society as a whole, even when it is difficult for individuals — justice protects the community.
Leadership that attends to justice even during the pressures of military campaign reflects the highest standard of moral governance.
practical_advice: When you are under pressure or moving quickly toward a goal, do not let urgency cause you to overlook matters of fairness and justice — address them as they arise.
Reflect on whether there are unresolved matters of fairness or accountability in your own relationships or responsibilities that deserve attention.
Trust that clear, principled responses to wrongdoing — even when difficult — ultimately create more peace and stability than avoidance.

title: The Tomb of Abu Righal and the Golden Bough
summary: While passing a grave on the way to al-Ta'if, the Prophet identified it as the tomb of Abu Righal of Thamud, who had been protected in the sanctuary but was struck down when he left it; the companions exhumed the grave and found the golden bough the Prophet described.
story: Ihn Ishaq then quoted IsmaCi1 b. Umayya as quoting Bujayr b. Abii Bujayr to have said, "I heard 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr say that he heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say when he went forth with him to al-?%]if and passed by a grave, 'This is the tomb of Abii Righnl,' he being the father of Thaqif. He was of Thamiid and one of those defending their SanChIary there. When he went out (with Abraha) he was afflicted by that same vengeance that came down upon his people at that place, and he was buried there. The proof of this is that along with him a golden bough was buried. If you exhume h i, you will find it.' "The men promptly did so and retrieved the bough, along with his remains." Abii Da'iid narrated this from Y&ya b. Mu'in, from Wahb b. Jarir b. Hzzim, from his father, from Muhammad b. Ishiiq. Al-Bayhaqi narrated it from a hadith of Yazid b. Zuray', from Rawh b. al-@sim, from Isma'il b. Umayya.
lessons: Sanctuary and worldly protection are no substitute for moral alignment with truth — Abu Righal's protection in the sanctuary did not shield him once he chose wrongdoing.
The Prophet's knowledge of the unseen, confirmed by physical evidence, is a sign of his prophethood and a source of certainty for believers.
A person who betrays a great trust or collaborates with oppressors may achieve temporary gain but ultimately shares in the fate of those they assisted.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether you are relying on any form of 'sanctuary' — status, position, connections — as a substitute for genuine integrity; true safety comes from righteousness.
When historical or spiritual lessons seem abstract, look for the 'golden bough' — the concrete evidence in your own experience that confirms what you know to be true.
Be cautious about aligning yourself with powerful but unjust causes simply for protection or advantage; consider where that alignment will ultimately lead.

title: The Muslim Camp Set Near al-Ta'if: Companions Killed by Arrows
summary: When the Prophet's army established camp too close to al-Ta'if, companions were struck and killed by arrows from the fortified city, forcing the army to withdraw to a safer position where a mosque was later built.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then travelled on and established his camp near al-TZ'if. Some of his men were killed by arrows because the camp had been set close to al-Ta'if. They therefore withdrew to the place where today stands his mosque, built by Thaqif after their acceptance of Islam. It was 'Amr b. Umayya b.
lessons: Even the best-led armies and communities make tactical errors that cost lives; acknowledging a mistake and correcting course is wisdom, not weakness.
The companions' willingness to face danger in God's cause, even at the cost of their lives, is a testament to the depth of their conviction and sacrifice.
Places of loss can be transformed into places of remembrance and worship — the mosque built at the withdrawal point is itself a testimony to this truth.
practical_advice: When a plan or approach is clearly causing harm, withdraw and reassess without shame — course correction is a sign of wisdom and leadership, not failure.
Honor the sacrifices that have been made in causes you care about by continuing that work with renewed care and intentionality.

Consider creating a personal 'place of remembrance' — a journal entry, a moment of prayer, or a small ritual — to mark and honor difficult experiences so they become sources of growth rather than sources of unresolved pain.

title: The Siege of al-Ta'if: Camp Established and Casualties from Arrows
summary: The Messenger of God established his camp near al-Ta'if, where Muslims suffered casualties from arrows shot by the defenders, leading them to withdraw and reposition near where a mosque was later built.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then travelled on and established his camp near al-TZ'if. Some of his men were killed by arrows because the camp had been set close to al-T2if. They therefore withdrew to the place where today stands his mosque, built by Thaqif after their acceptance of Islam. It was CAmr h. Umayya b. Wahb who built it. It had in it a column from which, so they say, a cracking sound could be heard every morning when sunlight struck it. He besieged them there for some 20 days.
lessons: Tactical adjustments in difficult circumstances are a sign of wisdom, not weakness — knowing when to reposition preserves lives and mission.
Suffering losses in pursuit of a righteous goal does not indicate abandonment by God; perseverance through hardship is itself an act of worship.
The physical traces of righteous deeds — like the mosque built after acceptance of Islam — serve as lasting reminders of patience and eventual victory.
practical_advice: When a situation is causing repeated harm, reassess your approach and reposition rather than rigidly continuing on the same path.
In times of loss, anchor yourself in the long-term purpose of your efforts rather than being overwhelmed by immediate setbacks.
Reflect on how past difficulties led to positive outcomes in your life to build confidence that current hardships may yield good results as well.

title: The Siege of al-Ta'if: Battle from Behind Fortifications and Cutting of Vines
summary: The Muslims besieged the fortress of al-Ta'if for over ten days, engaging in fierce battle while Thaqif fought back from behind their walls; the Prophet ordered grape vines cut down to pressure the defenders, who urged restraint.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went on to al-Tn'if, leaving the captives (from Hunayn) at al-Ji'rma, where they filled the tents of Mecca. The Messenger of God (SAAS) encamped at al-Akama, near the fortress of al-TS1if, for some ten or more days, engaging them in battle while they fought back from behind their fortifications. The only man to emerge was Abii Bakra h. Masriih, Ziynd's brother from his mother's side. The Messenger of God (SAAS) set him free. Many casualties were suffered there and they (the Muslims) cut down some of their grape vines to enrage them thereby. At this Thaqif asked, 'Don't ruin our properties; they will either remain ours or become yours.' Wrwa stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered each Muslim to cut down five date-palms or five vines. He sent out a crier to announce, 'Anyone who comes forth to us will be free.' A number of people did join him, including Abn Bakra b. Masrah, a brother of Ziyad b. Abii Sufyw on his mother's side. He then set them free and assigned each one to a Muslim who was to provide food and a mount.
lessons: Applying measured pressure is sometimes necessary to break through entrenched resistance, but wisdom lies in knowing the limits of such measures.
Even in conflict, the Prophet demonstrated mercy by immediately freeing those who came to him, showing that justice and compassion can coexist.
Patience during a prolonged standoff requires sustained resolve; the willingness to endure without quick resolution is itself a spiritual discipline.
practical_advice: When facing a stubborn obstacle in life, consider what legitimate forms of pressure or change you can apply without causing unnecessary destruction.
Extend goodwill and a clear offer of help to those around you who may be struggling — like the announcement of freedom, open invitations can change outcomes.
When a difficult situation drags on longer than expected, focus on what small, consistent actions you can take each day rather than being paralyzed by the larger challenge.

title: The Siege of al-Ta'if: Catapults, the Testudo, and Fierce Combat
summary: The Prophet used catapults and a testudo formation against the fortifications of al-Ta'if; the defenders cast heated nails on the Muslims sheltering beneath the testudo, killing some, after which the Prophet ordered the vines cut down.
story: Ibn Hishim stated that he also shot at them using catapults. A trustworthy source related to me that the Prophet (SAAS) was the first in Islam to fire a catapult, using them against the people of al-Tn'if. Ibn Ishaq stated that a number of the Muslims went beneath a testudo and moved it slowly forward to burn the walls of al-T2if and heated nails were cast down upon them. When they emerged from beneath the testudo, Thaqif shot arrows down at them, killing some men. At that point the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered that the Thaqif vines be cut down and the Muslims set about this task. Aba Sufyiin h. Harb and al-Mughira b.
