Shu'ba then advanced and called for safe passage to talk with them. This was granted and they invited some women of Quraysh and Bano Kinana to come out to them; they feared that they might be treated as captives if the fortress were conquered. The women refused and Aba al-Aswad b. Masciid asked the two men, 'Can't I make a better suggestion for you than that you put forward? You well know the whereabouts of the estate of Aba al-Aswad. The Messenger of God (SAAS) has been encamped at a valley called al-'Aqiq, between the lands of Banti al-Aswad and al-TZ'if. There is no property in al-T2if more distant from irrigation water nor more difficult to supply or more inaccessible to cultivate than it. If Muhammad cuts it down it wiU never produce again to him; so suggest that he either take it for himself or leave it alone for the sake of God and our kinship ties.' They claim that the Messenger of God (SAAS) did leave it alone for them. It was his view that it was Salmnn al-F*si who suggested using the catapult and that he himself operated it.
lessons: Ingenuity and the use of available means — including new tools and strategies — are legitimate expressions of effort when pursuing a just cause.
Even in the midst of fierce conflict, the Prophet demonstrated wisdom by sparing a property when presented with a reasonable plea, showing that mercy can coexist with strength.
When facing an entrenched obstacle that resists every effort, persistence combined with creative problem-solving is often the path forward.
practical_advice: When you feel stuck in a difficult situation, ask whether you are using all the tools and resources legitimately available to you, or whether you have limited yourself unnecessarily.
Practice the discipline of pausing, even in the middle of a stressful confrontation, to hear out a reasonable appeal — it may lead to a better outcome than continued escalation.
Identify one area of your life where creative thinking could open a new path, and commit time this week to brainstorming approaches you have not tried before.

title: The Mukhannath Hit and the Siege of Al-Ta'if: Banning Access to the Prophet's Household
summary: During the siege of al-Ta'if, the Prophet discovered that a mukhannath named Hit (or Mati') was describing the physical attributes of women to others, revealing awareness of women's appearances, and consequently banned him from accessing the households of the Prophet's wives.
story: (SAAS) said, 'I would not have this man know what is here. Don't have such people in your company."' This implies that if he were someone who understood that, then he would be included (for consideration) with those referred to in God Almighty's words, "or the children who are not conscious of the private parts of women" (siirat al-Niir; XXIV, 3 1). By the word al-mukhannath in the usage of the early authorities is meant those devoid of (sexual) interest in women; the word does not refer to homosexuals. For had he been like that, his execution would certainly have been necessary, as the hadith tradition indicates, and as Aba Bakr, 'the Trusting', may God be pleased with him, himself decreed execution. The meaning of the phrase given above, "she exposes four folds of flesh when approaching and eight when she turns her back" is a reference to the folds of flesh on her stomach; these would appear as four folds as she approached and each of these would become two as she moved away. This woman was named Badiyya, daughter of Ghaylm b. Salama, and she was a lady of Thaqif. Al-Bukhnri quoted Ibn Jurayj as giving the name of this mukhannath as Hit. This is widely accepted as fact. However, Ymus quoted Ibn Ishnq as stating, "With the Messenger of God (SAAS) there was a freed-man of his aunt Bint 'Amr b. 'Ayid, a mukhannath named Mati'. He would go into the presence of the wives of the Messenger of God (SAAS) in his home; he was not considered as having the normal desire for women that men have. Then he (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) overheard him saying to Khdid b. al-Walid, 'Khaid, if the Messenger of God (SAAS) should conquer al-Tn'if, then be sure that Badiyya, daughter of Ghaylm, does not slip away from you. She exposes four folds of flesh when she approaches you and eight when she turns her back.' When he heard this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'I would not have thought this man would notice such as that.' He then told his women, 'Let him not have access to you.' He was then banned from the house of the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Protecting boundaries around one's family and household is a prophetic practice rooted in wisdom and discernment rather than harshness.
Trust must be continuously evaluated based on observed behavior, not simply assumed based on initial appearances or long familiarity.
Leaders must act decisively to safeguard those under their care when trust is violated, even in the midst of larger challenges.
practical_advice: Periodically reflect on who has access to your private life and personal affairs, and whether that access remains appropriate based on observed conduct.
When you discover that someone has been paying attention to matters you considered private, take calm but firm action to re-establish appropriate boundaries.
In times of stress and difficulty, do not neglect the safety and dignity of your family; protective decisions made during hardship are just as valid as those made in calm times.

title: The Siege of Al-Ta'if: The Prophet's Vision and Decision to Depart Without Victory
summary: After besieging al-Ta'if for approximately thirty days without conquering it, the Prophet received a vision interpreted as a sign that victory would not come that day, consulted his companions and advisors, and made the difficult decision to withdraw, praying for the guidance of Thaqif rather than their destruction.
story: Al-Bukhari stated that it was related to him by 'Ali b. CAbd Allnh, quoting Sufynn, from 'Amr, from Aba alLCAbbas, the blind poet, from 'Ahd All& b. 'Amr, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) besieged al-Ta'if, but did not overcome them, he said, 'We'll be leaving tomorrow, if God wills it.' This disappointed them and they asked, 'Are we to leave without victory?' He replied, 'Then recommence the fighting.' Next day they did so but suffered casualties. He then said, 'We'll be leaving tomorrow, if God wills it.' This pleased them and the Messenger of God (SAAS) laughed." Sufyan, the sub-narrator, once used the wording, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) smiled." Muslim related this from a hadith of Sufyan b. Wyayna; he gave the line of transmission as being from 'Abd All& b. Wmar b. al-Khacpb, different from that given by al-Bukhzi. And (this line is given) in another similar version and in one from 'Abd All& b. CAmr h. al-'As. But God knows best. Al-Waqidi stated, "Kuthayyir b. Zayd b. al-Walid b. Rabah quotes Ahii Hurayra as saying, 'Fifteen days of the siege of al-T2if having elapsed, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sought the advice of Nawfal b. Mu'itwiya al-Dili, asking, "Nawfal, what is your view of maintaining it?" He replied, "Messenger of God, if you wait out a fox in its hole, you'll take it; and if you leave it (where it is) it will do you no harm."'" Muhammmad b. Ishaq stated, "I have been informed that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was engaged in the siege of Taqif, he asked Aba Bakr, 'Abii Bakr, I had a vision that I was presented with a bowl fdled with cream. A cock pecked at it and spilt its contents.' Abii Bakr, may God be pleased with him, replied, 'I do not think you will achieve with them today what you wish.' 'I don't think so either,' the Messenger of God (SAAS) said. "Khawla al-Sulamiyya, daughter of Hakim, then said, 'Messenger of God, please give to me, if God gives you victory, the jewellery of Badiyya, daughter of Ghaylm b. Salama, or that of al-Fwi'a, daughter of Wqayl'; these were known as the Thaqif women with the most jewels. It is said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'What, Khawla, if I am not awarded control over Thaqif?' "Khawla then left and reported to Wmar b. al-Khat~b what he had said and the latter went in to see the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked, 'Messenger of God, Khawla has reported to me a conversation she claims to have had with you.' 'I might have said it,' he replied. Wmar asked, 'Then have you not been awarded control over them?' 'No,' he answered. Wmar asked, 'Then should I not announce our departure?' 'Yes, do,' he told him. "Wmar then announced their departure. As the army moved off, Sacid b. Wbayd b. 'Asid b. Aba 'Amr h. 'Alhj commented, 'Well, their mhe is standing fast.' Wyayna h. Husn responded, 'Yes, I swear by God, nobly and valiantly.' One of the Muslims then addressed the latter, 'Wyayna, God Damn you, would you praise the polytheists for withstanding the Messenger of God (SAAS), when you came here to assist him?' Wyayna replied, 'I did not come with you to fight Thaqif; I wanted the Messenger of God (SAAS) to conquer al-Ta'if so that I could acquire a Thaqif woman with whom to have intercourse so she would bear me a son. We hold Thaqif in high regard.'" Ibn Lahica narrated, from Aha al-Aswad, from Wrwa, the story of Khawla, daughter of Hakim, and what the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said and how he had told Wmar to announce the departure. He went on, "And the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered the men that they not disband their force. Next morning the Messenger of God (SAAS) set forth with his men and as he mounted to leave, he said, '0 God, guide them and spare us the trouble of them!'" Al-Tirmidhi narrated, from a hadith of 'Abd All& h. 'Uthmm h. Khuthaym, from Aha al-Zuhayr, from Jabir, that they (the Muslim forces) said, "0 Messenger of God, the Thaqif arrows greatly harmed us; pray to God against them." He responded, "0 God, give guidance to Thaqif." He (al-Tirmidhi) then commented, "This hadith is hasan gharib." Yanus quoted Ibn Ishaq as saying that it was related to him by 'Abd Allsh b. Aba Bakr and 'Abd All& b. al-Mukarram, quoting well informed scholars as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) besieged the people of al-Ta'if for some 30 days and then left, not having defeated them. After he had returned to Medina, a delegation of them came to him, in Ramadm, and accepted Islam."
lessons: Accepting that a goal is not attainable at a given moment is not failure but wisdom; the Prophet's departure from al-Ta'if without victory eventually led to its peaceful acceptance of Islam.
Praying for the guidance of one's adversaries rather than their punishment reflects the highest standard of character and long-term thinking.
Consulting trusted advisors during difficult decisions strengthens leadership and distributes the weight of responsibility wisely.
practical_advice: When you have worked hard toward a goal and results are not forthcoming, take time to honestly assess whether to persist or strategically withdraw and try again later.
When someone has wronged you or opposed you, try replacing prayers for revenge with prayers for their guidance; this frees you emotionally and leaves the outcome in God's hands.
Seek counsel from people of wisdom and experience when facing decisions under pressure; their perspective may clarify what your own stress obscures.

title: The Martyrs of Al-Ta'if: Those Who Gave Their Lives During the Siege
summary: Following the siege of al-Ta'if, a list of twelve Muslim martyrs is recorded, including companions from Quraysh and the Ansar, among them Abdullah ibn Abi Bakr who was struck by an arrow and died later in Madinah.
story: The following is the list of names of those Muslims who were, according to Ibn Ishaq, martyred at al-Ta'if. From Quraysh: Sa'id h. Sa'id h. al-.X$ b. Umayya; Wrfuya h. Janah, ally of Banii Umayyya b. al-Asad h. al-Ghawth, 'Abd All& b. Aha Bakr al-Siddiq, who was struck by an arrow there and who died of his wound in Medina, after the death of the Messenger of God (SAAS); 'Ahd All& b. Aha Umayya b. al-Mughira al-Makhziimi, struck by an arrow that day; 'Ahd All& h. ' h i r h. Rahi'a, an ally of Bana 'Adi; al-Sa'ib b. al-Hzrith b. Qays b. 'Adi al-Sahmi, along with his brother 'Ahd All* Julayha b. 'Ahd All&, of Bana S a g h. Layth. From the ansay: from Khazraj: Thabit b. al-Jadha' al-Aslami; al-Harith b. Sahl h. Aba Sa$a'a al-Maini; al-Mundhir b. 'Abd Allah, of Bann SaSda. From al-Aws: Ruqaym b. Thahit h. Tha'laba h. Zayd h. Lawdhm h. Mu'awiya. Those martyred that day totalled twelve - seven men of Quraysh and four of the anjar. Also killed was one man of Banii Layth. May God be pleased with them all.
lessons: The sacrifice of those who gave their lives in service of a noble cause deserves to be remembered with honor and gratitude, not forgotten in the rush toward outcomes.
Martyrdom in the Islamic tradition is understood as an honorable end; grief for the fallen is balanced by faith that their souls are in a place of honor with God.

Even in campaigns that did not achieve their immediate military objective, great souls demonstrated commitment and paid the ultimate price, reminding us that effort and sincerity matter regardless of worldly outcome.
practical_advice: Take time to remember and honor those in your own life who made sacrifices so that you could be where you are today; gratitude for their contribution strengthens your sense of purpose.
When you experience loss alongside an unachieved goal, allow yourself to grieve the loss separately from evaluating the goal; both deserve their own space.
Reflect on the names and stories of those who sacrificed in Islamic history as a source of motivation and perspective when your own difficulties feel overwhelming.

title: Bujayr ibn Zuhayr's Poem Commemorating the Battles of Hunayn and Al-Ta'if
summary: After the Prophet's departure from al-Ta'if, the poet Bujayr ibn Zuhayr ibn Abi Sulma composed verses memorializing the battles of Hunayn and al-Ta'if, praising the discipline and might of the Muslim forces.
story: Ibn Ishnq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) left on his way back from al-Ta'if, Buiayr h. Zuhayr b. Aba Salmz spoke the following verses concerning the battles at Hunayn and al-Ta'if: 'It was a replay of the battles of the Hunayn valley, Aw@s and al-Abraq; Hawszin foolishly assembled their forces but broke up like birds scattered. They did not retain a single position against us, except for their walls and the base of their ditch. We paraded before them to bring them out, but they fortified against us behind a closed gate. Our men without armour withdrew to a great force, a squadron whose armour shone with death. Close-ranked, and green-hued; if sent charging against a fortress, it would be as though never constructed. They walk like hounds on tree-shed thorns, like horses led separately then brought together again, Each wearing armour that once firmly set looks like a pool shimmering under a breeze, Over coats of armour, the hems of which brushed our shoes, finely woven by Ds'ad and Muhaniq's family.'"
lessons: Poetry and art serve as a vital means of processing collective experiences of hardship, loss, and effort, giving form to emotions that would otherwise remain unspoken.
Commemorating the courage and discipline of those who strove together strengthens communal bonds and preserves collective memory for future generations.
Acknowledging what was not achieved alongside what was accomplished gives a more honest and complete picture of any significant event.
practical_advice: Consider using creative expression — writing, poetry, journaling, or art — as a way to process difficult experiences or significant life events rather than suppressing those feelings.
When you go through a hard experience with others, find ways to acknowledge the effort of everyone involved, not just the outcome, as this honors the full human story.
Reflect on moments of communal struggle in your own community or family history; understanding those stories can provide strength and context for your own challenges today.

title: Sakhr al-Ahmasi Holds the Fortress Until Thaqif Submits, Then Is Corrected by the Prophet
summary: After the Prophet left al-Ta'if, a companion named Sakhr made a personal vow to remain until Thaqif submitted, eventually escorting them to the Prophet; however, the Prophet corrected him twice for wrongfully holding a Muslim woman and a water supply that belonged to others.
story: Aba Dx'od stated that it was related to him by Wmar b. al-Khaffzb Aba H a f ~ , quoting al-Firyxhi, quoting Abm, quoting 'Amr - he being Ihn 'Ahd Allah h. Aba H%im - quoting Wthmm b. Aha H a i m , from his father, from his grandfather Sakhr -he being Aba al-'Ayla &&mas1 - who said that when he, Sakhr, heard that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was attacking Thaqif, he set off with his cavalry to assist him. When he arrived at al-Tz'if, however, he found him already gone. He then made a vow that he would not leave the fortress before they emerged to accept the authority of the Messenger of God (SAAS). And he did not leave them until they had done so. Sakhr then wrote to him as follows, "Messenger of God, Thaqif have now accepted your authority. I am accompanying them to you, along with my cavalry." (The account continues) "The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered that there be a general assembly for prayer and he spoke ten prayers for Banii al-Ahmas, saying, '0 God, bless d-Ahmas for both their cavalry and their foot soldiers.' "When they arrived al-Mughira b. Shu'ba spoke, '0 Messenger of God, Sakhr took my aunt and she adopted that which Muslims embrace.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then called for Sakhr and said, 'Sakhr, when people accept Islam, their blood and possessions are spared; return al-Mughira's aunt to h i . ' And he did return her. "He then asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) for that water supply belonging to Bana Salim; they had fled from Islam and abandoned that water. He asked, 'Messenger of God, settle me and my people there.' 'Yes,' he agreed and he agreed to settle h i and Aslam - the tribesmen of Aslam, that is - there. They then went to Sakhr and asked him to give the water supply to themselves, but he refused. And so they went again to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, 'Messenger of God, we have accepted Islam and went to Sakhr to have h i give us our water supply, hut he refused. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Sakhr, when people accept Islam their blood and possessions are spared. Deliver their water supply over to them.' He replied, 'Yes, 0 Prophet of God.' "I then saw the face of the Messenger of God (SAAS) turn red with embarrassment at his (Sakhr's) having taken the women and also the water!"
lessons: Zeal and devotion in service of a noble cause do not exempt a person from accountability; the Prophet corrected Sakhr's wrongdoing even while honoring his sacrifice.
Justice applies equally to all, including those who have rendered great service; holding someone's possessions or person unlawfully cannot be justified by good intentions.
The Prophet's visible discomfort at injustice — even minor injustice — shows that moral sensitivity is a sign of prophetic character, not weakness.
practical_advice: Examine whether any of your current actions, even those motivated by good intentions or past sacrifices, may be causing harm or injustice to others, and be willing to correct course.
When someone in authority points out a wrong you have committed, respond with humility and prompt correction rather than defensiveness, as Sakhr did when he complied immediately.
If you feel embarrassed after being corrected publicly, use that discomfort as a motivation for growth rather than resentment; embarrassment in service of justice is honorable.

title: Sakhr Returns the Water Supply After Accepting Islam
summary: After a man from Thaqif named Sakhr accepted Islam but refused to return a water supply to women of his tribe who had also accepted Islam, the Prophet intervened and instructed him to return it, visibly embarrassed by Sakhr's behavior.
story: they went again to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, 'Messenger of God, we have accepted Islam and went to Sakhr to have hi give us our water supply, hut he refused. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Sakhr, when people accept Islam their blood and possessions are spared. Deliver their water supply over to them.' He replied, 'Yes, 0 Prophet of God.' "I then saw the face of the Messenger of God (SAAS) turn red with embarrassment at his (Sakhr's) having taken the women and also the water!"
lessons: Justice in Islam extends to all people equally, regardless of tribal or personal loyalties.
Acceptance of Islam carries real obligations—rights must be honoured and oppression must cease.
A leader's visible discomfort at injustice is itself a moral signal to those around him.
practical_advice: When you witness an injustice being corrected, reflect on whether you yourself are withholding something rightfully belonging to others.
Use moments of embarrassment as prompts for self-examination rather than defensiveness.
If someone wrongs you, seek legitimate channels of redress rather than suffering in silence.

title: The Prophet's Prayer for Thaqif at Qarn al-Tha'alib
summary: After being rejected and mocked by the people of Ta'if, the Prophet was offered the chance to have the mountains levelled upon them by the angel of mountains, but he refused and prayed instead for their future guidance.
story: It has been related above how, following the death of his uncle, Aha Talib, the Messenger of God (SAAS) had gone earlier to al-Ta'if. He had invited them to come to God Almighty and to provide hi sanctuary, so he could go on conveying the message of His Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He, but they had rejected him and called him a liar. He had then departed for home in a state of depression from which he had only emerged at Qm al-Tha'dib. There he saw a rain cloud in which was Gabriel. The angel of the mountains had then called out to him, saying, "Muhammad, your Lord greets you. He has heard how your people have spoken to you and have rejected you. Would you like me to level the Akhshahayn mountains upon them?" The Messenger of God (SAAS) had replied, "No; give them respite so that God might bring forth from their loins those who will worship Him alone, associating no other with Him." His statement, "No; give them respite ..." was consistent with his not conquering their fortress so that they not be annihilated, and that the conquest should be delayed to allow them to come forth as Muslims thereafter, in Ramadan of the following year.
lessons: True mercy means holding hope for the redemption of even those who harm you.
Long-term vision and patience can transform enemies into allies and persecutors into believers.
Divine power is always available, but the spiritually mature person chooses compassion over retaliation.
practical_advice: When someone deeply wounds you, pause before reacting—consider whether your response will close off the possibility of their future growth.
Practice making du'a for those who have hurt you; it redirects anger into something constructive.
Remind yourself in moments of rejection that outcomes are not always in your timeframe; trust in a longer plan.

title: The Delegation of Hawazin Comes to al-Ji'rana Seeking Their Captives
summary: A delegation from the Hawazin tribe, having now accepted Islam, came to the Prophet at al-Ji'rana to plead for the return of 6,000 women and children taken captive after the Battle of Hunayn, appealing through poetry and shared history.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS), following his departure from al-Talif, went through D&na and made camp at al-Ji'rma, accompanied by his men and by many Hawain captives. One of his men bad asked him, as he was leaving Thaqif, "0 Messenger of God, speak a prayer against them," hut he had prayed, "0 God, guide Thaqif and bring them." (Ihn Ishaq continued) "At al-Ji'rma a delegation of Hawain came to him. At that time he held captive 6,000 women and children and innumerable camels and sheep." He went on, "'Amr h. Shu'ayb related to me (and there is a hadith of Yanus b. Bukayr also from 'Amr) quoting his father, from his grandfather, who said, 'We were with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Hunayn and after he had taken captives and possessions from Hawxzin, a delegation came to him at al-Jicrma from them; they had now accepted Islam. They addressed him as follows, "0 Messenger of God, we are one people, one tribe. You well know what disaster has befallen us. Be k i d to us and may God be kind to you."'"
lessons: Humility and sincere appeal can open doors that force cannot.
Islam's embrace of former enemies demonstrates that transformation is always possible.
A community's shared humanity and bonds of kinship deserve acknowledgment even in the aftermath of conflict.
practical_advice: When approaching someone you have wronged or who has power over you, speak honestly about your hardship rather than making demands.
Acknowledge shared history as a bridge to reconciliation.
When you find yourself in a position of power over others, let mercy guide your decisions more than precedent or legal entitlement.

title: Zuhayr ibn Surad's Poetic Plea for the Hawazin Captives
summary: The Hawazin spokesman Zuhayr ibn Surad recited moving verses before the Prophet, reminding him of the women who had nursed him and appealing to his noble character to secure the release of their captive women and children.
story: Their spokesman, Zuhayr b. Surad Ahn Surad, then arose and said, "Messenger of God, those in the enclosures for the captives are nothing hut your own maternal aunts and nursemaids who cared for you. If we had suckled Ihn Aba Shamir or d-Nucman b. al-Mundhir and then had suffered from them what we have from you, we would have at least hoped for some special consideration and compassion from them. And you are the Messenger of God, the most trustworthy of men." He then recited the following verses, "Be kind to us, Messenger of God, in your nobility, for you are the man from whom we hope and expect this. Be kind to womenfolk ruined by fate, their community destroyed and ruined. Fate has imposed upon us circumstances of great sorrow, the hearts of our people overwhelmed with misery and pain. If there does not reach them comforts that you deliver, 0 most wise of men when tested, Be kind to women from whom you suckled, your mouth filled with pearl-like drops of their pure milk. Be kind to women from whom yon suckled, for then your actions will adorn you and not be wasted. Do not render us like those whose joy is gone for ever; let us survive, for we are a noble people. We will surely express thanks for favours for, if given us, we will remember them forever hereafter."
lessons: Sincere and eloquent speech, grounded in truth and shared bonds, can move even the hardest of situations toward mercy.
Gratitude and the acknowledgment of past kindness are powerful tools for reconciliation.
Every person, regardless of their status, deserves to be heard when they are pleading for justice.
practical_advice: When advocating for others who cannot speak for themselves, anchor your appeal in shared humanity and genuine relationship.
Express gratitude explicitly and early when seeking help—it opens hearts.
Use creative expression such as writing or poetry to process and communicate grief that ordinary language struggles to convey.

title: The Prophet Returns Captives to Hawazin: Choosing Honour Over Wealth
summary: When the Hawazin were asked to choose between the return of their captives or their possessions, they chose their people, and the Prophet generously agreed and persuaded his companions to release all prisoners voluntarily.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, "What matters most to you - your women and children or your possessions?" "What?" they replied. "Would you have us choose between our honour and our goods? Of course, our women and children matter most to us." The Messenger of God (SAAS) went on, "As for what was mine or belonged to Banii 'Ahd d-Mu aalib, that is yours. After I have prayed with the people, say, 'We ask the Messenger of God to intercede with the Muslims; and we ask them to intercede with him for us for our women and children.' When you ask this, I will grant your wish and make a request on your behalf." When the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the noon prayer, they arose and they spoke as he had asked. He then said, "As for what was mine or belonged to Bann 'Abd al-Muytalih, that is now yours." The muhdjiriin responded, "Whatever (rights) we had now go to the Messenger of God." The an@r said the same. However, al-Aqrac b. Habis said, "For myself and Bana Tamim, no!" Wyayna stated, "For myself and Bana F a z w , no!" AlLCAbb%b. Mird% al-Sulami said, "For myself and Bana Sulaym, no!" But Banii Sulaym disagreed, saying, "What was ours is now that of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." 'Ahb% h. Mirdas then asked Bann Sulaym, "Would you disgrace me?" The Messenger of God (SAAS) then stated, "Whoever withholds his right shall have six camels due from the fust booty that we obtain. Now return their women and children to these people."
lessons: Human dignity and family bonds are more precious than material possessions—a value Islam upholds unequivocally.
Leadership means leading by personal example in generosity before calling others to follow.
Differences of opinion in a community do not have to derail a just outcome when a wise leader mediates.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult choice, ask yourself which option preserves human dignity and relationships rather than merely maximising material gain.
If you are in a position of leadership, model the generosity you hope to inspire in others before asking anything of them.
If members of your community are reluctant to be generous, find creative and fair ways to honour both their rights and the greater good.

title: The Prophet's Cloak Is Pulled Off by the Crowd Demanding Their Share of Booty
summary: After the Battle of Hunayn, the crowd pressed so insistently around the Prophet demanding their share of the spoils that a tree pulled off his cloak, prompting him to speak firmly about honesty, generosity, and the sin of stealing from the booty.
story: He then mounted and the people followed after hi, saying, "0 Messenger of God, divide up for us our share of the booty!" They forced him over close to a tree which pulled off his cloak. He asked them, "Please return my cloak to me. For I swear by H i who has my soul in His hand, if the camels I had for you numbered as many as aU the trees of Tihzma, I would divide them up among you. Moreover, you have never found me miserly, cowardly or untruthful!" He then went over to a camel and took from its hump a piece of its fur which he held up in two fingers, saying, "People, I swear by God, I have nothing from your booty - not even this hair - except for one-fifth of it. And that too is given back to you. 'A needle for a needle'." For theft of booty means sin, and hell-fire and disgrace on Resurrection Day for those who take it. One of the an@? then came carrying a strand of hair rope and said, "Messenger of God, I took this to sew up a saddle-cloth for a camel of mine with ulcers." The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I award to you my rights in it. Since word of this might spread, I don't need it." And he threw it from his hand.
lessons: Integrity means being scrupulous about what is and is not yours, even in small things.
A leader who demonstrates personal austerity and transparency earns lasting moral authority.
Crowds in a state of excitement can lose their moral compass; firm and calm words from a trusted leader can restore it.
practical_advice: When you feel the crowd around you pulling in one direction, pause and check whether that direction aligns with your values.
Practice being transparent about what belongs to you and what does not, especially in group settings where lines can blur.
If you have inadvertently taken something that was not yours, return it promptly and without rationalisation.

title: The Hawazin Delegation Chooses Captives Over Possessions at al-Ji'rana: The Bukhari Account
summary: According to the Sahih al-Bukhari account, the Prophet waited ten days after returning from Ta'if before distributing the Hawazin spoils, then gave the delegation a clear choice between captives and possessions, leading all the Muslims to voluntarily release the prisoners.
story: In the sahib collection of d-Bukhm there is a hadith from al-Layth, from Wqayl, from d-Zuhri, from Wrwa, from al-Miswar b. Makhrama and Marww b. al-Hakam. It states that when the delegation of Hawain came to hi, having accepted Islam, and asked to have their property and women returned, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told them, "There are with me those you see. The talk that is most favoured by me is what is most true. Choose one of two options - either the captives or their possessions. I have been waiting for you." (The narration continued) "He had delayed the distribution for ten days following his return from al-Ta'if. When it was clear to them that he would return to them only one of the two categories, they replied, 'We choose those of us who are captives.' He then arose among the Muslims and, having given appropriate praise to God, said, 'These brothers of yours have come in repentance and I have decided to return those of them taken captive. Whoever of you wishes to do that as a favour should do so. Those of you who would prefer to receive this (full) share, we will give it with the first booty God awards us.' The Muslims responded, 'We are pleased to return them as a favour, 0 Messenger of God.' He then told them, 'We do not know which of you have agreed and which have not. So go back and let your leaders inform us.' They did return and their leaders talked with them and came to him and informed hi that they (all) had agreed and had given their assent (to the release of the prisoners)."
lessons: Transparency and clear communication, even when the truth is hard, build lasting trust.
Patience in delaying a decision can allow wiser outcomes to emerge than hasty action would permit.
When a community acts collectively in generosity, the moral weight of that act is shared and multiplied.
practical_advice: When facing a complex decision involving others' rights, give yourself a defined waiting period before acting rather than rushing.
When asking a community to be generous, give them a real choice rather than pressure, and respect the process of genuine consent.
Calling former adversaries 'brothers' after their repentance is a powerful act of reconciliation—practise reframing former enemies as potential allies.

title: 'Abd Allah ibn 'Umar Finds His Slave-Girl Returned to Hawazin
summary: Ibn 'Umar had received a slave-girl from the Hawazin spoils, sent her to his relatives to prepare her for him, but when he returned he found she had been released back to her people along with all the other Hawazin captives.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated that he was informed by Aha Wajra, Yazid b. Wbayd al-Sa'di, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave to 'Ali h. Aha Talib a slave girl named Rap, daughter of Hild b. Hay% b. Wmayya, and to 'Uthmm b. 'Aff%n he gave a slave-girl called Zaynab, daughter of Hay& h. 'Amr b. Hay%. He also gave a slave girl to Wmar, hut he gave her to his son, 'Ahd Allah. Ibn Ishaq went on to state that Nafi' quoted Wmar's son 'Abd Alhh as having said, "I sent her off to my aunts of Banii Jumah to prepare her properly for me while I circumambulated the ka'ba and arranged to come and take her on my return from the mosque. When I had finished at the mosque, I went there and found everyone in turmoil. I asked what was going on and they replied that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had returned their wives and children (to Hawazin). I then said, 'Your woman friend is with Bana Jumah'"
lessons: The Prophet's decisions, even when unexpected, took precedence over personal arrangements—a lesson in submission to higher authority.
Events can overturn our plans entirely; adaptability and acceptance are essential qualities of faith.
Material possessions and arrangements are always secondary to the demands of justice and mercy.
practical_advice: When your plans are overturned by forces beyond your control, focus on understanding the larger purpose rather than dwelling on personal loss.
Practice holding your possessions and arrangements loosely, knowing that circumstances can change.
When confused by a sudden change, seek information calmly before reacting.

title: Return of Hawazin Captives and the Incident of Ibn Umar's Marriage
summary: After the Battle of Hunayn, the Prophet returned the Hawazin captives at a rate of six camels per person. Ibn Umar recounts how he had arranged to marry a Hawazin captive but found everyone in turmoil when the Prophet ordered their return.
story: 'Ahd Allah. Ibn Ishaq went on to state that Nafi' quoted Wmar's son 'Abd Alhh as having said, "I sent her off to my aunts of Banii Jumah to prepare her properly for me while I circumambulated the ka'ba and arranged to come and take her on my return from the mosque. When I had finished at the mosque, I went there and found everyone in turmoil. I asked what was going on and they replied that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had returned their wives and children (to Hawazin). I then said, 'Your woman friend is with Bana Jumab; go and get her.' And they did so."
lessons: Acting with justice and mercy, even when it is costly, is a hallmark of true leadership.
The Prophet's decision to return captives demonstrated that human dignity takes precedence over material gain.
Unexpected changes in circumstances are part of life; responding with calm acceptance reflects strong faith.
practical_advice: When plans are disrupted unexpectedly, pause before reacting and seek to understand the larger wisdom at play.
Practice letting go of attachments to outcomes, trusting that what is just will ultimately prevail.
Reflect on moments when you prioritized compassion over personal benefit and draw strength from those memories.

title: Uyayna ibn Hisn Refuses to Release the Elderly Hawazin Woman
summary: Uyayna ibn Hisn refused to release an elderly Hawazin captive hoping for a large ransom, but was persuaded by Zuhayr ibn Surad to release her in exchange for six camels.
story: lhn Ishzq continued, "Wyayna b. Hisn took an elderly Hawain woman, saying as he did so, 'I can see an old lady who I thii is of high standing in her tribe; ransom for her may he substantial.' When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned the captives at a rate of six camels per person, Wyayna refused to give her back. Zuhayr b. Surad then told him, 'Let her go; I swear, her mouth is not cool, her breasts are not rounded, she has no chid in her womb, she has no upset husband, and her milk is not abundant. It's not as if you've taken a woman who is fair-skinned and a virgin, or even someone who is plump and mature!' And so Wyayna gave her back in exchange for six camels."
lessons: Greed blinds a person to the humanity of others; it takes counsel from others to restore perspective.
Compassion and justice must extend to the most vulnerable members of society, including the elderly.
Persuasion grounded in truth can overcome stubbornness and selfishness.
practical_advice: When you notice greed influencing your decisions, seek counsel from a trusted and honest friend.
Remind yourself of the humanity and dignity of every person, regardless of their perceived social or material value.
Practice generosity in small ways daily to weaken the grip of materialistic thinking.

title: Division of Spoils at al-Ji'rana and the Warrior Compensated by the Prophet
summary: At al-Ji'rana, the Prophet divided the spoils of Hunayn and personally compensated a warrior whose foot he had tapped with a whip, giving him 80 ewes as recompense.
story: Al-Waqidi stated that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) divided up the spoils at al-Ji'rma, each warrior received four camels and forty sheep. Salama quoted Muhammad b. IshZq, from 'Abd Allah b. Ahii Bakr to the effect that a man who took part in the Battle of Hunayn said, "I was riding along on my camel at the side of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and I was wearing heavy hoots. My camel humped against that of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and the toe of my hoot struck and hurt his leg. He then tapped my foot with his whip, saying, 'You hurt me! Go further away from me!' So I moved off. Next day the Messenger of God (SAAS) wanted to see me and I assumed this was because I had struck his leg the day before. So I went to him, feelmg apprehensive, but he said, 'You struck my leg yesterday and hurt me and I tapped your foot with my whip. I have called for you to give you compensation for that.' H e then awarded me 80 ewes for the blow he had struck me.'"
lessons: The Prophet's commitment to justice extended even to himself — he held himself accountable to the same standards he applied to others.
True leadership is marked by accountability and humility, not the exercise of unchecked authority.
Anxiety about facing consequences for mistakes can dissolve when those in authority act with fairness and grace.
practical_advice: When you make a mistake that affects someone else, take initiative to acknowledge it and make amends rather than waiting to be confronted.
Reflect on moments when someone treated you more fairly than you expected — let that inspire you to extend the same grace to others.
When you feel anxious about facing a difficult conversation, prepare yourself by recalling times accountability led to a positive outcome.

title: The Bedouin Press the Prophet for Distribution of Spoils at al-Ji'rana
summary: After returning the Hawazin captives, the Muslim soldiers pressed the Prophet so urgently for the division of spoils that a tree tore off his cloak, prompting him to reassure them of his generosity and trustworthiness.
story: This is why, when he mounted, that the bedouin crowded around him asking that the division of the spoils take place, forcing him over to a tree which tugged off his cloak, so that he said, "Please return my cloak to me. For I swear, by Him who has my soul in His hand, if I had as many camels as these thorny trees, I would distribute them among you. Yon know me not to be misecly, untruthful or cowardly." This is also related in similar words by al-Bukhai, from Jubayr b. Mut'im. Since the men feared that Hawain would have their possessions returned to them as their women and children had been, they asked him to divide it up at al-Ji'r~a, as God Almighty and Glorious is He, had ordered hi. He gave preference and priority to some persons and some of the tribal leaders in this distribution and some of the anfar complained to hi until he addressed them and explained the wisdom of his actions, in order to placate them.
lessons: A leader of integrity does not waver under pressure but calmly reasserts their values and trustworthiness.
Material anxiety can cause people to forget the character and track record of those who have consistently served them well.
Patience in distributing resources wisely is more beneficial than yielding to the loudest demands.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by others' demands or pressure, ground yourself by clearly and calmly restating your values and intentions.
Remind yourself of your own track record of honesty and reliability when others question your motives under stress.
Practice delayed gratification by trusting in a fair process rather than demanding immediate resolution of your concerns.

title: Anas ibn Malik Recounts the Battle of Hunayn and the Siege of Ta'if
summary: Anas ibn Malik describes how the Muslim army initially fled at Hunayn when the polytheists advanced in impressive formation, but rallied at the Prophet's call and ultimately achieved victory, followed by a 40-day siege of Ta'if.
story: ImZmA b d stated, "'Arim related to us, quoting Mu'tarnir b. Sulaymzn, quoting his father, quoting al-Sumayc alSadosi, to the effect that Anas b. Malik said, 'Having conquered Mecca, we sent an expedition to Huuayn. The polytheists advanced in the most impressive lines I had ever seen. F i s t came the lines of cavalry, then the fighters, then the women and children, then the sheep and goats, then the camels. We were a large force, some 6,000 strong, and Khalid b. al-Walid was in command of our vanguard of cavalry. Our cavalry began to take shelter behind us but they left themselves open. It was not long before the bedouin forces along with others we know fled. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then called out, '0 muhdjit?in! O an~ar! Where are you now?'" Anas explained that this was according to an account ofhis aunt. He then went on, "We said, Labbayka! 'At your service, Messenger of God!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then advanced and by the time we came to them God had already defeated them. "We seized their property and went on to al-Ta'if which we besieged for 40 nights before returning to Mecca. There we stayed, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was awarding some men 100 (camels) while to another he would give 200."
lessons: Fear in the face of an overwhelming enemy is a natural human response; what matters is choosing to rally when called.
Victory is not always immediate — it may come after retreat, regrouping, and renewed commitment.
Steadfastness in following righteous leadership, even when conditions seem dire, leads to eventual success.
practical_advice: When fear causes you to retreat from a challenge, give yourself permission to regroup rather than treating it as failure — then return with renewed resolve.
Identify the voices in your life that call you back to your values when you are tempted to flee from difficulty.
Reflect on past moments when persevering through fear led to an unexpected breakthrough, and let that memory strengthen you.

title: The Ansar's Grievance Over Distribution of Spoils After Hunayn
summary: After Hunayn, the Ansar felt overlooked when the Prophet gave large shares of the spoils to Quraysh and other leaders but nothing to them, leading to expressions of resentment among their younger members.
story: Al-Bukhai stated that it was related to him by 'Abd Allah h. Muhammad, quoting Hish%m, quoting Ma'mar, from al-Znhri, who said that Anas b. Malik stated to him, "Some of the a q a r began talking when God gave to His messenger the spoils from Hawain and he began awarding some men 100 camels. They were saying, 'May God forgive the Messenger of God (SAAS) for giving to Quraysh but not us, even though our swords drip with their blood!'" Anas b. Malik went on, "What they were saying was reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he sent for the a q d r whom he gathered in a leather pavilion, allowing entry to no one else at the time. When they were assembled, the Prophet (SAAS) arose and said, 'What is this talk that has reached me?' The an@r elders replied, 'Our leaders, Messenger of God, have said nothing. Some of our young men, however, have been saying, "God forgive the Messenger of God! He is giving to Quraysh but not to us, even though our swords drip with their blood."' The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'I am giving to these men who were so recently polytheists in order to establish good will with them. Are you not content they go off with material things, while you tahe the Prophet to your homes? I swear by God, what you take home is far better than what they do.' They responded, 'We are content, Messenger of God.' He then told them, 'You will find others much advantaged. But be patient until you meet God and His messenger; for I will he there at the al-hawd the pool.'" Anas concluded, "But they were not patient."
lessons: Feelings of being overlooked are natural, but wisdom lies in understanding the larger strategy behind decisions that seem unfair.
True reward is not always material — the honor of a relationship with a person of great virtue surpasses worldly gains.
Leaders who address grievances directly and honestly, rather than ignoring them, build lasting trust and cohesion.
practical_advice: When you feel unrecognized for your contributions, seek a direct and respectful conversation with the relevant person rather than letting resentment build.
Distinguish between what you have received materially and what you have received in terms of trust, relationship, and spiritual growth.
Practice reframing feelings of rejection by asking: what non-material gifts have I been given that others have not?

title: The Prophet Addresses the Ansar's Complaint in His Pavilion
summary: The Prophet privately gathered the Ansar to address their resentment over the distribution of spoils, reminding them that their bond with him was worth more than any worldly wealth, and declaring he would always walk the path of the Ansar.
story: Al-Bukhm went on to relate, as did Muslim, from a hadith of Ibn 'Awf, quoting Hishxm h. Zayd, from his grandfather Anas b. Malik, who said, "At the battle of Hunayn, Hawivzin attacked. With the Prophet (SAAS) there were 10,000 warriors, along with some Meccans who had joined them. They (the Muslims) drew back, and so he called out, 'An:rtr!' And they responded, 'Labbayka, y a rasiil Allrthi, ma sacdayk! Labbayka! Nahnu b~ynayadayk!' 'At your service, Messenger of God, and at your assistance! We are here before you!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then dismounted and said, 'I am the servant and Messenger of God!' After that the polytheists were defeated and he gave both to those who had joined at Mecca and-to those who were muhGirm; he gave nothing to the aqzr. They began talking and he summoned them to his pavilion. He asked them, 'Are you not content to have them take away sheep and camels, while you go hack with the Messenger of God?' He went on, 'If the rest were to enter some valley, while the aqrtr climbed up a mountain defile, I would take the path of the aqar!'" In another narration from al-Bukhai on this subject, he (Anas b. Mdik) stated, "At the battle of Hunayn, Hawzzin, Gha@fa and others advanced, along with their livestock and families. With the Messenger of God (SAAS) there were 10,000 warriors and some men who had joined him at Mecca. They (the Muslims) withdrew, so that he was left alone. He made two calls to them, each caII separate from the other. Turning to his right, he called, 'Aqrtr!' They responded, 'At your service, Messenger of God! Be content, we are with you!' At the time he was riding his white mule. He dismounted and said, 'I am the servant and Messenger of God!' "The polytheists were defeated and he won much booty that day and he divided it between the muhqi'rin and the Meccans who had joined the Muslims (after the conquest). However, he gave the an:& nothing. The an:rtr said, 'If difficulties arise, we are summoned, but the spoils are given to others!' "When news of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he assembled them in a pavilion and asked them, ' A q ~ r , what is this report that has reached me?' They were silent and he asked, 'Aqar, are you'"
lessons: The deepest bonds are those of shared purpose and spiritual kinship, which no amount of material wealth can replace.
A wise leader differentiates between short-term material distribution and long-term relationship investment.
When we feel passed over, we may be blind to the greater honor we already hold in the eyes of those who truly value us.
practical_advice: When you feel left out or forgotten, reflect on the non-material ways you are valued by those who matter most to you.
If you are in a leadership role, address grievances privately and with warmth — public humiliation closes hearts, but honest private dialogue opens them.
Train yourself to measure success not only by what you receive materially but by the quality and depth of your relationships and contributions.

title: The Ansar's Grievance Over Hunayn Spoils and the Prophet's Assembly in the Pavilion
summary: After the battle of Hunayn, the Ansar felt resentful that the Prophet distributed all the spoils to the Meccans and other Arabs while giving them nothing. The Prophet assembled them privately and delivered a moving address reminding them of his bond with them and their true reward.
story: 'I am the servant and Messenger of God!' "The polytheists were defeated and he won much booty that day and he divided it between the muhqi'rin and the Meccans who had joined the Muslims (after the conquest). However, he gave the an:& nothing. The an:rtr said, 'If difficulties arise, we are summoned, but the spoils are given to others!' "When news of this reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he assembled them in a pavilion and asked them, ' A q ~ r ,what is this report that has reached me?' They were silent and he asked, 'Aqar, are you not content that others have worldly goods while you go away with the Messenger of God, having him for yourselves in your own homes?' 'Yes,' they replied. Then he said, 'If the men were to make their way through a valley and the an@r were to take a route through a mountain defile, I would follow them.' H i s h b asked, 'Abn Hamza' (i.e. Anas b. Mzlik), 'did you actually wimess that?' He replied, 'Where would I have been, away from him?'" Al-Bukhi then related it - as also did Muslim - from a hadith of Shu'ba, from Qatzda, from Anas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) assembled the an@r and said, 'Quraysh were still recently in the jnhiliyya and have greatly suffered. I wanted to aid them and to establish goodwill with them. Are you not content for others to return with worldly goods, while you go to your homespith the Messenger of God?' 'Yes,' they agreed. He then said, 'If the men were. to make their way into a valley while the an@r followed a route into a mountain defile, I would follow the valley - or the defile -of the an~ar.'" They also gave it from a hadith of Shu'ba, from Abii al-Tayyzh Yazid b. Hamid, from Anas in similar form. In that version they (the awrtr) had exclaimed, "By God, that's very strange! While our swords still drip with their blood, the spoils are divided up among them." He then addressed them in the words similar to those given above. Iman Ahmad stated, "CAffsn related to us, quoting Hammzd, quoting Thabit, from Anas b. Mzlik, to the effect that the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave from the spoils at Hunayn to Abii Sufyzn, Wyayna, al-Aqrac, Suhayl b. CAmr and others. The a q z r then said, 'Our swords drip with their blood, yet they go off with the spoils!' News of this reached the Prophet (SAAS) and he assembled the anjar in a pavilion of his until it was filled. He asked, 'Is there anyone here who is not of you?' They replied, 'No one except our cousin.' He commented, 'A cousin is of one's own people.' He then asked, 'Have you said such-and-such?' 'Yes,' they agreed. He observed, 'You are the shzczr, "the under-garments", while the people are the al-diihnr, "the outer-wear"." Are you content to have the others receive the sheep and camels while you take the Messenger of God to your homes?' 'Yes,' they agreed. He went on, 'The an@r are my karsh, "my very insides", and Caybati,lii "my closest family". If the men were to take a path through a valley and the an@r were to take a mountain defile, I would follow along the defile. Had it not been for the hijra, I would have been one of the aqar.' Hammad said, 'He gave out 100 camels (to the foregoing persons). He then named each one,of those.'" Ahmad is alone in giving this with this line of transmission. And the hadith meets the criteria set by Muslim. Iman Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Ibn Abii 'Adi, from Humayd, from Anas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "0 an@r, did I not come to you when you were lost and did not God then give you guidance through me? Did I not come to you when you were scattered, and did not God unite you through me? Did I not come to you when you were enemies (to one another) and did not God then place goodwill in your hearts?" "Yes, Messenger of God," they agreed. He asked, "Do you not say, 'You came to us in fear and we made you secure, in flight and we gave you sanctuary, in failure and we gave you aid?'" They replied, "Indeed yes; God has credit for His favour to us and to His messenger." The chain of authorities for this hadith is tripartite and it meets the criteria established by the phihayn, the two canonical collections. This hadith from Anas b. Mdik is tantamount to m~tarnarir. Al-Bukhai stated that Masa b. Ismacilrelated to him, quoting Wahih, quoting CAmr b. Y+ya, from 'Abbxd b. Tamim, from CAbd Allah b. Zayd b. 'A~im,who said, "When God gave booty to His messenger (SAAS) at the battle of Hunayn, he made distributions to those recently reconciled (to Islam) but gave nothing to the anSar. They seem to have been upset at not having received as had the others, and so he addressed them as follows, 'Aqar, did I not find you astray and did not then God guide you to me? Were you not divided, and did God not unite you through me? Were you not poor, and did God not unite you through me? Were you not poor, and did God not enrich you through me?' As he asked each of these, they replied, 'God and His messenger give many favours.' "He went on, 'If you had wished, you could have said, "You came to us soon-and-swforth." Are you not content to have others go off with the sheep and camels, while you take the Messenger of God away to your homes? Had it not been for the hijra I would have been one of the a q a r . If the people were to take a path into a valley and a mountain defile, I would follow into the valley and defile taken by the aqar. The a q a r are shicar,while the people are dithar. After me you will notice others given preference; be patient until yon meet me at al-;hewd, the pool before paradise.'" Muslim related it from a hadith of 'Amr b. Yahya al-Maini. Yiinus b. Bukayr quoted Muhammad h. Ishaq as saying, "It was related to me by CAsim b. Wmar h. Qatsda, from M a b a d b. Labid that Aba Sacid al-Khudari said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had won the spoils at the battle of Hunayn and had divided these up as he did to those Meccans of goodwill and to others of the Arabs, but had given none of it whatsoever to the an:ar, the latter men were resentful. They said, "So the Messenger of God (SAAS) has met with his own!" Saki b. Wbada went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, "0 Messenger of God, this tribe of aqar feel resentment towards you." "Why?" he asked. Saki explained, "Because you have been distributing booty to your own people and others but giving them, the anfar, nothing." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, "And where do you stand in that, SaCd?" "Well", he replied, "I'm just one man of my own people." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, "Assemble your people for me in this enclosure and when they are gathered, let me know." Sa'd then left, called out to them and they assembled in that enclosure. One of the rnuhzjrrin also came; he was allowed entry and went in. Others who came were sent away. Eventually all the an@, without exception, were gathered for h i . Sa'd then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, "Messenger of God, this tribe of an@r are now assembled where you told me to gather them." "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went and stood among them to address them. He gave fitting praise and thanks to God and went on to say, " A n ~ z rdid I not come to you when you were astray, and did not God then guide you? (Were you not) poor and did God not enrich you? Were you not enemies and did not God create good will between you?" "Yes," they agreed. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, "Can you not give me response, 0 a q ~ r ?" They replied, "What should we say, Messenger of God? How should we answer you? Our favours are due to God and to His messenger." He replied, "I swear by God, had you wished you could have said, 'You spoke the truth, again and again.' And 'You came to us an outcast and we sheltered you. You came in distress and we comforted you. You were fearful and we made you secure, failing and we gave you aid.'" They responded, "Our favours are due to God and to His messenger." "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, "Do you find within you, 0 angzv, a lust for those worldly things whereby I reconciled a people who had accepted Islam, while I entrusted you with that of Islam which God had allotted to you? Are you resendul, for the others to take sheep and camels to their homes, while you return with the Messenger of God to yours? I swear by Him who holds my soul in His hand, that if the people were to follow one mountain defile while the a q a r followed another, I would make my way through the defile of the a q z r . Had it not been for the hijra, I would have been a man of the anSzr. 0 God, have mercy on the anSzr, their children and their children's children!" "They wept at this until their beards were wet and said, 'We are content with God as our Lord and His messenger as distributor.' "He then left and they dispersed.'" Iman Ahmad related it thus from a hadith of Ibn Ishxq. None of the compilers of the canonical collections gave it with this line of transmission. It is (to be considered) ~ahih,L'autheutic". Iman Ahmad related it also from Yahyx h. Bukayr, from al-Fad1 b. Marzuq, from 'Afiya h. Sa'd albCAwfi,from Abii Sacidal-Khudari, who quoted one of the a q z y as having said, to his friends, "I swear, I told you, didn't I, that once things settled down, he would give preference to others over you?" They replied to him rudely and that reached the Messenger of God (SAAS). He then came to them and said some things the narrator said he did not recall. They replied, "Yes, Messenger of God." He told them, "You used not to ride horses" and every time he said something to them, they replied, "Yes, Messenger of God." He then related the rest of the hadiih as above. A b a d was also alone in giving this. Imam &ad was also alone in relating it from a hadiih of al-Acmash, from Abn Szlih, from Abii Sacid, in similar words. H e related it as well from Miisa b. Wqba, from Ibn Lahi'a, from Abii al-Zubayr, from Jabir.
lessons: True worth is not measured by material gain but by the depth of one's relationship with those they love and serve. The Ansar's greatest reward was carrying the Prophet himself in their hearts and homes, not gold or camels.

Honest grievances, when brought to a wise leader with sincerity rather than malice, can become opportunities for healing and deeper understanding. Sa'd ibn Ubada modeled the courage to speak truth to the Prophet respectfully.

Patience in the face of apparent injustice is a mark of spiritual maturity. The Prophet explicitly asked the Ansar to be patient even knowing that others would be preferred after him, trusting them to endure this with faith until they met him at al-hawd.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked or unrewarded despite your sacrifices, pause before reacting and ask yourself what intangible gifts you have received that others have not. Write them down and reflect on their true value.
If you carry a grievance, take the path of Sa'd ibn Ubada: bring your concern privately and respectfully to the appropriate person rather than letting resentment fester within the group.

When someone reminds you of the blessings God has given you through hardship — guidance after being lost, unity after division, strength after weakness — let that recounting move you to gratitude rather than comparison with others.

title: The Bedouin Who Grew Impatient with the Prophet's Promise
summary: A bedouin repeatedly pressed the Prophet for fulfilment of a promise at al-Ji'rāna. The Prophet, appearing displeased, turned to Abū Mūsā and Bilāl and transferred to them the spiritual blessing of 'rejoicing' before distributing blessed water.
story: Al-Bukhārī stated that Muhammad b. al-'Alā' related to him, quoting Usāma, from Burayд b. 'Abd Allāh, from Abū Burda, from Abū Mūsā, who said, 'I was with the Prophet (SAAS) while he was encamped at al-Ji'rāna, between Mecca and al-Madīna, Bilāl being there with him. A bedouin came up to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and demanded, "Well, aren't you going to fulfil your promise to me?" "Abshir! Rejoice!" The bedouin insisted, "You've told me that abshir too often already!"' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then went over to Abū Mūsā and Bilāl, looking angry and said, 'The bushrā, the rejoicing, has been given back; you two have it!' He then asked for a bowl of water in which he washed his hands and face. He then took a mouthful and spat it back in it, saying, 'Now you two drink it and pour the rest over your faces and necks and you two "rejoice".' They took the bowl and did as he asked. Umm Salama called out from behind the curtain, 'Keep some for your mother!' And they did leave some for her.
lessons: Impatience and ingratitude can rob a person of spiritual blessings that others then receive in their stead.
The Prophet's displeasure at rudeness did not diminish his generosity, but he redirected blessings toward those who showed gratitude and respect.
Contentment and gracious waiting are virtues that preserve one's portion of divine favor.
practical_advice: Practice patience when waiting for promises to be fulfilled; pressing others with impatience can push away the very good you are seeking.
Cultivate a spirit of gratitude for what you have received rather than fixating on what has not yet arrived.
When you feel overlooked or frustrated, take a moment to assess whether your manner of expressing that frustration is appropriate to the relationship and situation.

title: The Bedouin Who Pulled the Prophet's Mantle
summary: A bedouin grabbed the Prophet's rough-hemmed mantle so forcefully it left a mark on his shoulder, demanding payment from God's property. The Prophet responded with a smile and ordered the payment.
story: Al-Bukhārī also stated that Yaḥyā b. Bukayr related to him, quoting Mālik, from Isḥāq b. 'Abd Allāh, from Anas b. Mālik, who said, 'I was walking with the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was wearing a mantle from Najrān that had a rough hem. A bedouin came up to him and pulled on the mantle so roughly that I saw that its hem had made a mark on his shoulder. The bedouin said, "Order a payment to me from God's property you have!" He turned to him, laughed and then ordered that a payment be made to him.'
lessons: The Prophet's extraordinary forbearance in the face of rude and demanding behavior is a model of how to respond to provocation with grace.
Responding to rudeness with patience and even humor defuses tension and preserves dignity far better than retaliation.
Generosity and openhandedness, even toward those who approach poorly, reflect a lofty character.
practical_advice: When someone approaches you rudely or aggressively, try to respond with calmness or even lightness rather than mirroring their aggression.
Do not let another person's lack of manners determine your own conduct; your character is your own responsibility.
Look for opportunities to respond to difficult people with generosity; it often transforms the interaction entirely.

title: The Prophet's Statement About Ju'ayl b. Suraqa and the Wisdom of Distributing to New Muslims
summary: When asked why the Prophet gave 100 camels to 'Uyayna and al-Aqra' but not to Ju'ayl b. Suraqa, the Prophet explained that Ju'ayl's proven faith made him spiritually wealthier, while the others needed encouragement to accept Islam.
story: Ibn Isḥāq stated that it was related to him by Muḥammad b. Ibrāhīm b. al-Ḥārith al-Taymī, that one of his companions asked the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Messenger of God, since you gave 100 camels to each of 'Uyayna and al-Aqra', why did you not do so to Ju'ayl b. Surāqa al-Damrī?' He replied, 'By Him who holds the soul of Muḥammad in his hand, Ju'ayl is better than the very best of all those on earth who are like 'Uyayna and al-Aqra'! But I wanted to establish goodwill with them for them to accept Islam; I placed the trust of Ju'ayl b. Surāqa in his Islam.'
lessons: True spiritual worth is not measured by material reward; those of deep and proven faith are honored by being entrusted with their faith itself.
Wise leaders and caregivers calibrate their attention and resources based on who is most vulnerable or most in need, not on who is most outwardly deserving.
Being passed over for a worldly reward may in fact be a sign of the high regard in which one is held by those who know one's true character.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked or undervalued while others seem to receive more recognition, reflect on whether your own integrity and inner strength are themselves a form of honor.
Trust that those who truly know you and your character are aware of your worth, even when outward rewards do not reflect it.
Resist comparing yourself to others who receive more visible rewards; their circumstances and needs may be entirely different from yours.

title: Safwan b. Umayya: From Hatred to Love Through the Prophet's Generosity
summary: Safwān b. Umayya, who had initially been among those most hostile to the Prophet, declared that the Prophet's repeated generosity from the spoils of Hunayn transformed him from his greatest enemy into the most beloved of God's creation.
story: In the authentic hadīth of Safwān b. Umayya, he is quoted as saying, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) kept on making distributions to me from the spoils of Hunayn; he was previously the most hated person to me, but he gave until he became, for me, the most loved thing that God had created.'
lessons: Consistent generosity and goodwill can transform even the most deeply rooted enmity into love.
The Prophet's approach of winning hearts through kindness rather than compulsion is a timeless model of transformative leadership.
No heart is so hardened that sincere and repeated goodness cannot reach it.
practical_advice: If you are trying to mend a damaged relationship, consider whether consistent acts of genuine generosity and goodwill might open a door that arguments cannot.
Do not give up on difficult people too quickly; transformation often happens gradually through sustained kindness.
Reflect on moments when someone's generosity toward you softened your own heart, and let that memory inspire you to be generous toward others.

title: Mālik b. 'Awf Accepts Islam and Is Rewarded
summary: Mālik b. 'Awf al-Naṣrī, the commander who had led the Hawāzin against the Muslims at Hunayn, accepted Islam after being promised the return of his people and property and 100 camels. He then became an effective Muslim commander against Thaqīf.
story: Ibn Isḥāq stated that the Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke with a delegation from Hawāzin and asked them what had happened to Mālik b. 'Awf. They replied that he was in al-Ṭā'if with Banū Thaqīf. He told them, 'Inform him that if he comes to me as a Muslim, I will return to him his people and their belongings and will award him 100 camels.' Ibn Isḥāq went on, 'When Mālik heard this, he slipped away from Thaqīf - or in Mecca - accepted and went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at al-Ji'rāna, accepted Islam and became a good Muslim. He then restored to Mālik both his people and their belongings. When he also gave him 100 camels, Mālik b. 'Awf, may God be pleased with him, spoke the following verses, "Never have I seen nor heard of the like of Muḥammad among all mankind. He gives in good faith and generously when requested and if you wish he will foretell what is to come. When the squadron goes into action with its lances and strikes hard with its Indian-steel swords He is like a lion crouching over its cubs within its den."' Ibn Isḥāq went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed him in command over those of his people who had accepted Islam, those tribes being Thumnāla, Salima and Fahm. He (Mālik) had those do battle against Thaqīf; whenever they brought their flocks out, he would attack, thus causing them great difficulty.'
lessons: A genuine offer of reconciliation, made with real material and personal sacrifice, can turn an adversary into a devoted ally.
Embracing a new path, even after having been on the opposing side, requires courage and is honored when it is sincere.
Appointing people to roles that match their natural skills and character multiplies the benefit of their conversion and talents.
practical_advice: When seeking to reconcile with someone who has wronged you or opposed you, consider what genuine, substantive gesture of goodwill you can offer that demonstrates your sincerity.
If you have been on the wrong side of something and wish to change, know that a sincere change of heart, followed by sincere action, can open remarkable new doors.
Trust that skills and strengths you developed even in the wrong context can be redirected toward good when your heart and direction change.

title: The Prophet's Words About 'Amr b. Taghlib: Honoring Those Who Need No Material Gift
summary: The Prophet explained that he gave material gifts only to those he feared might be impatient or resentful, while trusting those of strong faith—like 'Amr b. Taghlib—to their Islam. 'Amr declared he would rather have those words than the finest livestock.
story: Al-Bukhārī stated that Qabīṣa related to him, quoting Sufyān, from al-A'mash, from Abū Wā'il, from 'Abd Allāh, who said, 'When the Prophet (SAAS) divided up the spoils of Hunayn, one of the anṣār said, "By doing this, he was not seeking to serve God!"' ... Al-Bukhārī stated that Mūsā b. Ismā'īl related to him quoting Jarīr b. Hāzim, quoting al-Ḥasan, who stated that 'Amr b. Tha'laba related to him as follows, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) made distribution to some and did not to others. It seems that some complained to him and he responded, "I give to those people I fear will be impatient and resentful; and to those who are self-sufficient and whose goodness is inspired in them by God, I give not. Of them is 'Amr b. Taghlib."' 'Amr went on, "I would rather have such words about me said by the Messenger of God (SAAS) than have the most choice animals of the herd!"' Abū Qāsim added to this, quoting Jarīr as saying, 'I heard al-Ḥasan quote 'Amr b. Taghlib as saying that the Messenger of God (SAAS) then brought property - or captives - and distributed it in this way.' In an account from al-Bukhārī he is quoted as saying, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) brought some property - or something - and gave to some men but not to others. It reached him that those who had been left out were complaining and so he made an address to them. He gave praise and thanks to God, then said, "And further ..."'
lessons: Being trusted to handle a situation without external support is often a greater compliment than being given material assistance.
Words of sincere recognition from those we respect can be more valuable than any material reward.
Inner sufficiency and contentment are gifts that free a person from dependence on external validation or material things.
practical_advice: When you are passed over for a reward or recognition, consider whether it might reflect trust in your resilience and self-sufficiency rather than neglect.
Invest in building inner contentment so that your sense of worth does not depend on whether others give you material things or public praise.
Identify one person in your life whose sincere good word about you would mean more to you than any prize, and let that relationship be one you nurture through honest conduct.

title: An Ignorant Man's Criticism of the Prophet's Distribution of Hunayn Spoils
summary: After the distribution of Hunayn spoils, one of the Ansar claimed the Prophet was not seeking God's pleasure in his distribution, prompting 'Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud to report this to the Prophet.
story: Al-Bukhārī stated that Qabīṣa related to him, quoting Sufyān, from al-A'mash, from Abū Wā'il, from 'Abd Allāh, who said, 'When the Prophet (SAAS) divided up the spoils of Hunayn, one of the anṣār said, "By doing this, he was not seeking to serve God!" I then went to the Messenger of God ...'
lessons: Judging the intentions of others, especially those known for their integrity and wisdom, from a position of incomplete understanding is a grave error.
Anger or dissatisfaction with outcomes we do not understand should prompt humble inquiry rather than rash judgment.
Those who observe injustice or hear troubling statements have a responsibility to bring them to the attention of those in authority through proper channels.
practical_advice: Before concluding that someone's decision was motivated by bad intentions, take time to understand the full context and reasoning behind that decision.
When you are frustrated with a decision that affects you, seek clarification through respectful inquiry rather than voicing hasty judgment to others.
If you hear someone making an unfair accusation against a person of integrity, consider whether you have a responsibility to gently challenge that accusation or seek to correct the record.

title: Dissatisfaction Over Distribution of Hunayn Spoils – The Companion Reports to the Prophet
summary: A man from the Ansar questions the Prophet's justice in distributing the spoils of Hunayn, and when the Companion reports this, the Prophet responds by invoking the patience of Prophet Moses.
story: Al-Bukhāri stated that Qabisa related to him, quoting Sufyān, from al-A'mash, from Abū Wā'il, from 'Abd Allāh, who said, "When the Prophet (SAAS) divided up the spoils of Hunayn, one of the anṣār said, 'By doing this, he was not seeking to serve God!' I then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and informed him of this. His face darkened and he exclaimed, 'May God have mercy on Moses! He was made to suffer even more than this but remained patient!'" Muslim related this from a ḥadīth of al-A'mash. Al-Bukhāri also stated that Qutayba b. Sa'id related to him, quoting Jarir, from Manṣūr, from Abū Wā'il, who quoted 'Abd Allāh as saying, "At Hunayn the Messenger of God (SAAS) gave preferable treatment to some persons. He gave 100 camels to each of al-Aqra' b. Ḥābis and 'Uyayna and also made distributions to others. One man said, 'By this division, service to God was not intended!' I said, 'Well, I'm going to report this to the Messenger of God.' He (the Messenger of God (SAAS)) exclaimed, 'May God have mercy on Moses; he was made to suffer even more than this but remained patient!'" He also related it thus from a ḥadīth of Manṣūr, from al-Mu'tamir. In one account given by al-Bukhāri, the wording is, "By God, this is a distribution that is devoid of justice; service to God was not intended." So I said, "I will inform the Messenger of God (SAAS) of this!" When I went to him and told him, he exclaimed, "And who will ever be just, if it not be God and His messenger! May God have mercy on Moses; he was made to suffer even more than this but remained patient!"
lessons: Leaders who act with justice will still face unjust criticism; what matters is sincerity before God, not the approval of others.
The example of the prophets teaches us that suffering criticism and ingratitude patiently is a mark of the highest character.
When we are wrongly accused or misunderstood, invoking the patience of those who endured greater trials helps restore perspective and calm.
practical_advice: When you face unfair criticism despite doing your best, recall that even the greatest people in history were accused unjustly — remind yourself that your account is with God, not with those who misjudge you.
Instead of reacting with anger to slander, take a breath and ask yourself: 'Have I acted with sincerity?' If yes, let the criticism pass without allowing it to destabilize you.
