Keep a list of historical or spiritual figures whose patience under trial inspires you, and return to their stories when you feel the sting of ingratitude.

title: Dhū al-Khuwaysira Challenges the Prophet's Justice at Hunayn
summary: A man named Dhū al-Khuwaysira from Banū Tamim publicly accuses the Prophet of injustice during the distribution of Hunayn spoils, prompting a stern prophetic response and a warning about a dangerous future faction.
story: Muhammad b. Isḥāq stated that it was related to him by Abū 'Ubayda b. Muhammad b. 'Ammār b. Yāsir, from Miqsam Abū al-Qāsim, the freed-man of 'Abd Allāh b. al-Ḥārith b. Nawfal, who said, "I and Ṭalīd b. Kilāb al-Laythī came to 'Abd Allāh b. 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ while he was circumambulating the ka'ba, his sandals hanging from his hand. We asked him, 'Were you present with the Messenger of God (SAAS) when the man from Banū Tamim spoke to him at the battle of Hunayn?' 'Yes,' he replied. 'A man of Banū Tamim named Dhū al-Khuwaysira came and stood nearby while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was distributing the spoils. Dhū al-Khuwaysira told him, "Muhammad, I have been watching what you have been doing today." "Yes", asked the Messenger of God (SAAS), "and what have you thought?" "I do not think you have been acting justly." The Prophet (SAAS) was very angry and said, "Confound you! If justice is not to be found with me, then with whom is it!" 'Umar b. al-Khaṭṭāb then asked, 'Should we not kill him?' He replied, 'No; leave him alone. He shall lead a faction who will go deeply into the faith and emerge as an arrow does from its target. When its head is examined, nothing will be seen upon it. Also, its shaft and its notch will have nothing upon them. It will have outstripped the insides and the blood.'" In both Ṣaḥīḥ collections, it is related from a ḥadīth of al-Zuhrī, from Abū Salama, from Abū Sa'id, who said, "While we were with the Messenger of God (SAAS), as he was making distributions of the spoils, Dhū al-Khuwaysir, a man of Banū Tamim, came over to him and said, 'Messenger of God, be just!' He responded, 'Confound you, who would be just if I were not? Then I would have disappointed and failed. If I were not just, who would be?' 'Umar b. al-Khaṭṭāb asked, 'Messenger of God, will you give me permission to behead him?' 'Leave him alone,' he replied. 'He has friends. One of you would feel contempt for his prayer and their prayer, his fasting and their fasting. They recite the Qur'ān, but their incantations do not come forth. They slip on through Islam as an arrow slips on through a target. If you examine its head, you'll see nothing on it; then its head binding, you'll see nothing; then its shaft, and you'll see nothing; then its feathers, you'll see nothing. It will have outstripped the insides and the blood. Their sign is that they will have among them a black man, one of whose upper arms reminds you of a woman's breasts, or like a piece of fat meat vibrating; they will appear at a time when people are disunited.' Abū Sa'id stated, 'I testify that I heard this from the Messenger of God (SAAS), and I also testify that 'Alī b. Abū Ṭālib fought them while I was present. He gave orders and that man was searched out and brought. I saw him to be as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had described him.'"
lessons: Outward religiosity without inner sincerity produces arrogance and the willingness to condemn even the most righteous of people.
True justice begins with trusting those who have demonstrated consistent integrity, rather than demanding they justify themselves to every critic.
The Prophet's restraint in not punishing the man who wronged him publicly is itself a lesson in measured, far-sighted leadership.
practical_advice: When someone you trust is accused of wrongdoing by a harsh or dismissive critic, look at the full pattern of that person's conduct before passing judgment.
If you find yourself angry at perceived injustice, pause and ask whether your anger is rooted in genuine principle or wounded pride.
Beware of religious or moral rigidity that makes you feel superior to others — sincerity, humility, and mercy are the true marks of faith.

title: Jābir's Account of the Man Who Demanded Justice at al-Ji'rāna
summary: At al-Ji'rāna, a man approaches the Prophet during the distribution of Hunayn spoils and demands justice; the Prophet affirms his own accountability before God.
story: Al-Layth b. Sa'd quoted from Yaḥyā b. Sa'id, from Abū al-Zubayr, who quoted Jābir b. 'Abd Allāh as saying, "A man came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at al-Ji'rāna while he was making distributions from the spoils of Hunayn. There was silver in Bilāl's cloak which he was taking and handing to people. The man said, 'Muhammad, be just!' He responded, 'Confound you, who will be just if not me? Otherwise I would have disappointed and failed.' 'Umar b. al-Khaṭṭāb asked, 'Will you permit me, Messenger of God, to kill this hypocrite?' He replied, 'God forbid that people say of me that I kill my men! (When) this fellow and his friends recite the Qur'ān, it doesn't emerge out of their throats; they have it pass straight on through, like an arrow exiting its target!'" Muslim related this from Muhammad b. Rumḥ, from al-Layth. Aḥmad stated that it was related to him by Abū 'Āmir, quoting Qurra, from 'Amr b. Dīnār, from Jābir, who said, "While the Messenger of God (SAAS) was distributing the spoils of Hunayn, a man went up to him and said, 'Do be just!' He responded, 'I will be made to suffer if I am not just!'" Al-Bukhāri related this from Muslim b. Ibrāhīm, from Qurra b. Khālid al-Sadūsī.
lessons: A leader who is truly just does not need to prove his justice to every detractor; his record speaks for him.
The Prophet's willingness to bear unjust accusations without retaliating models how to absorb social hostility with grace.
Hollow religious practice — reciting words without internalizing their meaning — produces a hardened heart incapable of recognizing goodness.
practical_advice: When your integrity is questioned despite your best efforts, remind yourself that you are ultimately accountable to God and not to every hostile voice.
Practice the discipline of not defending yourself aggressively when wrongly accused; a calm, clear response is more dignified and effective than anger.
Regularly reflect on whether your religious or ethical practices are changing your character from the inside, or merely performing well on the outside.

title: The Arrival of al-Shaymā', Foster-Sister of the Prophet, at al-Ji'rāna
summary: The Prophet's foster-sister al-Shaymā' is captured during the Hawāzin battle and brought before him; he recognizes her by a childhood mark, honors her warmly, and grants her freedom and gifts.
story: Ibn Isḥāq stated that a man of Banū Sa'd related to him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said at the battle with Hawāzin, 'If you capture Nijād - a man of Banū Sa'd b. Bakr - don't let him escape you.' (He had done something serious.) "When the Muslims took him, they led him and his family away. With him they took al-Shaymā', daughter of al-Ḥārith b. 'Abd al-'Uzzā, the foster-sister of the Messenger of God (SAAS). "They treated her roughly as they took her away and she said to the Muslims, 'You should know, I swear by God, that I am the foster-sister of your leader.' They did not believe her until they brought her to the Messenger of God (SAAS). "Yazīd b. 'Ubayd al-Sa'dī - he being Abū Wajza - said, 'When she was brought to the Messenger of God (SAAS) she said, "Messenger of God, I am your foster-sister." "How can you prove that?" he asked. She replied, "There is the mark of the bite you gave me on my back when I carried you on my hip." The Messenger of God (SAAS) recognized the mark and spread out his cloak for her, sat her down on it and treated her well. He told her, "If you wish, you may live with me in honour and affection. If you wish for me to provide for you and to let you return to your people, I will do so." She replied, "Yes, do provide for me and return me to my people." The Messenger of God (SAAS) gave her presents and returned her to her people. Banū Sa'd claim that he gave her a slave called Makhūl and a slave-girl, that she married them to one another and that descendants of that union still survive.'" Al-Bayhaqi related from a ḥadīth of al-Ḥakam b. 'Abd al-Malik, from Qatāda, who said, "On the day of the conquest of Hawāzin, a slave-girl came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, 'Messenger of God, I am your sister! I am Shaymā', daughter of al-Ḥārith.' He asked her, 'If you are telling the truth, then you have a mark I made that would not wear away.' She then exposed her upper arm and said, 'Yes, Messenger of God. When you were small, you gave me this bite.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then spread out his cloak for her and said, 'Ask and it will be given; intercede for someone and you will be interceded for.'"
lessons: Bonds of love and care formed in childhood are never erased by time, distance, or the upheavals of life.
Dignity and generosity toward those who cared for us in our most vulnerable moments reflects the deepest gratitude and nobility of character.
Even in the chaos of conflict, a moment of recognition and compassion can restore the humanity of all involved.
practical_advice: Reach out to someone from your past who showed you kindness when you were young or vulnerable — acknowledge what their care meant to you.
When someone from a difficult background or situation claims a connection to you, give them the benefit of the doubt and listen with an open heart before dismissing them.
Let the Prophet's gesture of spreading his cloak inspire you: find a small, tangible way to honor and comfort someone who is going through hardship.

title: The Foster-Parents of the Prophet Arrive and Are Honored
summary: A narration describes the Prophet honoring his foster-father by spreading his cloak for him, then honoring his foster-mother on his other side, and then standing to seat his foster-brother before him.
story: Abū Dāwūd stated in al-Marāsīl that Aḥmad b. Ṣāqid al-Hamdānī related to him, quoting Ibn Wahb, quoting 'Amr b. al-Ḥārith, (who said that) 'Umar b. al-Sā'ib related to him that he was informed that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was one day seated when his foster-father came up to him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) spread out part of his cloak for him and he sat upon it. Then his mother came and he laid out another part of his cloak, on his other side, for her to sit upon. His foster-brother next arrived and the Messenger of God (SAAS) arose and sat him down before him. It is explained above that all Hawāzin are connected to him due to his having been suckled by Banū Sa'd b. Bakr, who are a clan of Hawāzin. Their spokesman, Zuhayr b. Ṣurad, stated, "O Messenger of God, those in the enclosures for the captives are nothing but your own maternal aunts and nursemaids, so be kind to us, may God be kind to you." He then recited, in part, "Be kind to womenfolk from whom you suckled, your mouth filled with pearl-like drops of their pure milk."
lessons: Gratitude for those who nurtured us in childhood is a lifelong obligation that does not diminish with power, success, or the passage of time.
Standing to honor someone — literally or figuratively — is a profound expression of respect that costs nothing but means everything.
The Prophet's tender treatment of his foster-family even at the height of his authority teaches that true greatness is never too grand for personal warmth.
practical_advice: Think of the people who nurtured or raised you and find a meaningful way to show them honor and appreciation this week.
Practice small acts of physical or symbolic deference — like giving up your seat or making space — as daily reminders that humility and gratitude are lived, not just felt.
When you are in a position of influence or authority, be especially intentional about showing warmth to those who knew you before your success.

title: The Hawazin Delegation Pleads for Their Captives
summary: A delegation from the Hawazin tribe, including their spokesman Zuhayr ibn Surad, came to the Prophet after the Battle of Hunayn to plead for the release of their captive women and children, reminding him of his bonds of kinship through nursing.
story: arrived and the Messenger of God (SAAS) arose and sat him down before him. It is explained above that all Hawain are connected to him due to his having been suckled by Banii Sa'd b. Bakr, who are a clan of Hawzin. Their spokesman, Zuhayr b. Surad, stated, "0Messenger of God, those in the enclosures for the captives are nothing but your own maternal aunts and nursemaids, so be kind to us, may God be kind to you." He then recited, in part, "Be kind to womenfolk from whom you suckled, your mouth filled with pearl-like drops of their pure milk. Be kind to women from whom you suckled, for them your actions will adorn you and not be wasted" This, then, was the reason why Bakra's father was set free; by so doing, he restored to them all their previous and present rights and privileges, as individuals and as a group.
lessons: Acts of compassion and generosity, especially toward those who once cared for us, are among the highest expressions of gratitude and nobility of character.
The bonds formed in moments of vulnerability and dependence, such as in infancy, carry lasting moral weight and should not be forgotten even in positions of power.
True leadership means being moved by sincere appeals and being willing to show mercy even over those whom one has authority.
practical_advice: When you are in a position of strength or authority, reflect on those who once helped or nurtured you, and let gratitude inform your decisions.
If you are seeking mercy or leniency from someone, appeal honestly and sincerely to shared bonds and genuine emotion rather than argument alone.
Practice recognizing and honoring acts of past kindness in your daily relationships, even small ones, as this builds a culture of gratitude and mutual care.

title: Al-Nadir ibn al-Harith Embraces Islam at al-Ji'rana
summary: Al-Nadir ibn al-Harith, who had accompanied the Quraysh to the Battle of Hunayn still holding to his old faith and even harboring intentions to attack the Prophet if he was defeated, experienced a transformative moment of guidance at al-Ji'rana and embraced Islam.
story: Al-Waqidi related from Ibmhim b. Muhammad h. Shurahbil, from his father, who said, "Al-Nadir b. al-Hzrith b. Kalada was one of the most handsome of men. He used to say, 'Praise be to God who was kind enough to award us Islam and Muhammad (SAAS). And we will not die with the same (belief system) of our fathers, and that in which our brothers and cousins were killed.'" He then went on to relate his enmity for the Prophet (SAAS) and the fact that he went out with his people of Quraysh to Hunayn still believing in their old faith. He said, "If there were to be a defeat for Muhammad, we were prepared to attack him. But we were not able to accomplish that. When he was at al-Ji'rna, I was still unchanged. Then I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, 'Is that Nadir?' 'Labbayk! At your service,' I replied. He asked, 'Would you now like something better than what God prevented you from achieving at Hunayn?' I went quickly over to him and he said, 'It is now time for you to recognize how wrong you were.' I said, 'I realize that had there been any other (god) than God, he would have helped. I testify that there is no god but God alone and without associate!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'O God, make him ever more firm!' Al-Nadir then said, 'By Him who sent him with the truth, it was as though my heart was as firm as a rock in the faith and in enlightenment into the truth.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Praise be to God Who gave him guidance!'"
lessons: It is never too late for a person to turn away from a path of enmity or wrongdoing and embrace truth, as long as the opportunity for sincere repentance remains open.
Divine protection of truth often works by preventing harmful deeds, giving those with hardened hearts the chance to witness reality clearly and reconsider their path.
When a person sincerely acknowledges their wrongdoing and submits to the truth, the heart can transform in an instant into a place of certainty and peace.
practical_advice: If you have been holding onto resentment, opposition, or a harmful course of action, ask yourself honestly what evidence surrounds you that you may have been wrong, and be willing to change.
When you witness someone caught in confusion or hostility, respond with an invitation to reflection rather than condemnation, as the Prophet did by gently asking al-Nadir to recognize his error.
After making a sincere commitment to positive change, ask God to make you firm in it, and seek reminders and companions who reinforce your new direction.

title: The Umra of al-Ji'rana and the Number of the Prophet's Umra Pilgrimages
summary: After distributing the spoils of Hunayn at al-Ji'rana, the Prophet performed an umra pilgrimage, and scholars of hadith engaged in detailed discussion about the number and timing of his umra pilgrimages, including a notable difference of opinion between Ibn Umar and Aisha.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that Bahz and 'Abd al-Samad al-Ma'ni both quoted Hammam b. Yahya as having quoted Qatada as saying, "I asked Anas b. Malik the following, 'How many pilgrimages did the Messenger of God (SAAS) make?' 'One full hijja and four lesser 'umra pilgrimages. There was one 'umra at the time of al-Hudaybiyya truce, another in Dhii al-qa'da from Medina, another in Dhii al-Qa'da from al-Ji'rana, where he distributed the spoils of Hunayn, and then the 'umra he performed when he also performed the full hijja pilgrimage.'" Al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Da'ud and al-Tirmidhi all related this with various lines of transmission, from Hammam b. Yahya. Al-Tirmidhi characterized it as hasan sahih, "good and authentic". Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Abu al-Nadr, quoting Da'ud — meaning al-'Attar — from 'Amr, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the 'umra four times: that of al-Hudaybiyya, the 'umrat al-qada, the third from al-Ji'rana, and the fourth at the time of his full hajj." Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Majah related this from a hadith of Da'ud b. 'Abd al-Rahman al-'Attar al-Makki, from 'Amr b. Dinar. He considered it hasan, "good", as did al-Tirmidhi. Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Yahya b. Zakariyya b. Abi Za'ida, quoting Hajjaj b. Artat, from 'Amr b. Shu'ayb, from his father, from his grandfather, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr b. al-'As, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed three 'umra. All were in Dhii al-Qa'da. He would (continue to) make the call Labbayka! until he saluted the (Black) Stone." This tradition is gharib, "unilateral", from this source. These are the three 'umra that occurred during Dhii al-Qa'da; these are apart from the one that coincided with his full hijja, which took place in Dhu al-Hijja. If this source suggests the commencement of his Ihram, state of ritual consecration, through them in Dhii al-Qa'da, it could be because he did not include that of al-Hudaybiyya, since he was blocked from it and did not actually accomplish it. But God knows best. I observe that Nafi' and his master Ibn Umar denied that the Messenger of God (SAAS) fully accomplished the 'umra from al-Ji'rana. This is according to the account given by al-Bukhari. He stated that it was related to him by Abu al-Nu'man, quoting Hammad b. Zayd, from Ayyub, from Nafi', from Ibn Umar, who quoted 'Umar b. al-Khattab as having asked, "Messenger of God, (what should I do about) the vow I made before the coming of Islam to spend a day in i'tikaf prayer in seclusion?" He ordered him to fulfil the vow. (The narrator went on) "'Umar received two slave girls from the captives taken at Hunayn and he accommodated them in a house in Mecca. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) released the Hunayn captives and they began to run in the streets. 'Umar asked (his son), 'Abd Allah, what's this you see happening?' He replied, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) has released the captives.' 'Umar then said, 'Go and send the two slave-girls away.'" Nafi' went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not make the 'umra from al-Ji'rana. Had he done so, this fact would not have been unknown to 'Abd Allah (b. Umar)." Muslim related this from a hadith of Ayyub al-Sakhtiyani, from Nafi', from ('Abd Allah) Ibn Umar. Muslim also related it from Ahmad b. 'Abda al-Dabbi, from Hammad b. Zayd, from Ayyub, from Nafi', who said, "Reference was made in the presence of ('Abd Allah) Ibn Umar to the 'umra made by the Messenger of God (SAAS) from al-Ji'rana. Ibn Umar commented, 'He did not make an 'umra from there.'" This is very gharib, strange, to be reported of Ibn Umar by his freed-man Nafi', both of them thereby denying his having made an 'umra from there. The transmitters of ahadith other than them do maintain that he did so, as is shown by reports given from them in the mass of authoritative collections of ahadith of all kinds, including the military chronicles. This is also established in both Sahih collections in a hadith of 'Ata' b. Abi Rabah from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, who contradicted Ibn Umar's statement that "The Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the 'umra in Rajab." She stated, "May God forgive 'Abd al-Rahman's father! ('Abd Allah b. Umar, that is.) The Messenger of God (SAAS) never performed the 'umra other than in his presence, and he never did so in Rajab." Imam Ahmad stated that Ibn Numayr related to him, quoting al-A'mash, from Mujahid, who said, "'Urwa b. al-Zubayr asked Ibn Umar in what month the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the 'umra. He replied, 'In Rajab.' 'A'isha was listening to us. Ibn al-Zubayr referred to what Ibn Umar had said and asked her to comment. She replied, 'May God have mercy on Abu 'Abd al-Rahman! The Messenger of God (SAAS) never made an 'umra that Ibn Umar did not witness, and he never made any 'umra that was not in Dhii al-Qa'da.'" Al-Bukhari and Muslim quoted this from a hadith of Jarir, from Mansur, from Mujahid, in similar terms. Abu Dawud and al-Nasa'i also related this from a hadith of Zuhayr, from Abu Ishaq, from Mujahid, to the effect that Ibn Umar was asked how many times the Messenger of God (SAAS) had made the 'umra. He replied that he had done so twice. 'A'isha then commented, "Ibn Umar knows well that the Messenger of God (SAAS) made the 'umra three times, apart from the one he combined with the hajjat al-wada', the 'farewell pilgrimage'." Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Yahya b. Adam, quoting Mufaddal, from Mansur, from Mujahid, who said, "I went inside the mosque with 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr and there we saw Ibn Umar leaning against 'A'isha's compartment while people there were performing the al-duha prayer. 'Urwa asked him, 'Abu 'Abd al-Rahman, what is this prayer?' 'It is a bid'a,' 'an innovation',' he replied. 'Urwa asked, 'Abu 'Abd al-Rahman, how many times did the Messenger of God (SAAS) perform the 'umra?' He replied, 'Four times; one of them was during Rajab.' We then heard 'A'isha picking her teeth inside her compartment. 'Urwa asked her, '(Is it then as) Abu 'Abd al-Rahman says, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed the 'umra four times, one of which was in Rajab?' She replied, 'May God have mercy on Abu 'Abd al-Rahman! The Prophet (SAAS) never performed the 'umra without him being present, and he never did so in Rajab.'" Al-Tirmidhi related it thus from Ahmad b. Muni', from al-Hasan b. Musa, from Shayban, from Mansur. He categorized it as hasan sahih gharib, "good, authentic, (yet) unilateral". Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Rawh, quoting Ibn Jurayj, who stated that he was informed by Muzahim b. Abi Muzahim, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. 'Abd Allah, from Mukharrish al-Ka'bi, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) left al-Ji'rana one evening to make the 'umra, entered Mecca and did so, then travelled that same night back to al-Ji'rana, being back there by morning, as if he had spent the night there. After sunset that day, he left from al-Ji'rana along the Sarif valley, joining the route to Medina there in Sarif. Mukharrish concluded, "That is why his 'umra remained unknown to many people." Imam Ahmad related this similarly from Yahya b. Sa'id, from Ibn Jurayj; this hadith is one of those that only the latter gave. The significance of this is that (the fact of) the 'umra from al-Ji'rana is well established from authentic sources that cannot be refuted or denied. Those who deny this can have no argument to compare with those who affirm it. But God knows best. They are, moreover, essentially unanimous that it took place in Dhii al-Qa'da after the attack upon al-Ta'if and the division of the spoils of Hunayn. The treatment of this by the hafiz Abu al-Qasim al-Tabarani in his great compendium is as follows, "Al-Hasan b. Ishaq al-Tusturi related to us, quoting 'Uthman b. Abi Shayba, quoting Muhammad b. al-Hasan al-Asadi, quoting Ibrahim b. Tahman, from Abu al-Zubayr, from 'Umayr, the freed-man of 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from al-Ta'if, he made camp at al-Ji'rana where he distributed the spoils. He then made the 'umra pilgrimage from there, two days before the end of Shawwal.'" This is very gharib, strange; its chain of authorities is controversial. But God knows best.
lessons: Scholarly disagreement among honest, sincere people is a natural part of preserving and transmitting knowledge, and should be navigated with respect and careful examination of evidence rather than dismissiveness.
Even the closest companions of the Prophet, who witnessed events firsthand, could differ in their recollections, reminding us that human memory is fallible and humility in knowledge is essential.

The meticulous care with which Islamic scholars preserved and cross-referenced the Prophet's biography reflects a deep love and reverence, and teaches us to approach sacred knowledge with seriousness and diligence.
practical_advice: When you encounter conflicting accounts or opinions about an important matter, resist the urge to accept the first version you hear; instead, patiently examine multiple sources and seek the weight of evidence.
Practice intellectual humility by acknowledging when you may have been mistaken in a memory or belief, as 'A'isha modeled in gently correcting Ibn Umar without attacking his character.
Use moments of scholarly or personal disagreement as opportunities to deepen your understanding rather than to win an argument, keeping the pursuit of truth as your primary goal.

title: Ya'la Witnesses the Prophet Receiving Divine Revelation
summary: Ya'la ibn Umayya, having long wished to witness the Prophet receiving divine revelation, was granted the opportunity at al-Ji'rrana and observed the profound physical signs of revelation on the Prophet.
story: Al-Bukhsri stated that it was related to him by Ya'qiib b. Ibrhim, quoting IsmXCil,quoting Ibn Jurayj, who said that 'A@' informed h i that Safwm b. Yaqa h. Umayya told him that Ya'la used to say, "I wish I could see the Messenger of God (SAAS) whiie he is receiving divine revelation. Whiie the Messenger of God (SAAS) was at al-Jicma being sheltered from the sun by a cloth, along with some of his Companions came a bedouin wearing a cloak and heavily perfumed. He approached him and asked, ('Messenger of God, what is your view of a man who adopts the state of ihrzm for the 'umra and puts on a cloak, after having applied perfume to himself?')" Wmar h. al-Khamb then signalled to Yaqa to approach. When Ya'la did so and placed his head inside (beneath the sunshade) he saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) to he very red in the face. He was immersed in this (divine revelation) for some time and then he was relieved of it.
lessons: Sincere longing to draw closer to truth and divine guidance can be fulfilled in unexpected ways and at unexpected times.
Witnessing the weight of divine responsibility on the Prophet deepens appreciation for the tremendous burden of prophethood.
Some desires, when rooted in genuine spiritual curiosity rather than worldly ambition, are answered by God in His perfect timing.
practical_advice: Cultivate sincere spiritual aspirations and trust that God is aware of the yearnings of your heart.
When you witness someone carrying a great burden or responsibility, respond with empathy and support rather than mere curiosity.
Reflect on how the Prophet bore the weight of revelation as a reminder to be grateful for the guidance that has been preserved and made accessible to you.

title: Mu'awiya's Arrow at al-Marwa During the 'Umra from al-Ji'rrana
summary: Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan narrated that he narrowly missed the Prophet's head with an arrowhead at al-Marwa, an incident scholars identified as occurring during the 'Umra from al-Ji'rrana.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by Yahya h. Sa'id, from Ibn Jurayj, who said that al-Hasan b. Muslim related to him, from Tawus, that Ibn 'Ahh% informed him that Mu'awiya told h i, "I missed the Messenger of God (SAAS) with an arrow head." Or else the words he spoke were, "I saw him missed by an iron arrowhead (shot at him) at al-Marwa." Both authors of the sahib collections gave it from a hadith of Ibn Jurayj. Muslim also related it, from a hadith of Sufyan b. Wyayna, from Hisham b. Hujayr, from Tams, from Ihn 'Abbns, from Mu'awiya. Abii D2'tid and al-Nasa'i also related it from a hadith of 'Abd al-Razzaq, from Ma'mar, from Ibn Tawus, from his father. 'Ahd All& b. al-Imam Ahmad stated that it was related to him by 'Amr b. Muhammad al-Naqid, quoting Abii Ahmad al-Zubayri, quoting Sufyan, from Ja'far b. Muhammad, from his father, from Ibn 'Abb%, from Mu'awiya, who said, "I missed (the arrow I fired at the) head of the Messenger of God (SAAS) at al-Marwa." What is implied above is that it is inferred that this was at the 'umra from al-Ji'rma. This is because on the 'umra at al-Hudaybiyya, he did not enter Mecca but was prevented from so doing, as has been explained heretofore. Regarding the 'umrat al-yadz', Abii Sufyan had not yet accepted Islam and none of the Meccans remained there when the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered it, having preferred to leave for that three-day period of his stay. As for the 'umra that was performed in conjunction with his bay; it is unanimously accepted that he did not free himself thereof. And so it is obvious that the mistake committed by Mu'awiya, son of Abii Sufym, may God he pleased with them both, when he shot close to the head of the Messenger of God (SAAS) at al-Marwa, could only have occurred during the 'umra from al-Ji'rzna, as we have stated. But God, Ahnighty is He, knows best.
lessons: God's protection of His Prophet was manifest even in the face of accidental or deliberate harm, reinforcing the believer's trust in divine protection.
Mistakes, even serious ones, can occur in the holiest of places and among those closest to the Prophet, reminding us of human fallibility.
Scholarly precision in verifying historical accounts demonstrates the importance of accuracy and integrity when handling religious knowledge.
practical_advice: When you make a serious mistake, do not let shame paralyze you; acknowledge it honestly, seek forgiveness, and move forward.
Trust that God protects what He wills; your role is to remain steadfast and not succumb to fear of every potential harm.
Approach sensitive historical accounts with fairness and careful reasoning rather than rushing to judgment.

title: The Prophet Appoints 'Attab ibn Asid as Governor of Mecca and Returns to Medina
summary: After completing the 'Umra from al-Ji'rrana, the Prophet appointed 'Attab ibn Asid as his deputy over Mecca and Mu'adh ibn Jabal as a teacher of faith, then returned to Medina.
story: Muhammad h. Ishaq, may God have mercy upon him, stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left al-Ji'rma to perform the 'umra and gave orders that the battle spoils be kept at Majanna, near Marr al-Zahmn." I observe that it is clear that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had retained some of the spoils to employ it to gain goodwill with those bedouin he might meet between Mecca and Medina. Ihn Ishaq went on, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had completed this 'umra he headed back to Medina, leaving 'Attab b. Asid as his deputy over Mecca. He also left with him Musdh b. Jabal to instruct the people in the faith and to teach them the Qur'an." Wrwa and Miisa h. Wqba relate that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had left Mu'adh and 'Attab as his deputies in Mecca before he had gone out to battle Hawzzin, and then reappointed them when he returned to Medina. Ibn H i s h h stated that he was informed that Zayd b. Aslam stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed 'Attab b. Asid in charge of Mecca, he awarded him payment of one dirham per day. 'Attzb arose and gave the following address, 'May God make hungry indeed the liver of one who still hungers on a dirham! The Messenger of God (SAAS) has awarded me one dirham every day; I have need of no one!'" Ibn Ishaq went on, "The Cumra of the Messenger of God (SAAS) took place in Dhii al-Qa'da and he arrived in Medina at the end of that month or early in Dha al-Hijja." Ibn H i s h h stated that he arrived there six days before the end of Dhii al-Qa'da, according to Aba 'Amr al-Madini. "That year the people performed the haj in the same manner as the Arabs had previously. That year, 8 AH, 'Attxb b. Asid performed the hajj along with the Muslims. "The people of al-T2if continued their polytheism and their abstention (from Islam) in their own city for the period between Dhii al-Qakla and Ramad& of 9 AH."
lessons: True leadership involves delegating wisely, ensuring that communities left behind are both governed justly and educated spiritually.
Contentment with what one is given, however modest, is a mark of integrity and dignity in leadership.
The gradual spread of Islam through teaching and patient presence in newly opened lands reflects a model of sustainable community building.
practical_advice: When given a responsibility, no matter how modest the compensation, approach it with dignity and gratitude rather than complaint.
Invest in the education and spiritual development of those in your care, as knowledge is the most lasting gift a leader can leave behind.
After completing a significant task or journey, take time to return to your foundation and community, renewing your commitments and relationships.

title: Ka'b ibn Zuhayr's Journey from Enmity to Repentance and the Poem 'Banat Su'ad'
summary: Ka'b ibn Zuhayr, a poet who had mocked the Prophet, received a warning from his brother Bujayr that he faced execution unless he repented, prompting him to travel to Medina and seek the Prophet's pardon.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned (to Medina) after his departure from al-Tz'if, Bujayr b. Zuhayr b. Aba Sulma wrote to his full brother Ka'b b. Zuhayr to inform him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had executed at Mecca some of the men who had satirized and harmed him with their poetry. He also told him how the remaining Quraysh poets - Ihn al-Ziba'ra and Hubayra b. Aba Wahb - had fled in disarray. He advised him, 'If you value your life, then hurry to the Messenger of God (SAAS), for he does not execute anyone who comes to him in repentance. If you do not do this, then flee far away.' Ka'b had previously spoken the lines, 'Transmit to Bujayr a message from me: "Confound you for what you said! Can you explain to us, since you have not yet, for what other reason did he lead you To a religion which I fmd none of his ancestors followed, nor you find yours to have done. If you do not accept it, I'll not be sorry nor merely say, if you stumble, 'Good luck!' "Al-Ma'mon" has given you to drink by it a full cup; and after the first, 'al-Ma'miin' has also given you a second drink."' Ibn H i s h m stated that a scholar in the poetry also quoted to him the following verses, "Who will deliver a message from me to Bujayr: 'Do you agree with what I told you at the mountain slope? You have drunk a full cup with "al-Ma'mon", and after the first draught he also gave you a second. You abandoned good guidance by following h i; to what is it, damned that you are, that he has led you? Into a religion you'll fmd no man nor woman ever served, nor any brother of yours. If you do not do as I say, I'll not be sad; though I'm not one merely to say, "Good luck!" if you stumble.'" He dispatched this to Bujayr who was reluctant to keep it from the Messenger of God (SAAS) and fmaUy recited it to him. When he heard the words, saqE bzha al-Ma'miin, 'k-Ma'miin has given you to drink' he commented, "He speaks the truth here, terrible liar though he is. I am the 'A-Ma'mm', the 'trustworthy one'." When he heard the words, "Into a religion you'll find no man nor woman ever served" he commented, "That's right; you'll not find his father or mother in it." (Ibn Ishaq narrated) "Bujayr then wrote back to Ka'b the following verses, 'Who will inform Ka'b, "Would you blame me for serving something foolish when it is very serious? It is to God, alone, not to al-Vzza or to al-Liit you should flee, while flight and safety are still possible, At a time when none shall slip away and escape except those who are Muslim, pure of heart. For the religion of Zuhayr is nothing, and that of Abn Sulma is forbidden to me."' (Ibn Ishaq went on) "When this message reached Ka'b, he was very apprehensive and those about him who were his enemies spread false rumours about him, saying, 'He's as good as dead!' "When he could see no alternative, he recited his ode praising the Messenger of God (SAAS). In it he referred to his fear and to the slander being spread by his enemies. He then left and went to Medina and stayed with a man he knew of Juhayna, so I have been told. That man took him next morning to the Messenger of God (SAAS) as he was performing the morning prayer and prayed with him. The man of Juhayna then pointed out to him the Messenger of God (SAAS), saying, 'That is he. Go over to him and ask him for your safety.' "I have been told that Ka'b did approach the Messenger of God (SAAS), sat down beside h i and placed his own hand in his. The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not know who he was. Ka'b then said, 'Messenger of God, Ka'b b. Zuhayr has come to ask you to guarantee his safety as a
lessons: Even those who have actively opposed truth and spoken against righteous people are welcomed back when they sincerely repent, reflecting the boundless mercy available to the one who turns back.
The courage to seek forgiveness, even when one fears rejection or punishment, is itself a form of spiritual strength and growth.
A brother's sincere concern and honest warning, even when delivering difficult news, can be an act of profound love that saves a life.
practical_advice: If you have wronged someone, do not let fear of their reaction prevent you from seeking reconciliation; take the difficult step toward repair.
When someone you care about is heading toward harm, find the courage to speak truth to them clearly and urgently, as Bujayr did for Ka'b.
In moments of fear and uncertainty, channel your anxiety into action by moving toward resolution rather than fleeing from it.

title: Ka'b ibn Zuhayr Seeks Pardon from the Prophet
summary: Ka'b ibn Zuhayr, a poet who had mocked the Prophet and whose execution had been authorized, came to the Prophet in disguise through his brother Bujayr's encouragement, declared his Islam, and received pardon. He then recited a famous ode praising the Prophet.
story:
"I have been told that Ka'b did approach the Messenger of God (SAAS), sat down beside him and placed his own hand in his. The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not know who he was. Ka'b then said, 'Messenger of God, Ka'b b. Zuhayr has come to ask you to guarantee his safety as a Muslim who is repentant. Would you accept this from him if he were to approach you with this request?' 'Yes, I would,' the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him. 'Then,' said Ka'b, 'I am Ka'b b. Zuhayr!'"

Ibn Ishaq continued, "'Asim b. Umar b. Qatada related to me that one of the ansar then jumped on him, saying, 'Messenger of God, let me execute the enemy of God!' He replied, 'Leave him alone! He has come expressing regret and repentance.'

"Ka'b b. Zuhayr was enraged at the ansar for how one of them had treated him; this was because none of the muhajirun spoke anything but good about him. In the ode that he delivered when he came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) he said, 'Suad has gone away and my heart is sad, enslaved to her, unransomable, and chain-bound; And Suad, the day of departure, when they left, was like a gazelle, her eyes down-cast and black-rimmed ... The Messenger is a light-giving illumination, an unsheathed sword of God, of fine Indian steel Amidst a band of Quraysh whose spokesman said in Mecca's valley, when they accepted Islam - "Go away!" They left, but not from cowardice in battle, nor without shields or weapons. They walk like proud camels, striking out in defence when the short, black men take flight, Proud chiefs, dressed for battle in chain-mail made by Da'od, Shining and full, its rings joined together like those of the qafast plant. They do not exult if their lances strike home, nor are they despairing if themselves struck. Enemy thrusts only ever strike their throats, and they are not afraid of the pools of death.'"

Ka'b and Bujayr, both sons of Zuhayr, travelled out as far as Abraq al-Azzaf. There Bujayr told Ka'b, "Stay here until I go to this man" - meaning the Messenger of God (SAAS) - "and hear what he says." Ka'b did remain there, while Bujayr went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who offered Islam to him and he accepted it. When news of this reached Ka'b, he spoke the following, "Send from me a message to Bujayr: 'To what - confound the rest - is it he is leading you? To a faith you'll find no one - mother, father or brother of yours - ever belonged. Abu Bakr poured out a full cup of it for you, while al-Ma'mun refilled it again and again.'"

When these verses reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he made permissible the execution of Ka'b, he said, 'Whoever encounters Ka'b should kill him!' Bujayr wrote to his brother telling him this and that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had authorized his execution. He told him to escape while he could. Thereafter he again wrote him saying, "You should know that the Messenger of God (SAAS) accepts when anyone comes and makes an affirmation of his faith in Islam and considers the past over and done with. If this note of mine reaches you, do come and accept Islam."

And so Ka'b did accept Islam and composed his ode praising the Messenger of God (SAAS). He came and made his camel kneel at the door of the mosque of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and entered it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was there with his Companions, like a food-table, with ring after ring of people seated around it, looking first towards one group and talking with them, next to another and talking with them.

Ka'b stated, "So I made my mount kneel at the mosque door. I recognized the Messenger of God (SAAS) from his description and went and sat down near him. I then affirmed my acceptance of Islam, saying, 'I bear witness that there is no god but God and that you are Muhammad, the Messenger of God. Safety, Messenger of God!' 'And who are you?' he asked. 'Ka'b b. Zuhayr,' I replied. 'That fellow who recited ...?' asked the Messenger of God (SAAS). He then turned and asked, 'What was it he recited, Abu Bakr?' Abu Bakr then recited, 'Al-Ma'mun has given you to drink by it a full cup; after the first, al-Ma'mun has also given you a second drink.' Ka'b said, 'Messenger of God, I did not recite like that.' 'How did you speak, then?' he asked. Ka'b said that what he had said was, 'Al-Ma'mun has given you to drink by it a full cup; after the first al-Ma'mun has also given you a second drink.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'Yes indeed, Ma'mun, "trustworthy".' Ka'b then recited to him the entire ode, to its end."

Abu Umar b. Abd al-Barr stated in his book al-Istiab that when Ka'b had completed the verses, "The Messenger is a light giving illumination, an unsheathed sword of God, of fine Indian steel ... I was told that the Messenger of God threatened me; yet one hopes for pardon from the Messenger of God." Abu Umar went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) gestured to those with him to listen well." It is given in some accounts that the Messenger of God (SAAS) presented Ka'b with his cloak when he recited the ode. The hafiz Abu al-Hasan b. al-Athir gave a similar account in his work al-Ghaba. He stated, "It is this cloak that is in the possession of the Caliphs."
lessons: Sincere repentance, no matter how late or how grave the offense, is accepted by those of true mercy and noble character — the door of return is rarely as closed as fear makes it seem.
A brother's honest counsel, even when the news is difficult, can be the greatest gift one receives; Bujayr's letter warning Ka'b and then urging him toward Islam was an act of profound love.

Genuine praise born from a transformed heart carries a power that no forced flattery can match; Ka'b's ode endured through history precisely because it emerged from real fear turned into real gratitude.
practical_advice: If you are estranged from someone because of past wrongs you committed, consider taking the courageous step of seeking reconciliation — many people, like the Prophet, are more willing to forgive than your fear tells you.
When you feel condemned or beyond redemption, remember that your perception of how closed the door is may not reflect reality; reach out, express genuine remorse, and let the other person decide.
Use your gifts — whether writing, speaking, or creating — as a means of sincere expression and healing rather than only as weapons; Ka'b's poetry became his path back to community and dignity.

title: Ka'b ibn Zuhayr Praises the Ansar in Verse
summary: After Ka'b ibn Zuhayr's famous poem drew criticism from the Ansar for not praising them, he composed additional verses honoring their nobility, sacrifice, and steadfast support of the Prophet Muhammad.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, '"Asib b. Umar b. Qamda said, "When Ka'b, in his poem, spoke the words, "when the short black men take flight" he could only have been referring to the arqiir because of how one of us had treated him, whereas he had singled out the Quraysh muhajirun for his praise. Since the aviir were therefore angry at him, he recited the following verses after he had accepted Islam. In them he praised the ansar and referred to the suffering they had undergone with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and to their success: "Let those who love a noble life remain in the cavalry of the fine agar. They inherited noble qualities from father to son; the finest of men are they, sons of the finest, Men who hurl from their arms strong spears, long like Indian swords. And they stare ahead, their eyes red as embers but their sight untired. Men who expend their souls for their Prophet by death in battles of hand and horse, They cleanse themselves with piety by the blood of the unbelievers they spill. They act like fierce, strong-necked lions used to hunting in secluded valleys. If you go to them so they may protect you, you'll feel as safe as if in the haunts of young mountain goats. At the battle of Badr they struck 'Ali such a blow as to bring down all of Niza. If the tribes had my knowledge of them, those whom I oppose would believe me. (They are) a people who, at times when the stars have given no rain, will still feed well their guests.""'
lessons: Acknowledging those who have been overlooked or hurt by our words is a sign of moral maturity and integrity.
True community requires recognizing the contributions of all its members, not only those we feel most comfortable with.
Poetry and sincere expression can be a powerful means of reconciliation and healing damaged relationships.
practical_advice: If you realize your words or actions have unintentionally excluded or hurt someone, take steps to acknowledge and correct that, even if it requires humility.
Seek to recognize and vocally appreciate the sacrifices of those around you, especially those who support you in difficult times.
Use creative expression — writing, speaking, or art — as a constructive tool to mend relationships and express genuine gratitude.

title: The Prophet's Remark About Ka'b's Wife Suad
summary: When Ka'b recited the line 'Suad has gone away' to the Prophet, the Prophet asked who Suad was and upon learning it was Ka'b's wife, reassured him she had not truly gone away, clarifying the emotional rather than juridical nature of the separation.
story: It is related that when Ka'b recited the line, "Susd has gone away ..." the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, "And who is Susd?" "My wife, Messenger of God," responded Ka'b. "Nay, she did not!" he then said. However, that is not true. It seems to imply that he imagined that his wife had gone away because of his acceptance of Islam. But it is obvious that what he was referring to was an emotional distancing, not a juridical one. But God, Almighty is He, knows best.
lessons: Fear of losing loved ones due to one's faith is a recognized and deeply human experience that deserves compassion.
Leaders and guides who offer reassurance during times of emotional turmoil play a vital role in the well-being of those around them.
Not every emotional distance in a relationship means a permanent or legal separation; feelings of loss can be real without reflecting permanent reality.
practical_advice: When anxiety about your relationships arises due to major life changes, seek reassurance from trusted people rather than assuming the worst.
Reflect on whether the distance you feel from loved ones is emotional and temporary, or something more concrete that needs to be addressed directly.
If someone close to you has made a significant life decision, offer them reassurance and stability rather than letting them suffer in silence over fear of rejection.

title: Ka'b ibn Zuhayr Recites His Poem in the Mosque
summary: Ka'b ibn Zuhayr recited his renowned poem 'Suad Has Gone Away' to the Prophet Muhammad in the mosque, an event recorded through various chains of narration in Islamic historical tradition.
story: Ibn Hisham stated, "It is said that when Ka'b recited to him his poem, 'SUsd has gone away' the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Even though you have not spoken well of the ansar, don't they still deserve that?' And so Ka'b spoke these verses which are included in a qasida of his." He went on, "I learned on the authority of 'Ali b. Zayd b. Jad'an that Ka'b b. Zuhayr recited the verses of 'Susd has gone away' to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the mosque." The hafiz al-Bayhaqi related it, with its chain of authorities given above, back to Ibrahim b. al-Mundhir al-Hizami (who said), "Ma'n b. 'Isa related to me, al-Aftas, from Ibn Jad'an, who gave quoting Muhammad b. 'Abd al-Rahman it." This chain of transmission is mursal, incomplete.
lessons: Acts of sincere repentance and creative devotion, such as poetry in praise of the Prophet, can restore standing within a community after wrongdoing.
Sacred spaces like the mosque are not only for formal worship but have historically been places of community, reconciliation, and expression.
The careful preservation of chains of transmission reflects the Islamic tradition's commitment to accuracy and intellectual honesty.
practical_advice: Use meaningful, sincere expression to reconnect with a community or relationship that you may have strained through past words or actions.
Do not be deterred from seeking reconciliation because of imperfect circumstances; sincerity matters more than perfection of form.
When relying on historical or traditional sources, be honest about the strength and limitations of the evidence, as scholars of hadith modeled throughout Islamic history.

title: The Cloak of the Prophet Given to Ka'b ibn Zuhayr
summary: Historical accounts suggest the Prophet Muhammad presented Ka'b ibn Zuhayr with his cloak after Ka'b recited his famous ode, a cloak said to have later passed into the possession of the Caliphs, though the chain of transmission for this account is considered unsatisfactory by the author.
story: God belong all praise, all credit. I observe that it is given in some accounts that the Messenger of God (SAAS) presented Ka'b with his cloak when he recited the ode. Al-Sarsari wrote a verse on this in some of his mada'ih, "poems of praise". The hafiz Abu al-Hasan b. al-Athir gave a similar account in his work al-Ghaba. He stated, "It is this cloak that is in the possession of the Caliphs." I note that this matter is very broadly known. However, I do not see in any of these well-known written accounts any chain of authorities that I find satisfactory. But God knows best.
lessons: Generosity from a leader toward someone who has sincerely repented is a powerful symbol of forgiveness and belonging.
The transmission of physical objects linked to the Prophet carried deep symbolic meaning for the Muslim community across generations.
Scholarly integrity demands honesty about the reliability of traditions, even when those traditions are widely beloved and emotionally resonant.
practical_advice: When offering forgiveness to someone who has wronged you, consider whether a tangible gesture of welcome and reconciliation would help them feel truly accepted.
Appreciate the symbolic weight of objects and gestures in your own life and community as sources of connection and continuity.
When sharing stories or traditions, be transparent about their sources and reliability rather than overstating certainty for the sake of a compelling narrative.

title: Ka'b ibn Zuhayr — His Poetry and Legacy
summary: Ibn Abd al-Barr praises Ka'b ibn Zuhayr as one of the finest Arab poets, superior to his brother Bujayr though their father Zuhayr surpassed them both, and quotes some of his most celebrated verses on fate, hope, and the human condition.
story: Sheikh Abu Wmar b. 'Abd al-Barr, God have mercy upon him, stated in his book al-Isti'ab fi Ma'rifaat al-Ashab after having given some biographical details about Ka'b b. Zuhayr, "Ka'b b. Zuhayr was a prolific poet considered pre-eminent in his class, along with his brother Bujayr. Ka'b was the better poet of the two, while their father Zuhayr was superior to both. Among the fine poetry of Ka'b b. Zuhayr are the lines, 'Were I surprised by anything, it would be how a young man goes forward though his fate is hidden from him. He will strive to achieve what he cannot attain; the soul is one, while worry is all-encompassing. As long as a man lives, hope extends before him; the sight goes out as far as the full extent of space.'" Thereafter Ibn 'Abd al-Barr gave other verses too numerous to mention. He did not record the date of his death. Similarly, Abu al-Hasan b. al-Athir, in his book al-Ghaba fi Ma'rifat al-Sahaba did not give this information. However, it is related that his father died one year before the mission of the Messenger of God (SAAS). But God knows best.
lessons: Great poetry endures because it captures universal truths about the human struggle with uncertainty, ambition, and hope.
Every person strives toward goals whose outcome is unknown to them; humility before God's will is a source of peace rather than defeat.
The legacy a person leaves through their words and deeds can outlive biographical details that time forgets.
practical_advice: Reflect on the poetic wisdom of those who came before you as a source of comfort and perspective when you face uncertainty in your own life.
When you find yourself consumed by worry about outcomes beyond your control, remind yourself that hope and effort are within your power even when results are not.
Invest in forms of expression and legacy — writing, mentorship, creative work — that will carry meaning beyond your own lifetime.

title: Al-Suhayli's Verse on the Prophet's Description
summary: Al-Suhayli quotes what he considers among the finest verses of Ka'b ibn Zuhayr, which describe the Prophet Muhammad being carried by his brown she-camel, compared to the full moon on a dark night, embodying both religion and nobility.
story: Al-Suhayli stated, "Among the finest verses of Ka'b b. Zuhayr are those he spoke in praise of the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'The brown she-camel carries him on, wrapped in his cloak, like the full moon shining bright on a dark night. Inside his wrappings or folds of his cloak there is such religion and such nobility that God well knows.'"
lessons: The use of beautiful imagery to honor the Prophet reflects a tradition of expressing love and reverence through art and language.
Comparing the Prophet to the full moon is a recurring metaphor in Islamic poetry that conveys guidance, light, and serenity for all who behold him.
Authentic praise of noble figures inspires admiration and devotion that transcends the moment of composition.
practical_advice: Express gratitude and admiration for those who have deeply influenced your life through sincere and considered words, not only through actions.
Seek beauty and meaning in the natural world — the moon, the light, the night — as sources of spiritual reflection and connection to the divine.
Allow poetry, literature, and art to open your heart to deeper dimensions of faith and love that prose alone cannot always reach.

title: Overview of Major Events in 8 AH
summary: The year 8 AH was among the most consequential in Islamic history, encompassing the Battle of Mu'ta, the Conquest of Mecca, the Battle of Hunayn, the Siege of Ta'if, and the performance of Umra from al-Ji'rana.
story: Chapter Concerning those well-known events and deaths that occurred in 8 AH. In Jumada that year the battle of at-Mu'ta took place. In Ramadan there was the expedition resulting in the conquest of Mecca. Thereafter, in Shawwal, there was the conflict with Hawazin at Hunayn which was followed by the siege of al-Ta'if. In Dhu al-Qa'da the 'umra was performed from al-Ji'rana and the Messenger of God (SAAS) then returned to Medina for the remainder of that year. Al-Waqidi stated that his return to Medina from that journey came six days prior to the end of Dhu al-Hijja.
lessons: Major transformations in life — personal or communal — often come in rapid succession rather than in isolation, requiring sustained endurance and trust in God.
The rhythm of sacred observance, such as Umra, was maintained by the Prophet even in the midst of intense military and political events, reflecting the centrality of worship.
History is shaped by pivotal years; understanding the sequence and context of events helps us appreciate the depth of what those who came before us endured.
practical_advice: When multiple major challenges arrive at once in your life, resist the urge to feel that difficulty is endless; many transformations arrive together precisely because change is happening at a foundational level.
Maintain your spiritual practices and routines even during intense periods of difficulty or transition, as the Prophet maintained worship during the most consequential year of his mission.
Take time to study and understand the broader context of the events that shaped your community or faith tradition, as this deepens gratitude and resilience.

title: The Prophet Sends 'Amr ibn al-As to Collect Jizya from the Magians
summary: In 8 AH, the Prophet sent 'Amr ibn al-As to the sons of al-Julundi of Banu Azd in Oman, where the jizya tax was collected from the Magians and bedouins of that region.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "That year the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent 'Amr b. al-'As to Ja'far and 'Amr, sons of al-Julundi, of Banu Azd; the jizya tax was taken from the magi, 'the Magians', of the country of these two men and from the bedouins in the areas around there."
lessons: The Prophet's governance extended administrative and diplomatic reach even in the midst of major military campaigns, demonstrating comprehensive and organized leadership.
The institution of the jizya reflected a regulated relationship between the Muslim state and non-Muslim communities living within it, governed by defined rights and obligations.
Delegation of trust-worthy envoys to distant regions reflects the Prophet's confidence in his Companions and his commitment to organized governance.
practical_advice: Effective leadership requires the ability to delegate responsibilities to capable and trustworthy people, especially during periods of intense activity.
Learn about the historical and legal context of Islamic governance practices before forming judgments based on modern assumptions.
When you are entrusted with representing others in an official or formal capacity, carry that responsibility with integrity and fairness, as the Companions did.

title: The Prophet's Marriage to Fatima bint al-Dahhak and Her Seeking Refuge
summary: In Dhu al-Qa'da of 8 AH the Prophet married Fatima bint al-Dahhak al-Kilabiyya, but she invoked refuge in God from him upon his first visit, after which he left her; some accounts say she was given the choice between the worldly life and the Prophet and chose the former.
story: "That year the Messenger of God (SAAS) married Fatima, daughter of al-Dahhak b. Sufyan al-Kilabi, in Dhu al-Qa'da. She, however, spoke a plea of refuge in God from him, and so he forsook her. It is also said that he gave her the choice and that she chose the worldly life and so he left her." Sources suggest that she had been tricked into saying this by the other wives of the Prophet (SAAS), who were envious of her beauty.
lessons: The Prophet's response of leaving rather than pressing or coercing reflects his profound respect for personal choice and dignity, even in intimate matters.
Jealousy and envy within communities can lead to harmful actions against the innocent; guarding one's heart against envy is a spiritual imperative.
Major life decisions made under the influence of others' manipulation may carry long-lasting consequences; sincerity and independent judgment are essential.
practical_advice: Before making a major life decision, ensure your choice is genuinely your own and not the product of pressure, manipulation, or the influence of those with competing interests.
If you discover that you have been misled into a harmful decision, seek honest counsel and be willing to acknowledge the mistake with humility.
Be aware of the role envy can play in the advice and actions of those around you, and guard yourself from becoming an instrument of someone else's jealousy.

title: The Birth of Ibrahim, Son of the Prophet and Maria the Copt
summary: In Dhu al-Hijja of 8 AH, Ibrahim — the son of the Prophet Muhammad and Maria the Copt — was born, arousing envy among the Prophet's other wives; Abu Rafi' brought the good news to the Prophet, who rewarded him with a slave.
story: "In Dhu al-Hijja that year, Ibrahim, the son of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Mariya, the Copt, was born. The envy of his other wives, the ummahaat al-mu'minin, was aroused when she bore him a male child. The midwife was Salma, the freed-woman of the Messenger of God (SAAS). She came out and informed Abu Rafi', who went and delivered the good tidings to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who presented him with the gift of a slave. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent him (the baby) to Umm Barra, daughter of al-Mundhir b. Usayd b. Khidash b. 'Amir b. Ghanm b. 'Adi b. al-Najjar and her husband al-Bara' b. Aws b. Khalid b. al-Jazl b. 'Awf b. Mabdhal."
lessons: The birth of a child is among the greatest blessings, deserving of gratitude and celebration, as the Prophet demonstrated by rewarding the bearer of good news.
Envy within families and communities is a deeply human struggle that even the households of the most righteous are not immune to; awareness and spiritual discipline are necessary to manage it.
The care the Prophet took in placing his infant son with a nursing family reflects the importance of community and practical support in raising children.
practical_advice: Celebrate the good news in your community generously, as the Prophet rewarded Abu Rafi' for bringing him the glad tidings of Ibrahim's birth.
Be mindful of how envy can arise within close groups when blessings are unevenly distributed, and actively work to cultivate contentment and gratitude in your own heart.
When welcoming a new child or major blessing into your life, surround that blessing with care, community, and practical support rather than trying to manage it alone.

title: Destruction of the Idol Al-Uzza at Nakhla by Khalid ibn al-Walid
summary: Five days before the end of Ramadan in 8 AH, Khalid ibn al-Walid destroyed the temple of al-Uzza at Nakhla, between Mecca and Ta'if, as part of the broader campaign to eliminate idol worship following the Conquest of Mecca.
story: We earlier referred to the destruction by Khalid b. al-Walid of the temple where al-'Uzza was worshipped at Nakhla, between Mecca and al-Ta'if. That was five days prior to the end of Ramadan that year.
lessons: The elimination of practices harmful to spiritual and communal well-being sometimes requires decisive, courageous action by those entrusted with leadership.
The timing of the destruction of al-Uzza — during the sacred month of Ramadan — shows that the pursuit of righteousness does not wait for a more convenient season.
The transformation of a society's relationship with worship requires both inner conviction and outer action; the Prophet mobilized both among his Companions.
practical_advice: Identify harmful habits, attachments, or influences in your own life that function as modern 'idols' — things you rely on instead of God — and take concrete steps to remove them.
Do not delay necessary spiritual or personal transformation by waiting for a perfect moment; act in accordance with your values when the opportunity arises.
Support those around you who are working to change harmful patterns, as the Prophet sent his Companions to remove the idols rather than expecting the transformation to happen on its own.

title: Destruction of the Idol Suwa' at Ruhat by 'Amr ibn al-As
summary: In 8 AH, 'Amr ibn al-As destroyed the idol Suwa' at Ruhat, which had been worshipped by the tribe of Hudhayl; nothing was found in its treasury.
story: Al-Waqidi stated, "That year the destruction was carried out of Suwa', (the idol) at Ruhat that Hudhayl had worshipped. 'Amr b. al-'As, may God be pleased with him, destroyed it, but he found nothing in its treasury."
lessons: False objects of worship hold no real power; when examined honestly, the supposed treasures of idols are empty.
The willingness of the Companions to travel and act against deeply entrenched cultural practices shows the depth of their commitment to their faith.
The emptiness found in the idol's treasury is a metaphor for the ultimate emptiness of anything worshipped other than God.
practical_advice: When you finally confront a fear, habit, or false belief that has held power over you, you may find that it has far less substance than you imagined; courage reveals this.
Be willing to challenge cultural or social norms that conflict with your values, even when those norms have deep historical roots in your community.
Reflect on what you invest your energy and hope in, and ask honestly whether those things are truly worthy of what you are giving them.

title: Destruction of the Idol Manat at al-Mushallal by Sa'd ibn Zayd
summary: Sa'd ibn Zayd al-Ashhali destroyed the idol Manat at al-Mushallal, which had been venerated by the Aws and Khazraj, the Ansar tribes, as part of the systematic dismantling of pre-Islamic idol worship in 8 AH.
story: (The idol) Manat was also destroyed at al-Mushallal; it had formerly been venerated by Aws and Khazraj, the ansar tribes. Sa'b b. Zayd al-Ashhali, may God be pleased with him, destroyed it.
lessons: True faith requires breaking with even the most deeply rooted ancestral traditions when they conflict with divine guidance.
The fact that it was a member of the Ansar who destroyed an idol venerated by his own tribe shows the transformative power of sincere belief.
Collective acts of spiritual renewal — even when they involve dismantling the past — can mark a decisive turning point for a community.
practical_advice: Examine whether any inherited beliefs, practices, or loyalties in your own life conflict with your deepest spiritual convictions, and have the courage to address that tension.
Honor the courage it takes to break with community tradition for the sake of truth; recognize this in yourself and others without minimizing how difficult it is.
Understand that communal spiritual transformation is often built on the brave individual choices of people willing to act against the grain of their own social group.

title: The Banu Khath'am Destroy Their Temple — the 'Yemeni Ka'ba'
summary: Al-Bukhari records that the Banu Khath'am destroyed a temple they had built in imitation of the Ka'ba in Mecca, which they called the 'Yemeni Ka'ba', as part of the sweeping destruction of idolatrous sites following the Conquest of Mecca.
story: Al-Bukhari made reference, after the material relating to the conquest of Mecca, to Banu Khath'am's destruction of the temple where they used to worship, and which they called the "Yement", the "southern" or "Yemeni", Ka'ba, in imitation of the Ka'ba at Mecca. The latter they knew as the "Shami", the "northern" or "Syrian", Ka'ba.
lessons: Imitation of sacred symbols for the purposes of idol worship reflects the deep human longing for the divine, which must be guided toward genuine worship rather than substitutes.
The willingness of a community to destroy its own sacred sites when they recognized the truth reflects an extraordinary capacity for self-correction and spiritual renewal.
The Ka'ba in Mecca stands as the singular, unrivaled center of monotheistic worship; all imitations ultimately reveal the irreplaceable nature of the original.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether any of the structures, routines, or symbols in your spiritual life are genuine expressions of faith or are imitations that substitute for a deeper connection with God.
Be willing, as the Banu Khath'am were, to dismantle even elaborate and long-standing structures in your life when you recognize that they no longer serve truth.
Seek out authentic sources of spiritual nourishment rather than substitutes or imitations that may feel familiar but do not lead to genuine growth.

title: Jarir ibn 'Abd Allah and the Destruction of Dhu al-Khalasa
summary: The Prophet asked Jarir ibn 'Abd Allah al-Bajali to destroy the temple of Dhu al-Khalasa in Yemen; despite Jarir's weakness on horseback, the Prophet prayed for him, and Jarir led 150 cavalry to destroy the temple and convert the fortune-teller found there.
story: Al-Bukhari stated: "Yusuf b. Musa related to us, quoting Abu Usama, from Isma'il b. Abu Khalid, from Qays, from Jarir, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked me, "Will you not relieve me of Dhu al-Khalasa?" "Yes indeed!" I replied. So I set off with a force of 150 cavalry from (Banu) Ahmas, who were good horsemen. I used not to feel secure on horses and I told this to the Prophet (SAAS). He hit me on the chest with his hand so that I could see its imprint and said, "O God, set him firmly, and make him a guide who is rightly led." And I never fell off a horse thereafter.'" Dhu al-Khalasa was a temple in Yemen belonging to Banu Khath'am and Banu Bajayla which contained idols they worshipped. Al-Bukhari's account continued, "Jarir went there and destroyed it. When he arrived he found a man who foretold using divining arrows. He was told, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) is here! If he gets hold of you he will behead you!' "While one day he was using his divining arrows, Jarir came and stood over the man and said, 'You had better break them up and testify that there is no god but God! Otherwise, I'll strike your neck!' The fortune teller broke them and so testified. "Then Jarir sent one of the Banu Ahmas men, called Abu Artat to the Prophet (SAAS) to inform him of the good news. When the man reached him, he said, 'Messenger of God, by him who sent you with the truth, by the time I left it (the temple) it was like nothing but a scabby camel!' Thereat the Messenger of God (SAAS) blessed the horses and men of Banu Ahmas five times." Muslim related this with various lines, from Isma'il b. Abu Khalid, from Qays b. Abu Hazim, from Jarir b. 'Abd Allah al-Bajali, in similar words.
lessons: When we are honest with those in authority about our limitations, we open the door for divine and human support that can overcome those very weaknesses.
The Prophet's physical gesture of support — striking Jarir on the chest and praying for him — shows that leadership includes personally investing in the capacity of those you send on missions.
Even those who resist the truth can be transformed through direct, honest engagement rather than violence; the fortune-teller's conversion upon Jarir's challenge is a testament to this.
practical_advice: Do not hide your vulnerabilities from those who are guiding or leading you; honest disclosure invites support and can be the very thing that unlocks your capacity to succeed.
When you face a task that seems beyond your ability, seek the prayers and encouragement of those you trust, as Jarir did with the Prophet, and then move forward in trust.
When confronting harmful practices in your community, engage directly and with clarity rather than passivity, offering the person an honest path to change before resorting to harsher measures.
